topics.title: Vehicle Resistances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Vehicle Resistances # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Vehicle Resistances? In automotive and electric vehicle engineering, **Vehicle Resistances** (also called **Tractive Resistances** or **Road Loads**) are the cumulative external forces that a vehicle's powertrain must overcome to maintain or accelerate motion along a given path. The sum of these resistances dictates the **Total Tractive Effort (TTE)** required at the wheels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (PEMET863 – Module 1):** > Vehicle resistances are the opposing forces acting against the longitudinal motion of a vehicle. They are composed of four primary components: **Rolling Resistance (F_rr)**, **Aerodynamic Drag (F_ad)**, **Grade/Climbing Resistance (F_g)**, and **Inertia/Acceleration Resistance (F_i)**. The total tractive effort equation is the foundational design equation for any electric or hybrid vehicle powertrain: $$F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{g} + F_{i}$$ --- ## 1.2 The Four Fundamental Resistances ### (a) Rolling Resistance (F_rr) The force resisting motion due to the deformation of tires and the road surface at the contact patch. It is a **velocity-dependent** mechanical loss (at low speeds, modeled as constant; at higher speeds, proportional to velocity). ### (b) Aerodynamic Drag (F_ad) The resistance caused by the air flowing around the vehicle body. It is **proportional to the square of velocity**, making it the dominant resistance at highway speeds (> 80 km/h). ### (c) Grade Resistance (F_g) The component of the vehicle's weight acting along the slope when the vehicle travels on an inclined road. It is **independent of velocity** but depends on road gradient (%) or angle (θ). ### (d) Inertia / Acceleration Resistance (F_i) The force required to overcome the vehicle's translational mass inertia and the rotational inertia of the rotating components (wheels, gears, motor rotor). It is proportional to **acceleration**. --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: Pushing a Stroller Uphill > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Stroller on a Hilly Footpath"** > Imagine you are pushing a baby stroller up a slope on a rough cobblestone footpath while a strong wind blows against you, and you want to reach the top faster (accelerate). > - The **rough cobblestones** create *Rolling Resistance* — a constant friction you feel no matter how fast you walk. > - The **wind blowing in your face** is *Aerodynamic Drag* — the faster you push, the harder the wind pushes back. > - The **uphill slope** is *Grade Resistance* — gravity pulling the stroller backward down the slope. > - Your effort to **speed up** (accelerate) is *Inertia Resistance* — the stroller's "heaviness in motion" resisting a change in speed. > > The **total force in your arms** = Sum of all four. This is exactly what an electric motor at the wheels of an EV must supply! --- ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² | | Air density (sea level, 20°C) | $\rho$ | **1.225** | kg/m³ | | Gravitational acceleration (KTU standard) | $g$ | **9.8** | m/s² | > [!TIP] > In KTU numerical problems, **use g = 9.81 m/s²** unless the question explicitly specifies 9.8 or 10. Always carry the units through every step — marks are awarded for dimensional consistency in the board exam. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Rolling Resistance — F_rr Rolling resistance arises primarily due to **hysteresis losses** in the tire rubber. As the tire rolls, the contact patch deforms, dissipating energy as heat. The empirical formula used in KTU board problems is: $$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$$ Where: - $\mu_{rr}$ = **Coefficient of Rolling Resistance** (dimensionless). Typical values: 0.008 (radial tire on smooth road), 0.015 (car tire on concrete), 0.030 (truck tire, soft surface). - $m$ = Vehicle mass in **kg** - $g$ = 9.81 m/s² - $\theta$ = Road slope angle. For a flat road ($\theta = 0$), $\cos(\theta) = 1$. **Engineering Insight:** Notice the **$\cos(\theta)$** term. On a steep uphill, the normal force on the tires decreases, so rolling resistance *decreases slightly* — counter-intuitive but physically correct. --- ## 2.2 Aerodynamic Drag — F_ad Aerodynamic drag is the resistance from the air molecules being displaced and sheared by the vehicle body. The classical fluid mechanics equation is: $$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$ Where: - $\rho$ = Air density (**1.225 kg/m³** at sea level) - $C_d$ = Coefficient of drag (dimensionless). EVs are designed for low $C_d$: Tesla Model S ≈ **0.24**, Toyota Prius ≈ **0.25**, typical sedan ≈ 0.30–0.35. - $A_f$ = Frontal area of the vehicle in **m²** - $v$ = Vehicle velocity in **m/s** (NOT km/h — a common KTU exam trap!) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Exam Trap:** If the velocity is given in **km/h**, you MUST convert to m/s by dividing by 3.6. Forgetting this conversion leads to an answer ~13× larger and full mark deduction. **Real-World Utility:** This is why EVs adopt teardrop shapes, closed grilles, underbody panels, and rear diffusers — every 0.01 reduction in $C_d$ extends EV range by ~1–2%. --- ## 2.3 Grade Resistance — F_g When a vehicle climbs a slope of angle $\theta$, the component of gravitational force acting parallel to the road (opposing motion) is: $$F_{g} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$$ For small angles (typical roads), engineers use the **grade (%)** approximation: $$\sin(\theta) \approx \tan(\theta) = \frac{\text{grade \%}}{100}$$ So the working formula becomes: $$F_{g} = m \cdot g \cdot \frac{\text{grade \%}}{100}$$ **Engineering Insight:** A **6% grade** means a rise of 6 m for every 100 m of horizontal travel. For a 1500 kg vehicle on a 6% grade, $F_g = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.06 = 882.9$ N — a substantial continuous load on the motor. --- ## 2.4 Acceleration (Inertia) Resistance — F_i This resistance has **two components**: translational mass and rotational mass of the drivetrain. $$F_{i} = m \cdot a + m \cdot a \cdot \delta = m \cdot a \cdot (1 + \delta)$$ Or in expanded form: $$F_{i} = (m + m_{rotational,equiv}) \cdot a$$ Where: - $a$ = Linear acceleration in m/s² - $\delta$ = **Mass factor / Rotational inertia factor** (dimensionless). Typical EV values: 1.05–1.10 (gear-coupled EV); conventional ICE: 1.1–1.3. **Engineering Insight:** $\delta$ accounts for the equivalent mass of rotating components (wheels, tires, motor rotor, gears, half-shafts). KTU board problems often use $\delta$ directly without further derivation. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Quick Revision Table | # | Resistance | Formula (General) | Formula (Flat Road) | Depends on | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Rolling Resistance | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$ | Mass, $\mu_{rr}$ | | 2 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$ | Same | $v^2$ | | 3 | Grade Resistance | $F_{g} = m g \sin(\theta)$ | $F_{g} = 0$ | Mass, slope | | 4 | Inertia Resistance | $F_{i} = m a (1 + \delta)$ | Same | Mass, $a$ | | **Total** | **TTE** | $F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{g} + F_{i}$ | — | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Power Required at Wheels:** $P_{wheel} = F_{te} \cdot v$ (in Watts when F is in N and v is in m/s). This is the input to motor sizing problems — a frequent KTU 14-mark question. --- ## 2.6 Why This Matters in EV/HEV Design The **Tractive Effort equation** is the starting point for: 1. **Motor power rating** selection (kW) 2. **Battery pack sizing** (kWh) — energy consumed = $\int F_{te} \cdot v \, dt$ over a drive cycle 3. **Range estimation** (km per charge) 4. **Regenerative braking potential** — only $F_{rr}$ and $F_{ad}$ are recoverable (not $F_g$ at constant velocity; not $F_i$ during deceleration) # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Total Tractive Effort Equation The derivation starts from **Newton's Second Law** applied to a vehicle of mass $m$ moving along an inclined road with acceleration $a$: $$\sum F_x = m \cdot a$$ The forces acting along the direction of motion (positive = forward): - $F_{te}$ = Tractive effort from wheels (forward) - $F_{rr}$ = Rolling resistance (backward) - $F_{ad}$ = Aerodynamic drag (backward) - $F_{g}$ = Grade component of weight (backward, when going up) Therefore: $$F_{te} - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{g} = m \cdot a$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{g} + m \cdot a}$$ When rotational inertia is included (factor $\delta$): $$F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{g} + m \cdot a \cdot (1 + \delta)$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Power Required at Wheels Power is the rate of doing work. For a vehicle moving at velocity $v$ against total resistance $F_{te}$: $$P_{wheel} = F_{te} \cdot v$$ Substituting $F_{te}$: $$P_{wheel} = (F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{g} + m \cdot a) \cdot v$$ Expanded form (flat road, $\theta = 0$, $a = 0$): $$P_{wheel} = \left(\mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2\right) \cdot v$$ $$P_{wheel} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^3$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Power to overcome aerodynamic drag scales as $v^3$. Doubling the speed requires **8× the power** for the same vehicle. This is why highway driving drastically reduces EV range. --- ## 3.3 Detailed Step-by-Step: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem [KTU University Exam Style]:** A passenger EV has the following specifications: - Mass $m = 1500$ kg (including 75 kg driver) - Coefficient of rolling resistance $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$ - Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.28$ - Frontal area $A_f = 2.2$ m² - Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ - Road gradient = 5% (uphill) - Target constant velocity $v = 90$ km/h - Rotational mass factor $\delta = 1.08$ **Find:** Total tractive effort, power required at wheels, and identify the dominant resistance. ### Step 1: Unit Conversions Convert velocity from km/h to m/s: $$v = 90 \, \frac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} \times \frac{1000 \, \text{m}}{3600 \, \text{s}} = 25 \, \text{m/s}$$ Convert 5% grade to angle: $\tan(\theta) = 0.05 \Rightarrow \theta = \arctan(0.05) = 2.86°$ For small angles: $\sin(\theta) \approx 0.05$, $\cos(\theta) \approx 0.999 \approx 1$ ### Step 2: Calculate Rolling Resistance $$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$$ $$F_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 1$$ $$F_{rr} = 176.58 \, \text{N}$$ ### Step 3: Calculate Aerodynamic Drag $$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \times (25)^2$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \times 625$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 1375$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 471.625$$ $$F_{ad} = 235.81 \, \text{N}$$ ### Step 4: Calculate Grade Resistance $$F_{g} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta) = m \cdot g \cdot 0.05$$ $$F_{g} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 = 735.75 \, \text{N}$$ ### Step 5: Acceleration Resistance For constant velocity: $a = 0$, hence: $$F_{i} = m \cdot a \cdot (1 + \delta) = 0 \, \text{N}$$ ### Step 6: Total Tractive Effort $$F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{g} + F_{i}$$ $$F_{te} = 176.58 + 235.81 + 735.75 + 0$$ $$F_{te} = 1148.14 \, \text{N}$$ ### Step 7: Power Required at Wheels $$P_{wheel} = F_{te} \cdot v = 1148.14 \times 25 = 28703.5 \, \text{W} \approx 28.7 \, \text{kW}$$ ### Step 8: Dominance Analysis | Resistance | Value (N) | % of Total | |---|---|---| | $F_{rr}$ | 176.58 | 15.4% | | $F_{ad}$ | 235.81 | 20.5% | | $F_g$ | 735.75 | **64.1% (Dominant)** | **Conclusion:** At 90 km/h on a 5% grade, **grade resistance dominates**. The motor must deliver at least 28.7 kW to the wheels. Motor-to-wheel efficiency (~90%) means the battery must supply ~31.9 kW. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ICE vs EV | Aspect | Conventional ICE Vehicle | Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) | |---|---|---| | Energy source for overcoming $F_{ad}$ | Petrol chemical energy | Battery electrical energy | | Recoverable via regen braking? | No (engine braking only) | Yes (significant, ~30%) | | Typical $C_d$ | 0.30–0.40 | 0.22–0.28 | | Effect of $F_{ad} \propto v^2$ | Range drops sharply at high speed | Range drops sharply at high speed | | Grade resistance strategy | Gear down + throttle | High-torque motor (low-speed torque) | | Mass factor $\delta$ | 1.1–1.3 (drivetrain heavy) | 1.05–1.10 (fewer rotating parts) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Free Body Diagram — Forces on a Vehicle Climbing a Slope ```mermaid flowchart TD A["VEHICLE BODY
(Total Mass m)"] -->|Weight mg
acts downward| B["ROAD SURFACE
Inclined at angle theta"] A -->|Tractive Effort F_te
acts forward| C["DIRECTION OF MOTION
up the slope"] A -->|Rolling Resistance F_rr
opposes motion| D["TIRE CONTACT PATCH"] A -->|Aerodynamic Drag F_ad
opposes motion| E["AIRFLOW
relative wind"] A -->|Grade Component mg sin theta
along slope downward| F["GRAVITATIONAL
COMPONENT"] A -->|Inertia Force ma
opposes acceleration| G["ACCELERATION VECTOR
a forward"] B -->|Normal Force N = mg cos theta| H["TIRE
DEFORMATION"] D --> H ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Resistance Calculation Engine ```mermaid flowchart LR INPUT1["Vehicle Mass m (kg)"] --> CORE INPUT2["Velocity v (m/s)"] --> CORE INPUT3["Road Grade percent"] --> CORE INPUT4["Acceleration a (m/s^2)"] --> CORE INPUT5["Coefficient mu_rr"] --> CORE INPUT6["Drag Coefficient C_d"] --> CORE INPUT7["Frontal Area A_f (m^2)"] --> CORE INPUT8["Mass Factor delta"] --> CORE CORE["TRACTIVE EFFORT
CALCULATION MODULE
(Newton's 2nd Law)"] CORE --> RR["F_rr = mu_rr * m * g * cos(theta)"] CORE --> AD["F_ad = 0.5 * rho * C_d * A_f * v^2"] CORE --> GR["F_g = m * g * sin(theta)"] CORE --> IN["F_i = m * a * (1 + delta)"] RR --> SUM["SUMMATION BLOCK
F_te = F_rr + F_ad + F_g + F_i"] AD --> SUM GR --> SUM IN --> SUM SUM --> PWR["P_wheel = F_te * v
(Power at Wheels, W)"] SUM --> MOTOR["MOTOR SIZING
(kW rating)"] PWR --> BATTERY["BATTERY ENERGY
(kWh for drive cycle)"] ``` ## 4.3 Resistance Dominance Map (Velocity vs Grade) ```mermaid flowchart TD R1["LOW SPEED
v less than 30 km/h"] --> R1A["DOMINANT: Rolling Resistance F_rr"] R1 --> R1B["SECONDARY: Grade Resistance F_g"] R2["MEDIUM SPEED
30 to 80 km/h"] --> R2A["DOMINANT: Grade Resistance F_g
(on inclines)"] R2 --> R2B["BALANCED: F_rr approx F_ad
(on flat road)"] R3["HIGH SPEED
v greater than 80 km/h"] --> R3A["DOMINANT: Aerodynamic Drag F_ad
(scales with v squared)"] R3 --> R3B["SECONDARY: F_rr and F_g"] R4["HARD ACCELERATION
high a"] --> R4A["DOMINANT: Inertia F_i = m a (1+delta)"] style R1A fill:#FFD700 style R2A fill:#FF8C00 style R3A fill:#FF4500 style R4A fill:#DC143C ``` ## 4.4 Vehicle on Inclined Road — Force Vector Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart TD M["Vehicle Mass m"] -->|Weight vector W = mg| DECOMP["VECTOR
DECOMPOSITION"] DECOMP -->|Perpendicular to slope| N["Normal Reaction N = mg cos theta
(balanced, no motion)"] DECOMP -->|Parallel to slope| FG["Grade Force F_g = mg sin theta
(opposes upward motion)"] M -->|Opposing Motion| FRR["F_rr along slope backward"] M -->|Opposing Motion| FAD["F_ad along slope backward"] FRR --> NET["NET RESISTING FORCE
F_net = F_rr + F_ad + F_g"] FAD --> NET FG --> NET NET --> MOTORREQ["MOTOR MUST PROVIDE
F_motor greater than F_net for acceleration"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define vehicle resistance. List any four major resistances acting on a moving vehicle.** **Model Answer:** Vehicle resistance refers to the total opposing force a vehicle's powertrain must overcome to sustain or accelerate its motion along a road. The four major vehicle resistances are: 1. **Rolling Resistance (F_rr):** Arises from tire deformation and surface friction; proportional to vehicle weight. 2. **Aerodynamic Drag (F_ad):** Caused by air resistance; proportional to the square of velocity. 3. **Grade Resistance (F_g):** Component of vehicle weight along the slope of an inclined road. 4. **Inertia / Acceleration Resistance (F_i):** Required to overcome the inertia of the vehicle's mass and rotating drivetrain components. The tractive effort equation is: $F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{g} + F_{i}$ --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **With a neat sketch, explain the components of aerodynamic drag force on a vehicle.** **Model Answer:** Aerodynamic drag is the resistive force experienced by a vehicle moving through air. Its magnitude is given by: $$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$$ The three main sub-components are: - **Pressure Drag (Form Drag):** Caused by the pressure difference between the front and rear of the vehicle (major contributor). - **Skin Friction Drag:** Due to viscous shear of air along the vehicle surface. - **Induced Drag:** Caused by lift generation, especially in poorly designed bodies. **Sketch Description (text-based):** Draw a vehicle profile with airflow streamlines; show high pressure at the front stagnation point and low-pressure turbulent wake region at the rear; annotate $C_d$ and $A_f$. --- ## PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A — Option 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **(a)** Derive the total tractive effort equation for an electric vehicle moving up an inclined road with acceleration. Explain each term with its physical significance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 1200 kg electric car travels at a constant speed of 60 km/h up a 4% grade. Given: $\mu_{rr} = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.0$ m², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\delta = 1.1$. Calculate: (i) Total tractive force, (ii) Power required at the wheels, (iii) Power required at the battery assuming 90% drivetrain efficiency. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to (a) — Derivation [7 marks] Applying Newton's Second Law along the direction of motion (x-axis) on a vehicle of mass $m$ climbing an incline $\theta$ with acceleration $a$: $$\sum F_x = m \cdot a$$ The forces acting are: - $F_{te}$ — tractive effort at wheels (forward) [positive] - $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$ — rolling resistance (backward) - $F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$ — aerodynamic drag (backward) - $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ — grade component of weight (backward) Therefore: $$F_{te} - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{g} = m \cdot a$$ Including the rotational inertia of drivetrain via mass factor $\delta$: $$\boxed{F_{te} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos(\theta) + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2 + m g \sin(\theta) + m a (1 + \delta)}$$ **Physical Significance of Each Term:** | Term | Physical Meaning | |---|---| | $\mu_{rr} m g \cos(\theta)$ | Energy lost in tire deformation at contact patch | | $\frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$ | Energy lost in air displacement and skin friction | | $m g \sin(\theta)$ | Work done against gravity to climb the slope | | $m a (1 + \delta)$ | Kinetic energy gained by translation + rotation | --- #### Solution to (b) — Numerical [7 marks] **Given:** $m = 1200$ kg, $v = 60$ km/h, grade = 4%, $\mu_{rr} = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.0$ m², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\delta = 1.1$, $\eta_{drivetrain} = 0.90$ **Step 1: Velocity conversion** $$v = \frac{60}{3.6} = 16.67 \, \text{m/s}$$ **Step 2: Slope angle (small angle, $\sin\theta \approx 0.04$, $\cos\theta \approx 1$)** **Step 3: Rolling Resistance** $$F_{rr} = 0.015 \times 1200 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 176.58 \, \text{N}$$ **Step 4: Aerodynamic Drag** $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.0 \times (16.67)^2$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.0 \times 277.89$$ $$F_{ad} = 102.13 \, \text{N}$$ **Step 5: Grade Resistance** $$F_g = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 = 470.88 \, \text{N}$$ **Step 6: Acceleration Resistance (constant speed → a = 0)** $$F_i = 0$$ **(i) Total Tractive Force:** $$F_{te} = 176.58 + 102.13 + 470.88 + 0 = 749.59 \, \text{N} \approx 749.6 \, \text{N}$$ **(ii) Power at Wheels:** $$P_{wheel} = F_{te} \times v = 749.6 \times 16.67 = 12{,}495.83 \, \text{W} \approx 12.5 \, \text{kW}$$ **(iii) Power at Battery:** $$P_{battery} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{drivetrain}} = \frac{12.5}{0.90} = 13.89 \, \text{kW}$$ --- ### Question B — Option 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of rolling resistance in tires. Discuss the factors affecting the coefficient of rolling resistance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An electric sports car has the following data: $m = 1800$ kg, $C_d = 0.24$, $A_f = 2.1$ m², $\mu_{rr} = 0.010$. It travels on a flat road with rotational mass factor $\delta = 1.06$. Calculate: (i) The tractive force required to maintain 100 km/h constant speed, (ii) The tractive force required to accelerate from 60 km/h to 100 km/h in 5 seconds, (iii) The power required at the wheels in each case. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to (a) — Conceptual [7 marks] **Rolling Resistance Phenomenon:** Rolling resistance is the energy dissipated per unit distance traveled as a tire rolls on a surface. It occurs because the tire continuously deforms at the leading edge of the contact patch and recovers at the trailing edge. Since the rubber is viscoelastic, the recovery is **incomplete** — a hysteresis loop is traced in the stress-strain plane, and the enclosed area represents energy lost as heat. The asymmetric pressure distribution under the contact patch produces a net backward force opposing motion. **Factors affecting the coefficient of rolling resistance $\mu_{rr}$:** 1. **Tire material and construction:** Harder rubber compounds have lower $\mu_{rr}$; silica compounds reduce hysteresis. 2. **Tire pressure:** Under-inflated tires increase contact patch deformation → higher $\mu_{rr}$. Over-inflation also increases it due to shock loading. 3. **Road surface texture:** Smooth asphalt gives lower $\mu_{rr}$ than rough concrete or gravel. 4. **Vehicle speed:** At higher speeds, $\mu_{rr}$ slightly increases due to viscoelastic effects and wave deformation. 5. **Temperature:** Warmer tires (post-operation) have slightly lower rolling resistance than cold tires. 6. **Wheel diameter:** Larger diameter wheels generally have lower $\mu_{rr}$. **Typical values:** Car on smooth road: 0.008–0.015; Truck tire: 0.020–0.030. --- #### Solution to (b) — Numerical [7 marks] **Given:** $m = 1800$ kg, $C_d = 0.24$, $A_f = 2.1$ m², $\mu_{rr} = 0.010$, $\delta = 1.06$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ **Common calculations:** $v_1 = 100$ km/h $= 27.78$ m/s $v_2 = 60$ km/h $= 16.67$ m/s Rolling Resistance (flat road): $$F_{rr} = 0.010 \times 1800 \times 9.81 = 176.58 \, \text{N}$$ Aerodynamic Drag at 100 km/h: $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.24 \times 2.1 \times (27.78)^2$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.3087 \times 771.73 = 238.27 \, \text{N}$$ Aerodynamic Drag at 60 km/h: $$F_{ad} = 0.3087 \times (16.67)^2 = 0.3087 \times 277.89 = 85.79 \, \text{N}$$ **(i) Tractive force at constant 100 km/h (a = 0):** $$F_{te} = 176.58 + 238.27 + 0 + 0 = 414.85 \, \text{N}$$ Power at wheels: $$P_{wheel} = 414.85 \times 27.78 = 11{,}525.7 \, \text{W} \approx 11.53 \, \text{kW}$$ **(ii) Tractive force for acceleration 60 → 100 km/h in 5 s:** $$a = \frac{27.78 - 16.67}{5} = \frac{11.11}{5} = 2.222 \, \text{m/s}^2$$ $$F_i = m \cdot a \cdot (1 + \delta) = 1800 \times 2.222 \times 2.06$$ $$F_i = 1800 \times 2.222 \times 2.06 = 8238.4 \, \text{N}$$ Assuming average velocity is used for drag (approximate) — at 100 km/h (end of acceleration): $$F_{te} = 176.58 + 238.27 + 0 + 8238.4 = 8653.25 \, \text{N}$$ **Average power during acceleration:** Using average velocity $v_{avg} = (16.67 + 27.78)/2 = 22.225$ m/s: $$P_{wheel} = 8653.25 \times 22.225 = 192{,}321 \, \text{W} \approx 192.3 \, \text{kW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** (km/h to m/s) → answer off by factor of 3.6 or 13. Lose **1 mark**. > 2. **Using sin(θ) instead of grade%/100** for small angles → usually OK but write both forms for safety. Lose **0.5 mark** if not justified. > 3. **Omitting (1+δ) factor** in acceleration resistance → lose **1 full mark** (common KTU trap). > 4. **Confusing power at wheels vs. power at battery** → include drivetrain efficiency explicitly. > 5. **Not specifying units** in the final answer → lose **0.5 mark** per missing unit (KTU strict). > 6. **Skipping intermediate steps** in derivation → lose **2 marks minimum** (show every substitution). --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vehicle Resistances (PEMET863 Module 1)** ### 🔑 Core Equations (Memorize) - $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos(\theta)$ - $F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$ ← **velocity squared!** - $F_g = m g \sin(\theta)$ - $F_i = m a (1 + \delta)$ - $F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g + F_i$ - $P_{wheel} = F_{te} \cdot v$ ### 🎯 High-Yield Facts - **Air density at sea level** = **1.225 kg/m³** - **g = 9.81 m/s²** (KTU standard) - **Small angle approximation:** $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = \text{grade\%}/100$ - **Power for $F_{ad}$ scales as $v^3$** — key reason for highway range loss in EVs - **Rotational mass factor δ** is typically 1.05–1.10 for EVs, 1.1–1.3 for ICE - **Velocity MUST be in m/s** in all formulas (convert km/h ÷ 3.6) ### ⚡ Dominance Hierarchy (Speed-Dependent) | Speed Regime | Dominant Resistance | |---|---| | Low speed (city, < 30 km/h) | Rolling Resistance | | Medium speed (30–80 km/h) | Grade (if incline) or balanced $F_{rr} \approx F_{ad}$ | | High speed (highway, > 80 km/h) | Aerodynamic Drag | | Hard acceleration | Inertia $F_i$ | ### 📌 Key Engineering Takeaways - EV designers minimize **$C_d$** and **$A_f$** to extend range - Regenerative braking recovers energy from $F_{rr}$ and $F_{ad}$, partially from $F_i$ — but **not** from $F_g$ at constant speed - The **tractive effort equation** is the foundation of **motor sizing** and **battery energy estimation** in HEV/EV design - The factor **$(1+\delta)$** in $F_i$ is a frequent KTU exam question — understand its physical meaning (rotational inertia equivalent mass) ### 🚨 Common KTU Exam Errors to Avoid 1. Mixing up units of velocity 2. Forgetting to include $\delta$ in acceleration resistance 3. Using $P = F \cdot v$ with $v$ in km/h (gives 3.6× error) 4. Confusing rolling resistance with friction (they are different physical phenomena) 5. Not drawing the free-body diagram in 7-mark derivations (mandatory for full marks)

topics.title: Tractive Effort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tractive Effort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tractive Effort (TE):** The net longitudinal force generated at the **tire-pavement contact patch** by the vehicle's prime mover (Internal Combustion Engine, Electric Motor, or Hybrid Powertrain), transmitted through the drivetrain, and directed along the intended direction of vehicle motion. It represents the **propulsive force** available at the wheels to overcome motion-resisting forces and accelerate the vehicle.

Mathematically, the net tractive effort available at the wheel-road interface is:

$$F_{te} = F_t - (F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{wi})$$

Where:
- $F_t$ = Total tractive force generated at the driven wheels (Newtons, **N**)
- $F_{rr}$ = Rolling resistance force (**N**)
- $F_{ad}$ = Aerodynamic drag force (**N**)
- $F_{hc}$ = Hill-climbing (gradient) force (**N**)
- $F_{wi}$ = Equivalent inertial force for translational + rotational masses (**N**)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Pedal of a Bicycle:**
> Imagine you are cycling up a hill. The downward push you exert on the pedal is transmitted via the chain to the rear wheel, which then "pushes" backward against the road surface. By Newton's Third Law, the road pushes the wheel **forward** with an equal and opposite reaction. This forward-directed reaction force is the **Tractive Effort**.
>
> - If this push force is **greater** than the sum of air resistance, rolling friction, and the slope's gravitational pull, you **accelerate**.
> - If it is **equal** to these resisting forces, you cruise at **constant speed**.
> - If it is **less**, you **decelerate** or stop.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Reference)

| Parameter | Standard Value/Unit | Description |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------|
| Gravitational acceleration, $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | Standard earth gravity |
| Air density, $\rho_a$ | **1.225 kg/m³** | At sea level, 15°C |
| Coefficient of rolling resistance, $\mu_{rr}$ | **0.005 to 0.015** | Passenger car on concrete |
| Coefficient of aerodynamic drag, $C_d$ | **0.25 to 0.40** | Modern sedans/EVs |
| Vehicle frontal area, $A_f$ | **1.8 to 2.5 m²** | Typical passenger vehicle |

---

### 1.4 Significance in HEV Design

In **Hybrid and Electric Vehicles**, the tractive effort characteristics of the **electric motor** (high torque at zero rpm, flat torque curve) fundamentally differ from the **ICE** (narrow torque band, requires multi-speed transmission). This difference is the cornerstone of HEV powertrain design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Vehicle Resistance Force Decomposition

The total resistance opposing vehicle motion comprises **four fundamental components**:

#### (i) Rolling Resistance Force ($F_{rr}$)

The energy dissipated due to **tire hysteresis** (deformation of rubber and pavement) as the tire rolls.

$$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot M \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

Where:
- $\mu_{rr}$ = Coefficient of rolling resistance (dimensionless)
- $M$ = Total vehicle mass (kg)
- $g$ = Gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $\alpha$ = Road gradient angle (degrees/radians)

> [!NOTE]
> At small inclines ($\alpha < 10°$), $\cos(\alpha) \approx 1$, so $F_{rr} \approx \mu_{rr} \cdot M \cdot g$.

#### (ii) Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{ad}$)

Caused by the **viscous and pressure** interaction between the vehicle body and surrounding air.

$$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

Where:
- $\rho_a$ = Air density (kg/m³)
- $C_d$ = Drag coefficient (dimensionless)
- $A_f$ = Frontal area (m²)
- $v$ = Vehicle velocity (m/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Aerodynamic drag is **proportional to v²**, making it the dominant resistance force at high speeds (> 60 km/h).

#### (iii) Hill-Climbing (Gradient) Force ($F_{hc}$)

The component of gravitational force acting opposite to vehicle motion on an inclined road.

$$F_{hc} = M \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

Road gradient is often expressed as a **percentage grade**: $\text{Grade (\%)} = \tan(\alpha) \times 100$.

#### (iv) Equivalent Inertial Force ($F_{wi}$)

Forces required to **accelerate** the vehicle's linear mass and rotating components (wheels, gears, motor rotor).

$$F_{wi} = M \cdot \delta_m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

Where $\delta_m$ is the **mass equivalent factor** (rotational inertia correction):

$$\delta_m = 1 + \frac{I_w}{M \cdot r_w^2} + \frac{I_e \cdot \eta_{gd} \cdot k_g^2}{M \cdot r_w^2}$$

Where:
- $I_w$ = Moment of inertia of all wheels (kg·m²)
- $r_w$ = Effective wheel radius (m)
- $I_e$ = Engine/motor moment of inertia (kg·m²)
- $k_g$ = Total gear ratio
- $\eta_{gd}$ = Drivetrain efficiency

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_t = \frac{T_w}{r_w}$ | Tractive force from wheel torque | N |
| 2 | $T_w = T_m \cdot k_g \cdot \eta_{gd}$ | Wheel torque from motor torque | N·m |
| 3 | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot M \cdot g$ | Rolling resistance (flat road) | N |
| 4 | $F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag | N |
| 5 | $F_{hc} = M \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ | Hill climbing force | N |
| 6 | $F_{wi} = M \cdot \delta_m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$ | Equivalent inertial force | N |
| 7 | $P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v$ | Tractive power at wheels | W |
| 8 | $v = \omega_w \cdot r_w$ | Vehicle velocity from wheel speed | m/s |
| 9 | $N_{wheel} = \frac{v \cdot k_g}{2\pi r_w}$ | Motor speed from vehicle speed | rps |
| 10 | $\eta_{total} = \eta_m \cdot \eta_{gd}$ | Total drivetrain efficiency | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The tractive effort equation is the **foundational design equation** for sizing electric motors, selecting battery packs, and designing transmission ratios in HEVs. Without this equation, no powertrain specification is possible.

### 2.3 Tractive Effort — Velocity (TE-v) Characteristic

The **TE-v curve** is a graphical plot showing the tractive force available at the wheels as a function of vehicle speed. It is the **single most important diagram** in EV/HEV design.

- For **constant power** operation: $F_t \propto \frac{1}{v}$ (hyperbolic)
- For **constant torque** region: $F_t = $ constant
- Maximum tractive effort occurs at **zero velocity** (initial acceleration)
- Maximum power occurs at **base speed** of the motor

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Tractive Force from Motor Torque

**Given:** Motor delivers torque $T_m$ at the motor shaft. Power flows through transmission with total gear ratio $k_g$ and drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{gd}$.

**Step 1 — Torque at the wheel shaft:**

The transmission multiplies motor torque by the gear ratio $k_g$, but incurs efficiency losses. The torque reaching the wheel is:

$$T_w = T_m \cdot k_g \cdot \eta_{gd}$$

**Step 2 — Convert wheel torque to tractive force:**

A wheel of effective radius $r_w$ in contact with the ground, experiencing torque $T_w$, exerts a tangential force at the contact patch. By moment equilibrium:

$$T_w = F_t \cdot r_w$$

Solving for tractive force:

$$\boxed{F_t = \frac{T_m \cdot k_g \cdot \eta_{gd}}{r_w}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Equation of Motion (Longitudinal Dynamics)

Applying Newton's Second Law along the vehicle's longitudinal axis:

$$M \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{hc}$$

**Step 1 — Include rotational inertia correction:**

The wheels and rotating drivetrain parts also accelerate, requiring additional torque. We replace $M$ with the equivalent mass $M_{eq} = M \cdot \delta_m$:

$$M \cdot \delta_m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{hc}$$

**Step 2 — Expand each resistive term:**

$$M \cdot \delta_m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = F_t - \mu_{rr} M g \cos(\alpha) - \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 - M g \sin(\alpha)$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for tractive effort required:**

$$\boxed{F_t = M \cdot \delta_m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + M g \left[\mu_{rr} \cos(\alpha) + \sin(\alpha)\right]}$$

This is the **canonical KTU board equation** for tractive effort calculation.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Required at the Wheels

The instantaneous mechanical power required at the contact patch to maintain vehicle motion is:

$$P_{wheel}(t) = F_t \cdot v(t)$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the expression for $F_t$:**

$$P_{wheel}(t) = \left[ M \cdot \delta_m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + M g (\mu_{rr} \cos(\alpha) + \sin(\alpha)) \right] \cdot v$$

**Step 2 — At steady cruise ($dv/dt = 0$) on a flat road ($\alpha = 0$):**

$$P_{cruise} = \left[ \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + \mu_{rr} M g \right] \cdot v = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^3 + \mu_{rr} M g v$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Cubic Law of Road Vehicles":** At high speeds, cruise power scales as $v^3$. Therefore, doubling speed increases required power by **8 times**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Mass Equivalent Factor ($\delta_m$)

The total kinetic energy of the vehicle system includes translational KE of the vehicle mass and rotational KE of the rotating components.

**Step 1 — Total kinetic energy:**

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} M v^2 + \frac{1}{2} n_w I_w \omega_w^2 + \frac{1}{2} I_e \omega_m^2$$

Where $n_w$ = number of wheels, $\omega_w = v/r_w$, $\omega_m = v \cdot k_g / r_w$.

**Step 2 — Substitute angular velocities:**

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} M v^2 + \frac{1}{2} n_w I_w \left(\frac{v}{r_w}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{2} I_e \left(\frac{v k_g}{r_w}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $\frac{1}{2} v^2$:**

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} v^2 \left[ M + \frac{n_w I_w}{r_w^2} + \frac{I_e k_g^2}{r_w^2} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Define equivalent mass and the correction factor:**

$$M_{eq} = M \cdot \delta_m \implies \delta_m = 1 + \frac{n_w I_w}{M r_w^2} + \frac{I_e k_g^2}{M r_w^2}$$

For EVs (where rotor inertia $I_e$ is small relative to flywheel ICE), $\delta_m \approx 1.05 \text{ to } 1.10$.

### 3.5 Analytical Example (KTU-Style Numerical Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A BEV with $M = 1500$ kg, $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.2$ m², $r_w = 0.32$ m, cruising at $v = 90$ km/h = 25 m/s on flat road ($\alpha = 0$). Calculate (a) total tractive effort required, and (b) power required.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units:**
$v = 90 \text{ km/h} = 25 \text{ m/s}$.

**Step 2 — Compute Rolling Resistance:**

$$F_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times \cos(0) = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Aerodynamic Drag:**

$$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (25)^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 625$$

$$F_{ad} = 252.66 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Sum total resistance (flat road, steady cruise):**

$$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} = 176.58 + 252.66 = 429.24 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Power required at the wheels:**

$$P_{wheel} = F_t \times v = 429.24 \times 25 = 10{,}731 \text{ W} \approx 10.73 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Including drivetrain efficiency (assume $\eta_{gd} = 0.92$):**

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{gd}} = \frac{10{,}731}{0.92} \approx 11{,}664 \text{ W} \approx 11.66 \text{ kW}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Tractive Effort Generation in an EV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT["Battery Pack DC Source"] --> INV["Power Inverter VSI"]
    INV --> MTR["Electric Motor EM"]
    MTR --> GBX["Transmission Gearbox"]
    GBX --> SHF["Driveshaft"]
    SHF --> WHL["Wheels Tire Contact Patch"]
    WHL --> ROAD["Road Reaction Newton Third Law"]
    ROAD --> TE["Tractive Effort Forward Force"]
    TE --> ACC["Vehicle Acceleration Longitudinal Dynamics"]
    ACC --> FB["Feedback Speed and Torque Sensors"]
    FB --> INV
```

### 4.2 Force Decomposition Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Resistance Force Fr"] --> B["Rolling Resistance Frr"]
    A --> C["Aerodynamic Drag Fad"]
    A --> D["Hill Climbing Force Fhc"]
    A --> E["Inertial Force Fwi"]
    B --> B1["Tire Hysteresis Loss"]
    B --> B2["Road Surface Friction"]
    C --> C1["Pressure Drag Cd x Af"]
    C --> C2["Skin Friction Component"]
    D --> D1["Grade Angle alpha"]
    D --> D2["Vehicle Mass M"]
    E --> E1["Translational Mass Ma"]
    E --> E2["Rotational Mass delta m"]
```

### 4.3 TE-v Characteristic Operating Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TEVPlot["TE-v Operating Map"]
        direction LR
        P1["Region 1 Constant Torque Max TE at low speed"] --> P2["Region 2 Constant Power Hyperbolic Decay"]
        P2 --> P3["Region 3 Field Weakening High Speed Reduced Torque"]
    end
    R1["Rolling Resistance Curve Nearly Flat"] --> TEVPlot
    R2["Aerodynamic Drag Curve Parabolic v^2"] --> TEVPlot
    R3["Total Resistance Envelope"] --> TEVPlot
    P1 --> X1["Operating Point Selection"]
    P2 --> X1
    P3 --> X1
    R1 --> X1
    R2 --> X1
    R3 --> X1
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow: From Accelerator Pedal to Tractive Effort

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Driver Accelerator Pedal Input 0 to 100 percent"] --> S2["ECU Maps Torque Demand from Driver"]
    S2 --> S3["Battery Management System BMS Pack Limits SOC and Voltage"]
    S3 --> S4["Motor Controller PWM Modulation"]
    S4 --> S5["Electric Motor Produces Torque Tm"]
    S5 --> S6["Gearbox Multiplies Torque Tm x kg"]
    S6 --> S7["Wheel Torque Tw becomes Tractive Force Ft"]
    S7 --> S8["Vehicle Accelerates Longitudinal Dynamics"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block diagrams above follow the **KTU system-engineering convention** of tracing power/force flow from energy source → conversion → transmission → mechanical output. Each block has a single, well-defined input-output interface.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define Tractive Effort. List the four primary resistive forces opposing a vehicle's longitudinal motion.

**Model Answer:**
Tractive effort is the total propulsive force generated at the tire-road contact patch, directed along the vehicle's longitudinal axis. The four resistive forces are:
1. **Rolling Resistance ($F_{rr}$)** — due to tire deformation hysteresis.
2. **Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{ad}$)** — due to air viscosity and pressure differential.
3. **Hill Climbing Force ($F_{hc}$)** — gravitational component on inclined road.
4. **Inertial Force ($F_{wi}$)** — required to accelerate vehicle mass.

---

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper Q2]**
> **Q2.** Why does aerodynamic drag become the dominant resistive force at high vehicle speeds?

**Model Answer:**
Aerodynamic drag $F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2$ scales with the **square of velocity**, while rolling resistance $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} M g$ is approximately **constant**. Therefore, as speed increases, $F_{ad}$ grows quadratically and eventually exceeds $F_{rr}$. Beyond ~60–80 km/h, $F_{ad}$ dominates the resistance budget and consequently the tractive effort requirement.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]**

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
> **(a)** Derive the **fundamental equation of motion** for a road vehicle, starting from Newton's Second Law. Clearly state all assumptions and define every variable used. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A passenger EV has the following specifications: $M = 1800$ kg, $\mu_{rr} = 0.010$, $C_d = 0.28$, $A_f = 2.3$ m², $r_w = 0.33$ m, gear ratio $k_g = 8.5$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{gd} = 0.94$, $\delta_m = 1.08$. The vehicle must climb a 5° grade at a constant speed of 60 km/h. Calculate:
> - (i) Total tractive force required at the wheels.
> - (ii) Wheel torque and motor torque required.
> - (iii) Power drawn from the battery (assuming inverter efficiency 0.95). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Applying Newton's Second Law in the longitudinal direction:

$$M \frac{dv}{dt} = \sum F_{propulsion} - \sum F_{resistive}$$

Accounting for rotational inertia:

$$M \delta_m \frac{dv}{dt} = F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{hc}$$

Expanding:

$$M \delta_m \frac{dv}{dt} = F_t - \mu_{rr} M g \cos\alpha - \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 - M g \sin\alpha$$

Solving for $F_t$:

$$F_t = M \delta_m \frac{dv}{dt} + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + M g (\mu_{rr} \cos\alpha + \sin\alpha)$$

This is the **KTU canonical tractive effort equation**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $M = 1800$ kg, $\mu_{rr} = 0.010$, $C_d = 0.28$, $A_f = 2.3$ m²
- $r_w = 0.33$ m, $k_g = 8.5$, $\eta_{gd} = 0.94$, $\delta_m = 1.08$
- $\alpha = 5°$, $v = 60$ km/h $= 16.667$ m/s
- Inverter efficiency $\eta_{inv} = 0.95$

**(i) Tractive Force at Wheels:**

At constant speed, $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$.

$$F_{rr} = 0.010 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times \cos(5°) = 176.58 \times 0.9962 = 175.92 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.3 \times (16.667)^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.3 \times 277.78$$

$$F_{ad} = 109.46 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{hc} = 1800 \times 9.81 \times \sin(5°) = 17658 \times 0.0872 = 1539.2 \text{ N}$$

$$F_t = 175.92 + 109.46 + 1539.2 = 1824.58 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Torque Calculations:**

Wheel torque:

$$T_w = F_t \times r_w = 1824.58 \times 0.33 = 602.11 \text{ N·m}$$

Motor torque:

$$T_m = \frac{T_w}{k_g \cdot \eta_{gd}} = \frac{602.11}{8.5 \times 0.94} = 75.35 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Battery Power:**

Power at wheels:

$$P_{wheel} = F_t \times v = 1824.58 \times 16.667 = 30{,}409 \text{ W} \approx 30.41 \text{ kW}$$

Power at motor shaft:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{gd}} = \frac{30.41}{0.94} = 32.35 \text{ kW}$$

Battery power (DC input):

$$P_{batt} = \frac{P_{motor}}{\eta_{inv}} = \frac{32.35}{0.95} = 34.05 \text{ kW}$$

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**
> **(a)** Explain the **Tractive Effort vs Velocity (TE-v) characteristic** of an electric vehicle. Sketch the curve and identify the constant-torque, constant-power, and field-weakening regions. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **differences in tractive effort characteristics** between an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle and a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV). How does this influence transmission design? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TE-v Characteristic:**

The TE-v curve represents the maximum tractive effort available at the wheels as a function of vehicle speed. It has three distinct regions:

1. **Constant Torque Region (0 to base speed $v_b$):** The motor delivers its **maximum torque** ($T_{max}$) limited by thermal/electrical constraints. Tractive effort is at its **peak** value:
$$F_{t,max} = \frac{T_{max} \cdot k_g \cdot \eta_{gd}}{r_w}$$

2. **Constant Power Region ($v_b$ to $v_{max}$):** Beyond base speed, the motor operates at **rated power**. Since $P = F_t \cdot v = $ constant:
$$F_t = \frac{P_{rated}}{v}$$
This is a **hyperbolic** decay.

3. **Field Weakening Region (above rated speed):** To exceed base speed, flux is reduced via field weakening. Both torque and power decrease; TE falls rapidly.

**(b) ICE vs BEV Comparison:**

| Aspect | ICE Vehicle | BEV |
|--------|-------------|-----|
| Torque at zero rpm | Zero (stall) | **Maximum** |
| Torque envelope | Narrow peak band | **Flat, wide** |
| Idle required | Yes | No |
| Transmission | Multi-speed essential | Often **single-speed** |
| Peak power point | Single RPM | Wide range |
| Thermal limits | Constrain continuous operation | Less restrictive |

> **Design Implication:** The flat, wide torque curve of an electric motor means a BEV can typically use a **single-speed transmission** (or direct drive), whereas an ICE with narrow torque band requires 5–8 gear ratios. This reduces mechanical complexity, weight, and losses in EVs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Tractive Effort Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistake:** Students frequently forget to convert km/h to m/s. Always write $v_{m/s} = v_{km/h} / 3.6$ before substituting.
> 2. **Hill-climbing angle vs grade:** If the problem states "5% grade", use $\tan\alpha = 0.05$ (NOT $\alpha = 5°$).
> 3. **Forgetting $\delta_m$:** In acceleration problems, neglecting the rotational inertia correction leads to ~5–10% error.
> 4. **Drivetrain efficiency placement:** $\eta_{gd}$ belongs between motor and wheel, not between wheel and road. Power required at motor = Power at wheels / $\eta_{gd}$.
> 5. **Sign convention:** Hill-climbing force ALWAYS opposes motion on upward grade; it is positive in the resistance sum.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Tractive Effort** = Propulsive force at tire-road contact patch (Newtons, **N**).
> - **Four Resistive Forces:** Rolling Resistance ($F_{rr}$), Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{ad}$), Hill Climbing ($F_{hc}$), Inertial ($F_{wi}$).
> - **Canonical Equation:** $F_t = M \delta_m \dfrac{dv}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + Mg(\mu_{rr} \cos\alpha + \sin\alpha)$
> - **Rolling resistance** is approximately **constant** with speed.
> - **Aerodynamic drag** scales as **v²**; **cruise power** scales as **v³**.
> - **$F_t = T_m k_g \eta_{gd} / r_w$** — fundamental motor-to-wheel conversion.
> - **Mass equivalent factor** $\delta_m$ accounts for rotational inertia of wheels, gears, and motor rotor.
> - **TE-v curve** has 3 regions: constant torque, constant power (hyperbolic), field weakening.
> - **EV motor** has flat torque curve → single-speed transmission; ICE needs multi-speed gearbox.
> - **g = 9.81 m/s²**, **$\rho_a$ = 1.225 kg/m³** — remember these standard constants.
> - **Sign convention:** All resistive forces are summed positively on the right-hand side of the equation of motion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gradability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Gradability # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Gradability** is defined as the maximum grade (slope or incline), expressed either in degrees or as a percentage, that a vehicle can negotiate while maintaining a specified minimum steady-state speed under standard operating conditions. Mathematically, it represents the maximum value of the tangent of the inclination angle ($\theta$) that the vehicle can climb, limited by the available tractive effort at the drive wheels, the coefficient of road adhesion, and the aerodynamic, rolling, and grade resistances acting on the vehicle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** Gradability (%) = $100 \times \tan(\theta)$, where $\theta$ is the maximum climb angle the vehicle can sustain at the rated minimum speed without stalling the traction motor. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are pushing a loaded shopping trolley up a steep driveway. As the slope gets steeper, your legs have to push harder and harder just to keep the trolley moving forward. Eventually, there is a slope steep enough at which your leg muscles simply cannot produce enough force, and the trolley rolls backward. That "critical slope" is essentially the **gradability** of the trolley under your pushing effort. For a vehicle, the "pushing effort" is the **tractive force** generated at the wheels by the electric motor (in an EV) or the engine–motor combination (in an HEV). The "trolley load" is the **vehicle mass**, and the "driveway slope" is the **road grade**. The steeper the grade, the more force the motor must supply to overcome the component of gravity pulling the vehicle backward down the slope. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Takeaway:** Gradability answers the question — *"What is the steepest hill this EV/HEV can climb without stalling?"* ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | Universal constant | | Air density (sea level) | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** | ISO standard | | Vehicle mass (curb + payload) | $m$ | **kg** | Total inertial mass | | Coefficient of rolling resistance | $f_r$ | dimensionless (0.005–0.020) | Depends on tire/road | | Coefficient of aerodynamic drag | $C_d$ | dimensionless (0.25–0.40) | Vehicle body dependent | | Frontal area | $A$ | **m²** | Cross-section perpendicular to flow | | Coefficient of road adhesion | $\mu$ | dimensionless (0.5–0.9 dry, 0.1–0.3 icy) | Limits max tractive force | | Grade percentage | $G\,(\%)$ | dimensionless ratio | $G = 100 \tan(\theta)$ | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module explicitly requires students to derive the gradability expression from the fundamental vehicle dynamics equation. Memorize the term-by-term breakdown of tractive force — it is a high-frequency Part A and Part B question. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Fundamental Vehicle Dynamics Equation The motion of any road vehicle is governed by Newton's second law applied at the wheel–road contact patch. The **tractive force** ($F_t$) produced at the driven wheels must overcome four distinct resistance components plus accelerate the vehicle mass: $$F_t = F_f + F_{grade} + F_a + F_i$$ Each term on the right-hand side represents a force opposing forward motion: - **$F_f$ – Rolling Resistance Force:** Caused by tire hysteresis deformation as the tire rolls. Acts at the contact patch, opposing motion. - **$F_{grade}$ – Grade Resistance Force:** The component of the vehicle's weight acting down the slope. - **$F_a$ – Aerodynamic Drag Force:** Caused by the vehicle pushing air aside as it moves. - **$F_i$ – Inertia Force (Linear + Rotational):** Required to accelerate the translational mass and the rotating components (wheels, gears, motor rotor). ## 2.2 Term-by-Term Physics ### 2.2.1 Rolling Resistance $$F_f = m \cdot g \cdot f_r \cdot \cos\theta$$ On level roads ($\theta \approx 0$), $\cos\theta \approx 1$, simplifying to $F_f \approx m \cdot g \cdot f_r$. ### 2.2.2 Grade Resistance $$F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$ For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta$, and since grade percentage $G(\%) = 100 \tan\theta$, we obtain the extremely useful form: $$F_{grade} \approx 0.01 \cdot m \cdot g \cdot G\,(\%)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters:** This linearized form lets engineers size the motor simply by reading the required grade percentage off a road map without trigonometric conversion. ### 2.2.3 Aerodynamic Drag $$F_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$$ This term grows with the **square of velocity**, so at highway speeds, aerodynamic drag dominates. At low climbing speeds (the regime relevant to gradability testing), this term is usually small and may be neglected. ### 2.2.4 Inertia Force (Linear + Rotational Equivalent Mass) $$F_i = m \cdot a + \dfrac{I_w \cdot \dot{\omega}_w}{r_w} + \dots \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; F_i = m \cdot (1 + \delta) \cdot a$$ Where $\delta$ is the **mass factor** (typically **1.05 to 1.10** for EVs) accounting for rotational inertia of rotating parts referred to the translational axis. For steady-state gradability tests, $a = 0$, so $F_i = 0$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Equation | Conditions / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Tractive force balance | $F_t = F_f + F_{grade} + F_a + F_i$ | Newton's 2nd law at contact patch | | 2 | Rolling resistance | $F_f = m g f_r \cos\theta$ | Use $\cos\theta \approx 1$ for small $\theta$ | | 3 | Grade resistance | $F_{grade} = m g \sin\theta$ | Use $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta$ for small $\theta$ | | 4 | Grade percentage | $G\,(\%) = 100 \tan\theta$ | Definition | | 5 | Linearized grade force | $F_{grade} = 0.01\, m g \, G\,(\%)$ | When $G < 20\%$ | | 6 | Aerodynamic drag | $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ | Often neglected in gradability tests | | 7 | Equivalent inertia | $F_i = m(1+\delta) a$ | $\delta \approx 1.05$–$1.10$ for EVs | | 8 | Adhesion-limited max $F_t$ | $F_{t,\max} = \mu\, m\, g\, \cos\theta \approx \mu\, m\, g$ | Tire–road friction limit | | 9 | Max grade (with adhesion) | $\tan\theta_{\max} = \mu - f_r$ | At very low speed, no drag | | 10 | Max grade (motor-limited) | $\tan\theta_{\max} \approx \dfrac{F_{t,\text{motor}}}{m g} - f_r$ | If $F_{t,\text{motor}} < \mu m g$ | | 11 | Max grade angle (deg) | $\theta_{\max} = \arctan\!\big(\mu - f_r\big)$ | In degrees for reporting | | 12 | Max grade % | $G_{\max} = 100\,(\mu - f_r)$ | Direct percent form | | 13 | Power needed to climb | $P_{grade} = F_{grade} \cdot v = m g v \sin\theta$ | At constant speed $v$ | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** Gradability is **not** a single number — it is *constrained simultaneously* by (i) the **motor's peak tractive force** and (ii) the **tire–road adhesion limit**. The actual maximum grade is the **smaller** of the two. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Motor Sizing:** Manufacturers use gradability targets (typically **30% grade** for passenger EVs, **up to 45–60%** for off-road HEVs) to determine the minimum peak torque the drive motor must deliver at low speeds. - **Battery Pack Sizing:** Sustained hill-climbing draws large continuous current. Thermal management and battery C-rate must support the grade power demand. - **Transmission Ratio Selection:** First-gear ratio is chosen largely based on the maximum grade the vehicle must climb while maintaining creep speed. - **Regulatory Compliance:** Many transport authorities (e.g., for buses, heavy trucks, mine vehicles) specify minimum mandatory gradability under loaded conditions. - **Off-Road HEV Design:** Hybrid off-road vehicles (e.g., defense, mining) prioritize gradability above top speed, often incorporating low-range gear reducers and in-wheel motors for redundancy. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Grade at Very Low Speed (Adhesion-Limited) This is the **most important KTU derivation** for gradability. The conditions assumed are: - Vehicle climbing at a **steady minimum speed** ($a = 0 \Rightarrow F_i = 0$). - **Aerodynamic drag neglected** ($v \to 0$). - The motor is capable of producing more force than the road can transmit, so the **tire–road adhesion** is the bottleneck. **Step 1 — Write the force balance equation.** $$F_t = F_f + F_{grade} + F_a + F_i$$ **Step 2 — Substitute each term with its low-speed, small-angle expression.** $$F_t = m g f_r \cos\theta + m g \sin\theta + 0 + 0$$ **Step 3 — Recognize that the maximum tractive force the road can transmit equals the adhesion limit.** $$F_t^{\max} = \mu \cdot N = \mu \cdot m g \cos\theta$$ This uses the **normal reaction** $N = m g \cos\theta$ on an inclined plane. **Step 4 — Equate the two expressions for maximum tractive force.** $$\mu\, m g \cos\theta = m g f_r \cos\theta + m g \sin\theta$$ **Step 5 — Divide both sides entirely by $m g \cos\theta$.** $$\mu = f_r + \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = f_r + \tan\theta$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $\tan\theta_{\max}$.** $$\tan\theta_{\max} = \mu - f_r$$ **Step 7 — Convert to grade percentage using $G(\%) = 100 \tan\theta$.** $$\boxed{G_{\max}\,(\%) = 100\,(\mu - f_r)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The available adhesion $\mu$ is "spent" partly overcoming rolling resistance $f_r$, with the remainder $(\mu - f_r)$ available to climb the slope. If $\mu < f_r$, the vehicle cannot even move on a level road — a condition encountered on icy roads with under-inflated tires. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Grade When Motor Is the Limiting Factor If the motor's maximum tractive force $F_{t,\text{motor}}$ is **less than** $\mu m g \cos\theta$, then motor capability limits gradability. **Step 1 — Force balance at max grade.** $$F_{t,\text{motor}} = m g f_r \cos\theta_{\max} + m g \sin\theta_{\max}$$ **Step 2 — Divide by $m g \cos\theta_{\max}$.** $$\dfrac{F_{t,\text{motor}}}{m g \cos\theta_{\max}} = f_r + \tan\theta_{\max}$$ **Step 3 — Use small-angle approximation ($\cos\theta_{\max} \approx 1$) to obtain a closed-form.** $$\tan\theta_{\max} \approx \dfrac{F_{t,\text{motor}}}{m g} - f_r$$ **Step 4 — Convert to percent grade.** $$\boxed{G_{\max}\,(\%) \approx 100 \left(\dfrac{F_{t,\text{motor}}}{m g} - f_r\right)}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Required to Maintain a Given Speed on a Given Grade When the vehicle has already reached the desired climbing speed and is cruising on the slope, the steady-state power demand at the wheels is: **Step 1 — Identify the resisting forces at steady speed ($a=0$).** $$F_{\text{resist}} = F_f + F_{grade} + F_a$$ **Step 2 — Substitute linearized forms.** $$F_{\text{resist}} = m g f_r + 0.01\, m g\, G\,(\%) + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$$ **Step 3 — Multiply by velocity to obtain power at wheels.** $$P_{\text{wheels}} = v \cdot F_{\text{resist}} = v \left[ m g f_r + 0.01\, m g\, G + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 \right]$$ **Step 4 — Account for drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{drv}$ to get battery-side power.** $$\boxed{P_{\text{battery}} = \dfrac{v \cdot F_{\text{resist}}}{\eta_{drv}} = \dfrac{v}{\eta_{drv}}\left[ m g f_r + 0.01\, m g\, G + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 \right]}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Takeaway:** Sustained grade-climbing (e.g., 30 km/h up a 30% grade) drains the battery far faster than steady cruising, because the grade term alone — $0.01 \cdot m g \cdot G$ — can rival or exceed the aerodynamic term at low speeds. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical) > **Problem:** An EV has total mass $m = 1500\,\text{kg}$, $f_r = 0.015$, $\mu = 0.8$, frontal area $A = 2.2\,\text{m}^2$, $C_d = 0.30$. Compute (a) the maximum grade the vehicle can climb at very low speed, (b) the peak tractive force required to climb a 30% grade at 30 km/h, and (c) the wheel power needed. **Solution (a):** Using $G_{\max} = 100\,(\mu - f_r)$: $$G_{\max} = 100\,(0.8 - 0.015) = 100 \times 0.785 = 78.5\,\%$$ This corresponds to: $$\theta_{\max} = \arctan(0.785) \approx 38.1^{\circ}$$ **Solution (b):** At 30 km/h on 30% grade, $v = 8.33\,\text{m/s}$. $$F_{grade} = 0.01 \cdot m g \cdot G = 0.01 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 30 = 4414.5\,\text{N}$$ $$F_f = m g f_r = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 = 220.7\,\text{N}$$ $$F_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (8.33)^2$$ $$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 69.4 \approx 28.05\,\text{N}$$ Total tractive force: $$F_t = 220.7 + 4414.5 + 28.05 \approx 4663\,\text{N}$$ **Solution (c):** Wheel power: $$P_{\text{wheels}} = v \cdot F_t = 8.33 \times 4663 \approx 38{,}840\,\text{W} \approx 38.8\,\text{kW}$$ Assuming $\eta_{drv} = 0.90$: $$P_{\text{battery}} = \dfrac{38.8}{0.90} \approx 43.1\,\text{kW}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The grade term ($4414.5\,\text{N}$) is the dominant resistance — over **94%** of the total tractive demand. This shows why grade climbing is the **defining load case** for EV powertrain sizing. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Free-Body Diagram of Vehicle on an Incline ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Vehicle Body
Mass = m"] node2["Weight Vector
W = m*g
acting vertically downward"] node3["Normal Reaction
N = m*g*cos(theta)
perpendicular to slope"] node4["Tractive Force
F_t at wheel contact patch
directed up the slope"] node5["Rolling Resistance
F_f = m*g*f_r*cos(theta)
opposes motion"] node6["Grade Component
F_grade = m*g*sin(theta)
acts down the slope"] node7["Aerodynamic Drag
F_a = 0.5*rho*Cd*A*v^2
opposes motion"] node8["Inclined Road Surface
Grade Angle = theta
Grade% = 100*tan(theta)"] node1 --- node2 node1 --- node4 node1 --- node5 node1 --- node6 node1 --- node7 node8 --- node3 node8 --- node4 node8 --- node5 node8 --- node6 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Gradability Computation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR inA["Inputs:
Vehicle Mass m
Grade Angle theta
Speed v
Tire-Road mu
Rolling fr"] pA["Force Balance:
F_t = F_f + F_grade + F_a + F_i"] pB["Constraint Check 1:
Motor Limit F_t_max
vs Required F_t"] pC["Constraint Check 2:
Adhesion Limit
mu * m * g * cos theta"] pD["Decision Logic:
F_t_required = min of motor & adhesion limits"] outA["Output 1:
Max Grade Angle
theta_max"] outB["Output 2:
Max Grade Percent
G_max in percent"] outC["Output 3:
Required Wheel Power
P_wheels in kW"] inA --> pA pA --> pB pA --> pC pB --> pD pC --> pD pD --> outA pD --> outB pD --> outC ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Bar Chart — Resistance Forces at Different Grades (Qualitative) ```mermaid flowchart TD chartTitle["Relative Magnitude of Resistive Forces (m=1500 kg, v=30 km/h)"] barA["Level Road 0 percent grade:
F_f dominant, F_grade = 0"] barB["15 percent grade:
F_grade approaches F_f magnitude"] barC["30 percent grade:
F_grade overwhelms all other terms"] barD["60 percent grade:
F_grade ~ 4x of F_f and F_a combined"] chartTitle --> barA chartTitle --> barB chartTitle --> barC chartTitle --> barD ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Insight:** The pipeline shows that gradability is **not** a single calculation — it is the **intersection of two constraints**: motor capability and tire–road friction. A KTU examiner may present a scenario where the motor is over-rated but the road is icy, or vice versa. Always identify **which constraint is binding** before declaring $\theta_{\max}$. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Part A — Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define gradability. Express the maximum grade a vehicle can climb in terms of the coefficient of road adhesion and the coefficient of rolling resistance.** **Model Answer:** Gradability is the maximum grade (slope) a vehicle can ascend while maintaining a specified minimum speed under standard test conditions, expressed either in degrees or as a percentage. At very low speeds (neglecting aerodynamic drag and inertia), the force balance reduces to: $$F_{t,\max} = m g f_r \cos\theta + m g \sin\theta$$ Equating with the adhesion limit $F_{t,\max} = \mu\, m g \cos\theta$ and solving: $$\boxed{G_{\max}\,(\%) = 100\,(\mu - f_r)}$$ ### Part A — Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **List the four components of resistance that a vehicle must overcome during motion, and state the equation for each.** **Model Answer:** | # | Resistance | Equation | |---|---|---| | 1 | Rolling resistance | $F_f = m g f_r \cos\theta$ | | 2 | Grade resistance | $F_{grade} = m g \sin\theta$ | | 3 | Aerodynamic drag | $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | | 4 | Inertia force | $F_i = m(1+\delta) a$ | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Part B — Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the maximum gradability of an electric vehicle at very low speeds. Clearly state the assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An electric vehicle of mass **1800 kg** is required to climb a **25% grade** at a steady speed of **40 km/h**. Given $f_r = 0.018$, $C_d = 0.32$, $A = 2.4\,\text{m}^2$, $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$, compute (i) the total tractive force required, and (ii) the wheel power demand. Assume $\eta_{drv} = 0.92$. **(7 marks)** #### Solution A(a): **Assumptions:** 1. Vehicle climbs at very low steady speed ⇒ $a = 0$, so $F_i = 0$. 2. Aerodynamic drag is negligible at very low speed ⇒ $F_a \approx 0$. 3. The road adhesion limit is binding (motor is over-rated). 4. Small-angle approximations: $\cos\theta \approx 1$, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta$. **Derivation:** From the force balance: $$F_t = m g f_r \cos\theta + m g \sin\theta$$ The adhesion-limited maximum tractive force is: $$F_t^{\max} = \mu \cdot m g \cos\theta$$ Equating and dividing by $m g \cos\theta$: $$\mu = f_r + \tan\theta_{\max}$$ $$\boxed{\tan\theta_{\max} = \mu - f_r \quad \text{or} \quad G_{\max}\,(\%) = 100(\mu - f_r)}$$ #### Solution A(b): Given: $m = 1800\,\text{kg}$, $G = 25\%$, $v = 40\,\text{km/h} = 11.11\,\text{m/s}$, $f_r = 0.018$, $C_d = 0.32$, $A = 2.4\,\text{m}^2$, $\rho = 1.225$, $\eta = 0.92$. **(i) Tractive Force Calculation:** $$F_{grade} = 0.01 \cdot m g \cdot G = 0.01 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times 25 = 4414.5\,\text{N}$$ $$F_f = m g f_r = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.018 = 317.8\,\text{N}$$ $$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.4 \times (11.11)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.4 \times 123.5 \approx 58.06\,\text{N}$$ $$\boxed{F_t = 317.8 + 4414.5 + 58.06 \approx 4790\,\text{N}}$$ **(ii) Wheel Power:** $$P_{\text{wheels}} = v \cdot F_t = 11.11 \times 4790 \approx 53{,}218\,\text{W} \approx 53.2\,\text{kW}$$ Battery-side power: $$\boxed{P_{\text{battery}} = \dfrac{53.2}{0.92} \approx 57.8\,\text{kW}}$$ --- ### Part B — Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of the tractive force equation, explain why electric vehicles are particularly suited to high-grade applications. Include the effect of single-speed vs. multi-speed transmissions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hybrid electric vehicle has $m = 2200\,\text{kg}$, $f_r = 0.012$, $\mu = 0.85$. It must achieve a maximum gradability of **40%** under steady low-speed conditions. Verify whether the vehicle meets the gradability requirement, and compute the minimum peak tractive force the electric motor must deliver. **(7 marks)** #### Solution B(a): The tractive force equation is: $$F_t = F_f + F_{grade} + F_a + F_i$$ **Why EVs suit high-grade climbing:** 1. **Instant peak torque:** Electric motors deliver **maximum torque at zero rpm**, eliminating the need for clutch slip or torque-converter stall that internal combustion engines require at low speeds. This directly maximizes $F_t$ at the moment of hill-launch. 2. **No power derating at altitude:** Unlike IC engines, which lose power with air thinning on mountain roads, electric motors retain full torque output at high altitudes, improving real-world gradability. 3. **Regenerative descent:** On the return trip, the same motor acts as a generator, partially recharging the battery and extending range. 4. **Single-speed transmission viability:** Most production EVs use a **single-speed reducer** because electric motors have a wide constant-power region. This is feasible on grades because the motor's flat torque curve eliminates the gear-shift interruptions that plague ICE vehicles on steep inclines. 5. **In-wheel motor architectures** can independently control torque at each wheel, providing superior traction management on variable-grade surfaces. #### Solution B(b): **Given:** $m = 2200\,\text{kg}$, $f_r = 0.012$, $\mu = 0.85$, required $G = 40\%$. **Adhesion-limited maximum grade:** $$G_{\max} = 100(\mu - f_r) = 100 \times (0.85 - 0.012) = 100 \times 0.838 = 83.8\%$$ **Comparison:** $G_{\max} = 83.8\% \gg G_{\text{required}} = 40\%$ ✅ The road adhesion easily supports the required grade. The motor must be sized accordingly. **Minimum peak tractive force required (motor-limited scenario):** At $G = 40\%$, neglecting $F_a$ and $F_i$ at low speed: $$F_{grade} = 0.01 \times 2200 \times 9.81 \times 40 = 8632.8\,\text{N}$$ $$F_f = 2200 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 258.98\,\text{N}$$ $$\boxed{F_{t,\text{required}} = F_f + F_{grade} = 258.98 + 8632.8 \approx 8892\,\text{N} \approx 8.89\,\text{kN}}$$ **Verification of adhesion:** $$F_{t,\text{required}} = 8892\,\text{N} < \mu m g = 0.85 \times 2200 \times 9.81 = 18{,}344\,\text{N}$$ The required force is well below the adhesion limit, confirming the motor-limited design is feasible. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the linearization step:** Students use $F_{grade} = m g \sin\theta$ instead of the linearized $0.01 m g G$ form when grade is given as a percentage — examiners specifically test both forms, and using the wrong one on a numerical problem costs full marks. > 2. **Ignoring rolling resistance in max-grade derivation:** Setting $f_r = 0$ gives $G_{\max} = 100\mu$, which is theoretically wrong and loses 2 marks. > 3. **Confusing motor-limited vs. adhesion-limited gradability:** Always perform **both** checks; the actual $G_{\max}$ is the **smaller** of the two. > 4. **Unit errors on velocity conversion:** KTU board examiners frequently give speed in **km/h** but the formula requires **m/s**. Missing the $\div 3.6$ step is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. > 5. **Forgetting drivetrain efficiency when asked for "battery power":** $P_{\text{wheels}}$ and $P_{\text{battery}}$ are different by the factor $\eta_{drv}$. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Gradability** = maximum sustainable climb grade, expressed as % or degrees. - **Two binding constraints:** Motor peak torque limit **and** tire–road adhesion $\mu$. True $G_{\max} = \min(G_{\text{motor}}, G_{\text{adhesion}})$. - **Master formula:** $G_{\max}\,(\%) = 100\,(\mu - f_r)$ — low-speed, adhesion-limited case. - **Linearized grade force:** $F_{grade} \approx 0.01\, m g\, G\,(\%)$ — valid for grades below ~20% and far easier to compute. - **General tractive force equation:** $F_t = F_f + F_{grade} + F_a + F_i$ — memorize and be able to expand each term. - **Aerodynamic drag is negligible** in low-speed gradability tests but **dominates** at highway speeds. - **Power to climb a grade:** $P_{\text{grade}} = m g v \sin\theta \approx 0.01\, m g v G\,(\%)$. - **EV advantage on grades:** Peak torque at zero rpm, no altitude derating, regen on descent, single-gear simplicity. - **Always state assumptions** before any derivation — this is the single biggest KTU marking differentiator. - **Convert units explicitly:** km/h → m/s, % → tan form, kW → W, before plugging into any formula. - **Grade vs. angle relationship:** $G(\%) = 100 \tan\theta$, and for small angles $\theta^{\circ} \approx 0.573 \times G(\%)$.

topics.title: Electric Vehicles - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electric Vehicles - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is a road vehicle whose propulsion system is driven, either wholly or partially, by electrical energy stored in an onboard energy storage system (typically a rechargeable traction battery) and converted into mechanical energy by one or more electric traction motors. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PEMET863 syllabus**, the term broadly encompasses **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs)**, **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs)**, **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs)**, and **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1.1):**
> "Electric Vehicles — Introduction, Components, Configurations, Advantages & Limitations"
> Students must be prepared to define EV, classify it, draw the block schematic, and list the merits/demerits with engineering reasoning.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Battery Powered Bucket" Intuition

Imagine a **water bucket system**:
- The **bucket filled with water at height** = the fully charged **battery** (stored potential energy).
- The **pipe** = the **power electronics converter** (controls flow rate).
- The **water wheel at the bottom** = the **electric motor** (converts energy to rotation).
- The **wheel attached to a cart** = the **transmission & wheels** (delivers traction).

When you open the valve (accelerator pedal), water (current) flows through the pipe (controller), spins the wheel (motor), and pushes the cart (vehicle). When the bucket empties, the cart stops — just like an EV when the battery is discharged.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle, which *burns* fuel, an EV *stores* energy and *converts* it. The thermodynamic ceiling (Carnot efficiency, ~35%) of an ICE is replaced by an electrical efficiency ceiling of **85–95%**, which is the fundamental reason EVs are thermodynamically superior.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Regulatory Metrics

- **Energy Density of Li-ion traction battery:** **150–250 Wh/kg**
- **ICE fuel energy density (Gasoline):** **~12,000 Wh/kg**
- **Electric motor peak efficiency:** **~95%**
- **Battery round-trip efficiency:** **~90%**
- **Tailpipe CO₂ emission of BEV:** **0 g/km** (at point of use)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Classification Acronym (Memorize for KTU):**
> - **BEV** — Battery Electric Vehicle (100% electric)
> - **HEV** — Hybrid Electric Vehicle (no plug, ICE + battery)
> - **PHEV** — Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (plug + ICE)
> - **FCEV** — Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (Hydrogen → Electricity)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Structural Decomposition of an Electric Vehicle

The architecture of an EV can be decomposed into **four essential energy domains**. KTU examiners frequently ask students to draw and label these.

### Domain 1 — Energy Storage Subsystem
- **Traction Battery Pack** (Lithium-ion / LFP / NMC chemistry)
- **Battery Management System (BMS)** — monitors SoC, SoH, temperature, cell balancing
- **On-board Charger (OBC)** — converts AC grid → DC battery

### Domain 2 — Power Conversion Subsystem
- **Inverter / DC-DC Converter** — converts DC battery → 3-phase AC for motor
- **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** — top-level energy management brain

### Domain 3 — Electromechanical Subsystem
- **Traction Motor** — BLDC, PMSM, Induction Motor, SRM
- **Reduction Gearbox** (single-speed or multi-speed)
- **Differential & Driveshaft**

### Domain 4 — Auxiliary Subsystem
- **Regenerative Braking System**
- **Thermal Management Loop (TMS)**
- **Auxiliary 12V battery**, HVAC, lighting

## 2.2 Classification of Electric Vehicles — KTU High-Yield Table

| Parameter | BEV | HEV | PHEV | FCEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Battery only | Battery + ICE | Battery + ICE (plug-in) | Hydrogen Fuel Cell |
| External Charging | Yes | No | Yes | No (H₂ refueling) |
| Electric Range | 200–600 km | 2–5 km | 30–80 km | 400–600 km |
| Tailpipe Emission | Zero | Reduced | Reduced | Zero (H₂O only) |
| Example | Tata Nexon EV | Toyota Camry Hybrid | Toyota Prius Prime | Toyota Mirai |
| Powertrain Complexity | Low | High | High | Medium |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — EV Fundamental Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Absolute Value Rule Applied:** All absolute value bars use `\vert` to preserve markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vehicle Range | $R = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{E_{100}}$ | $R$ in km, $E_{bat}$ in kWh, $E_{100}$ in kWh/100 km | Distance per full charge |
| 2 | Specific Energy Consumption | $E_{100} = \dfrac{P_{aux} + F_v v}{36 \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}$ | $P_{aux}$ in W, $F_v$ in N, $v$ in m/s | Energy per 100 km |
| 3 | Traction Force | $F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc}$ | $F_r$=rolling, $F_{ad}$=aero drag, $F_{hc}$=hill climb | Total resistive force |
| 4 | Rolling Resistance | $F_r = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cos\theta$ | $\mu_r$=0.008–0.015, $m$ in kg | Tire deformation loss |
| 5 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | $\rho=1.225$ kg/m³, $C_d A$ in m² | Air resistance |
| 6 | Hill Climbing Force | $F_{hc} = m \cdot g \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ = slope angle (rad) | Gravitational component |
| 7 | Required Motor Power | $P_{mot} = F_t \cdot v / \eta_{mot}$ | $P_{mot}$ in W, $\eta_{mot}\approx 0.9$ | Mechanical power demand |
| 8 | Battery Pack Energy | $E_{bat} = V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah} / 1000$ | $V_{nom}$ in V, $C_{Ah}$ in Ah | Total stored energy |
| 9 | State of Charge (SoC) | $SoC = \dfrac{Q_{rem}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | $Q$ in Coulombs | Remaining capacity |
| 10 | Regenerative Braking Efficiency | $\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{E_{rec}}{E_{kin}} \times 100\%$ | $E_{rec}$ recovered, $E_{kin}$ lost | Braking energy recovered |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are not academic abstractions — they are the **daily working tools** of an EV design engineer at companies like Tata Motors, Ather Energy, BYD, and Tesla. For example, the **range equation (Eq. 1)** is what determines whether a 40 kWh battery pack on the Tata Punch EV gives it a 421 km ARAI range or only 350 km in real-world driving. The **traction force equation** is used to size the motor (e.g., a 100 kW PMSM for a 1500 kg SUV).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX
