## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vehicle Range from Battery Capacity

This derivation is a **KTU favorite** for the 14-mark Part B questions.

**Problem Statement:** Derive an expression for the range $R$ (in km) of a pure electric vehicle in terms of its battery pack energy, rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and drivetrain efficiency.

**Step 1 — Identify the resistive forces opposing motion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{acc} \\
\text{where } F_t &= \text{Total tractive force required (N)} \\
F_r &= \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cos\theta \quad \text{(Rolling resistance)} \\
F_{ad} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 \quad \text{(Aerodynamic drag)} \\
F_{hc} &= m \cdot g \sin\theta \quad \text{(Hill climbing)} \\
F_{acc} &= m \cdot a \quad \text{(Linear acceleration)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the instantaneous mechanical power demand at the wheels:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wheels} &= F_t \cdot v \\
P_{wheels} &= v \cdot \left[ \mu_r m g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m a \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Power is force times velocity. The motor must supply enough mechanical power at the wheels to overcome all four resistance components simultaneously.

**Step 3 — Account for drivetrain losses to get the electrical power drawn from the battery:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{battery} &= \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{\eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{trans}} \\
\text{Let } \eta_{drivetrain} &= \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{inv} \\
P_{battery} &= \dfrac{v \cdot \left[ \mu_r m g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m a \right]}{\eta_{drivetrain}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the energy consumed per unit distance (specific energy consumption):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{specific} &= \dfrac{P_{battery}}{v} \quad \text{(in Wh/km or kWh/100 km)} \\
E_{specific} &= \dfrac{\mu_r m g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m a}{\eta_{drivetrain}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Range Equation by dividing total battery energy by specific consumption:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{E_{bat}}{E_{specific}} \quad \text{(in km)} \\
\boxed{R = \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}{\mu_r m g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m a}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style 14-mark sub-part)

**Given:**
- Vehicle mass $m = 1500$ kg
- Rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_r = 0.012$
- Drag coefficient area $C_d A = 0.7$ m²
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Constant speed $v = 60$ km/h $= 16.67$ m/s
- Level road, $\theta = 0$, so $\cos\theta = 1, \sin\theta = 0$
- Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{drivetrain} = 0.85$
- Battery pack energy $E_{bat} = 40$ kWh

**Step A — Compute rolling resistance force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_r &= \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta \\
F_r &= 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 1 \\
F_r &= 176.58 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Compute aerodynamic drag force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{ad} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 \\
F_{ad} &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times (16.67)^2 \\
F_{ad} &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times 277.89 \\
F_{ad} &= 119.13 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Compute total tractive force (level road, no acceleration):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{acc} \\
F_t &= 176.58 + 119.13 + 0 + 0 \\
F_t &= 295.71 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Compute specific energy consumption:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{specific} &= \dfrac{F_t}{\eta_{drivetrain}} \quad \text{(in Wh/m, then convert)} \\
E_{specific} &= \dfrac{295.71}{0.85} = 347.89 \text{ W/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to kWh/100 km:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{100} &= 347.89 \text{ W/m} \times \dfrac{1000 \text{ m}}{1 \text{ km}} \times \dfrac{100 \text{ km}}{1} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ kW}}{1000 \text{ W}} \\
E_{100} &= 34.79 / 10 \approx 17.39 \text{ kWh/100 km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — Compute range:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{E_{bat}}{E_{100}} \times 100 \\
R &= \dfrac{40}{17.39} \times 100 \\
\boxed{R \approx 230 \text{ km}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note to Students:** The ARAI-rated range is typically **20–30% higher** than the theoretical range we derived here because ARAI uses a constant-speed no-wind test. Real-world range is what you computed.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — EV vs ICE

| Performance Metric | Electric Vehicle (BEV) | ICE Vehicle |
|---|---|---|
| Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency | 75–85% | 20–35% |
| Well-to-Wheel Efficiency | 30–45% (grid dependent) | 15–25% |
| Peak Torque Availability | Instant (0 RPM) | Requires RPM ramp-up |
| Number of Moving Parts | ~20 | ~2,000 |
| Service Interval | Every 1–2 years | Every 6 months |
| NVH Level | Very Low (Silent) | High |
| Refueling Time | 4–8 hours (AC) / 30–60 min (DC) | 3–5 minutes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[AC Grid Supply 230V] --> B1

    H1 --> I1[Differential and Wheels]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Q: Define an Electric Vehicle. List any four major components of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV).**

**Model Answer:**
An Electric Vehicle is a vehicle that uses one or more electric motors powered by an onboard energy storage system (typically a rechargeable battery) for propulsion. In a pure BEV, an Internal Combustion Engine is absent.

Four major components of a BEV:
1. **Traction Battery Pack** — stores electrical energy.
2. **Power Inverter / Controller** — converts DC to 3-phase AC for the motor.
3. **Electric Traction Motor** — converts electrical energy to mechanical motion.
4. **On-board Charger and BMS** — manages charging and battery health.

(Any four valid components with one-line definition — 3 marks)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]`
**Q: Differentiate between BEV, HEV, and PHEV in terms of energy source and external charging capability.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Energy Source | External Charging |
|---|---|---|
| **BEV** | Battery only | Yes |
| **HEV** | Battery + ICE | No (regen only) |
| **PHEV** | Battery + ICE | Yes |

BEVs have no ICE and rely entirely on grid charging. HEVs use the ICE as primary and battery only for assist/regen. PHEVs can run on battery alone for 30–80 km and also have an ICE for extended range, with plug-in capability.

(3 marks — for table + 2 lines of explanation)

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> In 14-mark Part B questions on EV introduction, students lose marks on three pitfalls: **(1)** Drawing the block diagram without labeling arrows or energy flow direction, **(2)** Forgetting to convert units (km/h to m/s) in derivations, **(3)** Writing only advantages without backing them with numerical/efficiency reasoning. Always show units and use the formula sheet properly.

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU Model Paper Pattern]`

**Q: A (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV). Describe the function of each block. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the schematic — reproduce it as a labeled block diagram in the exam.)*

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| Battery Pack | Stores DC electrical energy (e.g., 40 kWh Li-ion) |
| BMS | Monitors SoC, SoH, temperature, cell balancing |
| Inverter | Converts DC to 3-phase AC; controls motor speed/torque via PWM |
| Traction Motor | Converts electrical → mechanical energy (PMSM/BLDC) |
| Transmission | Reduces motor RPM and increases torque |
| OBC | Converts AC grid → DC for battery charging |
| DC-DC Converter | Steps down high-voltage to 12V for auxiliaries |

(7 marks: 4 for labeled diagram + 3 for function table)

---

**Q: A (b) Derive the expression for the range of an Electric Vehicle. A BEV has a 50 kWh battery, drivetrain efficiency of 88%, and is driven at a constant 80 km/h on a level road. The rolling resistance coefficient is 0.011, mass is 1800 kg, and CdA is 0.75 m². Compute the range. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — State the formula (derived in Section 3.1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}{\mu_r m g + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert velocity:**

$$
v = 80 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.22 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute rolling resistance force:**

$$
F_r = 0.011 \times 1800 \times 9.81 = 194.24 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute aerodynamic drag force:**

$$
F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.75 \times (22.22)^2 = 226.86 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Total tractive force:**

$$
F_t = 194.24 + 226.86 = 421.10 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Power at wheels:**

$$
P_w = 421.10 \times 22.22 = 9357.44 \text{ W} \approx 9.36 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Power drawn from battery (accounting for η):**

$$
P_b = \dfrac{9.36}{0.88} = 10.63 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 8 — Range:**

$$
R = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_b} \times v = \dfrac{50}{10.63} \times 80 \approx \boxed{376 \text{ km}}
$$

(7 marks: 2 for formula derivation + 5 for numerical computation with steps)

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) `[Alternative Choice]`

**Q: B (a) Classify Electric Vehicles into BEV, HEV, PHEV, and FCEV. Compare them on the basis of energy source, electric range, charging method, and emission. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*(Refer to Section 2.2 Classification Table for full content — the table itself is the answer backbone.)*

**Explanation to accompany the table:**
- **BEV** — Pure electric, charged from grid, zero range anxiety if infrastructure is good.
- **HEV** — Mild or full hybrid, cannot be plugged in, ~40% efficiency gain over ICE.
- **PHEV** — Bridges BEV and HEV, 30–80 km electric range covers daily commute.
- **FCEV** — Uses hydrogen, emits only water, but H₂ infrastructure is poor in India.

(7 marks: 3 for table + 2 for examples + 2 for comparative reasoning)

---

**Q: B (b) Discuss the advantages and limitations of Electric Vehicles over conventional ICE vehicles. Justify with numerical reasoning. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages (with numerical justification):**

1. **Higher Efficiency:** EV drivetrain ~85% vs ICE ~25%. For the same 100 km journey, EV uses ~17 kWh vs ICE burning ~6 liters of petrol.
2. **Zero Tailpipe Emission:** Direct CO₂ output = 0 g/km (vs ~120 g/km for a typical petrol car).
3. **Lower Operating Cost:** Electricity cost ≈ ₹1.2/km vs petrol cost ≈ ₹7/km at 2024 prices.
4. **Regenerative Braking:** Recovers 10–30% of kinetic energy in city driving.
5. **Low Maintenance:** ~20 moving parts vs ~2,000 in ICE — no oil change, fewer wear parts.

**Limitations:**

1. **Range Anxiety:** Practical range 200–400 km vs ICE 600–800 km.
2. **Charging Time:** 4–8 hours (AC) vs 5 minutes (petrol refill).
3. **Upfront Cost:** ₹15–25 lakh for a mid-segment EV vs ₹8–12 lakh for ICE.
4. **Battery Degradation:** Capacity drops to 70–80% after 8 years / 1,60,000 km.
5. **Charging Infrastructure:** ~12,000 public chargers in India vs 70,000+ petrol stations.

(7 marks: 3 for advantages with numbers + 2 for limitations + 2 for critical analysis)

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Electric Vehicles Introduction: High-Yield Summary**

- **EV Definition:** A vehicle propelled by one or more electric motors powered by an onboard energy storage system (battery / fuel cell).
- **4 Main Types to Memorize:** BEV (battery), HEV (hybrid, no plug), PHEV (plug-in hybrid), FCEV (hydrogen).
- **Core EV Subsystems:** Battery + BMS | Inverter/Converter | Traction Motor | Transmission | Charging System.
- **Master the Range Equation:** $R = E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain} / F_t$ — this is the **#1 favorite 14-mark derivation** for KTU.
- **Three Resistive Forces to Memorize:** $F_r$ (rolling) + $F_{ad}$ (aero drag) + $F_{hc}$ (hill climb).
- **Efficiency Argument:** EV ~85% tank-to-wheel vs ICE ~25% — always cite this number.
- **Key Indian Context:** Tata Nexon EV, Tata Punch EV, MG ZS EV, Ather 450X — know at least 2 examples.
- **ARAI Range Definition:** Tested at constant 60 km/h on a dynamometer — usually 20–30% higher than real-world.
- **Conversion to Memorize:** 1 km/h = 1000/3600 m/s = 0.2778 m/s.
- **Units Consistency:** Always express energy in **kWh**, force in **N**, velocity in **m/s**, power in **kW**.
- **Pitfall to Avoid:** Never mix kWh and Ah without specifying battery voltage.

**Memory Aid (Acronym — "BEV-HPF"):** **B**attery / **E**lectric motor / **V**CU — **H**ybrid (HEV) / **P**lug-in (PHEV) / **F**uel Cell (FCEV).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *The history of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs) traces the chronological evolution of automobile propulsion technologies from purely mechanical (steam/Internal Combustion Engine) origins, through the pioneering era of battery-electric traction in the 19th century, the mid-20th century ICE dominance, to the modern resurgence driven by oil crises, environmental concerns, semiconductor power electronics, and lithium-ion battery breakthroughs.*

In the KTU PEMET863 framework, this topic establishes the **contextual foundation (CO1: *Understand the fundamental concepts and societal relevance of HEV technologies*)** before progressing into architectures, powertrains, and energy storage.

### 1.1.1 Taxonomy of Vehicle Powertrain Eras

| Era | Period | Dominant Technology | Defining Event |
|-----|--------|--------------------|----------------|
| Pioneer Era | 1830 – 1900 | Steam, Electric, ICE (mixed) | First tricycle by Gustave Trouvé (1881) |
| Golden Age of EVs | 1900 – 1912 | Electric + ICE competition | EVs were ~28% of US car market |
| ICE Hegemony | 1912 – 1965 | Mass-produced ICE | Ford Model T assembly line (1908/1913) |
| Dormant Period | 1965 – 1990 | Refined ICE | OPEC oil shocks (1973, 1979) |
| Modern Resurgence | 1990 – Present | HEV / BEV / FCEV | Toyota Prius (1997), Tesla Roadster (2008) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Three-Brother Race":**
> Imagine three brothers (Steam, Electric, and Gasoline) born in the early 1800s competing to run a delivery business.
> - **Steam Brother** is heavy, slow to start, and needs water refills every few kilometers. By 1900 he retires.
> - **Electric Brother** is silent, clean, and starts instantly, but his "lunch box" (battery) is heavy and short-ranged. He loses the race in 1912 when Gasoline brother invents a small key (the self-starter).
> - **Gasoline Brother** becomes rich and dominates for 80 years.
> - **Then in the 1970s**, oil prices shoot up like a fever, environmental rules get strict, and Electric Brother — now with a much better lunch box (lithium-ion) — returns to the race. **Hybrid Brother** (half gasoline + half electric) joins as a compromise.
>
> This is exactly the history we are about to study — three powertrain brothers in a 200-year relay race.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Industry Metrics

- **1 horsepower (hp)** = **745.7 W** — the earliest unit used to benchmark the first electric motors vs. ICE.
- **Wh/kg** (Watt-hour per kilogram) — **specific energy** metric used historically to judge battery progress.
  * Lead-Acid (1900): **30–40 Wh/kg**
  * NiMH (1997 Prius): **60–80 Wh/kg**
  * Li-ion (2024): **250–300 Wh/kg**
- **Global EV stock growth** rose from **~17,000 units (2010)** to over **40 million units (2023)** — a CAGR > **80%**.
- **CO₂ emissions from transport** ≈ **16% of global CO₂** (IPCC AR6) — the modern *why* behind HEV/EV adoption.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Structured Chronological Analysis

### 2.1.1 The Pioneer Era (1830 – 1900)

- **1832 – Robert Anderson (Scotland)**: Built the first crude **non-rechargeable electric carriage** using primary cells. Demonstrated that electromagnetic force could propel a road vehicle.
- **1834 – Thomas Davenport (USA)**: Invented the first **DC electric motor** capable of driving a small printing press; later adapted to vehicles.
- **1859 – Gaston Planté (France)**: Invented the **lead-acid battery** — the first *rechargeable* battery. This is the single most important enabling invention for early EVs.
- **1881 – Gustave Trouvé (France)**: Demonstrated the first **practical three-wheeled electric vehicle** at the International Exposition of Electricity in Paris. Rechargeable, roadworthy, and publicly tested.
- **1890 – William Morrison (USA)**: Built the first **successful electric automobile in the United States** — a six-passenger wagon capable of ~14 mph (22.5 km/h).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this era matters for KTU:** Students must be able to *list at least three pioneer inventions and explain how each removed a technical barrier* (rechargeability, motor torque, road usability).

### 2.1.2 The Golden Age of Electric Vehicles (1900 – 1912)

- By 1900, **electric cars held ~28%** of the United States automobile market, **steam cars ~40%**, and **gasoline cars ~22%**.
- Famous EVs of the era: **Detroit Electric (1907–1939)**, **Baker Motor Vehicle**, **Columbia Electric**.
- EVs dominated in **cities** because they had **no crank-starting**, no exhaust fumes, and no gear changes — perfect for upper-class urban women drivers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reason for early EV popularity:** Women in long dresses could not safely hand-crank gasoline engines. The electric starter had not yet been invented.

### 2.1.3 The Fall — Why ICE Won (1912 – 1965)

The decline of EVs is attributed to **four converging technical breakthroughs** in the ICE ecosystem:

1. **1908 – Ford Model T**: Introduced **mass-production**, dropping car price from ~$850 to ~$260 (1925).
2. **1912 – Charles Kettering's Electric Starter**: Removed the dangerous hand crank, opening ICE cars to women and the elderly.
3. **Discovery of Texas crude oil (1901 – Spindletop)**: Made gasoline cheap and abundant.
4. **Improvement of road infrastructure**: Long-distance ICE travel became feasible.

By **1935**, electric vehicles had effectively **disappeared** from mass markets.

### 2.1.4 Dormant Period & Awakening (1965 – 1990)

- **1973 OPEC Oil Embargo**: Crude oil prices quadrupled; energy independence became a national-security agenda.
- **1976 – US Congress passes EPCA (Energy Policy and Conservation Act)**.
- **1979 – Second oil crisis**.
- **1990 – CARB (California Air Resources Board)** issued the **Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV) Mandate**, forcing automakers to develop EVs.

### 2.1.5 Modern Resurgence (1990 – Present)

- **1996 – GM EV1**: First mass-produced modern EV from a major OEM (lead-acid then NiMH).
- **1997 – Toyota Prius (Japan)**: World's first **mass-produced hybrid electric vehicle**. Launched globally in 2000.
- **2003 – Tesla Motors founded** by Martin Eberhard & Marc Tarpenning.
- **2008 – Tesla Roadster**: First highway-legal **BEV with Li-ion cells**, 320 km range.
- **2010 – Nissan Leaf**, **2010 – Chevy Volt** (EREV).
- **2010 – Reva (India)** becomes Mahindra e2o.
- **2015 – Paris Agreement**: 196 nations commit to climate targets.
- **2023 – Global EV sales cross 14 million units (~18% of new car market)**.

### 2.1.6 Indian Context — HEV/EV History in India

- **1994 – Reva Electric Vehicle Company** founded in Bangalore by Chetan Maini.
- **2001 – Reva** commercially launched as India's first modern EV.
- **2013 – National Electric Mobility Mission Plan 2020**.
- **2015 – FAME India Scheme (Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles)**.
- **2023 – FAME-II** extended; **PLI scheme** for Advanced Cell Chemistry (ACC) — ₹18,100 crore.
- **2023 – Tata Motors becomes India's largest EV manufacturer** with Tiago EV, Nexon EV, Tigor EV.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Cheat Sheet — Use ` \vert ` instead of ` \vert ` to avoid breaking markdown tables.**

| # | Formula / Metric | Description | Unit | Application |
|---|------------------|-------------|------|-------------|
| 1 | $E = V \cdot Q$ | Stored energy in battery | Wh | Early EV range estimation |
| 2 | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Specific energy | Wh/kg | Compare battery chemistries |
| 3 | $P = \eta \cdot V \cdot I$ | Motor output power | W | Pioneer-era motor sizing |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{E_{100km}}$ | Range from energy | km | Historic EV range plotting |
| 5 | $CAGR = \left(\dfrac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{n}} - 1$ | Compound Annual Growth Rate | % | EV market growth 2010–2023 |
| 6 | $M_{share} = \dfrac{V_{tech}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | Market share of a technology | % | Used in 1900 vs 1935 split |
| 7 | $CO_2^{saved} = d \cdot f_e \cdot \rho_c$ | Annual CO₂ saved per EV | kg/yr | Justifies modern EV adoption |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive historians** use these timelines to argue *technology cycles* and *S-curve adoption*.
- **Policymakers** use market-share formulas to set **fleet electrification targets**.
- **Engineers** use the **specific-energy progression** to forecast future range improvements.
- **Investors** use **CAGR** to size the EV industry (often > 30% per year for Li-ion cells).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mandate:** Every algebraic step is written out explicitly. No shortcuts. Each numerical substitution is justified with a textual explanation row.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Specific Energy Comparison Across Battery Eras

The **specific energy** of a battery cell determines the maximum theoretical range of an EV.

$$E_{sp} = \frac{E_{cell}}{m_{cell}}$$

**Step-by-Step Worked Example:**

A 1900 Detroit Electric used a **lead-acid pack** of mass $m = 250 \text{ kg}$ storing $E = 10 \text{ kWh}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{sp} &= \frac{10{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{250 \text{ kg}} \\
       &= 40 \text{ Wh/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Reasoning row:** Mass in denominator penalizes heavy chemistries — this is why 1900 EVs had ranges of only **80–100 km**.

Compare with a 2024 Li-ion cell of same mass:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{sp}^{Li} &= \frac{250 \text{ Wh/kg} \times 250 \text{ kg}}{250 \text{ kg}} \\
            &= 250 \text{ Wh/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Range improvement factor:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{range} &= \frac{E_{sp}^{Li}}{E_{sp}^{Pb}} \\
          &= \frac{250}{40} \\
          &= 6.25
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A 2024 Li-ion pack of the same mass delivers **6.25× the range** of a 1900 lead-acid pack — this single metric explains the modern EV boom.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CAGR of Global EV Stock (2010 → 2023)

**Given:**
- Initial value $V_i = 17{,}000$ units (2010)
- Final value $V_f = 40{,}000{,}000$ units (2023)
- Period $n = 13$ years

$$CAGR = \left(\frac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{n}} - 1$$

**Step-by-Step:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_f}{V_i} &= \frac{40{,}000{,}000}{17{,}000} \\
                &= 2352.94
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln\left(\frac{V_f}{V_i}\right) &= \ln(2352.94) \\
                                &= 7.764
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{n}\ln\left(\frac{V_f}{V_i}\right) &= \frac{7.764}{13} \\
                                           &= 0.5972
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
CAGR &= e^{0.5972} - 1 \\
     &= 1.817 - 1 \\
     &= 0.817 \\
     &= 81.7\% \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The global EV stock grew at a Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) of **≈ 81.7 % per year** between 2010 and 2023. This is the **single most important historical growth metric** for KTU board questions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Market Share of EVs in 1900 (United States)

**Given:**
- Total US automobile registrations in 1900 ≈ 4,192 vehicles (US DOT archives)
- Steam cars: ~1,680 | Electric cars: ~1,575 | Gasoline cars: ~937

$$M_{EV} = \frac{V_{EV}}{V_{total}} \times 100$$

**Step-by-Step:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{total} &= 1680 + 1575 + 937 \\
          &= 4192
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{EV} &= \frac{1575}{4192} \times 100 \\
       &= 37.57\% \text{ (some sources cite 28% due to different statistical bases)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** State clearly the source of the data and the year. Many textbooks cite **28%**, which is calculated from different census definitions. The student must mention **"approx. 28–38%"** to be safe.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Annual CO₂ Saved per EV (Justification Metric)

A typical mid-size ICE car emits **~120 g CO₂/km**. Average annual driving: **15,000 km**.

**Step-by-Step:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CO_2^{ICE} &= 15{,}000 \text{ km} \times 120 \text{ g/km} \\
           &= 1{,}800{,}000 \text{ g} \\
           &= 1800 \text{ kg/year}
\end{aligned}$$

For an EV charged from a grid with emissions factor $f_g$ (e.g., India's grid ≈ **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh**) consuming **15 kWh/100 km**:

$$\begin{aligned}
CO_2^{EV} &= 15{,}000 \text{ km} \times 0.15 \text{ kWh/km} \times 0.72 \text{ kg/kWh} \\
          &= 1620 \text{ kg/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Savings:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta CO_2 &= 1800 - 1620 \\
            &= 180 \text{ kg/year}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Each EV saves **~180 kg CO₂/year** even on India's coal-heavy grid. In countries with cleaner grids (Norway, France), savings exceed **1500 kg/year**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Key Historical Milestones

| Year | Innovator / Entity | Country | Contribution | KTU Significance |
|------|-------------------|---------|--------------|------------------|
| 1832 | Robert Anderson | Scotland | First electric carriage | Pioneer recognition |
| 1859 | Gaston Planté | France | Lead-acid battery | First rechargeable storage |
| 1881 | Gustave Trouvé | France | First practical EV | Demonstrated road viability |
| 1890 | William Morrison | USA | First US EV | Foundation of US EV industry |
| 1908 | Henry Ford | USA | Model T assembly line | Killed mass-market EV |
| 1912 | Charles Kettering | USA | Electric starter | Removed ICE usability barrier |
| 1973 | OPEC | — | Oil embargo | Triggered EV R&D funding |
| 1990 | CARB | USA | ZEV Mandate | Forced modern EV development |
| 1996 | GM | USA | EV1 | First modern OEM EV |
| 1997 | Toyota | Japan | Prius (HEV) | First mass-produced hybrid |
| 2008 | Tesla | USA | Roadster (BEV) | Proved Li-ion BEV viability |
| 2010 | Nissan | Japan | Leaf | First mass-market BEV |
| 2013 | Government of India | India | NEMMP 2020 | Started Indian EV policy era |
| 2023 | Tata Motors | India | Largest Indian EV maker | Domestic manufacturing growth |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Q: Briefly explain the contributions of Robert Anderson and Gaston Planté to the evolution of electric vehicles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Robert Anderson (1832, Scotland)** built the first crude electric carriage using non-rechargeable primary cells. *(1 mark)*
2. **Gaston Planté (1859, France)** invented the **lead-acid rechargeable battery**, which became the dominant energy storage medium for early EVs and remained so until the 1990s. *(1 mark)*
3. Together, these inventions demonstrated the **two pillars of EV technology** — an electric motor for propulsion and a rechargeable chemical battery for energy storage. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]`

**Q: List any three reasons for the decline of electric vehicles between 1912 and 1965.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Mass production** of Ford Model T (1908–1913) drastically reduced the price of gasoline cars. *(1 mark)*
2. **Charles Kettering's electric self-starter (1912)** eliminated the dangerous hand crank of ICE vehicles, removing a key ICE usability barrier. *(1 mark)*
3. **Discovery of abundant Texas crude oil (1901)** made gasoline cheap, reducing the range anxiety of ICE vehicles compared to the limited-range EVs of the era. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q: Discuss in detail the chronological evolution of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles from the pioneer era (1830) to the modern resurgence (2024). Highlight the contributions of at least six key inventors/organizations and explain the four major reasons for the temporary decline of EVs between 1912 and 1965.**

#### Part (a) — Chronological Evolution (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The history of HEVs and EVs spans nearly **two centuries** and can be divided into **five distinct eras**:

1. **Pioneer Era (1830–1900)**: *(1 mark)*
   - **1832** — Robert Anderson (Scotland) built the first non-rechargeable electric carriage.
   - **1834** — Thomas Davenport (USA) developed the first DC electric motor suitable for vehicle propulsion.
   - **1859** — Gaston Planté (France) invented the **lead-acid rechargeable battery**, the dominant EV energy storage for over a century.
   - **1881** — Gustave Trouvé (France) demonstrated the first **practical three-wheeled electric vehicle** in Paris.
   - **1890** — William Morrison (USA) built the first successful electric automobile in the United States.

2. **Golden Age of EVs (1900–1912)**: *(1 mark)*
   - Electric cars held approximately **28–38% of the US automobile market**.
   - Famous models: **Detroit Electric**, **Baker Electric**, **Columbia Electric**.
   - EVs were preferred by urban women drivers due to ease of operation (no crank start).

3. **Dormant Period (1912–1965)**: *(1 mark)*
   - EVs disappeared from mass markets due to ICE dominance, cheap fuel, and mass production.

4. **Awakening Period (1965–1990)**: *(1 mark)*
   - **1973 OPEC Oil Embargo** triggered renewed interest.
   - **1990** — **CARB** issued the **Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV) Mandate** in California.

5. **Modern Resurgence (1990–Present)**: *(2 marks)*
   - **1996** — GM EV1 (first OEM mass-produced EV).
   - **1997** — Toyota Prius (first mass-produced **HEV**).
   - **2008** — Tesla Roadster (first highway-legal **Li-ion BEV**).
   - **2010** — Nissan Leaf, Chevy Volt launched.
   - **2013–2023** — India's **NEMMP 2020, FAME-I, FAME-II, and PLI** schemes accelerated domestic adoption.

6. **Contributions of Key Inventors** *(1 mark for completing six names correctly)*
   - Anderson, Planté, Trouvé, Morrison, Kettering (ICE starter), Toyota (Prius), Tesla, Nissan.

#### Part (b) — Four Reasons for Decline of EVs (1912–1965) (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Mass Production of ICE Vehicles** *(2 marks)*
   - Henry Ford introduced the **moving assembly line** for the Model T in 1913.
   - Production time per car fell from **12.5 hours to 93 minutes**.
   - Price dropped from **\$850 (1908)** to **\$260 (1925)** — making ICE cars affordable for the middle class.

2. **Invention of the Electric Starter** *(2 marks)*
   - In 1912, **Charles Kettering** invented the electric self-starter for Cadillac.
   - This removed the dangerous hand crank, opening ICE cars to **women, the elderly, and the urban upper class** — exactly the demographic that previously preferred EVs.

3. **Abundant and Cheap Crude Oil** *(2 marks)*
   - The **1901 Spindletop oil strike (Texas)** unlocked a vast petroleum supply.
   - Combined with later Middle East discoveries, gasoline prices fell, eliminating the cost advantage of electricity.

4. **Limited Range and Heavy Batteries** *(1 mark)*
   - 1900s lead-acid batteries gave only **80–100 km range**.
   - Top speed was limited to ~**32 km/h**.
   - No modern fast-charging infrastructure existed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common mistakes that cost marks:**
> - Writing only **2 reasons** instead of the required **4** → lose up to **3 marks**.
> - Confusing **Charles Kettering** with **Nikola Tesla** (they are unrelated).
> - Writing "Ford invented the car" — Ford did **not** invent it; he invented **mass production**.
> - Quoting wrong dates (e.g., Prius as 1990 — it was 1997).
> - Using "electric car failed because it was slow" — the real reason is *systemic economic and infrastructural* factors.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Alternative Module Choice]`

**Q: With the help of a timeline and market-share calculations, explain how the electric vehicle rose, fell, and rose again. Compute the CAGR of the global EV stock from 2010 to 2023, given that the stock grew from 17,000 units to 40 million units.**

#### Part (a) — Rise, Fall, and Re-Rise of EVs (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Rise (1830–1912)** *(2 marks)*
   - Pioneers Anderson, Planté, Trouvé, Morrison proved EV feasibility.
   - By 1900, EVs were a credible **third of US car sales**.

2. **Fall (1912–1965)** *(2 marks)*
   - Ford's mass production + Kettering's starter + cheap oil → EV market share collapsed to nearly **0% by 1935**.

3. **Re-Rise (1990–Present)** *(3 marks)*
   - **1990 CARB Mandate** → forced OEM R&D.
   - **1996 GM EV1** → proved OEM EV feasibility.
   - **1997 Toyota Prius** → proved HEV mass-market viability (>5 million sold worldwide).
   - **2008 Tesla Roadster** → proved Li-ion BEV viability (320 km range).
   - **2010–2024** → global EV stock crossed **40 million units** (CAGR ≈ 82% per year).

#### Part (b) — CAGR Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** $V_i = 17{,}000$ (2010), $V_f = 40{,}000{,}000$ (2023), $n = 13$ years.

$$CAGR = \left(\frac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$$

**Step-by-Step:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_f}{V_i} &= \frac{40{,}000{,}000}{17{,}000} = 2352.94
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{n} &= \frac{1}{13} = 0.0769
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
2352.94^{0.0769} &= e^{0.0769 \times \ln(2352.94)} \\
                 &= e^{0.0769 \times 7.764} \\
                 &= e^{0.597} \\
                 &= 1.817
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
CAGR &= 1.817 - 1 = 0.817 = \mathbf{81.7\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** *(1 mark)* The global EV stock grew at a Compound Annual Growth Rate of approximately **81.7% per year** between 2010 and 2023, making it one of the fastest-growing technology markets in human history.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common mistakes on CAGR questions:**
> - Writing the formula as $CAGR = \frac{V_f - V_i}{n}$ — this is **arithmetic mean**, not CAGR. **Lose 2 marks**.
> - Forgetting to **subtract 1** at the end → answer becomes 1.817 or 181.7%. **Lose 1 mark**.
> - Using wrong unit of $n$ (using 13 months instead of 13 years). **Lose 2 marks**.
> - Not converting 40 million to **40,000,000** correctly. **Lose 1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Must-Know for KTU Board Exam"**

- [x] **1832** — Robert Anderson — first electric carriage.
- [x] **1859** — Gaston Planté — lead-acid battery.
- [x] **1881** — Gustave Trouvé — first *practical* EV.
- [x] **1890** — William Morrison — first US EV.
- [x] **1900** — EVs held **~28% of US car market**.
- [x] **1908** — Ford Model T — mass-produced ICE.
- [x] **1912** — Charles Kettering — electric starter (not Tesla).
- [x] **1973** — OPEC oil embargo.
- [x] **1990** — CARB ZEV Mandate (California).
- [x] **1996** — GM EV1.
- [x] **1997** — Toyota Prius (HEVs established).
- [x] **2003** — Tesla Motors founded.
- [x] **2008** — Tesla Roadster (Li-ion BEV).
- [x] **2010** — Nissan Leaf (mass-market BEV).
- [x] **2013** — India NEMMP 2020.
- [x] **2015** — FAME-I; **2019** — FAME-II.
- [x] **2023** — Global EV stock ≈ **40 million** (CAGR ≈ **81.7%/yr**).
- [x] **Four reasons for ICE dominance** — mass production, electric starter, cheap oil, heavy batteries.
- [x] **CAGR formula** = $\left(\dfrac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$.
- [x] **Specific energy growth**: Pb 40 → NiMH 70 → Li-ion 250 Wh/kg.
- [x] **India's grid emissions factor** ≈ 0.72 kg CO₂/kWh.
- [x] **CO₂ savings per EV/year** ≈ 180 kg (India) to 1500 kg (clean grid).
- [x] **KTU must-mention names**: Anderson, Planté, Trouvé, Morrison, Kettering, Toyota, Tesla, Nissan, Tata Motors, Maini.
- [x] **Always quote years** in board answers — no year = no mark.

topics.title: Social and Environmental Importance of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Social and Environmental Importance of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is a road vehicle that derives its propulsion energy from two or more distinct energy sources, typically a conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) coupled with an electric drivetrain (motor + battery). As per KTU 2024 module classification, electrified vehicles are categorized into four operational archetypes:

1. **Micro-Hybrid (μHEV)** — Stop-Start systems with a small starter-generator; ICE dominant.
2. **Mild Hybrid (MHEV)** — 48 V belt-driven starter-generator assisting ICE; no pure EV mode.
3. **Full Hybrid (HEV)** — ICE + high-voltage battery + electric motor; capable of short EV-only operation (e.g., Toyota Prius).
4. **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV)** — Externally chargeable larger battery (8–18 kWh); 20–60 km pure EV range.

A **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)** is a zero-tailpipe-emission vehicle propelled solely by one or more electric machines, drawing energy exclusively from an onboard rechargeable traction battery charged from the external grid (e.g., Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A vehicle's classification (HEV/BEV/FCEV) is determined by the **degree of electrification (DoE)** = $E_{elec}/E_{total}$, where DoE = 0 % → ICE, 0–20 % → MHEV, 20–50 % → HEV, 50–100 % → PHEV/BEV.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the global transportation ecosystem as a large office building whose only power supply has been a single **coal-fired generator** (the petroleum-based ICE vehicle). For a century, this generator was cheap, but it leaks smoke (CO₂, NOₓ, PM), is noisy (combustion knock), and depends on a single delivery truck (crude oil imports).

A **Hybrid** is like installing a **solar panel on the roof + battery backup** — the generator still runs, but much less often, and during idle hours the solar+battery silently powers the lights.

A **Pure Electric** is like **removing the generator entirely** and letting the solar+battery system run the whole building — cleaner air, less noise, and energy independence.

The "social and environmental importance" topic asks: *Why should society bother paying the upfront cost of switching the building's power source?* — The answer lies in **three pillars**: cleaner lungs, stable climate, and sovereign energy.

### 1.3 Key Standardized Constants and Metrics

The following KTU-referenced benchmark values must be memorized:

| Metric | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ emission factor — Gasoline | $EF_{gas}$ | **2.31 kg CO₂ / litre** |
| CO₂ emission factor — Diesel | $EF_{die}$ | **2.68 kg CO₂ / litre** |
| India grid emission factor (CEA 2023) | $EF_{grid}$ | **0.72 kg CO₂ / kWh** |
| ICE thermal efficiency (gasoline) | $\eta_{ICE}$ | **25 – 35 %** |
| Electric motor + inverter efficiency | $\eta_{EM}$ | **85 – 92 %** |
| WHO PM2.5 annual guideline | $C_{PM2.5}$ | **5 µg/m³** |
| Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI) PM limit (diesel cars) | $L_{PM}$ | **4.5 mg/km** |
| Paris Agreement warming target | $\Delta T$ | **1.5 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Bharat Stage VI norms are equivalent to Euro 6 norms and represent India's most stringent on-road emission standard. Sulfur content in fuel dropped to **10 ppm** from BS-IV's 50 ppm — a 5× reduction in fuel-borne sulfate particulates.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Social–Environmental Importance

#### Pillar A — Environmental Importance

1. **Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Mitigation**
   * Transportation contributes **~14 %** of global CO₂ emissions (IPCC AR6).
   * In India, the transport sector emits **~250 Mt CO₂/yr** (TERI 2023 estimates) — projected to triple by 2070 under a Business-As-Usual (BAU) scenario.
   * EVs powered by the renewable-rich grid can achieve **70 – 90 % lifecycle CO₂ reduction** versus ICE.

2. **Urban Air Quality Improvement**
   * Vehicular exhaust contains **NOₓ, CO, HC, PM2.5, PM10** — direct causes of asthma, COPD, cardiovascular mortality.
   * Delhi's annual PM2.5 average = **~110 µg/m³** (CPCB 2023) — 22× the WHO safe limit.
   * BEVs produce **zero tailpipe emissions** — directly attacking the urban-breathable-pollutant problem.

3. **Noise Pollution Reduction**
   * ICE idling noise ≈ **55–65 dB**; BEVs at low speed ≈ **40 dB** (AVAS mandatory in EU from 2019, under evaluation in India).
   * 10 dB reduction ≈ perceived **halving of loudness** (logarithmic human ear response).

#### Pillar B — Social Importance

1. **Public Health Dividend**
   * Lancet Commission on Pollution & Health (2019) attributes **~9 million premature deaths/yr** globally to pollution.
   * A study by IIT Delhi estimates India's transport-pollution health cost ≈ **3 – 4 % of GDP**.

2. **Energy Security & Import Bill Reduction**
   * India imports **~85 %** of its crude oil requirement (PPAC 2023).
   * Crude oil import bill: **~$160 billion (FY 2022–23)** — a major forex drain.
   * EVs use electricity that can be **domestically generated** (coal, solar, wind, hydro, nuclear).

3. **Equitable Mobility Access**
   * EVs enable **two/three-wheeler electrification** at low cost — directly serving India's rural and Tier-2/3 city transport demand.

#### Pillar C — Economic & Strategic Importance

1. **Lower Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** in high-utilization segments.
2. **Job creation** in battery manufacturing, charging infrastructure, power electronics.
3. **Technology leadership** — India targets **30 % EV penetration by 2030** (NITI Aayog).

### 2.2 Why and How — The Operational Logic

The transition from ICE to xEV follows a **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** efficiency logic:

> **ICE chain:** Crude oil → Refining → Distribution → Tank → Engine (≈ 25 % efficient) → Wheels.
> **EV chain:** Primary energy → Power plant → Grid → Charger → Battery → Motor (≈ 70 % efficient) → Wheels.

The BEV wins on **two fronts**:
* **Higher drivetrain efficiency** (motor ≈ 90 % vs. engine ≈ 30 %).
* **Decarbonization of the source** — as the grid gets cleaner (more solar/wind), the WTW emissions automatically fall *without any change to the vehicle*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU numerical problems in Module 1 are built on the following six equations. Memorize them.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m_{CO_2}^{ICE} = V_{fuel} \times EF_{fuel}$ | Mass of CO₂ from ICE per trip | kg |
| 2 | $m_{CO_2}^{EV} = E_{elec} \times EF_{grid}$ | Mass of CO₂ from EV per trip | kg |
| 3 | $\Delta m_{CO_2} = m_{CO_2}^{ICE} - m_{CO_2}^{EV}$ | CO₂ saved by substitution | kg |
| 4 | $\% \text{Reduction} = \frac{\Delta m_{CO_2}}{m_{CO_2}^{ICE}} \times 100$ | Percentage emission cut | % |
| 5 | $\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{source} \times \eta_{transmission} \times \eta_{drivetrain}$ | Overall well-to-wheel efficiency | decimal |
| 6 | $TCO = C_{purchase} + C_{fuel/elec} + C_{maint} - C_{residual}$ | Total Cost of Ownership | ₹ / $ |

**Boundary conditions (must mention in KTU answers):**
* ICE efficiency assumed at **η = 0.30** for gasoline unless otherwise stated.
* Grid emission factor **EF_grid = 0.72 kg/kWh** for India (2023 CEA).
* 1 litre of gasoline ≈ **8.9 kWh** of chemical energy (Lower Heating Value).
* EV battery DoD (Depth of Discharge) ≈ **80 %** for cycle-life calculations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are used in:
* **Government policy design** — FAME-II subsidy calculations use CO₂-equivalent reductions.
* **OEM marketing** — automotive brochures cite WTW emissions.
* **Fleet procurement** — corporations (Amazon, Flipkart, Tata Steel) use TCO for EV fleet decisions.
* **Urban planning** — municipal corporations use noise/emission maps derived from these formulas.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — CO₂ Emission Reduction by Substituting ICE with BEV

**Problem Setup (KTU-style numerical):**
A sedan travels **D = 15,000 km/year**. The ICE variant consumes **$FC_{ICE}$ = 6.5 L/100 km** of gasoline. The BEV variant consumes **$EC_{EV}$ = 14 kWh/100 km**. Calculate the annual CO₂ emission reduction (in kg) and the percentage reduction.

**Given:**
$D = 15{,}000$ km/yr ; $FC_{ICE} = 6.5$ L/100 km ; $EC_{EV} = 14$ kWh/100 km ; $EF_{gas} = 2.31$ kg CO₂/L ; $EF_{grid} = 0.72$ kg CO₂/kWh.

**Step 1 — Compute the annual fuel/energy consumed by each vehicle.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{fuel}^{ICE} &= FC_{ICE} \times \frac{D}{100} \\
&= 6.5 \times \frac{15{,}000}{100} \\
&= 6.5 \times 150 \\
&= 975 \text{ litres/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Total fuel = fuel consumption rate × distance ÷ 100 (since rate is per 100 km).

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{elec}^{EV} &= EC_{EV} \times \frac{D}{100} \\
&= 14 \times \frac{15{,}000}{100} \\
&= 14 \times 150 \\
&= 2{,}100 \text{ kWh/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Total electricity = energy consumption rate × distance ÷ 100.

**Step 2 — Compute the CO₂ emitted by each vehicle annually.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2}^{ICE} &= V_{fuel}^{ICE} \times EF_{gas} \\
&= 975 \times 2.31 \\
&= 2{,}252.25 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Each litre of gasoline, when combusted, releases 2.31 kg of CO₂ — a stoichiometric constant (44/12 × carbon mass fraction).

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2}^{EV} &= E_{elec}^{EV} \times EF_{grid} \\
&= 2{,}100 \times 0.72 \\
&= 1{,}512 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Each kWh of electricity, generated by the 2023-Indian grid mix, is responsible for 0.72 kg of CO₂ at the power-plant smokestack.

**Step 3 — Compute the absolute CO₂ saved annually.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta m_{CO_2} &= m_{CO_2}^{ICE} - m_{CO_2}^{EV} \\
&= 2{,}252.25 - 1{,}512 \\
&= 740.25 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The substitution eliminates this much upstream-to-tailpipe carbon.

**Step 4 — Compute the percentage reduction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Reduction} &= \frac{\Delta m_{CO_2}}{m_{CO_2}^{ICE}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{740.25}{2{,}252.25} \times 100 \\
&= 32.86 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Even on the *coal-heavy* Indian grid (2023), the BEV emits **~33 % less CO₂** than its gasoline ICE counterpart for the same duty cycle. If the grid is re-powered with renewables (2030 target), the reduction can rise to **70 – 80 %**.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Well-to-Wheel Efficiency Comparison

**Problem Setup:**
Compare the WTW efficiency of a gasoline ICE car (η_engine = 0.30) and a BEV (η_plant = 0.38 for Indian coal grid, η_transmission = 0.92, η_charger = 0.90, η_motor = 0.92).

**Step 1 — ICE WTW efficiency (assume refinery + transport losses bundled into a "source" term):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{WTW}^{ICE} &= \eta_{source}^{refining} \times \eta_{trans}^{distribution} \times \eta_{engine} \times \eta_{transmission}^{gearbox} \\
&= 0.85 \times 0.95 \times 0.30 \times 0.90 \\
&= 0.218 \;\;(\text{or } 21.8 \text{ \%})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Only ~22 % of the crude oil's chemical energy reaches the wheels.

**Step 2 — BEV WTW efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{WTW}^{BEV} &= \eta_{plant} \times \eta_{grid}^{trans} \times \eta_{charger} \times \eta_{battery}^{disch} \times \eta_{motor\,+\,inv} \times \eta_{gearbox} \\
&= 0.38 \times 0.92 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 \\
&= 0.260 \;\;(\text{or } 26.0 \text{ \%})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The BEV is **~19 % more efficient** than the ICE even on a coal-dominant grid.

**Step 3 — Projected BEV efficiency on a renewable-dominant (solar+wind) grid by 2040:**

If $\eta_{plant}^{RE} = 0.45$ (combined-cycle renewable equivalent, accounting for curtailment):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{WTW}^{BEV, 2040} &= 0.45 \times 0.92 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 \\
&= 0.308 \;\;(\text{or } 30.8 \text{ \%})
\end{aligned}
$$

The BEV's WTW efficiency climbs **automatically** as the grid decarbonizes — a fundamental *why* of long-term sustainability.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ICE vs. HEV vs. PHEV vs. BEV

| Parameter | ICE | HEV (Full) | PHEV | BEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tailpipe CO₂ | 184.8 g/km | 110 g/km | 60 g/km | **0 g/km** |
| WTW CO₂ (India grid) | 220 g/km | 165 g/km | 130 g/km | **108 g/km** |
| Energy cost / km | ₹ 7.0 | ₹ 5.0 | ₹ 3.5 | **₹ 1.8** |
| PM2.5 emitted (tailpipe) | 4.5 mg/km | 2.5 mg/km | 1.2 mg/km | **0 mg/km** |
| Noise at 30 km/h | 62 dB | 58 dB | 55 dB | **48 dB** |
| Refuel / Recharge time | 2 min | 2 min | 2 min + 2 h | **45 min (DC fast)** |
| Driving range | 700 km | 900 km | 600 km | **350 km** |
| Govt. subsidy (FAME-II) | Nil | Nil | ₹ 20,000 | **₹ 1.5 L (cars)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The matrix above is the *single most commonly asked comparative question* in KTU exams. Memorize the *trends* (BEV lowest in all environmental metrics) and the *order* (BEV < PHEV < HEV < ICE in emissions).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List any three social benefits of switching from conventional ICE vehicles to electric vehicles in the Indian context."**

*Model Answer (90–110 words):*

> The transition from ICE to EVs delivers three key social benefits in India. **First, energy security** — India imports ~85 % of its crude oil; EVs use domestically generated electricity, reducing the foreign-exchange drain (~$160 bn/yr). **Second, public health improvement** — vehicular exhaust (PM2.5, NOₓ) causes ~3–4 % GDP-equivalent health losses in India; EVs produce zero tailpipe emissions, directly improving urban air quality. **Third, equitable mobility access** — low-cost electric two/three-wheelers can serve rural and Tier-2 city populations, democratising personal transport and supporting the **"EV30@30"** NITI Aayog vision for inclusive growth.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Explain how the adoption of electric vehicles contributes to the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions."**

*Model Answer (90–110 words):*

> EVs reduce GHG emissions through **two distinct mechanisms**. *First*, on a tank-to-wheel (TTW) basis, BEVs emit zero CO₂ from the tailpipe — electricity, not hydrocarbon combustion, drives the wheels. *Second*, on a **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** basis, even when powered by India's current grid (CO₂ intensity = 0.72 kg/kWh), a BEV emits **~30–35 % less** CO₂ per km than a comparable gasoline car. Crucially, the WTW emissions of EVs *automatically decline* as the grid shifts to renewables (solar + wind) — by 2040, the reduction can exceed **70 %**, helping India meet its **Paris Agreement** commitments of limiting global warming to 1.5 °C above pre-industrial levels.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — *Internal Choice Provided*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **environmental importance** of hybrid and electric vehicles, with special reference to:
(i) Urban air pollution (PM2.5, NOₓ, CO, HC).
(ii) Greenhouse gas emissions and the Paris Agreement.
(iii) Noise pollution in urban traffic.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A delivery fleet runs **50 three-wheelers**, each covering **40 km/day**, **300 days/year**. The ICE variant consumes **3.5 L/100 km** of gasoline. The electric variant consumes **7 kWh/100 km**. Calculate:
(i) Annual CO₂ emissions from the ICE fleet.
(ii) Annual CO₂ emissions from the EV fleet (use grid factor = 0.72 kg/kWh).
(iii) Total annual CO₂ saved by full electrification.
(iv) Percentage reduction in CO₂ emissions.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Environmental Importance (7 marks):**

**Urban Air Pollution (2 marks):**
The combustion of gasoline/diesel in ICE vehicles releases **PM2.5, PM10, NOₓ, CO, HC, and SO₂**. These pollutants are responsible for asthma, bronchitis, lung cancer, and cardiovascular diseases. Indian cities like Delhi, Mumbai, and Bengaluru routinely record AQI in the *Poor–Severe* category (>200). Hybrid vehicles reduce tailpipe emissions by **40–60 %** through engine shut-off at idle and regenerative braking, while BEVs bring these emissions to **absolute zero** at the tailpipe — directly improving breathable air quality.

**Greenhouse Gas Emissions & Paris Agreement (3 marks):**
The transport sector contributes ~14 % of global GHG emissions. India's transport-related CO₂ emission is ~250 Mt CO₂/yr and projected to triple by 2070 (BAU scenario). To honour the Paris Agreement commitment of limiting global warming to **1.5 °C**, India must decarbonize road transport. Lifecycle analyses show that EVs achieve:
* **30 – 35 %** CO₂ reduction on the present Indian grid.
* **70 – 80 %** CO₂ reduction on a renewable-dominant 2040 grid.
* **Zero operational emissions** when powered by rooftop solar.

HEVs/PHEVs act as *bridge technologies* during the transition window.

**Noise Pollution (2 marks):**
ICE engines produce 55–70 dB of traffic noise; constant exposure above 65 dB causes hearing loss and sleep disturbance. EVs at low speeds operate at ~40 dB — a *logarithmic* halving of perceived loudness. The Indian Ministry of Road Transport is evaluating **AVAS (Acoustic Vehicle Alerting Systems)** for pedestrian safety, modelled on EU regulations from 2019.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$N = 50$ vehicles ; $D_{annual} = 40 \times 300 = 12{,}000$ km/vehicle/yr ; $FC = 3.5$ L/100 km ; $EC = 7$ kWh/100 km ; $EF_{gas} = 2.31$ kg/L ; $EF_{grid} = 0.72$ kg/kWh.

**Step 1 — Annual fuel/energy per vehicle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{fuel}^{ICE} &= 3.5 \times \frac{12{,}000}{100} = 420 \text{ L/vehicle/yr} \\
E_{elec}^{EV} &= 7 \times \frac{12{,}000}{100} = 840 \text{ kWh/vehicle/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — CO₂ per ICE vehicle (Step i):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2}^{ICE,\,veh} &= 420 \times 2.31 = 970.2 \text{ kg/yr} \\
M_{CO_2}^{ICE,\,fleet} &= 50 \times 970.2 = 48{,}510 \text{ kg/yr} \\
&= 48.51 \text{ tonnes/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — CO₂ per EV (Step ii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2}^{EV,\,veh} &= 840 \times 0.72 = 604.8 \text{ kg/yr} \\
M_{CO_2}^{EV,\,fleet} &= 50 \times 604.8 = 30{,}240 \text{ kg/yr} \\
&= 30.24 \text{ tonnes/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total CO₂ saved (Step iii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta M_{CO_2} &= 48{,}510 - 30{,}240 = 18{,}270 \text{ kg/yr} \\
&= 18.27 \text{ tonnes/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage reduction (Step iv):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Reduction} &= \frac{18{,}270}{48{,}510} \times 100 = 37.66 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**
(i) ICE fleet CO₂ = **48.51 t/yr**
(ii) EV fleet CO₂ = **30.24 t/yr**
(iii) CO₂ saved = **18.27 t/yr**
(iv) Reduction = **37.66 %**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternate Choice]*

**(a)** Explain the **social importance** of hybrid and electric vehicles under the following headings:
(i) Public health dividend.
(ii) Energy security and oil import reduction.
(iii) Job creation and industrial development.
(iv) Role in achieving Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A comparison is to be made between an **ICE car** and a **BEV car** for an annual mileage of **20,000 km**.
* ICE: mileage = **15 km/L**, fuel price = **₹ 105/L**.
* BEV: efficiency = **6 km/kWh**, electricity price = **₹ 8/kWh**.
* Grid emission factor = **0.72 kg/kWh** ; gasoline emission factor = **2.31 kg/L**.
Calculate:
(i) Annual fuel cost of the ICE car.
(ii) Annual electricity cost of the BEV.
(iii) Annual CO₂ emitted by each vehicle.
(iv) Total annual savings (₹ + kg CO₂) by going electric.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Social Importance (7 marks):**

**(i) Public Health Dividend (2 marks):**
WHO estimates pollution causes ~7 million premature deaths globally. The transport sector contributes ~25 % of ambient PM2.5 in Indian cities. EVs eliminate tailpipe PM, NOₓ, and CO — reducing respiratory and cardiovascular morbidity. The WHO's 2021 global air quality guidelines recommend PM2.5 ≤ **5 µg/m³**; Delhi's average is ~110 µg/m³, requiring urgent EV adoption.

**(ii) Energy Security (2 marks):**
India imports ~85 % of its crude oil ($160 bn/yr FY23). EVs using domestic electricity (coal, solar, wind, hydro, nuclear) reduce this dependency, strengthening the **rupee** and **balance-of-payments**.

**(iii) Job Creation (1.5 marks):**
The EV ecosystem creates jobs in **cell manufacturing, BMS design, motor-controller production, charging infrastructure**, and **recycling**. NITI Aayog estimates ~10 million direct + indirect jobs by 2030 in India's EV value chain.

**(iv) SDGs (1.5 marks):**
EVs directly support **SDG 3** (Good Health), **SDG 7** (Affordable & Clean Energy), **SDG 11** (Sustainable Cities), and **SDG 13** (Climate Action).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Annual fuel cost of ICE (i):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{fuel} &= \frac{20{,}000}{15} = 1{,}333.33 \text{ L/yr} \\
C_{ICE} &= 1{,}333.33 \times 105 = \text{₹ } 1{,}40{,}000/\text{yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual electricity cost of BEV (ii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{elec} &= \frac{20{,}000}{6} = 3{,}333.33 \text{ kWh/yr} \\
C_{BEV} &= 3{,}333.33 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 26{,}666.67/\text{yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual CO₂ emissions (iii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2}^{ICE} &= 1{,}333.33 \times 2.31 = 3{,}080 \text{ kg/yr} \\
m_{CO_2}^{BEV} &= 3{,}333.33 \times 0.72 = 2{,}400 \text{ kg/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total annual savings (iv):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Money saved} &= 1{,}40{,}000 - 26{,}666.67 = \text{₹ } 1{,}13{,}333.33/\text{yr} \\
\text{CO}_2 \text{ saved} &= 3{,}080 - 2{,}400 = 680 \text{ kg/yr} \;(0.68 \text{ t/yr})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**
(i) ICE annual fuel cost = **₹ 1,40,000/yr**
(ii) BEV annual electricity cost = **₹ 26,667/yr**
(iii) CO₂: ICE = **3,080 kg**, BEV = **2,400 kg**
(iv) Savings = **₹ 1,13,333/yr + 680 kg CO₂/yr**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in final numerical answers (kg, kg/yr, ₹, tonnes/yr) — **deduct 0.5–1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing tailpipe (TTW) emissions with Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** emissions — examiners specifically look for the distinction.
> 3. **Using incorrect grid emission factor** — always use **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh** unless the question explicitly states otherwise; this is the KTU 2024 expected value.
> 4. **Quoting PM2.5 values without units** — units are *mandatory* (µg/m³, not just "PM2.5").
> 5. **Omitting the policy linkage** — any answer on environmental importance must reference at least one Indian policy (FAME-II, BS-VI, PLI-ACC, or Paris Agreement/NDC).
> 6. **Numerical formatting** — write intermediate steps; do not jump from "given" to "final answer".

---

### 6. Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam Quick Recall (Read 5 minutes before entering the exam hall):**

* **CO₂ emission factors:** Gasoline = **2.31 kg/L** ; Diesel = **2.68 kg/L** ; Indian grid = **0.72 kg/kWh**.
* **Efficiency benchmarks:** ICE ≈ **25–35 %** ; Electric motor ≈ **85–92 %**.
* **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** = source × transmission × drivetrain — the master equation for any efficiency comparison.
* **Tailpipe emissions order (lowest to highest):** BEV < PHEV < HEV < ICE.
* **Three pillars of importance:** Environmental (climate, air, noise), Social (health, energy security, equity), Economic (TCO, jobs, innovation).
* **Key Indian policies to quote:** FAME-I (2015), FAME-II (2019), BS-VI (2020), PLI-ACC (2021), EV30@30 (NITI Aayog).
* **Numerical must-know formulas:** $m_{CO_2} = V_f \times EF$ and $\% \text{Reduction} = \frac{\Delta m}{m_{ICE}} \times 100$.
* **India facts:** ~85 % crude oil import dependency, ~$160 bn oil import bill, ~250 Mt CO₂/yr from transport.
* **Global targets:** Paris Agreement 1.5 °C ; UN SDG 3, 7, 11, 13 ; WHO PM2.5 guideline = 5 µg/m³.
* **Always mention the limitation of BEVs** — grid emission intensity (currently ~33 % savings only, not 100 %) — shows examiner your *maturity*.
* **Comparison matrix table** (Section 3.3) — memorize the trends, not exact numbers; examiners award partial credit for *correct ordering* even if numbers are approximate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EV Transmission - Configurations - Front Wheel Drive and Rear-Wheel Drive
Traction Motor Characteristics and Comparison with IC Engine Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Traction Motor Characteristics and Comparison with IC Engine Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Traction Motor Characteristics** refer to the operating behavior of an electric motor used for vehicle propulsion, primarily described by its **torque-speed (T-ω) curve**, **power-speed (P-ω) curve**, and **efficiency-speed (η-ω) curve**. For automotive traction, the ideal electric machine must deliver **high starting torque**, exhibit a **constant torque region** at low speeds, a **constant power region** over a broad mid-speed range, and a **natural falling characteristic** at very high speeds (field-weakening region).

**IC Engine Characteristics** describe the torque, power, and fuel-consumption behavior of an internal combustion engine, which produces **zero torque at zero speed (stall)**, requires a clutch to start from rest, has a relatively **narrow useful speed band**, and demands a **multi-speed transmission (gearbox)** to match the load requirements of a vehicle throughout its operating envelope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The core message of this module is establishing *why* an electric motor's inherent torque-speed curve is fundamentally superior to an IC engine's curve for traction duty — this is the engineering justification that drives the entire EV/HEV revolution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **weightlifter (Electric Motor)** versus a **marathon runner (IC Engine)** being asked to move a loaded cart from rest up a steep hill and then cruise on a flat highway at high speed.

- The **weightlifter** can instantly exert enormous force from a dead stop (high starting torque), can sustain heavy pulls at slow walking speed (constant torque region), can also jog while still pushing significant weight (constant power region), and can sprint without much load (field-weakening region). He needs **no clutch, no gearbox** — just one smooth, continuous effort.
- The **marathon runner**, however, cannot push at all from a standstill (stalls). Someone must first push the cart a bit (clutch engagement) before he can start running. He can run fast on flat ground but cannot produce the huge force needed for the hill — so you must give him **multiple gears** so he can change his leg-cadence to match the slope.

That, in essence, is the comparison: an electric motor is a "born tractive-effort machine," whereas an IC engine is a "constant-speed prime-mover" that must be *engineered around* with clutches and gearboxes to behave like a traction device.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics

- **Peak Power (P_peak):** in **kW** or **hp (1 hp = 0.746 kW)**
- **Peak Torque (T_max):** in **N·m**
- **Base Speed (ω_b):** the speed at which constant-power operation begins, in **rad/s**
- **Maximum Speed (ω_max):** in **rad/s**, typically 2× to 4× base speed
- **Constant-Power Speed Ratio (CPSR):** $\text{CPSR} = \omega_{max}/\omega_b$, ideally **> 3**
- **Vehicle Mass (m):** in **kg**
- **Wheel Radius (r):** in **m**

> [!NOTE]
> The **CPSR (Constant Power Speed Ratio)** is one of the most frequently tested metrics in KTU examinations for this topic. A higher CPSR means a smaller/cheaper transmission is required.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ideal Traction Motor Characteristics (Step-by-Step Logic)

The *ideal* torque-speed curve for a road-vehicle traction motor has three distinct zones:

- **Zone 1 — Constant-Torque Region (0 to ω_b):**
  - The motor delivers **maximum torque (T_max)** from zero speed up to the **base speed**.
  - Used for **launch, hill-climb, and low-speed acceleration**.
  - Power rises linearly with speed: $P = T_{max} \cdot \omega$.
  - This is naturally achieved in electric machines because current (and hence torque) is limited by the inverter's current limit, not by speed.

- **Zone 2 — Constant-Power Region (ω_b to ω_max):**
  - The motor maintains **rated peak power (P_peak)** by allowing torque to *fall inversely* with speed: $T = P_{peak}/\omega$.
  - Used for **high-speed cruising, merging onto highways, and overtaking**.
  - Achieved in electric machines by **field-weakening** (reducing the flux linkage $\psi$) so that back-EMF stays within the DC-bus voltage limit.

- **Zone 3 — Natural Drop-Off (above ω_max):**
  - Beyond maximum speed, both torque and power collapse as the inverter reaches its **voltage ceiling** and cannot sustain the current needed.
  - This is a *protective* zone — operation here is usually restricted to brief bursts.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Zone

- **Why constant torque at low speed?** Because tractive effort at launch is the *product* of motor torque and gear ratio. To move a 1500–2500 kg vehicle from rest, you need 250–400 N·m at the wheels. Since modern EV single-speed ratios are ~8:1 to ~10:1, the motor must deliver 25–50 N·m at standstill. Electric machines achieve this *passively* — there is no clutch, no stall, no need for fuel/air mixing.
- **Why constant power at high speed?** Aerodynamic drag rises with the *cube* of speed ($F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$) and rolling resistance rises linearly. To keep accelerating against $v^2$-type drag, the required power grows roughly with $v^3$ at the wheels — but the motor can hold its **electrical power limit** constant.
- **Why field-weakening?** In a PMSM or induction motor, the rotating magnetic field induces a back-EMF $E = k \psi \omega$. The inverter's DC-bus voltage $V_{dc}$ caps the line-to-line voltage. To spin faster, $\psi$ must be reduced — this is the **field-weakening** regime, and it directly produces the constant-power behavior.

### 2.3 IC Engine Characteristics — Why They Need a Gearbox

An IC engine exhibits the following inherent behaviors:

- **Zero torque at zero speed** — it cannot start from rest under load. A clutch or torque converter is mandatory.
- **Torque curve is "peaky"** — usable torque is available only over a narrow band, typically **1200–5000 rpm** for a passenger-car gasoline engine.
- **Power curve has a single peak** — outside that narrow band, power falls off rapidly.
- **No regenerative capability** — when the driver lifts off, the engine is *still consuming fuel* (or is decoupled, but never *recovers* energy).
- **Efficiency peaks only in a small operating island** (typically 25–35 % brake thermal efficiency for gasoline, 35–45 % for modern diesel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The IC engine's inability to deliver torque at low speed is the *single most important* reason every conventional car has a multi-speed transmission. The electric motor, by contrast, needs at most a **single-speed reduction gear** because its curve is naturally suited to traction.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Quantity | Symbolic Form | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Motor shaft power | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | W (or kW) | Converts torque to power at any speed |
| 2 | Torque from power | $T = P / \omega$ | N·m | Inverts the power–torque relationship |
| 3 | Wheel tractive force | $F_t = T_{wheel}/r_{wheel}$ | N | Force at tire contact patch |
| 4 | Wheel power | $P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v$ | W | Power required at road load |
| 5 | Tractive effort (accel + grade + drag + RR) | $F_t = m a + m g \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + m g C_{rr}\cos\theta$ | N | Longitudinal vehicle dynamics |
| 6 | Aerodynamic drag power | $P_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3$ | W | Quantifies why high-speed needs so much power |
| 7 | Rolling resistance | $F_{rr} = m g C_{rr}$ | N | Dominant at low speeds |
| 8 | Gear ratio (single-speed EV) | $i_g = \omega_m / \omega_w$ | dimensionless | Motor-to-wheel speed reduction |
| 9 | Tractive torque at wheels | $T_w = T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g$ | N·m | Includes gearbox efficiency |
| 10 | CPSR | $\text{CPSR} = \omega_{max}/\omega_b$ | dimensionless | Quality index of a traction motor's field-weakening range |
| 11 | Back-EMF | $E = k \psi \omega$ | V | Explains field-weakening requirement |
| 12 | Motor constant | $k_T = T/I$ | N·m/A | Relates torque to armature current |
| 13 | Energy stored in battery | $E_{bat} = V_{oc} \cdot Q$ (or $V \cdot Ah$) | Wh | Battery capacity for range calculation |
| 14 | Vehicle kinetic energy | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | Energy required for acceleration |
| 15 | Braking regen power limit | $P_{regen,max} = T_{max} \cdot \omega$ | W | Max energy recovered during deceleration |

> [!NOTE]
> In markdown tables, never use the literal `|` character inside cell content (it breaks the table). Use `\vert` or `\mid` for mathematical absolute-value or set-builder notation. Example: $\vert T_{max} \vert$ is written as `\vert T_{max} \vert` in LaTeX.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

These relationships govern every EV, HEV, and fuel-cell vehicle on the road. For instance, the **Tesla Model 3** uses a single-speed reduction gear of approximately **9:1** because its PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor) achieves a CPSR of roughly **4–5**. The **Toyota Prius** uses a **power-split planetary (CVT) transmission** specifically because its Atkinson-cycle engine and two motor-generators have *complementary* characteristics — the engine operates only in its narrow high-efficiency band while the motors handle the rest.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tractive Effort Required to Launch a Vehicle

We want to find the **torque required at the motor** to launch a vehicle of mass $m$ up a slope of angle $\theta$ with acceleration $a$, given a single-speed gearbox ratio $i_g$, gear efficiency $\eta_g$, and wheel radius $r_w$.

**Step 1 — Total tractive force at the tire contact patch:**
The vehicle must overcome four loads simultaneously: (i) linear acceleration $ma$, (ii) gravitational component along the slope $mg\sin\theta$, (iii) aerodynamic drag $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$, and (iv) rolling resistance on the slope $mg C_{rr}\cos\theta$.

$$
F_t = m a + m g \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g C_{rr} \cos\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Torque at the wheel required to produce this force:**
Force at the road equals wheel torque divided by wheel radius. Therefore:

$$
T_w = F_t \cdot r_w
$$

**Step 3 — Torque at the motor shaft (after the gearbox):**
The gearbox reduces (or multiplies) the speed by $i_g$ and amplifies torque by the same factor, reduced by the gear efficiency $\eta_g$. The motor torque is therefore:

$$
T_m = \frac{T_w}{i_g \cdot \eta_g}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine all steps into a single expression:**

$$
T_m = \frac{r_w}{i_g \cdot \eta_g}\left[ m a + m g \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g C_{rr} \cos\theta \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** This equation tells the motor designer the *minimum continuous torque rating* the machine must deliver. For a typical $m = 1500$ kg, $a = 0.3g$, $r_w = 0.33$ m, $i_g = 9$, $\eta_g = 0.97$, we get $T_m \approx 175$ N·m — well within the 250–400 N·m range of typical EV traction motors.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Constant-Power Speed Ratio (CPSR) From First Principles

**Step 1 — In the constant-torque region (below base speed):**
The motor delivers its full-rated current $I_{max}$, and the flux is held at its rated value $\psi_r$. Torque is:

$$
T_{max} = \tfrac{3}{2} \, p \, \psi_r \, I_{max} \quad \text{(for a 3-phase PMSM)}
$$

Power grows linearly with speed: $P = T_{max} \cdot \omega$.

**Step 2 — At base speed $\omega_b$:**
The back-EMF $E_b = k \psi_r \omega_b$ reaches the inverter voltage limit $V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}$. To spin faster, the controller must reduce the flux (field-weakening), keeping the back-EMF equal to the available voltage.

**Step 3 — In the field-weakening region (constant power):**
The flux is reduced inversely with speed, $\psi = \psi_r \cdot \omega_b / \omega$, so that $E \approx$ constant. Torque becomes:

$$
T = \frac{P_{peak}}{\omega} = \frac{T_{max} \cdot \omega_b}{\omega}
$$

**Step 4 — Define CPSR:**

$$
\text{CPSR} = \frac{\omega_{max}}{\omega_b} = \frac{P_{peak}}{T_{max} \cdot \omega_{min}^{cogging}}
$$

In practice, CPSR is simply measured as $\omega_{max}/\omega_b$ at the points where the constant-torque line meets the constant-power envelope on the T-ω curve.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Why the IC Engine Loses to the Electric Motor on Tractive Effort

**Step 1 — IC engine usable torque band:**
A typical gasoline engine produces ≥ 80 % of its peak torque only between **~2000 rpm and ~5000 rpm**, and *zero* torque below ~800 rpm (idle speed).

**Step 2 — Electric motor usable torque band:**
A PMSM delivers rated torque from **0 rpm to base speed** (often 4000–6000 rpm) and constant power to 12,000–18,000 rpm.

**Step 3 — The "missed area" argument:**
For a road vehicle, the motor's torque curve is overlaid on a road-load curve (force vs. speed). The usable operating region is the area *under* the torque curve *above* the road-load curve.

For the IC engine, almost the *entire* low-speed region (0 to ~1500 rpm) is **wasted** — the curve does not exist there. A multi-speed transmission is required to shift the operating point into the engine's narrow band at every vehicle speed.

For the electric motor, the curve covers *every* vehicle speed from 0 to max — so a single-speed gearbox is sufficient.

**Step 4 — Mathematical expression of efficiency loss:**
If a 5-speed gearbox is used and the average transmission efficiency is $\eta_g \approx 0.92$, total drivetrain efficiency is $0.92^5 \approx 0.66$. An EV's single-speed drivetrain with $\eta_g \approx 0.97$ gives $0.97^1 = 0.97$ — a ~31 percentage-point advantage in mechanical efficiency alone, *before* the electrical-efficiency advantage is even counted.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Motor-Type vs. Characteristic

| Motor Type | Starting Torque | CPSR | Peak Efficiency | Notes for Traction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DC Series | Very High (2–3× rated) | ~2 | 85–90 % | Classic EV motor (older EVs); commutation limits speed |
| Separately Excited DC | High, controllable | ~2–3 | 85–90 % | Field current allows weak field; used in some HEVs |
| Induction Motor (3-phase) | High (2–3×) | 3–4 | 92–95 % | Tesla Model S, most industrial EVs |
| PMSM | High (2×) | 3–5 | 95–97 % | Best efficiency, dominant in modern BEVs |
| Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) | Very High | 4–6 | 90–93 % | High CPSR, robust; Renault Fluence Z.E. |
| BLDC | High (1.5–2×) | 2–3 | 93–96 % | Two-wheeler EVs (e.g., Ola, Ather) |

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example

**Given:** Vehicle mass $m = 1200$ kg, $C_d A = 0.65$ m², $C_{rr} = 0.012$, wheel radius $r_w = 0.31$ m, gear ratio $i_g = 8.5$, $\eta_g = 0.96$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Find:** Motor torque required to cruise at $v = 90$ km/h = 25 m/s on a level road.

**Step 1 — Compute each load term:**
Acceleration $a = 0$ (steady cruise). Slope $\theta = 0$.

**Step 2 — Aerodynamic drag:**

$$
F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.65 \cdot 25^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.65 \cdot 625
$$

$$
F_d = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.65 \cdot 625 = 248.78 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Rolling resistance:**

$$
F_{rr} = 1200 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 = 141.26 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Total tractive force:**

$$
F_t = 0 + 0 + 248.78 + 141.26 = 390.04 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Torque at the wheel:**

$$
T_w = F_t \cdot r_w = 390.04 \cdot 0.31 = 120.91 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 6 — Torque at the motor shaft:**

$$
T_m = \frac{T_w}{i_g \cdot \eta_g} = \frac{120.91}{8.5 \cdot 0.96} = \frac{120.91}{8.16} = 14.82 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 7 — Required motor power at 25 m/s:**

$$
P = F_t \cdot v = 390.04 \cdot 25 = 9751 \text{ W} \approx 9.75 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 8 — Equivalent motor speed:**

$$
\omega_m = (v/r_w) \cdot i_g = (25/0.31) \cdot 8.5 = 80.65 \cdot 8.5 = 685.5 \text{ rad/s} \approx 6545 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Verification:** $P = T_m \cdot \omega_m = 14.82 \cdot 685.5 = 10\,159$ W. The ~4 % discrepancy is due to rounding in $T_m$ — the calculation is internally consistent.

> [!NOTE]
> This worked example shows that even a small passenger EV needs only **~15 N·m** at the motor to cruise on a flat highway at 90 km/h. The motor's *real* design constraint is the *peak* torque for 0–100 km/h acceleration (typically 250–400 N·m) — cruising torque is almost trivial.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Traction Motor's Operating Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Demand: Accelerator Pedal] --> B[VCM Vehicle Control Module]
    B --> C{MCU Motor Control Unit}
    C --> D{Inverter DC to AC at Variable Frequency and Voltage}
    D --> E[Traction Motor PMSM or Induction]
    E --> F[Single Speed Reduction Gearbox]
    F --> G[Half Shafts]
    G --> H[Wheels and Tires]
    E --> E1[Zone 1: 0 to base speed, Constant Torque, Current Limited]
    E --> E2[Zone 2: base speed to max speed, Constant Power, Field Weakened]
    E --> E3[Zone 3: above max speed, Both Torque and Power Falling]
    E1 --> I[Torque Output rises with current, Inverter in PWM saturation]
    E2 --> J[Flux reduced, Back EMF held at Voltage Ceiling]
    E3 --> K[Voltage and Current both limited, Protective Region]
    I --> L[Tractive Effort at Wheel]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Longitudinal Vehicle Dynamics: Acceleration, Hill Climb, Cruise]
    E -.-> N[Regenerative Braking: Motor acts as Generator, Current reversed]
    N --> O[DC Bus and Battery Pack Charging]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Side-by-Side Comparison: IC Engine vs Electric Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IC[IC Engine Characteristics]
        I1[Zero torque at zero speed, requires clutch] --> I2[Narrow peaky torque band, 2000 to 5000 rpm]
        I2 --> I3[Power curve has a single peak]
```
