```mermaid
        I3 --> I4[Requires 5 to 7 speed gearbox and clutch]
        I4 --> I5[No regenerative braking capability]
        I5 --> I6[Thermal efficiency 25 to 40 percent]
    end
    subgraph EM[Electric Motor Characteristics]
        E1[High starting torque from zero rpm] --> E2[Constant torque up to base speed]
        E2 --> E3[Constant power up to max speed via field weakening]
        E3 --> E4[Single speed reduction gear typically sufficient]
        E4 --> E5[Regenerative braking built in, current reversed]
        E5 --> E6[Motor efficiency 90 to 97 percent]
    end
    IC --- Outcome1[Conventional Powertrain: Complex, Heavy, Less Efficient]
    EM --- Outcome2[Electric Powertrain: Simple, Light, Highly Efficient]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Decision Logic for Motor Selection in an EV/HEV

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Vehicle Class and Mission Profile] --> Q1{Is it a 2 wheeler or small 3 wheeler}
    Q1 -- Yes --> M1[BLDC or PMSM hub motor, air cooled, 1 to 5 kW]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is it a passenger car or SUV}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is range or efficiency the priority}
    Q3 -- Range --> M2[PMSM, oil cooled, 100 to 250 kW, single speed]
    Q3 -- Cost --> M3[Induction motor, 100 to 200 kW, single speed]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Is it a heavy truck or bus}
    Q4 -- Yes --> M4[Multiple PMSM or induction units, 200 to 500 kW, 2 or 3 speed gearbox]
    M1 --> End[Selected Traction Motor]
    M2 --> End
    M3 --> End
    M4 --> End
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three diagrams above together map the **functional architecture**, the **comparative characteristic behavior**, and the **design selection logic** for traction motors. These are the kinds of high-level flow illustrations KTU examiners expect to see in 14-mark answers for Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
*Why does an electric traction motor not require a multi-speed transmission in most electric vehicles, while an IC engine does?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An electric motor produces **high torque even at zero speed** and can maintain **constant power over a wide speed range** (typically 3–5 times the base speed) using field-weakening. Its torque–speed curve naturally covers the entire operating envelope of a road vehicle from launch to top speed. In contrast, an IC engine produces **zero torque at zero speed**, has a **narrow peaky torque band** between ~2000–5000 rpm, and **cannot start from rest under load**. To match the road-load demand at all vehicle speeds, a multi-speed transmission (and clutch) is required to shift the engine's operating point into its useful band. Hence, the electric motor needs at most a single-speed reducer, while the IC engine needs 5–7 gears. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*Define Constant-Power Speed Ratio (CPSR) and state its typical value for a PMSM traction motor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**CPSR** is the ratio of the **maximum speed of the motor to its base speed**, i.e. $\text{CPSR} = \omega_{max}/\omega_b$. It quantifies the **width of the constant-power region** in the motor's torque–speed curve. A higher CPSR means the motor can deliver rated power over a wider speed range, reducing or eliminating the need for a multi-speed gearbox. For a typical PMSM traction motor, the CPSR is **3 to 5**; for a switched reluctance motor, it can reach **4 to 6**; for a DC series motor, it is only about **2**. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]*

**Q. (a)** With the help of a torque–speed characteristic diagram, explain the **ideal traction motor characteristics** required for an electric vehicle. Clearly mark the **constant-torque region**, the **constant-power region**, and the **natural drop-off region**, and explain the physical reasoning behind each region. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Constant-Torque Region (0 to base speed ω_b):** The motor delivers its **maximum rated torque (T_max)** from standstill up to ω_b. This is achieved by **limiting the stator current** to its maximum value (set by the inverter's current rating). Power rises linearly: $P = T_{max} \cdot \omega$. This region is essential for **launching the vehicle, hill-climbing, and low-speed acceleration**, where the tractive effort requirement is high. Electric machines naturally exhibit this behavior because torque is directly proportional to current ($T = k_T I$). **(2.5 marks)**

**2. Constant-Power Region (ω_b to ω_max):** Beyond ω_b, the back-EMF $E = k\psi\omega$ would exceed the inverter's voltage ceiling. The controller therefore **reduces the flux linkage ψ** (field-weakening), which keeps the back-EMF at the voltage limit while allowing speed to rise. Torque falls inversely with speed: $T = P_{peak}/\omega$, so power remains **constant at the rated peak value P_peak**. This region is used for **high-speed cruising, merging, and overtaking**. **(2.5 marks)**

**3. Natural Drop-Off Region (above ω_max):** Beyond ω_max, both the **available voltage and the available current** are at their limits, and no further field-weakening is possible. Torque and power both fall off rapidly. This is a **protective region** — the motor may briefly operate here but cannot sustain continuous power. **(1 mark)**

**Physical reasoning summary:** Electric machines can transition from constant-torque to constant-power control *electronically* — by changing current and flux — without any mechanical gear change. This is the fundamental reason why electric traction needs at most a single-speed gearbox. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b)** A passenger electric vehicle has the following data: total mass $m = 1400$ kg, rolling resistance coefficient $C_{rr} = 0.011$, drag coefficient times frontal area $C_d A = 0.7$ m², wheel radius $r_w = 0.32$ m, single-speed gear ratio $i_g = 9.2$, gear efficiency $\eta_g = 0.97$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **Calculate: (i) the tractive force required to maintain a steady 100 km/h on a level road, (ii) the torque required at the motor shaft, and (iii) the motor power at cruising speed.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $v = 100$ km/h $= 27.78$ m/s, $a = 0$, $\theta = 0$.

**Step (i) — Tractive force:**

$$
F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 27.78^2
$$

$$
F_d = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 771.7 = 330.92 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{rr} = m g C_{rr} = 1400 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.011 = 151.07 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_t = F_d + F_{rr} = 330.92 + 151.07 = 481.99 \text{ N} \approx 482 \text{ N}
$$

**(2 marks for this part)**

**Step (ii) — Motor torque:**

$$
T_m = \frac{F_t \cdot r_w}{i_g \cdot \eta_g} = \frac{481.99 \cdot 0.32}{9.2 \cdot 0.97} = \frac{154.24}{8.924} = 17.28 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(2.5 marks for this part)**

**Step (iii) — Motor power:**

$$
P = F_t \cdot v = 481.99 \cdot 27.78 = 13\,390 \text{ W} \approx 13.4 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\text{Verification: } P = T_m \cdot \omega_m, \quad \omega_m = (v/r_w) \cdot i_g = 86.81 \cdot 9.2 = 798.6 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
P = 17.28 \cdot 798.6 = 13\,800 \text{ W} \approx 13.8 \text{ kW} \quad (\text{rounding-consistent})
$$

**(2.5 marks for this part)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]*

**Q. (a)** Compare in detail the **torque-speed characteristics, power characteristics, and operational behavior of an IC engine and an electric traction motor**. Use a comparative table and explain why the electric motor is inherently more suitable for vehicle traction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | IC Engine | Electric Traction Motor |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Torque at zero speed | **Zero** (cannot start under load) | **Maximum rated torque** (high) |
| 2 | Usable speed band | Narrow, ~2000–5000 rpm | Wide, 0 to ~12,000–18,000 rpm |
| 3 | Torque curve shape | Peaky, with a clear maximum | Flat plateau then inverse-fall |
| 4 | Power curve | Single peak, narrow band | Flat plateau (constant power region) |
| 5 | Transmission required | Multi-speed (5–7 gears) + clutch | Single-speed reducer (or none) |
| 6 | Starting device | Clutch / torque converter | None (direct start) |
| 7 | Efficiency (best) | 25–45 % thermal | 90–97 % electrical |
| 8 | Regenerative braking | Not possible | Inherent (reverses current) |
| 9 | Emissions at point of use | CO₂, NOₓ, PM | Zero tailpipe |
| 10 | Noise, vibration | High | Very low |
| 11 | Cooling demand | High (combustion heat) | Moderate (copper + iron losses) |
| 12 | Overload capability | ~1.1–1.3× rated | ~2–3× rated (for short bursts) |

**(4 marks for the table and core contrasts)**

**Why electric motor is more suitable for traction:** The IC engine's narrow torque band requires a complex mechanical transmission to keep the engine in its useful operating region at all vehicle speeds, incurring frictional and shifting losses. The electric motor's naturally broad operating envelope — high torque at zero speed plus a constant-power region from base to maximum speed — eliminates the need for a multi-speed gearbox, simplifies the drivetrain, allows regenerative energy recovery, and offers substantially higher efficiency. These four benefits (simplicity, efficiency, recovery, packaging) are the engineering rationale for the global shift to electric traction. **(3 marks)**

---

**(b)** A PMSM traction motor rated **75 kW peak** delivers **250 N·m peak torque**. Calculate the **base speed, the maximum speed for a CPSR of 4, and the constant-power region speed range.** If the vehicle is geared with $i_g = 8.5$ and wheel radius $r_w = 0.33$ m, determine the **maximum vehicle speed** corresponding to motor maximum speed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Base speed from peak torque and peak power:**

$$
P_{peak} = T_{max} \cdot \omega_b
$$

$$
\omega_b = \frac{P_{peak}}{T_{max}} = \frac{75\,000}{250} = 300 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Convert to rpm: $\omega_b = 300 \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = 2864.8$ rpm $\approx 2865$ rpm

**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 2 — Maximum speed from CPSR:**

$$
\text{CPSR} = \frac{\omega_{max}}{\omega_b} = 4
$$

$$
\omega_{max} = 4 \cdot 300 = 1200 \text{ rad/s} = 11\,459 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Constant-power speed range:**

$$
\text{Range: } 300 \text{ rad/s to } 1200 \text{ rad/s} \quad \text{or} \quad 2865 \text{ rpm to } 11\,459 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Wheel angular speed at motor max speed:**

$$
\omega_w = \frac{\omega_{max}}{i_g} = \frac{1200}{8.5} = 141.18 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Maximum vehicle speed:**

$$
v_{max} = \omega_w \cdot r_w = 141.18 \cdot 0.33 = 46.59 \text{ m/s}
$$

Convert to km/h: $v_{max} = 46.59 \cdot 3.6 = 167.7$ km/h

**(2 marks for the conversion and final answer)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this topic (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** km/h to m/s (multiply by 1/3.6), rpm to rad/s (multiply by $2\pi/60$). Examiners *will* deduct 1 mark for unit-mismatch errors even if the numerical answer is close.
> 2. **Omitting gear efficiency:** Many students write $T_m = T_w/i_g$ and forget the $\eta_g$ factor in the denominator. This costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing "torque at the wheel" with "torque at the motor"** — the question may ask for either. Read carefully.
> 4. **In the comparison question**, students often write a wall-of-text paragraph instead of a **tabular comparison**. A clean comparative table earns more marks faster.
> 5. **In CPSR calculations**, students forget to convert between rad/s and rpm. Always state the unit.
> 6. **In the IC engine vs motor question**, students often write only that the motor is "more efficient" without giving the *quantitative* reason (e.g., 90–97 % vs 25–45 %) and the *system-level* reason (no multi-speed transmission, regenerative braking).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Ideal traction motor T-ω curve:** constant-torque region (0 → ω_b) → constant-power region (ω_b → ω_max) → natural drop-off (above ω_max).
- **CPSR = ω_max / ω_b**; for PMSM ~3–5, for SRM ~4–6, for DC series ~2.
- **Electric motor delivers maximum torque at zero speed** — no clutch needed.
- **IC engine delivers zero torque at zero speed** — needs clutch + multi-speed gearbox.
- **IC engine usable band:** ~2000–5000 rpm, peaky.
- **EV traction motor usable band:** 0 to 12,000–18,000 rpm, broad.
- **Tractive force equation:** $F_t = ma + mg\sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + mg C_{rr}\cos\theta$.
- **Wheel torque:** $T_w = F_t \cdot r_w$.
- **Motor torque:** $T_m = T_w / (i_g \cdot \eta_g)$.
- **Motor power:** $P = T_m \cdot \omega_m = F_t \cdot v$.
- **EVs use a single-speed reducer;** conventional cars use 5–7 speeds.
- **PMSM is the dominant modern traction motor** for BEVs (high efficiency, high power density).
- **Induction motor** is the next most common (Tesla Model S/X legacy, lower cost).
- **Field-weakening** in the constant-power region is achieved by reducing flux $\psi$ so back-EMF stays within the inverter voltage limit.
- **Aerodynamic drag scales as $v^2$** (force) and $v^3$ (power) — high-speed cruising is power-hungry.
- **Rolling resistance scales linearly with mass** — low-speed load is mostly mass-driven.
- **Regenerative braking is unique to electric machines** — energy is recovered by operating the motor as a generator.
- **Gear efficiency stacks multiplicatively** in a multi-speed transmission; for a 5-speed gearbox at 92 % each, total is only ~66 %.
- **EV single-speed drivetrain efficiency** is ~97 % — a ~31 percentage-point mechanical advantage over an IC-engine car.
- **Unit conversion essentials:** $1$ km/h $= 1/3.6$ m/s; $1$ rpm $= 2\pi/60$ rad/s; $1$ hp $= 746$ W.
- **Memory aid — "MOTOR wins because: S.E.R.V."** — **S**tart-up torque, **E**fficiency, **R**egeneration, **V**ersatility (one gear).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Electric Vehicles - Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Electric Vehicles - Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)

A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is a vehicle that uses two or more distinct energy sources, typically an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and one or more **Electric Machines (EM)**, working together (or independently) to propel the vehicle, thereby improving fuel economy, reducing emissions, and enhancing overall powertrain efficiency relative to a conventional ICE-only vehicle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An HEV combines an ICE (using conventional fuel such as petrol/diesel) and an electric drivetrain (comprising an electric motor, power electronics, and an energy storage system such as a battery or ultracapacitor) to deliver vehicle propulsion. The key feature is **on-board energy regeneration** through regenerative braking and intelligent energy management.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking to a market **5 km away**, carrying a bag of groceries that is initially light but gets heavier on the way back. You have **two friends** to help:
- One friend is **strong but slow** (the **ICE** — powerful, but wasteful at low speeds and in city traffic).
- The other friend is **fast and quick** (the **Electric Motor** — efficient, quiet, ideal for short sprints and low-speed cruising).

A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle** is exactly like a clever person who calls upon the **strong friend** for long highway stretches where steady high-power output is needed, and the **quick friend** for stop-and-go city traffic, hill starts, and slow manoeuvres. The two friends share the load optimally, and the **quick friend can also charge the bag** (regenerative braking) on the way downhill — something a single strong friend cannot do as efficiently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:** The HEV is **not** simply an electric car with a backup engine, nor a regular car with a starter motor. It is a **fully integrated dual-powertrain system** that intelligently blends two prime movers to overcome the inherent efficiency weaknesses of each operating alone.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in HEV Design

The following performance and design metrics are the **standard benchmarks** used across the HEV industry and in KTU numerical problems:

- **Fuel Economy:** Measured in **km/litre** (Indian/European standard) or **mpg** (US standard). The CAFE (Corporate Average Fuel Economy) standard targets a fleet average of **≈ 4.7 L/100 km** for new cars in India by 2026.
- **Energy Storage Capacity:** Battery packs in HEVs typically range from **0.8 kWh to 2 kWh** (full hybrids); PHEVs use **4 to 18 kWh**.
- **Electric Machine Power:** Mild hybrids use **≈ 7–15 kW**; full hybrids use **≈ 30–120 kW**.
- **System Voltage:** Conventional HEVs operate at high voltage DC bus levels of **≈ 144 V to **650 V** (Toyota Prius = 201.6 V; Camry Hybrid = 245 V).
- **State of Charge (SoC) Window:** Maintained between **≈ 40 % and 80 %** to maximize battery cycle life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter for KTU:** Numerical problems in Module 1 frequently use SoC windows, voltage levels, and fuel-economy comparisons between a conventional and hybrid powertrain. Memorise the Toyota Prius architecture benchmarks as the **de-facto reference vehicle** in the syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Need Hybrid Electric Vehicles? — The Engineering Motivation

The conventional ICE vehicle suffers from two major design penalties:

1. **Very low efficiency at part-load and idle.** A petrol engine is only **≈ 30–35 %** efficient at its best operating point, and drops to **≈ 5–10 %** during idling or low-speed city driving.
2. **100 % of braking energy is wasted as heat** in the brake pads and rotors.

An HEV addresses both:

- The **electric motor** (efficiency **≈ 85–95 %**) takes over during idle and low-load operation, eliminating the inefficient ICE regime.
- **Regenerative braking** recovers a portion of the kinetic energy that would otherwise be dissipated as heat.

---

### 2.2 The Two Fundamental Energy Paths in an HEV

| Energy Path | Conventional Vehicle | Hybrid Electric Vehicle |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuel-to-Wheel (Tank-to-Wheel)** | Chemical → Thermal → Mechanical (≈ 20–35 %) | Chemical + Electrical → Mechanical (≈ 35–45 %) |
| **Kinetic-to-Heat (Braking)** | 100 % wasted | Partially recovered as electrical energy |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table is the **master equation reference** for HEV concept problems in the KTU ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DOH = \dfrac{P_{em}}{P_{em} + P_{ice}}$ | Degree of Hybridization | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{W_{out}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | Brake Thermal Efficiency | % |
| 3 | $E_{bat} = V_{bus} \cdot I_{bat} \cdot t$ | Battery Energy Stored | Wh or kWh |
| 4 | $P_{regen} = \tau_{em} \cdot \omega_{em} \cdot \eta_{em}$ | Regenerative Braking Power | W or kW |
| 5 | $FC_{new} = FC_{old} \cdot \left(1 - f_{sav}\right)$ | New Fuel Consumption | L/100 km |
| 6 | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 + \dfrac{1}{E_{cap}}\int_{0}^{t} V \cdot I \cdot dt$ | State of Charge | % |
| 7 | $\eta_{tank\text{-}to\text{-}wheel} = \eta_{engine} \cdot \eta_{transmission} \cdot \eta_{final\text{-}drive}$ | Drivetrain Efficiency | % |
| 8 | $E_{kinetic} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Vehicle Kinetic Energy | J |
| 9 | $P_{trac} = F_{trac} \cdot v = (F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} + F_{accel}) \cdot v$ | Tractive Power Demand | W or kW |
| 10 | $F_{rr} = m g C_{rr} \cos\theta$ | Rolling Resistance | N |
| 11 | $F_{aero} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A v^{2}$ | Aerodynamic Drag | N |
| 12 | $F_{grade} = m g \sin\theta$ | Grade Resistance | N |
| 13 | $F_{accel} = m a$ | Acceleration Resistance | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Note the **DOH formula** carefully. A value of **0** corresponds to a pure ICE vehicle, **1** corresponds to a pure EV, and intermediate values classify the level of hybridization (micro/mild/full). This single equation is worth **3 to 7 marks** in KTU exams.

---

### 2.4 Degree of Hybridization (DOH) — Classification Rule

The Degree of Hybridization is the single most important **classification parameter** for an HEV:

$$DOH = \frac{P_{em}}{P_{em} + P_{ice}}$$

- If $DOH \leq 0.05$ → **Micro Hybrid** (e.g., auto start-stop, e.g., Mahindra Scorpio Micro-Hybrid)
- If $0.05 < DOH \leq 0.25$ → **Mild Hybrid** (e.g., Maruti Suzuki Ciaz SHVS, Honda City i-MMD at low assist)
- If $0.25 < DOH < 1.0$ → **Full Hybrid** (e.g., Toyota Camry Hybrid, Toyota Prius)
- If $DOH \approx 1.0$ and grid-chargeable → **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV)** (e.g., Toyota Prius Prime, MG Hector Plus)
- If $DOH = 1.0$ → **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)** — not technically an HEV

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The HEV concept is the **dominant transitional technology** between pure ICE and pure BEV powertrains. From 2025–2035, global automotive OEMs (Toyota, Hyundai, Tata, BYD) are using HEV/PHEV architectures as a **bridge technology** to comply with BS-VII and Euro-7 emission norms. In India, the **CAFE-II norms** and the **FAME-II subsidies** actively push manufacturers toward hybridization and electrification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fuel Economy Improvement of a Hybrid Over a Conventional Vehicle

Let us derive the **percentage improvement in fuel economy** when a regenerative braking system is added to a conventional vehicle. This is a **standard KTU Module 1 numerical problem**.

---

**Given Data:**

- Mass of vehicle: $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$
- Initial speed: $v_i = 25 \text{ m/s}$ (≈ 90 km/h)
- Final speed: $v_f = 0 \text{ m/s}$ (full stop)
- Conventional brake thermal efficiency (waste heat): $\eta_{brake} = 100\text{ %}$ (all kinetic energy → heat)
- HEV regenerative + friction split: **60 %** regenerative, 40 % friction
- Electric motor + charging efficiency: $\eta_{em} = 0.90$
- Fuel Lower Heating Value (LHV) of petrol: $LHV = 44 \text{ MJ/kg}$
- Density of petrol: $\rho_f = 0.74 \text{ kg/L}$
- Engine efficiency at part load: $\eta_{eng} = 0.20$

---

**Step 1: Calculate the kinetic energy dissipated during braking.**

$$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (25)^{2}$$

Expanding the square:
$$v^{2} = 25 \times 25 = 625 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

Substituting:
$$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times 625 = 750 \times 625$$

$$E_{kinetic} = 468{,}750 \text{ J} = 468.75 \text{ kJ}$$

> *Textual interpretation:* The vehicle has 468.75 kJ of kinetic energy just before braking begins. In a conventional car, **all of it becomes waste heat** in the brake discs.

---

**Step 2: Calculate the energy recovered in the HEV.**

$$E_{recovered} = f_{regen} \cdot \eta_{em} \cdot E_{kinetic}$$

$$E_{recovered} = 0.60 \times 0.90 \times 468{,}750$$

$$E_{recovered} = 0.54 \times 468{,}750 = 253{,}125 \text{ J} = 253.125 \text{ kJ}$$

> *Textual interpretation:* The regenerative system converts 60 % of the kinetic energy into electrical energy, but loses 10 % to motor and conversion losses, giving **253.125 kJ of useful electrical energy** that can be stored in the battery.

---

**Step 3: Convert the recovered electrical energy back to equivalent fuel mass.**

The energy content of petrol fuel that would have produced 253.125 kJ at 20 % engine efficiency:

$$Q_{fuel} = \frac{E_{recovered}}{\eta_{eng}} = \frac{253{,}125}{0.20} = 1{,}265{,}625 \text{ J} = 1265.625 \text{ kJ}$$

Mass of fuel equivalent:
$$m_{fuel} = \frac{Q_{fuel}}{LHV} = \frac{1{,}265{,}625}{44 \times 10^{6}} \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{fuel} = 2.8764 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg} = 28.764 \text{ g}$$

Volume of fuel equivalent:
$$V_{fuel} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{\rho_f} = \frac{28.764}{0.74} = 38.87 \text{ mL} = 0.03887 \text{ L}$$

> *Textual interpretation:* A single aggressive stop from 90 km/h saves the equivalent of about **39 mL of petrol**. Over a city drive with **200 such braking events**, this becomes **≈ 7.77 litres saved per 100 km** in purely theoretical regenerative conditions.

---

**Step 4: Express the percentage fuel savings for one full stop.**

The fuel that *would have been burnt* to deliver the kinetic energy (at 20 % efficiency) is:

$$Q_{used} = \frac{E_{kinetic}}{\eta_{eng}} = \frac{468{,}750}{0.20} = 2{,}343{,}750 \text{ J}$$

Fuel mass equivalent:
$$m_{used} = \frac{2{,}343{,}750}{44 \times 10^{6}} = 0.05327 \text{ kg} = 53.27 \text{ g}$$

Volume:
$$V_{used} = \frac{53.27}{0.74} = 71.99 \text{ mL}$$

Percentage saving per stop:
$$\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{V_{fuel}}{V_{used}} \times 100 = \frac{38.87}{71.99} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Saving} = 53.99 \text{ %}$$

> *Textual interpretation:* A single braking event in this idealized HEV scenario recovers **≈ 54 %** of the fuel-equivalent energy that was originally burnt to accelerate the car. Real-world recovery is 10–25 % due to drivetrain losses, battery limits, and friction brake blending.

---

**Step 5: Final consolidated result.**

$$\boxed{\text{Fuel saved per stop} \approx 39 \text{ mL}, \quad \text{Equivalent improvement} \approx 54 \text{ %}}$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: ICE vs. HEV Energy Flow

The following matrix compares the energy flow at three critical operating points:

| Operating Mode | Conventional ICE | HEV (with Regen + EM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Idling at traffic light** | Burns fuel: ≈ 0.5 L/hr continuously | ICE stops; EM off. Zero fuel consumption. |
| **Hard braking (90 → 0 km/h)** | 100 % KE → brake heat (wasted) | ≈ 54 % KE → battery; 46 % → friction heat |
| **Low-speed city cruising (20 km/h)** | ICE runs at 8 % efficiency | EM runs at 90 % efficiency; ICE off |
| **Highway cruising (80 km/h)** | ICE runs at 32 % efficiency | ICE runs at 32–35 %; EM assists only on inclines |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Hybrid Electric Vehicle

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional interaction between the major HEV sub-systems**. This is the standard figure KTU expects students to draw in 7-mark and 14-mark descriptive questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Tank\nConventional Fuel] --> B[Internal Combustion Engine\nICE]
    C[Battery Pack\nHigh Voltage Energy Storage] --> D[Power Electronics\nInverter or Converter]
    B --> E[Transmission\nGearbox and Clutch]
    D --> F[Electric Motor\nand Generator Unit]
    E --> G[Final Drive\nDifferential and Axles]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Road Wheels\nVehicle Propulsion]
    F -.->|Regenerative Braking| C
    J[Vehicle Control Unit\nVCU or EMS] --> B
    J --> D
    J -.->|Energy Management| C
    I[Driver Input\nAccelerator and Brake Pedal] --> J
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** indicate power flow (mechanical or electrical).
- **Dotted arrows** indicate control signals and bidirectional energy flow (regen).
- The **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** is the brain that decides, in real-time, *how much* of the propulsion demand each prime mover (ICE or EM) should supply.

---

### 4.2 Classification Flowchart of Hybrid Powertrains

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hybrid Electric Vehicle HEV] --> B{Degree of\nHybridization}
    B -->|DOH less than 0.05| C[Micro Hybrid\nStart Stop Only]
    B -->|DOH 0.05 to 0.25| D[Mild Hybrid\n48V Belt Starter]
    B -->|DOH 0.25 to 1.0| E[Full Hybrid\nSeries or Parallel]
    B -->|DOH equals 1.0\nand Grid Charge| F[Plug In Hybrid\nPHEV]
    E --> G{Architecture Type}
    G -->|Single Path\nEM on Driveline| H[Series Hybrid]
    G -->|Dual Path\nICE and EM on Wheels| I[Parallel Hybrid]
    G -->|Power Split Device| J[Series Parallel Hybrid]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The architecture classification (**Series / Parallel / Series-Parallel**) is a **favourite 7-mark question**. Memorise the **Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS)** as the textbook example of a **Series-Parallel (Power-Split)** architecture.

---

### 4.3 Power Flow Schematic in Series vs. Parallel HEV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Series
    S1[ICE] --> S2[Generator]
    S2 --> S3[Battery]
    S3 --> S4[Power Inverter]
    S1 -.->|Mechanical Lost| SX[Not Connected to Wheels]
    S4 --> S5[Electric Motor]
    S5 --> SW[Drive Wheels]
    end

    subgraph Parallel
    P1[ICE] --> PT[Transmission]
    P2[Electric Motor] --> PT
    PT --> PW[Drive Wheels]
    PB[Battery] --> PI[Inverter]
    PI --> P2
    P2 -.->|Regen| PB
    end
```

**Series HEV** — the ICE drives only a generator; the wheels are driven **exclusively** by the electric motor. Think of it as a "range-extended EV" (e.g., Chevrolet Volt in series mode, BMW i3 REx).

**Parallel HEV** — both the ICE and the electric motor can deliver mechanical power to the wheels through a common transmission (e.g., Honda Civic Hybrid IMA, Hyundai Sonata Hybrid).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV). Differentiate between a micro-hybrid and a full-hybrid vehicle with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is a road vehicle that combines a conventional internal combustion engine (ICE) with an electric drivetrain (electric motor + battery + power electronics) for propulsion, in order to improve fuel efficiency and reduce emissions.

| Feature | Micro Hybrid | Full Hybrid |
|---|---|---|
| **DOH range** | $\leq 0.05$ | $0.25 < DOH < 1.0$ |
| **Electric drive capability** | None (only start-stop) | Pure electric drive possible at low speed |
| **Battery voltage** | 12 V | 144 V to 650 V |
| **Regenerative braking** | Limited / none | Yes, full |
| **Example** | Maruti Suzuki Alto K10 (idle start-stop) | Toyota Camry Hybrid, Toyota Prius |

**(3 marks – 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for comparison table)**

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]
**Explain the concept of regenerative braking. Why is it considered a key advantage of HEVs over conventional vehicles?**

**Model Answer:**

**Regenerative braking** is the process of converting the vehicle's kinetic energy during deceleration into electrical energy using the electric machine acting as a generator, and storing it in the battery. In a conventional vehicle, this kinetic energy is dissipated entirely as heat in the brake pads, making it an irreversible loss.

**Key advantages:**
1. Recovers up to **30–60 %** of braking energy (depending on drive cycle).
2. Reduces brake wear → lower maintenance cost.
3. Improves urban fuel economy by **15–25 %**.
4. Lowers tailpipe emissions (because less fuel is burnt to replace the recovered energy).

**(3 marks – 1.5 for concept + 1.5 for advantages)**

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q1A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a typical Hybrid Electric Vehicle. Identify and briefly describe the function of any **five (5)** major components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hybrid electric bus has the following specifications:
- Mass $m = 12{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- Maximum speed $v = 25 \text{ m/s}$
- Brake split: **55 %** regenerative, **45 %** friction
- Electric motor efficiency during regen: $\eta_{em} = 0.88$
- Battery pack capacity: $E_{cap} = 6 \text{ kWh}$
- Initial SoC: $SoC_0 = 50\text{ %}$

If the bus decelerates uniformly from maximum speed to rest, calculate:
1. The total kinetic energy dissipated. **(2 marks)**
2. The electrical energy recovered and stored in the battery. **(3 marks)**
3. The final SoC after the braking event. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Component Description (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the diagram. The five major components are:

1. **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE):** Primary mechanical power source during steady-state highway driving.
2. **Electric Motor / Generator:** Provides electric propulsion, regenerative braking, and ICE starting (acting as generator during regen).
3. **Battery Pack (Traction Battery):** Stores electrical energy; typically NiMH or Li-ion; maintains a constrained SoC window.
4. **Power Electronics Inverter:** Converts DC battery voltage to AC for the motor (and AC to DC during charging).
5. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU):** Decides the power split between ICE and EM based on driver demand, SoC, and efficiency maps.

**(Award 1.5 marks for diagram + 1 mark each for 5 component descriptions, totalling 6.5; round to 7)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Total kinetic energy dissipated:**

$$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 12{,}000 \times (25)^{2}$$

$$E_{kinetic} = 6000 \times 625 = 3{,}750{,}000 \text{ J} = 3750 \text{ kJ}$$

**2. Electrical energy recovered:**

$$E_{recovered} = f_{regen} \cdot \eta_{em} \cdot E_{kinetic}$$

$$E_{recovered} = 0.55 \times 0.88 \times 3{,}750{,}000$$

$$E_{recovered} = 0.484 \times 3{,}750{,}000 = 1{,}815{,}000 \text{ J} = 1815 \text{ kJ}$$

Convert to kWh:
$$E_{recovered} = \frac{1815}{3600} = 0.5042 \text{ kWh}$$

**3. Final SoC:**

$$\Delta SoC = \frac{E_{recovered}}{E_{cap}} \times 100\text{ %} = \frac{0.5042}{6} \times 100 = 8.40\text{ %}$$

$$SoC_{final} = 50\text{ %} + 8.40\text{ %} = 58.40\text{ %}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{E_{kinetic} = 3750 \text{ kJ}, \quad E_{recovered} = 1815 \text{ kJ} \ (0.504 \text{ kWh}), \quad SoC_{final} = 58.4\text{ \%}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q1B]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three main architectures** of Hybrid Electric Vehicles — Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel (Power-Split). Compare them using a suitable comparison table covering at least **six (6) parameters**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild-hybrid passenger car has the following data:
- ICE maximum power: $P_{ice} = 80 \text{ kW}$
- Electric motor maximum power: $P_{em} = 12 \text{ kW}$
- Conventional fuel economy: $FC_{old} = 14 \text{ km/L}$
- HEV fuel economy improvement: $f_{sav} = 22\text{ %}$

Calculate:
1. The Degree of Hybridization (DOH) of the vehicle. Classify the hybrid type. **(2 marks)**
2. The new fuel economy $FC_{new}$ of the HEV. **(2 marks)**
3. The annual fuel cost saving for a driver covering **15,000 km/year**, with petrol price ₹105/L. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture Explanation (7 marks):**

**Series Hybrid:** The ICE is mechanically decoupled from the wheels. The ICE drives a generator to produce electricity, which either charges the battery or directly powers the electric motor that drives the wheels. The vehicle is effectively an **electric car with an on-board range extender**. Example: Chevrolet Volt (series mode), Nissan e-Power.

**Parallel Hybrid:** Both the ICE and the EM are mechanically connected to the drive wheels through a clutch and transmission. They can work **independently or in combination** to propel the vehicle. Example: Honda Insight (1st gen), Honda Civic Hybrid.

**Series-Parallel Hybrid (Power-Split):** Uses a **planetary gear set** (Toyota THS) to mechanically couple the ICE, EM, and generator. Power can be split variably between the mechanical and electrical paths, giving the **best of both architectures**. Example: Toyota Prius, Toyota Camry Hybrid.

| Parameter | Series | Parallel | Series-Parallel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical path to wheels | None (EM only) | Both ICE and EM | Both, via planetary gear |
| ICE efficiency operation | Always optimal (drives generator) | Variable, driver-demand based | Optimal via power split |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Cost | Medium | Low | High |
| City cycle efficiency | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| Highway efficiency | Medium (double conversion) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Example | BMW i3 REx | Honda Civic IMA | Toyota Prius |

**(Award 1.5 marks for series + 1.5 for parallel + 1.5 for series-parallel + 2.5 for the comparison table)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Degree of Hybridization:**

$$DOH = \frac{P_{em}}{P_{em} + P_{ice}} = \frac{12}{12 + 80} = \frac{12}{92}$$

$$DOH = 0.1304 \approx 0.13$$

Since $0.05 < DOH \leq 0.25$, this is classified as a **Mild Hybrid**.

**2. New Fuel Economy:**

$$FC_{new} = \frac{FC_{old}}{1 - f_{sav}} = \frac{14}{1 - 0.22} = \frac{14}{0.78}$$

$$FC_{new} = 17.949 \text{ km/L} \approx 17.95 \text{ km/L}$$

**3. Annual Fuel Cost Saving:**

Fuel consumed (conventional) per year:
$$V_{conv} = \frac{15{,}000}{14} = 1071.43 \text{ L}$$

Fuel consumed (HEV) per year:
$$V_{hev} = \frac{15{,}000}{17.95} = 835.65 \text{ L}$$

Fuel saved per year:
$$\Delta V = 1071.43 - 835.65 = 235.78 \text{ L}$$

Annual cost saving:
$$Saving = \Delta V \times \text{Price} = 235.78 \times 105 = \text{₹ } 24{,}756.9$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{DOH = 0.13 \ (\text{Mild Hybrid}), \quad FC_{new} = 17.95 \text{ km/L}, \quad \text{Annual saving} \approx \text{₹ } 24{,}757}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **DOH Formula Trap:** Many students write $DOH = P_{em}/P_{ice}$ instead of $P_{em}/(P_{em} + P_{ice})$. This is the **most common single-mark loss** in KTU Module 1.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch in Energy:** J → kWh conversion requires division by **3600**, not 1000. Write the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 3. **Architecture Confusion:** Series-Parallel is **not** the same as a complex series hybrid. A planetary gear set is the **defining physical signature** of a power-split architecture — mention it explicitly for full marks.
> 4. **Forgetting SoC limits:** Real HEVs do **not** allow full 0–100 % SoC swing. The "battery capacity" in problems is **not** the same as the usable capacity. Read the problem statement carefully.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **HEV Definition:** Vehicle with **two or more energy sources** — typically ICE + Electric Motor + Battery.
- **Why HEVs:** To overcome the low part-load efficiency of the ICE and to recover braking energy that is otherwise wasted as heat.
- **Degree of Hybridization (DOH):** $DOH = P_{em} / (P_{em} + P_{ice})$. Ranges from 0 (pure ICE) to 1 (pure EV).
- **Classification by DOH:** Micro ($< 0.05$) → Mild ($0.05$–$0.25$) → Full ($0.25$–$1.0$) → Plug-In (grid-chargeable).
- **Three Architectures:** Series (EM drives wheels), Parallel (both drive wheels), Series-Parallel / Power-Split (planetary gear coupling).
- **Key Subsystems:** ICE, Electric Motor/Generator, Battery Pack, Power Inverter, Transmission, VCU.
- **Regenerative Braking:** Converts KE → Electrical energy using the EM as a generator. Recovers **30–60 %** of braking energy in real-world drive cycles.
- **Stop-Start (Micro-Hybrid):** Engine shuts off at idle; restarts via a belt-driven starter-generator. Saves **3–8 %** fuel.
- **Full Hybrid:** Can run in **pure EV mode** at low speeds. Example: **Toyota Prius** (1.8 L Atkinson engine + 53 kW EM + 1.31 kWh NiMH battery at 201.6 V).
- **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV):** Has a larger battery (**4–18 kWh**), grid-chargeable, and can run **30–80 km** on pure electric mode.
- **Standard HEV Voltage Bus:** 144 V to 650 V DC.
- **SoC Operating Window:** Typically maintained at **40 % to 80 %** to maximize cycle life.
- **Real-world HEV fuel economy improvement** vs. conventional: **+20 % to +40 %** in city; **+10 % to +15 %** in highway.
- **Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS)** is the **textbook example** of a series-parallel power-split architecture — remember this for the 14-mark question.
- **Formula priority order for KTU:** DOH → KE formula → Energy recovered → SoC update → Fuel economy improvement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — HEV Concept.** The next topic in Module 1 is **"Internal Combustion Engine vs. Electric Powertrain — A Comparative Study"**, which builds directly on the regenerative braking and DOH concepts covered above.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)** is a powertrain architecture in which the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) is **mechanically decoupled** from the driving wheels. The ICE is used **solely as a range extender** to drive a generator, producing electrical energy that is subsequently used to charge the battery or feed the traction motor through power electronic converters.

In this topology, **there is no mechanical path** between the prime mover (ICE) and the wheels. Energy flows in a **single electrical series path**:

$$\text{ICE} \rightarrow \text{Generator} \rightarrow \text{Power Electronics} \rightarrow \text{Battery/UC} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Traction Motor} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology:**
> - **Prime Mover (APU):** Auxiliary Power Unit — ICE + Generator combination
> - **Traction Motor:** The only component mechanically linked to the wheels
> - **Energy Buffer:** Battery pack and/or Ultracapacitor
> - **Power Electronics Interface:** Rectifier, DC-DC Converter, Inverter

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **diesel-electric locomotive (train)** running on tracks.

- The diesel engine in the locomotive **does NOT turn the wheels directly**.
- Instead, the diesel engine spins a **big generator** to produce electricity.
- This electricity then powers **electric traction motors** mounted on the wheels.

A **Series HEV operates on exactly this principle**, just miniaturized for a passenger car.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way:**
> - **ICE = A power plant on wheels** (generates electricity)
> - **Battery = A fuel reserve / energy bank** (stores electricity)
> - **Traction Motor = The actual "muscle"** that turns the wheels
>
> The wheels **only "see" the electric motor**. They never feel the engine vibration directly. This is why series HEVs feel incredibly smooth and silent at low speeds!

### Real-World Examples:
| Vehicle | Application |
|---|---|
| **Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1)** | Range-extended passenger EV |
| **Nissan e-Power (Note/Serenity)** | Compact series hybrid |
| **BMW i3 with REX** | Range Extender variant |
| **City Buses (Proterra, BYD K9)** | Heavy-duty urban transit |
| **Diesel-Electric Locomotives** | Rail traction reference design |

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following standard performance metrics are used to characterize Series HEVs:

- **SOC (State of Charge):** Typically maintained between **30% – 80%** during operation
- **ICE Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC):** Best point range **220 – 260 g/kWh**
- **System Voltage:** Modern SHEVs operate on **200 V – 800 V** DC bus architecture
- **Regenerative Braking Recovery:** Up to **70%** of kinetic energy recoverable in stop-and-go traffic

---

## 1.4 Why Series? — The Engineering Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Motivation:** In conventional vehicles, the ICE must constantly vary its speed and torque to match unpredictable driving conditions. This causes it to operate most of the time at **sub-optimal efficiency points**, wasting fuel.

In a **Series HEV**, the ICE can be:
1. **Decoupled** from wheel-speed fluctuations
2. **Operated at a single sweet spot** on its BSFC map (constant RPM, constant load)
3. **Switched OFF entirely** when the battery has enough charge (pure EV mode)

This decouples **energy generation** from **energy consumption** — a fundamental architectural advantage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Power Flow

The Series HEV powertrain operates through a **strict sequential electrical energy conversion chain**. Let us dissect each transition:

### Step 1: Chemical → Mechanical Energy
- The **ICE** burns fuel (petrol/diesel/CNG) and produces **rotational mechanical power**.

$$P_{\text{ICE}} = \tau_{\text{ICE}} \cdot \omega_{\text{ICE}}$$

Where:
- $\tau_{\text{ICE}}$ = Engine torque (N·m)
- $\omega_{\text{ICE}}$ = Engine angular velocity (rad/s)

### Step 2: Mechanical → Electrical Energy
- The **Generator** (typically a 3-phase AC synchronous or induction machine) converts mechanical power into electrical power.

$$P_{\text{gen}} = \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot P_{\text{ICE}}$$

### Step 3: AC → DC Rectification
- The **Rectifier / DC-DC Converter** conditions the generator output to match the DC bus voltage.

$$P_{\text{DC-bus}} = \eta_{\text{rect}} \cdot P_{\text{gen}}$$

### Step 4: Energy Storage / Buffering
- The **Battery (or Ultracapacitor)** absorbs or supplies power based on the SOC and demand.

$$P_{\text{bat}} = V_{\text{bus}} \cdot I_{\text{bat}}$$

### Step 5: DC → AC Inversion
- The **Traction Inverter** converts DC bus power into variable-frequency 3-phase AC.

$$P_{\text{inv}} = \eta_{\text{inv}} \cdot P_{\text{DC-bus}}$$

### Step 6: Electrical → Mechanical Energy at Wheels
- The **Traction Motor** drives the wheels through a **single-speed reduction gearbox**.

$$P_{\text{wheel}} = \eta_{\text{motor}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gear}} \cdot P_{\text{inv}}$$

### Step 7: Regenerative Braking (Reverse Path)
- During braking, the traction motor acts as a **generator**, reversing Steps 6→5→4 to recover kinetic energy.

$$P_{\text{regen}} = \eta_{\text{regen}} \cdot P_{\text{kinetic,brake}}$$

---

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Control Strategy Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The Operating Modes of a Series HEV:**

| Mode | ICE State | Battery State | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure EV Mode** | OFF | Discharging | Low-speed city driving |
| **APU Mode (Charge Sustaining)** | ON @ optimal point | Ni (Sustained) | Highway cruising |
| **APU + Boost Mode** | ON @ max power | Discharging | Hard acceleration / hill climb |
| **Regenerative Mode** | OFF | Charging | Braking / deceleration |
| **Charging Mode (Stationary)** | ON | Charging | Plug-in charging via APU |

### Thermostatic Control Strategy (KTU High-Yield Concept):
The most common control strategy maintains battery SOC within a band:

$$\text{SOC}_{\text{low}} \leq \text{SOC}(t) \leq \text{SOC}_{\text{high}}$$

- If SOC drops below SOC_low → **Start ICE** at its **optimal BSFC point**.
- If SOC rises above SOC_high → **Stop ICE**, run in pure EV mode.

This is called the **On/Off (Thermostat) Strategy** and is the most frequently asked concept in KTU exams.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 90% of KTU numerical problems on Series HEVs.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{\text{demand}} = \frac{v}{\eta_{\text{total}}} \cdot (F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{acc}})$ | Total tractive power at wheels | kW |
| 2 | $F_{\text{roll}} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{\text{rr}} \cdot \cos\theta$ | Rolling resistance force | N |
| 3 | $F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_{\text{d}} \cdot A \cdot v^{2}$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N |
| 4 | $F_{\text{grade}} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | Hill climbing force | N |
| 5 | $F_{\text{acc}} = m \cdot a$ | Acceleration force | N |
| 6 | $P_{\text{wheel}} = F_{\text{total}} \cdot v$ | Power at wheels | W |
| 7 | $P_{\text{ICE,optimal}} = P_{\text{demand}} \cdot \tfrac{\eta_{\text{chain,total}}}{\eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot \eta_{\text{conv}}}$ | Required ICE output for given demand | W |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{system}} = \eta_{\text{ICE}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot \eta_{\text{conv}} \cdot \eta_{\text{bat}} \cdot \eta_{\text{inv}} \cdot \eta_{\text{motor}}$ | Overall system efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $E_{\text{battery}} = \tfrac{V_{\text{bus}} \cdot C_{\text{rated}}}{1000}$ | Battery energy capacity | kWh |
| 10 | $\Delta \text{SOC} = \tfrac{E_{\text{delivered}}}{E_{\text{battery,usable}}} \times 100\%$ | SOC change during a cycle | % |
| 11 | $\omega_{\text{motor}} = \tfrac{v \cdot G}{r_{\text{wheel}}}$ | Motor speed from vehicle speed | rad/s |
| 12 | $\tau_{\text{motor}} = \tfrac{F_{\text{total}} \cdot r_{\text{wheel}}}{G \cdot \eta_{\text{gear}}}$ | Motor torque from tractive force | N·m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Note on notation:** In the table above, $\eta$ (eta) represents the efficiency of each component. Always use `\vert` for absolute value notation in your answers to avoid LaTeX rendering errors in KTU digital evaluation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Series HEV architecture is **the dominant choice** in the following engineering domains:

1. **Urban Public Transit Buses** — Frequent stops benefit from regenerative braking; smooth torque delivery eliminates gear shifts.
2. **Delivery Vans & Last-Mile Logistics** — Predictable urban routes allow ICE-on-ICE-off optimization.
3. **Range-Extended EVs (REEV)** — Acts as a "psychological range insurance" for buyers afraid of charging infrastructure gaps.
4. **Military & Off-Road Vehicles** — Silent watch capability (ICE off, battery-only motor operation for stealth).
5. **Diesel-Electric Submarines & Locomotives** — The original industrial application of this architecture.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Overall System Efficiency of a Series HEV

### Given:
A Series HEV with the following component efficiencies:
- $\eta_{\text{ICE}} = 0.35$ (engine thermal efficiency at optimal point)
- $\eta_{\text{gen}} = 0.92$ (generator electrical efficiency)
- $\eta_{\text{conv}} = 0.95$ (rectifier/DC-DC converter efficiency)
- $\eta_{\text{batt}} = 0.98$ (battery charge-discharge round-trip efficiency)
- $\eta_{\text{inv}} = 0.96$ (traction inverter efficiency)
- $\eta_{\text{motor}} = 0.93$ (traction motor efficiency)

### Find:
The **overall system efficiency** when the vehicle runs in **APU Mode** (ICE → Generator → Battery → Inverter → Motor → Wheels).

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify the energy flow path.
$$\text{Fuel} \rightarrow \text{ICE} \rightarrow \text{Generator} \rightarrow \text{Converter} \rightarrow \text{Battery} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Motor} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **multiplicative efficiency rule** for cascaded energy conversions. Since energy passes sequentially through each stage, the overall efficiency is the **product of individual stage efficiencies**.

$$\eta_{\text{system}} = \eta_{\text{ICE}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot \eta_{\text{conv}} \cdot \eta_{\text{batt}} \cdot \eta_{\text{inv}} \cdot \eta_{\text{motor}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$\eta_{\text{system}} = 0.35 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.96 \times 0.93$$

**Step 4:** Compute step by step (left to right multiplication).

$$\eta_{\text{system}} = 0.35 \times 0.92 = 0.3220$$

$$0.3220 \times 0.95 = 0.3059$$

$$0.3059 \times 0.98 = 0.2998$$

$$0.2998 \times 0.96 = 0.2878$$

$$0.2878 \times 0.93 = 0.2677$$

**Step 5:** Express as a percentage.

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{system}} \approx 0.2677 \text{ or } 26.77\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Even with **perfect** downstream electrical components ($\eta = 1$ for everything except ICE), the maximum possible Series HEV efficiency is capped at the **ICE's thermal efficiency (~35–40%)**. This is the **fundamental thermodynamic bottleneck** of the Series architecture.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: ICE Power Sizing for Steady-State Cruise

### Given:
A Series HEV must cruise at a constant speed $v = 90 \text{ km/h}$ on a flat road.
- Vehicle mass $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$
- Rolling resistance coefficient $C_{\text{rr}} = 0.012$
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_{\text{d}} = 0.30$
- Frontal area $A = 2.2 \text{ m}^{2}$
- Air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
- Drivetrain efficiency (motor + inverter + gear) $\eta_{\text{drive}} = 0.85$
- Total electrical chain efficiency (gen → conv → batt → inv) $\eta_{\text{elec}} = 0.82$
- Assume pure APU mode (no battery discharge)

### Find:
The **required ICE power output** to maintain cruise.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Convert vehicle speed to SI units.

$$v = 90 \text{ km/h} = \tfrac{90 \times 1000}{3600} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the forces acting on the vehicle at steady-state cruise (no acceleration, no grade).

$$\theta = 0^{\circ} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta = 1, \quad \sin\theta = 0$$

Therefore:

$$F_{\text{grade}} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad F_{\text{acc}} = 0$$

The total resistive force is:

$$F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{aero}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the rolling resistance force.

$$F_{\text{roll}} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{\text{rr}} \cdot \cos\theta = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 1$$

$$F_{\text{roll}} = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the aerodynamic drag force.

$$F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_{\text{d}} \cdot A \cdot v^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (25)^{2}$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 625$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 252.66 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5:** Compute total tractive force.

$$F_{\text{total}} = 176.58 + 252.66 = 429.24 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6:** Compute power required at the wheels.

$$P_{\text{wheel}} = F_{\text{total}} \cdot v = 429.24 \times 25 = 10731 \text{ W} \approx 10.73 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7:** Trace the power back upstream through the drivetrain and electrical chain.

Power at motor shaft (input to drivetrain):

$$P_{\text{motor,elec-input}} = \tfrac{P_{\text{wheel}}}{\eta_{\text{drive}}} = \tfrac{10.73}{0.85} = 12.62 \text{ kW}$$

Power that the ICE-driven generator must produce (output of electrical chain):

$$P_{\text{gen,out}} = \tfrac{P_{\text{motor,elec-input}}}{\eta_{\text{elec}}} = \tfrac{12.62}{0.82} = 15.39 \text{ kW}$$

Power required at ICE shaft (input to generator):

$$P_{\text{ICE}} = \tfrac{P_{\text{gen,out}}}{\eta_{\text{gen}}} = \tfrac{15.39}{0.92} = 16.73 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8:** Final result.

$$\boxed{P_{\text{ICE,required}} \approx 16.73 \text{ kW} \approx 22.43 \text{ HP}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** Although only **~10.7 kW** is needed at the wheels, the ICE must deliver **~16.7 kW** to overcome cumulative conversion losses. This **~56% oversizing** is a hallmark of Series HEV design.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Series vs. Conventional vs. Parallel

| Parameter | Conventional ICEV | Series HEV | Parallel HEV |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical path to wheels** | Direct | None | Yes (parallel) |
| **ICE operating flexibility** | Poor (follows wheel demand) | Excellent (decoupled) | Moderate |
| **Overall efficiency** | 20 – 25% | 26 – 32% | 30 – 38% |
| **Component count** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Battery size** | None / Small | Large | Small |
| **Traction motor size** | None | Very Large | Medium |
| **Best suited for** | Highway cruising | Urban / stop-go | Mixed driving |
| **Regenerative braking** | None | Excellent | Good |
| **Complexity** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Series HEV — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below maps the **complete energy and signal flow** through the Series HEV powertrain, showing every energy conversion stage and the bidirectional regenerative path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FUEL[Fuel Tank] --> ICE[Internal Combustion Engine]
    ICE -- Mechanical Shaft --> GEN[Three Phase Generator]
    GEN -- AC Output --> RECT[Rectifier and DC-DC Converter]
    RECT -- DC Power --> DCBUS[DC Bus 400V to 800V]
    DCBUS --> BATT[Li-ion Battery Pack]
    DCBUS --> INV[Three Phase Traction Inverter]
    INV -- Variable Frequency AC --> MOTOR[Permanent Magnet Traction Motor]
    MOTOR -- Reduction Gear --> TRANS[Single Speed Transmission]
    TRANS -- Final Drive --> WHEELS[Drive Wheels]
    WHEELS -. Regenerative Braking .-> MOTOR
    MOTOR -. AC Regen .-> INV
    INV -. DC Regen .-> DCBUS
    DCBUS -. Charge Battery .-> BATT
    CTRL[Vehicle Control Unit VCU] -. SOC and Torque Command .-> ICE
    CTRL -. Enable Charge .-> GEN
    CTRL -. PWM Control .-> INV
    CTRL -. Torque Demand .-> MOTOR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Solid arrows** = forward power flow (energy delivery to wheels)
> - **Dotted arrows** = regenerative feedback path (energy recovery to battery)
> - **Dashed arrows from VCU** = control signals (not power)
> - The ICE and WHEELS are on **opposite ends of the chain** — they share no mechanical coupling.

---

## 4.2 Series HEV — Operating Mode State Machine

This state diagram illustrates the **thermostatic control logic** that governs ICE on/off switching based on battery SOC and driver demand.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> EV_Mode : Vehicle Start SOC above 50 percent
    EV_Mode --> APU_Mode : SOC drops below SOC low threshold
    APU_Mode --> Boost_Mode : Accelerator pedal demand high
    Boost_Mode --> APU_Mode : Demand returns to cruise level
    APU_Mode --> EV_Mode : SOC rises above SOC high threshold
    APU_Mode --> Regen_Mode : Brake pedal pressed
    EV_Mode --> Regen_Mode : Brake pedal pressed
    Regen_Mode --> EV_Mode : Brake released SOC restored
    Boost_Mode --> Regen_Mode : Sudden brake demand
    EV_Mode --> [*] : Ignition OFF
    APU_Mode --> [*] : Ignition OFF
```

---

## 4.3 Series HEV — Energy Conversion Cascade

A vertical cascade view that highlights **why efficiency drops at every stage** and where the major losses occur.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FUEL CHEMICAL ENERGY 100 percent] --> B[ICE Thermal Conversion eta 35 percent]
    B --> C[Mechanical Shaft Output]
    C --> D[Generator eta 92 percent]
    D --> E[AC Electrical Output]
    E --> F[Rectifier and DC-DC eta 95 percent]
    F --> G[DC Bus Power]
    G --> H[Battery Buffer eta 98 percent]
    H --> I[Inverter eta 96 percent]
    I --> J[Motor Mechanical Output eta 93 percent]
    J --> K[Transmission eta 97 percent]
    K --> L[WHEEL ENERGY 26.8 percent]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight from the cascade:**
> The **single largest loss** is at the **ICE stage** (~65% of fuel energy is lost as heat). All downstream electrical components collectively lose only about **~8%**. This is why **improving ICE thermal efficiency** (e.g., via Atkinson cycle, HCCI, or fuel cells) is the most impactful Series HEV research direction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle. List its main components.**

**Model Answer:**

A Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV) is a hybrid powertrain configuration in which the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) is **not mechanically coupled** to the driving wheels. Instead, the ICE drives a generator to produce electrical energy, which charges a battery or directly powers an electric traction motor that drives the wheels.

**Main Components:**
1. Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) — acts as range extender / APU
2. Electric Generator (3-phase AC machine)
3. Power Electronics Rectifier / DC-DC Converter
4. Energy Storage System (Li-ion Battery / Ultracapacitor)
5. Traction Inverter
6. Electric Traction Motor (PMSM / Induction)
7. Single-speed Reduction Transmission
8. Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Thermostatic (On/Off) Control Strategy used in Series HEVs?**

**Model Answer:**

The Thermostatic Control Strategy is a rule-based energy management method where the ICE is **switched ON or OFF** based on the battery State of Charge (SOC) thresholds.

- If **SOC < SOC_low** → ICE is **turned ON** at its optimal BSFC operating point to charge the battery.
- If **SOC > SOC_high** → ICE is **turned OFF**, and the vehicle runs in **pure EV mode** using battery power alone.

This strategy keeps SOC within a narrow band (typically 30%–70%), minimizes fuel consumption by running the ICE only at its most efficient point, and is computationally simple — making it ideal for real-time VCU implementation.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, you must answer **ONE** full question (either Option A or Option B). Both options carry equal marks.

---

### **OPTION A** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Question A:**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. Discuss all five operating modes in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Series HEV has the following specifications:
- Vehicle mass $m = 1800 \text{ kg}$
- $C_{\text{rr}} = 0.010$, $C_{\text{d}} = 0.28$, $A = 2.4 \text{ m}^{2}$, $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
- Cruise speed $v = 60 \text{ km/h}$ on a $5\%$ gradient
- Generator efficiency $\eta_{\text{gen}} = 0.90$
- Power converter efficiency $\eta_{\text{conv}} = 0.94$
- Battery + Inverter + Motor combined efficiency $\eta_{\text{trail}} = 0.80$

Calculate the **ICE power required** in kW. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Option A:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The **working principle** of a Series HEV is based on the concept of **mechanical decoupling** between the ICE and the wheels.

**Block Diagram Description:**
The powertrain consists of the following elements connected in **electrical series**:

1. **ICE** drives the **Generator** through a mechanical coupling.
2. **Generator output (AC)** is rectified by the **Converter** and fed to the **DC Bus**.
3. **Battery** is connected to the DC bus in parallel (acts as energy buffer).
4. **Inverter** draws power from DC bus and feeds **3-phase AC to the Traction Motor**.
5. **Traction Motor** drives the wheels via a **single-speed reduction gear**.

**Five Operating Modes:**

- **Mode 1 — Pure EV Mode:** ICE is OFF. Battery alone powers the traction motor. Used during low-speed city driving or when SOC is high.
- **Mode 2 — Pure APU Mode:** ICE runs at its optimal BSFC point. Generator output exactly matches traction motor demand. SOC remains constant.
- **Mode 3 — APU + Boost Mode:** ICE runs at maximum power, AND battery discharges simultaneously to meet peak demand (acceleration, hill climb).
- **Mode 4 — Regenerative Braking Mode:** ICE is OFF. Traction motor acts as generator. Kinetic energy is converted to electrical energy and stored in battery.
- **Mode 5 — Charging Mode:** ICE runs while vehicle is stationary (plug-in charging via APU or idle charging).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert speed to SI units.

$$v = 60 \text{ km/h} = \tfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the grade angle. For 5% gradient, $\tan\theta = 0.05$.

$$\theta = \arctan(0.05) = 2.86^{\circ}$$

Therefore:

$$\sin\theta \approx 0.0499, \quad \cos\theta \approx 0.9988$$

**Step 3:** Compute rolling resistance force.

$$F_{\text{roll}} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{\text{rr}} \cdot \cos\theta = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.010 \times 0.9988$$

$$F_{\text{roll}} = 176.32 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4:** Compute aerodynamic drag force.

$$F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_{\text{d}} \cdot A \cdot v^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.28 \times 2.4 \times (16.67)^{2}$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.28 \times 2.4 \times 277.78$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 112.00 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5:** Compute grade-climbing force.

$$F_{\text{grade}} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.0499$$

$$F_{\text{grade}} = 881.13 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6:** Compute total tractive force (no acceleration, $F_{\text{acc}} = 0$).

$$F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{grade}}$$

$$F_{\text{total}} = 176.32 + 112.00 + 881.13 = 1169.45 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7:** Compute power at wheels.

$$P_{\text{wheel}} = F_{\text{total}} \cdot v = 1169.45 \times 16.67 = 19,494 \text{ W} \approx 19.49 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8:** Trace power back through the electrical chain to ICE.

Combined downstream efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{down}} = \eta_{\text{trail}} \times \eta_{\text{conv}} \times \eta_{\text{gen}} = 0.80 \times 0.94 \times 0.90 = 0.6768$$

ICE power required:

$$P_{\text{ICE}} = \tfrac{P_{\text{wheel}}}{\eta_{\text{down}}} = \tfrac{19.49}{0.6768}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{ICE}} \approx 28.80 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### **OPTION B** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Question B:**

**(a)** Compare **Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicles** based on architecture, efficiency, complexity, component sizing, and typical applications. Use a comparative table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Thermostatic Control Strategy** for Series HEVs. A Series HEV has a battery pack with usable energy of **15 kWh** and an average traction motor demand of **12 kW** at constant cruise. If the SOC band is **40%–70%**, calculate:
   - (i) The **usable energy window** in kWh. **(2 marks)**
   - (ii) The **maximum cruise duration** in pure EV mode starting from SOC = 70%. **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) The **minimum SOC after 30 minutes** of APU mode if ICE charges at 14 kW and demand is 12 kW. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Option B:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | Series HEV | Parallel HEV | Series-Parallel HEV |
|---|---|---|---|
| Architecture | ICE → Gen → Batt → Motor → Wheels | ICE and Motor both drive wheels (mechanical sum) | Uses planetary gearset (Toyota THS) for power split |
| ICE-Wheel Coupling | None (fully decoupled) | Direct mechanical | Partial (variable) |
| System Efficiency | 26 – 32% | 30 – 38% | 34 – 40% |
| Complexity | High (large motor + battery) | Medium | Very High |
| Battery Size | Large | Small | Medium |
| Traction Motor Size | Very Large | Medium | Medium |
| Transmission | Single-speed | Multi-speed possible | Planetary CVT-like |
| Regenerative Braking | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| Typical Application | City buses, delivery vans | SUVs, trucks | Passenger cars (Toyota Prius) |
| Example | Nissan e-Power, Chevy Volt | Honda Insight (early) | Toyota Prius, Ford Escape Hybrid |

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:**

**(i) Usable energy window in kWh:**

$$E_{\text{usable,band}} = (SOC_{\text{high}} - SOC_{\text{low}}) \times E_{\text{batt}}$$

$$E_{\text{usable,band}} = (0.70 - 0.40) \times 15 = 0.30 \times 15 = 4.5 \text{ kWh}$$

**(ii) Maximum cruise duration in pure EV mode starting from SOC = 70%:**

Available energy from SOC 70% down to SOC 40%:

$$E_{\text{available}} = 4.5 \text{ kWh} \quad \text{(same as above)}$$

$$t_{\text{max,EV}} = \tfrac{E_{\text{available}}}{P_{\text{demand}}} = \tfrac{4.5 \text{ kWh}}{12 \text{ kW}} = 0.375 \text{ h} = 22.5 \text{ minutes}$$

**(iii) Minimum SOC after 30 minutes of APU mode:**

Net charging power (ICE charging minus demand):

$$P_{\text{net-charge}} = P_{\text{ICE-charge}} - P_{\text{demand}} = 14 - 12 = 2 \text{ kW}$$

Energy added to battery in 30 minutes:

$$E_{\text{added}} = 2 \text{ kW} \times 0.5 \text{ h} = 1.0 \text{ kWh}$$

Starting SOC = 70%, so SOC increase:

$$\Delta SOC = \tfrac{E_{\text{added}}}{E_{\text{batt}}} \times 100\% = \tfrac{1.0}{15} \times 100\% = 6.67\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{SOC}_{\text{final}} = 70\% + 6.67\% = 76.67\%}$$

But SOC cannot exceed 100%. Since 76.67% < 70% upper threshold is exceeded → **ICE will switch OFF via thermostatic control**, and the vehicle will revert to EV mode. For the purpose of the calculation, the **theoretical minimum SOC after 30 min is N/A (system stays at or above 70%)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. In **Part (b)(iii)**, many students forget that **thermostatic control auto-switches the ICE OFF** when SOC exceeds the upper threshold. The answer must acknowledge this, or you **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. In **Part A numerical**, students often **forget the grade force** or use $\sin\theta = 0.05$ directly instead of $\sin(\arctan 0.05)$. Both will be penalized.
> 3. In **Part (a) block diagrams**, you **must show the regenerative braking path** as a dotted/feedback line. Diagrams without regen arrows lose **2 marks**.
> 4. **Do not confuse Series HEV with Series-Parallel (PSHEV)**. Series has NO mechanical link from ICE to wheels. If your diagram shows a shaft between ICE and wheels, you will receive **ZERO marks** for the architecture section.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Series HEV**

- **Definition:** Series HEV = ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from wheels; only an **electrical path** connects ICE to wheels.
- **Power Flow:** ICE → Generator → Rectifier → DC Bus → Inverter → Motor → Wheels.
- **Key Equation to Memorize:**
  $$\eta_{\text{system}} = \eta_{\text{ICE}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot \eta_{\text{conv}} \cdot \eta_{\text{batt}} \cdot \eta_{\text{inv}} \cdot \eta_{\text{motor}}$$
- **Main Advantage:** ICE can operate at its **single most efficient point** (constant RPM, constant load) regardless of driving conditions.
- **Main Disadvantage:** **Multiple energy conversions** lead to cumulative losses; requires **large motor and battery**.
- **Five Operating Modes:** Pure EV, Pure APU, APU + Boost, Regenerative Braking, Charging.
- **Thermostatic Control:** ICE ON when SOC < SOC_low; ICE OFF when SOC > SOC_high.
- **Tractive Force Equation:**
  $$F_{\text{total}} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{\text{rr}} \cdot \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{\text{d}} A v^{2} + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot a$$
- **Best Application Domain:** **Urban buses, delivery vehicles, range-extended EVs**.
- **Real-World Examples:** Chevrolet Volt Gen 1, Nissan e-Power, BMW i3 REX, diesel-electric locomotives.
- **Typical System Efficiency Range:** **26% – 32%** (limited primarily by ICE thermal efficiency).
- **Battery SOC Operating Window:** Typically maintained between **30% and 80%**.
- **Regenerative Braking Recovery:** Up to **70%** of kinetic energy recoverable in stop-and-go traffic.
- **Single Most Common Exam Mistake:** Drawing a mechanical shaft between ICE and wheels — this defines a **Parallel** HEV, not Series. **Zero marks** for incorrect architecture.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parallel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV):** A parallel hybrid electric vehicle is a hybrid powertrain architecture in which **two or more distinct energy sources — typically an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) and one or more Electric Machines (EM)** — are mechanically coupled to the driveline in such a way that **both power sources can directly propel the vehicle either independently or simultaneously**, by summing their mechanical output torques at a common coupling point (clutch, gearbox, or planetary gear set).

In the **Parallel Hybrid configuration**, the ICE and the Electric Motor are connected in a *parallel* mechanical arrangement. The mechanical power from both sources is **added (summed)** at a coupling device and then transmitted to the wheels through a conventional transmission (manual, automatic, or CVT) and final drive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Series Hybrid:** In a *series* hybrid, only the electric motor drives the wheels — the ICE merely charges the battery. In a *parallel* hybrid, **BOTH the ICE and the electric motor can deliver mechanical power to the wheels at the same time.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you and a friend are pushing a broken-down car together down a road.

- **You (the ICE)** are a strong, muscular person who can push hard for a long time but gets tired (burns fuel) and cannot push when standing still.
- **Your friend (the Electric Motor)** is a smaller, less powerful person but can push even from a standstill (high low-speed torque) and recovers energy when the car rolls downhill.
- **Sometimes only your friend pushes** (pure EV mode at low speeds / parking).
- **Sometimes only you push** (highway cruising — ICE alone).
- **Sometimes BOTH of you push together** (hard acceleration / hill climbing — combined torque).

This "side-by-side" pushing is exactly the **Parallel Hybrid** architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> The word "Parallel" comes from the electrical term *parallel connection* — where two power sources feed the same mechanical "load" (the wheels) along parallel paths rather than one feeding the other in a chain (series).

## 1.3 Standardized Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Combined System Power | $P_{total}$ | **50 – 300** | kW |
| ICE Rated Power | $P_{ICE}$ | **40 – 200** | kW |
| Electric Motor Power | $P_{EM}$ | **20 – 100** | kW |
| Battery Pack Voltage | $V_{batt}$ | **200 – 400** | V (DC) |
| Battery Pack Energy | $E_{batt}$ | **1 – 18** | kWh |
| Degree of Hybridization | $DoH$ | **0.2 – 0.5** | dimensionless |

The **Degree of Hybridization (DoH)** is defined formally as:

$$DoH = \frac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}}$$

This is a **critical exam metric** for the KTU 2024 syllabus. A DoH of **0** represents a pure ICE vehicle, and a DoH of **1** represents a pure EV.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle

A typical parallel hybrid powertrain consists of the following components arranged in parallel branches:

1. **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** — primary heat engine.
2. **Electric Motor / Generator (EM/EG)** — reversible machine that acts as motor or generator.
3. **Battery Pack** — high-voltage energy storage (Li-ion, NiMH).
4. **Power Electronics Converter (Inverter)** — DC/AC conversion between battery and motor.
5. **Mechanical Coupling Device** — clutch, torque coupler, or planetary gear set.
6. **Transmission** — manual, automatic (AT), CVT, or automated manual (AMT).
7. **Final Drive (Differential)** — transmits power to wheels.
8. **Energy Management Controller (ECU / VCU)** — orchestrates power split between ICE and EM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-1 Highlight:** The transmission in a parallel hybrid is **NOT optional** — the mechanical coupling requires speed/torque matching, and the transmission is the primary torque summation device. This is a major difference from series hybrids.

## 2.2 Operating Modes of a Parallel Hybrid

The beauty of the parallel architecture is its **multi-mode operation**. A KTU 2024 examiner expects you to know each mode, the active components, and the underlying *why*.

| Mode | ICE State | EM State | Battery State | Vehicle Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. ICE Only** | ON (propelling) | OFF | Idle | Highway cruising, light load |
| **2. EM Only (EV mode)** | OFF | ON (propelling) | Discharging | Low-speed city driving, parking |
| **3. Combined / Hybrid Mode** | ON (propelling) | ON (propelling) | Discharging | Hard acceleration, hill climbing |
| **4. ICE + Charging** | ON (propelling + gen) | Generator mode | Charging | Cruising with battery SoC low |
| **5. Regenerative Braking** | OFF | Generator mode | Charging | Deceleration / braking |
| **6. Start/Stop** | OFF (idle-stopped) | OFF | Idle | Vehicle stationary (traffic signal) |
| **7. Battery Assist** | ON (propelling) | ON (boost) | Discharging | Transient peak power demand |

## 2.3 Why the Parallel Architecture?

### 2.3.1 The "Why" Behind Parallel Coupling
- **No double energy conversion loss:** Unlike series hybrids (where mechanical → electrical → mechanical), the parallel path allows direct mechanical drive when the ICE is efficient, avoiding conversion losses.
- **Compact driveline:** The electric motor can be integrated into the existing transmission bell-housing, requiring minimal chassis modification.
- **High-efficiency highway operation:** The ICE can directly drive wheels at its best operating point (BSFC map), bypassing the motor.
- **Cost-effective:** Smaller battery and motor compared to series, since both power sources share the propulsion load.

### 2.3.2 The "How" — Torque Summation Principle
In parallel hybrids, the total tractive torque at the wheels is the **sum of contributions** from the ICE and the EM, scaled by their respective gear ratios:

$$T_{wheels} = \eta_{trans} \cdot \left( T_{ICE} \cdot i_{ICE} + T_{EM} \cdot i_{EM} \right)$$

where:
- $T_{wheels}$ = total torque at the wheels
- $T_{ICE}$ = torque produced by the ICE
- $T_{EM}$ = torque produced by the electric motor
- $i_{ICE}, i_{EM}$ = gear ratios of ICE and EM branches
- $\eta_{trans}$ = transmission efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most important equation** in Module 1 for parallel hybrids. It captures the essence of the parallel architecture — the *algebraic summation* of mechanical power.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{total} = P_{ICE} + P_{EM} \cdot \eta_{m}$ | Mechanical power summation (parallel) | kW |
| 2 | $P_{EM} = \eta_{m} \cdot T_{EM} \cdot \omega_{EM}$ | Electric motor mechanical power | kW |
| 3 | $P_{ICE} = T_{ICE} \cdot \omega_{ICE}$ | ICE mechanical output power | kW |
| 4 | $DoH = \dfrac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}}$ | Degree of Hybridization | dimensionless |
| 5 | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 + \dfrac{1}{E_{batt}} \int_{0}^{t} I_{batt}(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Battery state of charge dynamics | % |
| 6 | $T_{wheels} = \eta_{trans}(T_{ICE} \cdot i_{ICE} + T_{EM} \cdot i_{EM})$ | Torque summation at wheels | N·m |
| 7 | $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{regen}$ | Regenerative braking energy recovered | J |
| 8 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{P_{fuel} + P_{electrical,in}}$ | Overall drivetrain efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_{req} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^3 + m g f v + m a v$ | Tractive power demand at wheels | W |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{P_{ICE}}{\eta_{ICE} \cdot LHV_{fuel}}$ | Fuel mass flow rate | kg/s |

> [!TIP]
