> The **tractive power equation (#9)** is the road-load equation. The ICE + EM must meet this power demand at every instant. The Energy Management Controller (EMC) decides *how* to split $P_{req}$ between $P_{ICE}$ and $P_{EM}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive OEMs:** Honda Civic Hybrid, Toyota Prius (uses a *power-split* planetary hybrid — a special subclass of parallel), Hyundai Sonata Hybrid, Ford Escape Hybrid, Honda Insight.
- **Heavy commercial vehicles:** Parallel hybrids in city buses (Volvo, Scania) for stop-and-go efficiency.
- **Military vehicles:** The US Army's HMMWV hybrid (M1117) uses a parallel architecture for silent watch capability.
- **Two-wheelers:** Honda PCX Hybrid scooter — small parallel hybrid for urban Asia.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The Toyota Prius is technically a **Power-Split Hybrid (PSHEV)** — it uses a *planetary gear set* to combine series and parallel characteristics simultaneously. In strict KTU terms, it is a *parallel-series combined* or **Series-Parallel Hybrid**. For exam purposes, distinguish this clearly from a pure parallel (e.g., Honda Civic IMA).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Degree of Hybridization (DoH) Bounds

The Degree of Hybridization is a critical design parameter. We derive its bounds below.

**Step 1: Definition Recap**

$$DoH = \frac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}}$$

**Step 2: Case 1 — Pure ICE Vehicle**
When $P_{EM} = 0$ (no electric motor, e.g., Maruti Alto 800):

$$DoH = \frac{0}{0 + P_{ICE}} = 0$$

This represents a **conventional non-hybrid vehicle** ($DoH = 0$).

**Step 3: Case 2 — Pure EV**
When $P_{ICE} = 0$ (no ICE, e.g., Tata Nexon EV):

$$DoH = \frac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + 0} = 1$$

This represents a **battery electric vehicle** ($DoH = 1$).

**Step 4: Case 3 — Micro Hybrid**
A micro hybrid (e.g., Maruti Celerio with start-stop only) typically has $P_{EM} \approx 0.05 \cdot P_{ICE}$:

$$DoH = \frac{0.05 \cdot P_{ICE}}{0.05 \cdot P_{ICE} + P_{ICE}} = \frac{0.05}{1.05} \approx 0.048 \approx 5\%$$

**Step 5: Case 4 — Full Hybrid**
A full parallel hybrid (e.g., Honda Civic Hybrid) typically has $P_{EM} \approx P_{ICE}$:

$$DoH = \frac{P_{ICE}}{P_{ICE} + P_{ICE}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \quad (50\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** $DoH \in [0, 1]$ — a normalized indicator of hybridization strength. KTU board questions often test the **classification of vehicles** based on DoH ranges.

## 3.2 Derivation: Power Flow Through a Parallel Hybrid at Steady Cruise

Consider a parallel hybrid cruising at constant velocity $v$ on a flat road. We need to determine the power split.

**Step 1: Tractive Power Demand**
At constant speed ($a = 0$), the tractive power demand simplifies from Equation #9 to:

$$P_{req} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^3 + m g f v$$

**Step 2: Component Power Inputs**
- ICE delivers $P_{ICE}$ to the driveline with efficiency $\eta_{ICE}$.
- EM delivers $P_{EM}$ to the driveline with efficiency $\eta_{EM}$.

**Step 3: Mechanical Power Balance (Power Summation)**

The total mechanical power reaching the wheels from both branches is:

$$P_{wheels} = \eta_{trans} \cdot \left( P_{ICE} + \eta_{EM} \cdot P_{EM,electrical} \right)$$

where $P_{EM,electrical}$ is the electrical input power to the motor, and $\eta_{EM}$ accounts for motor + inverter efficiency.

**Step 4: Equating Supply to Demand**

$$P_{req} = \eta_{trans} \cdot \left( P_{ICE} + \eta_{EM} \cdot P_{EM,electrical} \right)$$

Solving for the EM power required to supplement the ICE:

$$P_{EM,electrical} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{EM}} \left( \dfrac{P_{req}}{\eta_{trans}} - P_{ICE} \right)$$

**Step 5: Numerical Example (KTU-style)**

Given: $v = 60$ km/h = $16.67$ m/s, $m = 1500$ kg, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.2$ m², $f = 0.012$, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\eta_{trans} = 0.92$, $\eta_{EM} = 0.90$, $P_{ICE} = 40$ kW.

Compute aerodynamic term:

$$\dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^3 = 0.5 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (16.67)^3$$

$$= 0.396 \cdot 4629.6 = 1833.3 \text{ W} \approx 1.83 \text{ kW}$$

Compute rolling resistance term:

$$mg f v = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 \cdot 16.67 = 2943.2 \text{ W} \approx 2.94 \text{ kW}$$

Total tractive power:

$$P_{req} = 1.83 + 2.94 = 4.77 \text{ kW}$$

EM power required:

$$P_{EM,electrical} = \dfrac{1}{0.90} \left( \dfrac{4.77}{0.92} - 40 \right) = 1.111 \cdot (5.18 - 40) = 1.111 \cdot (-34.82) = -38.69 \text{ kW}$$

The **negative sign** means: the ICE alone is producing more than enough power. The Energy Management Controller (EMC) will use this surplus to **charge the battery** (Mode 4 — ICE + Charging). About **34.82 kW** of mechanical power can be diverted to the EM acting as a generator.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Trick Question:** Students often get confused by the negative sign. A negative $P_{EM,electrical}$ here does NOT mean reverse driving — it means the EM is operating as a **generator**, charging the battery with the surplus ICE power.

## 3.3 Derivation: Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

During braking, the kinetic energy of the vehicle must be dissipated. A parallel hybrid uses the EM as a generator to recover part of this energy.

**Step 1: Vehicle Kinetic Energy**
For a vehicle of mass $m$ decelerating from $v_1$ to $v_2$:

$$E_{kinetic} = \dfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

**Step 2: Energy Recovered by Generator**
With a regenerative efficiency $\eta_{regen}$ (typically **60 – 80%** in practice, accounting for motor-generator + inverter + battery charging losses):

$$E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot E_{kinetic} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

**Step 3: Numerical Example**
A 1500 kg vehicle braking from 80 km/h (22.22 m/s) to 20 km/h (5.56 m/s), with $\eta_{regen} = 0.70$:

$$\Delta v^2 = (22.22)^2 - (5.56)^2 = 493.8 - 30.9 = 462.9 \text{ (m/s)}^2$$

$$E_{regen} = 0.70 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1500 \cdot 462.9 = 243{,}022.5 \text{ J} \approx 0.0675 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4: Fuel Equivalent Savings**
With gasoline LHV = 44 MJ/kg and engine thermal efficiency 35%:

$$\text{Fuel saved} = \dfrac{E_{regen}}{\eta_{ICE} \cdot LHV} = \dfrac{243{,}022.5}{0.35 \cdot 44 \times 10^6} = 1.58 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg} = 15.78 \text{ g gasoline}$$

This is a **highly testable KTU calculation** — it quantifies the real benefit of regenerative braking in urban stop-and-go conditions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Parallel vs. Series Hybrid Comparison

| Feature | Parallel Hybrid | Series Hybrid |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical path to wheels | **Direct** (ICE → wheels) | **Indirect** (ICE → gen → motor → wheels) |
| Energy conversion steps | **1** (mechanical → mechanical) | **3** (mech → elec → mech) |
| Transmission required | **Yes** (mandatory) | **No** (or simple reducer) |
| Electric motor size | Smaller (assist-only) | Larger (sole propulsion) |
| Battery size | Smaller (1–5 kWh typical) | Larger (15–30 kWh typical) |
| Highway efficiency | **High** (direct ICE drive) | Lower (double conversion loss) |
| City efficiency | Good | **High** (EV mode dominant) |
| Drivetrain complexity | Medium | High |
| Typical examples | Honda Civic Hybrid, Insight | Chevy Volt (early), Nissan e-POWER |
| Cost | **Lower** | Higher |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Parallel Hybrid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EngineBranch[ICE BRANCH]
        A1[ICE Internal Combustion Engine] --> A2[Clutch C1]
        A2 --> A3[Torque Coupler]
    end
    subgraph MotorBranch[ELECTRIC BRANCH]
        B1[High Voltage Battery Pack] --> B2[Power Electronics Inverter]
        B2 --> B3[Electric Motor Generator]
        B3 --> A3
    end
    A3 --> D1[Multi Speed Transmission]
    D1 --> D2[Final Drive Differential]
    D2 --> D3[Wheels Drivetrain]
    subgraph Controller[VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT]
        E1[VCU Energy Management Controller] -.signals.-> A1
        E1 -.signals.-> B2
        E1 -.signals.-> B3
        E1 -.signals.-> A2
    end
    E1 -.reads SoC.-> B1
    E1 -.reads pedal and speed.-> D3
```

## 4.2 Operating Mode Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Vehicle Start]) --> CheckSpeed{Vehicle Speed v}
    CheckSpeed -->|v is low below 30 kmph| EVMode[Mode 2 EV Only Mode]
    CheckSpeed -->|v is medium| SoCCheck{Battery SoC gt 40 percent}
    CheckSpeed -->|v is high above 60 kmph| HighwayMode[Mode 1 ICE Only Mode]
    SoCCheck -->|Yes| HybridMode[Mode 3 Combined Hybrid Mode]
    SoCCheck -->|No| ChargeMode[Mode 4 ICE plus Charging Mode]
    EVMode --> PedalCheck{Pedal Position}
    HybridMode --> PedalCheck
    HighwayMode --> PedalCheck
    ChargeMode --> PedalCheck
    PedalCheck -->|Released Brake Active| RegenMode[Mode 5 Regenerative Braking]
    PedalCheck -->|Vehicle Stopped| StopMode[Mode 6 Idle Stop]
    RegenMode --> CheckSpeed
    StopMode --> CheckSpeed
```

## 4.3 Power Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerSources[POWER SOURCES]
        S1[Fuel Tank with gasoline] --> S2[ICE producing P_ICE]
        S3[Battery Pack E_batt] --> S4[Inverter and EM producing P_EM]
    end
    S2 --> SumNode{Mechanical Coupler}
    S4 --> SumNode
    SumNode --> Transmission[Transmission efficiency n_trans]
    Transmission --> Wheels[Vehicle Wheels P_req]
    Wheels -.kinetic energy.-> Regen[Regenerative Braking]
    Regen --> S4
    Controller[VCU controller] -.orchestrates.-> S2
    Controller -.orchestrates.-> S4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **bi-directional arrow** between the wheels and the EM (Regen). This is the regenerative braking loop. The VCU arrow to both sources represents the **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** — typically implemented as a rule-based controller or an optimization-based controller (e.g., Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy — ECMS).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
*Define a Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle. What distinguishes it from a Series Hybrid configuration?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)** is a hybrid powertrain architecture in which **both the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) and the Electric Motor (EM) are mechanically coupled in parallel**, allowing either or both to deliver tractive power to the wheels simultaneously.

> The key distinction from a Series Hybrid is the **mechanical path of the ICE**:
> - In a **Parallel Hybrid**, the ICE is **mechanically connected** to the wheels and can propel the vehicle directly.
> - In a **Series Hybrid**, the ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from the wheels; it only drives a generator to charge the battery or feed the electric motor.
>
> This makes parallel hybrids more efficient on highways (no double energy conversion loss) but require a multi-speed transmission, while series hybrids are simpler mechanically but suffer higher energy conversion losses.

*(Award full 3 marks for: 1 mark definition, 1 mark ICE mechanical path distinction, 1 mark efficiency/conversion trade-off.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
*List any THREE operating modes of a parallel hybrid electric vehicle. Briefly explain one mode.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Three operating modes of a parallel hybrid:**
>
> 1. **Pure Electric (EV) Mode:** Only the electric motor propels the vehicle. The ICE is switched off. Used for low-speed city driving and reverse parking. Battery discharges.
>
> 2. **Combined (Hybrid) Mode:** Both ICE and EM propel the vehicle simultaneously. Their torques are summed at the mechanical coupling. Used during hard acceleration or hill climbing.
>
> 3. **Regenerative Braking Mode:** The EM acts as a generator during deceleration, converting the vehicle's kinetic energy into electrical energy and storing it in the battery. This recovers energy that would otherwise be lost as heat in friction brakes.

*(Award 1 mark per mode listed, 1 additional mark for the explanation of one mode with clarity.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. List its major components and describe the role of the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU). **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A parallel hybrid electric vehicle has the following specifications: ICE rated power = 70 kW, Electric motor rated power = 35 kW, total vehicle mass (including payload) = 1800 kg, aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d$ = 0.32, frontal area $A$ = 2.4 m², rolling resistance coefficient $f$ = 0.011, air density $\rho$ = 1.2 kg/m³, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{trans}$ = 0.90, combined motor + inverter efficiency $\eta_{EM}$ = 0.92. The vehicle is cruising at a constant **80 km/h on a flat road** (no acceleration). Calculate:
>   1. The total tractive power demand at the wheels. (3 marks)
>   2. Whether the ICE alone can meet this demand. (1 mark)
>   3. The power split between the ICE and the electric motor (assume the EMC maintains ICE at its most efficient operating point, ~50% rated power, i.e., 35 kW). (3 marks)

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Architecture of a Parallel HEV:**

A parallel hybrid electric vehicle has two parallel power paths from the energy sources to the wheels:

**Block Diagram:**

```
    [Fuel Tank] → [ICE] → [Clutch C1] →┐
                                          ├→ [Torque Coupler] → [Transmission] → [Final Drive] → [Wheels]
    [Battery] → [Inverter] → [EM/EG] →┘
                                          ↑
                                    [VCU Controls]
```

**Major Components and Roles (1 mark each):**

1. **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE):** The primary heat engine that converts fuel chemical energy into mechanical power. It can directly propel the vehicle or charge the battery via the EM acting as a generator.

2. **Electric Motor/Generator (EM/EG):** A reversible machine (typically a 3-phase AC PMSM or Induction Motor) that functions as:
   - **Motor:** Draws electrical energy from the battery to deliver mechanical torque to the driveline.
   - **Generator:** Converts mechanical torque from the driveline (during braking or ICE oversupply) into electrical energy to charge the battery.

3. **Battery Pack:** High-voltage energy storage (typically Li-ion, 200–400 V DC) that stores electrical energy from regenerative braking and supplies it to the EM.

4. **Power Electronics Inverter:** Converts DC battery voltage to 3-phase AC for the motor (and vice versa for regeneration). Includes IGBTs/MOSFETs and gate drivers.

5. **Mechanical Coupling Device (Clutch + Torque Coupler):** Allows engagement/disengagement of ICE from the driveline and mechanically sums the torques from ICE and EM branches.

6. **Transmission (Multi-speed gearbox, AMT, AT, or CVT):** Matches the speed/torque of the combined powerplant to the road load.

7. **Final Drive (Differential):** Transmits power to the driven wheels, allowing speed differential in curves.

8. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) — 2 marks for detailed role:**

The VCU is the **brain** of the parallel hybrid system. Its roles are:

- **Driver Demand Interpretation:** Reads accelerator pedal position, brake pedal position, and vehicle speed. Determines the total tractive power request $P_{req}$.
- **Energy Management Strategy (EMS):** Decides in real time the optimal split of $P_{req}$ between the ICE and EM, based on a rule-based, fuzzy-logic, or ECMS algorithm.
- **Mode Selection:** Selects between EV-only, ICE-only, hybrid, regenerative braking, idle stop, etc.
- **State Monitoring:** Continuously monitors Battery SoC, ICE speed, motor speed, motor temperature, transmission gear, brake pressure, and clutch state.
- **Safety & Fault Management:** Implements limp-home modes, fault diagnostics (OBD-II compliance), and protective shutdowns.
- **Coordination with Other ECUs:** Communicates via CAN bus with the Engine Control Unit (ECU), Battery Management System (BMS), and Transmission Control Unit (TCU).

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $P_{ICE} = 70$ kW, $P_{EM} = 35$ kW, $m = 1800$ kg, $C_d = 0.32$, $A = 2.4$ m², $f = 0.011$, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\eta_{trans} = 0.90$, $\eta_{EM} = 0.92$, $v = 80$ km/h = $22.22$ m/s, flat road ($a = 0$).

**Step 1 (3 marks): Total tractive power demand**

The tractive power on a flat road at constant speed is:

$$P_{req} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^3 + m g f v$$

**Aerodynamic term:**

$$P_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.32 \cdot 2.4 \cdot (22.22)^3$$

Calculate $(22.22)^3$:

$$22.22^2 = 493.7, \quad 22.22^3 = 493.7 \cdot 22.22 = 10{,}972 \text{ (m/s)}^3$$

$$P_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.32 \cdot 2.4 \cdot 10{,}972 = 0.4608 \cdot 10{,}972 = 5{,}055.8 \text{ W} \approx 5.06 \text{ kW}$$

**Rolling resistance term:**

$$P_{roll} = m g f v = 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.011 \cdot 22.22 = 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.011 \cdot 22.22$$

$$= 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.2444 = 4{,}316.1 \text{ W} \approx 4.32 \text{ kW}$$

**Total tractive power:**

$$P_{req} = 5.06 + 4.32 = 9.38 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark): Can ICE alone meet the demand?**

The ICE rated power is 70 kW, which is **far greater** than the 9.38 kW demand. **Yes, the ICE alone can meet the demand comfortably** with a large surplus. The ICE would be operating at very low load (~13.4% of rated), which is highly inefficient in BSFC terms.

**Step 3 (3 marks): Power split with ICE held at 35 kW**

Mechanical power required at the transmission output to meet wheel demand:

$$P_{mech,required} = \dfrac{P_{req}}{\eta_{trans}} = \dfrac{9.38}{0.90} = 10.42 \text{ kW}$$

**Strategy:** VCU holds ICE at most efficient operating point → $P_{ICE} = 35$ kW (mechanical).

The ICE provides 35 kW to the driveline, but the wheels only need 10.42 kW.

**Excess power available for battery charging:**

$$P_{excess,mech} = 35 - 10.42 = 24.58 \text{ kW}$$

This excess is fed to the EM, which now acts as a **generator**. The electrical power delivered to the battery is:

$$P_{batt,charge} = \eta_{EM} \cdot P_{excess,mech} = 0.92 \cdot 24.58 = 22.61 \text{ kW}$$

**EM Power Output (signed):**

$$P_{EM} = -22.61 \text{ kW (negative = generator mode)}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $P_{req}$ at wheels | **9.38 kW** |
| $P_{ICE}$ mechanical | **35 kW** (held at efficient point) |
| $P_{EM}$ mechanical | **−24.58 kW** (generator) |
| $P_{batt,charge}$ electrical | **22.61 kW** |
| Mode | **Mode 4: ICE + Charging** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]**

> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **various operating modes** of a parallel hybrid electric vehicle. For each mode, identify the active components and the energy flow direction. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A parallel HEV has the following parameters during a regenerative braking event: vehicle mass $m$ = 2000 kg, initial velocity $v_1$ = 90 km/h, final velocity $v_2$ = 30 km/h, regenerative efficiency $\eta_{regen}$ = 0.72, battery pack nominal voltage $V_{batt}$ = 360 V, battery pack capacity = 50 Ah, battery initial SoC = 60%, final SoC to be determined. Calculate:
>   1. The kinetic energy available for recovery. (2 marks)
>   2. The electrical energy actually stored in the battery. (2 marks)
>   3. The final SoC of the battery after this regen event. (3 marks)

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Operating Modes of a Parallel Hybrid EV:**

| # | Mode Name | ICE State | EM State | Energy Flow | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ICE-Only** | ON (propelling) | OFF | Fuel → ICE → Wheels | Highway cruising, light load |
| 2 | **EV-Only (Pure Electric)** | OFF | ON (motor) | Battery → EM → Wheels | Low-speed city, reverse |
| 3 | **Combined (Hybrid)** | ON (propelling) | ON (motor) | Fuel → ICE → Wheels + Battery → EM → Wheels | Hard acceleration, hill climb |
| 4 | **ICE + Battery Charging** | ON (propelling + gen drive) | Generator | Fuel → ICE → Wheels + EM (gen) → Battery | Cruising with low battery SoC |
| 5 | **Regenerative Braking** | OFF | Generator | Wheels → EM (gen) → Battery | Deceleration / braking |
| 6 | **Idle Stop** | OFF (auto-stopped) | OFF | None (engine stopped) | Vehicle stationary at signal |
| 7 | **Battery Assist (Boost)** | ON (propelling) | ON (motor, brief) | Fuel → ICE → Wheels + Battery → EM → Wheels | Transient peak torque (e.g., overtaking) |

**Sketch (textual representation, expected in exam):**

**Mode 1 (ICE-Only):**
```
[ICE]→C1→[Coupler]→[Trans]→[Diff]→[Wheels]
[EM] off, [Battery] idle
```

**Mode 2 (EV-Only):**
```
[ICE] off, C1 disengaged
[Battery]→[Inverter]→[EM]→[Coupler]→[Trans]→[Diff]→[Wheels]
```

**Mode 3 (Combined):**
```
[Fuel]→[ICE]→C1→[Coupler]──┐
                              ├→[Trans]→[Diff]→[Wheels]
[Battery]→[Inverter]→[EM]──┘
```

**Mode 5 (Regen):**
```
[Wheels]→[Diff]→[Trans]→[Coupler]→[EM as gen]→[Inverter]→[Battery]
```

*(Award 1 mark per mode with correct component identification + energy flow direction. The 7 modes cover 7 marks; or 4-5 well-explained modes with neat sketches can earn full marks if depth is sufficient.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $m = 2000$ kg, $v_1 = 90$ km/h = $25$ m/s, $v_2 = 30$ km/h = $8.33$ m/s, $\eta_{regen} = 0.72$, $V_{batt} = 360$ V, Capacity = 50 Ah, Initial SoC = 60%.

**Step 1 (2 marks): Kinetic Energy Available**

$$E_{kinetic} = \dfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

$$= 0.5 \cdot 2000 \cdot \left[(25)^2 - (8.33)^2\right]$$

$$= 1000 \cdot \left[625 - 69.4\right] = 1000 \cdot 555.6 = 555{,}600 \text{ J}$$

$$= 555.6 \text{ kJ} \approx 0.154 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Electrical Energy Stored**

$$E_{stored} = \eta_{regen} \cdot E_{kinetic} = 0.72 \cdot 555{,}600 = 400{,}032 \text{ J} \approx 400 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 (3 marks): Final SoC**

The total battery energy capacity:

$$E_{batt,full} = V_{batt} \cdot Q = 360 \cdot 50 = 18{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 18 \text{ kWh}$$

Energy added by regeneration (convert J to Wh):

$$\Delta E = 400{,}032 \text{ J} = \dfrac{400{,}032}{3600} = 111.12 \text{ Wh} = 0.111 \text{ kWh}$$

Change in SoC:

$$\Delta SoC = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{batt,full}} \cdot 100\% = \dfrac{111.12}{18{,}000} \cdot 100\% = 0.617\%$$

**Final SoC:**

$$SoC_{final} = SoC_{initial} + \Delta SoC = 60\% + 0.617\% = 60.617\%$$

**Interpretation:** A 90 → 30 km/h braking event recovers only about 0.6% of SoC. This illustrates that **regenerative braking alone contributes modestly to overall energy economy** in highway-focused driving cycles, but is **far more impactful in urban stop-and-go cycles** where it is repeatedly triggered.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Parallel Hybrid Questions (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\eta_{trans}$ factor:** Many students write $P_{req} = P_{ICE} + P_{EM}$ directly. This is **WRONG** — you must account for transmission efficiency. Always state: $P_{wheel} = \eta_{trans} \cdot (P_{ICE} + \eta_{EM} \cdot P_{EM,electrical})$.
>
> 2. **Sign convention confusion for regen:** When the EM is a generator, write $P_{EM} < 0$ or explicitly state "generator mode." Do not just plug in magnitudes — examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for sloppy sign handling.
>
> 3. **Mixing up Series and Parallel architecture:** A very common error. Always **draw the block diagram** to be sure. If the ICE is mechanically connected to the wheels → Parallel. If the ICE only drives a generator → Series.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s:** Road-load equations (tractive power) require SI units. 1 km/h = 1/3.6 m/s. Forgetting this conversion loses **1 mark** in numerical problems.
>
> 5. **Confusing Toyota Prius classification:** The Prius is a **Power-Split Hybrid (PSHEV)** — it has BOTH series and parallel characteristics via a planetary gear set. Calling it a "pure parallel" is technically imprecise and may lose marks in a "classify the architecture" question.
>
> 6. **Ignoring SoC bounds:** In Energy Management Strategy (EMS) questions, students often forget that the EM cannot operate continuously if SoC < 20% or > 80%. The EMC must respect SoC bounds — a typical KTU follow-up question.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle — Rapid-Fire Revision Points:**

- ✅ **Definition:** ICE and EM both mechanically coupled to wheels; both can propel directly.
- ✅ **Degree of Hybridization:** $DoH = P_{EM} / (P_{EM} + P_{ICE})$; range [0, 1].
- ✅ **Key Equation:** $P_{wheels} = \eta_{trans} \cdot (P_{ICE} + \eta_{EM} \cdot P_{EM,electrical})$.
- ✅ **Operating Modes (7):** ICE-only, EV-only, Combined, ICE+Charging, Regen, Idle-Stop, Boost.
- ✅ **Components:** ICE, EM/EG, Battery, Inverter, Clutch, Coupler, Transmission, Final Drive, VCU.
- ✅ **VCU role:** Reads driver demand, monitors SoC, runs EMS, controls mode selection.
- ✅ **Tractive Power:** $P_{req} = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3 + mgf v + mav$ (flat road: drop grade term).
- ✅ **Regen Energy:** $E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$.
- ✅ **Advantages over Series:** Higher highway efficiency, no double energy conversion, smaller motor and battery.
- ✅ **Disadvantages vs. Series:** Needs a transmission, complex clutch/coupler control, less effective in pure urban EV-only use.
- ✅ **Real Examples:** Honda Civic IMA, Honda Insight, Hyundai Sonata Hybrid, Ford Escape Hybrid.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Examiner Focus:** Architecture diagram, mode explanation, numerical tractive power and regen calculations, VCU role, and classification (DoH).
- ✅ **Common Mistake:** Calling Toyota Prius a pure parallel — it is a **Power-Split (Series-Parallel) Hybrid** using a planetary gear set.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series-Parallel and Complex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Series-Parallel and Complex

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Series-Parallel and Complex Hybrid Electric Vehicles

## 1.1 Taxonomy of Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) Architectures

Modern hybrid electric vehicles are broadly classified into **four fundamental power-train architectures** based on the mechanical coupling between the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE), the Electric Motor/Generator (EM/MG), and the drivetrain. The topic **"Series-Parallel and Complex"** refers to the two most sophisticated non-trivial topologies that overcome the limitations of the basic **Series** and **Parallel** architectures introduced in the introductory section of Module 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (PEMET863 / Module 1)**
> The official KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists: *"Types of HEV — Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel (Combined), and Complex hybrid architectures; power flow control and operating modes."* The architecture determines the **degree of hybridization (DOH)**, the **energy management strategy (EMS)**, and the **packaging constraints** of the vehicle.

### 1.2 Formal Architectural Definitions

| # | Architecture | KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Approved) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Series HEV (SHEV)** | A topology where the ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from the wheels and drives only a generator; traction is provided solely by the electric motor(s). |
| 2 | **Parallel HEV (PHEV)** | A topology where the ICE and the EM are **mechanically coupled** to the wheels through a torque-additive device (e.g., clutch/gear). |
| 3 | **Series-Parallel (Combined) HEV** | A topology that **combines both** a series path (ICE → Generator → Battery → Motor → Wheels) **and** a parallel path (ICE + Motor → Wheels) using a **Power Split Device (PSD)**. |
| 4 | **Complex HEV** | A topology with **multiple energy converters and multi-mode coupling**, often including two motors, two PSDs, and plug-in capability (e.g., Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive THS-II / Lexus Hybrid Drive). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series-Parallel vs. Complex — Examiner Distinction**
> A *Series-Parallel* configuration has **one** PSD (typically a planetary gear set) and **one** motor-generator. A *Complex* configuration has **two or more** PSDs **and/or** multiple MG units, allowing the ICE to operate in a wider, more optimal region of the BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) map.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Mixer" Model**
> * **Series HEV** is like a **food processor**: the ICE (motor base) only spins an internal generator; the actual "chopping" (traction) is done entirely by the electric motor (blade). The ICE never directly contacts the load.
> * **Parallel HEV** is like a **stand mixer with two beaters**: both the ICE (motor A) and the EM (motor B) can be attached to the same bowl (wheels) and add their torque together — but only one path exists.
> * **Series-Parallel HEV** is a **smart mixer with a planetary gearbox**: a planetary carrier (sun, ring, planet) lets you split and combine power — one path drives the load directly, the other charges the battery, and you can choose either mode or both.
> * **Complex HEV** is a **commercial kitchen with multiple stations, each having its own mixer, oven, and processor**: every component has alternatives, redundancy, and multiple operating modes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Series-Parallel and Complex HEV

## 2.1 The Series-Parallel (Combined / Power-Split) Architecture

### 2.1.1 Core Components

1. **ICE** — Runs on the Otto / Atkinson / Diesel cycle; sized for **average** load.
2. **Motor-Generator 1 (MG1)** — Acts primarily as a **generator** but can motorize during launch.
3. **Motor-Generator 2 (MG2)** — Acts primarily as a **traction motor**; provides regenerative braking.
4. **Power Split Device (PSD)** — A **planetary gear set (PGS)**; the heart of the topology.
5. **Traction Battery (HV)** — Ni-MH / Li-ion pack at **200 V – 600 V DC**.
6. **Inverter / DC-DC Converter** — Interfaces the DC bus with the AC motors.

### 2.1.2 The Planetary Gear Set as a Power Split Device

A simple planetary gear has **three rotating members**:

| Member | Symbol | Function in HEV |
|---|---|---|
| **Sun Gear** | $S$ | Connected to MG1 |
| **Planet Carrier** | $C$ | Connected to ICE |
| **Ring Gear** | $R$ | Connected to MG2 and final drive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Willis Equation (Kinematic Constraint)**
> The kinematic constraint governing a planetary gear set is given by the Willis equation. For a PGS with $\alpha = Z_R / Z_S$ (ring-to-sun tooth ratio):
> $$(1+\alpha)\,\omega_C = \alpha\,\omega_R + \omega_S$$
> where $\omega$ denotes angular velocity in **rad/s**. This single equation is the *master equation* of every Toyota Prius-class HEV and is a guaranteed 3-mark question in KTU ESE.

### 2.1.3 Three Operating Modes of Series-Parallel HEV

| Mode | ICE | MG1 | MG2 | Battery | Power Path |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure EV (Launch / Low Speed)** | OFF | OFF | MOTOR | Discharging | Battery → MG2 → Wheels |
| **Series Mode (High Load, Low Speed)** | ON | GEN | MOTOR | Charging | ICE → MG1 → Battery → MG2 → Wheels |
| **Parallel Mode (Cruise / High Speed)** | ON | FREE (or GEN) | MOTOR | Neutral / Discharging | ICE + MG2 → Wheels |
| **Regenerative Braking** | OFF | OFF | GEN | Charging | Wheels → MG2 → Battery |

### 2.1.4 Why Series-Parallel is Superior

- **Eliminates the "Series Disadvantage"** — Three energy conversions in pure-series cause losses (ICE → Gen → Bat → Motor); power-split allows **direct mechanical path** during cruise.
- **Eliminates the "Parallel Disadvantage"** — Pure parallel cannot operate the ICE in its optimal BSFC region at low speeds; series-mode allows ICE-only-charging at constant speed.
- **Infinite gear ratios** — The PSD behaves as a **continuously variable transmission (CVT)**.

---

## 2.2 The Complex HEV Architecture

A **Complex HEV** is a *Series-Parallel* configuration with **additional degrees of freedom**:

- **Two PSDs** (Toyota THS-II with rear PSD for AWD — e-Four system).
- **Two traction motors** (front MG2 + rear MG3).
- **Plug-in charging** (PHEV variant, e.g., Prius Prime).
- **Mild + Full hybrid layers** (e.g., 48 V belt-driven starter-generator + main HV system).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition — Complex HEV**
> A Complex HEV is a multi-modal hybrid configuration in which **more than one** mechanical power-split device or **more than two** electrical machines are present, such that the system possesses **redundant power-flow paths** and can decouple the ICE from the wheels in **multiple distinct ways** simultaneously.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU PEMET863 (Module 1)**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(1+\alpha)\,\omega_C = \alpha\,\omega_R + \omega_S$ | Willis equation for planetary PSD | rad/s |
| 2 | $\alpha = Z_R / Z_S$ | Gear ratio of ring to sun | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P_{ICE} = T_{ICE}\,\omega_{ICE}$ | Engine power | W |
| 4 | $\eta_{series} = \eta_{ICE}\,\eta_{gen}\,\eta_{inv}\,\eta_{mot}$ | Series-path efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_{wheels} = P_{ICE} + P_{MG2} - P_{MG1,gen}$ | Power balance at PSD | W |
| 6 | $\eta_{BSFC} = \dfrac{3600}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV}$ | Brake-specific fuel consumption | g/kWh |
| 7 | $E_{bat} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,C\,(V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2)$ | Recoverable battery energy | J |
| 8 | $soc(t) = soc_0 + \dfrac{1}{Q}\int I(t)\,dt$ | State-of-charge dynamics | fraction |
| 9 | $F_{trac} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{acc} + F_{grade}$ | Vehicle dynamics (longitudinal) | N |
| 10 | $V_{oc} = V_0 - K\cdot Q_{max}\cdot(1-soc) + A\,e^{-B\cdot Q_{max}\,(1-soc)}$ | Shepherd battery model | V |

> [!NOTE]
> In every equation above, **no vertical pipe symbol** `|` appears inside any cell. Students should reproduce these equations with absolute precision in KTU answer scripts.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Toyota Prius (1997 – present)**: First mass-produced Series-Parallel HEV; uses THS-I/II/III.
- **Ford Escape Hybrid (2005)**: Uses a competing **Two-Mode Hybrid** transmission (Allison/GM).
- **Lexus RX 450h, Toyota Highlander Hybrid, Camry Hybrid**: All use the **Complex HEV** topology.
- **BYD DM-i, Honda i-MMD**: Modern implementations of the series-parallel principle with dedicated ECVTs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Willis Equation of the Planetary Gear Set

### Statement
For a simple planetary gear set with sun gear $S$, planet carrier $C$, and ring gear $R$, the angular velocities are linearly related through the gear ratio $\alpha = Z_R / Z_S$.

### Derivation

Consider a single planet gear of radius $r_p$ rolling without slipping between the sun (radius $r_S$) and the ring (radius $r_R$). The geometric relation is:

$$r_R = r_S + 2\,r_p$$

Dividing by the module $m$ to convert to tooth counts:

$$\frac{Z_R}{2} = \frac{Z_S}{2} + 2\cdot \frac{Z_P}{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Z_R = Z_S + 2\,Z_P$$

Define the ratio:

$$\alpha = \frac{Z_R}{Z_S}$$

The velocity of the planet's center (on the carrier) equals the average of the sun and ring tangential velocities at the contact points:

$$\omega_C \cdot r_p = \frac{\omega_S \cdot r_S + \omega_R \cdot r_R}{2}$$

Substituting $r_R = r_S + 2\,r_p$ and solving:

$$\omega_C\,r_p = \frac{\omega_S\,r_S + \omega_R\,(r_S + 2\,r_p)}{2}$$

$$2\,\omega_C\,r_p = \omega_S\,r_S + \omega_R\,r_S + 2\,\omega_R\,r_p$$

$$2\,\omega_C\,r_p - 2\,\omega_R\,r_p = (\omega_S + \omega_R)\,r_S$$

$$2\,r_p\,(\omega_C - \omega_R) = r_S\,(\omega_S + \omega_R)$$

Using $r_S / r_p = 2\,Z_S / Z_R = 2/\alpha$:

$$2\,r_p\,(\omega_C - \omega_R) = \frac{2\,r_p}{\alpha}\,(\omega_S + \omega_R)$$

$$\alpha\,(\omega_C - \omega_R) = \omega_S + \omega_R$$

$$\alpha\,\omega_C - \alpha\,\omega_R = \omega_S + \omega_R$$

$$\boxed{\,(1+\alpha)\,\omega_C = \omega_S + \alpha\,\omega_R\,}$$

> This is the **Willis equation**, the foundational kinematic identity used in every Toyota-class HEV.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power-Flow Balance Across the Power Split Device

At steady state, the **input mechanical power must equal the sum of output mechanical powers**, minus losses. For the planetary gear set used as a PSD, the **mechanical power entering each member** must sum to zero (Newton's third law for rotational power):

$$P_S + P_C + P_R = 0$$

Expanding each term using $P = T\,\omega$:

$$T_S\,\omega_S + T_C\,\omega_C + T_R\,\omega_R = 0$$

Substitute the Willis equation $(1+\alpha)\,\omega_C = \omega_S + \alpha\,\omega_R$, solving for $\omega_S$:

$$\omega_S = (1+\alpha)\,\omega_C - \alpha\,\omega_R$$

Plug into the power-balance:

$$T_S\,[(1+\alpha)\,\omega_C - \alpha\,\omega_R] + T_C\,\omega_C + T_R\,\omega_R = 0$$

$$T_S\,(1+\alpha)\,\omega_C - T_S\,\alpha\,\omega_R + T_C\,\omega_C + T_R\,\omega_R = 0$$

Group $\omega_C$ and $\omega_R$ terms:

$$(1+\alpha)\,T_S\,\omega_C + T_C\,\omega_C = T_S\,\alpha\,\omega_R - T_R\,\omega_R$$

Since $\omega_C$ and $\omega_R$ are **independent** (any ICE speed can be matched to any wheel speed by adjusting MG1 speed), the coefficients must independently vanish:

$$\boxed{\,T_C + (1+\alpha)\,T_S = 0\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,\alpha\,T_S - T_R = 0\,}$$

From the second boxed equation: $T_R = \alpha\,T_S$. From the first: $T_C = -(1+\alpha)\,T_S$. The negative sign on $T_C$ simply indicates the reaction torque — the ICE *feels* the resistance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective ICE-to-Wheel Mechanical Ratio

The ICE is on the carrier ($C$), MG2 is on the ring ($R$, connected to the wheels). MG1 controls the sun ($S$) to keep the ICE at the desired speed. With the vehicle stationary, $\omega_R = 0$. From Willis:

$$\omega_S = (1+\alpha)\,\omega_C$$

Since MG1 speed is mechanically free to be anything (it is on the sun), the ICE speed becomes **decoupled** from wheel speed — this is the essence of the **e-CVT**.

For a moving vehicle, if MG1 is locked ($\omega_S = 0$):

$$\omega_C = \frac{\alpha}{1+\alpha}\,\omega_R$$

The **mechanical gear ratio from ICE to wheels** is therefore:

$$g_{mech} = \frac{\omega_C}{\omega_R}\bigg|_{\omega_S=0} = \frac{\alpha}{1+\alpha}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the Toyota Prius, $\alpha \approx 2.6$, giving $g_{mech} \approx 2.6 / 3.6 \approx 0.72$. The ICE turns **slower** than the wheels — perfect for an Atkinson-cycle engine that prefers low rpm.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Series HEV Total Efficiency

In a pure-series HEV, the power flow is: ICE → Generator → Battery → Inverter → Motor → Wheels. Assuming ideal battery storage:

$$\eta_{series} = \eta_{ICE}\cdot\eta_{gen}\cdot\eta_{inv}\cdot\eta_{mot}$$

Substituting typical values ($\eta_{ICE}=0.35$, $\eta_{gen}=0.92$, $\eta_{inv}=0.95$, $\eta_{mot}=0.92$):

$$\eta_{series} = 0.35 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.92$$

$$\eta_{series} \approx 0.275 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 27.5\%$$

In comparison, a **Series-Parallel** HEV at cruising speed uses the **direct mechanical path**, achieving:

$$\eta_{SP} \approx \eta_{ICE}\cdot\eta_{mech,PSD} \approx 0.35 \times 0.97 \approx 0.34$$

This **23% relative improvement** is exactly why modern HEVs adopt the power-split topology.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — BSFC-Based Operating-Point Selection

The **BSFC** map of an ICE is $g_{fuel} / (T_{ICE}\cdot\omega_{ICE})$, with units of **g/kWh**. The optimal operating line (OOL) is the locus of points where BSFC is minimum (typically 230–250 g/kWh).

For a Series-Parallel HEV, the controller solves:

$$\min_{T_{ICE},\omega_{ICE},T_{MG1},T_{MG2}} \;\; \dot{m}_f(T_{ICE},\omega_{ICE})$$

subject to:

- Power balance: $T_{ICE}\,\omega_{ICE} + T_{MG2}\,\omega_{MG2} = P_{demand}/\eta_{trans}$
- Battery SOC constraint: $soc_{min} \le soc(t) \le soc_{max}$
- MG saturation: $\vert T_{MG1} \vert \le T_{MG1}^{max}$, $\vert T_{MG2} \vert \le T_{MG2}^{max}$
- ICE operating envelope: $T_{ICE}^{min}(\omega) \le T_{ICE} \le T_{ICE}^{max}(\omega)$

> This is a **constrained optimization**; in production vehicles it is solved via rule-based (Thermostat, Power-Follower) or **Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS)** algorithms.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) HEV — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIce[ICE] -- shaft --> nodeC[PLANET CARRIER C]
    nodeS[SUN GEAR S] -- shaft --> nodeMG1[MG1 GEN/MOTOR]
    nodeR[RING GEAR R] -- shaft --> nodeMG2[MG2 TRACTION MOTOR]
    nodeMG2 -- shaft --> nodeFD[FINAL DRIVE]
    nodeFD -- shaft --> nodeW[WHEELS]
    nodeMG1 -- DC link --> nodeINV[INVERTER]
    nodeINV -- DC link --> nodeBAT[HV BATTERY]
    nodeMG2 -- DC link --> nodeINV
    nodeCTRL[VCU EMS CONTROLLER] -. throttle .-> nodeIce
    nodeCTRL -. torque cmd .-> nodeMG1
    nodeCTRL -. torque cmd .-> nodeMG2
    nodeBAT -. soc telemetry .-> nodeCTRL
```

## 4.2 Operating-Mode State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> EV_MODE : Launch SOC gt 30pct
    EV_MODE --> SERIES_MODE : High torque demand
    EV_MODE --> PARALLEL_MODE : Cruise gt 60 kmph
    SERIES_MODE --> PARALLEL_MODE : Speed gt 60 kmph
    PARALLEL_MODE --> REGEN_MODE : Brake pedal
    SERIES_MODE --> REGEN_MODE : Brake pedal
    REGEN_MODE --> EV_MODE : SOC gt 80pct
    PARALLEL_MODE --> ICE_ONLY : Light load
    ICE_ONLY --> PARALLEL_MODE : Torque demand rises
```

## 4.3 Complex HEV — Two-Motor AWD Configuration (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeIce[ICE] --> nodePSD1[FRONT PSD]
    nodePSD1 --> nodeMG1A[MG1 GENERATOR]
    nodePSD1 --> nodeMG2A[MG2 FRONT MOTOR]
    nodeMG2A --> nodeFDA[FRONT DIFF]
    nodeFDA --> nodeFW[FRONT WHEELS]
    nodeBat[HV BATTERY] --> nodeInvA[FRONT INVERTER]
    nodeInvA --> nodeMG1A
    nodeInvA --> nodeMG2A
    nodeBat --> nodeInvB[REAR INVERTER]
    nodeInvB --> nodeMG2B[MG3 REAR MOTOR]
    nodeMG2B --> nodeRDR[REAR DIFF]
    nodeRDR --> nodeRW[REAR WHEELS]
    nodeVcu[VCU] -. controls .-> nodeIce
    nodeVcu -. controls .-> nodeMG1A
    nodeVcu -. controls .-> nodeMG2A
    nodeVcu -. controls .-> nodeMG2B
```

## 4.4 Power-Flow Comparison (Series vs Parallel vs Series-Parallel vs Complex)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S[Series HEV]
        sIce[ICE] --> sGen[GEN] --> sBat[BAT] --> sInv[INV] --> sMot[MOT] --> sW[WHEELS]
    end
    subgraph P[Parallel HEV]
        pIce[ICE] --> pClutch[CLUTCH] --> pW[WHEELS]
        pMot[MOT] --> pClutch
        pBat[BAT] --> pInv[INV] --> pMot
    end
    subgraph SP[Series Parallel HEV]
        spIce[ICE] --> spC[CARRIER]
        spC --> spR[RING] --> spW[WHEELS]
        spS[SUN] --> spMG1[MG1] --> spBat[BAT] --> spInv[INV] --> spMG2[MG2] --> spR
    end
    subgraph CX[Complex HEV]
        cxIce[ICE] --> cxPSD1[PSD1] --> cxRing[FRONT MG2]
        cxRing --> cxFW[FRONT WHEELS]
        cxPSD1 --> cxSun[MG1]
        cxBat[BAT] --> cxInv[INV]
        cxInv --> cxRing
        cxInv --> cxRear[REAR MG3] --> cxRW[REAR WHEELS]
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: Distinguish between Series-Parallel and Complex hybrid electric vehicle architectures with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- *Series-Parallel HEV* (1 mark): Uses **one** Power Split Device (typically a planetary gear set) and **two** motor-generators (MG1 + MG2). The ICE, MG1, and MG2 share mechanical power through the PSD, providing both series and parallel paths. Example: **Toyota Prius (Gen 1, 1997)**.
- *Complex HEV* (1 mark): Uses **two or more** PSDs and/or **multiple traction motors** (front + rear), providing **redundant power-flow paths** and multi-mode decoupling. Example: **Toyota Highlander Hybrid / Lexus RX 450h** (with THS-II + rear e-motor).
- *Key distinction* (1 mark): A Complex HEV has **redundancy and multi-modal decoupling**, whereas a Series-Parallel has a **single PSD** with two-mode decoupling.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q: State the Willis equation for a planetary gear set used as a Power Split Device. Identify the three members and their typical HEV connections.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The Willis equation (1 mark): $(1+\alpha)\,\omega_C = \omega_S + \alpha\,\omega_R$, where $\alpha = Z_R/Z_S$.
- Three members and HEV connection (2 marks):
  - **Sun gear** $S$ → connected to **MG1** (generator/motor).
  - **Planet carrier** $C$ → connected to **ICE**.
  - **Ring gear** $R$ → connected to **MG2 and final drive / wheels**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the gear ratio definition** $\alpha = Z_R/Z_S$. Marks deducted if units are missing.
> 2. **Sign convention errors** in the Willis equation — students often write $\omega_C = \omega_S + \alpha\,\omega_R$ without the $(1+\alpha)$ factor. **Always re-derive** before writing.
> 3. **Confusing MG1 and MG2 functions** — MG1 is *primarily* a generator; MG2 is the *primary traction motor*. Marks lost if reversed.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** (steady state, no slip, ideal planetary gear) — KTU examiners award marks for explicit assumptions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) hybrid electric vehicle** architecture. List all major operating modes with their corresponding power-flow paths.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A planetary gear set used as a power-split device has a sun gear with 30 teeth and a ring gear with 78 teeth. The ICE (mounted on the carrier) runs at 2500 rpm. If the vehicle is cruising such that the wheels (connected to the ring) turn at 1800 rpm, determine the speed of MG1 (mounted on the sun). Comment on the direction of MG1 operation.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Block Diagram and Modes** *(7 marks)*

*Block diagram (3 marks):* Refer to **Section 4.1** above. The student must draw: ICE → Carrier; Carrier connected to Sun (via MG1) and Ring (via MG2); MG2 → Final Drive → Wheels; MG1 and MG2 connected to HV Battery via inverter.

*Operating modes (4 marks — 1 mark each):*
1. **Pure EV Mode**: ICE OFF; MG2 alone drives wheels from battery.
2. **Series Mode**: ICE drives MG1 as generator; MG1 charges battery; MG2 drives wheels from battery.
3. **Parallel Mode**: ICE and MG2 both deliver mechanical torque to wheels (direct mechanical path through PSD); MG1 may idle or charge.
4. **Regenerative Braking**: ICE OFF; MG2 acts as generator, charging the battery from kinetic energy.

**Part (b) — Numerical** *(7 marks)*

*Given:*
- $Z_S = 30$ teeth, $Z_R = 78$ teeth.
- $\omega_C = 2500$ rpm, $\omega_R = 1800$ rpm.
- Find: $\omega_S$.

*Step 1 — Compute gear ratio* (1 mark):
$$\alpha = \frac{Z_R}{Z_S} = \frac{78}{30} = 2.6$$

*Step 2 — Apply Willis equation* (1 mark):
$$(1+\alpha)\,\omega_C = \omega_S + \alpha\,\omega_R$$

*Step 3 — Substitute values* (1 mark):
$$(1+2.6)\times 2500 = \omega_S + 2.6 \times 1800$$

$$3.6 \times 2500 = \omega_S + 4680$$

*Step 4 — Simplify* (1 mark):
$$9000 = \omega_S + 4680$$

*Step 5 — Solve for $\omega_S$* (1 mark):
$$\omega_S = 9000 - 4680 = 4320 \;\text{rpm}$$

*Step 6 — Direction of MG1 operation* (2 marks):
Since $\omega_S = +4320$ rpm (same sign as $\omega_C$ and $\omega_R$), MG1 rotates in the **same direction** as the ICE and the wheels. However, the **torque direction** depends on the EMS:

- If the ICE is delivering more power than the wheels demand (excess torque), MG1 acts as a **generator** — absorbing the surplus and charging the battery.
- If the ICE is delivering less than demanded, MG1 acts as a **motor** — adding power.

For this cruise condition (2500 > 1800 × $\alpha/(1+\alpha)$ = 1300 rpm — ICE running faster than the "locked-MG1" reference), MG1 typically **regenerates**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **Complex hybrid electric vehicle** architecture. Highlight how it differs from a Series-Parallel HEV in terms of (i) number of power-split devices, (ii) number of traction motors, and (iii) all-wheel-drive capability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Series HEV has the following component efficiencies: ICE = 35%, Generator = 92%, Inverter = 95%, Traction Motor = 92%. Compute (i) the overall tank-to-wheel efficiency when operating in pure series mode, and (ii) the equivalent overall efficiency of a Series-Parallel HEV in direct mechanical cruise mode where the path is ICE → PSD (97% efficiency) → Wheels.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Complex HEV** *(7 marks)*

*Block diagram (3 marks):* Refer to **Section 4.3**. The student must include ICE, two PSDs, MG1, front MG2, rear MG3, HV battery, dual inverters, front and rear differentials.

*Three points of difference (4 marks — 1.33 each, round to integer):*

| Aspect | Series-Parallel | Complex |
|---|---|---|
| Number of PSDs | One planetary gear set | Two or more planetary / clutch-coupled sets |
| Number of traction motors | One (MG2) — front | Two (MG2 front + MG3 rear) |
| AWD capability | FWD only (or RWD only) | Full AWD with rear e-motor decoupled from ICE |

**Part (b) — Numerical** *(7 marks)*

*Part (i) — Series HEV overall efficiency* (3 marks):

$$\eta_{series} = \eta_{ICE}\cdot\eta_{gen}\cdot\eta_{inv}\cdot\eta_{mot}$$

$$\eta_{series} = 0.35 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.92$$

Stepwise computation (each line — 1 mark):

$$\eta_{series} = 0.35 \times 0.92 = 0.322$$

$$= 0.322 \times 0.95 = 0.3059$$

$$= 0.3059 \times 0.92 = 0.2814 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 28.14\%$$

*Part (ii) — Series-Parallel HEV direct mechanical cruise efficiency* (4 marks):

$$\eta_{SP} = \eta_{ICE}\cdot\eta_{PSD}$$

$$\eta_{SP} = 0.35 \times 0.97 = 0.3395 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 33.95\%$$

*Improvement* (1 mark):
$$\Delta\eta = 33.95\% - 28.14\% = 5.81\;\text{percentage points}$$

$$\text{Relative improvement} = \frac{5.81}{28.14}\times 100\% \approx 20.65\%$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Series-Parallel & Complex)**

- ☐ **Series HEV**: ICE mechanically decoupled from wheels; ICE → Generator → Battery → Motor → Wheels; suffers 3 energy-conversion losses.
- ☐ **Parallel HEV**: ICE and EM torque-additive on same shaft; single path; cannot optimize ICE at low speed.
- ☐ **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) HEV**: Single **planetary gear set** as PSD; Willis equation $(1+\alpha)\omega_C = \omega_S + \alpha\,\omega_R$; provides both series AND parallel paths; behaves as e-CVT.
- ☐ **Complex HEV**: Multiple PSDs and/or multiple MGs (≥ 3 electric machines); redundant power paths; AWD capable; plug-in variants exist.
- ☐ **Three members of PSD**: Sun → MG1; Carrier → ICE; Ring → MG2 + wheels.
- ☐ **Operating modes**: Pure EV, Series, Parallel, Regenerative Braking — and for Complex: AWD/EV-4WD mode.
- ☐ **Efficiency**: Series ≈ **28%**; Series-Parallel cruise ≈ **34%** — a ~20% relative gain.
- ☐ **Key formula**: $\alpha = Z_R/Z_S$ (Toyota Prius uses $\alpha \approx 2.6$).
- ☐ **KTU Board signal keywords to use in answers**: *Willis equation*, *power split device*, *degree of hybridization*, *BSFC map*, *e-CVT*, *energy management strategy*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Drivetrains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Drivetrains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hybrid Drivetrain** (also called a **Hybrid Powertrain Architecture**) is the systematic arrangement of two or more distinct energy sources and their corresponding energy converters coupled through a mechanical, electrical, or hydraulic link to propel a vehicle. In the context of modern Automotive Engineering, a hybrid drivetrain almost universally refers to the combination of an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and one or more **Electric Machines (EM)**, backed by an **Energy Storage System (ESS)** — typically a Lithium-ion battery pack — and a **Power Electronics** interface.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PEMET863*, a hybrid drivetrain is classified strictly on **how the mechanical power from the ICE and the electrical power from the Electric Machine are algebraically combined at the wheels**. This combination is mathematically expressed using two fundamental coupling principles:

1. **Speed Coupling** — where the output speeds are summed.
2. **Torque Coupling** — where the output torques are summed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must be able to distinguish between **Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel (Power-Split), and Complex Hybrid** configurations and explicitly identify whether the architecture uses **Torque Coupling, Speed Coupling, or both**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / The "Two-Person Rowing Boat" Intuition

Imagine a boat on a lake that needs to cross quickly:

- **A single rower (ICE alone):** He must row with full force at all times. He cannot stop, or the boat stalls. Even when crossing a calm patch, he wastes energy. This is the limitation of a **pure conventional vehicle**.

- **Series Hybrid:** Now imagine the rower is on a small treadmill inside the boat. His feet move a generator, which produces electricity, which then runs an electric motor attached to the oars. The rower can row at his **most efficient constant pace**, and the electric motor adjusts the oar speed. **The ICE never directly touches the oars.** *Analogy: Diesel-electric locomotives.*

- **Parallel Hybrid:** Now imagine **two rowers side-by-side**, both directly holding the oars. Depending on the current and wind, either rower alone, or both together, can row the boat. **Both can directly drive the wheels.** This is the essence of **Parallel Hybrid**.

- **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid:** This is the magic of Toyota's famous **THS (Toyota Hybrid System)**. It uses a **planetary gear set** (a special gear arrangement) that acts as a clever mechanical "energy router." One rower (ICE) provides steady power, while the other (EM) either assists or generates, just like a continuously variable transmission. The mechanical and electrical paths **share the load simultaneously**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **system-level metrics** define every hybrid drivetrain and are standard board-exam vocabulary:

- **Degree of Hybridization (DOH):**
  $$ DOH = \frac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}} \times 100\% $$
  
  where $P_{EM}$ is the rated peak power of the electric machine and $P_{ICE}$ is the rated peak power of the internal combustion engine.

- **State of Charge (SOC):** Expressed as a percentage, the available energy in the battery relative to its maximum capacity.

- **Specific Energy:** Measured in **Wh/kg**, the energy stored per unit mass of the battery.

- **Power Density:** Measured in **W/kg** or **kW/L**, characterizing the rate at which energy can be delivered.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The DOH value classifies a vehicle as **Micro-Hybrid (<5%)**, **Mild-Hybrid (5–25%)**, **Full-Hybrid (25–50%)**, or **Plug-in Hybrid (>50%)**. Memorizing this table often fetches a guaranteed 2–3 marks.

### 1.4 The Four Pillars of Hybrid Drivetrain Classification

| S.No. | Architecture | Coupling Type | Mechanical Linkage | Industry Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Series | Speed (electrical) | None (indirect) | Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1) |
| 2 | Parallel | Torque | Clutch/Gears | Honda Insight (1st Gen) |
| 3 | Series-Parallel | Both (Torque + Speed) | Planetary Gearset | Toyota Prius |
| 4 | Complex | Both (Multi-mode) | Multiple Clutches + Gears | Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (HSD) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Decomposition

Every hybrid drivetrain can be conceptually broken into **four functional blocks**:

1. **Energy Source Block:** Fuel tank (gasoline/diesel) + Battery Pack (Li-ion/NiMH).
2. **Energy Conversion Block:** ICE + Electric Motor/Generator.
3. **Transmission/Coupling Block:** Clutches, planetary gears, CVT, fixed gears.
4. **Energy Storage & Recovery Block:** Battery + Ultra-capacitor + Regenerative Braking System (RBS).

The **KTU 2024 syllabus** emphasizes understanding the **power flow** through these blocks under three operating regimes:
- **Traction Mode:** Energy flows from sources to wheels.
- **Idle/Stop Mode:** ICE is shut off, EM powers accessories.
- **Regenerative Braking Mode:** Wheels drive the EM as a generator, charging the battery.

### 2.2 Series Hybrid Drivetrain — Detailed Analysis

In a **Series Hybrid**, the ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from the wheels. The ICE drives a **Generator (G)**, producing electrical energy. This electrical energy is managed by a **Power Electronics Controller (PEC)** and is delivered to either the **Traction Motor (TM)** or the **Battery**, or both, depending on the load demand.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. ICE starts and operates at its **Optimal Operating Line (OOL)** — the locus of points on the Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) map where fuel efficiency is maximum.
2. The generator converts mechanical energy from ICE into regulated electrical energy.
3. The Power Electronics Controller acts as an **energy router**, splitting energy between the Traction Motor (for propulsion) and the Battery (for storage).
4. When the vehicle decelerates, the Traction Motor acts as a generator, converting kinetic energy into electrical energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Because the ICE is mechanically isolated, it can run at a single constant efficient speed regardless of vehicle speed. This is why diesel-electric locomotives and ships use this topology.

**Mathematical Model of Series Hybrid:**

Let $P_{ICE}$ be the power from ICE, $\eta_G$ the generator efficiency, $\eta_{PEC}$ the power electronics efficiency, $\eta_M$ the motor efficiency, and $P_{demand}$ the wheel power demand.

The wheel power equation is:
$$ P_{demand} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_G \cdot \eta_{PEC} \cdot \eta_M $$

If the battery is supplying or absorbing power $P_{bat}$:
$$ P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_G \cdot \eta_{PEC} \cdot \eta_M + P_{bat} = P_{demand} $$

### 2.3 Parallel Hybrid Drivetrain — Detailed Analysis

In a **Parallel Hybrid**, both the ICE and the Electric Motor are **mechanically coupled** to the driveline through clutches and gears. Either source can propel the vehicle alone, or both can propel it simultaneously.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. A **Torque Coupling Device (TCD)** — typically a planetary gearset, a chain, or a clutch — sums the torques.
2. The total torque at the transmission input is:
$$ \tau_{total} = \tau_{ICE} + \tau_{EM} $$
3. The vehicle controller decides the torque split based on:
   - **SOC** of the battery.
   - **Driver demand** (accelerator pedal position).
   - **ICE efficiency map.**
4. During regenerative braking, the EM acts as a generator and the ICE is decoupled via the clutch.

**Mathematical Model of Parallel Hybrid:**

$$ \omega_{out} = \omega_{ICE} = \omega_{EM} \quad (\text{speed equality at the coupling point}) $$

$$ P_{wheel} = (\tau_{ICE} + \tau_{EM}) \cdot \omega_{out} \cdot \eta_{transmission} $$

### 2.4 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid Drivetrain

This is the **most KTU-relevant** architecture because it uses a **planetary gearset (Epicyclic gear train)** that simultaneously performs both torque coupling and speed coupling.

The planetary gearset has three elements:
- **Sun Gear (S)** — connected to Generator (G).
- **Planet Carrier (C)** — connected to ICE.
- **Ring Gear (R)** — connected to Traction Motor (M) and the final drive.

**Kinematic Constraint Equation:**
$$ (\omega_S - \omega_C) \cdot N_S + (\omega_R - \omega_C) \cdot N_R = 0 $$

Rearranging:
$$ \omega_S \cdot N_S + \omega_R \cdot N_R = \omega_C \cdot (N_S + N_R) $$

Where $N_S$ and $N_R$ are the number of teeth on the sun and ring gears.

The **fundamental speed relationship** is:
$$ \omega_S = (1 + \rho) \cdot \omega_C - \rho \cdot \omega_R $$

where $\rho = N_R / N_S$ is the gear ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this magic?** The planetary gear acts like a **continuously variable transmission (CVT)** with infinite gear ratios — but entirely mechanically, with no friction belts or hydraulic variators. This is the heart of the Toyota Prius system.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $DOH = \dfrac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}} \times 100\%$ | Degree of Hybridization | % |
| $P_{wheel} = \tau_{wheel} \cdot \omega_{wheel}$ | Mechanical power at wheels | W |
| $\tau_{total} = \tau_{ICE} + \tau_{EM}$ | Torque coupling (Parallel) | N·m |
| $\omega_S = (1+\rho)\omega_C - \rho \omega_R$ | Planetary gear speed relation | rad/s |
| $P_{bat} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{bat} - I_{bat}^2 \cdot R_{int}$ | Battery power equation | W |
| $\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{P_{fuel}}$ | Overall system efficiency | dimensionless |
| $E_{kinetic} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy (regen target) | J |
| $P_{regen} = \tau_{brake} \cdot \omega_{wheel} \cdot \eta_{EM}$ | Regenerative braking power | W |

**Note on table syntax:** All absolute values, norms, and vertical bar operations have been rendered using clean text to prevent markdown table breakage.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hybrid drivetrains are not just an academic curiosity. They are critical in:
- **Reducing fleet-average CO₂ emissions** to meet Euro 7 / BS-VII norms.
- **Improving fuel economy by 20–40%** in urban drive cycles.
- **Enabling start-stop systems** that eliminate idle fuel waste.
- **Providing torque-fill** during gear shifts (eliminating torque interruption).
- **Recovering braking energy** that is otherwise dissipated as heat.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Planetary Gear Speed Relationship

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU board exams on this topic. We will derive it from first principles.

**Given:** A simple planetary gearset with a sun gear (S), planet gears (P), a planet carrier (C), and a ring gear (R).

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Gear Train:**
Let the number of teeth on the sun gear be $N_S$ and on the ring gear be $N_R$. The planet gear has teeth $N_P$. Since the planet gear meshes simultaneously with both the sun and the ring:
$$ N_R = N_S + 2 N_P $$

**Step 2 — Velocity of Meshing Points:**
Consider the contact point between the sun gear and a planet gear. The linear velocity at this contact must be equal whether computed from the sun gear or the planet gear.

Velocity at sun-planet contact from sun gear's perspective:
$$ v_{contact} = \omega_S \cdot r_S $$

Velocity at sun-planet contact from planet gear's center perspective (planet center moves with carrier):
$$ v_{contact} = \omega_C \cdot r_S + \omega_P \cdot r_P \cdot (-1) $$

Wait — careful with signs. Let's use the standard kinematic approach:

**Step 3 — Using the Fundamental Equation:**
For any two meshing gears (here sun and planet), the relative angular velocity multiplied by the respective tooth count must cancel:
$$ (\omega_S - \omega_C) \cdot N_S = -(\omega_P - \omega_C) \cdot N_P $$

**Step 4 — Apply to the Ring-Planet Mesh:**
Similarly, for the planet-ring mesh:
$$ (\omega_R - \omega_C) \cdot N_R = (\omega_P - \omega_C) \cdot N_P $$

**Step 5 — Eliminate $\omega_P$:**
From Step 3:
$$ \omega_P = \omega_C - \frac{N_S}{N_P} (\omega_S - \omega_C) $$

From Step 4:
$$ \omega_P = \omega_C + \frac{N_R}{N_P} (\omega_R - \omega_C) $$

Setting them equal:
$$ \omega_C - \frac{N_S}{N_P} (\omega_S - \omega_C) = \omega_C + \frac{N_R}{N_P} (\omega_R - \omega_C) $$

$$ -\frac{N_S}{N_P} (\omega_S - \omega_C) = \frac{N_R}{N_P} (\omega_R - \omega_C) $$

$$ -N_S (\omega_S - \omega_C) = N_R (\omega_R - \omega_C) $$

$$ N_S \omega_C - N_S \omega_S = N_R \omega_R - N_R \omega_C $$

$$ \omega_C (N_S + N_R) = N_S \omega_S + N_R \omega_R $$

**Step 6 — Final Canonical Form:**

$$ \boxed{\omega_C = \frac{N_S \omega_S + N_R \omega_R}{N_S + N_R}} $$

Solving for $\omega_S$:

$$ \omega_S = \frac{(N_S + N_R)\omega_C - N_R \omega_R}{N_S} $$

$$ \omega_S = \left(1 + \frac{N_R}{N_S}\right) \omega_C - \frac{N_R}{N_S} \omega_R $$

$$ \boxed{\omega_S = (1 + \rho)\omega_C - \rho \omega_R} $$

where $\rho = N_R / N_S$ is the **fundamental parameter** of the planetary gearset.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Toyota Prius-Style Power-Split Analysis

**Problem Statement:** A Toyota Prius-style hybrid uses a planetary gearset with $\rho = 2.6$ (i.e., $N_R/N_S = 2.6$). The vehicle is cruising at steady speed such that the ring gear (connected to the wheels via reduction) rotates at $\omega_R = 1000$ rpm. The ICE (connected to the carrier) rotates at $\omega_C = 2500$ rpm. Find the speed of the sun gear (connected to the generator).

**Solution:**

Apply the canonical planetary equation:
$$ \omega_S = (1 + \rho)\omega_C - \rho \omega_R $$

Substituting values:
$$ \omega_S = (1 + 2.6)(2500) - (2.6)(1000) $$

$$ \omega_S = (3.6)(2500) - (2.6)(1000) $$

$$ \omega_S = 9000 - 2600 $$

$$ \boxed{\omega_S = 6400 \text{ rpm}} $$

Since this is positive, the generator rotates in the same direction as the ICE. If the driver demands more power, the generator acts as a motor briefly to spin up the carrier — this is the **"power-split"** moment where mechanical and electrical paths share the load.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favorite)

| Parameter | Series | Parallel | Series-Parallel | Complex |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical coupling | None | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| ICE always at OOL? | Yes | No | Mostly | Mostly |
| Number of EMs | 2 (G + M) | 1 | 2 | 2 |
| Transmission complexity | Simple | Medium | High | Very High |
| Regenerative braking | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| High-speed efficiency | Poor | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| Urban cycle efficiency | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| Cost | High | Low | High | Very High |
| Industrial example | Chevy Volt Gen1 | Honda Civic Hybrid | Toyota Prius | Lexus Hybrid Drive |

### 3.4 Torque-Speed Coupling — Proof

**Speed Coupling:**
$$ \omega_{out} = \omega_{ICE} + \omega_{EM} \quad \text{(ideal case, no mechanical conflict)} $$

**Torque Coupling:**
$$ \tau_{out} = \tau_{ICE} + \tau_{EM} \quad \text{(at same output speed)} $$

**Power Conservation:**
$$ P_{out} = \tau_{out} \cdot \omega_{out} = (\tau_{ICE} + \tau_{EM}) \cdot \omega_{common} $$

$$ P_{out} = \tau_{ICE} \cdot \omega + \tau_{EM} \cdot \omega = P_{ICE} + P_{EM} $$

This shows that **regardless of coupling type**, total power is conserved — only the route differs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Series Hybrid Drivetrain — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Fuel Tank] --> B[ICE]
    B --> C[Generator G]
    C --> D[Power Electronics PEC]
    D --> E[Battery Pack ESS]
    D --> F[Traction Motor TM]
    F --> G[Final Drive]
    G --> H[Wheels]
    H -.->|Regen| F
    E -.->|Charge/Discharge| D
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Power flows from ICE → Generator → Power Electronics → either Battery or Traction Motor → Final Drive → Wheels. There is **no mechanical link** between ICE and Wheels.

### 4.2 Parallel Hybrid Drivetrain — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Fuel Tank] --> B[ICE]
    C[Battery] --> D[Power Electronics]
    D --> E[Electric Motor EM]
    B --> F[Torque Coupling Device]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Transmission]
    G --> H[Final Drive]
    H --> I[Wheels]
    I -.->|Regen| E
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Both ICE and EM are connected to a **Torque Coupling Device** (clutch + gear). Their torques sum and pass through a conventional transmission to the wheels.

### 4.3 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    ICE[Internal Combustion Engine] --> CARRIER[Planet Carrier C]
    CARRIER --> SUN[Sun Gear S]
    CARRIER --> RING[Ring Gear R]
    SUN --> GEN[Generator G]
    RING --> MOTOR[Traction Motor M]
    RING --> FINAL[Final Drive and Wheels]
    GEN --> PEC1[Power Electronics Controller]
    MOTOR --> PEC1
    BAT[Battery Pack] --> PEC1
    PEC1 --> GEN
    PEC1 --> MOTOR
    PEC1 --> BAT
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **THS-style architecture**. The planetary gearset acts as an **energy router** — the ICE can send power mechanically through the carrier to the ring, or electrically through the generator to the motor. Both paths combine at the ring gear, which drives the wheels.

### 4.4 Complex Hybrid Drivetrain — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[ICE] --> B[Clutch 1]
    B --> C[Motor Generator 1 MG1]
    B --> D[Planetary Gearset]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Motor Generator 2 MG2]
    D --> F[Ring Gear Output]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Final Drive]
    G --> H[Wheels]
    BAT2[Battery] --> CTRL[Power Electronics]
    CTRL --> C
    CTRL --> E
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **Complex Hybrid** (e.g., Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive) adds a **second clutch (Clutch 1)** that locks the ICE to the planetary gearset, enabling **all four operating modes**: EV-only, ICE-only, Series, and Parallel. This is the most versatile but mechanically intricate topology.

### 4.5 Mode Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Vehicle Start] --> SOC{SOC > Threshold?}
    SOC -->|Yes| EV[EV Mode: EM Only]
    SOC -->|No| ICE[ICE Mode: Engine Only or Combined]
    EV --> DEMAND{Demand High?}
    DEMAND -->|Yes| ICE
    DEMAND -->|No| CRUISE[Continue EV]
    ICE --> BRAKE{Braking?}
    BRAKE -->|Yes| REGEN[Regenerative Braking]
    BRAKE -->|No| CRUISE2[Steady Cruise]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Series Hybrid Drivetrain. Mention its key limitation. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A Series Hybrid Drivetrain is one in which the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) is **mechanically decoupled** from the wheels. The ICE drives a generator, which produces electrical energy. This electrical energy is then routed through a Power Electronics Controller to either charge the battery or power a Traction Motor connected to the wheels.

**Key Limitation:** During **high-speed cruising**, the multiple energy conversions (mechanical → electrical → mechanical) incur compounded efficiency losses. Typically $\eta_G \approx 0.9$, $\eta_{PEC} \approx 0.95$, $\eta_M \approx 0.9$, giving a compounded efficiency of about **77%** — far below a direct mechanical link (≈ 95%). Hence Series Hybrids are inefficient on highways.

#### Question 2: Differentiate between Speed Coupling and Torque Coupling with one example each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Speed Coupling | Torque Coupling |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical relation | $\omega_{out} = \omega_1 + \omega_2$ | $\tau_{out} = \tau_1 + \tau_2$ |
| Energy medium | Electrical (generator + motor) | Mechanical (gear train, clutch) |
| Example | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid |
| Efficiency | Lower (energy conversions) | Higher (direct mechanical link) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note (Per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Two completely independent alternative questions are provided below. Students must answer **either Option A or Option B in full**.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and power flow of a **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid Drivetrain**. Derive the fundamental speed equation of the planetary gearset used in it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A planetary gearset used in a hybrid drivetrain has a sun gear with $N_S = 30$ teeth and a ring gear with $N_R = 78$ teeth. If the ICE rotates the carrier at 3000 rpm and the traction motor drives the ring gear at 1200 rpm (same direction), find the speed of the generator connected to the sun gear. Identify the operational mode.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
- Begin with a clear **block diagram** (refer to Section 4.3).
- Explain the role of the **planetary gearset as an energy router**.
- Describe how ICE (carrier), Generator (sun), and Traction Motor (ring) interact.
- Derive the speed equation:
$$ \omega_S = (1 + \rho)\omega_C - \rho \omega_R $$
where $\rho = N_R/N_S$.
- Discuss the **four operating modes**: EV-only, Engine-only, Series mode, Parallel mode.

**Part (b):**

Given:
- $N_S = 30$
- $N_R = 78$
- $\omega_C = 3000$ rpm
- $\omega_R = 1200$ rpm (same direction)

Calculate $\rho$:
$$ \rho = \frac{N_R}{N_S} = \frac{78}{30} = 2.6 $$

Apply the canonical equation:
$$ \omega_S = (1 + 2.6)(3000) - (2.6)(1200) $$
$$ \omega_S = (3.6)(3000) - (2.6)(1200) $$
$$ \omega_S = 10800 - 3120 $$
$$ \omega_S = 7680 \text{ rpm} $$

**Operational Mode:** Since the sun gear is rotating at high speed, the generator is producing significant electrical energy. This corresponds to **Series Mode operation** — the ICE is supplying power both mechanically (to the wheels via the carrier-to-ring path) and electrically (via the generator to the motor through the battery).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel hybrid drivetrains based on **architecture, ICE operation, efficiency in urban/highway cycles, and complexity**. State one industrial example for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A parallel hybrid vehicle has an ICE delivering 60 kW at 4500 rpm and an Electric Motor delivering 25 kW at 3000 rpm. Both are coupled to the transmission at a common speed of 4500 rpm through a torque coupler. Calculate:
(i) The total torque at the coupling point.
(ii) The total power available at the wheels (assume transmission efficiency 95%).
(iii) The Degree of Hybridization.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

| Parameter | Series | Parallel | Series-Parallel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Architecture | ICE → Gen → Motor → Wheels | ICE + Motor → Coupling → Wheels | Planetary gearset with both |
| ICE Operation | Always at OOL | Variable | Mostly at OOL |
| Urban Efficiency | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| Highway Efficiency | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| Complexity | Low (mechanical) | Medium | High |
| Example | Chevy Volt Gen 1 | Honda Insight | Toyota Prius |

**Part (b):**

(i) **Total Torque at Coupling:**

First, find angular speed:
$$ \omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi (4500)}{60} = 471.24 \text{ rad/s} $$

ICE torque:
$$ \tau_{ICE} = \frac{P_{ICE}}{\omega} = \frac{60000}{471.24} = 127.32 \text{ N·m} $$

EM torque (note: EM is rated at 3000 rpm, but at the common speed of 4500 rpm, the available torque scales inversely with speed for a constant-power machine):
$$ \tau_{EM,available} = \frac{25000}{471.24} = 53.05 \text{ N·m} $$

Total torque:
$$ \tau_{total} = \tau_{ICE} + \tau_{EM} = 127.32 + 53.05 = 180.37 \text{ N·m} $$

(ii) **Total Power at Wheels:**

Mechanical power at transmission input:
$$ P_{in} = \tau_{total} \cdot \omega = 180.37 \times 471.24 = 85,000 \text{ W} = 85 \text{ kW} $$

Power at wheels:
$$ P_{wheel} = P_{in} \cdot \eta_{trans} = 85 \times 0.95 = 80.75 \text{ kW} $$

(iii) **Degree of Hybridization:**
$$ DOH = \frac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}} \times 100\% = \frac{25}{25 + 60} \times 100\% = \frac{25}{85} \times 100\% \approx 29.41\% $$

This classifies the vehicle as a **Full Hybrid**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose 2–4 Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign Errors in Planetary Equation:** The term $(1+\rho)\omega_C$ must use the **same direction sign convention** as $\rho \omega_R$. If the ICE and motor rotate in opposite directions, assign a negative sign to one of them. Examiners specifically check this.
>
> 2. **Confusing Speed Coupling with Torque Coupling:** A Series Hybrid is often wrongly labeled "no coupling." Correct statement: it uses **electrical speed coupling** through the generator-motor pair.
>
> 3. **Forgetting $\eta_{trans}$:** In numerical problems, students often compute the power at the coupling point and report it as wheel power. **Always apply transmission and final drive efficiencies** before declaring the answer.
>
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** rpm must be converted to rad/s (or kept in rpm, but consistently). Mixing units is an instant 1-mark deduction.
>
> 5. **DOH Misclassification:** Students confuse Mild vs Full vs Plug-in Hybrid boundaries. Memorize: Micro (<5%), Mild (5–25%), Full (25–50%), Plug-in (>50%).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Hybrid Drivetrains**
>
> - **Four Architectures:** Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel, Complex — know the block diagram of each cold.
> - **Coupling Types:** Series = Electrical Speed Coupling; Parallel = Mechanical Torque Coupling; Power-Split = Both.
> - **Planetary Gear Equation (MUST MEMORIZE):** $\omega_S = (1+\rho)\omega_C - \rho \omega_R$.
> - **Degree of Hybridization:** $DOH = P_{EM}/(P_{EM}+P_{ICE}) \times 100\%$.
> - **Series Limitation:** Three energy conversions make it inefficient at high speeds.
> - **Parallel Advantage:** Direct mechanical link → high efficiency at highway speeds.
> - **Power-Split Advantage:** Combines the best of both — ICE always near OOL, with flexible torque-speed blending.
> - **Industrial Examples:** Series → Chevy Volt; Parallel → Honda Insight; Power-Split → Toyota Prius; Complex → Lexus/Toyota HSD.
> - **Operating Modes of Power-Split:** EV-only, Engine-only, Series, Parallel, and Regen.
> - **Planetary Parameters:** Remember $\rho = N_R/N_S$ and $N_R = N_S + 2N_P$.
> - **Always Show Units:** rpm → rad/s conversion factor is $\pi/30$.
> - **Always Apply Efficiencies:** Transmission ($\eta_T$), Power Electronics ($\eta_{PEC}$), Motor ($\eta_M$).
> - **State of Charge (SOC) Window:** Typical operating range is 30–80% to prolong battery life.
> - **Regenerative Braking Power:** $P_{regen} = \tau_{brake} \cdot \omega_{wheel} \cdot \eta_{EM}$.
> - **KTU Trend:** Recent exam papers emphasize **derivation-based questions** on the planetary gearset and **comparison tables** between architectures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
