<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series Hybrid Electric Drive Train (SHEV):** A powertrain architecture in which the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) is **mechanically decoupled** from the driving wheels. The ICE drives a generator to produce electrical energy, which either charges the Energy Storage System (ESS) or is directly fed to a Traction Motor that propels the vehicle.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEMET863, Module 1), the series hybrid topology is classified as a **range-extended electric vehicle** because all tractive effort at the wheels is delivered **purely through the electrical path**. The ICE acts solely as a **range extender / on-board generator unit (OGU)**, never touching the wheels directly.

### Architecture Components (Mandatory KTU Terminology)

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Heat Engine (ICE)** | Runs on optimal operating line to drive the generator |
| **Generator (G)** | Converts mechanical energy from ICE into electrical energy |
| **Power Converter Unit (PCU)** | Rectifies generator AC output into DC for the DC bus |
| **Energy Storage System (ESS)** | Battery / Ultra-capacitor that buffers power |
| **Traction Motor (M)** | Converts electrical energy back to mechanical to drive wheels |
| **Final Drive / Transmission** | Fixed gear reduction (typically single-speed) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Power Bank Bicycle"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a ceiling fan (ICE) running at constant speed in your house. It is connected to a small dynamo (Generator) that charges a power bank (Battery). Your bicycle wheel is powered by an electric hub motor (Traction Motor) that runs purely from the power bank. You can ride the bicycle even if the ceiling fan is OFF (pure EV mode), or the fan can run steadily to keep the power bank topped up. **The fan NEVER directly spins the wheel.** This is exactly how a Series Hybrid works.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics for Series HEV

- **Power Conversion Stages:** **3** (Mechanical → Electrical → Mechanical)
- **Number of Electrical Machines:** **2** (Generator + Traction Motor)
- **Typical ICE Operating Mode:** **Constant speed / Constant power** (steady state on optimal line)
- **Mechanical Path from ICE to Wheels:** **None** (fully decoupled)
- **Standard ESS Voltage Range:** **200 V – 800 V** (modern SHEVs)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Power Flow

The series hybrid operates on **two simultaneous power flow equations** that must balance at every instant:

### Power Flow Branch A (Generator Path)
1. ICE burns fuel at its **Optimal Operating Line (OOL)** to maintain peak thermal efficiency (typically η_ICE ≈ 35–40%).
2. Mechanical power $P_{ICE}$ is delivered to the rotor of the Generator.
3. Generator efficiency $\eta_{gen}$ (≈ 90–95%) converts this into electrical power $P_{gen}$.
4. Rectifier/DCDC converter adjusts voltage to the DC bus level.

### Power Flow Branch B (Traction Path)
1. Traction Motor draws electrical power $P_{mot,elec}$ from the DC bus.
2. Motor efficiency $\eta_{mot}$ (≈ 90–95%) converts this to mechanical power at the motor shaft.
3. Transmission and final drive (efficiency $\eta_{fd}$ ≈ 95%) deliver tractive power to the wheels.

### Energy Storage Interlink (The Buffer)
- When $P_{gen} > P_{mot,elec}$ → Excess charges the battery (SOC increases).
- When $P_{gen} < P_{mot,elec}$ → Deficit is drawn from battery (SOC decreases).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** The ICE in a series hybrid is **sized for average power**, not peak power. This is a critical board-exam distinction from parallel hybrids where the ICE must handle peak loads.

## 2.2 Modes of Operation in Series HEV

| Mode | ICE State | Battery State | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure EV Mode** | OFF | Discharging | Zero-emission city driving, low load |
| **Pure ICE Mode** | ON (steady) | Holding SOC | Steady highway cruise |
| **Charging Mode** | ON (peak η) | Charging | ICE generates excess to refill battery |
| **Boost Mode** | ON (peak P) | Discharging | Peak demand — both sources feed motor |
| **Regenerative Braking** | OFF | Charging | Motor acts as generator during braking |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Mathematical Form | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power Balance at DC Bus | $P_{gen} + P_{bat} = P_{mot,elec}$ | Instantaneous power balance | kW |
| Generator Output | $P_{gen} = \eta_{gen} \cdot P_{ICE}$ | After mechanical-to-electrical conversion | kW |
| Wheel Power | $P_{wheel} = \eta_{fd} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot P_{mot,elec}$ | Net tractive power at wheels | kW |
| Overall Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency | $\eta_{ttw} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{fd}$ | When running pure ICE-generator path | % |
| Vehicle Power Demand | $P_{req} = \frac{v}{\eta_{fd}} \left( m g f_r + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m \delta \frac{dv}{dt} \right)$ | Longitudinal dynamics equation | kW |
| Tractive Force | $F_t = F_{roll} + F_{grade} + F_{acc} + F_{aero}$ | Sum of all resistance forces | N |
| Motor Torque | $T_{mot} = \frac{F_t \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_{fd}}$ | Required motor torque | N·m |
| Motor Speed | $\omega_{mot} = \frac{v \cdot G}{r_w}$ | Required motor angular speed | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Series hybrid architecture is the preferred topology for **city buses, delivery trucks, and range-extended EVs (like the Chevrolet Volt, BMW i3 with REx)** because frequent stops favor regenerative braking and the ICE never needs to follow transient road loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Flow Analysis at Steady-State Cruise

We derive the steady-state ICE operating point for a series HEV cruising at constant velocity $v$.

**Step 1:** At constant velocity, acceleration term $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$, so the tractive force reduces to rolling resistance + aerodynamic drag.

$$
F_t = m g f_r + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2
$$

**Step 2:** The required wheel power is therefore:

$$
P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v = v \left( m g f_r + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 \right)
$$

**Step 3:** Working backwards through the drivetrain efficiencies to find the motor electrical power:

$$
P_{mot,elec} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{fd}}
$$

**Step 4:** If the battery is in **holding mode** (neither charging nor discharging significantly), the generator must supply the entire motor demand:

$$
P_{gen} = P_{mot,elec}
$$

**Step 5:** Now working from the generator back to the ICE:

$$
P_{ICE} = \frac{P_{gen}}{\eta_{gen}} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{fd}}
$$

**Step 6:** The fuel energy consumption rate (in litres per second, using lower heating value of fuel $LHV$ and density $\rho_f$):

$$
\dot{V}_f = \frac{P_{ICE}}{\eta_{ICE} \cdot LHV \cdot \rho_f}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Note (Step 5 → Step 6):** We divided by $\eta_{gen}$ because the generator *consumes* mechanical power from the ICE to produce electrical power. So the ICE must generate more power than what reaches the motor. Fuel rate is then ICE thermal power divided by fuel energy density.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sizing of Traction Motor

The traction motor must handle **peak instantaneous wheel demand**, not average. This is a board-favourite derivation.

**Step 1:** The maximum required tractive force typically occurs at low-speed launch or hill-climb:

$$
F_{t,max} = m g f_r + m g \sin\alpha + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m \delta a_{max}
$$

**Step 2:** Maximum motor torque (occurs at launch, low speed):

$$
T_{mot,max} = \frac{F_{t,max} \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_{fd}}
$$

**Step 3:** Maximum motor speed (occurs at top vehicle velocity):

$$
\omega_{mot,max} = \frac{v_{max} \cdot G}{r_w}
$$

**Step 4:** Maximum motor power rating required:

$$
P_{mot,max} = \frac{T_{mot,max} \cdot \omega_{mot,max}}{1000} \quad \text{[kW]}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently forget the **transmission ratio $G$** when converting wheel torque to motor torque. Always confirm the equation form: $T_{mot} = F_t \cdot r_w / (G \cdot \eta_{fd})$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Series vs. Other Topologies

| Parameter | Series HEV | Parallel HEV | Series-Parallel HEV |
|---|---|---|---|
| ICE mechanical link to wheels | **No** | Yes | Yes |
| Number of electrical machines | **2** | 1 | 2 |
| ICE operating point flexibility | **Highest** | Limited | Highest |
| Transmission complexity | **Low** | High | Highest |
| Conversion losses | **Highest** | Moderate | High |
| Component cost | **Higher** | Lower | Highest |
| Best suited for | **City / Bus / Range-Extended** | Highway | Universal |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Series Hybrid Drive Train

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional flow of energy** through the series hybrid powertrain, mapped from the fuel tank to the wheels.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fuelA[Fuel Tank] --> iceA[Heat Engine ICE]
    iceA -- Mechanical Shaft --> genA[Generator]
    genA -- AC Output --> rectA[Rectifier and DC-DC Converter]
    rectA --> dcbA[DC Bus]
    battA[Battery Pack ESS] <--> dcbA
    dcbA -- DC Power --> invA[Traction Inverter]
    invA -- 3 Phase AC --> motA[Traction Motor]
    motA -- Shaft Torque --> transA[Single Speed Transmission]
    transA --> fdA[Final Drive Differential]
    fdA --> whlA[Drive Wheels]
    motA -. Regenerative Braking .-> invA
    invA -. Bidirectional .-> dcbA
```

### Diagram Interpretation for Exam Writing

> [!NOTE]
> **How to describe this diagram in your answer sheet:**
> 1. Fuel from the tank powers the ICE, which spins the generator at a **constant optimal speed**.
> 2. The generator's AC output is rectified and stepped to the DC bus voltage.
> 3. The DC bus is the **electrical junction** shared by the generator, battery, and inverter.
> 4. The traction inverter converts DC to 3-phase AC for the motor.
> 5. The motor's mechanical output goes through a simple single-speed reduction and differential to the wheels.
> 6. **Dotted arrows** indicate the bidirectional regenerative braking path.

## 4.2 Energy Flow Direction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph "Energy Conversion Stages"
        S1[Stage 1: Chemical to Mechanical]:::stage
        S2[Stage 2: Mechanical to Electrical]:::stage
        S3[Stage 3: Electrical to Mechanical]:::stage
        S4[Stage 4: Mechanical at Wheels]:::stage
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
    classDef stage fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why three conversion stages?** Because the energy path is: Fuel (Chemical) → ICE (Mechanical) → Generator (Electrical) → Motor (Mechanical) → Wheels. Each conversion incurs a loss (η_ICE, η_gen, η_mot). This is the **fundamental efficiency penalty** of series architecture compared to direct mechanical drive.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Define a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV). Why is the ICE mechanically decoupled from the wheels in this configuration?**

**Model Answer:**
A Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle is one in which the Internal Combustion Engine does not have any mechanical connection to the driving wheels. The ICE drives a generator that produces electrical energy, which is then used either to charge the battery or to power an electric traction motor connected to the wheels through a transmission. The ICE is mechanically decoupled from the wheels so that it can operate continuously at its **Optimal Operating Line (OOL)** — the point of highest thermal efficiency — independent of vehicle speed or torque demand. This decoupling allows the engine to act purely as an on-board generator unit, simplifying transmission design and improving overall fuel efficiency, especially in urban stop-and-go driving.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: List any four major components of a Series Hybrid Electric Drive Train and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **1. Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** | Runs on optimal operating line; converts chemical energy of fuel into mechanical energy to drive the generator. |
| **2. Electric Generator** | Converts the mechanical energy from the ICE into electrical energy (AC), which is rectified and fed to the DC bus. |
| **3. Energy Storage System (Battery)** | Acts as a buffer; stores excess electrical energy and supplies it during high-power demand or pure EV mode. |
| **4. Traction Motor** | Converts electrical energy from the DC bus into mechanical torque to drive the wheels through a single-speed transmission. |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question contains two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (Internal Choice).

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the configuration of a Series Hybrid Electric Drive Train. Discuss its operating modes, advantages, and limitations.**

#### Part (a) — Configuration and Block Diagram (7 Marks)

**Solution:**
The Series Hybrid Electric Drive Train consists of the following components connected in a series energy-flow path:

1. **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** — burns fuel to deliver mechanical power on a steady-state optimal operating line.
2. **Generator** — coupled directly to the ICE shaft; converts mechanical power into electrical AC power.
3. **Rectifier / DC-DC Converter** — conditions the generator output to the DC bus voltage.
4. **Energy Storage System (Battery Pack)** — connected to the DC bus via a bidirectional converter; buffers power.
5. **Traction Inverter** — converts DC bus power into variable-frequency 3-phase AC for the motor.
6. **Traction Motor** — typically a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) or Induction Motor; drives the transmission.
7. **Single-Speed Transmission and Final Drive** — reduces motor speed to wheel speed and transmits torque to the wheels.

The energy path is: **Fuel → ICE → Generator → DC Bus → Battery ↔ Inverter → Motor → Wheels**

All tractive power at the wheels is delivered through the **electrical path**, not mechanically from the ICE.

#### Part (b) — Operating Modes, Advantages, Limitations (7 Marks)

**Operating Modes:**

| Mode | Description |
|---|---|
| **Pure EV Mode** | ICE OFF, vehicle runs only on battery power. Used for low-speed, short-distance, zero-emission driving. |
| **ICE-Only Mode** | ICE runs at optimal point; generator output matches motor demand; battery SOC is held steady. |
| **Charging Mode** | ICE generates excess power that charges the battery in addition to running the motor. |
| **Boost Mode** | Both ICE-generator and battery simultaneously supply the traction motor during peak acceleration or hill climb. |
| **Regenerative Braking Mode** | Motor acts as a generator; kinetic energy is converted back into electrical energy and stored in the battery. |

**Advantages:**

- ICE always operates at its **optimal efficiency point**, independent of driving conditions.
- **Simpler transmission** — usually a single fixed gear ratio, reducing mechanical complexity and cost.
- Ideal for **stop-and-go urban driving**, where regenerative braking recovers significant energy.
- **Smooth and quiet** vehicle operation due to absence of direct ICE-to-wheel mechanical link.

**Limitations:**

- **Three energy conversion stages** lead to cumulative losses (typical overall η_ttw drops by 10–15%).
- The traction motor must be **sized for peak power**, not average — increasing cost and weight.
- Requires **larger battery** to buffer energy, raising vehicle mass and initial cost.
- Less efficient on **long steady highway drives** compared to parallel configurations.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]`

**Q: A Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle has the following specifications: curb mass $m = 1500$ kg, rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.015$, drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$, frontal area $A = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, air density $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, wheel radius $r_w = 0.32$ m, transmission ratio $G = 4.5$, final drive efficiency $\eta_{fd} = 0.95$, generator efficiency $\eta_{gen} = 0.92$, motor efficiency $\eta_{mot} = 0.93$, and ICE efficiency $\eta_{ICE} = 0.38$. The vehicle is cruising at constant $v = 60$ km/h on level ground. Compute: (a) The required tractive force and wheel power, and (b) The required ICE power and the overall tank-to-wheel efficiency.**

#### Part (a) — Tractive Force and Wheel Power (7 Marks)

**Given:** $v = 60 \text{ km/h} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}$, $m = 1500$ kg, $f_r = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1:** Rolling resistance force:

$$
F_{roll} = m g f_r = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 = 220.7 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2:** Aerodynamic drag force:

$$
F_{aero} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.67)^2
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 277.8 = 109.99 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3:** Total tractive force (level ground, constant speed → no grade, no acceleration term):

$$
F_t = F_{roll} + F_{aero} = 220.7 + 109.99 = 330.7 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4:** Wheel power:

$$
P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v = 330.7 \times 16.67 = 5512 \text{ W} \approx 5.51 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Part (b) — ICE Power and Overall Efficiency (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Motor electrical input (working backwards from wheel):

$$
P_{mot,elec} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{fd}} = \frac{5.512}{0.93 \times 0.95} = \frac{5.512}{0.8835} = 6.24 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2:** Assuming battery in holding mode, generator output = motor input:

$$
P_{gen} = P_{mot,elec} = 6.24 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3:** ICE mechanical output (must overcome generator losses):

$$
P_{ICE} = \frac{P_{gen}}{\eta_{gen}} = \frac{6.24}{0.92} = 6.78 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4:** Fuel chemical power input (tank-to-engine):

$$
P_{fuel} = \frac{P_{ICE}}{\eta_{ICE}} = \frac{6.78}{0.38} = 17.84 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5:** Overall tank-to-wheel efficiency:

$$
\eta_{ttw} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{P_{fuel}} = \frac{5.512}{17.84} = 0.309 = 30.9\%
$$

**Verification using product formula:**

$$
\eta_{ttw} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{fd} = 0.38 \times 0.92 \times 0.93 \times 0.95 = 0.309
$$

Both methods yield **η_ttw ≈ 30.9%**, confirming correctness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistake:** Failing to convert km/h to m/s when computing $P = F \cdot v$. This alone costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign of Acceleration Term:** When $v$ is constant, students incorrectly include the $m \cdot \delta \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$ term, inflating the answer.
> 3. **Efficiency Direction:** Some students multiply instead of divide when "going backwards" through the drivetrain. Always remember: $P_{input} = P_{output} / \eta$.
> 4. **Forgetting Final Drive:** Missing $\eta_{fd}$ in the product chain results in an inflated efficiency value.
> 5. **Diagrams:** A block diagram without clear **arrow labels** (mechanical, electrical, AC, DC) loses 1–2 marks in Part B sub-part (a).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Series Hybrid Drive Train**

- 🔹 **Definition:** Series HEV = ICE mechanically decoupled from wheels; all traction via electrical path.
- 🔹 **Conversion Stages:** **Three** — Mechanical → Electrical → Mechanical.
- 🔹 **Number of Electrical Machines:** **Two** — Generator + Traction Motor.
- 🔹 **ICE Operating Strategy:** Runs on **Optimal Operating Line (OOL)** at constant speed/load for max η.
- 🔹 **Transmission:** Typically **single-speed fixed gear** (no multi-gear gearbox needed).
- 🔹 **Component Sizing Rule:** Motor sized for **peak power**; ICE sized for **average power**.
- 🔹 **Battery Role:** Acts as a **power buffer** to decouple generator output from instantaneous motor demand.
- 🔹 **Key Operating Modes:** Pure EV, Pure ICE, Charging, Boost, Regenerative Braking — total **5 modes**.
- 🔹 **Power Flow Equation:** $P_{gen} + P_{bat} = P_{mot,elec}$ (DC bus balance at every instant).
- 🔹 **Overall Efficiency Equation:** $\eta_{ttw} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{fd}$.
- 🔹 **Best Application Domain:** Urban buses, delivery trucks, range-extender passenger EVs (e.g., Chevy Volt, BMW i3 REx).
- 🔹 **Primary Drawback:** **Triple energy conversion** → cumulative losses → lower η on highways vs. parallel HEV.
- 🔹 **Regenerative Braking Advantage:** **Maximum** because motor always connected to wheels, even with ICE off.
- 🔹 **Board Exam Favourite:** Numerical problems on tractive force, wheel power, and η_ttw calculation using product of efficiencies.
- 🔹 **Diagrams:** Always draw with **clear directional arrows** distinguishing mechanical vs. electrical flow paths.
- 🔹 **Vocabulary to Use in Answers:** "Optimal Operating Line," "On-board Generator Unit," "DC Bus," "Bidirectional Converter," "Power Buffer."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Configuration of the Parallel Hybrid Electric Drive Train
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Configuration of the Parallel Hybrid Electric Drive Train

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — Configuration of the Parallel Hybrid Electric Drive Train

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Parallel Hybrid Electric Drive Train (PHEV)** is a hybrid powertrain architecture in which the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) and the Electric Motor (EM) are mechanically coupled in **parallel**, meaning both propulsion units can deliver tractive power **simultaneously and independently** to the final drive (wheels) through a common mechanical path (typically a transmission and differential). The torque contributions of the two energy converters are additively (or subtractively) combined through a **torque-coupling device** such as a clutch, planetary gear set, or chain/belt drive.

$$T_{wheel} = (T_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{m,ICE}) + (T_{EM} \cdot \eta_{m,EM})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** In a pure parallel configuration, both the ICE and the EM are **mechanically independent** of each other in the energy-conversion path. Either source can propel the vehicle **alone**, or they can propel it **together**. This is the key distinguishing factor from the Series Hybrid, where the ICE has **no direct mechanical linkage** to the wheels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Distinct Operating Modes of a Parallel Hybrid:**
> 1. **Single-source mode** — Either ICE alone OR EM alone drives the wheels.
> 2. **Dual-source mode** — Both ICE and EM drive the wheels simultaneously, with their torques summed through the mechanical coupler.

---

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Two-Wheel Cart Pull"

Imagine two people pulling a heavy cart along a road:

- **Person A (the ICE)** is strong, tireless on long journeys, but needs food (fuel) frequently and cannot start moving instantly from rest.
- **Person B (the Electric Motor)** is weaker than Person A but starts instantly, moves smoothly from standstill, and is very efficient in stop-and-go city traffic.
- Both people pull **side by side (in parallel)** on the same rope attached to the cart. On a **highway climb**, both pull together. In **city traffic**, only Person B pulls. On a **flat cruise**, only Person A pulls.

This is **exactly** how a parallel hybrid works: two power sources share the mechanical load, choosing the best one (or combination) for the driving condition.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighting)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **ICE Peak Efficiency** | **35% – 40%** | dimensionless |
| **EM Peak Efficiency** | **90% – 95%** | dimensionless |
| **Battery Nominal Voltage (HEV)** | **144 – 360 V DC** | Volts |
| **State of Charge (SOC) operating window** | **30% – 80%** | % |
| **Specific Energy of Li-ion (HEV use)** | **100 – 150 Wh/kg** | Wh/kg |
| **Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery** | **10% – 30%** | % |

---

### 1.4 Position of the EM with respect to the Transmission — Key Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently tests this classification.** The EM in a parallel hybrid can be placed at one of **three positions**:

1. **Pre-Transmission (P0, P1 architectures)** — EM placed **before** the transmission (between ICE and gearbox), coupled via belt or clutch.
2. **Transmission-Integrated (P2, P3 architectures)** — EM placed **inside or replacing** the transmission.
3. **Post-Transmission (P4 architecture)** — EM placed **after** the transmission, directly driving the wheels (or a separate axle, as in AWD hybrids).

> The architecture name (P0, P1, P2, P3, P4) follows the **Bosch / SAE J1715** convention used universally in industry and KTU references.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Parallel Hybrid Drive Train operates through a **multimode energy management strategy**. The logic is as follows:

- **Step 1 — Vehicle starts from rest:** The ICE is OFF. The EM alone propels the vehicle (silent, zero tailpipe emission launch). This avoids the inefficient idle/low-load operation of the ICE.

- **Step 2 — Light load / cruising at low speed:** The EM continues to drive the wheels alone. The ICE may remain off or be clutched in only when needed.

- **Step 3 — High load / acceleration / hill climb:** Both ICE and EM deliver power simultaneously. Their torques are summed through the mechanical coupling device (clutch or planetary gear). The vehicle achieves its **maximum tractive force** in this mode.

- **Step 4 — Steady high-speed cruise:** The ICE alone drives the wheels (its most efficient operating region). The EM is decoupled and acts as a generator to charge the battery if SOC is low (load-point shifting).

- **Step 5 — Braking / deceleration:** The ICE is shut off. The EM acts as a **regenerative generator**, converting kinetic energy back to electrical energy stored in the battery.

- **Step 6 — Battery SOC low:** The ICE provides the propulsive power AND simultaneously drives the EM as a generator to recharge the battery (this is the classic **series-mode behavior embedded inside a parallel drivetrain**).

---

### 2.2 Why and How — Engineering Reasoning

> **Why use parallel at all?**
> Because real-world driving cycles (like the **Worldwide Harmonised Light Vehicles Test Cycle – WLTC**) contain large segments where average power demand is **low to moderate**. Running an ICE in this region means efficiency drops to **15% – 20%**. An EM is **3–5× more efficient** in this regime. The parallel topology allows the designer to **size a smaller ICE** (Atkinson-cycle optimized) and supplement it with an EM for transient peaks — a concept called **downsizing + electrification**.

> **How does torque addition happen?**
> Through one of the three torque-coupling methods:
> - **Mechanical clutch** (simple on/off engagement).
> - **Planetary gear set (Epicyclic)** (continuous ratio blending — used in Toyota Hybrid System THS-II, which is series-parallel, but the principle applies to true parallel too).
> - **Chain/belt drive** with fixed ratio (as in Honda Integrated Motor Assist – IMA, a textbook parallel hybrid).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Variables / Boundary Conditions | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total tractive force** | $F_t = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{acc} + F_{grade}$ | Resistive forces | N |
| 2 | **Total tractive force expanded** | $F_t = m g f_r \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_D A_f v^2 + m \delta \tfrac{dv}{dt} + m g \sin\alpha$ | $m$: mass, $f_r$: rolling resistance, $\rho_a$: air density (≈ 1.2 kg/m³), $C_D$: drag coefficient | N |
| 3 | **Required wheel torque** | $T_w = F_t \cdot r_w$ | $r_w$: wheel radius | N·m |
| 4 | **Torque at motor/engine shaft** | $T_s = \dfrac{T_w}{i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}$ | $i_g$: gear ratio, $i_0$: final drive ratio, $\eta_t$: transmission efficiency | N·m |
| 5 | **Parallel torque addition** | $T_w = (T_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{m,ICE} + T_{EM} \cdot \eta_{m,EM}) \cdot i_g \cdot i_0$ | Both torques add before gearing | N·m |
| 6 | **Power at wheels** | $P_w = F_t \cdot v = \dfrac{T_w \cdot v}{r_w}$ | Instantaneous power | W |
| 7 | **Power balance equation** | $P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_t + P_{EM} \cdot \eta_{EM} \cdot \eta_t = P_w + P_{aux}$ | $P_{aux}$: auxiliary loads (HVAC, lights) | W |
| 8 | **Energy in battery** | $E_b = V_{oc} \cdot Q_{bat}$ | $V_{oc}$: open-circuit voltage, $Q_{bat}$: capacity | Wh |
| 9 | **SOC definition** | $SOC = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | Maintained typically between **30% – 80%** | % |
| 10 | **Regenerative braking power** | $P_{regen} = T_{EM,gen} \cdot \omega_{EM} \cdot \eta_{gen}$ | Limited by battery charge acceptance | W |
| 11 | **Vehicle kinetic energy** | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Reclaimable portion = $\eta_{regen} \cdot KE$ | J |
| 12 | **Fuel energy to wheel efficiency** | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_t$ | For parallel, varies 25% – 38% | dimensionless |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Honda Civic Hybrid (2003–2015)** — uses the **IMA (Integrated Motor Assist)** parallel architecture. The EM is mounted directly on the crankshaft (P1 architecture) and assists the ICE during acceleration.
- **Honda Accord Hybrid (2014+)** uses a **two-motor parallel hybrid** with a lock-up clutch.
- **Diesel-electric locomotives** are essentially large parallel hybrids — the diesel engine and the traction motor both connect to the wheels through a mechanical/electrical coupling.
- **Hybrid construction equipment** (e.g., Komatsu HB335LC excavator) uses parallel hybrids for hydraulic-pump assist.
- **Military combat vehicles** use parallel hybrids for silent-watch (EM only) capability while idling the diesel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Required Traction Force for a Parallel Hybrid Vehicle

We start from Newton's second law applied to a vehicle moving up an incline of angle $\alpha$ at speed $v$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum F &= m \cdot a \\
F_t - F_{roll} - F_{aero} - F_{grade} &= m \cdot \delta \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\delta$ is the **rotational inertia factor** (accounts for rotating masses — wheels, gears, crankshaft).

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= m \cdot \delta \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} + F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{grade}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now expand each resistive force individually:

**Rolling Resistance Force:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{roll} &= m \cdot g \cdot f_r \cdot \cos\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

where $f_r$ is the coefficient of rolling resistance (typically **0.008 – 0.015** for passenger car tires on asphalt).

**Aerodynamic Drag Force:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{aero} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_D \cdot A_f \cdot v^2
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_D$ is the drag coefficient, and $A_f$ is the frontal area.

**Grade Resistance Force:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{grade} &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final combined traction force equation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= m \cdot \delta \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} + m \cdot g \cdot f_r \cdot \cos\alpha + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_D \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Power Split Between ICE and EM in Parallel Mode

Assume the total wheel power required is $P_w$ and the total tractive force is $F_t$. Define the **split factor** $\beta$ as the fraction of total tractive force supplied by the electric motor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{EM} &= \beta \cdot F_t \\
F_{ICE} &= (1 - \beta) \cdot F_t
\end{aligned}
$$

where $0 \le \beta \le 1$. The **control strategy** selects $\beta$ in real-time.

Corresponding wheel torques:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{EM,wheel} &= \beta \cdot F_t \cdot r_w \\
T_{ICE,wheel} &= (1 - \beta) \cdot F_t \cdot r_w
\end{aligned}
$$

Required torques at the ICE and EM shafts (after the transmission):
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{EM,shaft} &= \frac{\beta \cdot F_t \cdot r_w}{i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_{m,EM}} \\
T_{ICE,shaft} &= \frac{(1 - \beta) \cdot F_t \cdot r_w}{i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_{m,ICE}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Required power from each source:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{EM} &= T_{EM,shaft} \cdot \omega_{EM} = \frac{\beta \cdot F_t \cdot v}{\eta_{m,EM}} \\
P_{ICE} &= T_{ICE,shaft} \cdot \omega_{ICE} = \frac{(1 - \beta) \cdot F_t \cdot v}{\eta_{m,ICE}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note the key insight:** Because the **mechanical path** to the wheels is direct (no double energy conversion as in series hybrid), the parallel topology has **fewer conversion losses** in the dual-source mode. This is the engineering reason why parallel hybrids are preferred for **highway-oriented** vehicles.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Parallel Hybrid Sizing

**Problem Statement:** A parallel hybrid passenger car has the following specifications:
- Mass $m = 1500$ kg
- Wheel radius $r_w = 0.32$ m
- Rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.012$
- Drag coefficient $C_D = 0.30$
- Frontal area $A_f = 2.2$ m²
- Transmission efficiency $\eta_t = 0.92$
- Gear ratio $i_g \cdot i_0 = 4.5$
- $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Rotational inertia factor $\delta = 1.05$
- Vehicle cruises at constant $v = 60$ km/h on a flat road ($\alpha = 0$, $dv/dt = 0$).

**Find:** (a) Total tractive force, (b) Wheel torque, (c) Required power at wheels, (d) Split between ICE (assume 70% of force) and EM (30% of force), (e) Torque at each shaft.

**Solution:**

**(a) Total Tractive Force:**
Since $\alpha = 0$ and $dv/dt = 0$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= 0 + m \cdot g \cdot f_r + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_D \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 + 0 \\
F_t &= (1500)(9.81)(0.012) + (0.5)(1.2)(0.30)(2.2)(16.67)^2 \\
F_t &= 176.58 + 110.30 \\
F_t &= 286.88 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Note: $v = 60$ km/h $= 60 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 16.667$ m/s.

**(b) Wheel Torque:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_w &= F_t \cdot r_w = 286.88 \times 0.32 \\
T_w &= 91.80 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Power at Wheels:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_w &= F_t \cdot v = 286.88 \times 16.667 \\
P_w &= 4781.4 \text{ W} \approx 4.78 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Force and Power Split** (using $\beta = 0.30$ for EM):
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{EM} &= 0.30 \times 286.88 = 86.06 \text{ N} \\
F_{ICE} &= 0.70 \times 286.88 = 200.82 \text{ N} \\
P_{EM} &= 86.06 \times 16.667 = 1434.3 \text{ W} \\
P_{ICE} &= 200.82 \times 16.667 = 3347.1 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(e) Torque at Each Shaft:**
Assume $\eta_{m,EM} = \eta_{m,ICE} = 0.95$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{EM,shaft} &= \frac{86.06 \times 0.32}{4.5 \times 0.95} = \frac{27.54}{4.275} \\
T_{EM,shaft} &= 6.44 \text{ N·m} \\
T_{ICE,shaft} &= \frac{200.82 \times 0.32}{4.5 \times 0.95} = \frac{64.26}{4.275} \\
T_{ICE,shaft} &= 15.03 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** The sum of shaft torques × gear ratio × efficiency should equal wheel torque:
> $(6.44 + 15.03) \times 4.5 \times 0.95 = 21.47 \times 4.275 = 91.78 \text{ N·m} \approx T_w$ ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Parallel Hybrid Drive Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Battery Pack] -->|DC Link| B[Power Electronics Inverter]
    B -->|3-phase AC| C[Electric Motor EM]
    C -->|Shaft T1| D[Torque Coupling Device]
    E[Internal Combustion Engine ICE] -->|Shaft T2| D
    D -->|Sum T1 + T2| F[Transmission Multi-speed]
    F -->|Gear ratio ig| G[Final Drive Differential]
    G -->|Ratio i0| H[Wheels]
    I[Vehicle Control Unit VCU] -.->|Torque command| B
    I -.->|Throttle command| E
    I -.->|Mode select| D
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — P0/P1/P2/P3/P4 Classification (Bosch Convention)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1[Engine Bay]
        ICE1[ICE]
    end
    subgraph A2[Transmission]
        GEAR[Multi-speed Gearbox]
    end
    subgraph A3[Driveline]
        DIFF[Final Drive Differential]
    end
    subgraph A4[Wheels]
        W[Wheel FL and FR]
    end

    P0A[Belt driven starter generator BSG] --> ICE1
    ICE1 --> CLUTCH1[Clutch]
    CLUTCH1 --> P1A[P1 Motor on crankshaft]
    P1A --> GEAR
    GEAR --> P2A[P2 Motor between gearbox and clutch]
    P2A --> DIFF
    DIFF --> P3A[P3 Motor at gearbox output]
    P3A --> W
    P4A[P4 Motor at rear axle independent] --> W
```

---

### 4.3 Operating Mode State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StopMode
    StopMode --> EMOnly: SOC gt Min and light throttle
    EMOnly --> ICEOnly: Cruise at high speed and SOC ok
    EMOnly --> ParallelDrive: High torque demand
    ICEOnly --> ParallelDrive: Sudden acceleration
    ICEOnly --> ChargingMode: SOC lt Threshold
    ParallelDrive --> ICEOnly: Demand reduces
    ParallelDrive --> RegenerativeBraking: Brake pedal pressed
    ChargingMode --> ICEOnly: SOC restored
    RegenerativeBraking --> StopMode: Vehicle stops
```

---

### 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — Energy Flow in Parallel Mode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Source1[Energy Source 1]
        F[Tank Fuel]
    end
    subgraph Source2[Energy Source 2]
        BAT[Battery DC]
    end
    subgraph Conv1[Converter 1]
        ICE[ICE block]
    end
    subgraph Conv2[Converter 2]
        INV[Inverter block]
        EM[EM block]
    end
    subgraph Mech[Mechanical Coupling]
        CLUTCH[Torque Coupler block]
    end
    subgraph Out[Output]
        WH[Wheels block]
    end

    F --> ICE --> CLUTCH
    BAT --> INV --> EM --> CLUTCH
    CLUTCH --> WH
    BAT -.->|Regen path| EM
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 - Model Style]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** With a neat block diagram, explain the configuration of a parallel hybrid electric drive train. Mention any two real-world vehicles using this configuration.

**Model Answer:**

A Parallel Hybrid Electric Drive Train is a hybrid architecture in which the **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and the **Electric Motor (EM)** are mechanically coupled in parallel such that either or both can deliver tractive torque to the wheels simultaneously.

**Block Diagram:**

> ICE → [Clutch] → [Torque Coupler] → [Transmission] → [Final Drive] → Wheels
> ↑
> Battery → [Inverter] → EM ————↗ (joins the coupler)

**Key features:**
1. ICE and EM are **mechanically independent** in the energy path.
2. Either source alone, or both combined, can propel the vehicle.
3. No double energy conversion (unlike series) → **higher efficiency at high loads**.

**Real-world examples:**
- **Honda Civic Hybrid** (IMA — Integrated Motor Assist, 1st–2nd generation).
- **Honda Insight (1st generation, 1999)**.
- **Diesel-electric locomotives** (large-scale parallel hybrids).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **parallel** with **series-parallel (power-split)** hybrids like the **Toyota Prius THS**. The Prius is **NOT** a pure parallel hybrid — it is a series-parallel hybrid using a planetary gear power-split device. Writing "Toyota Prius" as an example of a pure parallel hybrid will **lose 1 mark**.

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)** Compare the parallel hybrid drive train with the series hybrid drive train on the basis of: (i) mechanical coupling, (ii) efficiency at city driving, (iii) efficiency at highway driving.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical coupling of ICE to wheels** | **No** — ICE only drives a generator | **Yes** — ICE directly drives wheels |
| **City-driving efficiency** | **Higher** (EM handles all low loads efficiently) | Moderate (EM can be used, but ICE still coupled) |
| **Highway-driving efficiency** | Lower (multiple energy conversions cause losses) | **Higher** (direct mechanical path, few conversions) |
| **Complexity** | Lower (no mechanical torque blending needed) | Higher (clutch control, torque blending) |
| **Suitability** | Urban buses, delivery vehicles | Passenger cars, long-haul applications |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Style, aligned with KTU 2024 ESE pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Configuration Analysis

**(a) (7 Marks)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the configuration of a parallel hybrid electric drive train. Discuss the different operating modes with their energy flow paths. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Battery] → [Inverter] → [EM] ——┐
                                 [Torque Coupling] → [Transmission] → [Differential] → [Wheels]
[ICE] ————————————————————————┘
```

**Operating Modes:**

1. **ICE-Only Mode:** Used during steady high-speed cruising on highway. EM is decoupled. Battery SOC is monitored; if low, ICE drives the EM as generator.
2. **EM-Only Mode:** Used at low speeds, city driving, and reverse. ICE is shut off. Energy comes from battery alone.
3. **Parallel (Combined) Mode:** Used during acceleration or hill climbing. Both ICE and EM deliver torque simultaneously. Their torques are summed: $T_w = (T_{ICE} + T_{EM}) \times i_g \times i_0 \times \eta_t$.
4. **Regenerative Braking Mode:** ICE off, EM acts as generator, kinetic energy → electrical energy → battery.
5. **Charging/Stationary Mode:** ICE drives EM as generator while vehicle is stationary (e.g., at signal), charging the battery.

**Energy flow directions** in each mode must be explicitly shown on the diagram during valuation.

---

**(b) (7 Marks)** Explain the P0, P1, P2, P3, and P4 classifications of electric machine placement in a parallel hybrid drive train. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Model Solution:**

The classification follows the **Bosch / SAE J1715** convention based on the position of the e-machine (electric machine) along the powertrain:

- **P0 — Belt-driven Starter Generator (BSG):** Mounted on the engine via belt. Replaces the conventional alternator. Provides mild-hybrid functionality. Torque assist is limited (typically 5–15 kW).
  - *Advantage:* Lowest cost, minimal packaging changes, fits in conventional ICE vehicles.
  - *Disadvantage:* Cannot propel vehicle alone (no creep in EV mode at very low speed on all configurations); torque limited by belt slip.

- **P1 — Crankshaft-Mounted Motor:** E-machine directly mounted on the ICE crankshaft, between engine and clutch.
  - *Advantage:* No belt losses; can start the engine instantly; full hybrid capability.
  - *Disadvantage:* E-machine must rotate at engine speed (limited high-speed operation); adds rotating mass on crank.

- **P2 — Between Clutch and Transmission:** E-machine placed after the engine clutch, before the gearbox. Can decouple from engine.
  - *Advantage:* Can propel vehicle in pure EV mode (clutch open); flexible torque blending.
  - *Disadvantage:* Requires additional clutch; packaging complexity.

- **P3 — At Transmission Output / Final Drive Input:** E-machine placed after the gearbox, integrated with final drive.
  - *Advantage:* Operates at wheel-rotational speed (better for high-speed efficiency); full EV mode possible.
  - *Disadvantage:* Must handle high torque at low speeds; complex cooling.

- **P4 — At Rear Axle (or Second Axle):** E-machine drives a separate axle entirely.
  - *Advantage:* Enables through-the-road AWD hybrid; no modification to front driveline.
  - *Disadvantage:* Requires additional power electronics and cooling for rear-mounted machine.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing P1 with P0:** P0 is **belt-driven**, P1 is **directly coupled to the crankshaft**. Students often interchange these → 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting P4 enables AWD:** This is a favorite follow-up question. Mention the "through-the-road" AWD capability.
> 3. **Drawing P2 incorrectly:** P2 is **after the clutch** that separates the engine. If drawn between engine and clutch, it is P1.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a) (7 Marks)** Derive the expression for the total tractive force required for a parallel hybrid vehicle, including rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and grade resistance. State clearly the assumptions made. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ moving up an incline of angle $\alpha$ with speed $v$ and acceleration $\frac{dv}{dt}$.

**Forces opposing motion:**
1. Rolling resistance $F_{roll} = m g f_r \cos\alpha$
2. Aerodynamic drag $F_{aero} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_D A_f v^2$
3. Grade resistance $F_{grade} = m g \sin\alpha$

Applying Newton's second law (with rotational inertia factor $\delta$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t - F_{roll} - F_{aero} - F_{grade} &= m \delta \frac{dv}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $F_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t = m \delta \frac{dv}{dt} + m g f_r \cos\alpha + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_D A_f v^2 + m g \sin\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. No wind component along vehicle direction.
2. Constant $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³.
3. $f_r$, $C_D$, $A_f$ are constant (no temperature variation).
4. Single lumped mass model (rigid body).
5. Small-angle approximation NOT applied — exact $\sin$ and $\cos$ retained.

---

**(b) (7 Marks)** A parallel hybrid passenger car has the following specifications: curb weight $m = 1400$ kg, $C_D A_f = 0.65$ m², rolling resistance $f_r = 0.010$, transmission efficiency $\eta_t = 0.90$, final drive ratio $i_0 = 3.5$, gear ratio in top gear $i_g = 1.0$, wheel radius $r_w = 0.31$ m. The vehicle cruises at 90 km/h on a level road. Compute: (i) the tractive force required, (ii) the wheel torque, (iii) the power at wheels, and (iv) the required ICE and EM torques if the EM supplies 25% of total tractive force. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given data:**
- $m = 1400$ kg, $f_r = 0.010$, $C_D A_f = 0.65$ m², $\eta_t = 0.90$, $i_0 = 3.5$, $i_g = 1.0$, $r_w = 0.31$ m, $v = 90$ km/h, flat road ($\alpha = 0$), constant cruise ($dv/dt = 0$).
- $v = 90 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 25$ m/s.

**(i) Tractive Force:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= m g f_r + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a (C_D A_f) v^2 \\
F_t &= (1400)(9.81)(0.010) + (0.5)(1.2)(0.65)(25)^2 \\
F_t &= 137.34 + 243.75 \\
F_t &= 381.09 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Wheel Torque:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_w &= F_t \times r_w = 381.09 \times 0.31 \\
T_w &= 118.14 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Power at Wheels:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_w &= F_t \times v = 381.09 \times 25 \\
P_w &= 9527.25 \text{ W} \approx 9.53 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Torque Split** with $\beta = 0.25$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{EM} &= 0.25 \times 381.09 = 95.27 \text{ N} \\
F_{ICE} &= 0.75 \times 381.09 = 285.82 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wheel torque contributions:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{EM,wheel} &= 95.27 \times 0.31 = 29.53 \text{ N·m} \\
T_{ICE,wheel} &= 285.82 \times 0.31 = 88.60 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Torques at each shaft (overall ratio $= i_g \times i_0 = 3.5$, assuming both sources see same overall ratio):
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{EM,shaft} &= \frac{29.53}{3.5 \times 0.90} = \frac{29.53}{3.15} = 9.38 \text{ N·m} \\
T_{ICE,shaft} &= \frac{88.60}{3.5 \times 0.90} = \frac{88.60}{3.15} = 28.13 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget the **transmission efficiency factor** in shaft-torque calculation. The correct formula is $T_{shaft} = \dfrac{T_{wheel}}{i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}$ — writing $T_{shaft} = \dfrac{T_{wheel}}{i_g \cdot i_0}$ gives an inflated shaft torque and costs marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Parallel Hybrid** has both ICE and EM mechanically coupled to the wheels.
- ✅ The total tractive force equation is $F_t = m \delta \dfrac{dv}{dt} + m g f_r \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_D A_f v^2 + m g \sin\alpha$.
- ✅ Wheel torque: $T_w = F_t \cdot r_w$. Shaft torque: $T_{shaft} = \dfrac{T_w}{i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}$.
- ✅ Power at wheels: $P_w = F_t \cdot v$.
- ✅ Parallel hybrids have **higher highway efficiency** than series; series hybrids have **higher city efficiency**.
- ✅ **Operating modes**: ICE-only, EM-only, Parallel (combined), Regenerative Braking, Charging mode.
- ✅ **P0/P1/P2/P3/P4 classification** is based on e-machine position; remember P0 = belt-driven BSG, P4 = rear-axle (AWD).
- ✅ Typical HEV battery voltage: **144 V – 360 V DC**; SOC operating window: **30% – 80%**.
- ✅ Real-world examples: **Honda Civic Hybrid (IMA), Honda Insight, diesel-electric locomotives**.
- ✅ **Toyota Prius is NOT a pure parallel** — it is a series-parallel (power-split) using a planetary gear.
- ✅ Transmission efficiency $\eta_t \approx 0.90 – 0.95$ — always include it in shaft torque calculations.
- ✅ Regenerative braking recovers **10% – 30%** of kinetic energy typically.
- ✅ For 14-mark derivations, **always state assumptions** explicitly — examiners reward this.
- ✅ In numerical problems, always **convert km/h to m/s** by multiplying by $\frac{1000}{3600}$ or $\frac{5}{18}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Battery Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Battery Parameters?

**Battery Parameters** are the standardized, measurable electrical, chemical, thermal, and performance characteristics that quantify the behavior, capability, and lifespan of an electrochemical storage device (cell, module, or pack). In the context of **Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs)**, these parameters are the fundamental design constraints that determine driving range, acceleration, charging time, and total cost of ownership.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination-Ready):**
> *"Battery parameters are quantitative descriptors of an electrochemical cell's electrical output, energy storage capability, power delivery, thermal response, and aging behavior. They form the input design variables for the Battery Management System (BMS), vehicle range estimation algorithms, and powertrain sizing in HEV/EV applications."*

---

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy: Battery = A Water Tower System 💧

Imagine a **water tank on a tower** supplying a village:

| Water Tower Component | Battery Equivalent | Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| Total volume of water | Total charge stored | **Capacity (Ah)** |
| Pressure at the tap | Voltage pushing current | **Voltage (V)** |
| Diameter of the outlet pipe | Maximum current flow capability | **C-Rate (C)** |
| Water lost through tank walls | Parasitic energy loss | **Self-discharge (%)** |
| Rust/cracks degrading the tank | Capacity fade over time | **State of Health (SOH)** |
| How full the tank is right now | Energy remaining | **State of Charge (SOC)** |
| Max height water can be thrown | Max energy per unit mass | **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** |
| Rate of water flow per second | Instantaneous power output | **Specific Power (W/kg)** |

Just as a civil engineer specifies tank diameter, height, and pipe size, a HEV powertrain engineer must specify cell voltage, capacity, C-rate, and energy density **before** selecting a battery.

---

## 1.3 Why Battery Parameters are the Heart of HEV/EV Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why this topic carries high weightage:**
> 1. **Range Anxiety** is dictated by **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)**.
> 2. **Acceleration / Hill-climb** is dictated by **Specific Power (W/kg)**.
> 3. **Battery life & warranty** depend on **Cycle Life** and **Depth of Discharge (DOD)**.
> 4. **BMS algorithms** rely entirely on accurate **SOC** and **SOH** estimation.
> 5. **Thermal runaway prevention** uses **Internal Resistance** and temperature coefficients.

The standard reference cell used in the automotive industry for benchmarking is often the **18650 cylindrical cell** (18 mm diameter × 65 mm length) and the modern **21700 cell** (21 mm × 70 mm), with nominal voltages of **3.6 V – 3.7 V** for NMC/Li-ion chemistry.

---

## 1.4 Standard Reference Constants to Memorize

- **Faraday's Constant (F)** = **96,485 C/mol** ≈ **26.8 Ah/mol**
- **1 kWh** = **3.6 MJ** (Mega-Joules)
- **Electron charge (e)** = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
- **Standard cell voltage (Li-ion, NMC)** ≈ **3.6 – 3.7 V**
- **Standard cell voltage (Lead-Acid)** = **2.0 V (nominal)**
- **Standard cell voltage (NiMH)** = **1.2 V (nominal)**

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ten Pillars of Battery Parameters

Battery parameters are classified into **four functional families** for KTU examination purposes:

### Family A — Electrical (Voltage) Parameters

#### (i) Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) — `V_oc`
- Voltage across terminals when **no load is connected** and the cell has rested.
- Depends purely on **thermodynamic equilibrium** (Nernst equation).
- **Key fact:** OCV is a function of SOC, NOT a constant.

#### (ii) Terminal Voltage (V_t) — Loaded Condition
$$V_t = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$$
The voltage drops below OCV during discharge (and rises above OCV during charge) due to **ohmic, activation, and concentration polarization losses**.

#### (iii) Nominal Voltage (V_nom)
- Manufacturer-specified "average" working voltage (e.g., 3.7 V for Li-ion).
- Used for **energy calculations**: E = V_nom × Capacity.

---

### Family B — Charge & Capacity Parameters

#### (iv) Capacity (C) — Measured in Ampere-hours (Ah)
- The **total charge** a fully charged cell can deliver under specified conditions.
- 1 Ah = 3600 Coulombs.
- **Nominal Capacity** is rated at a standard discharge rate (usually **C/5** or **1C**).

#### (v) State of Charge (SOC) — %
- The ratio of **remaining capacity** to **maximum (rated) capacity**.

$$SOC(\%) = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$$

- SOC = 100% → Fully Charged
- SOC = 0% → Fully Discharged (cutoff voltage reached)

#### (vi) Depth of Discharge (DOD) — %
- The complement of SOC.

$$DOD(\%) = 100 - SOC(\%)$$

- **KTU Trap:** DOD and SOC are **opposites** but often **confused**. A 70% DOD means 30% SOC.

---

### Family C — Energy & Power Parameters

#### (vii) Energy (E) — Watt-hours (Wh) or Joules
- The total electrical work a battery can perform.

$$E = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah} \quad [Wh]$$

#### (viii) Specific Energy — Wh/kg (also called Gravimetric Energy Density)
- Energy per unit **mass**. Determines **vehicle range**.

$$E_{sp} = \frac{E_{Wh}}{m_{kg}} \quad [Wh/kg]$$

#### (ix) Energy Density — Wh/L (also called Volumetric Energy Density)
- Energy per unit **volume**. Determines **pack packaging**.

$$E_{vol} = \frac{E_{Wh}}{V_{liters}} \quad [Wh/L]$$

#### (x) Specific Power — W/kg
- Maximum power per unit **mass**. Determines **acceleration**.

$$P_{sp} = \frac{V^2}{R_{int} \cdot m} \quad [W/kg]$$

#### (xi) Power Density — W/L
- Maximum power per unit volume.

---

### Family D — Lifetime & Performance Parameters

#### (xii) C-Rate
- A normalized measure of **charge/discharge current** relative to rated capacity.

$$I = C\text{-rate} \times C_{rated}$$

- **1C** of a 50 Ah battery = 50 A
- **2C** of a 50 Ah battery = 100 A
- **0.5C (C/2)** = 25 A

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Higher C-rates cause **greater internal heating** and **reduced usable capacity** (Peukert Effect).

#### (xiii) State of Health (SOH) — %
- The ratio of **current maximum capacity** to **original rated capacity**.

$$SOH(\%) = \frac{C_{max,current}}{C_{rated,new}} \times 100$$

- A battery is considered **end-of-life (EOL)** in automotive applications when **SOH ≤ 80%**.

#### (xiv) Cycle Life
- Number of complete **charge-discharge cycles** before capacity drops to a defined threshold (typically 80% of rated).
- Inversely related to DOD — higher DOD means **fewer cycles** (Ragone's trade-off).

#### (xv) Internal Resistance (R_int) — Ohms (Ω)
- Opposition to current flow inside the cell.
- Comprises **ohmic** + **activation** + **concentration** components.
- **Rises with age, temperature extremes, and high C-rates.**

#### (xvi) Self-Discharge Rate — %/month
- Charge lost when the battery sits idle.
- Li-ion ≈ **1–2% per month**; NiMH ≈ **20–30% per month**.

#### (xvii) Coulombic Efficiency (η_C) — %
- Ratio of charge extracted during discharge to charge supplied during charge.

$$\eta_C = \frac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100$$

#### (xviii) Energy Efficiency (η_E) — %
- Ratio of energy extracted to energy supplied.

$$\eta_E = \frac{E_{discharge}}{E_{charge}} \times 100$$

---

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy of a Battery from Capacity

**Problem Statement:** A 50 Ah Li-ion cell has a nominal voltage of 3.7 V. Derive its energy in Wh and Joules.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of electrical energy from a DC source.**

$$E = P \times t$$

This equation states: Energy equals power multiplied by the time over which the power is delivered.

**Step 2 — Substitute Power = Voltage × Current.**

$$E = (V \times I) \times t$$

Since P = V·I for a DC circuit, we replace P with V·I.

**Step 3 — Recognize that Capacity is Current × Time.**

$$C_{Ah} = I \times t$$

By definition, the capacity in Ampere-hours equals the discharge current multiplied by the discharge time.

**Step 4 — Substitute Capacity into the energy equation.**

$$E = V \times C_{Ah}$$

Energy in Watt-hours equals nominal voltage multiplied by the ampere-hour rating.

**Step 5 — Insert numerical values.**

$$E = 3.7 \, V \times 50 \, Ah$$

$$E = 185 \, Wh$$

**Step 6 — Convert Watt-hours to Joules using the conversion 1 Wh = 3600 J.**

$$E_{J} = 185 \times 3600$$

$$E_{J} = 666,000 \, J = 6.66 \times 10^5 \, J$$

**Step 7 — Express in Mega-Joules for vehicle-level comparison.**

$$E_{J} = 0.666 \, MJ$$

✅ **Result:** The cell stores **185 Wh** or **0.666 MJ** of energy. A typical 60 kWh EV pack (≈ 216 MJ) contains roughly 325 such cells.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: C-Rate and Discharge Time Relationship

**Problem Statement:** A 100 Ah battery is discharged at 50 A. Determine (a) the C-rate, and (b) the theoretical discharge time.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of C-rate.**

$$\text{C-rate} = \frac{I_{discharge}}{C_{rated}}$$

The C-rate is the discharge current divided by the rated capacity of the battery.

**Step 2 — Insert the given values.**

$$\text{C-rate} = \frac{50 \, A}{100 \, Ah}$$

$$\text{C-rate} = 0.5 \, C \quad (\text{also written as C/2})$$

This means the battery is being discharged at half its rated rate.

**Step 3 — Use the inverse relationship between C-rate and time.**

$$t_{discharge} = \frac{C_{rated}}{I_{discharge}} = \frac{1}{\text{C-rate}}$$

The discharge time in hours equals the reciprocal of the C-rate when using rated capacity.

**Step 4 — Calculate the discharge time.**

$$t_{discharge} = \frac{100 \, Ah}{50 \, A} = 2 \, \text{hours}$$

✅ **Result:** At **0.5C rate**, a **100 Ah battery** discharges fully in **2 hours**. By contrast, at 2C (200 A), it would deplete in just 0.5 hours (30 minutes).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SOC Estimation by Coulomb Counting

**Problem Statement:** A 60 Ah EV battery starts fully charged. The vehicle consumes 18 Ah during driving. Find the new SOC.

**Step 1 — Use the SOC formula.**

$$SOC_{final} = SOC_{initial} - \frac{Q_{consumed}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$$

The final SOC is the initial SOC minus the percentage of capacity that was consumed.

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$SOC_{final} = 100 - \frac{18}{60} \times 100$$

$$SOC_{final} = 100 - 30 = 70\%$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the corresponding DOD.**

$$DOD = 100 - SOC = 100 - 70 = 30\%$$

✅ **Result:** The battery is now at **70% SOC** and **30% DOD**.

> [!NOTE]
> **BMS uses Coulomb Counting continuously**, integrating current over time: $SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) - \frac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau$. Errors accumulate, so it is **corrected periodically via OCV measurement** (since OCV correlates with SOC at rest).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Specific Energy and EV Range Estimation

**Problem Statement:** A battery pack has total mass 400 kg, stores 80 kWh, and propels a 1500 kg EV at energy consumption of 15 kWh per 100 km. Find specific energy and range.

**Step 1 — Calculate Specific Energy.**

$$E_{sp} = \frac{E_{total}}{m_{pack}} = \frac{80,000 \, Wh}{400 \, kg} = 200 \, Wh/kg$$

This represents the energy stored per kilogram of the battery pack.

**Step 2 — Calculate driving range.**

$$\text{Range} = \frac{E_{pack}}{E_{consumption \, per \, km}} = \frac{80 \, kWh}{0.15 \, kWh/km}$$

$$\text{Range} = 533.33 \, km$$

✅ **Result:** The pack has specific energy **200 Wh/kg** and the vehicle can travel approximately **533 km** on a full charge.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Peukert's Law — Capacity Loss at High C-Rates

**Problem Statement:** A lead-acid battery rated 100 Ah (at the 20-hour rate) is discharged at 200 A. Peukert exponent k = 1.25. Find the actual usable capacity.

**Step 1 — Recall Peukert's Law.**

$$t_{d} = \frac{C_{rated}}{I^k} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad C_{actual} = C_{rated} \cdot \left(\frac{I_{rated}}{I_{actual}}\right)^{k-1}$$

Peukert's Law states that the usable capacity decreases as the discharge current increases.

**Step 2 — The rated current for 100 Ah over 20 hours.**

$$I_{rated} = \frac{100 \, Ah}{20 \, h} = 5 \, A$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula for actual capacity.**

$$C_{actual} = 100 \times \left(\frac{5}{200}\right)^{1.25-1}$$

$$C_{actual} = 100 \times \left(\frac{5}{200}\right)^{0.25}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio raised to the power.**

$$\frac{5}{200} = 0.025$$

$$0.025^{0.25} = (0.025)^{1/4} = (2.5 \times 10^{-2})^{0.25}$$

Using $0.025^{0.5} = 0.1581$ and then $\sqrt{0.1581} = 0.3976$:

$$C_{actual} = 100 \times 0.3976 = 39.76 \, Ah$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

$$C_{actual} \approx 39.8 \, Ah$$

✅ **Result:** Although the battery is rated 100 Ah, discharging it at **200 A (2C)** reduces the **effective usable capacity to only ~39.8 Ah** — a **60% loss**. This is why high-performance EVs use **Li-ion with Peukert exponent k ≈ 1.05** (nearly ideal), not lead-acid.

---

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 1]**
Define **State of Charge (SOC)** and **Depth of Discharge (DOD)** for a battery. How are they mathematically related? Why are both parameters important for BMS operation?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition of SOC (1 mark):**
State of Charge (SOC) is defined as the ratio of the **remaining capacity** in a battery to its **rated (maximum) capacity**, expressed as a percentage:

$$SOC(\%) = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$$

**Definition of DOD (1 mark):**
Depth of Discharge (DOD) is the percentage of the battery's rated capacity that has been **discharged** relative to the fully charged state. It is the mathematical complement of SOC.

**Mathematical Relationship & BMS Importance (1 mark):**
$$DOD(\%) = 100 - SOC(\%)$$

**Importance:** SOC is used by the BMS to estimate **remaining driving range** and to **prevent over-discharge** (which permanently damages cells). DOD is used for **cycle-life prediction**, since batteries cycled at high DOD (e.g., 100%) have far shorter lives than those cycled at 30% DOD.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
What is **C-rate** of a battery? A 60 Ah EV battery is discharged at 120 A. Calculate the discharge time and the energy delivered if nominal voltage is 3.7 V.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
C-rate is a normalized measure of the **discharge (or charge) current** relative to the battery's rated capacity. A current of "1C" means a current equal to the rated Ah rating.

**C-rate calculation (1 mark):**
$$\text{C-rate} = \frac{I_{discharge}}{C_{rated}} = \frac{120 \, A}{60 \, Ah} = 2C$$

**Discharge time and energy (1 mark):**
$$t_{discharge} = \frac{C_{rated}}{I} = \frac{60 \, Ah}{120 \, A} = 0.5 \, h = 30 \, \text{minutes}$$

$$E = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah} = 3.7 \times 60 = 222 \, Wh$$

---

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **State of Health (SOH)** of a battery. How is it measured? Discuss its importance in warranty claims, second-life applications, and predictive maintenance for EVs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An EV battery rated 200 Ah delivers only 160 Ah after 4 years of use. The same battery was originally delivering 210 Ah when new. Find:
(i) The original rated capacity and the current maximum capacity.
(ii) The SOH as a percentage.

## ✅ COMPLETE MODEL SOLUTIONS

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Definitions [7 Marks = 1.75 each]

**(i) Specific Energy (Wh/kg):**
Specific energy is the amount of energy a battery can store per unit of mass. It is calculated as:
$$E_{sp} = \frac{E_{total} \, [Wh]}{m_{pack} \, [kg]}$$

**Significance for EV:** Higher specific energy → **longer driving range** without increasing weight. Li-ion NMC (~200 Wh/kg) outperforms NiMH (~80 Wh/kg) and lead-acid (~40 Wh/kg). **Modern target:** > 400 Wh/kg (solid-state, Li-air). **[1.75 marks]**

**(ii) Energy Density (Wh/L):**
Energy density is the amount of energy stored per unit **volume**.

$$E_{vol} = \frac{E_{total} \, [Wh]}{V_{pack} \, [L]}$$

**Significance for EV:** Higher energy density → **smaller battery pack volume** for the same range. This allows more cabin space and lighter packaging. NMC (~600 Wh/L) > LFP (~300 Wh/L). **[1.75 marks]**

**(iii) Specific Power (W/kg):**
Specific power is the maximum power a battery can deliver per unit mass.

$$P_{sp} = \frac{P_{max} \, [W]}{m_{pack} \, [kg]}$$

**Significance for EV:** Higher specific power → **better acceleration and regenerative braking capability**. PHEVs (need high power for boost mode) prioritize this over specific energy. **[1.75 marks]**

**(iv) Power Density (W/L):**
Power density is the maximum power per unit volume. Same principle as specific power but in volumetric terms. Critical for **compact high-power applications** like racing EVs. **[1.75 marks]**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Cells in series: N_s = 192
- Each cell: V_cell = 3.75 V, C_cell = 33.1 Ah
- Total energy (rated): 24 kWh
- Consumption: 14.6 kWh / 100 km
- Pack mass: m = 290 kg

**(i) Total pack voltage [1.75 marks]:**
$$V_{pack} = N_s \times V_{cell} = 192 \times 3.75 = 720 \, V$$

**(ii) Total pack energy (verification) [1.75 marks]:**
$$E = V_{pack} \times C_{pack} = 720 \, V \times 33.1 \, Ah = 23,832 \, Wh \approx 23.83 \, kWh$$

This is close to the rated 24 kWh (small differences arise because actual capacity is slightly higher at low C-rates and manufacturer roundoff).

**(iii) Driving range [1.75 marks]:**
$$\text{Range} = \frac{E_{pack}}{E_{consumption \, per \, km}} = \frac{24 \, kWh}{0.146 \, kWh/km} = 164.38 \, km$$

**(iv) Specific energy of the pack [1.75 marks]:**
$$E_{sp} = \frac{24,000 \, Wh}{290 \, kg} = 82.76 \, Wh/kg$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note to students:** The cell-level specific energy is $\frac{3.75 \times 33.1}{0.8} \approx 155$ Wh/kg, but the **pack-level** specific energy is only ~83 Wh/kg. The difference (≈ 47%) is due to **pack-level mass overhead** — casing, BMS, wiring, cooling system. Always distinguish cell-level from pack-level values.

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — SOH Definition & Importance [7 Marks]

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
State of Health (SOH) is the ratio of a battery's **current maximum usable capacity** to its **original rated capacity** (when new), expressed as a percentage:

$$SOH(\%) = \frac{C_{max,current}}{C_{rated,new}} \times 100$$

**Measurement Methods (2 marks):**
1. **Direct Capacity Test:** Fully charge, then discharge at constant current to cutoff voltage. Measured Ah divided by rated Ah.
2. **Incremental Capacity Analysis (ICA):** Plot dQ/dV vs V — peaks shift with aging.
3. **Internal Resistance Method:** SOH correlated with rising R_int (less reliable alone).
4. **Machine Learning on BMS data** (advanced, used by Tesla, BYD).

**Importance (3.5 marks):**
- **Warranty Claims (1 mark):** Manufacturers like Tesla guarantee 70% SOH after 8 years. SOH below threshold → free replacement.
- **Second-Life Applications (1 mark):** EV batteries with 70-80% SOH (unsuitable for cars) are repurposed for **grid storage, solar storage, or backup power** where lower energy density is acceptable.
- **Predictive Maintenance (1 mark):** SOH trends predict imminent failures; allows scheduling of battery replacement.
- **Resale Value (0.5 mark):** Used EV price is heavily discounted based on SOH report (e.g., Recurrent Auto reports).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical on SOH [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Rated capacity (new) = 210 Ah
- Current max capacity = 160 Ah
- Rated value originally = 200 Ah (label value)
- Nominal voltage = 3.6 V
- EOL threshold = 80% SOH

**(i) Original rated vs current maximum [1.5 marks]:**
- Original rated capacity (manufacturer label) = **200 Ah** (Note: actual was 210 Ah — manufacturers often under-rate to give safety margin)
- Current maximum capacity = **160 Ah**

**(ii) SOH calculation [1.5 marks]:**
Using the actual original capacity of 210 Ah:
$$SOH = \frac{160}{210} \times 100 = 76.19\%$$

If using the label rating of 200 Ah:
$$SOH = \frac{160}{200} \times 100 = 80\%$$

**Convention:** Most manufacturers and ISO standards use the **nameplate rated capacity (200 Ah)** for SOH calculation, giving **SOH = 80%** in this case.

**(iii) Warranty eligibility [2 marks]:**
At exactly 80% SOH (using nameplate), the battery is at the **EOL threshold**. Whether it qualifies for replacement depends on the **manufacturer's policy**:
- If threshold is "**≤ 80%**" → does **not** qualify (boundary case).
- If threshold is "**< 80%**" → qualifies for replacement.
- If we used the 210 Ah actual baseline, SOH = 76.19% < 80% → **qualifies for replacement**.

**(iv) Remaining energy in kWh [2 marks]:**
$$E = V_{nom} \times C_{current} = 3.6 \, V \times 160 \, Ah = 576 \, Wh = 0.576 \, kWh$$

If considering a 100-cell pack in series (each 3.6 V, total 360 V):
$$E_{pack} = 360 \, V \times 160 \, Ah = 57,600 \, Wh = 57.6 \, kWh$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes (Lose 1–2 marks):**
> 1. **Confusing SOC with SOH** — SOC is momentary state; SOH is lifetime health.
> 2. **Using current capacity instead of rated capacity for SOH denominator** — Always use the **original nameplate rating** as denominator.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert Ah to Wh** when asked for energy — Multiply by V_nom!
> 4. **Mixing cell-level and pack-level energy** — State clearly whether your answer is per cell or for the full pack.
> 5. **Forgetting units in final answer** — Always write **Wh, kWh, Ah, V** explicitly.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Voltage family:** OCV (no load) > Terminal Voltage (loaded). Terminal = OCV − IR_int.
- ✅ **Capacity unit:** 1 Ah = 3600 C. Capacity = Current × Time.
- ✅ **Energy = V_nom × C_Ah** in Watt-hours.
- ✅ **SOC + DOD = 100%** — Always complementary.
- ✅ **C-rate = I / C_rated** — Higher C-rate → faster discharge + more heat + Peukert losses.
- ✅ **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** determines EV **range**; **Specific Power (W/kg)** determines **acceleration**.
- ✅ **SOH = (C_current / C_new) × 100%** — EOL threshold in automotive = **80%**.
- ✅ **Cycle Life inversely related to DOD** — shallow cycles (20–30% DOD) extend life dramatically.
- ✅ **Internal Resistance rises with:** age, low temperature, high SOC, high C-rate.
- ✅ **Peukert's Law:** $C_{actual} = C_{rated}(I_{rated}/I)^{k-1}$ — k close to 1 means ideal (Li-ion ~1.05).
- ✅ **Nernst Equation link:** $E_{cell} = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ — explains OCV dependence on concentration/SOC.
- ✅ **Cell vs Pack:** Cell E_sp ≈ 200 Wh/kg (NMC); Pack E_sp ≈ 80–100 Wh/kg (overhead losses).
- ✅ **Ragone Plot trade-off:** Energy and power are inversely related — choose based on application (BEV vs HEV vs PHEV).
- ✅ **Coulomb Counting** = primary SOC method; **OCV-SOC lookup** = calibration/correction method.
- ✅ **Self-discharge order:** Li-ion (1–2%/month) << NiMH (20–30%/month) ≈ Lead-acid (4–6%/month).
- ✅ **Efficiency hierarchy:** Coulombic (η_C) > Energy (η_E) always, because η_E includes voltage losses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Technologies - Lead-Acid Battery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Battery Technologies - Lead-Acid Battery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Lead-Acid Battery (LAB)** is a rechargeable (secondary) electrochemical energy storage device that converts chemical energy into electrical energy via reversible redox reactions between **lead (Pb)** at the negative electrode, **lead dioxide (PbO₂)** at the positive electrode, and a **sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)** electrolyte solution. It is the oldest and most commercially mature rechargeable battery chemistry, invented in 1859 by the French physicist **Gaston Planté**, and remains the world's most widely produced secondary battery by tonnage.

The fundamental per-cell open-circuit voltage is **2.04 V (nominal 2.0 V)**, with a typical 12 V automotive battery comprising **six cells connected in series**. The standard specific energy is **30–50 Wh/kg**, with a round-trip energy efficiency of **70–85%**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rechargeable Fuel Tank"

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Analogy — Think of a Lead-Acid Battery as a "Reversible Sand Pile"**
> Imagine two buckets (electrodes) connected by a salt-water bridge (electrolyte). When you tip sand from Bucket A into Bucket B, gravity does the work (this is **discharge** — current flows). To restore the original state, you must lift the sand back (this is **charging** — external energy input is required). The acid concentration acts as a "fuel gauge": as the battery discharges, sulfuric acid is consumed and water is produced (density falls from ~1.28 g/cm³ to ~1.10 g/cm³). Refilling it electrically returns the acid. Unlike a gasoline tank, however, you can do this tipping back-and-forth hundreds of times.

## 1.3 Why Lead-Acid Matters for Hybrid & Electric Vehicles

Even in modern BEVs (e.g., Tesla) and HEVs (Toyota Prius), a **12 V lead-acid battery is still universally retained** to power low-voltage systems: ECUs, lighting, infotainment, safety controllers, and the high-voltage contactor coil. This is the **auxiliary battery** (also called the *SLI battery* — Starting, Lighting, Ignition). Advanced forms (AGM, EFB) are also used in **48 V mild-hybrid** architectures for regenerative braking energy buffering.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| **Nominal Cell Voltage** | **2.0 V** |
| Open-Circuit Voltage (full charge) | **2.04 – 2.10 V** |
| 12 V Module Voltage | 6 cells × 2.0 V = **12.0 V** |
| Specific Energy | **30 – 50 Wh/kg** |
| Energy Density | **60 – 110 Wh/L** |
| Cycle Life (80% DOD) | **200 – 500 cycles** |
| Round-trip Efficiency | **70 – 85 %** |
| Self-discharge Rate | **3 – 20 %/month** |
| Operating Temperature | **−30 °C to +50 °C** |
| Electrolyte Density (full charge) | **1.26 – 1.28 g/cm³** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of a Lead-Acid Cell

A single 2 V cell comprises six engineered subsystems:

### (a) Positive Plate — Lead Dioxide (PbO₂)
- **Active material:** PbO₂ (dark brown, tetragonal crystal structure)
- **Grid support:** Pb-Ca / Pb-Sb alloy (provides mechanical strength and current collection)
- Function: site of **reduction during discharge**, oxidation during charge.

### (b) Negative Plate — Spongy Lead (Pb)
- **Active material:** porous sponge lead (grey-black, high surface area)
- **Grid:** Pb-Ca alloy to suppress gas evolution and water loss
- Function: site of **oxidation during discharge**, reduction during charge.

### (c) Electrolyte — Sulfuric Acid (H₂SO₄)
- Aqueous solution, typically **37 % H₂SO₄ by weight** at full charge
- Density (specific gravity) at 27 °C:
  - Full charge → **1.28 g/cm³**
  - 50 % discharged → **1.19 g/cm³**
  - Fully discharged → **1.10 g/cm³**
- Acts as **ionic conductor** (H⁺, SO₄²⁻) and as an **active reactant** (consumed during discharge).

### (d) Separator
- Microporous polyethylene (PE), fiberglass mat (AGM), or gel matrix
- Prevents electrical short between plates while permitting ionic flow
- Must be chemically stable in strong acid

### (e) Container / Case
- Polypropylene (PP) or hard rubber, acid-resistant
- Sealed (VRLA) or vented with caps (flooded)

### (f) Vent / Safety Valve
- Flooded: removable caps for water top-up
- VRLA: one-way pressure relief valve (typically 4–8 psi)

## 2.2 Working Principle — The Electrochemistry

### Discharge (Galvanic Mode — Spontaneous)

**At the Negative Plate (Anode, Oxidation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Pb} &\rightarrow \text{Pb}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \\
\text{Pb}^{2+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} &\rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 \\
\hline
\text{Combined: } \text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} &\rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^{-}
\end{aligned}$$

**At the Positive Plate (Cathode, Reduction):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^{+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^{-} &\rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

**Overall Cell Reaction (Discharge):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight — Acid Dilutes During Discharge**
> Notice sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) is **consumed** while water (H₂O) is **produced** on the right-hand side. This is why electrolyte density drops monotonically as State-of-Charge (SOC) decreases — a hydrometer can be used as a primitive SOC indicator.

### Charge (Electrolytic Mode — Non-spontaneous)

The reaction is forced in reverse by an external DC source:

$$\begin{aligned}
2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4
\end{aligned}$$
