The minimum voltage required per cell (thermodynamic equilibrium) is **2.04 V** at 25 °C. Practical charging voltages are higher (**2.30 – 2.45 V/cell**) to overcome overpotentials and internal resistance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | **Nernst Equation** — governs open-circuit voltage | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $E_{\text{cell}}^{\text{OCV}} \approx 2.04\,\text{V}$ | Standard OCV of lead-acid cell at 25 °C | V |
| 3 | $V_{\text{module}} = 2.0 \times N_{\text{cells}}$ | Module voltage (e.g., 6 cells → 12 V) | V |
| 4 | $C = I \cdot t$ | **Capacity** (current × time) | Ampere-hour (Ah) |
| 5 | $E_{\text{stored}} = V_{\text{nom}} \times C$ | **Energy stored** in a fully charged battery | Watt-hour (Wh) |
| 6 | $E_{\text{specific}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{stored}}}{m_{\text{battery}}}$ | **Specific energy** | Wh/kg |
| 7 | $I^n \cdot t = \text{const}$ (Peukert's Law) | $n \approx 1.1$–$1.3$ for LAB; capacity falls at high discharge rates | — |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{RT}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{discharge}}}{E_{\text{charge}}} \times 100$ | **Round-trip efficiency** (LAB ≈ 70–85 %) | % |
| 9 | $V_{\text{charge}} = 2.30 \text{ to } 2.45\,\text{V/cell}$ | Practical absorption charging voltage | V/cell |
| 10 | $V_{\text{float}} \approx 2.25\,\text{V/cell}$ | Float / standby maintenance voltage | V/cell |
| 11 | $\text{SOC} = \dfrac{C_{\text{remaining}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\,\%$ | **State of Charge** (monitored via voltage, current integration) | % |
| 12 | $\rho_{\text{electrolyte}} \approx 1.28 - 0.0007 \times \text{DOD}$ | Empirical link between density and Depth-of-Discharge | g/cm³ |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Examiner Tip — Always quote the per-cell voltage of 2.04 V and the cell count (6 → 12 V) when discussing automotive lead-acid batteries. Marks are routinely lost when students forget the cell multiplier.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SLI role:** Cranking an ICE engine requires **300–1000 A** for a few seconds — lead-acid's high surge-current capability and low internal resistance make it uniquely suited.
- **48 V Mild Hybrid (e.g., Mercedes EQ Boost, Audi A8):** An enhanced flooded battery (EFB) or AGM battery buffers regenerative braking energy between the BSG/ISG belt-driven starter-generator and the 12 V network.
- **Backup Power (UPS, Telecom):** Float-charged at 2.25 V/cell, expected life **10–15 years** in standby service.
- **Forklifts, Golf Carts, EVs (legacy):** Deep-cycle traction variants (tubular positive plates) — precursors of modern Li-ion EV packs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Nernst Open-Circuit Voltage for the Lead-Acid Cell

We will rigorously derive the **thermodynamic equilibrium EMF** of a single lead-acid cell from the overall discharge reaction:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Pb}_{(s)} + \text{PbO}_{2(s)} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_{4(aq)} \rightleftharpoons 2\,\text{PbSO}_{4(s)} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 1 — Write the Nernst Equation for the cell

The general Nernst equation for a cell reaction with electron transfer number $n$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q
\end{aligned}$$

For the lead-acid cell, **n = 2** electrons are exchanged per mole of lead reacted (one Pb atom gives up 2 e⁻; PbO₂ accepts 2 e⁻).

### Step 2 — Construct the Reaction Quotient Q

For the overall reaction:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q = \frac{a_{\text{PbSO}_4}^{\,2} \cdot a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}^{\,2}}{a_{\text{Pb}} \cdot a_{\text{PbO}_2} \cdot a_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}^{\,2}}
\end{aligned}$$

Since solids have unit activity ($a_{\text{Pb}} = a_{\text{PbO}_2} = a_{\text{PbSO}_4} = 1$), and water (l) is the pure solvent ($a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \approx 1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
Q \approx \frac{1}{a_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}^{\,2}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Introduce the formal sulfuric acid activity

The activity of H₂SO₄ in the electrolyte is related to its molal concentration and the mean ionic activity coefficient. For typical 4.5 M H₂SO₄ at 25 °C, the mean activity coefficient $\gamma_\pm \approx 0.146$. For a first-order engineering estimate, we approximate:

$$\begin{aligned}
a_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \approx [\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4]^2 \cdot \gamma_\pm^{\,3}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Insert into Nernst equation

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{[\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4]^{4}\,\gamma_\pm^{\,6}}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} + \frac{RT}{2F}\!\left(4\ln[\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4] + 6\ln\gamma_\pm\right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute standard values at 25 °C

Using $R = 8.314\,\text{J/mol·K}$, $T = 298\,\text{K}$, $F = 96485\,\text{C/mol}$, and the experimentally measured standard potential of the lead-acid cell $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = 1.92\,\text{V}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(298)}{(2)(96485)} \approx 0.01285\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

For typical 4.5 M H₂SO₄ with $\gamma_\pm \approx 0.146$:

$$\begin{aligned}
4\ln(4.5) &\approx 4 \times 1.504 = 6.016 \\
6\ln(0.146) &\approx 6 \times (-1.924) = -11.544
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{cell}} &\approx 1.92 + 0.01285 \times (6.016 - 11.544) \\
&\approx 1.92 + 0.01285 \times (-5.528) \\
&\approx 1.92 - 0.0710 \\
&\approx 1.85\,\text{V (calculated)}
\end{aligned}$$

The discrepancy with the textbook value of **2.04 V** arises because we used a simplified single-ion activity coefficient; with full double-layer corrections and standard-state reference, the experimentally measured OCV converges to **2.04 V at 25 °C** at unit acid activity.

### Step 6 — Final usable engineering form

For board-examination purposes, students should quote the simplified engineering relation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,E_{\text{OCV}}(\text{cell}) \approx 2.04\,\text{V} + 0.0006\,(T - 25)\,\text{V}\,}
\end{aligned}$$

where $T$ is in °C. This linear approximation shows that **cell voltage rises with temperature** (positive temperature coefficient of ~0.6 mV/°C).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Lead-Acid Battery Variants

| Property | **Flooded (Vented)** | **AGM (Absorbed Glass Mat)** | **Gel (Silica-Gel)** | **EFB (Enhanced Flooded)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte state | Free liquid | Absorbed in fiberglass mat | Gelled (fumed silica) | Free liquid + polyester scrim |
| Maintenance | Periodic water top-up | Sealed, zero maintenance | Sealed, zero maintenance | Sealed, low maintenance |
| Cycle life @ 50 % DOD | 300–500 | 500–800 | 600–1000 | 400–700 |
| Internal resistance | Moderate (~10 mΩ for 50 Ah) | Low (~6 mΩ) | Higher (~15 mΩ) | Low (~7 mΩ) |
| Charge acceptance | Baseline | ~2× flooded | ~1.5× flooded | ~1.7× flooded |
| Cost | Lowest | ~2× flooded | ~1.8× flooded | ~1.4× flooded |
| Typical use | SLI, traction, UPS | Start-stop ICE, mild HEV | Deep-cycle, solar | Start-stop ICE, micro-hybrid |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Insight — Start-Stop Systems**
> Modern cars with automatic engine shut-off at traffic lights demand batteries that can accept **partial state-of-charge (PSoC) cycling** — partial charging hundreds of times per trip. Flooded batteries fail here due to acid stratification; AGM and EFB excel due to acid immobilization and carbon-additive enhanced plates. This is why KTU frequently asks about start-stop batteries in 2024 scheme questions.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Battery Sizing for an Auxiliary Load

**Problem:** A 12 V lead-acid battery (6 cells) with capacity rating $C_{20 = 50\,\text{Ah}}$ (i.e., 50 Ah when discharged over 20 h at 25 °C) must power a 60 W cabin lighting load for **8 hours**. Verify that the battery can support this without dropping below the 50 % DOD cutoff.

### Step 1 — Compute load current

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{load}} = \frac{P_{\text{load}}}{V_{\text{bus}}} = \frac{60\,\text{W}}{12\,\text{V}} = 5.0\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Total charge consumed

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{used}} = I_{\text{load}} \times t = 5.0\,\text{A} \times 8\,\text{h} = 40\,\text{Ah}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Peukert's correction for the 5 A discharge rate

Peukert's law with $n = 1.20$ and the 20-hour rating ($C_{20} = 50\,\text{Ah}$, $I_{20} = 2.5\,\text{A}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{available at 5 A}} = C_{20} \times \left(\frac{I_{20}}{I_{\text{load}}}\right)^{n-1}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{available}} = 50 \times \left(\frac{2.5}{5.0}\right)^{0.20} = 50 \times (0.5)^{0.20}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.5)^{0.20} = e^{0.20 \ln(0.5)} = e^{-0.1386} \approx 0.8706
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{available}} \approx 50 \times 0.8706 \approx 43.5\,\text{Ah}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Depth-of-Discharge

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DOD} = \frac{Q_{\text{used}}}{C_{\text{available}}} \times 100 = \frac{40}{43.5} \times 100 \approx 91.9\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Verdict

The DOD of **91.9 %** exceeds the 50 % recommended cutoff → **the battery is undersized**. Either (a) select $C_{20} = 100\,\text{Ah}$, or (b) increase system voltage (e.g., 24 V or 48 V mild-hybrid architecture) to reduce current draw.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Board Pitfall — Always include Peukert's correction when dealing with high-rate discharge. KTU examiners specifically test whether students remember that LAB loses usable capacity at higher currents (a phenomenon called rate-capacity effect).**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Lead-Acid Cell Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Positive Plate - PbO2 Active Material on Pb-Ca Grid] --> C[Microporous Polyethylene Separator with Sulfuric Acid Electrolyte]
    B[Negative Plate - Spongy Pb Active Material on Pb-Ca Grid] --> C
    C --> D[Cell Container - Polypropylene Case]
    D --> E[Vent Cap - Pressure Relief and Water Top-up Access]
    C --> F[External Load - Headlamps ECU Starter Motor]
    F -- Charging Current from Alternator or Charger --> G[DC Voltage Source 2.30 to 2.45 V per Cell]
    G --> A
    G --> B
    A -- 2.04 V OCV at 25 C --> H[Battery Management System BMS Voltage and Temperature Sensor]
    B --> H
    H --> I[SOC Estimator - Coulomb Counting plus Nernst OCV Lookup]
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Reading Guide**
> - **A** & **B:** The two electrodes. Together they generate a 2.04 V OCV when connected via the electrolyte (C).
> - **G:** External charger (in EVs: the DC-DC converter from the high-voltage bus; in ICE: the alternator regulated to 14.4 V).
> - **H/I:** Modern LAB systems increasingly use a BMS — historically absent on flooded batteries but standard on AGM/EFB in start-stop and 48 V systems.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Charge–Discharge Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Battery at 100 percent SOC]) --> DischCheck{Load Connected?}
    DischCheck -- Yes --> DischMode[Discharge Mode - Spontaneous Galvanic Reaction]
    DischMode --> DischReact[Pb plus PbO2 plus 2 H2SO4 yields 2 PbSO4 plus 2 H2O plus 2 e minus]
    DischReact --> SocDrop[SOC decreases, Electrolyte density falls from 1.28 to 1.10 g per cm3]
    SocDrop --> SocCheck{SOC below 20 percent?}
    SocCheck -- No --> DischCheck
    SocCheck -- Yes --> Recharge[Recharge Required]
    DischCheck -- No --> ChgCheck{External Charger Connected?}
    ChgCheck -- Yes --> ChgMode[Charge Mode - Non-spontaneous Electrolysis]
    ChgMode --> ChgReact[2 PbSO4 plus 2 H2O yields Pb plus PbO2 plus 2 H2SO4]
    ChgReact --> SocRise[SOC rises, Acid density restored]
    SocRise --> ChgCheck
    ChgCheck -- No --> Idle[Battery on Open Circuit Standby]
    Idle --> Start
    Recharge --> ChgMode
```

## 4.3 Cell-to-Pack Stacking Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Cell1[Cell 1 - 2.04 V OCV] --> Series1{Series Connection Busbar}
    Cell2[Cell 2 - 2.04 V OCV] --> Series1
    Cell3[Cell 3 - 2.04 V OCV] --> Series1
    Cell4[Cell 4 - 2.04 V OCV] --> Series1
    Cell5[Cell 5 - 2.04 V OCV] --> Series1
    Cell6[Cell 6 - 2.04 V OCV] --> Series1
    Series1 --> Pack[12 V Automotive Lead-Acid Pack]
    Pack --> AuxLoad[Auxiliary Loads - ECUs Lights Infotainment]
    Pack --> HvBus[48 V Mild Hybrid DC-DC Converter Interface]
```

> [!NOTE] **Why Six Cells?** Six cells in series × 2.04 V OCV = **12.24 V nominal**, which matches the automotive bus voltage set by the alternator (regulated to 14.0–14.8 V during charging). The legacy 12 V standard dates back to the early 20th-century electrical-system choice and persists due to cost inertia.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT] **Mark Distribution Reminder — KTU 2024 Scheme ESE**
> - **Part A:** 3 marks each, no choice, 4–5 questions per module, CO-mapped.
> - **Part B:** 14 marks each, **internal choice** between Q-A and Q-B, CO-mapped, sub-parts typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.
> - Bloom's levels: Apply (L3), Analyse (L4), Evaluate (L5).

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a lead-acid battery. List the active materials of its positive and negative plates and state its nominal cell voltage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A lead-acid battery is a **rechargeable (secondary) electrochemical storage device** in which electrical energy is obtained from the reversible redox reaction between **lead dioxide (PbO₂)** at the positive electrode and **spongy lead (Pb)** at the negative electrode in an electrolyte of **dilute sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)**. *(1 mark)*

- **Positive plate active material:** Lead dioxide, PbO₂ *(1 mark)*
- **Negative plate active material:** Sponge lead, Pb *(0.5 mark)*
- **Nominal cell voltage:** **2.04 V** *(0.5 mark)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between flooded and VRLA lead-acid batteries. Give one example application for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Flooded (Vented) | VRLA (Valve-Regulated) |
|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte state | Free liquid | Immobilized (AGM mat or Gel) |
| Maintenance | Periodic distilled water top-up required | Sealed — zero maintenance |
| Gas evolution | Hydrogen + oxygen vented freely | Internal recombination (~99 %) |
| Orientation | Must be upright | Any orientation allowed |
| Example application | Conventional SLI in old ICE cars | Start-stop vehicles, UPS, 48 V mild-hybrid auxiliary battery *(2 marks for distinguishing features + 1 mark for correct application example)* |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **construction of a lead-acid cell**. Label all key components and state the function of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **overall electrochemical reaction** during discharge and charge. Show explicitly how the **electrolyte density** varies with state-of-charge and explain the phenomenon of **acid stratification** in flooded batteries. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q-A (a)

**Construction of a Lead-Acid Cell — 7 marks:**

1. **Positive Plate (1.5 marks):**
   - Active material: **PbO₂** (dark brown)
   - Supported on a **Pb-Ca or Pb-Sb alloy grid**
   - Site of reduction during discharge (cathode)

2. **Negative Plate (1 mark):**
   - Active material: **sponge Pb** (porous grey-black)
   - Supported on a Pb-Ca alloy grid (Ca suppresses gassing)
   - Site of oxidation during discharge (anode)

3. **Electrolyte (1.5 marks):**
   - **Aqueous H₂SO₄**, ~37 % by weight at full charge
   - Density 1.26–1.28 g/cm³ at 25 °C (full charge)
   - Acts as ionic conductor and active reactant

4. **Separator (1 mark):**
   - Microporous polyethylene, fiberglass (AGM), or silica-gel matrix
   - Prevents internal short-circuit while allowing ionic flow

5. **Container + Vent (1 mark):**
   - Polypropylene case, acid-resistant
   - Vented (flooded) with removable caps OR sealed (VRLA) with one-way pressure relief valve (4–8 psi)

6. **Terminals (1 mark):**
   - Cast Pb-Ca alloy posts; positive terminal slightly larger in some standards

---

#### Model Solution — Q-A (b)

**Discharge Half-Reactions (3 marks):**

Negative plate (oxidation):
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^{-}
\end{aligned}$$

Positive plate (reduction):
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^{+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

**Overall Discharge Reaction (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\,\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy}
\end{aligned}$$

**Charge Reaction (Reverse) (1 mark):**
$$\begin{aligned}
2\,\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{External DC}} \text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4
\end{aligned}$$

**Electrolyte Density vs SOC (1 mark):**
- Full charge: H₂SO₄ maximum → **density ≈ 1.28 g/cm³**
- 50 % DOD: density ≈ **1.19 g/cm³**
- Fully discharged: H₂SO₄ minimum → **density ≈ 1.10 g/cm³**
- Therefore, **density is a direct SOC indicator** (used with hydrometer).

**Acid Stratification (bonus content):**
During cycling, especially in flooded batteries subjected to partial state-ofarge (PSoC), the heavier acid concentrates at the bottom (high density) while the top becomes water-rich (low density). This **stratification** causes non-uniform plate utilization, accelerated sulfation, and reduced capacity. AGM and EFB batteries with immobilized electrolyte and forced acid circulation systems avoid this issue.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **three classical charging stages** of a lead-acid battery (bulk, absorption, float) with voltage-current waveforms. Mention the typical voltages for a 12 V module. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **45 Ah, 12 V lead-acid battery** is to power a **120 W headlamp + 30 W fan** load for **6 hours**. Using Peukert's law with $n = 1.25$ and the 20-hour rating as reference, compute the **depth-of-discharge** reached. Is the battery adequately sized? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q-B (a)

**Three Classical Charging Stages of a Lead-Acid Battery:**

**Stage 1 — Bulk (Constant Current) Phase (3 marks):**
- The charger delivers a **constant current** (typically **0.1 C to 0.3 C**, i.e., 10–30 % of rated Ah).
- For 45 Ah battery: I_bulk ≈ **4.5 A to 13.5 A**.
- Battery voltage rises gradually from OCV (~12.0 V) toward the absorption set-point.
- Bulk phase ends when voltage reaches **~14.4 V (2.40 V/cell)**.

**Stage 2 — Absorption (Constant Voltage) Phase (2 marks):**
- Charger now holds voltage constant at **14.4 V (2.40 V/cell)** for 12 V module.
- Current **tapers exponentially** as SOC approaches 100 %.
- Duration: 2–4 hours typical; terminated when current falls below **0.01 C** (e.g., 0.45 A for 45 Ah).
- Ensures full recharge without over-gassing.

**Stage 3 — Float (Maintenance) Phase (2 marks):**
- Voltage reduced to **13.5–13.8 V (2.25 V/cell)** to maintain full charge.
- Compensates for self-discharge (~3–5 %/month for flooded).
- Battery can remain in float indefinitely without damage.

---

#### Model Solution — Q-B (b)

**Given:**
- Battery: $C_{20} = 45\,\text{Ah}$, $V_{\text{nom}} = 12\,\text{V}$
- Load: $P = 120 + 30 = 150\,\text{W}$
- Duration: $t = 6\,\text{h}$
- Peukert exponent: $n = 1.25$

**Step 1 — Load current (1 mark):**
$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{load}} = \frac{P}{V_{\text{bus}}} = \frac{150}{12} = 12.5\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Total charge demanded (1 mark):**
$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{demand}} = I_{\text{load}} \times t = 12.5 \times 6 = 75\,\text{Ah}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Peukert's effective capacity at 12.5 A (3 marks):**

The 20-hour rate current:
$$\begin{aligned}
I_{20} = \frac{C_{20}}{20} = \frac{45}{20} = 2.25\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

Applying Peukert:
$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{eff}} = C_{20} \times \left(\frac{I_{20}}{I_{\text{load}}}\right)^{n-1}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{eff}} = 45 \times \left(\frac{2.25}{12.5}\right)^{0.25}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{2.25}{12.5} = 0.18
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.18)^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \ln(0.18)} = e^{0.25 \times (-1.715)} = e^{-0.4287} \approx 0.6514
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{eff}} = 45 \times 0.6514 \approx 29.3\,\text{Ah}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Depth of Discharge (1 mark):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DOD} = \frac{Q_{\text{demand}}}{C_{\text{eff}}} \times 100 = \frac{75}{29.3} \times 100 \approx 256\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verdict (1 mark):**

Since the demand (75 Ah) far exceeds the **effective available capacity** (29.3 Ah), the battery will be **completely discharged in less than 2.5 hours**. The 45 Ah battery is **severely undersized** for this load.

**Recommendation:** Either upgrade to $C_{20} \approx 200\,\text{Ah}$, or reduce load (e.g., use LED headlamps reducing 120 W → 40 W), or move to a 24 V/48 V architecture to halve the current draw.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the cell multiplier (6 × 2.04 V = 12.24 V)** when computing pack voltage. Always state **"6 cells × 2.04 V = 12 V nominal"** to earn the panel-mark.
> 2. **Confusing anode/cathode direction during charge vs discharge.** In discharge, the **negative plate is the anode (oxidation site)**; during charge, the same plate becomes the **cathode (reduction site)**. Examiners specifically mark this.
> 3. **Omitting Peukert's correction** in numerical problems — KTU awards 2 marks specifically for invoking $I^n t = \text{const}$.
> 4. **Confusing specific energy (Wh/kg) with energy density (Wh/L)** — the former is mass-based, the latter volume-based.
> 5. **Writing "lead acid is being phased out of EVs"** — incorrect! The **12 V auxiliary lead-acid battery is still mandated** in essentially all production EVs and HEVs as of 2024.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Lead-Acid Battery**

- ✅ **Inventor:** Gaston Planté, 1859 — oldest rechargeable chemistry still in use.
- ✅ **Cell voltage:** **2.04 V** OCV at 25 °C, 6 cells = 12 V automotive module.
- ✅ **Active materials:** Positive = **PbO₂**; Negative = **Pb** (both converted to **PbSO₄** during discharge).
- ✅ **Electrolyte:** **H₂SO₄ (aq)**, density **1.28 g/cm³** (full) → **1.10 g/cm³** (empty).
- ✅ **Overall reaction:** $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- ✅ **Specific energy:** **30–50 Wh/kg** (low compared to Li-ion at 150–250 Wh/kg).
- ✅ **Cycle life:** 200–500 cycles at 80 % DOD; up to 1000 cycles for AGM at 50 % DOD.
- ✅ **Charge methods:** **CC (bulk) → CV (absorption 14.4 V) → Float (13.5–13.8 V)** for 12 V module.
- ✅ **Variants:** **Flooded, AGM, Gel, EFB** — the latter two dominate start-stop and 48 V mild-hybrid applications.
- ✅ **Peukert's law:** $I^n \cdot t = \text{const}$; $n = 1.1$–$1.3$; capacity drops at high discharge rates.
- ✅ **EV role today:** **Auxiliary 12 V battery** (mandatory even in Tesla, Toyota, Hyundai BEVs) + **48 V mild-hybrid** energy buffer (AGM/EFB).
- ✅ **Key equations:** $E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$, $C = I \cdot t$, $E = V \cdot C$, $\eta_{\text{RT}} \approx 75\,\%$.
- ✅ **Stratification issue:** Affects only flooded batteries; resolved by AGM immobilization or forced acid circulation.
- ✅ **Sulfation:** Hard crystalline PbSO₄ forms if left in discharged state — kills capacity permanently. Always recharge promptly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nickel-Cadmium Battery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Nickel-Cadmium Battery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Nickel-Cadmium (Ni-Cd) Battery** is a secondary (rechargeable) alkaline electrochemical storage device that utilizes **nickel(III) hydroxide [NiO(OH)]** as the positive electrode (cathode during discharge), **cadmium (Cd)** as the negative electrode (anode during discharge), and a **potassium hydroxide (KOH) aqueous electrolyte** as the ionic conduction medium. It belongs to the family of alkaline storage batteries and operates on the principle of reversible redox reactions between nickel and cadmium hydroxides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Ni-Cd batteries are classified under **"Alkaline Batteries"** in Module 2 of PEMET863. The focus is on construction, electrochemistry, charge–discharge characteristics, advantages, limitations, and the role of Ni-Cd in early HEV/EV applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Refillable Water Bottle"**
> Imagine a sturdy, reusable steel water bottle (the Ni-Cd cell). You can pour water out (discharge → Cd → Cd(OH)₂ + electrons) and refill it from a tap (charge → reverse reaction). The bottle is robust, works even when not perfectly full, and survives thousands of refills — but it has a "memory": if you repeatedly only half-empty it, it "remembers" that smaller capacity and refuses to deliver more. This is the famous **Memory Effect** of Ni-Cd. The alkaline electrolyte is the "air" inside that lets the chemistry "breathe" without spilling, and the vent cap prevents pressure build-up.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Cell Voltage** | **1.2** | V |
| **Theoretical Specific Energy** | **244** | Wh/kg |
| **Practical Specific Energy** | **40 – 60** | Wh/kg |
| **Energy Density (Volumetric)** | **50 – 150** | Wh/L |
| **Operating Temperature Range** | **−20 to +45** | °C |
| **Internal Resistance (per cell)** | **6 – 12** | mΩ |
| **Self-Discharge Rate (per month)** | **10 – 20** | % |
| **Cycle Life (to 80% capacity)** | **1500 – 2000+** | cycles |
| **Electrolyte** | **KOH (~30% w/w aqueous)** | — |

### 1.4 Why Ni-Cd Matters for HEV/EV

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Before lithium-ion technology matured, **Ni-Cd was the workhorse of early hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs)**, notably the **Honda Insight (1st gen, 1999)** and the **Ford Ranger EV**. KTU examiners expect students to know the historical context: Ni-Cd offered **high cycle life, robustness, and high discharge rates**, but was eventually displaced by **Ni-MH** and then **Li-ion** due to **cadmium toxicity** and **memory effect**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Architecture (Cell Anatomy)

A Ni-Cd cell is built as a sealed cylindrical or prismatic jelly-roll (or pocket-plate) configuration consisting of the following components:

- **Positive Plate (Cathode during discharge):** Nickel hydroxide **Ni(OH)₂** is pasted onto a nickel-plated steel substrate. During charge it is oxidized to nickel oxyhydroxide **NiO(OH)**.
- **Negative Plate (Anode during discharge):** Metallic **cadmium (Cd)** in powdered form, pasted onto a cadmium-plated or nickel-plated steel substrate.
- **Separator:** A non-woven nylon or polypropylene mat that physically separates the two electrodes while allowing ionic conduction.
- **Electrolyte:** **Potassium hydroxide (KOH), ~30% by weight**, density ~1.30 g/cm³. The electrolyte is **not consumed** during the charge–discharge cycle (only water is lost over time) — this is a key alkaline-cell advantage.
- **Cell Container:** Nickel-plated steel can, hermetically sealed with a **pressure-relief safety vent** that opens at ~**2 – 4 MPa** internal pressure.
- **Current Collectors:** Nickel foam or nickel-plated steel for the cathode; Cd-plated steel for the anode.

### 2.2 Electrochemistry — The "How" and "Why"

The Ni-Cd cell operates via **two independent reversible redox couples** shuttling hydroxyl ions (OH⁻) between the electrodes through the alkaline electrolyte.

**Discharge Reactions (Galvanic mode — battery delivers energy):**

- **Anode (Oxidation, Cd → Cd²⁺ + 2e⁻):**
  Cd + 2OH⁻ → Cd(OH)₂ + 2e⁻
  - *Why:* Cadmium has a more negative standard reduction potential than nickel, so it is preferentially oxidized, releasing electrons to the external circuit.

- **Cathode (Reduction, NiOOH + H₂O + e⁻ → Ni(OH)₂ + OH⁻):**
  2NiO(OH) + 2H₂O + 2e⁻ → 2Ni(OH)₂ + 2OH⁻
  - *Why:* The Ni³⁺ in NiO(OH) is reduced to Ni²⁺ in Ni(OH)₂, consuming the electrons that arrived via the external circuit.

- **Overall Discharge Reaction:**
  2NiO(OH) + Cd + 2H₂O → 2Ni(OH)₂ + Cd(OH)₂

**Charge Reactions (Electrolytic mode — battery stores energy):**
The discharge reactions are simply reversed. Note the electrolyte KOH **does not appear in the net reaction** — only water is consumed/produced at each electrode, which is why concentration polarization is minimal.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates every equation a KTU student needs for the 14-mark problems on Ni-Cd batteries. Memorize these — they appear verbatim (or with minor numeric substitution) in past papers.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Definitions | Typical Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Standard electrode potentials | V | Predicts open-circuit voltage |
| 2 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -n F E^{\circ}_{cell}$ | n = electrons transferred, F = Faraday constant = **96485 C/mol** | J/mol | Thermodynamic spontaneity check |
| 3 | $E^{\circ}_{cell} = 1.30\ \text{V}$ (theoretical Ni-Cd) | Standard cell EMF | V | Upper bound for practical voltage |
| 4 | $E_{nominal} = 1.2\ \text{V}$ (practical, loaded) | Working voltage per cell | V | Battery pack sizing: $N_{cells} = V_{pack}/1.2$ |
| 5 | $E_{specific} = \dfrac{E_{cell} \cdot Q}{m}$ | Specific energy; Q = capacity (Ah), m = mass (kg) | Wh/kg | Vehicle range estimation |
| 6 | $C\text{-rate} = \dfrac{I}{C_{rated}}$ | Discharge current / rated capacity | 1/h | Determines usable capacity & voltage sag |
| 7 | $E_{practical} = E_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$ | Terminal voltage under load | V | Power delivery calculations |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{discharge}}{E_{charge}} \times 100\%$ | Coulombic / energy efficiency | % | Energy lost as heat |
| 9 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{E_{oc}^{\,2}}{4 R_{int}}$ | Maximum transferable power | W | Peak-power capability for HEV |
| 10 | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Capacity in ampere-hours | Ah | Energy stored: $E = Q \cdot V$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The **theoretical EMF of Ni-Cd is 1.30 V**, but the **nominal operating voltage is 1.2 V**. Many students write "1.2 V" for both — lose 1 mark instantly. The difference arises from internal resistance, overpotentials, and the fact that loaded voltage < OCV.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Standard Cell EMF from Half-Cell Potentials

**Step 1 — Recall the standard reduction potentials (vs. Standard Hydrogen Electrode, SHE):**

$$
E^{\circ}_{\text{NiO(OH)/Ni(OH)\textsubscript{2}}} = +0.49\ \text{V (vs. SHE)}
$$

$$
E^{\circ}_{\text{Cd(OH)\textsubscript{2}/Cd}} = -0.81\ \text{V (vs. SHE)}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify cathode and anode.**
The half-cell with the **more positive** potential is the **cathode** (reduction during discharge). Hence, the nickel electrode is the cathode, and the cadmium electrode is the anode.

**Step 3 — Apply the cell EMF formula.**

$$
E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode}
$$

$$
E^{\circ}_{cell} = (+0.49\ \text{V}) - (-0.81\ \text{V})
$$

**Step 4 — Arithmetic evaluation.**

$$
E^{\circ}_{cell} = 0.49 + 0.81 = +1.30\ \text{V}
$$

**Conclusion:** The **theoretical open-circuit voltage** of a Ni-Cd cell is **1.30 V**. Under practical loaded conditions, the terminal voltage settles at a nominal **1.2 V** per cell.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Specific Energy (Wh/kg)

The theoretical specific energy of any battery is:

$$
E_{sp,\,th} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}_{cell}}{M_{cell}}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the molar mass of the active species (per 2-electron transfer):**

The overall discharge reaction is:
$$
2\text{NiO(OH)} + \text{Cd} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{Cd(OH)}_2
$$

Molar masses (g/mol):
- NiO(OH) → 91.7
- Cd → 112.4
- H₂O → 18.0

**Step 2 — Sum the molar masses of reactants per the balanced stoichiometry (1 mole of Cd basis = 2 electrons):**

$$
M_{active} = 2(91.7) + 112.4 + 2(18.0)
$$

$$
M_{active} = 183.4 + 112.4 + 36.0 = 331.8\ \text{g/mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Plug into the specific-energy formula** (using n = 2, F = 96485 C/mol, E° = 1.30 V):

$$
E_{sp,\,th} = \dfrac{2 \times 96485 \times 1.30}{331.8}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$
2 \times 96485 \times 1.30 = 192970 \times 1.30 = 250861\ \text{J/g}
$$

**Step 5 — Division:**

$$
E_{sp,\,th} = \dfrac{250861}{331.8} = 756.1\ \text{J/g}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert J/g to Wh/kg** (1 Wh = 3600 J):

$$
E_{sp,\,th} = \dfrac{756.1}{3.6} = 210.0\ \text{Wh/kg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The widely cited textbook value of **244 Wh/kg** assumes only Cd and NiO(OH) (ignoring the water's mass contribution to denominator), which is the upper theoretical limit. Using the full stoichiometric mass of reactants gives ~210 Wh/kg, which is still 3–5× higher than the **practical 40–60 Wh/kg** — the gap is explained by current-collector mass, electrolyte mass, casing, and unused active material.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pack Sizing for an HEV Application

**Problem Statement:** A small HEV requires a **144 V** traction pack. How many Ni-Cd cells are needed in series, and what is the minimum required pack specific energy if the vehicle carries a **200 kg** battery pack and the target range energy is **10 kWh**?

**Step 1 — Number of series cells (using nominal voltage 1.2 V):**

$$
N_{series} = \dfrac{V_{pack}}{V_{cell,\,nominal}} = \dfrac{144}{1.2} = 120\ \text{cells}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy stored per cell (assume 100 Ah cell, full discharge to 1.0 V cut-off):**

$$
E_{cell} = V_{avg} \times Q = 1.15\ \text{V (approx.)} \times 100\ \text{Ah} = 115\ \text{Wh}
$$

**Step 3 — Total pack energy:**

$$
E_{pack} = 120 \times 115 = 13800\ \text{Wh} = 13.8\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Pack specific energy:**

$$
E_{sp,\,pack} = \dfrac{E_{pack}}{m_{pack}} = \dfrac{13800\ \text{Wh}}{200\ \text{kg}} = 69\ \text{Wh/kg}
$$

**Conclusion:** The Ni-Cd pack delivers **~69 Wh/kg** at the pack level, which is consistent with the literature value of **50–60 Wh/kg for early Honda Insight Ni-Cd packs**, validating the calculation methodology.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Charge / Discharge Behaviour

| Phase | Anode Process | Cathode Process | Electrolyte | Voltage Trend |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Discharge (start)** | Cd + 2OH⁻ → Cd(OH)₂ + 2e⁻ | 2NiO(OH) + 2H₂O + 2e⁻ → 2Ni(OH)₂ + 2OH⁻ | Invariant (KOH) | 1.30 V → 1.20 V |
| **Discharge (mid)** | Same as above; Cd(OH)₂ grows | NiO(OH) depleted → Ni(OH)₂ grows | Invariant | ~1.20 V (flat plateau) |
| **Discharge (end)** | Cd nearly exhausted | NiO(OH) nearly exhausted | H₂O consumed | Sharp drop to 1.0 V |
| **Charge (start)** | Cd(OH)₂ + 2e⁻ → Cd + 2OH⁻ | 2Ni(OH)₂ + 2OH⁻ → 2NiO(OH) + 2H₂O + 2e⁻ | Invariant | 1.20 V → 1.45 V |
| **Charge (overcharge)** | O₂ evolution: 4OH⁻ → O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ | O₂ recombination at Cd surface (sealed cell) | KOH regenerated | Holds at ~1.45 V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The flat discharge plateau near **1.2 V** is the operational signature of Ni-Cd — KTU examiners frequently ask why Ni-Cd is preferred for high-discharge applications: **flat voltage curve ⇒ predictable power delivery**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Ni-Cd Cell Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Positive Terminal: Nickel-plated steel cap] --> B[Positive Plate: NiOOH / NiOH2 pasted on Ni foam]
    B --> C[Separator: Non-woven nylon / polypropylene mat]
    C --> D[Negative Plate: Cd / CdOH2 pasted on Cd-plated steel]
    D --> E[Negative Terminal: Nickel-plated steel can base]
    F[KOH Electrolyte approx 30 percent w/w fills all void space] -.-> B
    F -.-> C
    F -.-> D
    G[Pressure Relief Safety Vent opens at 2 to 4 MPa] -.-> A
    H[External Load or Charger connects across A and E] -.-> A
    H -.-> E
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ni-Cd Pack in an HEV

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BatteryPack["Ni-Cd Traction Battery Pack 144 V nominal"]
        subgraph CellRow1["Series String 1: 60 cells x 1.2 V = 72 V"]
            C1A[Cell 1: 1.2 V] --> C1B[Cell 2: 1.2 V] --> C1C[Cell 3: 1.2 V] --> C1N[Cell 60: 1.2 V]
        end
        subgraph CellRow2["Series String 2: 60 cells x 1.2 V = 72 V"]
            C2A[Cell 61: 1.2 V] --> C2B[Cell 62: 1.2 V] --> C2C[Cell 63: 1.2 V] --> C2N[Cell 120: 1.2 V]
        end
    end
    CellRow1 --> BMS[Battery Management System BMS]
    CellRow2 --> BMS
    BMS --> Inverter[DC to AC Inverter]
    Inverter --> Motor[3-phase AC Traction Motor]
    Motor --> Wheels[Drive Wheels via Transmission]
    BMS --> Charger[On-board Charger from AC mains]
    Charger -.-> CellRow1
    Charger -.-> CellRow2
```

### 4.3 Charge–Discharge Voltage Profile (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1: Initial voltage drop 1.30 to 1.22 V] --> P2[Phase 2: Flat plateau region 1.22 to 1.18 V about 80 percent of capacity]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Knee region rapid drop 1.18 to 1.0 V]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Cutoff at 1.0 V end of discharge]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Reverse charge curve ramps to 1.45 V during charging]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Overcharge plateau at 1.45 V with oxygen recombination]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **flat discharge plateau** (Phase 2) is the most exam-relevant feature: it is the reason Ni-Cd cells deliver **constant power** over the majority of their discharge cycle — a critical advantage for traction applications.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PEMET863]`
> **List any three advantages of Nickel-Cadmium batteries over Lead-Acid batteries for EV applications.**

**Model Answer (3 points):**
1. **Higher specific energy (40–60 Wh/kg vs. 30–40 Wh/kg for Pb-Acid)** ⇒ lower vehicle mass for the same range.
2. **Much longer cycle life (1500–2000+ cycles vs. 300–500 for Pb-Acid)** ⇒ lower lifetime cost.
3. **Flat discharge voltage plateau (~1.2 V) and superior high-rate discharge capability** ⇒ better power delivery for acceleration.
4. *(Bonus point)* Operates over a wider temperature range and is **sealed/maintenance-free** in vented-cell or sealed-cell variants.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **What is the "Memory Effect" in Ni-Cd batteries, and how is it mitigated?**

**Model Answer (3 points):**
- The **Memory Effect** (also called "voltage depression") is a phenomenon in which a Ni-Cd cell that is repeatedly recharged after only being **partially discharged** "remembers" the shallower discharge depth and progressively loses usable capacity at the deeper end.
- It is caused by the formation of stable, large-grained **cadmium hydroxide (Cd(OH)₂)** crystals at the anode during shallow-cycling, which reduces the active surface area.
- **Mitigation:** (i) **Periodic full-discharge conditioning** (full cycle every 1–3 months) to dissolve the large crystals, (ii) avoiding prolonged shallow-cycling, (iii) using a **smart charger with refresh-cycle mode**, (iv) storing the battery at a controlled temperature.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
> **(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **construction and working principle** of a sealed Nickel-Cadmium cell. Discuss the half-cell and overall cell reactions during discharge. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Ni-Cd battery pack has **120 cells in series**, each rated **1.2 V, 100 Ah**. Calculate: (i) the **nominal pack voltage**, (ii) the **theoretical energy stored** in kWh, and (iii) the **maximum deliverable power** if the internal resistance of each cell is 8 mΩ and the pack is connected to a matched load. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question 3A (a)**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The sealed Ni-Cd cell is constructed in a **cylindrical jelly-roll** configuration:
- **Positive electrode:** Ni(OH)₂ paste coated onto a perforated **nickel foam** current collector, charged to NiO(OH) before sealing.
- **Negative electrode:** Cd metal powder paste coated onto a **Cd-plated steel** strip.
- **Separator:** A porous **non-woven nylon or polypropylene** membrane (10–25 mils thick) between the two electrodes.
- **Electrolyte:** Aqueous **KOH (30% w/w, density ≈ 1.30 g/cm³)** saturating the separator and active material.
- **Casing:** **Nickel-plated steel can** providing mechanical strength and acting as the negative terminal; **nickel-plated steel cap** as the positive terminal.
- **Safety vent:** A **bimetallic resealable pressure vent** on the cap, set to open at 2–4 MPa to release H₂/O₂ gas generated during overcharge.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
During **discharge**, cadmium is oxidized at the anode releasing electrons, while NiO(OH) is reduced at the cathode. The hydroxyl ions (OH⁻) carry the ionic current through the KOH electrolyte. The cell delivers a nominal voltage of **1.2 V** with a flat discharge curve.

**Half-Cell & Overall Reactions (2 marks):**

- Anode (oxidation):  Cd + 2OH⁻ → Cd(OH)₂ + 2e⁻
- Cathode (reduction):  2NiO(OH) + 2H₂O + 2e⁻ → 2Ni(OH)₂ + 2OH⁻
- Overall:  2NiO(OH) + Cd + 2H₂O → 2Ni(OH)₂ + Cd(OH)₂

### **Model Solution — Question 3A (b)**

**(i) Nominal pack voltage (2 marks):**
$$
V_{pack} = N_{series} \times V_{cell} = 120 \times 1.2\ \text{V} = 144\ \text{V}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical energy stored (3 marks):**
$$
E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times Q = 144\ \text{V} \times 100\ \text{Ah} = 14400\ \text{Wh} = 14.4\ \text{kWh}
$$

**(iii) Maximum power under matched load (2 marks):**
Total internal resistance of 120 cells in series:
$$
R_{int,\,pack} = 120 \times 8\ \text{m}\Omega = 960\ \text{m}\Omega = 0.96\ \Omega
$$
For maximum power transfer, $R_{load} = R_{int}$:
$$
P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^{\,2}}{4 R_{int}} = \dfrac{(144)^2}{4 \times 0.96} = \dfrac{20736}{3.84} = 5400\ \text{W} \approx 5.4\ \text{kW}
$$

---

> **Q3B (Alternative Choice for Internal-Option Compliance).** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
> **(a)** Explain the **charge and discharge characteristics** of a Ni-Cd battery with a voltage-vs-time diagram. Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of Ni-Cd batteries in HEV applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **theoretical specific energy** of a Ni-Cd cell from the half-cell reactions and Faraday's laws. Compare the theoretical value with the practical value and explain the **two main reasons** for the discrepancy. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question 3B (a)**

**Charge–Discharge Characteristics (3 marks):**
- **Discharge curve:** Begins at ~1.30 V, drops to ~1.22 V, holds a **flat plateau at ~1.2 V for ~80% of the capacity**, then exhibits a "knee" dropping sharply to the **1.0 V cutoff**.
- **Charge curve:** Voltage rises from 1.2 V to ~1.45 V, then a **flat overcharge plateau at 1.45 V** during which **oxygen evolution at the cathode** and **oxygen recombination at the cadmium anode** keep pressure stable (in sealed cells).

**Advantages for HEV (2 marks):**
- High cycle life (1500–2000+ cycles), robust under vibration, wide temperature operation, fast charge acceptance, high discharge current capability, flat voltage plateau.

**Disadvantages for HEV (2 marks):**
- **Toxicity of cadmium** ⇒ environmental hazard and complex recycling (this is the principal reason Ni-Cd is banned in many consumer applications).
- **Memory effect** requires periodic conditioning cycles.
- **Lower specific energy (40–60 Wh/kg)** than Ni-MH and Li-ion, limiting EV range.
- Higher self-discharge (~10–20% per month).

### **Model Solution — Question 3B (b)**

**Derivation (4 marks):** Refer to Section 3.2 above. The theoretical specific energy, using only the active mass of **Cd + 2 NiO(OH)** = 112.4 + 2(91.7) = 295.8 g per 2-electron transfer, is:

$$
E_{sp,\,th} = \dfrac{n F E^{\circ}}{M_{active}} = \dfrac{2 \times 96485 \times 1.30}{295.8 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
E_{sp,\,th} = \dfrac{250861\ \text{J}}{0.2958\ \text{kg}} = 848000\ \text{J/kg} = 235.6\ \text{Wh/kg}
$$

(Using full stoichiometric mass including H₂O → ~210 Wh/kg; the textbook canonical value is **244 Wh/kg**.)

**Comparison & Reasons for Discrepancy (3 marks):**
- **Theoretical:** ~210–244 Wh/kg | **Practical:** 40–60 Wh/kg | **Ratio:** ~4–5× gap.
- **Reason 1 — Inactive mass:** The current collectors (Ni foam, steel substrate), separator, KOH electrolyte, cell casing, vent, and external wiring add **60–70%** to the cell's mass without contributing to charge storage.
- **Reason 2 — Under-utilization of active material:** Practical discharge is limited to a **depth-of-discharge (DoD) of ~80%** to protect the electrode, and the cell is not operated at the full 1.30 V EMF — voltage drop under load further reduces delivered energy.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Ni-Cd Questions — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing 1.2 V (nominal) with 1.30 V (theoretical EMF).** Examiners allocate 1 mark for correctly identifying both values; using only one is half-marks at best.
> 2. **Writing the wrong electrolyte.** KOH (alkaline) is the *only* correct answer. Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) would be the lead-acid battery — instant 0/2 if written for Ni-Cd.
> 3. **Forgetting the water in the overall reaction.** The full reaction is 2NiO(OH) + Cd + **2H₂O** → 2Ni(OH)₂ + Cd(OH)₂. Omitting H₂O loses 1 mark and breaks stoichiometric balance.
> 4. **Confusing anode and cathode during discharge.** Remember: in a *galvanic* (discharging) cell, the **anode is negative** (oxidation — Cd) and the **cathode is positive** (reduction — NiOOH). Reversing them is a 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Ignoring the memory effect.** Many students list advantages but forget this critical limitation — the examiner often allocates 1 mark specifically for mentioning it.
> 6. **Cadmium toxicity is a *disadvantage*, not an advantage.** The EU Battery Directive (2006/66/EC) restricts Ni-Cd use in consumer products precisely because of this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 1 hour before the exam.**

- **Chemistry type:** Alkaline secondary battery; electrolyte = **KOH (aqueous, ~30% w/w)**, *not consumed* in net reaction.
- **Nominal voltage:** **1.2 V/cell** (loaded); **Theoretical EMF = 1.30 V**; **Cutoff = 1.0 V**.
- **Positive electrode:** Ni(OH)₂ ⇌ NiO(OH) + H⁺ + e⁻ (Ni²⁺/Ni³� redox couple, E° = +0.49 V vs. SHE).
- **Negative electrode:** Cd + 2OH⁻ ⇌ Cd(OH)₂ + 2e⁻ (E° = −0.81 V vs. SHE).
- **Overall discharge:** 2NiO(OH) + Cd + 2H₂O → 2Ni(OH)₂ + Cd(OH)₂.
- **Theoretical specific energy:** **~244 Wh/kg** (canonical) or ~210 Wh/kg (full stoichiometry).
- **Practical specific energy:** **40–60 Wh/kg**.
- **Cycle life:** **1500–2000+** cycles to 80% capacity.
- **Key advantages:** High cycle life, flat discharge plateau, robust, wide temperature range, high-rate discharge.
- **Key disadvantages:** **Cadmium toxicity**, **memory effect**, **higher self-discharge (10–20%/month)**, lower specific energy than Ni-MH/Li-ion.
- **Historical HEV use:** Honda Insight (1st gen, 1999), Ford Ranger EV — displaced by **Ni-MH** in Toyota Prius and ultimately by **Li-ion**.
- **Memory effect cause:** Recrystallization of large Cd(OH)₂ grains at the anode; mitigated by **periodic full discharge**.
- **Pressure vent:** Opens at **2–4 MPa** to vent H₂/O₂ generated on overcharge; in sealed cells, **O₂ recombines at the Cd anode** to regenerate Cd(OH)₂, suppressing pressure build-up.
- **Formula must-knows:** $E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode}$; $P_{max} = V_{oc}^{2}/(4R_{int})$; $E = Q \times V$; $N_{series} = V_{pack}/V_{cell}$.
- **Why alkaline over acid?** KOH is non-consumed ⇒ no concentration change ⇒ **flat discharge curve**; also less corrosive to internal components.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nickel-Metal-Hydride (NiMH) Battery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Nickel-Metal-Hydride (NiMH) Battery # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Nickel-Metal-Hydride (NiMH) Battery** is a rechargeable alkaline secondary electrochemical storage device in which the **positive electrode (cathode during discharge)** is constructed from **nickel hydroxide [Ni(OH)₂ / NiOOH]** and the **negative electrode (anode during discharge)** is a **hydrogen-absorbing intermetallic alloy (Metal Hydride, MH)**. The electrolyte is a **concentrated alkaline solution of potassium hydroxide (KOH)**, typically 30 % by weight, with small additions of LiOH and NaOH to improve cycle life and charge acceptance. The NiMH cell is an evolutionary successor to the Nickel-Cadmium (NiCd) cell, replacing the toxic cadmium electrode with a reversible hydrogen-storage alloy (commonly **AB₅-type mischmetal alloys** such as LaNi₅, or **AB₂-type alloys** such as ZrNi₂). It is a **"hydrogen shuttling"** system — hydrogen, not a metallic ion, is the active species that migrates between the two electrodes during charge and discharge. > [!NOTE] > **Historical Note for KTU Context:** The NiMH battery was commercially introduced by **Ovonic Battery Company (Stanford Ovshinsky)** in the early 1990s. The **Toyota Prius (Gen-1, 1997)** was the world's first mass-produced HEV to use NiMH traction batteries, cementing NiMH as the dominant EV/HEV chemistry for nearly two decades before lithium-ion took over in the late 2000s. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rechargeable Hydrogen Sponge" Imagine two water tanks connected by a pipe. One tank contains a **sponge that can absorb and release water reversibly** (the Metal Hydride electrode), and the other contains a **special chemical that can bind and unbind oxygen-hydrogen groups** (the Nickel Hydroxide electrode). When you discharge the system, hydrogen ions flow out of the sponge, travel through the alkaline electrolyte, and combine with the nickel electrode. When you charge it, an external power supply forces hydrogen ions *back* into the sponge, "re-inflating" it for the next use. - The **sponge** = Metal Hydride (MH) anode. - The **chemical tank** = Nickel Oxyhydroxide (NiOOH) cathode. - The **pipe water** = H⁺ ions + electrons (via external circuit). - The **pump** = External charger. The beauty is that **no free hydrogen gas is ever produced** inside the cell — hydrogen is stored atomically inside the metal lattice, making NiMH intrinsically safer than NiH₂ or PEM fuel cells. ## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Recall Values) | Parameter | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Nominal Cell Voltage | **1.20 – 1.25** | V | | Open-Circuit Voltage (fresh charge) | **1.35 – 1.40** | V | | End-of-Discharge Voltage | **1.00** | V | | Specific Energy (Gravimetric) | **60 – 110** | Wh/kg | | Energy Density (Volumetric) | **140 – 300** | Wh/L | | Specific Power | **250 – 1000** | W/kg | | Cycle Life (80 % DOD) | **500 – 2000** | cycles | | Operating Temperature | **−20 to +45** | °C | | Self-Discharge Rate | **15 – 30 %/month** at 20 °C | — | | Coulombic Efficiency | **> 90 %** | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** NiMH is covered under Module 2 (Energy Storage) because it represents the **bridge technology** between conventional lead-acid starter batteries and modern Li-ion traction packs. For an HEV powertrain, the NiMH pack typically delivers **1.0 – 1.6 kWh usable energy** at **28 – 38 kW peak power**, which is the sweet spot for full-HEVs (Toyota THS-II system). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Electrochemistry of the NiMH Cell ### 2.1.1 Half-Cell Reactions **During Discharge (cell delivers energy):** - **Positive electrode (cathode):** $$\text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^{-}$$ - **Negative electrode (anode):** $$\text{MH} + \text{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^{-}$$ - **Net cell reaction:** $$\text{NiOOH} + \text{MH} \longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{M}$$ ### 2.1.2 Half-Cell Reactions During Charge (regenerative braking / grid charging) The reactions are simply **reversed**: $$\text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^{-}$$ $$\text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{MH} + \text{OH}^{-}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Notice that **no net change occurs in the electrolyte concentration** during cycling — only water and hydroxyl ions shuffle between electrodes. This is why NiMH cells can be **sealed** (no electrolyte maintenance), unlike flooded lead-acid cells. ## 2.2 Thermodynamic Cell Voltage — Nernst Equation Application The standard electromotive force (EMF) of the NiMH couple is derived from the Gibbs free energy of formation: $$E^{0}_{cell} = \frac{-\Delta G^{0}_{rxn}}{nF}$$ For the NiOOH / MH couple, the experimentally determined standard potential is: $$E^{0} = 1.32 \text{ V (at 25 °C)}$$ Under actual operating conditions, the cell voltage follows the Nernst equation: $$E_{cell} = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(Q)$$ Where: - $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $T$ = Absolute temperature in Kelvin - $n$ = Number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction = **1** - $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol** - $Q$ = Reaction quotient (activity ratio of products to reactants) For NiMH, since all species are solids except water, the simplified form is: $$E_{cell} \approx E^{0} - \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{a_{\text{Ni(OH)}_2} \cdot a_{\text{M}}}{a_{\text{NiOOH}} \cdot a_{\text{MH}} \cdot a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)$$ ## 2.3 The Metal Hydride Anode — Hydrogen Storage Alloys The hydrogen-absorbing alloy is the heart of the NiMH cell. Two principal families are used in HEV applications: | Alloy Type | Composition Example | Capacity (mAh/g) | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | **AB₅ (Mischmetal)** | LaNi₅, La₀.₈Ce₀.₂Ni₃.₅Co₀.₇Mn₀.₃Al₀.₃ | 280 – 320 | Most common, used in Toyota Prius | | **AB₂ (Laves Phase)** | Zr(V₀.₅Mn₀.₅)₂Ni, Ti-Zr-Ni-V | 350 – 420 | Higher capacity, used in Ovonic cells | | **Mg-based (A₂B)** | Mg₂Ni | ~500 (theoretical) | Research stage, slow kinetics | The hydrogen absorption reaction is: $$\text{M} + \frac{x}{2}\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{MH}_x$$ During battery operation, this hydrogen is provided **electrochemically** (from water reduction), not from gaseous H₂. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **all the equations you must memorize** for any NiMH question in Part A or Part B of the KTU ESE. Use `\vert` for absolute-value symbols in tables to preserve markdown formatting. | # | Formula / Identity | Meaning / Application | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E^{0}_{cell} = \dfrac{-\Delta G^{0}_{rxn}}{nF}$ | Standard EMF from Gibbs free energy | V | | 2 | $E_{cell} = E^{0} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln(Q)$ | Nernst equation (temperature-dependent EMF) | V | | 3 | $E_{cell}\big\vert_{T} = E^{0} + \dfrac{\Delta S}{nF}(T - 298)$ | Linearized temperature correction | V | | 4 | $E_{pack} = N_s \cdot E_{cell}$ | Pack voltage from series cells | V | | 5 | $C_{pack} = \dfrac{C_{cell}}{N_p}$ | Pack capacity from parallel cells | Ah | | 6 | $E_{stored} = V_{nom} \times C \times N_s \times N_p$ | Total pack energy stored | Wh | | 7 | $SE = \dfrac{V \cdot C}{m_{cell}}$ | Specific Energy (gravimetric) | Wh/kg | | 8 | $ED = \dfrac{V \cdot C}{V_{cell}}$ | Energy Density (volumetric) | Wh/L | | 9 | $\eta_{coulombic} = \dfrac{C_{dis}}{C_{ch}} \times 100$ | Coulombic (Ah) efficiency | % | | 10 | $\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{E_{dis}}{E_{ch}} \times 100$ | Energy (Wh) efficiency | % | | 11 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \dfrac{1}{C_N}\int_{0}^{t} I(\tau)\,d\tau$ | State of Charge dynamics | dimensionless | | 12 | $DOD = 1 - SOC$ | Depth of Discharge | dimensionless | | 13 | $SOH = \dfrac{C_{actual}}{C_{rated}} \times 100$ | State of Health | % | | 14 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}}$ | Maximum deliverable power (Thevenin match) | W | | 15 | $\rho_{H} = \dfrac{N_H \cdot M_H}{N_A \cdot V_{unit}}$ | Volumetric hydrogen density in alloy | atoms/m³ | **Where:** - $N_s$ = number of cells in series - $N_p$ = number of cells in parallel - $C_N$ = nominal cell capacity - $R_{int}$ = internal resistance of cell - $V_{oc}$ = open-circuit voltage ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in HEV Powertrains The NiMH pack in a Toyota Prius Gen-2 consists of **28 modules, each containing 6 cells** (1.2 V × 168 = **201.6 V nominal pack**). During **regenerative braking**, the electric motor acts as a generator and pushes current *into* the pack — converting kinetic energy to chemical energy. During **acceleration**, the pack drives the motor (M/G) in synergy with the ICE. The **battery management system (BMS)** maintains SOC between **40 % and 80 %** to maximize calendar life — a classic HEV "buffered" operating window that is mathematically justified because NiMH wear is non-linear at extreme SOC values. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Cell EMF from Gibbs Free Energy **Problem:** Given the standard enthalpies and entropies of formation for the NiMH couple, derive the standard EMF and its temperature coefficient. | Species | $\Delta H^{0}_{f}$ (kJ/mol) | $S^{0}$ (J/mol·K) | |---|---|---| | NiOOH | −489.5 | 88.0 | | MH (as LaNi₅H₆) | −190.0 | 154.0 | | Ni(OH)₂ | −538.0 | 80.0 | | M (as LaNi₅) | −160.0 | 145.0 | **Step 1:** Write the net cell reaction: $$\text{NiOOH} + \text{MH} \longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{M}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate $\Delta H^{0}_{rxn}$: $$\Delta H^{0}_{rxn} = \left[\Delta H^{0}_{f,Ni(OH)_2} + \Delta H^{0}_{f,M}\right] - \left[\Delta H^{0}_{f,NiOOH} + \Delta H^{0}_{f,MH}\right]$$ $$\Delta H^{0}_{rxn} = [(-538.0) + (-160.0)] - [(-489.5) + (-190.0)]$$ $$\Delta H^{0}_{rxn} = -698.0 - (-679.5) = -18.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate $\Delta S^{0}_{rxn}$: $$\Delta S^{0}_{rxn} = \left[S^{0}_{Ni(OH)_2} + S^{0}_{M}\right] - \left[S^{0}_{NiOOH} + S^{0}_{MH}\right]$$ $$\Delta S^{0}_{rxn} = [80.0 + 145.0] - [88.0 + 154.0]$$ $$\Delta S^{0}_{rxn} = 225.0 - 242.0 = -17.0 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$ **Step 4:** Calculate $\Delta G^{0}_{rxn}$ at 25 °C (298 K): $$\Delta G^{0}_{rxn} = \Delta H^{0}_{rxn} - T \Delta S^{0}_{rxn}$$ $$\Delta G^{0}_{rxn} = -18{,}500 - (298)(-17.0)$$ $$\Delta G^{0}_{rxn} = -18{,}500 + 5{,}066 = -13{,}434 \text{ J/mol}$$ **Step 5:** Derive the standard EMF using $E^{0} = -\Delta G^{0}_{rxn} / (nF)$ with $n = 1$: $$E^{0} = \frac{-(-13{,}434)}{1 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{13{,}434}{96{,}485}$$ $$E^{0} = 0.1392 \text{ V} \text{ (for the MH half-cell only)}$$ **Step 6:** Combine with the NiOOH/Ni(OH)₂ couple standard potential (≈ 1.30 V vs. SHE) to obtain full cell EMF: $$E^{0}_{cell} = 1.30 + 0.14 = 1.44 \text{ V} \text{ (theoretical, fresh charge)}$$ **Step 7:** Compute temperature coefficient $\left(\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_p$: $$\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_p = \frac{\Delta S^{0}_{rxn}}{nF} = \frac{-17.0}{96{,}485}$$ $$\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_p = -1.76 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V/K} = -0.176 \text{ mV/K}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A *negative* temperature coefficient means the cell voltage **drops as temperature rises**. At 45 °C (typical HEV pack under-hood temperature), the voltage drops by approximately **−3.5 mV** from the 25 °C baseline. The BMS uses this dependency to estimate internal temperature from voltage measurements when sensor data is unavailable. ## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Specific Energy of a NiMH HEV Pack **Problem:** A HEV NiMH module has the following specifications: each cell is rated 6.5 Ah at 1.2 V, mass = 175 g. A pack contains 168 cells in series (28 modules × 6 cells). Calculate (a) pack voltage, (b) pack energy, (c) pack specific energy, (d) range if pack is used in a BEV application with 12 kWh/100 km consumption. **Step (a) — Pack voltage:** $$V_{pack} = N_s \times V_{cell} = 168 \times 1.2 = 201.6 \text{ V}$$ **Step (b) — Pack energy:** $$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times C_{cell} = 201.6 \times 6.5 = 1{,}310.4 \text{ Wh} = 1.31 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step (c) — Pack specific energy:** $$m_{pack} = 168 \times 0.175 = 29.4 \text{ kg}$$ $$SE_{pack} = \frac{E_{pack}}{m_{pack}} = \frac{1{,}310.4}{29.4} = 44.6 \text{ Wh/kg}$$ (Note: this is *lower* than single-cell specific energy due to packaging overhead — typical of modules/packs vs. individual cells.) **Step (d) — EV range:** $$\text{Range} = \frac{E_{pack}}{E_{cons}/100 \text{ km}} = \frac{1.31}{12/100} = 10.9 \text{ km}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion of derivation:** A 1.3 kWh NiMH pack would only deliver ~11 km of BEV range — confirming why NiMH is **fundamentally a HEV (hybrid) chemistry, not a BEV chemistry**. In HEVs the pack only needs to buffer a few kW of regen/boost power, not store the entire trip's energy. ## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — SOC Update During a Discharge Pulse **Problem:** A 6.5 Ah NiMH cell is at SOC = 0.65. A constant current of 13 A is drawn for 12 minutes. Compute the new SOC. **Step 1:** Charge removed in Coulombs: $$\Delta Q = I \cdot t = 13 \times (12 \times 60) = 13 \times 720 = 9{,}360 \text{ C}$$ **Step 2:** Convert to Ah: $$\Delta Q_{Ah} = \frac{9{,}360}{3{,}600} = 2.6 \text{ Ah}$$ **Step 3:** Apply SOC update equation (current direction: discharge, so SOC decreases): $$SOC_{new} = SOC_{old} - \frac{\Delta Q}{C_N} = 0.65 - \frac{2.6}{6.5}$$ $$SOC_{new} = 0.65 - 0.40 = 0.25 \quad (\text{i.e., } 25\%)$$ **Step 4:** Verify depth of discharge: $$DOD = 1 - SOC = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75 \quad (75\%)$$ This is acceptable for NiMH (rated for ≥ 80 % DOD), but the BMS should flag it as approaching the safe lower limit (typically 30 % SOC = 70 % DOD). # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 NiMH Cell Internal Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Positive Cap
Nickel Plated Steel] --> B[Positive Current Collector
Foamed Nickel] B --> C[Positive Electrode
Ni OH 2 Sintered Paste] C --> D[Separator
Polyolefin Non Woven] D --> E[Negative Electrode
MH Alloy Powder with Binder] E --> F[Negative Current Collector
Nickel Plated Steel Mesh] F --> G[Negative Cap
Nickel Plated Steel] H[Electrolyte
30 percent KOH plus LiOH] -.Fills entire interior.- C H -.Fills entire interior.- D H -.Fills entire interior.- E I[Safety Vent
Re seals at 2 to 4 bar] -.Pressure relief.- A style A fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000 style G fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000 style C fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 style E fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#000 style D fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#000 style H fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#000 style I fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Charge / Discharge Reaction Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DISCHARGE_MODE [DISCHARSE - Cell delivers energy] D1[NiOOH Solid] -->|gains e-| D2[Ni OH 2 Solid] D3[MH Solid] -->|loses H+| D4[M Solid] D5[H plus ions migrate through KOH electrolyte] -.-> D1 D6[Electrons flow through external load] -.-> D3 end subgraph CHARGE_MODE [CHARGE - External power input] C1[Ni OH 2 Solid] -->|loses e-| C2[NiOOH Solid] C3[M Solid] -->|gains H+| C4[MH Solid] C5[H plus ions forced back to anode] -.-> C3 C6[Electrons pushed by charger] -.-> C1 end style D1 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 style D2 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 style D3 fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#000 style D4 fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#000 style C1 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 style C2 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 style C3 fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#000 style C4 fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.3 NiMH in HEV Powertrain — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR ICE[Internal Combustion Engine] -->|mechanical| PL[Planetary Gearset Power Split Device] PL -->|mechanical| M1[Traction Motor Generator MG1] PL -->|mechanical| M2[Traction Motor Generator MG2] M1 -->|3 phase AC| INV1[Inverter DC to AC] M2 -->|3 phase AC| INV2[Inverter DC to AC] INV1 --> DCBUS[High Voltage DC Bus 201.6 V] INV2 --> DCBUS DCBUS --> BAT[NIMH Battery Pack 1.3 kWh] DCBUS --> DC[DC DC Converter] DC --> LVS[12 V Low Voltage System] BAT -.BMS telemetry.-> ECU[HEV Energy Management ECU] ECU -.Torque and SOC commands.-> INV1 ECU -.Torque and SOC commands.-> INV2 ECU -.Throttle and brake arbitration.-> ICE style ICE fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 style BAT fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#000 style PL fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style ECU fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#000 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **"List any three advantages of NiMH batteries over NiCd batteries for HEV applications."** **Model Answer (3 key points, 1 mark each):** 1. **Higher specific energy:** NiMH delivers 60–110 Wh/kg versus NiCd's 40–60 Wh/kg — meaning more energy storage per kg, critical for vehicle mass constraints. 2. **Environmental compatibility:** NiMH uses hydrogen-absorbing alloys (e.g., LaNi₅) instead of toxic cadmium (Cd), eliminating hazardous-waste disposal problems and aligning with EU RoHS / End-of-Life Vehicle directives. 3. **Memory effect is significantly reduced:** NiMH tolerates partial state-of-charge cycling much better than NiCd, which is essential for HEV duty cycles that operate continuously between SOC = 40 % and 80 %. --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **"What is the function of the metal hydride alloy in a NiMH cell? Name two commonly used alloy families."** **Model Answer:** - **Function:** The metal hydride alloy serves as the **negative electrode (anode)** and acts as a **reversible hydrogen-storage medium**. During charge, it absorbs hydrogen (electrochemically generated at its surface) to form MHₓ; during discharge, it releases hydrogen ions back to the electrolyte. The alloy does not change its bulk crystalline structure during cycling — only its hydrogen content varies. - **Two alloy families:** 1. **AB₅-type (Mischmetal)** — e.g., La₀.₈Ce₀.₂Ni₃.₅Co₀.₇Mn₀.₃Al₀.₃ — used by Panasonic for Toyota Prius. 2. **AB₂-type (Laves Phase)** — e.g., Zr(V₀.₅Mn₀.₅)₂Ni or Ti-Zr-Ni-V — used by Ovonic Battery Company. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice) ### Question Choice A — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **A. (a)** With neat reaction equations, explain the **electrochemistry of a NiMH cell during both charge and discharge cycles**. Identify the active species, electrolyte, and state the role of the metal hydride electrode. **(7 Marks)** **A. (b)** A HEV traction NiMH pack contains **240 cells** arranged as **40 in series × 6 in parallel**. Each cell is rated at **8 Ah** with nominal voltage of **1.2 V** and internal resistance of **11 mΩ**. Compute: (i) Pack nominal voltage, (ii) Pack energy in kWh, (iii) Maximum theoretical power deliverable from the pack (assuming ideal Thévenin matching), (iv) The pack's specific energy if the total pack mass is **72 kg**. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Question Choice B — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **B. (a)** Compare the construction, performance, and environmental impact of **NiMH versus NiCd versus Li-ion** traction batteries suitable for HEVs. Use a comparison table and discuss why Toyota selected NiMH for the Gen-1/Gen-2 Prius. **(7 Marks)** **B. (b)** A NiMH cell undergoes a discharge test at a constant 2C rate (i.e., 2 × 6.5 A = 13 A) from 100 % SOC. Using the simplified Peukert-style relationship **C_actual = C_rated × (I_nom / I)^(n−1)** with Peukert exponent **n = 1.05** for NiMH, determine: (i) The actual deliverable capacity at 2C discharge, (ii) The discharge time to end voltage, (iii) The energy delivered if the average discharge voltage is 1.18 V. **(7 Marks)** --- ### 5.2.1 Complete Model Solutions for Question Choice A #### Solution to A(a) — Electrochemistry (7 marks) | Step | Concept | Marks | |---|---|---| | 1 | Identify cell type: Alkaline rechargeable, KOH electrolyte | 1 | | 2 | Write discharge half-reactions correctly | 2 | | 3 | Write charge half-reactions (reverse of discharge) | 1 | | 4 | Identify role of MH electrode as H-storage alloy | 2 | | 5 | State that electrolyte concentration does not change | 1 | **Discharge reactions:** $$\text{Cathode: } \text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^{-}$$ $$\text{Anode: } \text{MH} + \text{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^{-}$$ $$\text{Net: } \text{NiOOH} + \text{MH} \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{M}$$ **Charge reactions:** Reverse the above three reactions. **Role of MH alloy:** It functions as a solid-state hydrogen sponge. Hydrogen atoms are absorbed into the intermetallic lattice during charge (forming MHₓ) and released as H⁺/H₂O during discharge, without generating gaseous H₂ inside the cell. **Electrolyte:** ~30 % KOH + small LiOH/NaOH additives — provides OH⁻ conduction path; its overall concentration remains nearly invariant, enabling sealed-cell construction. --- #### Solution to A(b) — Numerical (7 marks) **Given:** $N_s = 40$, $N_p = 6$, $C_{cell} = 8$ Ah, $V_{cell} = 1.2$ V, $R_{int} = 11$ mΩ, $m_{pack} = 72$ kg. **(i) Pack nominal voltage:** $$V_{pack} = N_s \times V_{cell} = 40 \times 1.2 = 48.0 \text{ V}$$ **(ii) Pack energy:** $$C_{pack} = N_p \times C_{cell} = 6 \times 8 = 48 \text{ Ah}$$ $$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times C_{pack} = 48.0 \times 48 = 2{,}304 \text{ Wh} = 2.304 \text{ kWh}$$ **(iii) Maximum deliverable power (Thevenin):** $$R_{pack} = \frac{N_s \cdot R_{cell}}{N_p} = \frac{40 \times 0.011}{6} = 0.0733 \text{ Ω}$$ $$P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{pack}} = \frac{48^2}{4 \times 0.0733} = \frac{2{,}304}{0.2933}$$ $$P_{max} = 7{,}855 \text{ W} \approx 7.86 \text{ kW}$$ **(iv) Pack specific energy:** $$SE_{pack} = \frac{E_{pack}}{m_{pack}} = \frac{2{,}304}{72} = 32.0 \text{ Wh/kg}$$ (Notice again that pack-level SE is lower than single-cell SE due to packaging/cooling/BMS overhead.) --- ### 5.2.2 Complete Model Solutions for Question Choice B #### Solution to B(a) — Comparison (7 marks) | Parameter | NiCd | NiMH | Li-ion (NMC) | |---|---|---|---| | Nominal Voltage | 1.2 V | **1.2 V** | 3.6 – 3.7 V | | Specific Energy | 40 – 60 Wh/kg | **60 – 110 Wh/kg** | 150 – 250 Wh/kg | | Memory Effect | Severe | Mild | None | | Toxicity | High (Cd) | Low | Low | | Self-Discharge | Moderate (~10 %/mo) | High (~20–30 %/mo) | Low (~2 %/mo) | | Cycle Life | 1000 – 2000 | **500 – 2000** | 1000 – 4000 | | Cost per kWh | Medium | Medium-High | Falling rapidly | | HEV Suitability | Poor (memory + toxic) | **Excellent (used in Prius 1997–2015)** | Excellent (post-2010) | **Why Toyota chose NiMH for Gen-1/Gen-2 Prius:** (1) **Mature manufacturing technology** available by 1997, (2) **Proven safety** — no thermal runaway risk as with early Li-ion, (3) **Excellent power density** for 30+ kW peak bursts needed during acceleration, (4) **Wide operating-temperature tolerance** for under-hood environment, (5) **Long cycle life** under shallow cycling (40–80 % SOC window). #### Solution to B(b) — Numerical (7 marks) **Given:** $C_{rated} = 6.5$ Ah, $I = 2C = 13$ A, $n = 1.05$, $V_{avg} = 1.18$ V. **(i) Actual deliverable capacity at 2C:** $$C_{actual} = C_{rated} \times \left(\frac{I_{nom}}{I}\right)^{n-1}$$ With $I_{nom} = C_{rated}$ (= 1C = 6.5 A): $$\frac{I_{nom}}{I} = \frac{6.5}{13} = 0.5$$ $$C_{actual} = 6.5 \times (0.5)^{0.05}$$ Using $\ln(0.5) = -0.693$ and $0.05 \times (-0.693) = -0.0347$: $$C_{actual} = 6.5 \times e^{-0.0347} = 6.5 \times 0.9659 = 6.28 \text{ Ah}$$ **(ii) Discharge time:** $$t = \frac{C_{actual}}{I} = \frac{6.28}{13} = 0.483 \text{ hours} = 28.97 \text{ minutes}$$ **(iii) Energy delivered:** $$E_{delivered} = V_{avg} \times C_{actual} = 1.18 \times 6.28 = 7.41 \text{ Wh}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost students marks in NiMH-related questions:** > > 1. **Writing NiOOH + H⁺ instead of NiOOH + H₂O + e⁻** — In alkaline electrolyte, the proton is **never free**; it travels as H₂O/OH⁻. Writing "H⁺" loses 1 mark. > 2. **Confusing anode/cathode during charge vs discharge** — Remember: anode is where oxidation occurs, cathode is where reduction occurs. The *sign* flips with charge/discharge, but the *electrodes themselves* do not swap. Students who write "during discharge the Ni electrode becomes the anode" lose 2 marks. > 3. **Forgetting that Nernst equation uses natural log (ln), not log₁₀** — Convert correctly using $2.303 \cdot \log_{10}(Q)$, or stay with ln throughout. > 4. **Wrong pack voltage formula** — Students often write $V_{pack} = N_p \cdot V_{cell}$ instead of $N_s \cdot V_{cell}$. **Series adds voltage; parallel adds capacity.** > 5. **Ignoring internal resistance for power calculation** — Always use Thévenin matching ($P_{max} = V_{oc}^2 / 4R$) for "maximum deliverable power" questions. Multiplying V × I without considering voltage sag is incorrect. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — NiMH Battery (Module 2)** - [x] NiMH is an **alkaline rechargeable cell** with KOH electrolyte. - [x] **Cathode:** Ni(OH)₂ / NiOOH; **Anode:** Metal Hydride alloy (AB₅ or AB₂ type). - [x] **Nominal voltage:** **1.2 V** per cell; **Open-circuit (fresh charge):** ~1.35 V. - [x] **Specific energy:** 60–110 Wh/kg; **Cycle life:** 500–2000 cycles at 80 % DOD. - [x] **Net discharge reaction:** NiOOH + MH → Ni(OH)₂ + M *(no net electrolyte change)*. - [x] **Nernst equation:** $E_{cell} = E^0 - (RT/nF) \ln(Q)$, with $n=1$ and $E^0 \approx 1.32$ V. - [x] **Pack voltage:** $V_{pack} = N_s \times V_{cell}$; **Pack capacity:** $C_{pack} = N_p \times C_{cell}$. - [x] **Max power (Thévenin):** $P_{max} = V_{oc}^2 / (4 R_{int})$. - [x] **Temperature coefficient of EMF:** ~ −0.176 mV/K for the MH couple. - [x] **HEV operating SOC window:** typically **40 % – 80 %** to maximize calendar life. - [x] **Toyota Prius Gen-1/Gen-2 uses NiMH** — 201.6 V nominal, 1.3 kWh, 168 cells (28 modules). - [x] **Advantages over NiCd:** higher energy density, no toxic Cd, less memory effect. - [x] **Advantages/disadvantages vs Li-ion:** Safer & cheaper but heavier and lower energy density. - [x] **Self-discharge rate is HIGH (~20–30 %/month)** — a key drawback for plug-in applications. - [x] **Hydrogen stays atomic in the alloy lattice** — no gaseous H₂ inside the cell ⇒ safer than NiH₂ cells.

topics.title: Li-Polymer Battery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Li-Polymer Battery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Lithium-Polymer (Li-Po / LiPo) Battery** is a rechargeable electrochemical energy storage device that employs a **solid or gel-type polymer electrolyte** (instead of the conventional liquid organic electrolyte used in standard Li-ion cells) to facilitate the transport of lithium ions (Li⁺) between the positive electrode (cathode) and the negative electrode (anode) during charge–discharge cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET863 – Module 2):**
> A Li-polymer battery is a subclass of the lithium-ion family in which the liquid electrolyte is replaced (partially or fully) by a **polymer electrolyte membrane**. The most commercially deployed variant is the **Lithium-Ion Polymer (Li-Po / Li-Poly)** cell using a *gel-polymer electrolyte* sandwiched between the anode and cathode, which also functions as the separator.

The three recognized sub-classes defined in the KTU syllabus framework are:

| Sub-Class | Polymer Electrolyte Form | State | Conductivity (S/cm) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dry Polymer Li-Po** | PEO + Li-salt (e.g., LiClO₄) | Solid | ≈ 10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁶ |
| **Gel Polymer Li-Po** | PVDF-HFP + Li-salt + plasticizer | Quasi-solid (gel) | ≈ 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³ |
| **Composite Polymer Li-Po** | Polymer + ceramic filler (Al₂O₃, SiO₂) | Hybrid solid | ≈ 10⁻⁴ |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **wet sponge sandwich** (a Li-ion cell) versus a **moist jelly-filled sandwich** (a Li-Po cell):

- In a **Li-ion battery**, the lithium ions swim across a *liquid electrolyte* — much like water flowing through a soaked sponge. The sponge must be sealed very tightly, or the liquid leaks.
- In a **Li-Po battery**, the ions move through a *gel-like polymer* — imagine a slice of jelly between two bread slices. The jelly doesn't spill, can be moulded into any shape, and is lighter than the equivalent volume of liquid.

This simple picture explains *why* Li-Po cells can be **flexible, ultra-thin, leak-proof, and packaged in aluminium-foil pouches** instead of rigid metal cans — a property of enormous importance for **aerodynamic EV battery pack design** where the pack must conform to the chassis floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Carrier:** The lithium ions (Li⁺) themselves remain the *active charge carrier* in both Li-ion and Li-Po cells. Only the *transport medium* (the electrolyte) changes. Hence the chemistry of Li-Po is fundamentally **identical** to Li-ion — only the packaging and safety profile differ.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Nominal Cell Voltage:** **3.6 – 3.8 V** (per cell, LiCoO₂ chemistry)
- **Charge Cut-off Voltage:** **4.20 V** (per cell)
- **Discharge Cut-off Voltage:** **2.70 – 3.00 V** (per cell)
- **Specific Energy:** **100 – 265 Wh/kg**
- **Energy Density:** **250 – 670 Wh/L**
- **Coulombic Efficiency:** ≈ **99.9 %**
- **Self-Discharge Rate:** ≈ **5 % per month**
- **Operating Temperature Window:** **−20 °C to +60 °C**
- **Cycle Life:** **300 – 1000 cycles** (chemistry dependent)
- **Electrolyte Ionic Conductivity (Gel type):** ≈ **10⁻⁴ – 10⁻³ S/cm**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Tip for KTU Exam:** The famous *“3.7 V, 200 Wh/kg, 5 % per month”* triplet is the Li-Po fingerprint expected in viva and short-answer questions.

---

## 1.4 Distinguishing Li-Po from Li-ion (Syllabus Snapshot)

| Feature | Li-ion (Liquid) | Li-Polymer (Gel) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Electrolyte state | Liquid organic (EC, DMC) | Gel / Solid polymer |
| Separator | Polypropylene / Polyethylene | Polymer electrolyte **acts as separator** |
| Packaging | Rigid metal can (cylindrical/prismatic) | Flexible aluminium-laminate pouch |
| Form factor | Fixed | Custom shapes (curved, ultra-thin) |
| Weight | Heavier | Lighter |
| Leakage risk | Possible | Negligible |
| Energy density (Wh/L) | 250 – 620 | 250 – 670 |
| Cost | Lower | Slightly higher |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
