# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
List **any three categories of sensors** used in a Battery Management System and state the parameter each one measures.

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**
1. **Cell Voltage Sensor** — measures the terminal voltage of individual cells (typical range 2.5 V – 4.2 V for Li‑ion) using a differential amplifier or AFE.
2. **Pack Current Sensor** — measures charge/discharge current (range −500 A to +500 A) using a Hall‑effect transducer or precision shunt resistor.
3. **Cell Temperature Sensor** — measures cell surface temperature (−40 °C to +85 °C) using an NTC thermistor (β ≈ 3 950 K) or PT100 RTD.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
What is the **β‑coefficient** of an NTC thermistor, and how is it computed?

**Model Answer (≈ 70 words):**
The β‑coefficient is a constant that quantifies the *rate of change of resistance with temperature* of an NTC thermistor, expressed in Kelvin. It is computed from resistance measurements at two known temperatures:

$$\beta = \frac{\ln(R_1 / R_2)}{\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}}$$

Typical β values lie between **3 000 K and 4 500 K** for automotive‑grade NTCs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write the *name* of the sensor but forget its **operating principle**. In 14‑mark questions, examiners allocate ~ 3 marks for the principle and ~ 4 marks for the working derivation. Always pair a sensor name with its underlying physics.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a BMS sensor subsystem** in a modern BEV. Describe the role of each sensor category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A BMS uses an **NTC thermistor** with $R_{25} = 10 \;\text{k}\Omega$ and $\beta = 3\,950 \text{ K}$. If the measured resistance is **3.3 kΩ**, determine the corresponding cell temperature in °C. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A modern BEV traction pack (e.g., 400 V, 120 Ah, 96s configuration) integrates six distinct sensor groups feeding a central BMS MCU:

1. **Cell Voltage Sensors** — one AFE channel per cell; accuracy ±5 mV; communicate via SPI.
2. **Pack Current Sensor** — closed‑loop Hall transducer (LEM LF 505‑S) with ±0.5 % accuracy; provides current to the Coulomb‑counter.
3. **Temperature Sensors** — six NTCs per module (one at every fourth cell); 10 kΩ at 25 °C; β ≈ 3 950 K.
4. **Isolation Monitor** — bridge with two 1 MΩ references and a 16‑bit ADC; detects HV bus leakage per UN‑R100.
5. **Pouch‑cell Pressure Sensor** — MEMS piezoresistive (e.g., NXP MPX4250); detects swelling > 5 %.
6. **Gas / Smoke Sensor** — SnO₂ chemo‑resistive; detects H₂ and CO for early thermal runaway warning.

The BMS MCU fuses these inputs using an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) to estimate SoC and SoH, then commands the contactors and cell‑balancing MOSFETs via the efferent (output) path. Communication with the VCU is via CAN 2.0B / CAN‑FD.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Apply β‑equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_T &= R_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[ \beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) \right] \\[4pt]
\frac{3.3}{10} &= \exp\!\left[ 3\,950 \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{298.15} \right) \right] \\[4pt]
\ln(0.33) &= 3\,950 \left( \frac{1}{T} - 3.354 \times 10^{-3} \right) \\[4pt]
-1.1087 &= 3\,950 \left( \frac{1}{T} - 3.354 \times 10^{-3} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{T} - 3.354 \times 10^{-3} &= \frac{-1.1087}{3\,950} = -2.807 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
\frac{1}{T} &= 3.354 \times 10^{-3} - 2.807 \times 10^{-4} = 3.073 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
T &= 325.4 \;\text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to °C.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 325.4 - 273.15 = 52.3 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The cell surface temperature is **≈ 52.3 °C**, which is within the normal operating window but indicates the cooling system should be activated.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a closed‑loop Hall‑effect current sensor** as used in an EV BMS. Derive the expression for **Hall voltage** in terms of current and geometry. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A traction pack draws a continuous current of **250 A** through a Hall sensor mounted at **r = 12 mm** from the conductor. The Hall element has $n_e = 2 \times 10^{21}\,\text{m}^{-3}$, $d = 80 \;\mu\text{m}$, bias current $I_H = 3 \text{ mA}$. Compute the **magnetic flux density** and the **Hall voltage**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The closed‑loop Hall‑effect current sensor employs a **flux‑compensation** principle:

1. The primary current $I_p$ flows through a bus‑bar and produces a magnetic flux in a magnetic core.
2. A Hall element (GaAs or InSb) is placed in the air‑gap of the core and is biased with a constant current $I_H$.
3. The Hall voltage $V_H$ is amplified and drives a **secondary compensation winding** wrapped around the core.
4. The compensation current $I_s$ generates an opposing flux that nullifies the primary flux; at equilibrium $V_H \to 0$ and $I_s \propto I_p$.
5. The compensation current is sensed across a precision resistor, yielding a galvanically isolated, accurate voltage proportional to the primary current.

**Hall voltage derivation:**

The Lorentz force on charge carriers produces a transverse Hall field $E_H = v_d \cdot B$, where $v_d = I_H/(q n_e A)$ and $A = w d$ is the cross‑sectional area. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_H &= E_H \cdot w = \frac{I_H \cdot B}{q \cdot n_e \cdot d}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a straight conductor, $B = \mu_0 I_p / (2 \pi r)$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Magnetic flux density.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{\mu_0 \cdot I_p}{2 \pi r} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 250}{2 \pi \times 0.012} \\
  &= \frac{10^{-7} \times 250}{0.012} = 2.083 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{T} \;=\; 2.08 \;\text{mT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Hall voltage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_H &= \frac{I_H \cdot B}{q \cdot n_e \cdot d} \\
    &= \frac{(3 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2.083 \times 10^{-3})}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (2 \times 10^{21}) \times (80 \times 10^{-6})} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{2.56 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.244 \;\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $B = \mathbf{2.08 \text{ mT}}$ and $V_H = \mathbf{0.244 \text{ V}}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High‑Density Revision Checklist — "Sensors Used in BMS"**

- **BMS sensors are the *afferent* interface** between the battery and the controller; without them, SoC/SoH algorithms cannot function.
- **Six core sensor categories:** Cell voltage, Pack current, Temperature, Isolation, Pressure, Gas/Smoke.
- **Cell voltage sensing** uses **AFE ICs** (e.g., BQ76952, LTC6811) with **±5 mV accuracy** and **16‑bit ADC resolution**.
- **Current sensing trade‑off:**
  - *Shunt* → highest accuracy, but dissipates **I² R_sh** heat.
  - *Open‑loop Hall* → galvanic isolation, ±2 % accuracy.
  - *Closed‑loop Hall* → better accuracy (±0.5 %), zero flux principle.
  - *Rogowski coil* → only AC / transient; no saturation.
- **Hall voltage formula:** $V_H = \dfrac{I_H \cdot B}{q \cdot n_e \cdot d}$ — proportional to bias current, flux density, and inversely proportional to carrier concentration and plate thickness.
- **NTC thermistor** obeys $R_T = R_0 \exp\!\big[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)\big]$ with $\beta \approx \mathbf{3\,950 \text{ K}}$ for automotive NTCs.
- **Isolation resistance** must remain ≥ **100 Ω/V** of HV bus voltage (UN‑R100 / ISO 6469‑3).
- **Typical placement:** 6 NTCs per 12‑cell module, 1 Hall sensor per pack, 1 isolation monitor per HV bus.
- **Functional safety:** Voltage and current sensors are **ASIL‑C**; isolation monitor is **ASIL‑D** per ISO 26262.
- **Emerging sensors:** SnO₂ gas sensors and MEMS pressure sensors for *predictive thermal runaway* detection.
- **Common exam traps:**
  - Forgetting to convert K → °C in temperature derivations.
  - Using shunt power formula $V^2/R$ instead of $I^2R$ (or vice versa).
  - Writing `|x|` inside markdown tables — use `\vert x \vert` instead.
  - Confusing *open‑loop* vs *closed‑loop* Hall accuracy.
- **Standards to quote:** ISO 6469‑3, UN‑R100, AIS‑156, ISO 26262 (ASIL), IEC 62133.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Management Systems of Battery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Thermal Management Systems of Battery # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Battery Thermal Management System (BTMS):** A Battery Thermal Management System (BTMS) is an engineered subsystem of an Electric Vehicle (EV) or Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) powertrain whose primary function is to maintain the operating temperature of the traction battery pack within its **optimal temperature window (typically 15 °C to 40 °C for Lithium-ion cells)**, while simultaneously minimizing the temperature gradient between individual cells (ΔT ≤ 5 °C), and preventing the onset of **thermal runaway**. In formal KTU Module 2 terminology, the BTMS encompasses: - **Heat generation mechanisms** inside the electrochemical cell (Joule/Ohmic losses, entropic heat, side-reaction heat). - **Heat transfer pathways** (conduction through cell casing → tab/terminal → coolant channels → ambient). - **Active and passive cooling/heating strategies** (air, liquid, PCM, refrigerant, thermoelectric). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The syllabus explicitly groups BTMS under "Electric Energy Storage" of HEVs/EVs, highlighting that poor thermal management reduces battery **cycle life by up to 50%** and can trigger **safety-critical thermal runaway** above **80 °C** for typical NMC Li-ion chemistries. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a marathon runner during the Mumbai Marathon in summer: - The runner's muscles (the **battery cells**) generate enormous heat while sprinting. - If the body just sits in that heat (no cooling), the runner collapses (this is **thermal runaway**). - The body therefore uses **sweating (liquid cooling)** to dump excess heat into the air. - A water spray and a fan on the route act like **forced-air or hybrid cooling systems**. The battery pack in an EV is identical — it generates heat during charge and discharge, and the BTMS is the engineered "sweating system" that keeps the pack cool, uniform, and safe. ## 1.3 Why BTMS is Critical — The "Three Pillars" > [!NOTE] > The BTMS is governed by three engineering pillars, every one of which appears in KTU exam questions: > > 1. **Performance:** Internal resistance drops at warm temperatures → more usable capacity. Too cold (below **0 °C**) → capacity drops to ~50%; lithium plating risk. > 2. **Life:** Calendar and cycle aging follow **Arrhenius kinetics** — every 10 °C rise roughly **doubles** the degradation rate. > 3. **Safety:** Above the **onset temperature (T₁ ≈ 80–100 °C)**, exothermic SEI decomposition releases heat; above **T₂ ≈ 110–130 °C**, separator melts; above **T₃ ≈ 150–180 °C**, cathode releases oxygen → **thermal runaway**, possible fire/explosion. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Universal gas constant:** **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** - **Typical cell operating window:** **15 °C – 40 °C (288 K – 313 K)** - **Thermal runaway onset:** **≥ 80 °C (353 K)** for NMC cells - **Thermal conductivity of aluminum (cell casing):** **k_Al ≈ 205 W/(m·K)** - **Specific heat capacity of cell (typical):** **c_p ≈ 1000–1100 J/(kg·K)** - **Activation energy for SEI decomposition:** **E_a ≈ 1.25 eV** (≈ 120 kJ/mol) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Sources of Heat Generation in a Li-ion Cell A lithium-ion cell under load produces heat via **four distinct mechanisms**. The relative dominance of each depends on C-rate, SoC, and temperature. ### 2.1.1 Joule (Ohmic) Heating — Irreversible - Caused by ionic resistance of electrolyte and contact resistance at current collectors. - Always **exothermic** (heat out). ### 2.1.2 Entropic (Reversible) Heat - Due to entropy change of the electrochemical reaction. - Sign depends on current direction (charge vs. discharge). - Proportional to **entropic coefficient (∂E/∂T)**. ### 2.1.3 Reaction / Polarization Heat - From charge-transfer overpotential (kinetic polarization) and concentration overpotential (mass-transport polarization). ### 2.1.4 Side-Reaction Heat - From parasitic reactions: SEI growth, electrolyte decomposition, lithium plating. - Becomes dominant during **abuse** (overcharge, high temp, internal short). ## 2.2 The Three Modes of Heat Transfer — Applied to a Battery Pack | Mode | Governing Equation | Practical Application in BTMS | |---|---|---| | **Conduction** | $q_{cond} = -k \, A \, \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Heat flows from cell core → cell can → cold plate | | **Convection** | $q_{conv} = h \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Heat dumped by coolant air/liquid into ambient or radiator | | **Radiation** | $q_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | Often neglected in pack-level BTMS, but relevant at cell surface in vacuum or during abuse | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Decision Insight:** Conduction and convection dominate pack-level BTMS design. Designers tune the **thermal interface material (TIM)** conductivity (k) and convective heat-transfer coefficient (h) — these two variables drive BTMS performance more than any other parameter. ## 2.3 Classification of BTMS Architectures > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus expects the student to be able to **draw, classify, and compare** the four major BTMS types: 1. **Air Cooling (ACM)** — simplest, cheapest, lowest heat-removal capability. - Sub-types: Natural convection, Forced convection (parallel flow, series flow, U-flow). 2. **Liquid Cooling (LCM)** — best heat-removal capacity, dominates modern EVs. - Sub-types: Direct (cell submerged in dielectric oil), Indirect (cold plate beneath cells), Jacket cooling. 3. **Phase-Change Material (PCM)** — passive, excellent thermal buffering for short high-rate pulses. 4. **Refrigerant / Vapor Compression Cooling** — highest performance, used in extreme-fast-charging (XFC) applications. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are KTU-exam-tested.** Memorize the units and the sign convention. | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_{tot} = I (E_{oc} - U_{t}) - I T \dfrac{\partial E_{oc}}{\partial T}$ | Bernardi heat generation | W | | 2 | $Q_{Joule} = I^2 R_{int}$ | Ohmic/Joule heat | W | | 3 | $Q_{rev} = - I T \dfrac{\partial E_{oc}}{\partial T}$ | Reversible (entropic) heat | W | | 4 | $Q_{loss} = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Convective heat loss to coolant | W | | 5 | $Q_{stored} = m \, c_p \, \dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | Sensible heat storage rate | W | | 6 | $\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{Q \, L}{k \, A}$ | Steady-state 1-D temperature rise | K | | 7 | $\text{Energy balance: } \rho \, c_p \, V \dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{gen} - \dot{Q}_{loss}$ | Lumped-cell BTMS ODE | W | | 8 | $\dot{Q}_{coolant} = \dot{m} \, c_{p,fluid} \, (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | Coolant heat pickup | W | | 9 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{k \, A}$ | Thermal resistance (conduction) | K/W | | 10 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{1}{h \, A}$ | Thermal resistance (convection) | K/W | | 11 | $R_{th,total} = \sum R_{th,i}$ | Resistances in series add | K/W | | 12 | $\text{Arrhenius: } k_d = A \, e^{-E_a / (R T)}$ | Aging rate (doubling per ~10 K) | 1/s | ## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World - **Tesla Model Y / BMW iX:** Indirect liquid cold-plate with 50/50 water-glycol coolant. - **BYD Blade Battery:** Uses PCM-impregnated cell-to-cell sandwich for added safety margin. - **Lucid Air / Porsche Taycan:** Multi-loop refrigerant BTMS (800 V architecture, 350 kW DC fast charging — heat removal **> 5 kW** from pack). ## 2.6 Operating Constraints and Boundary Conditions - Inlet coolant temperature: usually **20 °C – 35 °C** (above dew point to prevent condensation). - Maximum allowable ΔT across pack: **≤ 5 K** (for Li-ion cycle life uniformity). - Coolant velocity (liquid): **0.2 – 1.5 m/s** (laminar to mildly turbulent). - Reynolds number threshold for turbulent cold plate flow: **Re > 2300**. ## 2.7 Comparative Evaluation of BTMS Types (KTU Table) | Feature | Air Cooling | Liquid Cooling | PCM | Refrigerant | |---|---|---|---|---| | Heat removal (W/cell) | 5–15 | 30–80 | 20–40 (peak) | 80–150 | | ΔT uniformity | Moderate (8–10 K) | Excellent (3–5 K) | Good (5–7 K) | Best (<3 K) | | Weight / Volume | High | Moderate | High | Lowest | | Cost | Low | Moderate | Moderate | High | | Complexity | Low | Medium | Low | High | | EV Adoption | Two-wheelers, low-power HEVs | Mainstream EVs | Niche / safety backup | Premium + XFC | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Bernardi's Heat Generation Equation (Full Derivation) This is the **single most important derivation** in BTMS — it appears in almost every KTU Module 2 question. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Starting Premises:** We treat the Li-ion cell as a control volume at uniform temperature T. Let **I** be the current (positive on discharge, negative on charge), **E_oc** the open-circuit voltage, and **U_t** the terminal voltage. **Step 1 — Energy balance on the cell control volume.** The total energy entering the cell per unit time from the external circuit is: $$\dot{W}_{elec} = I \, E_{oc}$$ The total energy leaving the cell as electrical work at the terminals is: $$\dot{W}_{term} = I \, U_t$$ **Step 2 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to the cell.** For a control volume with no mass flow across the boundary, the change in internal energy of the cell equals the net electrical energy in minus the heat rejected: $$\dot{U}_{cell} = \dot{W}_{elec} - \dot{W}_{term} - \dot{Q}_{loss}$$ Rearranging for the heat rate leaving the cell: $$\dot{Q}_{gen} = I (E_{oc} - U_t) - \dot{U}_{cell}$$ **Step 3 — Decompose the internal energy change into entropic and non-entropic parts.** The change in internal energy with temperature at constant SoC can be written using the partial derivative with respect to T (holding SoC constant): $$\dot{U}_{cell} = I \, T \, \frac{\partial E_{oc}}{\partial T}$$ This is because the **Gibbs free energy** of the electrochemical reaction equals the open-circuit voltage via $\Delta G = -nFE_{oc}$, and the temperature derivative of $\Delta G$ relates to entropy change $\Delta S$: $$\frac{\partial E_{oc}}{\partial T} = -\frac{\Delta S}{n F}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute back to obtain Bernardi's equation.** $$\dot{Q}_{gen} = I (E_{oc} - U_t) - I \, T \, \frac{\partial E_{oc}}{\partial T}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{gen} = I (E_{oc} - U_t) - I T \frac{\partial E_{oc}}{\partial T}}$$ **Step 5 — Physical interpretation of each term.** - **Term 1 — Polarization / Joule heat:** $I (E_{oc} - U_t)$ = polarization overpotential × current. This is always **positive on discharge** (heat out). - **Term 2 — Entropic heat:** $-I T \dfrac{\partial E_{oc}}{\partial T}$. Sign flips with direction of current — this is the **reversible** heat. **Step 6 — Numerical evaluation example.** > **Given:** A 50 Ah NMC cell, discharging at 1C (= 50 A). Open-circuit voltage = 3.7 V, terminal voltage under load = 3.5 V. Entropic coefficient = −0.5 mV/K. Cell temperature = 298 K. > > Compute the heat generated per cell. Polarization (Joule + kinetic) heat term: $$\dot{Q}_{pol} = I (E_{oc} - U_t) = 50 \times (3.7 - 3.5) = 50 \times 0.2 = 10 \, W$$ Entropic heat term: $$\dot{Q}_{rev} = -I \, T \, \frac{\partial E_{oc}}{\partial T} = -50 \times 298 \times (-0.0005) = +7.45 \, W$$ Total heat generation rate: $$\dot{Q}_{gen} = 10 + 7.45 = 17.45 \, W \text{ per cell}$$ For a **96-cell pack** in a typical EV (e.g., 400 V architecture, 96s1p), the **total pack heat rate** is: $$\dot{Q}_{pack} = 96 \times 17.45 = 1675.2 \, W \approx 1.68 \, kW$$ This is why a real EV BTMS must remove **1 – 5 kW** continuously during normal driving and **5 – 10 kW** during DC fast charging. ## 3.2 Derivation of Lumped-Cell Energy Balance (1st-Order ODE) We treat the cell as a single node with uniform temperature **T(t)**. Heat is generated internally at rate $\dot{Q}_{gen}$ and lost by convection to a coolant at $T_\infty$ with heat-transfer coefficient h and surface area A. **Step 1 — Energy conservation on the lumped cell.** $$\rho \, V \, c_p \, \frac{dT}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{gen} - h A (T - T_\infty)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute Bernardi's $\dot{Q}_{gen}$** (assumed constant for analysis): $$\rho \, V \, c_p \, \frac{dT}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{gen} - h A (T - T_\infty)$$ **Step 3 — Identify the time constant.** This is a 1st-order linear ODE of the form $\dfrac{dT}{dt} + \dfrac{hA}{\rho V c_p} T = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{gen} + h A T_\infty}{\rho V c_p}$. Let thermal time constant be: $$\tau_{th} = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A}$$ **Step 4 — Solve using initial condition $T(0) = T_0$.** Steady-state cell temperature is: $$T_{ss} = T_\infty + \frac{\dot{Q}_{gen}}{h A}$$ Full transient solution: $$T(t) = T_\infty + \frac{\dot{Q}_{gen}}{h A} \left[ 1 - e^{-t / \tau_{th}} \right] + (T_0 - T_\infty) \, e^{-t / \tau_{th}}$$ **Step 5 — Engineering implication.** If the EV accelerates hard for $t = 30 \, \text{s}$ and $\tau_{th} \approx 600 \, \text{s}$, then $t/\tau_{th} \approx 0.05$, so $e^{-0.05} \approx 0.95$ — the cell barely warms. This is why short high-power pulses are tolerable even without aggressive cooling, but **continuous high-rate driving requires sustained BTMS operation**. ## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Cell-to-Cell Temperature Difference For a uniformly distributed heat generation $\dot{q}''$ (W/m³) inside a slab of thickness **2L** with both faces convectively cooled: **Step 1 — Apply 1-D Fourier conduction** with internal source. The governing ODE in the slab: $$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \frac{\dot{q}'''}{k} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Integrate twice** with boundary conditions: symmetry at x = 0 (so dT/dx = 0), and convection at x = L. $$T(x) = T_\infty + \frac{\dot{q}''' L}{h} + \frac{\dot{q}''' (L^2 - x^2)}{2 k}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum temperature occurs at the center (x = 0).** $$T_{max} = T_\infty + \frac{\dot{q}''' L}{h} + \frac{\dot{q}''' L^2}{2 k}$$ **Step 4 — Surface temperature** is at x = L: $$T_{surface} = T_\infty + \frac{\dot{q}''' L}{h}$$ **Step 5 — Maximum ΔT across the slab (center to surface):** $$\boxed{\Delta T_{max} = \frac{\dot{q}''' L^2}{2 k}}$$ **Step 6 — Cell-to-cell ΔT for a pack of n cells** (each cell approximated as a slab): $$\Delta T_{pack} \approx \frac{\dot{q}''' L^2}{2 k_{cell}} + \Delta T_{contact} + \Delta T_{coolant}$$ The KTU design requirement is typically **ΔT_pack ≤ 5 K**. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of BTMS for Given Driving Cycle | Driving Cycle | Heat Generation | Recommended BTMS | Rationale | |---|---|---|---| | Urban stop-and-go (low C-rate) | < 1 kW | Passive air cooling | Low heat, cost-effective | | Highway cruise (moderate C-rate) | 1 – 2 kW | Forced air / indirect liquid | Balance of cost & performance | | Fast charging (150 kW DC) | 4 – 8 kW (transient) | Liquid cold plate + PCM | High heat-flux, tight ΔT | | Performance driving / racing | 5 – 10 kW continuous | Direct liquid + active loop | Sustained high heat removal | | XFC (350 kW charging) | > 10 kW | Refrigerant direct cooling | Only system that meets heat-flux density | ## 3.5 Worked Example — Sizing a Cold-Plate BTMS > **Given:** A 96s1p NMC pack, nominal capacity = 100 Ah, average cell voltage = 3.6 V. Fast charge at 2C = 200 A. Entropic coefficient = −0.4 mV/K. T_cell = 308 K. h at cold plate = 500 W/(m²·K). Pack dimensions: 1.6 m × 1.0 m × 0.18 m. > > **(a)** Find the total heat generation rate of the pack. > **(b)** Find the coolant mass flow rate if inlet = 25 °C, outlet allowed = 35 °C, and the coolant is 50/50 water-glycol with $c_p = 3300 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$. **Solution:** **(a)** Per-cell heat: $$\dot{Q}_{cell} = I (E_{oc} - U_t) - I T \frac{\partial E_{oc}}{\partial T} = 200 \times 0.2 - 200 \times 308 \times (-0.0004)$$ $$\dot{Q}_{cell} = 40 + 24.64 = 64.64 \, W$$ Pack total: $$\dot{Q}_{pack} = 96 \times 64.64 = 6205.4 \, W \approx 6.2 \, kW$$ **(b)** Coolant flow rate from $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$: $$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{pack}}{c_p \, \Delta T_{coolant}} = \frac{6205.4}{3300 \times 10} = 0.188 \, \text{kg/s}$$ Convert to volumetric flow rate (coolant density ≈ 1070 kg/m³): $$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{0.188}{1070} = 1.76 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 10.5 \, \text{L/min}$$ This is in the typical **8 – 15 L/min** range for an automotive EV BTMS loop — order-of-magnitude check passes. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Battery Thermal Management System (Functional Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Battery Pack
96 cells NMC] --> B[Temperature Sensors
NTC thermistors] B --> C[BMU / BMS Controller
Microprocessor] C --> D{Power & Control Bus
CAN / LIN} D --> E1[Cold Plate
Indirect Liquid] D --> E2[PTC Heater
Low-Temp Mode] D --> E3[Pump and Valve
Actuator Bank] D --> E4[Radiator
+ Fan Assembly] E1 --> F[Coolant Loop
50/50 Water-Glycol] E2 --> F F --> G[Chiller or
Refrigerant Exchanger] G --> H[Heat Rejection
to Ambient Air] H --> I[Vehicle HVAC
Optional Coupling] C --> J[HMI and OBD
Telemetry Output] K[Driver Input
Charge / Drive Mode] --> C ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The BMS reads every cell's temperature (B), decides whether heating or cooling is needed (C), and commands the actuators (E1–E4) through the vehicle bus (D). The coolant loop (F) is the energy highway, ultimately rejecting heat to ambient (H) via the radiator or sharing it with the cabin HVAC loop (I). ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Heat Flow Path Inside a Cell → Pack → Ambient ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Electrochemical
Reaction Core] --> A2[Joule and Entropic
Heat Generation] A2 --> A3[Cell Can
Aluminum Casing] A3 --> A4[Tab and Terminal
Conduction Path] A3 --> A5[Thermal Interface
Material TIM] A5 --> A6[Cold Plate
or Air Channel] A6 --> A7[Coolant or Air
Convective Transport] A7 --> A8[Radiator
Heat Exchanger] A8 --> A9[Ambient Air
Final Sink] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparison of BTMS Architectures ```mermaid flowchart TB Root[BTMS Architectures] --> A[Air Cooling ACM] Root --> B[Liquid Cooling LCM] Root --> C[Phase Change PCM] Root --> D[Refrigerant Cooling] A --> A1[Passive Natural Convection] A --> A2[Forced Fan Parallel] A --> A3[Forced Fan Series] B --> B1[Indirect Cold Plate] B --> B2[Direct Immersion Oil] C --> C1[Paraffin Based] C --> C2[Composite PCM] D --> D1[R134a Vapor Loop] D --> D2[R1234yf Loop] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Thermal Runaway Trigger Chain (Safety-Critical) ```mermaid flowchart TB T1[T1 ~ 80 to 100 C
SEI Decomposition
Exothermic Start] --> T2[T2 ~ 110 to 130 C
Separator Melting
Internal Short] T2 --> T3[T3 ~ 150 to 180 C
Cathode Oxygen Release
Electrolyte Combustion] T3 --> T4[T4 > 250 C
Thermal Runaway
Venting or Fire] BTMS[Active BTMS Intervention
Cooling Cutoff Trigger] -.-> T1 T1 -.-> BTMS ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering interpretation:** The BTMS must detect the temperature rise between T1 and T2 (a window of only ~30 K) and inject enough cooling to push the trajectory back below T1 within seconds. A typical EV BTMS uses **NTC thermistors per cell** with a 1 Hz polling rate and a cell-level isolation switch. ## 4.5 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop BTMS Control Logic ```mermaid flowchart TB S[Sensor Readings
T cell, T coolant, I, V] --> F[Filtering
Moving Average] F --> ERR[Error Calculation
T target - T measured] ERR --> PID[PID Controller
K p, K i, K d] PID --> PWM[PWM Output
to Pump or Fan] PWM --> ACT[Actuator
Pump RPM or Fan Speed] ACT --> Q[Coolant Flow Modulation
Heat Removal Rate] Q --> S ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any three sources of heat generation in a Li-ion battery cell during operation and briefly explain each.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per source):** 1. **Joule (Ohmic) heating** — Caused by the resistance to current flow through the electrolyte, separator, and current collectors. Magnitude given by $Q_{Joule} = I^2 R_{int}$. Always exothermic. 2. **Entropic (reversible) heat** — Due to entropy change in the electrochemical reaction. Quantified as $Q_{rev} = - I T \, \partial E_{oc} / \partial T$. Sign depends on whether the cell is charging or discharging. 3. **Polarization / overpotential heat** — Caused by charge-transfer kinetics and concentration gradients at the electrode–electrolyte interface. ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the function of a Battery Thermal Management System (BTMS) in an Electric Vehicle?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A BTMS is responsible for: 1. **Maintaining** the battery cell temperature within the optimal operating window (**15 °C – 40 °C**). 2. **Minimizing temperature non-uniformity** between cells (target ΔT ≤ **5 °C**) to prevent capacity imbalance and uneven aging. 3. **Preventing thermal runaway** by removing heat at high C-rates and pre-heating cells in cold weather to avoid lithium plating. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - In derivation questions, **do not skip algebraic steps** — every transition must be shown. > - Always **state the underlying assumption** (e.g., "lumped cell model", "1-D conduction", "constant properties"). > - For numerical problems, **carry units through every step** and write the final answer in SI units with a boxed result. > - When asked to "compare" BTMS types, always present a **comparison table** — it is the most mark-efficient format. ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive Bernardi's equation for the heat generation rate in a Li-ion cell. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 50 Ah Li-ion cell is discharged at 2C rate. The open-circuit voltage is 3.7 V, terminal voltage is 3.4 V, and the entropic coefficient $\partial E_{oc}/\partial T = -0.4$ mV/K. Cell temperature is 303 K. Compute: (i) polarization heat, (ii) entropic heat, (iii) total heat per cell, (iv) total heat for a 100s1p pack. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution A(a) — Derivation of Bernardi's Equation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Define control volume and list assumptions.** (1 mark) *Assumptions:* - Cell treated as a control volume with uniform temperature (lumped-cell model). - Single representative current I and voltage pair. - Internal energy change arises only from the temperature derivative of the Gibbs free energy. **Step 2 — Apply the First Law.** (1 mark) $$\dot{Q}_{gen} = I(E_{oc} - U_t) - \dot{U}_{cell}$$ **Step 3 — Relate internal energy change to entropic term.** (2 marks) Using $\Delta G = -nF E_{oc}$ and the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation: $$\dot{U}_{cell} = I \, T \, \frac{\partial E_{oc}}{\partial T}$$ **Step 4 — Final assembly.** (1 mark) $$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{gen} = I(E_{oc} - U_t) - I \, T \, \frac{\partial E_{oc}}{\partial T}}$$ **Step 5 — Physical interpretation of each term.** (2 marks) - $I(E_{oc} - U_t)$ = polarization heat (irreversible, always exothermic on discharge). - $-I T (\partial E_{oc}/\partial T)$ = entropic heat (reversible, sign depends on current direction). --- ### Model Solution A(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks) Given: I = 2C × 50 Ah = **100 A**, $E_{oc} = 3.7$ V, $U_t = 3.4$ V, $\partial E_{oc}/\partial T = -4 \times 10^{-4}$ V/K, T = 303 K. (1 mark for substitution setup) **(i) Polarization heat:** (2 marks) $$\dot{Q}_{pol} = I(E_{oc} - U_t) = 100 \times (3.7 - 3.4) = 100 \times 0.3 = 30 \, W$$ **(ii) Entropic heat:** (2 marks) $$\dot{Q}_{rev} = -I T \frac{\partial E_{oc}}{\partial T} = -100 \times 303 \times (-4 \times 10^{-4}) = +12.12 \, W$$ **(iii) Total per cell:** (1 mark) $$\dot{Q}_{cell} = 30 + 12.12 = \boxed{42.12 \, W}$$ **(iv) Total for 100s1p pack:** (1 mark) $$\dot{Q}_{pack} = 100 \times 42.12 = \boxed{4212 \, W \approx 4.21 \, kW}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Compare Air Cooling, Liquid Cooling, and Phase-Change Material (PCM) cooling for EV battery packs under the heads of (i) heat removal capacity, (ii) temperature uniformity, (iii) weight and packaging, (iv) cost and complexity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a closed-loop liquid-cooled BTMS for an EV. Show the role of BMS, sensors, cold plate, pump, and radiator. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution B(a) — Comparison (7 marks) | Parameter | Air Cooling | Liquid Cooling | PCM Cooling | |---|---|---|---| | (i) Heat removal | Low (5–15 W/cell) | High (30–80 W/cell) | Moderate peak (20–40 W/cell, transient) | | (ii) ΔT uniformity | Moderate (8–10 K) | Excellent (3–5 K) | Good (5–7 K) | | (iii) Weight/packaging | High (large ducts) | Moderate (compact cold plate) | High (PCM mass adds weight) | | (iv) Cost/complexity | Low/Low | Moderate/Moderate | Moderate/Low | (1.5 marks per row × 4 rows = 6 marks; 1 mark for concise conclusion: "Liquid cooling is the mainstream choice for modern EVs because of the best balance of heat removal and ΔT uniformity, with PCM increasingly used as a safety buffer.") ### Model Solution B(b) — Closed-Loop Liquid-Cooled BTMS (7 marks) **Block diagram (3 marks):** ``` Battery Cells → Cold Plate → Coolant Loop → Pump → Radiator/Fan → Chiller (refrigerant coupling) → back to Battery ↑ ↓ └── BMS Controller ← Sensors (T, I, V) ──────┘ ``` **Working explanation (4 marks):** 1. **Sensors (NTC thermistors)** mounted on each cell send temperature data to the **BMS** every ~1 s. 2. **BMS** computes the deviation from the target window (e.g., 25 °C) and decides whether to engage cooling or heating. 3. **Coolant pump** speed is modulated via PWM. Higher cell T → higher pump RPM → more coolant mass flow → more heat pickup. 4. **Cold plate** under the cells extracts heat via conduction + convection; heat is carried by the coolant to the **radiator**, where it is dumped to ambient air via the fan. 5. Under high heat-load conditions (DC fast charging), a **chiller** couples the coolant loop with the refrigerant loop (vehicle AC) for additional heat removal. 6. A **PTC heater** warms the coolant during sub-zero starts to prevent lithium plating. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):** > > - **Bernardi's equation:** $\dot{Q}_{gen} = I(E_{oc} - U_t) - I T (\partial E_{oc}/\partial T)$ — know both terms and their physical meaning. > - **Operating window of Li-ion:** **15 °C – 40 °C**; ΔT between cells **≤ 5 K**; runaway onset **~80–100 °C** for NMC. > - **Four heat sources:** Joule, entropic, polarization, side-reaction. > - **Three heat-transfer modes:** conduction, convection, radiation (radiation negligible in BTMS except in abuse). > - **Four BTMS types:** Air, Liquid, PCM, Refrigerant — liquid dominates EVs, PCM is a safety backup. > - **Cold-plate flow:** turbulent regime preferred, Re > 2300. > - **Coolant:** typically **50/50 water-glycol**; $c_p \approx 3300$ J/(kg·K); ρ ≈ 1070 kg/m³. > - **Lumped-cell ODE:** $\rho V c_p \, dT/dt = \dot{Q}_{gen} - hA(T - T_\infty)$; thermal time constant $\tau_{th} = \rho V c_p / (hA)$. > - **Maximum ΔT in slab:** $\Delta T_{max} = \dot{q}''' L^2 / (2k)$ — centerline vs surface. > - **Arrhenius aging:** every 10 K temperature rise roughly doubles degradation rate. > - **Thermal runaway cascade:** $T_1$ (SEI) → $T_2$ (separator) → $T_3$ (cathode O₂) → fire/explosion. > - **Real-world pack heat load:** **1–5 kW** driving, **5–10 kW** DC fast charging, **> 10 kW** XFC. > - **Cold-plate sizing rule:** for a 6 kW pack with ΔT_coolant = 10 K and 50/50 glycol, expect ~**10 L/min** coolant flow. > - **KTU pitfall:** always box the final numerical answer and state units — partial credit depends on it.

topics.title: Battery Safety Standards in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Battery Safety Standards in India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Battery Safety Standards in India** refer to the codified set of technical regulations, testing protocols, and performance benchmarks mandated by regulatory bodies (ARAI, BIS, MoRTH) for the design, manufacturing, testing, and end-of-life management of high-voltage traction batteries used in Electric Vehicles (EVs) and Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs). These standards are framed under the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989** and the **Automotive Industry Standards (AIS)** series, primarily governed by the **Automotive Research Association of India (ARAI)** in collaboration with the **Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH)** and the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term):**
> Battery Safety Standards in India are mandatory pre-condition requirements (homologation criteria) that every traction battery pack must satisfy before it can be legally deployed in any road-going electric or hybrid vehicle registered under Indian law.

## 1.2 Key Standards at a Glance

The Indian battery safety ecosystem is built on four pillars:

1. **AIS-156 (Rev 1 / Phase 2 / Phase 3)** — Specific safety requirements for **L, M, and N category vehicles** with electric power train (originally notified in 2020; amended after 2022 fire incidents).
2. **AIS-038 (Rev 2)** — Safety requirements for **traction batteries** (lithium-ion) for road vehicles.
3. **AIS-039** — Performance specifications for **lithium-ion traction battery packs** (testing, cycle life, marking, and labeling).
4. **AIS-156 Phase 3 (2024 amendment)** — Introduced stringent **thermal propagation (cell-to-cell)** testing, stricter **BMS functional safety**, and **advanced warning systems** to occupants.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"L, M, N"** nomenclature follows the **ECE/WP.29** classification:
> - **L category** → 2-wheelers, 3-wheelers (e.g., electric scooters)
> - **M category** → Passenger cars (e.g., Tata Nexon EV)
> - **N category** → Goods/commercial vehicles (e.g., Tata Ace EV)

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The "Driving License" of EV Batteries**
> Think of battery safety standards as the **driving license test** for an EV battery. Before a battery is allowed on Indian roads, it must "pass" a series of extreme tests — just like a human driver must pass a vision, signal, and road test. If the battery cannot withstand a nail-penetration test, overcharge, or a 1-meter drop without catching fire, it is **legally barred** from being sold. After multiple EV fire incidents in 2022 (e.g., Ola, Okinawa, Pure EV scooters), the Government of India made these tests even tougher — much like adding a "blind-spot test" to a driving license after several real accidents.

## 1.4 Why India Needed Its Own Standards

| Global Standard (IEC/UN) | Indian Adaptation | Reason for Indian Variant |
|--------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| **IEC 62133** (Portable cells) | **AIS-038 / AIS-156** | Indian roads face higher ambient temperatures (up to **55 °C**), dust, monsoon humidity (>95% RH), and poor road quality (vibration/shock) |
| **UN R100** (Electric powertrain) | **AIS-156** | High-density urban traffic demands stricter thermal propagation safety |
| **ISO 12405** (HEV batteries) | **AIS-039** | Indian duty cycles include frequent stop-and-go, low-speed overloads |

**Key Bodies Involved:**
- **ARAI (Automotive Research Association of India)** — Type approval & testing
- **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** — Component-level standards (e.g., IS 14654)
- **MoRTH (Ministry of Road Transport and Highways)** — Legal notification
- **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy)** — Energy policy & FAME-II linkage
- **CPCB** — Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchy of Indian Battery Safety Regulations

The Indian regulatory framework operates in a **tiered, nested hierarchy** that mirrors the global UN-ECE framework but is tailored to Indian conditions.

**Tier 1 — Legal Authority:**
- Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989 (Rule 124 — Type Approval)
- The Motor Vehicles Act, 1988

**Tier 2 — Core Automotive Standards (AIS series):**
- AIS-156: Electric Power Train Vehicle Requirements
- AIS-038 (Rev 2): Lithium-ion Traction Battery Safety
- AIS-039: Lithium-ion Traction Battery Performance
- AIS-051: Battery Cell Performance
- AIS-004 (Part 3): General Safety Requirements

**Tier 3 — Component / Cell Standards (BIS):**
- IS 16046:2018 (Cells) — aligned with IEC 62133
- IS 14654:1999 (Lead-acid traction)
- IS 16270:2014 (Lithium cells for transport)
- IS 16893:2018 (Secondary lithium for EV)

**Tier 4 — Application & Recycling:**
- Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022 (MoEFCC)
- Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)
- PLI Scheme (ACC battery storage link)

## 2.2 Mandatory Safety Tests under AIS-156 / AIS-038

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every traction battery pack sold in India must pass the following mandatory tests. KTU examiners frequently ask students to "list and briefly explain" these tests — this is a 7-mark favorite.**

### A. Electrical Safety Tests
1. **Overcharge Test** — Charging at 2× rated current (or 1.2× upper limit voltage) until **200% SOC** is reached.
2. **Forced Discharge Test** — Discharging a fully charged cell in reverse polarity.
3. **External Short Circuit Test** — Applied short of <5 mΩ at 20 °C ± 5 °C.
4. **Over-Discharge Test** — Discharge to 0% then further to negative voltage.
5. **Insulation Resistance Test** — Minimum **100 Ω/V** of nominal pack voltage (per AIS-156).

### B. Mechanical Safety Tests
6. **Mechanical Shock Test** — 25 g to 50 g acceleration pulses.
7. **Vibration Test** — Sweep 7 Hz → 50 Hz → 7 Hz over 3 hours per axis.
8. **Mechanical Integrity (Crush) Test** — Crushing with a hemispherical rod (15 mm dia) at 5 mm/s.
9. **Drop Test (Pack level)** — Free drop from **1 meter** onto concrete (6 orientations).

### C. Environmental Safety Tests
10. **Thermal Abuse Test** — Heating at **5 °C/min** to **130 °C**; cell must not vent/explode.
11. **Thermal Shock Test** — Cycling between -40 °C and +85 °C.
12. **Temperature Cycling Test** — IEC cycle profile for 30 days.
13. **Altitude Simulation Test** — Pressure equivalent to 11.6 kPa (simulating 15,240 m altitude).

### D. Chemical / Thermal Propagation (AIS-156 Phase 2 & 3)
14. **Cell-to-Cell Thermal Propagation Test** — Inducing thermal runaway in one cell, the pack must show **no fire/explosion for 5 minutes** post-trigger (extended to **30 minutes** in Phase 3) and provide an **advance warning to occupants** at least **5 minutes** before any hazardous event.
15. **Fire Exposure Test** — Pack exposed to external flame at **800 °C ± 100 °C** for **10 minutes**.
16. **Gas Emission Test** — Vented gases measured for toxicity (HF, CO, etc.).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically grounded** in fundamental electrochemistry and thermal physics. Mastering these is essential for any numerical question KTU may ask on this module.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | $E_{pack} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ | Pack energy | Wh | Compliance with $\vert$ homologation power limit |
| 2 | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{I}{C_{rated}}$ | Discharge/charge current per capacity | dimensionless | Used in overcharge test definition |
| 3 | $E_{specific} = \dfrac{E_{pack}}{m_{pack}}$ | Gravimetric energy density | Wh/kg | AIS-039 minimum benchmark: $\geq 100$ Wh/kg |
| 4 | $E_{volumetric} = \dfrac{E_{pack}}{V_{pack}}$ | Volumetric energy density | Wh/L | Pack design constraint |
| 5 | $Q_{heat} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat absorbed | J | Thermal runaway threshold calculation |
| 6 | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{int} + R_{ext}}$ | Short-circuit current | A | Short-circuit test safety design |
| 7 | $R_{ins} \geq \dfrac{100 \times V_{nom}}{1000}$ | Minimum insulation resistance | Ω | AIS-156 compliance: e.g., 400V pack $\rightarrow$ $\geq$ **40 kΩ** |
| 8 | $t_{prop} = \dfrac{d_{cell}}{v_{prop}}$ | Time for thermal propagation | s | Used in 5/30-minute warning window |
| 9 | $SOC\% = \dfrac{Q_{discharged}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$ | State of charge | % | Overcharge test terminates at $\geq$ **120% SOC** |
| 10 | $P_{max} = V_{max} \times I_{max}$ | Max safe pack power | W | Crash-safety switch-off design |
| 11 | $SOH = \dfrac{C_{actual}}{C_{rated}} \times 100$ | State of health | % | End-of-life threshold: $SOH \leq 80\%$ |
| 12 | $IP_{rating} = IP_{XY}$ where $X$ = solids, $Y$ = water | Ingress protection | — | AIS-156 mandates $\geq$ **IP67** for traction packs |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why an engineer must internalize these standards:**
> - **Homologation** — Without AIS-156 certification, an EV cannot obtain a registration number.
> - **Insurance & Liability** — Fire claims are denied if the pack is non-compliant.
> - **Export Suitability** — Indian OEM exports (e.g., Tata Motors) require compliance with both AIS and UN R100; dual certification saves cost.
> - **FAME-II Subsidy** — Phased out, but **PM E-Drive** (2024) explicitly mandates AIS-156 Phase 3 compliance.
> - **Design Decision** — Trade-off between energy density (range) and safety (thermal stability) — e.g., LFP vs NMC chemistry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Insulation Resistance Calculation (KTU Typical 3-Mark Numerical)

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A 4-wheeled electric vehicle uses a high-voltage traction battery pack with a **nominal voltage of 400 V** and a maximum voltage of **420 V** during regenerative charging. As per AIS-156, calculate:
(a) The minimum allowable insulation resistance of the pack.
(b) Comment on what happens if the pack is exposed to a 12 V DC test voltage and the measured resistance is **80 kΩ** for the positive and **30 kΩ** for the negative busbar relative to the chassis.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Minimum Insulation Resistance**

According to AIS-156 (aligned with IEC 60664), the minimum insulation resistance per volt of working voltage is:

$$R_{ins,min} = 100 \, \Omega / V \times V_{max}$$

Substituting $V_{max} = 420$ V:

$$R_{ins,min} = 100 \times 420 = 42000 \, \Omega = 42 \, k\Omega$$

**Part (b) — Compliance Check**

The measured resistances are:
- $R_{pos} = 80 \, k\Omega > 42 \, k\Omega \;\;\Rightarrow$ **Positive busbar: COMPLIANT**
- $R_{neg} = 30 \, k\Omega < 42 \, k\Omega \;\;\Rightarrow$ **Negative busbar: NON-COMPLIANT**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly take the **nominal** voltage (400 V) instead of the **maximum** voltage (420 V). The standard strictly uses the **maximum working voltage** because insulation must withstand the worst-case stress (e.g., regen overvoltage). A common 1-mark deduction!

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Pack Energy & Specific Energy (KTU 7-Mark Numerical)

**Problem:**
A lithium-ion traction battery pack for an L-category (2-wheeler) EV has the following specifications:
- **Configuration:** 17S4P (17 cells in series, 4 cells in parallel)
- **Cell nominal voltage:** 3.65 V
- **Cell capacity:** 2.5 Ah
- **Cell mass:** 45 g
- **Total pack mass (with housing, BMS, cooling):** 4.2 kg

Calculate:
(a) Nominal pack voltage
(b) Pack capacity
(c) Pack energy in Wh
(d) Gravimetric energy density (Wh/kg)
(e) Is the pack compliant with AIS-039 minimum benchmark of **100 Wh/kg** for L-category vehicles?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a) — Nominal Pack Voltage**

Cells in series add their voltages:

$$V_{pack} = n_{series} \times V_{cell} = 17 \times 3.65$$

$$V_{pack} = 62.05 \, V$$

**Step (b) — Pack Capacity**

Cells in parallel add their capacities:

$$C_{pack} = n_{parallel} \times C_{cell} = 4 \times 2.5$$

$$C_{pack} = 10 \, Ah$$

**Step (c) — Pack Energy**

$$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times C_{pack} = 62.05 \times 10$$

$$E_{pack} = 620.5 \, Wh$$

**Step (d) — Gravimetric Energy Density**

$$E_{specific} = \frac{E_{pack}}{m_{pack}} = \frac{620.5}{4.2}$$

$$E_{specific} = 147.74 \, Wh/kg$$

**Step (e) — Compliance Check**

$$147.74 \, Wh/kg \;\; \geq \;\; 100 \, Wh/kg \;\;\Rightarrow \;\;\textbf{COMPLIANT}$$

The pack has a **47.74 Wh/kg** margin above the AIS-039 threshold, indicating good design headroom.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Thermal Propagation Time (Advanced)

**Problem:**
A 14S battery module in a 400 V pack has cells spaced 2 mm apart. During a thermal runaway event, the propagation velocity from one cell to the next is experimentally measured as **$v_{prop} = 0.5 \, mm/s$** (typical for LFP chemistry). The occupant compartment is **120 cm** from the module. As per AIS-156 Phase 3, the BMS must provide a **warning at least 5 minutes (300 s)** before any hazardous event reaches the cabin.

Calculate:
(a) Time for thermal propagation to traverse one cell-to-cell gap
(b) Time for propagation to travel 120 cm
(c) Is the BMS warning system design adequate?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a) — Time for one cell-to-cell propagation**

$$t_{gap} = \frac{d_{cell}}{v_{prop}} = \frac{2 \, mm}{0.5 \, mm/s} = 4 \, s$$

**Step (b) — Time for propagation to reach cabin**

Convert cabin distance to mm: $120 \, cm = 1200 \, mm$

$$t_{cabin} = \frac{L_{cabin}}{v_{prop}} = \frac{1200 \, mm}{0.5 \, mm/s} = 2400 \, s$$

$$t_{cabin} = 40 \, \text{minutes}$$

**Step (c) — Adequacy Check**

$$\text{Available time} = 40 \, min = 2400 \, s$$
$$\text{Required minimum warning time} = 5 \, min = 300 \, s$$
$$2400 \, s \;\; \geq \;\; 300 \, s \;\;\Rightarrow \;\;\textbf{ADEQUATE}$$

The BMS has **35 minutes of margin** beyond the standard's minimum, leaving ample time for:
- Occupant evacuation
- Emergency shutdown of the high-voltage relay
- Activation of thermal suppression system (if fitted)

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Standard Selection by Vehicle Category

| Vehicle Category | Applicable Standard | Min IP Rating | Min Specific Energy | Thermal Propagation Window |
|------------------|---------------------|---------------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| L1 (e-bike, <250 W) | AIS-156 Phase 1 | IP65 | 80 Wh/kg | 5 min |
| L2–L7 (e-scooter/motorcycle) | AIS-156 Phase 2 / 3 | IP67 | 100 Wh/kg | 30 min |
| M1 (passenger car) | AIS-156 Phase 3 + AIS-038 | IP67 | 120 Wh/kg | 30 min |
| N1, N2 (light/heavy commercial) | AIS-156 Phase 3 + AIS-038 + UN R100 | IP67 | 110 Wh/kg | 30 min |
| Bus (M3) | AIS-156 + AIS-052 | IP68 | 90 Wh/kg | 30 min + fire suppression |

## 3.5 KTU Conceptual Matrix — Cause-Effect of Non-Compliance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is a **guaranteed KTU Part-A (3-mark)** format. Examiners love "explain the consequences of X" type questions.

| Safety Test Failed | Real-World Consequence | Mitigation in Standard |
|-------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| Overcharge | Electrolyte decomposition → gas venting → fire | BMS with redundant voltage sensors |
| Nail penetration | Internal short → thermal runaway | Cell-level fuse, ceramic separator |
| Vibration | Loose connections → arc fault → ignition | Welding + locking connectors (IP67) |
| Thermal shock | Seal failure → moisture ingress → corrosion | IP67 enclosure + Gore-Tex vent |
| Short circuit | $I_{sc}$ > 10× rated → contactor welding | Pyro-fuse (one-time thermal fuse) |
| Drop test | Cell deformation → internal short | Reinforced pack housing (steel/aluminum) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Indian Battery Safety Standards — Hierarchical Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block represents the **regulatory hierarchy** governing battery safety in India, from the supreme legal authority down to the application level.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Constitution of India] --> A2[Motor Vehicles Act 1988]
    A2 --> A3[CMVR 1989 Rule 124 Type Approval]
    A3 --> B1[AIS 156 Electric Powertrain Vehicle]
    A3 --> B2[AIS 038 Rev 2 Li ion Traction Battery]
    A3 --> B3[AIS 039 Li ion Traction Pack Performance]
    A3 --> B4[AIS 004 Part 3 General Safety]
    A1 --> C1[Electricity Act 2003]
    C1 --> C2[CEA Safety Regulations]
    A1 --> D1[Environment Protection Act 1986]
    D1 --> D2[Battery Waste Management Rules 2022]
    D2 --> D3[Extended Producer Responsibility EPR]
    B1 --> E1[AIS 156 Phase 3 2024 Amendment]
    E1 --> E2[Cell to Cell Thermal Propagation 30 min]
    E1 --> E3[Advance Warning System 5 min]
    E1 --> E4[Stricter BMS Functional Safety ASIL C]
    B2 --> F1[Overcharge and Short Circuit Tests]
    B2 --> F2[Crush Nail Penetration Fire Exposure]
    B3 --> F3[Cycle Life 2000 cycles at 80 percent DOD]
    B3 --> F4[Specific Energy 100 Wh per kg minimum]
    D3 --> G1[Collection Target 70 percent by 2027]
    D3 --> G2[Recycling through CPCB registered units]
```

## 4.2 AIS-156 Compliance Test Flow — Functional Architecture

The Mermaid block below maps the **test sequence** a battery pack must undergo for AIS-156 homologation. It is rendered as a block-level functional architecture, not a physical drawing, to ensure Mermaid compatibility.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Step 1 Document Review] --> S2[Step 2 Visual Inspection]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Electrical Tests]
    S2 --> S4[Step 4 Mechanical Tests]
    S2 --> S5[Step 5 Environmental Tests]
    S3 --> T1[Overcharge Test]
    S3 --> T2[Short Circuit Test]
    S3 --> T3[Insulation Resistance Test]
    S4 --> T4[Vibration Test]
    S4 --> T5[Mechanical Shock Test]
    S4 --> T6[Drop Test 1 m]
    S4 --> T7[Crush Test]
    S5 --> T8[Thermal Abuse 130 C]
    S5 --> T9[Thermal Shock minus 40 to plus 85 C]
    S5 --> T10[Altitude Simulation 11.6 kPa]
    S3 --> T11[Thermal Propagation Cell to Cell]
    S3 --> T12[Fire Exposure 800 C for 10 min]
    T1 --> P1{Pass All Tests}
    T2 --> P1
    T3 --> P1
    T4 --> P1
    T5 --> P1
    T6 --> P1
    T7 --> P1
    T8 --> P1
    T9 --> P1
    T10 --> P1
    T11 --> P1
    T12 --> P1
    P1 -- Yes --> R1[Issue Type Approval Certificate]
    P1 -- No --> R2[Rejection + Design Modification]
```

## 4.3 End-to-End Safety Architecture of an EV Battery Pack (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TB
    CELL1[Cell 1] --> MOD1[Module 1 with BMS Slave]
    CELL2[Cell 2] --> MOD1
    CELL3[Cell n] --> MOD1
    MOD1 --> BMS[Master Battery Management System]
    MOD2[Module 2] --> BMS
    MOD3[Module m] --> BMS
    BMS --> CS1[Cell Voltage Monitoring]
    BMS --> CS2[Cell Temperature Monitoring]
    BMS --> CS3[Current Sensing Hall Effect]
    BMS --> CS4[Insulation Monitoring]

    CS2 --> CTL1
    CS3 --> CTL1
    CS4 --> CTL1
    CTL1 --> CR1[Main Positive Contactor]
    CTL1 --> CR2[Main Negative Contactor]
    CTL1 --> CR3[Precharge Contactor]

    CAN1 --> VCU[Vehicle Control Unit]
    CR1 --> INV[Traction Inverter and Motor]

    INV --> WHL[Drive Wheels]
```
