| $H_{ci}$ (kA/m) | 1200–2000 | 700–1600 | 800–2700 | Higher $H_{ci}$ → thermal stability |
| $T_c$ (°C) | 720 | 800–850 | 310–400 | SmCo wins in hot environments |
| $\alpha_{Br}$ (%/°C) | -0.04 | -0.03 | -0.10 to -0.13 | SmCo is ~3× more temperature stable |
| $\beta_{Hci}$ (%/°C) | -0.25 | -0.20 | -0.45 to -0.60 | SmCo resists demagnetization better |
| Density (g/cm³) | 8.4 | 8.4 | 7.5 | NdFeB lighter per volume |
| Corrosion resistance | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (needs coating) | SmCo wins; NdFeB needs Ni/epoxy |
| Cost (relative) | Very high | Very high | Moderate (but Dy-critical) | NdFeB cheaper but Dy supply risky |
| Operating T (°C) | up to 250 | up to 350 | up to 150 (standard), 200 (UH grade) | EV motors stay cool → NdFeB chosen |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flow — Permanent Magnet Families

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PERMANENT MAGNET MATERIALS] --> B[Conventional]
    A --> C[Rare Earth]
    A --> D[Bonded]
    B --> B1[Alnico]
    B --> B2[Hard Ferrite SrO.6Fe2O3]
    C --> C1[SmCo Family]
    C --> C2[NdFeB Family]
    C1 --> C1a[SmCo5 1:5 type 1960s]
    C1 --> C1b[Sm2Co17 2:17 type 1970s]
    C2 --> C2a[Nd2Fe14B Sintered 1984]
    C2 --> C2b[NdFeB Bonded Injection Molded]
    D --> D1[NdFeB + Nylon / PPS binder]
    D --> D2[SmCo + Epoxy binder]
```

### 4.2 Magnet Selection Decision Flow for EV Traction Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[START: Define EV Specs] --> Q1{Operating Temperature exceeds 150 C?}
    Q1 -- YES --> SMCO[Select Sm2Co17 or SmCo5]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Maximum Torque Density required?}
    Q2 -- YES --> NDFEB[Select NdFeB Grade N48 or higher]
    Q2 -- NO --> FERR[Consider Ferrite if cost-critical]
    SMCO --> Q3{Dy free required?}
    NDFEB --> Q3{Dy free required?}
    Q3 -- YES --> DYF[Dy free NdFeB e.g. grain boundary diffused]
    Q3 -- NO --> DYD[Standard Dy doped NdFeB]
    SMCO --> END1[Final Validation: B H curve at Tmax]
    NDFEB --> END1
    FERR --> END1
    DYF --> END1
    DYD --> END1
```

### 4.3 Demagnetization Curve (J-H and B-H) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SC[Sm2Co17 at 25 C]
        SBr[B_r 1.10 T] --> SHci[H_ci 1600 kA/m]
        SHci --> SBHmax[BH_max 30 MGOe]
    end
    subgraph NC[NdFeB N48SH at 25 C]
        NBr[B_r 1.39 T] --> NHci[H_ci 1590 kA/m]
        NHci --> NBHmax[BH_max 45 MGOe]
    end
    subgraph SC2[Sm2Co17 at 200 C]
        SBr2[B_r 1.02 T] --> SHci2[H_ci 1300 kA/m]
        SHci2 --> SBHmax2[BH_max 26 MGOe]
    end
    subgraph NC2[NdFeB N48SH at 200 C]
        NBr2[B_r 1.16 T] --> NHci2[H_ci 800 kA/m]
        NHci2 --> NBHmax2[BH_max drops sharply]
    end
    style SC2 fill:#fff4cc
    style NC2 fill:#ffd6d6
    style SC fill:#e6f7ff
    style NC fill:#e6ffe6
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The amber and red cells highlight that at 200 °C, NdFeB's energy product drops dramatically due to its low $T_c$ and steep $\beta_{Hci}$, while SmCo retains its strength — a key reason aerospace/military EVs prefer SmCo.

### 4.4 Manufacturing Process Block Flow — Sintered NdFeB

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Raw Materials Nd Pr Fe B] --> M1[Vacuum Induction Melting]
    M1 --> H1[Hydrogen Decrepitation]
    H1 --> J1[Jet Milling to 3-5 micron powder]
    J1 --> P1[Pressing in Magnetic Field align grains]
    P1 --> S1[Sintering 1000-1100 C]
    S1 --> A1[Annealing 500-900 C]
    A1 --> M2[Machining to Final Shape]
    M2 --> C1[Surface Coating Ni Cu Ni or Epoxy]
    C1 --> T1[Magnetization 3-5 T Pulse]
    T1 --> Q1[QC Inspection B_r H_ci Flux Mapping]
    Q1 --> O1[Ship to EV Motor Assembly]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*List any three advantages of NdFeB magnets over SmCo magnets for EV traction motor application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Higher energy product** — NdFeB delivers 30–52 MGOe versus 16–32 MGOe for SmCo, enabling smaller and lighter motors. (1 mark)
2. **Lower density** — 7.5 g/cm³ versus 8.4 g/cm³, giving higher torque-to-weight ratio, critical for vehicle range. (1 mark)
3. **Lower raw material cost** (excluding Dy content) — Sm and Co are scarcer and more expensive than Nd and Fe. (1 mark)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Why are SmCo magnets preferred over NdFeB magnets in aerospace and high-temperature EV applications?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Higher Curie temperature** (~800–850 °C vs. 310–400 °C) — SmCo retains magnetization far above operating range. (1 mark)
2. **Lower temperature coefficients** ($\alpha_{Br} \approx -0.03\%/°C$ vs. $-0.10\%/°C$) — flux remains stable at elevated temperatures. (1 mark)
3. **Superior corrosion and oxidation resistance** — does not require Ni/epoxy coating, making it reliable in extreme environments. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — SmCo Magnet Focus

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Series A]**
**(a)** With the aid of a B-H hysteresis loop, explain the second-quadrant demagnetization curve and mark the points $B_r$, $H_{cb}$, $H_{ci}$ and the **knee of the curve**. State the significance of each point in EV motor design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the magnetic and thermal properties of **SmCo₅ (1:5)** and **Sm₂Co₁₇ (2:17)** magnet types. Justify which one is preferred for a **high-speed traction motor** operating at 180 °C ambient. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

The **B-H hysteresis loop** plots flux density $B$ against applied magnetic field $H$. The portion lying in the second quadrant ($H < 0$, $B > 0$) is the **demagnetization curve**, which describes how a magnet responds to an opposing field.

**Key Points (7 marks distributed as below):**

- **Remanence $B_r$ (1 mark):** The flux density remaining in the magnet when the applied field is reduced to zero. Represents the open-circuit strength. For a 1.2 T NdFeB in a motor, $B_r$ sets the air-gap flux and hence the back-EMF.
- **Normal Coercivity $H_{cb}$ (1 mark):** The reverse field required to reduce $B$ to zero. Useful for B-based design formulas.
- **Intrinsic Coercivity $H_{ci}$ (1 mark):** The reverse field required to reduce the **magnetization $J$** to zero. This is the true measure of the magnet's resistance to demagnetization.
- **Knee of the curve (2 marks):** The point on the J-H curve where the slope changes from steep (linear reversible) to gradual (curved irreversible). If the operating point is forced **below the knee** (typically below $0.8 \times B_r$), the magnet loses flux permanently. In EV PMSMs, **short-circuit currents and demagnetizing $d$-axis currents** at high temperature must never drive the rotor flux past the knee.
- **Maximum Energy Product $(BH)_{max}$ (2 marks):** The largest rectangle that fits inside the second quadrant; equals the magnet's volumetric energy density and determines the minimum magnet volume required for a given motor rating.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students confuse $H_{cb}$ with $H_{ci}$. Remember: $H_{cb}$ is defined on the **B-H curve** (where $B = 0$), while $H_{ci}$ is defined on the **J-H (intrinsic) curve** (where $J = 0$). For SmCo and NdFeB grades, $H_{ci}$ is typically **1.5× to 3× larger** than $H_{cb}$. Loss of 2 marks if confused.

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | SmCo₅ (1:5) | Sm₂Co₁₇ (2:17) |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| $(BH)_{max}$ | 16–24 MGOe | 24–32 MGOe |
| $B_r$ | 0.85–1.05 T | 1.0–1.15 T |
| $H_{ci}$ | 1200–2000 kA/m | 700–1600 kA/m |
| $T_c$ | ~720 °C | ~800–850 °C |
| $\alpha_{Br}$ | -0.04 %/°C | -0.03 %/°C |
| Best use | Low to moderate T | **High T + high $B_r$** |

**Justification for 180 °C ambient (3 marks):**

For a high-speed traction motor operating at 180 °C ambient (e.g., a motor mounted near an internal combustion engine in a hybrid, or an aerospace EV), **Sm₂Co₁₇ is preferred** because:

1. **Higher $B_r$ (1.0–1.15 T vs. 0.85–1.05 T)** allows smaller magnet volume for the same air-gap flux. (1 mark)
2. **Higher Curie temperature (~850 °C)** provides a much larger thermal safety margin above 180 °C. (1 mark)
3. **Higher $(BH)_{max}$** (up to 32 MGOe) gives the most compact rotor and lowest rotor inertia — essential for high-speed dynamic response. (1 mark)

The lower $H_{ci}$ of Sm₂Co₁₇ is acceptable because both 1:5 and 2:17 types easily exceed the demagnetization demands at 180 °C (their knee voltages are well above what the stator current can produce at that temperature).

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — NdFeB Magnet Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**(a)** Explain the crystal structure and composition of **Nd₂Fe₁₄B** magnets. Discuss why **Dysprosium (Dy)** and **Terbium (Tb)** are added to NdFeB magnets used in EV motors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PMSM for an EV uses **N42UH NdFeB magnets** with $B_r = 1.30$ T, $H_{ci} = 1990$ kA/m at 20 °C, $\alpha_{Br} = -0.10\,\%/°C$, $\beta_{Hci} = -0.55\,\%/°C$. Calculate the remanence and intrinsic coercivity at the maximum operating temperature of **180 °C**. Comment on the suitability of this magnet grade for the application. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Crystal Structure and Composition (3 marks):**
Nd₂Fe₁₄B has a **tetragonal crystal structure** (space group P4₂/mnm) with lattice parameters $a \approx 0.880$ nm and $c \approx 1.221$ nm. The unit cell contains 68 atoms: 8 Nd, 56 Fe, and 4 B. The Nd atoms occupy specific sites that create a large magnetocrystalline anisotropy along the **c-axis**, locking the magnetization direction. Fe atoms carry the dominant magnetic moment, while B stabilizes the structure. The compound is manufactured via powder metallurgy (sintering).

**Role of Dysprosium (Dy) and Terbium (Tb) (4 marks):**

1. **Enhance $H_{ci}$ at high temperature (2 marks):** Dy and Tb substitute Nd in the grain boundary phase and form a thin **(Nd,Dy)₂Fe₁₄B shell** with very high anisotropy field ($H_A \approx 12{,}000$ kA/m for Dy₂Fe₁₄B versus $H_A \approx 6000$ kA/m for Nd₂Fe₁₄B). This shell resists magnetization reversal at high T, dramatically raising $H_{ci}$.
2. **Compensate for $\beta_{Hci}$ losses (1 mark):** Since $H_{ci}$ falls by ~0.55 %/°C in pure NdFeB, doping with 4–6 wt% Dy reduces the effective temperature coefficient to ~-0.45 %/°C, preserving the safety margin against the knee of the demagnetization curve.
3. **Strategic concern (1 mark):** Dy and Tb are scarcer than Nd, so heavy doping increases cost and supply risk — a major reason research focuses on **grain boundary diffusion** techniques that use Dy more efficiently (less than 1 wt%).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often state that Dy **increases $B_r$** — this is **incorrect**. Dy slightly **reduces** $B_r$ (because Dy-Fe has lower saturation moment than Nd-Fe) but **increases $H_{ci}$**. Marks deducted if $B_r$ is claimed to rise with Dy addition.

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Temperature difference (1 mark):**
$$\Delta T = 180 - 20 = 160 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Remanence at 180 °C (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_r(180) &= B_r(20) \left[1 + \alpha_{Br} \cdot \Delta T\right] \\
         &= 1.30 \times \left[1 + (-0.0010)(160)\right] \\
         &= 1.30 \times 0.84 \\
         &= 1.092 \text{ T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Intrinsic Coercivity at 180 °C (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{ci}(180) &= H_{ci}(20) \left[1 + \beta_{Hci} \cdot \Delta T\right] \\
            &= 1990 \times \left[1 + (-0.0055)(160)\right] \\
            &= 1990 \times (1 - 0.88) \\
            &= 1990 \times 0.12 \\
            &= 238.8 \text{ kA/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Suitability (2 marks):**

The N42UH (U = Ultra High, H = High $H_{ci}$) grade maintains $H_{ci} \approx 239$ kA/m at 180 °C. For a typical surface-mounted PMSM with a stator $d$-axis demagnetizing field $H_d \leq 200$ kA/m at the magnet surface during a 3-phase short-circuit at 180 °C, the magnet remains **above the knee** of the J-H curve and is therefore **suitable**. However, the steep $\beta_{Hci}$ of -0.55 %/°C means that at 200 °C, $H_{ci}$ would drop below 160 kA/m, making the design unsafe — alternative would be **N40AH** or **N38EH** grade with better thermal tolerance.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SmCo & NdFeB Magnets**

- **NdFeB = Highest Energy Product** (up to 52 MGOe), $B_r$ up to 1.45 T — king of EV traction motors.
- **SmCo = Highest Temperature Stability** — $T_c$ 720–850 °C, used in aerospace, military, >150 °C apps.
- **Curie temperature order:** Alnico (~800 °C) > Sm₂Co₁₇ (~850 °C) > SmCo₅ (~720 °C) > NdFeB (~310–400 °C).
- **Energy product ideal limit:** $(BH)_{max,ideal} = B_r^2 / (4\mu_0)$ — NdFeB approaches this theoretical ceiling.
- **Dysprosium doping** raises $H_{ci}$ of NdFeB at the cost of $B_r$ and $$$; modern research focuses on grain-boundary diffusion.
- **Coercivity falls 5× faster than remanence** with temperature — this is the **#1 cause** of EV magnet failures.
- **B-H curve knee** must not be crossed under any operational $d$-axis current, even at peak hot-spot temperature.
- **1 MGOe = 7.958 kJ/m³** — always convert units in KTU numerical problems.
- **NdFeB corrosion:** requires Ni-Cu-Ni, Zn, or epoxy coating; SmCo needs no protective layer.
- **Density:** NdFeB 7.5 g/cm³ < SmCo 8.4 g/cm³ → NdFeB preferred for weight-critical EV applications.
- **Permeance coefficient $P_c$** of the magnetic circuit sets the operating point — keep it > 4 to stay away from the knee.
- **Anisotropy field $H_A = 2K_1/(\mu_0 M_s)$** — for thermal stability, $H_{ci} \geq H_A$ at max operating temperature.
- **Modern trends:** Dy-free NdFeB, recycled magnets, and SmFeN as a future low-cost high-T alternative to SmCo.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PM Motors - BLDC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** PM Motors - BLDC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor** is a synchronous electric machine in which the rotor carries high-energy **permanent magnets (PMs)** (typically Neodymium-Iron-Boron or Samarium-Cobalt) and the stator carries a polyphase (usually three-phase) distributed armature winding. Electronic commutation is performed by an external solid-state inverter driven by rotor-position feedback (Hall sensors or sensorless back-EMF detection), replacing the mechanical brush-commutator assembly of a conventional DC motor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The defining electrical signature of a BLDC motor is its **trapezoidal back-EMF waveform** combined with **rectangular (square-wave) stator currents**. This combination produces theoretically ripple-free DC output torque — the underlying reason it is labelled a "DC" machine despite its AC stator currents.

**Standard KTU Definitions to Memorise:**

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| Synchronous Motor | Rotor rotates at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$ |
| Permanent Magnet (PM) Motor | Flux produced by rotor-mounted magnets, not field windings |
| Brushless DC (BLDC) | PM synchronous motor with trapezoidal back-EMF |
| PMSM | PM synchronous motor with sinusoidal back-EMF |
| Electronic Commutation | Switching of stator phases based on rotor position |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a **revolving door with magnets instead of bars**:

A conventional brushed DC motor works like a person standing in the centre of a revolving door and pushing each panel in turn — the "brushes" are his hands making physical contact to give the push. A BLDC motor removes the human: the magnets are on the revolving door itself (the rotor), and an **electronic controller plays the role of the hands**, deciding exactly *which* panel to push, *when*, and *for how long*. The controller knows the door's position from tiny **Hall-effect sensors** (like compass needles), so the push is always perfectly timed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** A BLDC motor is to a brushed DC motor what an automatic sliding door is to a hand-pulled one — same function (movement), but with the friction-causing contact replaced by seamless, programmable electronics.

## 1.3 Why BLDC Motors Dominate Modern Electric Vehicles

- **No brush wear** → maintenance-free, long life (>20,000 hours).
- **High power density** (typically **3–5 kW/kg**) — critical for vehicle packaging.
- **High efficiency** (**85–95 %**) across wide speed range.
- **Excellent thermal performance** — rotor losses are negligible (only stator I²R losses).
- **Precise speed/torque control** with closed-loop electronic commutation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever an examiner asks "Why BLDC in EVs?", always mention these four pillars: (1) High efficiency, (2) High power density, (3) Regenerative braking capability, (4) No brush maintenance. Add an example such as the **Tata Nexon EV** or **Tesla Model 3** rear drive unit.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical EV Value |
|---|---|
| Specific Power | **3–5 kW/kg** |
| Peak Efficiency | **95–97 %** |
| Operating Speed | **4,000–10,000 rpm** |
| Number of Poles (P) | **4, 6, 8 (even)** |
| Magnet Material | **NdFeB (Neodymium)** |
| Back-EMF Constant $K_e$ | **0.05–0.15 V·s/rad** |
| Torque Constant $K_t$ | **0.05–0.15 N·m/A** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Constructional Architecture

### 2.1.1 Stator (Stationary Part)
- Laminated silicon-steel core with **slotted** (or slotless for low cogging) construction.
- Three-phase distributed winding — either **wye (Y) connected** with the neutral point un-accessed, or delta-connected.
- Each phase winding is displaced by **120° electrical** in space.

### 2.1.2 Rotor (Rotating Part)
- Surface-mounted Permanent Magnets (SPM) — most common in BLDC.
- Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM) — for field-weakening operation at high speed.
- Magnet pole pairs $p = P/2$ where $P$ = total number of poles.

### 2.1.3 Position Sensors
- Three **Hall-effect sensors** mounted on the stator at 60° (electrical) intervals.
- Provide digital high/low signals identifying rotor angular position.
- Alternatively, **sensorless** schemes use zero-crossing of back-EMF.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Why It Works

The BLDC motor operates on the **Lorentz Force Law**: a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force $F = BIL$.

**Step-by-step operating logic:**
1. The controller energises two of the three stator phases (the third is floated).
2. The energized phases create a magnetic field vector along a specific direction.
3. The PM rotor tries to align its own field with the stator field → produces torque.
4. As the rotor moves to alignment, Hall sensors detect new position → controller switches to the next phase pair.
5. Sequence repeats at high frequency → continuous rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commutation Sequence for 3-Phase BLDC:** The six-step commutation sequence (also called *six-state* or *120° conduction*) follows the pattern: **AB → AC → BC → BA → CA → CB → AB**. Each step advances the stator field vector by **60° electrical**.

## 2.3 Trapezoidal Back-EMF — The Defining Characteristic

Unlike PMSM (sinusoidal), the BLDC has a **trapezoidal back-EMF waveform**. Each phase winding sees a flat-topped voltage profile of 120° electrical, zero for 60°, and negative flat-topped for another 120°.

**Why trapezoidal?**
- Concentrated (non-overlapping) stator windings produce non-sinusoidal flux linkage.
- Surface-mounted magnets give nearly uniform air-gap flux under the pole arc.

## 2.4 Torque and Speed Relationships — The Physics

### 2.4.1 Voltage Equation (per phase, simplified)
For any one energized phase:
$$V_s = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + e_b$$

where $e_b = K_e \, \omega_m$ is the back-EMF, $R_a$ and $L_a$ are armature resistance and inductance.

### 2.4.2 Torque Equation
For a BLDC with two phases conducting simultaneously at current $I_a$:
$$T_e = 2 \, K_t \, I_a$$

because the constant $K_t$ is defined *per phase*, and two phases carry current at any instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual "Why":** The factor of 2 appears because at any commutation step, **exactly two of the three phases** carry equal and torque-producing currents, while the third floats. The total machine torque is the algebraic sum of the two active phases.

### 2.4.3 Power Equation
$$P_m = T_e \, \omega_m = e_b \, I_a = K_e \, \omega_m \, I_a$$

### 2.4.4 Speed Equation (from voltage equation, steady-state, ignoring inductance)
$$\omega_m = \frac{V_s - I_a R_a}{K_e}$$

At no-load ($I_a = 0$):
$$\omega_{no\text{-}load} = \frac{V_s}{K_e}$$

This is the **open-circuit speed** — purely determined by supply voltage and the motor's $K_e$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Mandate:** All formulas below are examinable. Memorise with units and the conditions under which each applies.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | rpm |
| 2 | $\omega_m = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60}$ | Mechanical angular speed | rad/s |
| 3 | $\theta_e = \dfrac{P}{2} \, \theta_m$ | Electrical to mechanical angle | rad |
| 4 | $e_b = K_e \, \omega_m$ | Back-EMF | V |
| 5 | $V_s = I_a R_a + L_a \dfrac{dI_a}{dt} + e_b$ | Per-phase voltage equation | V |
| 6 | $T_e = K_t \, I_a$ | Torque produced (single-phase equivalent) | N·m |
| 7 | $T_e = 2 \, K_t \, I_a$ | Torque produced (two-phase conducting) | N·m |
| 8 | $K_e = K_t$ (in SI) | Torque–EMF constant equality | V·s/rad = N·m/A |
| 9 | $P_m = T_e \, \omega_m$ | Mechanical output power | W |
| 10 | $P_e = V_s I_a$ | Electrical input power | W |
| 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_m}{P_e} = 1 - \dfrac{I_a R_a}{V_s}$ | Efficiency | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_s - I_a R_a}{K_e}$ | Speed under load | rad/s |
| 13 | $P_{mech, loss} = T_{loss} \, \omega_m$ | Mechanical loss power | W |
| 14 | $E_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \, V_{ph}$ | Line-to-line EMF (for balanced star) | V |
| 15 | $f_{comm} = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\omega_m}{2\pi}$ | Commutation frequency per phase | Hz |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students frequently write $K_e$ in rpm/Volt or $K_t$ in oz·in/A. Always use **SI units** ($K_e$ in V·s/rad and $K_t$ in N·m/A) to satisfy KTU's standardised valuation scheme. The equality $K_e = K_t$ in SI is a *direct consequence of energy conservation* — examiners love testing this!

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of BLDC |
|---|---|
| Two-wheeler EVs (Ola, Ather) | Hub-mounted BLDC, peak torque 8–20 N·m |
| Four-wheeler traction (Tata Nexon, Hyundai Kona) | Interior PM BLDC, 100–150 kW |
| Power steering | BLDC actuator for steer-by-wire |
| HVAC blowers | Small BLDC for cabin ventilation |
| Industrial servo drives | High-precision BLDC for CNC spindles |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Back-EMF Equation for a BLDC Motor

### Step 1 — Starting from Faraday's Law
The back-EMF induced in a single conductor cutting a magnetic field is given by:
$$e = B \, l \, v$$

where $B$ is flux density, $l$ is conductor length, $v$ is relative velocity.

### Step 2 — Conductor Velocity in terms of Rotor Speed
For a rotor of radius $r$ rotating at angular speed $\omega_m$:
$$v = r \, \omega_m$$

### Step 3 — Substitute
$$e = B \, l \, r \, \omega_m$$

### Step 4 — Sum over $Z$ Conductors per Phase
With $Z$ series conductors per phase winding, total phase EMF:
$$E_{ph} = Z \, B \, l \, r \, \omega_m$$

### Step 5 — Introduce the Flux per Pole
The flux per pole $\Phi = B \cdot A_p = B \cdot \pi r l / p$ (for $p$ pole pairs), so $B \, l \, r = \dfrac{p}{\pi} \, \Phi \, 2$ — careful reformulation gives the standard form:

$$\boxed{E_{ph} = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A}}$$

where:
- $P$ = number of poles
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)
- $N$ = speed (rpm)
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $A$ = number of parallel paths (= 1 for wave, = P for lap)

In the standard SI compact form (defining $K_e$):

$$E_b = K_e \, \Phi \, \omega_m \quad \text{or simply} \quad E_b = K_e \, \omega_m \;\;(\Phi \text{ const for PM})$$

### Step 6 — Magnitude Justification
For surface-mounted NdFeB magnets, $\Phi$ is constant (no field weakening below base speed), so $E_b$ varies **linearly** with $\omega_m$. This linearity is fundamental to the BLDC's predictable torque-speed curve.

## 3.2 Derivation: Torque Equation from Energy Conservation

### Step 1 — Power Equivalence
Mechanical output power must equal electrical air-gap power (in steady state, ignoring losses):
$$P_{mech} = P_{elec, gap}$$

$$T_e \, \omega_m = E_b \, I_a$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $E_b = K_e \, \omega_m$
$$T_e \, \omega_m = K_e \, \omega_m \, I_a$$

### Step 3 — Cancel $\omega_m$ (assuming non-zero speed)
$$\boxed{T_e = K_e \, I_a = K_t \, I_a}$$

This elegant result shows **torque is directly proportional to armature current** in a BLDC — a linear, DC-like characteristic, hence the name "Brushless DC".

### Step 4 — Account for Two-Phase Conduction
In a 3-phase BLDC, two phases carry current at any time:
$$T_e = 2 \, K_t \, I_a$$

where $I_a$ is the per-phase (or DC-link) current.

## 3.3 Derivation: Speed–Torque Characteristic

### Step 1 — Substitute voltage equation in steady state ($dI/dt = 0$)
$$V_s = I_a R_a + K_e \, \omega_m$$

### Step 2 — Express $I_a$ from torque equation
$$I_a = \frac{T_e}{K_t}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute
$$V_s = \frac{T_e}{K_t} \, R_a + K_e \, \omega_m$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $\omega_m$
$$\boxed{\omega_m = \frac{V_s}{K_e} - \frac{R_a}{K_e K_t} \, T_e}$$

This is the **linear drop-line** characteristic of a BLDC: speed decreases linearly with torque, with slope $\dfrac{R_a}{K_e K_t}$ and intercept $\dfrac{V_s}{K_e}$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Six-Step Commutation Sequence

### Step 1 — Electrical Cycle Length
One full mechanical revolution = $P/2$ electrical cycles. Each electrical cycle = **360° electrical** = **6 commutation steps of 60° each**.

### Step 2 — Phase Energisation Pattern

| Step | Electrical Angle | Energised Phases | Current Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0° – 60° | A → B | A: +ve, B: –ve |
| 2 | 60° – 120° | A → C | A: +ve, C: –ve |
| 3 | 120° – 180° | B → C | B: +ve, C: –ve |
| 4 | 180° – 240° | B → A | B: +ve, A: –ve |
| 5 | 240° – 300° | C → A | C: +ve, A: –ve |
| 6 | 300° – 360° | C → B | C: +ve, B: –ve |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Each phase conducts for **120° electrical** (positive direction) and **120° electrical** (negative direction), floating for the remaining **120° electrical** in the centre.

### Step 3 — Hall Sensor Truth Table

| Hall A | Hall B | Hall C | Active Phases |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | A → B |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | A → C |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | B → C |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | B → A |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | C → A |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | C → B |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Speed, Torque & Power at Key Operating Points

| Operating Point | Speed $\omega_m$ | Torque $T_e$ | Power $P$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| No-load | $\dfrac{V_s}{K_e}$ (max) | 0 | 0 |
| Rated | $\omega_{rated}$ | $T_{rated}$ | $P_{rated}$ |
| Base speed | $\omega_{base}$ (start of field-weakening) | $T_{max}$ | $P_{peak}$ |
| Maximum | $\dfrac{V_s}{K_e}$ | 0 | 0 |
| Stall | 0 | $T_{stall} = \dfrac{V_s K_t}{R_a}$ (max) | 0 |
| Max Power | $\dfrac{V_s}{2 K_e}$ | $\dfrac{T_{stall}}{2}$ | $\dfrac{V_s^2}{4 R_a}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Maximum mechanical power is delivered at **half the no-load speed** and **half the stall torque** — analogous to the maximum power transfer theorem in circuit theory.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 4-pole, 3-phase BLDC motor has a back-EMF constant $K_e = 0.1$ V·s/rad and an armature resistance per phase $R_a = 0.5$ Ω. The motor is supplied by a 48 V DC bus through a six-step inverter. Compute: (a) no-load speed, (b) stall torque, (c) speed at $T = 5$ N·m, and (d) maximum mechanical power.

**Solution:**

**(a) No-load speed:**
$$\omega_{nl} = \frac{V_s}{K_e} = \frac{48}{0.1} = 480 \text{ rad/s} \approx 4584 \text{ rpm}$$

**(b) Stall torque:**
$$T_{stall} = \frac{V_s \, K_t}{R_a} = \frac{48 \times 0.1}{0.5} = 9.6 \text{ N·m}$$

**(c) Speed at $T = 5$ N·m:**
$$\omega = \frac{V_s}{K_e} - \frac{R_a}{K_e K_t} \, T = 480 - \frac{0.5}{0.1 \times 0.1} \times 5 = 480 - 250 = 230 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(d) Maximum mechanical power:**
$$P_{max} = \frac{V_s^2}{4 R_a} = \frac{48^2}{4 \times 0.5} = \frac{2304}{2} = 1152 \text{ W} = 1.152 \text{ kW}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: BLDC Motor Drive System for EV

```mermaid
graph LR
    bat["Battery Pack High Voltage DC Bus"] --> dcdc["DC DC Converter Auxiliary"]
    bat --> inv["Three Phase VSI Inverter IGBT MOSFET"]
    inv --> mtr["BLDC Motor Three Phase Stator"]
    mtr --> shaft["Output Shaft Differential Wheels"]
    mtr --> hall["Hall Effect Position Sensors 120 deg"]
    hall --> ecu["VCU Electronic Control Unit"]
    acc["Accelerator Pedal Throttle Signal"] --> ecu
    brk["Brake Pedal Regen Signal"] --> ecu
    ecu --> inv
    ecu -. telemetry .-> dash["Driver Display SOC Speed"]
    dcdc --> ecu
```

## 4.2 Power Circuit: Three-Phase Full-Bridge Inverter Feeding BLDC

```mermaid
graph TB
    vdc["Vdc Battery 48V 400V"] --> s1["Switch T1 IGBT"]
    vdc --> s2["Switch T3 IGBT"]
    vdc --> s3["Switch T5 IGBT"]
    s1 --> phA["Phase A Winding"]
    s2 --> phB["Phase B Winding"]
    s3 --> phC["Phase C Winding"]
    phA --> s4["Switch T4 IGBT"]
    phB --> s5["Switch T6 IGBT"]
    phC --> s6["Switch T2 IGBT"]
    s4 --> gnd["DC Negative Bus"]
    s5 --> gnd
    s6 --> gnd
    phA -. star point .-> phB
    phB -. star point .-> phC
    ctrl["Gate Driver Logic Circuit"] --> s1
    ctrl --> s2
    ctrl --> s3
    ctrl --> s4
    ctrl --> s5
    ctrl --> s6
    hall["Hall Sensor Feedback 3 bit Gray code"] --> ctrl
```

## 4.3 Six-Step Commutation Sequence Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    start(["Motor Start Command"]) --> readHall["Read Hall Sensors H1 H2 H3"]
    readHall --> decode["Decode 3 bit Position Code"]
    decode --> step1["State 1: T1 ON T6 ON Phases A to B"]
    step1 --> wait1["Wait 60 deg electrical"]
    wait1 --> step2["State 2: T1 ON T2 ON Phases A to C"]
    step2 --> wait2["Wait 60 deg electrical"]
    wait2 --> step3["State 3: T3 ON T2 ON Phases B to C"]
    step3 --> wait3["Wait 60 deg electrical"]
    wait3 --> step4["State 4: T3 ON T4 ON Phases B to A"]
    step4 --> wait4["Wait 60 deg electrical"]
    wait4 --> step5["State 5: T5 ON T4 ON Phases C to A"]
    step5 --> wait5["Wait 60 deg electrical"]
    wait5 --> step6["State 6: T5 ON T6 ON Phases C to B"]
    step6 --> wait6["Wait 60 deg electrical"]
    wait6 --> step1
    step1 -. stop command .-> stop(["Motor Coast or Brake"])
```

## 4.4 Torque–Speed Characteristic Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    regionA["Region 1: Constant Torque Below Base Speed"] --> regionB["Region 2: Constant Power Field Weakening"]
    regionB --> regionC["Region 3: Constant Voltage Series Characteristic"]
    regionA -. base speed .-> regionB
    regionB -. max speed .-> regionC
    ptA["Max Torque at Zero Speed"] --> regionA
    ptB["Max Power Corner Point"] --> regionB
    ptC["No Load Speed"] --> regionC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Region 1 (constant torque) is used during **vehicle launch and hill climbing**, Region 2 (constant power) covers **normal cruising**, and Region 3 (series characteristic) is **emergency overspeed** — typically not used in EV operation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]
**Define a BLDC motor. List any four advantages of using BLDC motors in electric vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor** is a synchronous electric machine in which the field flux is produced by **permanent magnets** mounted on the rotor, while the armature winding on the stator is electronically commutated using a solid-state inverter and rotor-position feedback.

**Four advantages for EV application:**
1. **High efficiency (90–95%)** → extended driving range per charge.
2. **High power-to-weight ratio** → compact and lightweight traction drive.
3. **Maintenance-free operation** (no brushes) → reduced life-cycle cost.
4. **Excellent speed range and dynamic response** → smooth acceleration and regenerative braking.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Key (3 marks):** Definition 1 mark + any 4 advantages 2 marks (0.5 each). Add a diagram-free statement that "commutation is electronic" to fetch the 1 mark for the definition.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Explain the concept of trapezoidal back-EMF in BLDC motors. How does it differ from sinusoidal back-EMF in PMSM?**

**Model Answer:**
The back-EMF induced in a BLDC phase winding has a **trapezoidal shape** as a function of rotor position — it rises linearly, holds constant for 120° electrical (flat top), falls linearly to zero, then becomes negative symmetrically. This is because the air-gap flux distribution produced by surface-mounted magnets is essentially rectangular under the pole arc, and the stator winding is **concentrated** (non-overlapping).

**Key Differences:**

| Feature | BLDC (Trapezoidal) | PMSM (Sinusoidal) |
|---|---|---|
| Back-EMF | Trapezoidal | Sinusoidal |
| Winding | Concentrated | Distributed |
| Current | Square-wave 120° | Sinusoidal |
| Torque ripple | Higher (~5–15%) | Very low (<2%) |
| Control | Simple 6-step | Complex (FOC/SVPWM) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]

#### (a) Explain the construction and working principle of a three-phase BLDC motor with a neat block diagram of the drive system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

*Stator:*
- Laminated silicon-steel stamping stack.
- Slots housing a three-phase Y-connected distributed/concentrated winding.
- Phases A, B, C displaced by 120° electrical in space.

*Rotor:*
- Surface-mounted (or interior) **permanent magnets** (NdFeB preferred).
- Number of poles typically 4, 6, or 8.

*Sensors & Electronics:*
- Three Hall-effect sensors at 60° (electrical) intervals.
- Inverter (six IGBT/MOSFET switches T1–T6) and VCU/Controller.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

1. The VCU reads the three Hall sensor outputs to determine rotor position.
2. Based on this position, the controller activates exactly **two switches** (one from the positive rail, one from the negative rail) to energise **two of the three stator phases** in the correct polarity.
3. Current in the energised phases produces a stator magnetic field vector that interacts with the rotor PM field, generating a torque $T_e = K_t I_a$ that rotates the rotor.
4. As the rotor advances by 60° electrical, Hall sensors change state → controller switches to the next pair (six-step sequence: AB → AC → BC → BA → CA → CB).
5. Continuous switching at high frequency (kHz range) produces smooth, controlled rotation.

The block diagram (already shown in Section 4.1) depicts the integration: **Battery → Inverter → BLDC Motor → Hall Sensors → VCU → Inverter** forming a closed position-feedback loop.

---

#### (b) Derive the EMF and torque equations of a three-phase BLDC motor, and obtain its torque–speed characteristic. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**EMF Equation Derivation (2.5 marks):**

Starting from Faraday's law for a single conductor: $e = B \, l \, v$, with $v = r \, \omega_m$ and integrating over $Z$ conductors per phase:

$$E_b = K_e \, \Phi \, \omega_m$$

For a PM machine, $\Phi$ is constant → $E_b = K_e \, \omega_m$ (linear in speed).

**Torque Equation Derivation (2.5 marks):**

Equating air-gap power and mechanical power:
$$T_e \, \omega_m = E_b \, I_a$$
$$T_e \, \omega_m = K_e \, \omega_m \, I_a$$
$$\boxed{T_e = K_e \, I_a = K_t \, I_a}$$

For two-phase conduction: $T_e = 2 \, K_t \, I_a$.

**Torque–Speed Characteristic (2 marks):**

From the steady-state voltage equation $V_s = I_a R_a + K_e \omega_m$, substituting $I_a = T_e/K_t$:

$$\boxed{\omega_m = \frac{V_s}{K_e} - \frac{R_a}{K_e K_t} \, T_e}$$

This is a straight line with slope $-R_a / (K_e K_t)$ — the classic BLDC linear droop characteristic.

---

### Question B: [Internal Choice — KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]

#### (a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **electronic commutation** and **drive circuit** of a BLDC motor used in EV applications. Discuss the role of Hall-effect sensors. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Electronic Commutation (3 marks):**

Electronic commutation replaces the mechanical brush-commutator assembly. The VCU executes a **six-step (120° conduction) algorithm**:
- Reads the 3-bit Hall sensor code → identifies rotor position.
- Activates the appropriate pair of IGBTs (T1–T6) to drive current through two phases.
- Floats the third phase (back-EMF measurable for sensorless variant).
- Repeats every 60° electrical.

**Drive Circuit (2.5 marks):**

A **three-phase full-bridge Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** built from 6 switches (IGBTs for high-power EVs, MOSFETs for low-voltage two-wheelers) is used. Each leg has a high-side and low-side switch; PWM modulation of the high-side switch controls average phase voltage.

**Role of Hall Sensors (1.5 marks):**
- Provide **absolute rotor position** information to the VCU.
- Three sensors at 60° electrical spacing produce 6 unique 3-bit codes (one per commutation step).
- Enable **closed-loop speed/torque control** and **fast dynamic response**.
- Alternative **sensorless** methods use **back-EMF zero-crossing detection** but introduce starting-torque limitations.

Block diagram reference: Section 4.1.

---

#### (b) Explain the torque-speed characteristics of BLDC motors. Compare **inner-rotor** and **outer-rotor** BLDC configurations, with reference to EV applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Torque-Speed Characteristic (3 marks):**

The BLDC has three distinct operating regions:

| Region | Speed Range | Torque | Power | Vehicle Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I — Constant Torque | 0 to $\omega_{base}$ | $T_{max}$ constant | Increasing linearly | Launch, hill climb |
| II — Constant Power | $\omega_{base}$ to $\omega_{max}/2$ | Decreasing as $1/\omega$ | $P_{max}$ constant | Cruising |
| III — Series | Above $\omega_{max}/2$ | Falling rapidly | Falling | Not used in EVs |

Below base speed, voltage is regulated by PWM. Above base speed, **field-weakening** (advancing commutation angle) is employed to extend speed range.

**Inner-Rotor vs Outer-Rotor (4 marks):**

| Feature | Inner Rotor | Outer Rotor |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor position | Inside stator | Outside stator (cup-shaped stator) |
| Rotor inertia | Lower | Higher |
| Power density | Moderate | **Higher** (larger magnet area) |
| Torque ripple | Lower | Higher |
| Cooling | Easy (rotor air-cooled via shaft) | Difficult (rotor enclosed) |
| Cogging torque | Lower | Higher |
| EV Application | Traction motors (inboard) | **In-wheel hub motors** (2W EVs, e-bikes) |
| Example | Tata Nexon EV traction motor | Ola Electric hub motor |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently confuse BLDC with PMSM or fail to mention the **two-phase simultaneous conduction** when writing the torque equation, costing 1–2 marks. Always state: "In a 3-phase BLDC, at any instant two phases are energised — therefore $T_e = 2 K_t I_a$, not $K_t I_a$." Also, do **not** confuse *electronic commutation frequency* with *PWM switching frequency* — they are different by an order of magnitude.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Card — Print This Section**

- **BLDC = Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor with Trapezoidal back-EMF + Square-wave currents.**
- **Rotor = PM, Stator = 3-phase winding, Commutator = Electronic (VSI).**
- **Six-step commutation** with **120° conduction** per phase.
- **Torque:** $T_e = 2 K_t I_a$ (two phases conducting).
- **Speed:** $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_s}{K_e} - \dfrac{R_a \, T_e}{K_e K_t}$.
- **No-load speed:** $\omega_{nl} = \dfrac{V_s}{K_e}$.
- **Stall torque:** $T_{stall} = \dfrac{V_s K_t}{R_a}$.
- **Max mechanical power** at $\omega = \omega_{nl}/2$, $T = T_{stall}/2$.
- **Three Hall sensors** at 60° spacing → 6 unique position codes.
- **Electrical angle = (P/2) × Mechanical angle.**
- **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120 f / P$.
- **Efficiency:** 90–95% (no rotor copper loss).
- **EV applications:** Traction (inboard) and in-wheel hub motors.
- **Inner rotor → low inertia, easy cooling.** **Outer rotor → high torque, hub-mount.**
- **Equivalence in SI units:** $K_e = K_t$ (energy-conservation consequence).
- **PWM frequency (kHz) ≠ Commutation frequency (Hz).**
- **Regenerative braking:** Motor acts as generator; $T$ opposes motion → current flows back to battery via inverter diodes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Principles and Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Basic Principles and Construction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs), **electric machines (traction motors)** are electromechanical energy conversion devices that serve as the primary propulsion unit, converting electrical energy from the battery (or other energy storage) into mechanical energy at the wheels, and conversely acting as generators during regenerative braking. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > An *electric traction machine* is a rotating electrical machine specifically designed for vehicular traction applications, characterized by **high torque density (Nm/kg)**, **high power density (kW/kg)**, **wide constant-power speed range (CPSR)**, **high efficiency (>90%)**, and the ability to operate in **four quadrants** (forward motoring, forward braking, reverse motoring, reverse braking). The four most common machine topologies studied under the KTU Module 3 syllabus are: 1. **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** — including Interior PMSM (IPMSM) and Surface-mounted PMSM (SPMSM). 2. **Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)** — trapezoidal back-EMF variant of PMSM. 3. **Three-Phase Squirrel-Cage Induction Motor (IM)**. 4. **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Water Wheel and Pump" of an EV:** > Imagine an electric machine as a **reversible water wheel** connected to two tanks — one filled with "electrons" (the battery) and one filled with "motion" (the wheels). > - **Motoring mode:** Electric current (water) is poured from the battery tank onto the wheel, spinning it and pushing the vehicle forward. > - **Generating mode (regenerative braking):** The wheels (now driven by the car's momentum) physically turn the wheel backward, pumping "electrons" back up into the battery tank for reuse. > - **Magnet + Coil** = the spinning wheel blades + water flow channels. > - The **inverter** acts as the "valve operator" that decides when water (current) flows and in which direction. ### 1.3 Why Electric Machines Dominate EV Propulsion - **Instant peak torque** from zero speed (no clutch/gearbox required for launch). - **High efficiency (90–98%)** across a wide speed range, far superior to ICE engines (25–40%). - **No tailpipe emissions** when sourced from clean electricity. - **Regenerative capability** — energy recovery during deceleration. - **Compact, modular** and can be integrated directly into the wheel hub (in-wheel motor architecture). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU Performance Metrics to Remember:** > - Rated Power: **50 kW – 300 kW** (passenger EV traction) > - Rated Speed: **4,000 – 15,000 rpm** > - Peak Efficiency: **> 95%** > - Volumetric Power Density: **> 5 kW/L** > - Specific Power: **> 3 kW/kg** > - Constant Power Speed Range (CPSR): typically **3:1 to 5:1** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle of Rotating Electric Machines All rotating electric machines obey the **fundamental Lorentz force law** and **Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction**: - **Motor action:** A current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force $F = B I L$ (Lorentz force). The cumulative tangential force on multiple conductors produces **torque**. - **Generator action:** When conductors cut magnetic flux lines, an EMF is induced: $e = B L v$ (Faraday's law). This is the principle behind regenerative braking in EVs. The **two essential parts** of every traction machine are: 1. **Stator** — the stationary outer part carrying armature windings (in most EV motors) or field windings. 2. **Rotor** — the rotating inner part carrying permanent magnets (PMSM/BLDC), squirrel-cage bars (IM), or salient poles (SRM). ### 2.2 Construction and Principle of PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor) #### Construction - **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core (0.35 mm thickness to reduce eddy current losses) with **three-phase distributed windings** embedded in semi-closed slots. Same as a conventional 3-phase AC machine stator. - **Rotor:** Carries **high-energy permanent magnets** (typically **NdFeB — Neodymium-Iron-Boron**, or **SmCo — Samarium-Cobalt** for high-temperature applications). - **SPMSM (Surface-mounted):** Magnets glued on the outer surface of the rotor core. Low rotor saliency. $L_d = L_q$. - **IPMSM (Interior):** Magnets embedded inside the rotor laminations. Produces magnetic saliency and **reluctance torque** in addition to magnet torque. $L_d \neq L_q$. #### Working Principle - The stator is supplied with a **three-phase sinusoidal AC waveform** through an inverter. - A **rotating magnetic field** is produced at the synchronous speed: $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$, where $f$ is supply frequency and $P$ is the number of poles. - The rotor magnets **lock-in (synchronize)** with this rotating field and rotate at exactly the same synchronous speed — hence the name *"synchronous"*. - There is **no slip** (unlike induction motors); the rotor speed is rigidly tied to the supply frequency. > [!NOTE] > **Why PMSM is the most popular EV traction motor (e.g., Tesla Model 3, Toyota Prius, Nissan Leaf):** > - Highest **torque density** and **power density** among all traction machines. > - Highest **efficiency** (no rotor copper losses; magnets provide field without excitation current). > - Excellent **dynamic response** (low rotor inertia). > - Compact and lightweight — ideal for vehicle packaging. ### 2.3 Construction and Principle of BLDC Motor #### Construction - **Stator:** Three-phase concentrated (or distributed) windings on a laminated core — structurally identical to PMSM stator. - **Rotor:** Permanent magnets (NdFeB) mounted on the rotor surface in a **trapezoidal flux distribution** pattern. #### Working Principle - Stator is supplied with **rectangular/trapezoidal DC pulses** (not sinusoidal) at precise rotor-position-dependent switching instants. - A **rotor position sensor** (Hall-effect sensor or encoder) tells the controller the rotor angle, and the inverter **electronically commutates** the phases. - Because there are **no mechanical brushes**, the motor is termed *brushless* — but it is essentially a **DC motor inverted inside-out** (rotating permanent magnet, stationary armature). ### 2.4 Construction and Principle of Three-Phase Induction Motor #### Construction - **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core with three-phase distributed windings placed in slots — produces a rotating magnetic field when energized. - **Rotor:** **Squirrel-cage rotor** — a laminated cylinder with **axially-oriented aluminum or copper bars** short-circuited at both ends by end-rings. No windings, no brushes, no magnets, no position sensors — the **simplest, most rugged, and most economical** rotor. #### Working Principle - The stator's rotating magnetic field **cuts the stationary rotor conductors**, inducing EMF and current in the short-circuited bars (Faraday's law). - The induced rotor current interacts with the stator field to produce **Lorentz force** and hence torque. - The rotor must rotate at a speed slightly **less than synchronous speed** to maintain relative motion and induced current. The difference is called **slip**: $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$ - The rotor "chases" the rotating field but never catches it — hence *asynchronous* motor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Slip Range for Traction IM:** EV/HEV applications operate over a wide slip range (typically $s = 0$ to $0.1$ at rated load). Field-weakening techniques extend the constant-power region beyond base speed. ### 2.5 Construction and Principle of Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) #### Construction - **Stator:** Laminated salient-pole structure (typically **6 or 8 poles**) with concentrated windings on each pole. Opposite poles are connected in series to form a phase. - **Rotor:** **Simple laminated salient-pole rotor** with **no windings, no magnets, no cage** — the most mechanically robust rotor in the family. #### Working Principle - Operates on the principle that a salient rotor always tries to **align with the minimum reluctance (maximum inductance)** position when a phase is energized. - When phase A is energized, rotor pole 1 moves to align with stator pole A; then phase B is switched on, and rotor moves to align with phase B, and so on — producing continuous rotation. - Torque is purely **reluctance torque** — produced by the tendency of the rotor to find the lowest-reluctance path. - Requires **rotor position feedback** and a **unipolar asymmetric half-bridge inverter** for commutation. ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU exam question from this module.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description | |---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------| | 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | rpm | $f$ = supply frequency (Hz), $P$ = number of poles | | 2 | Synchronous angular speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | rad/s | Used in torque/EMF equations | | 3 | Slip (IM only) | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless | $N_r$ = actual rotor speed | | 4 | Rotor speed (IM) | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | rpm | Asynchronous operation | | 5 | Induced EMF per phase | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi_m \, K_w$ | Volts | $N_{ph}$ = turns/phase, $\phi_m$ = flux/pole, $K_w$ = winding factor | | 6 | Electromagnetic torque | $T_e = \dfrac{P_{gap}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_{ph} \, V_{ph} \, \sin\delta}{X_s}$ | Nm | $\delta$ = load/torque angle, $X_s$ = synchronous reactance | | 7 | Power developed (air-gap) | $P_{gap} = \dfrac{3 \, V_{ph} \, E_{ph} \, \sin\delta}{X_s}$ | Watts | Per-phase equivalent circuit | | 8 | Mechanical output power | $P_{out} = T_e \cdot \omega_r$ | Watts | Output at shaft | | 9 | Rotor copper loss (IM) | $P_{rcl} = s \cdot P_{gap}$ | Watts | Slip-frequency rotor loss | | 10 | Torque of PMSM | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot [\psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q]$ | Nm | $I_d, I_q$ = d-q axis currents | | 11 | Torque of SRM | $T_e = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot i^2 \cdot \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Nm | Variable reluctance principle | | 12 | Lorentz force (single conductor) | $F = B \, I \, L$ | Newtons | Basis of all motor action | | 13 | Induced EMF (single conductor) | $e = B \, L \, v$ | Volts | Basis of all generator action | | 14 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{losses}}$ | % (decimal) | Always $\eta < 1$ | | 15 | Per-phase power (balanced 3-$\phi$) | $P_{3\phi} = 3 \, V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | Watts | Used in induction motor analysis | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **PMSM** dominates modern EV traction (Tesla, BMW i-series, Lucid Air) due to its unmatched torque/weight ratio. - **BLDC** is widely used in **two-wheelers, e-rickshaws, and light EVs** (e.g., Hero Electric, Ather) due to low-cost electronic controllers and simple trapezoidal commutation algorithms. - **Induction motors** are used in **Tesla Model S, Model X, Tata Tigor EV** (early models), and **Renault Zoe** — robust, magnet-free (no rare-earth dependency), and tolerant of high temperatures. - **SRM** is used in **low-cost applications** and niche EV segments (e.g., some mining/industrial EVs) — no magnets, no rotor windings → cheapest, most rugged option. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Synchronous Speed Equation **Statement:** Derive an expression for the synchronous speed of a rotating magnetic field produced by a balanced three-phase winding. **Step 1 — Define the number of poles:** For a $P$-pole machine (always an even number, since poles come in N-S pairs), the electrical angle per mechanical revolution is: $$\theta_e = \frac{P}{2} \times 2\pi = P\pi \text{ electrical rad / mechanical rev}$$ **Step 2 — Define the electrical angular frequency:** The supply frequency $f$ (Hz) means the stator MMF (magnetomotive force) waveform completes $f$ electrical cycles per second. Hence the electrical angular velocity is: $$\omega_e = 2\pi f \text{ (electrical rad/s)}$$ **Step 3 — Relate electrical and mechanical angular velocity:** Since the electrical angle is $P/2$ times the mechanical angle: $$\omega_e = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \omega_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_m = \frac{2}{P} \cdot \omega_e = \frac{2}{P} \cdot 2\pi f = \frac{4\pi f}{P} \text{ (rad/s)}$$ **Step 4 — Convert mechanical angular velocity to RPM:** Mechanical speed in RPM ($N_s$) is the mechanical angular velocity in rad/s converted via the factor $60 / (2\pi)$: $$N_s = \omega_m \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{4\pi f}{P} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{120 f}{P}$$ $$\boxed{\,N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} \text{ rpm}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Doubling the number of poles halves the synchronous speed at the same frequency. Doubling the supply frequency doubles the synchronous speed. This single equation controls the entire speed behavior of PMSM, BLDC, and synchronous IM in an EV — the inverter varies $f$ to vary speed. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: EMF Equation of a Three-Phase AC Machine **Statement:** Derive the RMS EMF induced per phase in a three-phase AC traction machine. **Step 1 — Flux linkage of a single coil of $N_c$ turns:** For one coil with $N_c$ turns linked by a sinusoidal flux $\phi(t) = \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$: $$\psi_{coil}(t) = N_c \cdot \phi(t) = N_c \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 2 — Induced EMF by Faraday's law:** Faraday's law gives the instantaneous EMF as the negative rate of change of flux linkage: $$e_{coil}(t) = -\frac{d\psi_{coil}}{dt} = N_c \phi_m \omega \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 3 — Peak value of coil EMF:** The peak (maximum) value of the induced EMF is: $$E_{peak} = N_c \phi_m \omega = 2\pi f \cdot N_c \phi_m$$ **Step 4 — Average value over a half-cycle (for sinusoid, average = $2/\pi$ times peak):** For a sinusoidal waveform, the average value of a half-cycle is: $$E_{avg} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot E_{peak} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot 2\pi f N_c \phi_m = 4 f N_c \phi_m$$ **Step 5 — Form factor correction (RMS/avg for sinusoid = $\pi/(2\sqrt{2}) = 1.11$):** The RMS value of a pure sinusoid is 1.11 times the average: $$E_{rms} = 1.11 \cdot E_{avg} = 1.11 \times 4 f N_c \phi_m = 4.44 f N_c \phi_m$$ **Step 6 — Include winding factor $K_w$ (chord, distribution, pitch effects):** The actual flux linked per coil is less than ideal due to short-pitched and distributed windings. This is accounted for by the **winding factor** $K_w = K_d \cdot K_p \cdot K_c$ (typically $0.85 - 0.95$): $$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi_m \, K_w$$ $$\boxed{\,E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi_m \, K_w \text{ Volts (RMS per phase)}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always quote this equation with the form factor **4.44** clearly shown — many students write 4.44 × ƒ × N × φ and lose the explicit derivation. The factor 4.44 = (2 × √2 × π/2) comes from $2 \times \pi/\sqrt{2}$. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Electromagnetic Torque of a Three-Phase Machine **Statement:** Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque from the air-gap power. **Step 1 — Air-gap power (mechanical power developed):** From the per-phase equivalent circuit, the air-gap power transferred across the air gap is: $$P_{gap} = 3 \cdot \frac{V_{ph} \cdot E_{ph} \cdot \sin\delta}{X_s}$$ where $V_{ph}$ is the phase voltage, $E_{ph}$ is the back-EMF per phase, $\delta$ is the torque/load angle, and $X_s$ is the synchronous reactance per phase. **Step 2 — Relate air-gap power to electromagnetic torque:** The mechanical power developed equals torque times the synchronous angular velocity (since the rotating field moves at $\omega_s$): $$P_{gap} = T_e \cdot \omega_s$$ **Step 3 — Solve for torque:** $$T_e = \frac{P_{gap}}{\omega_s} = \frac{3 \, V_{ph} \, E_{ph} \, \sin\delta}{\omega_s \, X_s}$$ **Step 4 — Express in terms of supply frequency and poles:** $$\omega_s = \frac{4\pi f}{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_e = \frac{3 P \, V_{ph} \, E_{ph} \, \sin\delta}{4\pi f \, X_s}$$ $$\boxed{\,T_e = \frac{3 P \, V_{ph} \, E_{ph} \, \sin\delta}{4\pi f \, X_s} = \frac{P_{gap}}{\omega_s} \text{ (Nm)}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Maximum torque occurs at $\delta = 90°$ ($\sin\delta = 1$). This is the *pull-out* or *breakdown torque* of the machine — a critical design limit for EV acceleration torque. ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Slip and Rotor Frequency of an Induction Motor **Step 1 — Define slip:** Slip is the fractional difference between synchronous and rotor speed: $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$ **Step 2 — Rotor speed in terms of slip:** $$N_r = N_s (1 - s) \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_r = \omega_s (1 - s)$$ **Step 3 — Rotor frequency:** The relative speed between the rotating field and rotor conductors is $N_s - N_r = s N_s$. The frequency of the EMF/current induced in the rotor is therefore: $$f_r = s \cdot f$$ $$\boxed{\,f_r = s \cdot f, \quad N_r = N_s(1-s)\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse $f$ and $f_r$. At standstill ($N_r = 0$), $s = 1$ and $f_r = f$ (rotor "sees" full stator frequency). At synchronous speed ($N_r = N_s$), $s = 0$ and $f_r = 0$ — no induced rotor current, no torque (this is why IM never reaches synchronous speed under load). ### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — EV Traction Machines | Feature | PMSM (IPM) | BLDC | Induction Motor | SRM | |---------|------------|------|-----------------|-----| | **Rotor** | Interior PM (NdFeB) | Surface PM (NdFeB) | Squirrel cage | Salient steel, no magnets | | **Stator supply** | 3-$\phi$ sinusoidal | 3-$\phi$ trapezoidal | 3-$\phi$ sinusoidal | Unipolar pulsed | | **Speed relation** | Synchronous ($N_r = N_s$) | Synchronous ($N_r = N_s$) | Asynchronous ($N_r < N_s$) | Variable, position-driven | | **Torque density** | **Highest** | High | Moderate | Moderate | | **Power density** | **Highest** | High | Moderate | Moderate | | **Peak efficiency** | 95 – 97% | 93 – 95% | 90 – 94% | 90 – 93% | | **Rotor losses** | None (PM) | None (PM) | Yes (cage heating) | None (no winding) | | **Cooling complexity** | Moderate | Moderate | High (rotor heats) | Low (simple rotor) | | **Rare-earth dependency** | **High** | **High** | **None** | **None** | | **Position sensor** | Encoder / resolver | Hall sensors | **Not required** | Encoder / resolver | | **Cost** | High | Moderate-High | Low | Low | | **Torque ripple** | Low | Moderate | Low | **High** (main drawback) | | **Field weakening** | Easy (negative $I_d$) | Easy (advance angle) | Difficult | Easy (advance angle) | | **Examples in EVs** | Tesla 3/Y, Prius, Leaf | E-bikes, Ather 450 | Tesla S/X (early), Renault Zoe | Mining/industrial EVs | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EV Traction Motor Drive System ```mermaid flowchart LR BAT[Battery Pack
High-Voltage DC] --> INV[Power Inverter
DC to 3-Phase AC] INV --> CTRL[Motor Controller
VCU ECU] CTRL -->|Gate signals PWM| INV SENSE1[Rotor Position Sensor
Hall or Resolver] --> CTRL SENSE2[Current Sensors
Phase Currents] --> CTRL INV --> M1[U Phase Winding] INV --> M2[V Phase Winding] INV --> M3[W Phase Winding] M1 --> ROTOR[Rotor
PM or Cage or Salient] M2 --> ROTOR M3 --> ROTOR ROTOR -->|Mechanical Power| TRANS[Transmission
Single-Speed or 2-Speed] TRANS --> WHEEL[Drive Wheels] WHEEL -->|Regen Energy| ROTOR ROTOR -->|Back EMF| INV ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The controller reads rotor position and phase currents, computes PWM duty cycles, and fires the inverter's IGBTs/SiC-MOSFETs at the precise instants. During regenerative braking, the rotor's kinetic energy is converted back into a 3-phase back-EMF, which the inverter rectifies back into DC to recharge the battery. ### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram of PMSM Construction ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STATOR[STATOR - Stationary Outer Part] SC[Stator Laminated Core
Silicon Steel 0.35mm] W1[Phase U Winding] W2[Phase V Winding] W3[Phase W Winding] SH[Stator Housing with
Cooling Jacket] SC --- W1 SC --- W2 SC --- W3 SH --- SC end subgraph AIRGAP[AIR GAP - Mechanical Clearance 0.5 to 1.5mm] AG[Critical Magnetic Coupling Zone] end subgraph ROTOR[ROTOR - Rotating Inner Part] RC[Rotor Laminated Core] PM1[PM - North Pole] PM2[PM - South Pole] SHAFT[Drive Shaft] RC --- PM1 RC --- PM2 SHAFT --- RC end STATOR --> AIRGAP --> ROTOR ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **The air gap** is the most critical region of the machine — it determines magnetic coupling, torque capability, and audible noise. A larger air gap reduces torque but improves mechanical safety and reduces cogging torque. ### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram of Squirrel-Cage Induction Motor ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STIM[STATOR - Stationary] SCL[Stator Laminated Core] SP1[Stator Pole 1] SP2[Stator Pole 2] SP3[Stator Pole 3] WIND[3-Phase Distributed
Windings in Slots] SCL --- SP1 SCL --- SP2 SCL --- SP3 SP1 --- WIND end subgraph ROTIM[ROTOR - Rotating Squirrel Cage] RCL[Rotor Laminated Cylinder] RB1[Rotor Bar - Aluminum] RB2[Rotor Bar - Aluminum] RB3[Rotor Bar - Aluminum] RING[End Ring - Short Circuit] SHAFT2[Rotor Shaft] RCL --- RB1 RCL --- RB2 RCL --- RB3 RB1 --- RING RB2 --- RING RB3 --- RING RING --- SHAFT2 end STIM -->|Magnetic Field Cuts Bars| ROTIM ``` ### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram of Switched Reluctance Motor ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STSR[STATOR - 6 Salient Poles] SPA[Stator Pole A1] SPB[Stator Pole B1] SPC[Stator Pole C1] PAW[Phase A Winding] PBW[Phase B Winding] PCW[Phase C Winding] SPA --- PAW SPB --- PBW SPC --- PCW end subgraph ROTSR[ROTOR - 4 Salient Poles - No Windings No Magnets] RPA[Rotor Pole 1] RPB[Rotor Pole 2] RPC[Rotor Pole 3] RPD[Rotor Pole 4] RPA --- RPB RPC --- RPD end STSR -->|Phase A Energized - Rotor Pole 1 Aligns with Stator Pole A1| ROTSR ROTSR -->|Next Phase Energized - Rotation Step| STSR ``` ### 4.5 Operating Quadrant Diagram — EV Traction Motor ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Q1[QUADRANT I - Forward Motoring] Q1D[Speed positive, Torque positive
Normal Driving] end subgraph Q2[QUADRANT II - Forward Braking Regen] Q2D[Speed positive, Torque negative
Regenerative Braking] end subgraph Q3[QUADRANT III - Reverse Motoring] Q3D[Speed negative, Torque negative
Reverse Driving] end subgraph Q4[QUADRANT IV - Reverse Braking Regen] Q4D[Speed negative, Torque positive
Reverse Regen] end Q1 -->|Reduce speed via regen| Q2 Q2 -->|Stop and reverse| Q3 Q3 -->|Brake in reverse| Q4 Q4 -->|Resume forward| Q1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why four-quadrant operation matters in EVs:** Unlike industrial motors that may only run in Quadrant I, EV traction motors must seamlessly transition through all four quadrants — accelerating, regenerative braking, reverse driving, and reverse braking — often within a single drive cycle. This demands sophisticated vector control algorithms (FOC / DTC) running in the inverter. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 3]** **Q1. List any four desirable characteristics of an electric machine used for EV traction applications.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **High torque density and power density** (≥ 3 kW/kg) for compact, lightweight packaging. 2. **High efficiency** (≥ 90–95%) over a wide operating range to maximize vehicle range. 3. **Wide constant-power speed range (CPSR)** to avoid multi-gear transmissions. 4. **Four-quadrant operation** capability — forward/reverse motoring and forward/reverse regenerative braking. 5. *Bonus point:* High reliability, low maintenance, and tolerance to harsh automotive environments (vibration, temperature). --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam, Module 3]** **Q2. Differentiate between a PMSM and a BLDC motor in terms of construction, supply waveform, and back-EMF shape.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | PMSM | BLDC | |--------|------|------| | Stator winding | Distributed, sinusoidal MMF | Concentrated, trapezoidal MMF | | Supply waveform | Sinusoidal 3-phase current | Rectangular/trapezoidal 3-phase current | | Back-EMF shape | Sinusoidal | Trapezoidal | | Rotor PM arrangement | Usually interior (IPMSM) for reluctance torque | Usually surface-mounted (SPMSM) | | Control algorithm | FOC (Field-Oriented Control) | Six-step / 120° trapezoidal commutation | | Torque ripple | Lower | Higher | --- ### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Exam, Module 3 - Choice A]** **Q. (a) With a neat construction diagram, explain the working principle of a three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor used in EV traction. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** A three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor consists of a **stationary stator** and a **rotating rotor**, separated by a small air gap. **Construction (3 marks):** - **Stator:** A laminated silicon-steel hollow cylinder is mounted inside a cast-iron frame. Three-phase distributed windings are placed in the semi-closed slots of the stator core. The three windings are spatially displaced by **120° electrical** and are connected in either star or delta. - **Rotor:** A laminated cylindrical core mounted on the shaft, carrying **aluminum (or copper) bars** placed axially in semi-closed rotor slots. All bars are **short-circuited at both ends by conducting end-rings**, forming a closed electrical path resembling a squirrel cage — hence the name. - **No brushes, no slip rings, no permanent magnets** — making it the most rugged and economical traction machine. **Working Principle (4 marks):** 1. When a balanced 3-phase AC supply is given to the stator windings, a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** is produced at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$ rpm. 2. This RMF **cuts the stationary rotor conductors** at a relative speed of $(N_s - N_r)$ rpm, inducing an EMF in the rotor bars (Faraday's law). 3. Since the rotor bars are short-circuited, **induced current flows** through the bars and end-rings. 4. This current-carrying rotor conductor, now placed in the stator's magnetic field, experiences a **Lorentz force** $F = BIL$, producing torque. 5. By Lenz's law, the rotor rotates in the **same direction as the RMF** to **reduce the relative motion** that caused the induced current. The rotor accelerates until the developed torque equals the load torque. 6. The rotor cannot reach synchronous speed (otherwise no relative motion, no induced current, no torque). It runs at a speed $N_r = N_s(1-s)$ where $s$ is the **slip** (typically 2–8% in EV operation). **Conclusion:** The squirrel-cage IM is robust, magnet-free, and inexpensive — making it the choice for early Tesla models and many commercial EVs. --- **Q. (b) Derive the EMF equation of a three-phase AC machine. A 4-pole, 50 Hz, three-phase induction motor has 60 turns per phase with a winding factor of 0.96. If the flux per pole is 0.05 Wb, calculate the induced EMF per phase. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Derivation (3 marks):** The average EMF per turn = $4 f \phi_m$ (derived from Faraday's law applied to one conductor cutting a sinusoidally distributed flux over a half-cycle). Multiplying by $N_{ph}$ turns per phase and including the winding factor $K_w$: $$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi_m \, K_w \quad \text{Volts (RMS per phase)}$$ **Numerical Solution (4 marks):** Given: - $P = 4$ poles (note: number of poles does not appear in the EMF equation directly) - $f = 50$ Hz - $N_{ph} = 60$ turns/phase - $K_w = 0.96$ - $\phi_m = 0.05$ Wb Substituting: $$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 60 \times 0.05 \times 0.96$$ Step-by-step evaluation: $$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 = 222$$ $$E_{ph} = 222 \times 60 = 13{,}320$$ $$E_{ph} = 13{,}320 \times 0.05 = 666$$ $$E_{ph} = 666 \times 0.96 = 639.36 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{\,E_{ph} \approx 639.4 \text{ V (RMS per phase)}\,}$$ If connected in star, the line voltage is $V_L = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph} = 1.732 \times 639.36 \approx 1107.4$ V. --- ### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Main Exam, Module 3 - Choice B]** **Q. (a) With neat sketches, explain the construction and working principle of a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM). Discuss why PMSM is preferred for modern EV traction. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Construction (3 marks):** - **Stator:** A laminated silicon-steel core with **three-phase distributed windings** placed in semi-closed slots — identical in structure to the stator of a three-phase induction motor. The windings produce a sinusoidal MMF distribution. - **Rotor:** A laminated steel rotor carrying **high-energy permanent magnets** (typically **NdFeB — Neodymium Iron Boron**, or **SmCo — Samarium Cobalt** for high-temperature applications). - In **SPMSM (Surface-mounted PMSM)**, magnets are bonded to the outer surface of the rotor core using adhesives and protective sleeves (carbon-fiber banding for high-speed operation). - In **IPMSM (Interior PMSM)**, magnets are inserted inside rectangular cavities machined in the rotor laminations. IPMSM offers **reluctance torque** in addition to magnet torque, along with mechanical robustness and field-weakening capability. - **Shaft, bearings, housing, and cooling jacket** complete the assembly. A **resolver or encoder** is mounted on the shaft for rotor position feedback (essential for synchronous operation). **Working Principle (3 marks):** 1. The stator is supplied with a **balanced 3-phase sinusoidal AC voltage** from a 3-phase voltage-source inverter (VSI), whose frequency is varied to control speed (V/f or FOC strategy). 2. A **rotating magnetic field** is produced at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$. 3. The **permanent magnet rotor locks in synchronism** with this rotating field and rotates at exactly $N_r = N_s$ — no slip, no rotor copper loss, no rotor magnetic field weakening (in SPM). 4. Torque is produced by the **interaction of stator armature field and rotor magnet field**; the load angle $\delta$ between them determines the torque magnitude. 5. Speed is controlled by varying inverter frequency; torque is controlled by varying current magnitude (and phase in IPMSM via $I_d$ and $I_q$). **Why PMSM is preferred for modern EVs (1 mark):** - **Highest torque and power density** → lighter, more compact traction system. - **Highest efficiency** (>95%) → longer driving range per charge. - **Excellent dynamic response** → smoother acceleration and regenerative braking. - **No rotor copper losses** → simpler thermal management. - Used in **Tesla Model 3/Y, Toyota Prius, Nissan Leaf, BMW i3, Lucid Air** — the de-facto industry standard. --- **Q. (b) A 3-phase, 8-pole, 50 Hz PMSM is supplied with a balanced sinusoidal voltage. Calculate the synchronous speed. If the rotor is also rotating at 750 rpm, what type of machine behaviour does this indicate? (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - Number of poles, $P = 8$ - Supply frequency, $f = 50$ Hz - Rotor speed, $N_r = 750$ rpm **Calculation (4 marks):** Synchronous speed: $$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{8} = \frac{6000}{8} = 750 \text{ rpm}$$ $$\boxed{\,N_s = 750 \text{ rpm}\,}$$ **Interpretation (3 marks):** Since the rotor speed $N_r = 750$ rpm **exactly equals** the synchronous speed $N_s = 750$ rpm: - The rotor is **in perfect synchronism** with the stator's rotating magnetic field. - This is **normal PMSM behaviour** — the rotor magnet is "locked" to the rotating field. - The **load angle** $\delta$ between the rotor magnet axis and the stator field axis is non-zero (to develop torque), but the rotor speed is rigidly tied to the supply frequency. - **There is no slip** in a PMSM; the concept of slip is unique to induction motors. - If the load exceeds the breakdown (pull-out) torque, the rotor will **fall out of synchronism** and stall — this is the **stability limit** of PMSM operation. If $N_r \neq N_s$, the motor is not in synchronism → either stalled or operating abnormally (controller malfunction). --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Module 3 Questions:** > 1. **Confusing "synchronous speed" with "rotor speed"** — They are equal only in PMSM/BLDC; they differ in IM by the slip. > 2. **Forgetting the form factor 4.44 in the EMF equation** — Many students write $E = 2\pi f N \phi$ (which is peak, not RMS) and lose 1–2 marks. > 3. **Omitting the winding factor $K_w$** — Always include it in the EMF equation; without it, the answer is idealized and incorrect. > 4. **Wrong units in the EMF equation** — Frequency in Hz, flux in Wb, turns is a pure number → EMF in Volts. > 5. **Mixing up "motoring" and "generating" quadrants** — Clearly state the operating mode and the sign of torque/power. > 6. **Incomplete construction diagrams** — Always label stator, rotor, windings, air gap, and shaft. Unlabelled diagrams fetch partial marks only. > 7. **PMSM vs BLDC confusion** — A common trap: BLDC has trapezoidal back-EMF; PMSM has sinusoidal. Stator construction is similar; rotor and supply waveform differ. > 8. **Slip equation sign** — $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ is positive for normal motoring; negative for super-synchronous generating (rare in EV IM, but valid). ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Basic Principles and Construction of EV Traction Machines** - ✅ Electric machines in EVs operate on **Lorentz force** (motoring) and **Faraday's induction** (generating/regen). - ✅ All rotating machines have a **stator (stationary) + rotor (rotating) + air gap**. - ✅ **Synchronous speed equation:** $N_s = 120f/P$ — controls all EV motor speeds. - ✅ **EMF equation per phase:** $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi_m \, K_w$ — must include form factor and winding factor. - ✅ **Torque equation:** $T_e = P_{gap}/\omega_s$ — maximum at $\delta = 90°$ (pull-out torque). - ✅ **PMSM** — Permanent magnet rotor, sinusoidal supply, synchronous operation, highest efficiency, **industry standard for modern EVs**. - ✅ **BLDC** — Permanent magnet rotor, trapezoidal supply, electronically commutated, popular in **2-wheelers and light EVs**. - ✅ **Induction motor** — Squirrel-cage rotor, asynchronous, slip $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$, **rugged and magnet-free**, used in **early Tesla and Renault Zoe**. - ✅ **SRM** — Salient rotor with no PM and no windings, operates on variable reluctance principle, robust but high torque ripple, used in **niche/industrial EVs**. - ✅ Traction machines must have **high torque density, high efficiency (>90%), wide CPSR, four-quadrant operation**, and tolerance to automotive environments. - ✅ **Four-quadrant operation** = forward motoring + forward regen + reverse motoring + reverse regen — essential for EV drive cycles. - ✅ **NdFeB magnets** are standard for PMSM/BLDC; **SmCo** for high-temperature applications. - ✅ Always label: **stator, rotor, air gap, windings, magnets/cage/salient poles, shaft** in any construction diagram. - ✅ $1$ hp $= 746$ W; $1$ kW $= 1.341$ hp — useful for power conversions in EV problems. - ✅ EV traction motors typically operate at **400 V or 800 V** DC bus voltage in modern systems.

topics.title: Switched Reluctance Motors (SRM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Switched Reluctance Motors (SRM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 3 — SWITCHED RELUCTANCE MOTORS (SRM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)** is a **doubly salient, singly excited** rotating electrical machine that produces torque purely on the principle of *variable reluctance*. The stator carries concentrated field windings (DC-excited in a switched manner via a power electronic converter), while the rotor is a simple, robust, **salient-pole laminated steel structure with no permanent magnets, no rotor windings, and no rotor cage**. Torque is generated solely by the tendency of the magnetic circuit to align itself in the position of **minimum reluctance** (maximum inductance) when a stator phase is excited.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Checklist (Memorize This)**
> 1. **Doubly salient** → salient poles on BOTH stator and rotor.
> 2. **Singly excited** → field originates ONLY from the stator winding.
> 3. **No magnets, no rotor copper** → rotor is magnet-free and brushless.
> 4. **Reluctance torque** → torque = (1/2) · i² · (dL/dθ).
> 5. **Switched excitation** → rotor position feedback controls the converter switching.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnetic Dog Chasing a Bone"

Imagine a dog (the magnetic flux) that always wants to take the **shortest, easiest path** (minimum reluctance) between two bones (the excited stator poles). The dog is excited only when you throw a bone on a specific side. Now imagine a rotor (the moving "path"): it physically rotates so the path from the excited stator pole to the nearest rotor pole becomes shorter — that pulling action **is the reluctance torque**.

- The flux **does not slip** like in induction motors — it simply seeks the lowest-reluctance alignment.
- Because there are no magnets, the rotor is **extremely cheap, rugged, and fault-tolerant** — a huge reason SRM is favored for EV traction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SRM Matters in EVs**
> - Wide constant-power speed range (ideal for cruising + hill climbing).
> - Excellent fault tolerance (one phase fails, motor still runs on the rest).
> - Very high efficiency (no rotor copper loss, no magnet loss).
