> - Tolerance to high temperatures (no rare-earth magnets to demagnetize).

---

### 1.3 Classification & Position in the EV Powertrain Family

| Motor Type | Excitation | Magnet-Free? | Typical EV Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor) | Rotor magnets | No | Tesla Model 3, Toyota Prius |
| **SRM (Switched Reluctance Motor)** | **Stator only** | **Yes** | **Renault, Tesla-2026 patent, niche EVs** |
| Induction Motor (IM) | Induced (rotor) | Yes | Tesla Model S (early) |
| BLDC | Rotor magnets | No | Two-wheeler EVs |

**Bold** SRM is the **only magnet-free, doubly salient, variable-reluctance** machine in commercial EV consideration — this is its strategic identity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Features

**Stator:**
- Even number of salient poles (typical: **6 stator poles / 4 rotor poles** → `6/4 SRM` is the textbook default).
- Concentrated, short-pitched coils wound around each stator pole.
- Coils diametrically opposite are connected in series to form one **phase**.

**Rotor:**
- Laminated silicon-steel salient poles, **NO windings, NO magnets, NO cage**.
- Rotor poles are typically **2 fewer** than stator poles (e.g., 6/4, 8/6, 10/8, 12/10).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pole-Pair Rule for SRM:** For an `m`-phase SRM with Ns stator poles and Nr rotor poles, the step angle (in mechanical degrees) is:
>
> Step angle = (360° × m) / (N_s × N_r)
>
> Example: 6/4, 3-phase SRM → Step = (360 × 3) / (6 × 4) = **45° per stroke**.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Position sensing:** Rotor-position sensors (Hall sensors / encoders / sensorless estimators) detect rotor angle θ.
2. **Phase energization:** Based on θ, the controller fires the IGBTs of one phase, energizing two diametrically-opposed stator poles.
3. **Reluctance pull:** The energized stator pole pair creates a magnetic field that pulls the nearest rotor pole into **alignment** (minimum reluctance).
4. **Sequential commutation:** As the rotor aligns with phase A, phase A is turned OFF and phase B is turned ON — the rotor "steps" forward.
5. **Continuous rotation:** Sequential phase excitation yields smooth continuous rotation in the desired direction.

### 2.3 Inductance Profile L(θ) — The Heart of an SRM

The phase inductance is a periodic function of rotor position θ:

- **L_min (Unaligned):** Rotor pole is between two stator poles — flux path has maximum air-gap → minimum inductance.
- **L_max (Aligned):** Rotor pole is centered under the stator pole — flux path is fully iron → maximum inductance.
- **Rising slope (dL/dθ > 0):** Rotor pole approaching alignment → **motoring torque** produced.
- **Falling slope (dL/dθ < 0):** Rotor pole moving past alignment → **generating (braking) torque** produced.

### 2.4 Torque Production — The Physics

The instantaneous torque developed by one phase is governed by the **co-energy derivative**:

$$
T_e = \frac{\partial W'_f(i,\theta)}{\partial \theta} \Bigg\vert_{i = \text{const}}
$$

For a linear magnetic circuit where L depends only on θ:

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} \cdot i^2 \cdot \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

**Observations:**
- Torque is **independent of current direction** → unipolar converter is sufficient (only one switch direction needed).
- Torque is **proportional to i²** → inherently constant torque capability per ampere.
- Torque sign is determined **purely by the sign of dL/dθ** (rising inductance = motoring).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,i^2\,\tfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Instantaneous electromagnetic torque | N·m | Linear magnetics, $\tfrac{dL}{d\theta}>0$ for motoring |
| 2 | $V_\phi = R i + L(\theta)\,\tfrac{di}{dt} + i\,\tfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}\,\omega$ | Phase voltage equation | V | $V_\phi$ = DC bus voltage during excitation |
| 3 | $W'_f = \tfrac{1}{2}\,L(\theta)\,i^2$ | Co-energy (linear system) | J | Linear magnetics assumption |
| 4 | $W_f = \int_0^{\lambda}\,\tfrac{\lambda}{L(\theta)}\,d\lambda$ | Stored field energy | J | Magnetizing branch |
| 5 | $\omega_{base} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{i_{peak}\,(L_{max}-L_{min})}$ | Base (corner) speed | rad/s | Limited by voltage drop across dL/dt term |
| 6 | $L(\theta) = L_{min} + (L_{max}-L_{min}) \cdot f(\theta)$ | Inductance profile (periodic) | H | $f(\theta)$ normalized (0 → 1) |
| 7 | $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360°\,m}{N_s\,N_r}$ | Step angle per stroke | deg | `m` phases, Ns stator, Nr rotor |
| 8 | $P_{cu} = m\,I_{rms}^2\,R_\phi$ | Total stator copper loss | W | All `m` phases, R = phase resistance |
| 9 | $T_{avg} = \dfrac{W_{cycle}}{\theta_{cycle}} = \dfrac{m \cdot W'_{cycle}}{2\pi/N_r}$ | Average torque per revolution | N·m | One full electrical cycle |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out}+P_{cu}+P_{iron}+P_{sw}}$ | Drive efficiency | – | Includes converter switching loss |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV traction** (Renault Fluence Z.E., Tesla future SRM patents): magnet-free → no rare-earth supply chain risk.
- **Aerospace fuel pumps & actuators:** high reliability, fault-tolerant, high-temperature tolerant.
- **High-speed industrial spindles:** rotors tolerate >25,000 rpm with no magnets to fly apart.
- **Wind turbine pitch & yaw systems:** rugged, low maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation #1 — The Fundamental SRM Torque Equation from Co-Energy

**Given:** A singly-excited electromagnetic system with phase flux linkage λ and current i, inductance L(θ) where θ is rotor position.

**Step 1 — Define co-energy.**
For a magnetic system with a single electrical port, the magnetic co-energy is defined as the energy supplied by the source:

$$
W'_f(i,\theta) = \int_0^{i} \lambda(i',\theta)\,di'
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the flux linkage.**
In a linear magnetic circuit, flux linkage is proportional to current:

$$
\lambda(i,\theta) = L(\theta) \cdot i
$$

Inserting into the co-energy integral:

$$
W'_f(i,\theta) = \int_0^{i} L(\theta)\cdot i'\,di'
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate (note L is a function of θ, NOT of i′).**
Since L(θ) is constant with respect to the integration variable i′, pull it out:

$$
W'_f(i,\theta) = L(\theta)\int_0^{i} i'\,di' = L(\theta) \cdot \left[\frac{(i')^2}{2}\right]_0^{i}
$$

$$
W'_f(i,\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,L(\theta)\,i^2
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the co-energy theorem for torque.**
Torque equals the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to angular position, at constant current:

$$
T_e = \frac{\partial W'_f(i,\theta)}{\partial \theta} \Bigg\vert_{i}
$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate.**
Since only L(θ) depends on θ:

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2}\cdot i^2 \cdot \frac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta}
$$

**Step 6 — Final canonical form.**

$$
\boxed{\,T_e = \frac{1}{2}\,i^2\,\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}\,}
$$

This is the **single most important equation** of the entire SRM module for KTU exams.

### 3.2 Derivation #2 — Phase Voltage Equation (Including Back-EMF Term)

**Given:** Phase winding has resistance R, inductance L(θ), and rotor turns at angular velocity ω.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to one phase.**

$$
V_\phi = R\,i + \frac{d\lambda}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Express flux linkage.**
Flux linkage depends on both i and θ:

$$
\lambda(i,\theta) = L(\theta)\cdot i
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the chain rule to dλ/dt.**
Because λ depends on two variables (i and θ), use total time derivative:

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = \frac{\partial \lambda}{\partial i}\cdot\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{\partial \lambda}{\partial \theta}\cdot\frac{d\theta}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute each partial derivative.**

$$
\frac{\partial \lambda}{\partial i} = L(\theta)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \lambda}{\partial \theta} = i\cdot\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute.**

$$
V_\phi = R\,i + L(\theta)\,\frac{di}{dt} + i\,\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}\,\frac{d\theta}{dt}
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize ω = dθ/dt.**

$$
\boxed{\,V_\phi = R\,i + L(\theta)\,\frac{di}{dt} + i\,\omega\,\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Term Interpretation (Vital for KTU):**
> - **R·i** → Ohmic (copper) drop.
> - **L(θ)·di/dt** → Transformer EMF (magnetizing component).
> - **i·ω·dL/dθ** → **Motional Back-EMF** (the speed-dependent term that limits high-speed operation).

### 3.3 Derivation #3 — Average Torque Per Revolution

**Given:** An `m`-phase SRM with N_r rotor poles, ideal aligned/unaligned square-wave inductance.

**Step 1 — Energy converted per stroke.**
During one motoring stroke (inductance rise from L_min to L_max), the energy converted (per cycle, from co-energy difference):

$$
W_{stroke} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,i_{peak}^2\,(L_{max} - L_{min})
$$

**Step 2 — Strokes per revolution.**
For one rotor pole pair, one full electrical revolution has Nr strokes. With m phases distributed, total strokes per mechanical revolution:

$$
N_{strokes} = m \cdot N_r
$$

**Step 3 — Average torque.**
Average torque is total energy converted per revolution divided by 2π (mechanical rad):

$$
T_{avg} = \frac{m \cdot N_r \cdot W_{stroke}}{2\pi}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
\boxed{\,T_{avg} = \frac{m\,N_r\,i_{peak}^2\,(L_{max}-L_{min})}{4\pi}\,}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operating Quadrants

| Quadrant | dL/dθ | Current State | Torque Sign | Mode | EV Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| I (Forward Motoring) | > 0 | Energized during rising L | Positive | Acceleration | Driving |
| II (Forward Generating) | < 0 | Energized during falling L | Negative | Regeneration | Braking |
| III (Reverse Motoring) | > 0 (reverse sweep) | Energized during rising L (rev) | Negative | Reverse driving | Reverse gear |
| IV (Reverse Generating) | < 0 (reverse sweep) | Energized during falling L (rev) | Positive | Reverse regen | Plausible |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love asking: *"How is four-quadrant operation achieved in an SRM?"* The answer lies in the fact that **only the dL/dθ sign and the firing sequence** determine torque direction — **NOT current polarity**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — SRM Drive System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DCbus["DC Bus Supply / Battery Pack"] --> Conv["Asymmetric H Bridge Power Converter"]
    Cmd["Torque Reference from Accelerator Pedal"] --> Ctrl["Digital Controller DSP or Microcontroller"]
    Pos["Rotor Position Sensor Hall Encoder or Resolver"] --> Ctrl
    Curr["Current Sensor per Phase"] --> Conv
    Ctrl --> Conv
    Conv --> PhA["SRM Phase A Winding"]
    Conv --> PhB["SRM Phase B Winding"]
    Conv --> PhC["SRM Phase C Winding"]
    PhA --> Mech["Salient Pole Rotor Mechanical Output Shaft"]
    PhB --> Mech
    PhC --> Mech
    Mech --> Load["EV Transmission and Wheels"]
    Mech -. feedback .-> Pos
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Per-Phase Asymmetric H-Bridge Converter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vplus["DC Bus Positive Rail"] --> Sa1["Switch T1 IGBT"]
    Sa1 --> NodeA["Phase A Terminal Top"]
    Da1["Diode D1"] --> NodeA
    NodeA --> PhAW["Phase A Winding"]
    PhAW --> NodeB["Phase A Terminal Bottom"]
    NodeB --> Sa2["Switch T2 IGBT"]
    NodeB --> Da2["Diode D2"]
    Sa2 --> Vminus["DC Bus Negative Rail"]
    NodeA -. freewheel through D1 D2 .-> NodeB
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each SRM phase requires **its own asymmetric H-bridge** because current is unidirectional (always positive during motoring) but needs to be **energized, freewheeled, and demagnetized** in three distinct modes.

### 4.3 Torque Production Sequence Diagram — 6/4 SRM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Rotor at theta equals 0 degrees Phase A fully unaligned"] --> P1["Fire Phase A Inductance rising dL d theta greater than zero Motoring torque"]
    P1 --> P2["Rotor moves to 30 degrees Phase A fully aligned L is maximum"]
    P2 --> P3["Turn OFF Phase A Fire Phase B Rotor advances another 30 degrees"]
    P3 --> P4["Phase B aligned Turn OFF Fire Phase C Another 30 degrees"]
    P4 --> P5["Mechanical revolution complete After 12 strokes"]
```

### 4.4 Closed-Loop Speed Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OmegaRef["Reference Speed from Driver"] --> SumNode["Summing Junction"]
    OmegaFB["Measured Speed from Encoder"] --> SumNode
    SumNode --> PIctrl["PI Speed Controller Outputs Torque Reference"]
    PIctrl --> TorqueMap["Torque to Current Reference Lookup"]
    TorqueMap --> HystCtrl["Hysteresis Current Controller"]
    HystCtrl --> GateDrv["Gate Driver Signals to IGBTs"]
    GateDrv --> SRMplant["SRM Plant"]
    SRMplant --> MechOut["Mechanical Speed and Torque"]
    MechOut --> OmegaFB
    MechOut --> Load2["EV Drivetrain Load"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Draw and explain the closed-loop speed control block diagram of an SRM drive."* The four-block chain (Speed controller → Current reference → Hysteresis controller → Power converter → SRM) is the **canonical answer template**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]**
**Define a switched reluctance motor and highlight two features that make it suitable for electric vehicle traction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A switched reluctance motor (SRM) is a doubly salient, singly excited variable-reluctance machine in which torque is produced by the tendency of the magnetic circuit to align the rotor to a position of minimum reluctance when a stator phase is energized through a power electronic converter. **[2 marks]**

Two features suitable for EV traction:
1. **Magnet-free rotor** → no rare-earth dependency, robust at high temperatures, low cost. **[0.5 mark]**
2. **Wide constant-power operating region** → allows high starting torque and high-speed cruising in a single gear. **[0.5 mark]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**Why does the SRM require a position sensor while an induction motor does not?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The SRM requires a position sensor because torque production depends on the **synchronization between the rotor position and the stator phase excitation**. The controller must energize each phase **only during the rising-inductance region** (where dL/dθ > 0) to produce positive torque, otherwise torque reverses or becomes negative. **[2 marks]**

An induction motor, by contrast, produces torque by **slip-induced relative motion** between the rotating stator field and the rotor — the rotor inherently lags the field, so torque is produced without position feedback. **[1 mark]**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Main Exam, Module 3]**

**(a) Explain the construction and working principle of a 6/4, 3-phase switched reluctance motor with neat diagrams. Discuss the variation of inductance with rotor position. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

- **Stator:** Made of laminated silicon steel with **6 salient poles**. Each pole carries a concentrated coil. Diametrically-opposite coils (A-A', B-B', C-C') are connected in series to form the three phases.
- **Rotor:** A simple laminated steel cylinder with **4 salient poles**. It carries no windings, no magnets, and no cage.
- **Sensors:** A rotor position sensor (Hall-effect or optical encoder) mounted on the non-drive end provides the rotor angle θ to the controller.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

- When Phase A is energized, the stator pair A-A' produces a magnetic field. The rotor pole nearest to the energized pair experiences a **reluctance pull** and rotates toward alignment with A-A'. Once aligned (maximum inductance, dL/dθ = 0), Phase A is switched OFF and Phase B is energized, advancing the rotor by **30 mechanical degrees** (the step angle). Sequential excitation of A → B → C → A produces continuous rotation.

**Inductance Variation (2 marks):**

- At **θ = 0° (unaligned):** rotor pole is exactly midway between stator poles → maximum air-gap, minimum inductance **L_min**.
- As θ increases from 0° → 30°, the rotor pole approaches alignment, air-gap shrinks, and L rises from L_min to **L_max** over the **rising-slope region** (motoring zone).
- At **θ = 30° (aligned):** rotor pole fully overlaps stator pole → minimum air-gap, maximum inductance **L_max**, torque production ceases (dL/dθ = 0).
- Beyond 30°, L falls → falling-slope region (regeneration zone if energized).

**Diagram (to be drawn on exam sheet):** Show cross-section of 6/4 SRM with phase coils A-A', B-B', C-C' and rotor position marked at three instants; mark L_min and L_max positions on the inductance profile graph.

---

**(b) Derive the instantaneous torque equation of a switched reluctance motor starting from co-energy. Comment on why SRM torque is independent of current direction. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):** *(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation)*

Starting from co-energy definition:

$$
W'_f(i,\theta) = \int_0^{i} L(\theta)\,i'\,di' = \tfrac{1}{2}\,L(\theta)\,i^2
$$

Torque from co-energy theorem:

$$
T_e = \left.\frac{\partial W'_f}{\partial \theta}\right\vert_{i} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,i^2\,\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

**Why torque is independent of current direction (2 marks):**

The torque equation contains **i²**, not i. Therefore, the sign of torque depends **only on the sign of dL(θ)/dθ**, not on whether i is positive or negative. This means:

- A **unipolar converter** (only positive current direction) is sufficient to drive an SRM in all four quadrants.
- The direction of rotation is determined by the **firing sequence of phases**, not by current polarity.
- This simplifies converter design and reduces switching losses compared to bipolar-fed machines like induction motors or PMSMs.

---

#### **QUESTION B — (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2025, Supplementary Exam, Module 3]**

**(a) Draw and explain the asymmetric H-bridge power converter used for one phase of an SRM drive. Discuss the three operating modes of the converter. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Converter Topology (2 marks):**

Each phase of the SRM requires its **own asymmetric H-bridge** consisting of two IGBT switches (T1, T2) and two freewheeling diodes (D1, D2). The phase winding is connected between the midpoints of the two legs (refer Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram).

**Three Operating Modes (5 marks):**

**Mode 1 — Magnetization (Energization):**
- Both T1 and T2 are switched ON.
- DC bus voltage V_dc is applied across the phase winding.
- Phase current rises (di/dt = (V_dc − Ri)/L).
- Energy is drawn from the supply and stored in the magnetic field.

**Mode 2 — Freewheeling (Zero-Voltage Loop):**
- T1 remains ON, T2 is turned OFF.
- D1 conducts (current freewheels through T1 and D1).
- Voltage across winding ≈ 0 (only R·i drop).
- Current decays slowly (only due to R).
- Used to reduce switching losses while maintaining torque-producing current.

**Mode 3 — Demagnetization:**
- Both T1 and T2 are switched OFF.
- Current is forced through D1 and D2 back into the DC bus.
- **Negative voltage (−V_dc)** is applied across the winding.
- Current falls rapidly (di/dt negative, fast turn-off).
- Magnetic energy is **returned to the source** — a major efficiency advantage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The asymmetric bridge can apply **+V_dc, 0, or −V_dc** to the phase — the three discrete voltage states of an SRM controller. This is the foundation of **hysteresis current control** and **PWM voltage control**.

---

**(b) Explain the closed-loop speed control strategy of an SRM drive with a block diagram. How is torque direction reversed for regenerative braking? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Closed-Loop Speed Control Block Diagram (4 marks):**

Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.4 of these notes. The control chain is:

1. **Outer speed loop:** The reference speed ω_ref (from accelerator pedal mapping) is compared with measured speed ω_fb from an encoder. Error → **PI controller** outputs torque reference T_ref.
2. **Torque-to-current mapping:** A lookup table or function converts T_ref into the required current magnitude i_ref.
3. **Inner current loop:** A **hysteresis controller** compares i_ref with measured phase current i_actual and issues gate pulses to the converter IGBTs.
4. **Converter output:** The asymmetric H-bridge drives the SRM phases, which produce mechanical torque and speed.

**Regenerative Braking — Reversing Torque Direction (3 marks):**

To reverse torque for regenerative braking:

- The firing angle is **advanced** so that excitation occurs during the **falling-inductance region** (dL/dθ < 0).
- According to the torque equation $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,i^2\,\tfrac{dL}{d\theta}$, when dL/dθ < 0, the torque becomes **negative**.
- The rotor drives the motor as a generator, and the demagnetization mode of the H-bridge pumps current back into the DC bus → **battery charging**.
- The control software simply **overlays a negative torque command** on the same converter topology — no hardware change required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Motoring vs Generating Zones:** Students frequently fire the phase during the **aligned** region (dL/dθ = 0), producing **zero torque**. Always check the firing angle θ_on is between the unaligned and aligned positions.
> 2. **Ignoring Saturation:** In a real SRM, L(θ,i) is non-linear. For 7-mark questions, the linear torque equation is acceptable, but for full marks, mention that saturation reduces L_max and the actual torque is lower than predicted.
> 3. **Converter Drawing:** Drawing a standard three-phase inverter (as used for PMSM/IM) instead of the asymmetric H-bridge will fetch **0 marks** for that sub-question.
> 4. **Forgetting the dL/dθ Motoring Sign:** Without explicitly stating dL/dθ > 0, the motoring justification is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD RAPID REVISION — PRINT THIS SECTION**

- ⭐ **SRM = Doubly Salient + Singly Excited + Magnet-Free Rotor.** If you forget everything else, remember this.
- ⭐ **Torque equation:** $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\,i^2\,\tfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ — memorize verbatim.
- ⭐ **Torque sign** is governed **only by dL/dθ**, NOT by current direction → unipolar converter is enough.
- ⭐ **Phase voltage** has three terms: $V = Ri + L\,\tfrac{di}{dt} + i\,\omega\,\tfrac{dL}{d\theta}$.
- ⭐ **L_min (unaligned) ↔ L_max (aligned)** — the rising slope between them is the **motoring zone**.
- ⭐ **Step angle** $= \dfrac{360°\,m}{N_s\,N_r}$ — practice with 6/4, 8/6, 12/10 examples.
- ⭐ **Asymmetric H-bridge per phase** — three modes: Magnetize (+V), Freewheel (0), Demagnetize (−V).
- ⭐ **Demagnetization returns energy to DC bus** — gives SRM inherent partial regenerative capability.
- ⭐ **Position sensor is mandatory** because torque production is position-dependent.
- ⭐ **Fault tolerant:** any phase can fail → motor still operates at reduced torque (key EV advantage).
- ⭐ **Wide constant-power range** due to series-connected nature of phase winding (no field weakening required as in PMSM).
- ⭐ **No rotor copper loss** → highest efficiency among low-cost motor topologies.
- ⭐ **No magnets** → no back-EMF at standstill → SRM is **safe for flooded/short-circuit EV conditions**.
- ⭐ **Acoustic noise and torque ripple** are SRM's main drawbacks (often tested as a 3-mark "limitation" question).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Principles and Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles and Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electric Machines Used in HEVs and EVs

In the context of **Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs)**, the electric machine is the heart of the powertrain. It functions interchangeably as a **motor** (converting electrical energy from the battery into mechanical energy for propulsion) and as a **generator** (recovering kinetic energy through regenerative braking). The "Basic Principles and Construction" topic covers the electromagnetic working principles and the physical architecture of the machines most commonly used in modern electric traction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Module 3 emphasizes machines suitable for traction duty — high power density, high efficiency over a wide speed range, robustness, and regenerative braking capability. The four principal machines studied are **DC, Induction (IM), Permanent Magnet Synchronous (PMSM), and Switched Reluctance (SRM)** machines.

## 1.2 Faraday's Law — The Heart of Every Electric Machine

Every machine discussed in this module ultimately relies on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**:

$$\varepsilon = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon$ = induced electromotive force (EMF) in **Volts (V)**
- $N$ = number of turns of the coil
- $\Phi$ = magnetic flux linking the coil, in **Webers (Wb)**
- $t$ = time in **seconds (s)**

And on **Lorentz Force** for torque production:

$$\vec{F} = I \, (\vec{L} \times \vec{B})$$

Where:
- $F$ = force in **Newtons (N)**
- $I$ = current in **Amperes (A)**
- $L$ = length of the conductor in the field in **meters (m)**
- $B$ = magnetic flux density in **Tesla (T)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Bicycle Dynamo:**
> Imagine a small dynamo attached to a bicycle wheel. As the wheel rotates, a magnet spins past a coil, changing the magnetic flux linking the coil, and a small voltage is induced that lights up your headlamp. That is **Faraday's Law in action**. Now scale that concept up — instead of a tiny bicycle light, you have a 100 kW traction motor spinning at 10,000 rpm to propel a car. The physical principle is identical; only the engineering scale changes.

## 1.3 Categorical Overview of Traction Machines

The following machines are mandated by the KTU 2024 PEMET863 syllabus for Module 3:

| Sl. No. | Machine Type | KTU Notation | Traction Suitability |
|:-------:|:-------------|:------------:|:---------------------|
| 1 | DC Machine | DCM | Historically used; now obsolete for EVs |
| 2 | Three-Phase Induction Motor | IM | Widely used (Tesla Model S/X legacy) |
| 3 | Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor | PMSM | Dominant in modern EVs (Toyota Prius, Nissan Leaf, Tesla Model 3) |
| 4 | Brushless DC Motor | BLDC | Used in e-bikes, small EVs, two-wheelers |
| 5 | Switched Reluctance Motor | SRM | Used in heavy-duty and some commercial EVs |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — "DIPS-B"**: **D**C, **I**nduction, **P**MSM, **S**RM, **B**LDC. These five machines form the core of KTU Module 3.

## 1.4 Why Construction Matters in EV Applications

A vehicle traction motor is fundamentally different from an industrial motor. The construction must satisfy:

1. **High Power Density** — More kW per kg of motor mass.
2. **Wide Constant-Power Speed Range (CPSR)** — To enable high-speed cruising without oversizing the inverter.
3. **High Efficiency** — Over a broad torque-speed envelope (not just at a single rated point).
4. **Robustness Against Vibration and Temperature** — Under-hood environment with thermal cycling.
5. **Inherent Safety During Faults** — Fail-safe behavior during short-circuit or overspeed conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** Many students assume that since PMSMs and BLDCs use permanent magnets, they are essentially the same machine. **They are not.** The PMSM has a **sinusoidal** back-EMF and is driven by sinusoidal currents (FOC), while the BLDC has a **trapezoidal** back-EMF and is driven by rectangular 120° block currents. KTU questions frequently test this distinction.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DC Machine — Principle and Construction

### 2.1.1 Working Principle

A DC machine operates on the principle that when a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences a mechanical force ($F = BIL$). Conversely, when conductors move in a magnetic field, an EMF is induced ($E = BLv$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of the DC machine as a "bar magnet pushing electrons through a wire." The commutator acts as a mechanical "switch" that keeps the current direction in each coil aligned with the magnetic field, producing unidirectional torque.

### 2.1.2 Constructional Components

The major parts of a DC machine are:

1. **Stator (Yoke and Pole Core):** Provides mechanical support and carries the magnetic flux. Made of cast iron or cast steel.
2. **Field Winding (Pole Coils):** Wound around the pole cores; produces the main field flux.
3. **Armature Core:** Laminated silicon steel cylinders mounted on the shaft; carries the armature winding.
4. **Armature Winding:** Embedded in slots on the armature core; where EMF is induced.
5. **Commutator:** A cylindrical assembly of copper segments insulated from each other; acts as a mechanical rectifier/inverter.
6. **Brushes:** Carbon blocks that slide on the commutator, providing electrical connection to the external circuit.

## 2.2 The Three-Phase Induction Motor (IM)

### 2.2.1 Working Principle

The induction motor works on the principle of a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** produced by a balanced three-phase stator winding. The RMF cuts the stationary rotor conductors, inducing a current in them (Lenz's Law). The interaction between the induced rotor current and the RMF produces torque.

$$\vec{F}_{mech} = I_{rotor} \cdot \vec{L} \times \vec{B}_{RMF}$$

### 2.2.2 Construction

- **Stator:** Three-phase distributed winding housed in laminated silicon-steel slots. The three phases are spatially displaced by **120° electrical**.
- **Rotor:** Two variants:
  - **Squirrel-Cage Rotor** — Conducting bars shorted by end rings; rugged, cheap, maintenance-free. **Dominant in EVs.**
  - **Wound Rotor** — Has a three-phase winding connected to slip rings for external resistance control; rarely used in EVs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The squirrel-cage induction motor is the *workhorse* of legacy EV designs. Its mechanical simplicity, low cost, and ability to operate in harsh environments make it a strong candidate — but it suffers from lower efficiency and higher rotor losses compared to PMSMs.

## 2.3 The Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)

### 2.3.1 Working Principle

The PMSM has a wound stator (like an induction motor) and a rotor fitted with **permanent magnets** (typically **NdFeB — Neodymium Iron Boron**, or **SmCo — Samarium Cobalt**). The stator's three-phase winding produces a rotating magnetic field that "locks" with the rotor magnets, causing synchronous rotation.

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$

Where:
- $N_s$ = synchronous speed in **rpm**
- $f$ = supply frequency in **Hz**
- $P$ = number of poles

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine two bar magnets — one fixed (the stator's rotating field) and one mounted on a freely spinning shaft (the rotor with permanent magnets). The fixed magnet "chases" the spinning one and they lock together, rotating in perfect synchronism. That is essentially what a PMSM does.

### 2.3.2 Construction

- **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core with a three-phase distributed winding, identical to an induction motor's stator.
- **Rotor:** Permanent magnets surface-mounted or interior-mounted on a steel shaft.
  - **Surface-Mounted PMSM (SPM):** Magnets glued on the rotor surface; air-gap flux density is essentially rectangular.
  - **Interior PMSM (IPM):** Magnets embedded inside the rotor laminations; provides **reluctance torque** in addition to magnetic torque — higher power density.

## 2.4 The Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)

### 2.4.1 Working Principle

The BLDC motor is essentially an "inside-out DC machine" — the permanent magnets are on the rotor and the windings are on the stator. The commutation that was previously done mechanically (by brushes and commutator) is now performed **electronically** using an inverter with rotor position feedback (Hall sensors or sensorless estimation).

The stator winding is energized in a six-step (trapezoidal) sequence. Each step energizes two of the three phases for 120° electrical, producing a rotating stator field that "drags" the magnet rotor along.

### 2.4.2 Construction

- **Stator:** Laminated core with concentrated (not distributed) windings to produce a **trapezoidal** back-EMF.
- **Rotor:** 2 to 8 pole permanent magnets surface-mounted.
- **Position Sensors:** Three Hall-effect sensors placed 120° apart for rotor position feedback.

## 2.5 The Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)

### 2.5.1 Working Principle

The SRM is the simplest of all machines in construction — it has **no permanent magnets, no rotor windings, and no brushes**. Torque is produced purely by the principle of **minimum reluctance**: when a stator phase is energized, the rotor (a salient-pole iron piece) moves to align its salient axis with the energized stator pole, thereby minimizing the magnetic reluctance of the flux path.

$$\tau = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Picture a child trying to pull a metal pin into a hole to minimize the gap between a magnet and the pin. The SRM is driven by the same principle — the rotor "wants" to align itself with the energized stator pole to reduce the magnetic "effort" (reluctance).

### 2.5.2 Construction

- **Stator:** Salient poles (e.g., 6 or 8 poles) with concentrated phase windings. Phases are independent — no physical overlap.
- **Rotor:** Simple laminated steel with salient poles and **no windings or magnets** — extremely robust and cheap to manufacture.
- **Power Electronics:** An asymmetric half-bridge converter is mandatory for phase commutation.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Equation / Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use | Units |
|:-------:|:------------------|:---------------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, K_w$ | $E$ = RMS phase EMF, $f$ = frequency, $N$ = turns/phase, $\phi$ = flux/pole, $K_w$ = winding factor | EMF of AC machines (IM, PMSM) | V, Hz, Wb |
| 2 | $E_{BLDC} = K_e \, \omega_m$ | Trapezoidal back-EMF of BLDC, $K_e$ = back-EMF constant | BLDC voltage equation | V, rad/s |
| 3 | $N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | IM, PMSM rotor speed | rpm |
| 4 | $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip of induction motor | IM rotor speed control | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\tau = \frac{P_{mech}}{\omega_m}$ | Developed mechanical torque | All rotating machines | Nm |
| 6 | $\tau_{PMSM} = \frac{3}{2} \, p \, \left[ \psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q \right]$ | PMSM torque: magnetic + reluctance | PMSM vector control | Nm |
| 7 | $\tau_{SRM} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ | SRM torque from varying inductance | SRM commutation | Nm |
| 8 | $T_{max} = \frac{3 \, V^2}{\omega_s \, X_s}$ | Pull-out torque of synchronous machine | PMSM field-weakening limit | Nm |
| 9 | $P_{gap} = 3 \, V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi$ | Air-gap power of 3-$\phi$ machine | Efficiency analysis | W |
| 10 | $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{losses}}$ | Machine efficiency | Performance evaluation | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For derivation questions, *always* start from the fundamental Faraday's law $e = -\frac{d\psi}{dt}$ where $\psi$ is the flux linkage. The integral form of the EMF equation $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, K_w$ is derived by assuming a sinusoidal flux distribution. For BLDC, the form factor is different (1.0 for square wave) and a separate derivation is expected.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — EMF Equation of an AC Machine (IM / PMSM)

### Given:
- A single coil of $N$ turns rotating at a constant angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s in a uniform magnetic field of flux density $B$ Tesla.
- The flux linking the coil varies sinusoidally as $\phi(t) = \Phi_{max} \cos(\omega t)$.

### To Derive:
The RMS value of the induced EMF per phase.

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Write the flux linkage expression.**

The flux linking a single turn at any instant $t$ is:

$$\phi(t) = \Phi_{max} \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Faraday's Law to find the instantaneous EMF for a single turn.**

$$e_{turn} = -\frac{d\phi}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}\left[\Phi_{max} \cos(\omega t)\right]$$

$$e_{turn} = +\omega \, \Phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)$$

The peak value of the induced EMF per turn is:

$$E_{turn,peak} = \omega \, \Phi_{max}$$

**Step 3 — Convert angular frequency $\omega$ to electrical frequency $f$.**

Since $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$E_{turn,peak} = 2\pi f \, \Phi_{max}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the average EMF per turn over a half-cycle.**

The average value of $|\sin(\omega t)|$ over a half-cycle is $\frac{2}{\pi}$, so:

$$E_{turn,avg} = 2\pi f \, \Phi_{max} \cdot \frac{2}{\pi} = 4 \, f \, \Phi_{max}$$

**Step 5 — Account for multiple turns $N$ per phase.**

If the phase winding has $N$ turns in series:

$$E_{avg} = 4 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{max}$$

**Step 6 — Convert average to RMS (form factor of pure sine = 1.11).**

$$E_{rms} = 1.11 \times 4 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{max} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{max}$$

**Step 7 — Incorporate the winding factor $K_w$.**

A distributed winding reduces the EMF slightly because the induced EMFs in spatially displaced coils are not in perfect phase. The **winding factor** $K_w$ accounts for this:

$$\boxed{E_{rms,ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \Phi_{max} \, K_w}$$

Where:
- $E_{rms,ph}$ = RMS EMF per phase in **V**
- $f$ = supply frequency in **Hz**
- $N_{ph}$ = number of turns per phase
- $\Phi_{max}$ = maximum flux per pole in **Wb**
- $K_w = K_d \cdot K_p$ (distribution factor $\times$ pitch factor), typically **0.95 to 0.98** for a well-designed distributed winding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This is the foundational equation used to design every AC machine winding. For a PMSM, $\Phi_{max}$ is determined by the magnet material; for an IM, it is determined by the magnetizing current. By sizing $N_{ph}$, the designer controls the back-EMF and the constant-torque speed range.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Synchronous Speed and Slip Relationship

### Given:
- A 3-phase, 4-pole induction machine supplied at 50 Hz.

### To Derive:
- The synchronous speed and the slip under various load conditions.

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Recall the relationship between electrical and mechanical revolutions.**

In one full electrical cycle, the rotating magnetic field advances by **two poles**. Therefore, in one second (i.e., $f$ cycles), the field advances by $2f$ poles. Since the field has $P$ poles, the number of complete revolutions per second is:

$$\text{rev/s} = \frac{2f}{P}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to revolutions per minute (rpm).**

$$N_s = 60 \times \frac{2f}{P} = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$

$$\boxed{N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation for $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$, $P = 4$.**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = \frac{6000}{4} = 1500\,\text{rpm}$$

**Step 4 — Define the slip $s$.**

Slip is the relative difference between synchronous speed and actual rotor speed, expressed as a fraction or percentage:

$$\boxed{s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}}$$

Where $N_r$ is the actual rotor speed in **rpm**.

**Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation: If $N_r = 1440\,\text{rpm}$, find slip and rotor frequency.**

$$s = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 = 4\,\%$$

Rotor frequency (the frequency of currents induced in the rotor bars):

$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2\,\text{Hz}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** At standstill, $N_r = 0$ and $s = 1$, so the rotor frequency equals the stator frequency. At synchronous speed, $s = 0$. For an EV's induction motor, the controller typically operates the machine between $s = 0.02$ and $s = 0.10$ for the highest efficiency — this is the basis of indirect vector control (FOC) of induction motors in EVs.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PMSM Torque in the d-q Reference Frame

### Given:
- A surface-mounted or interior PMSM with $p$ pole pairs.
- $d$-axis aligned with the rotor's north pole.
- $q$-axis 90° electrical ahead of the $d$-axis.

### To Derive:
The electromagnetic torque in terms of $d$-axis and $q$-axis currents.

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define the flux linkages in the rotor reference frame.**

The $d$-axis flux linkage includes the permanent magnet flux $\psi_m$ plus the $d$-axis armature reaction:

$$\psi_d = L_d \, I_d + \psi_m$$

The $q$-axis flux linkage has only the armature reaction (no magnet on the $q$-axis):

$$\psi_q = L_q \, I_q$$

**Step 2 — Write the per-phase voltage equations (assuming steady-state operation).**

$$V_d = R_s \, I_d - \omega_e \, L_q \, I_q$$

$$V_q = R_s \, I_q + \omega_e \, L_d \, I_d + \omega_e \, \psi_m$$

**Step 3 — Compute the input electrical power.**

The instantaneous three-phase input power is:

$$P_{in} = \frac{3}{2}\left(V_d I_d + V_q I_q\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the voltage equations and neglect copper losses for clarity.**

Assuming $R_s$ is small, the dominant terms are:

$$P_{in} \approx \frac{3}{2}\left[\omega_e L_d I_d I_q + \omega_e L_q I_q I_d + \omega_e \psi_m I_q\right]$$

$$P_{in} \approx \frac{3}{2}\omega_e \psi_m I_q + \frac{3}{2}\omega_e (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q$$

**Step 5 — Convert electrical power to mechanical torque.**

Since $P_{mech} = \tau_e \cdot \omega_m$ and $\omega_e = p \cdot \omega_m$:

$$\tau_e \cdot \omega_m = \frac{3}{2} \, p \, \omega_m \left[\psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q\right]$$

Cancel $\omega_m$ from both sides:

$$\boxed{\tau_e = \frac{3}{2} \, p \, \psi_m \, I_q + \frac{3}{2} \, p \, (L_d - L_q) \, I_d \, I_q}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the two terms.**

- The **first term** is the **magnetic (excitation) torque** — proportional to the permanent magnet flux and the $q$-axis current.
- The **second term** is the **reluctance torque** — present only when $L_d \neq L_q$ (i.e., interior PMSM). It is **zero** for surface-mounted PMSMs where $L_d = L_q$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** This is one of the most-tested equations in the PEMET863 module. Memorize the form: $\tau = \frac{3}{2} p [\psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q]$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — EMF Constant of a BLDC Motor

### Given:
- A BLDC motor with $p$ pole pairs, $N$ turns per phase, and flux per pole $\Phi$.

### To Derive:
The back-EMF constant $K_e$ relating line-to-line back-EMF to angular velocity.

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Recognize that only two phases are conducting at any instant (six-step commutation).**

The conduction pattern is $A-B$, $A-C$, $B-C$, $B-A$, $C-A$, $C-B$ (60° each). At any instant, two phases carry current in series, so the total flux cut is $2 N \Phi$.

**Step 2 — Compute the back-EMF at constant rotor speed $\omega_m$.**

The back-EMF per electrical revolution is built from the rate of change of flux linkage as the rotor sweeps from one alignment to the next:

$$E_{peak,phase} = 2 \, N \, \Phi \, p \, \omega_m$$

This represents the peak line-to-line back-EMF because two phases are in series.

**Step 3 — Define the back-EMF constant.**

$$K_e = \frac{E_{peak,phase}}{\omega_m} = 2 \, N \, \Phi \, p$$

**Step 4 — Write the motor voltage equation (steady state).**

$$V_{DC} = 2 \, I \, R_{phase} + 2 \, E_{phase}$$

In terms of the constant:

$$\boxed{V_{DC} = 2 \, I \, R_{phase} + 2 \, K_e \, \omega_m}$$

Where $V_{DC}$ is the DC bus voltage of the inverter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** The back-EMF constant $K_e$ is critical for selecting the BLDC motor for a given battery voltage. If the back-EMF at maximum speed exceeds the DC bus voltage, the motor cannot be controlled — a phenomenon called "voltage saturation." EV designers must size $K_e$ to ensure $K_e \omega_{max} < V_{DC}/2$.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Machine Selection for EV Traction

| Parameter | DC Machine | Induction Motor | PMSM | BLDC | SRM |
|:----------|:----------:|:---------------:|:----:|:----:|:---:|
| Power Density | Low | Medium | **Very High** | High | Medium |
| Efficiency | 70–80% | 85–92% | **92–97%** | 88–93% | 85–90% |
| Cost | Low | Low | High (PM material) | Medium-High | Low |
| Rotor Robustness | Medium | **High** | Medium | Medium | **Very High** |
| Field Weakening | Easy | Easy | Hard (PM limit) | Hard | Easy |
| Constant-Power Range | Wide | Medium | Narrow (w/o IPM) | Narrow | Wide |
| Inherent Sensorless | Easy | Hard | Medium | Medium | **Easy** |
| Reliability | Low (brushes) | High | High | High | **Very High** |
| EV Application | Obsolete | Legacy EVs (Tesla S) | **Modern EVs** | 2-wheelers | Commercial EVs |

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Common EV Traction Machine Architectures

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of how each traction machine interfaces with the EV powertrain components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C5[SRM]
    C1 --> D[Mechanical Coupling]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[Transmission and Differential]
    E --> F[Wheels for Vehicle Propulsion]
    G[Vehicle Control Unit VCU] --> B
    G --> H1[Resolver Encoder for PMSM IM]
    G --> H2[Hall Sensors for BLDC]
    G --> H3[No Position Sensor for SRM]
    H1 --> B
    H2 --> B
    H3 --> B
    I[Cooling System Liquid Air] --> C1
    I --> C2
    I --> C3
    I --> C4
    I --> C5
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — PMSM Construction Cross-Section

The following diagram maps the **internal constructional layout** of a typical interior permanent magnet synchronous motor (IPMSM) used in modern EVs.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Outer Stator Lamination Stack Silicon Steel] --> A1[Three-Phase Distributed Winding Embedded in Stator Slots]
    B[Air Gap 0.5 to 1.5 mm] --> C[Rotor Assembly]
    C --> C1[Permanent Magnets NdFeB Buried in Rotor V-Shape]
    C --> C2[Rotor Iron Bridge Connecting Magnets]
    C --> C3[Salient Pole Rotor Lamination]
    C --> C4[Shaft Coupled to Transmission]
    D[End Winding and Slot Insulation] --> A1
    E[Stator Housing with Cooling Jacket] --> A
    F[Position Sensor Resolver] --> C4
    G[DC Bus 400V from Inverter] --> A1
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — SRM Power Path and Phase Excitation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Link Capacitor] --> B[Asymmetric Half-Bridge Converter]
    B --> P1[Phase A Winding]
    B --> P2[Phase B Winding]
    B --> P3[Phase C Winding]
    P1 --> R1[Stator Pole A1 and A2]
    P2 --> R2[Stator Pole B1 and B2]
    P3 --> R3[Stator Pole C1 and C2]
    R1 --> RT[Rotor Salient Pole Pair]
    R2 --> RT
    R3 --> RT
    RT --> SH[Common Shaft]
    S[Rotor Position Sensor Optical or Sensorless] --> CTRL[SRM Controller]
    CTRL --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Asymmetric Half-Bridge?** The SRM's phase windings are magnetically independent. The asymmetric H-bridge allows each phase to be excited independently, allows freewheeling for soft chopping, and permits demagnetization (negative voltage applied to the winding) for fast current turn-off — critical for high-speed SRM operation in EVs.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **List the major constructional parts of a DC machine and state the function of the commutator.**

**Model Answer:**

The major constructional parts of a DC machine are:
1. **Yoke (Frame):** Provides mechanical support to the entire machine and carries the magnetic flux. Made of cast iron or cast steel.
2. **Pole Core and Pole Shoes:** The pole core carries the field winding, and the pole shoes spread the flux uniformly across the air gap.
3. **Field Winding (Pole Coils):** Wound around the pole cores; produces the main working flux when DC current is passed.
4. **Armature Core:** A laminated cylindrical iron core mounted on the shaft; carries the armature winding and reduces eddy-current losses.
5. **Armature Winding:** Insulated copper conductors placed in slots on the armature core; EMF is induced in these conductors.
6. **Commutator:** A cylindrical structure made of hard-drawn copper segments separated by mica insulation. It acts as a **mechanical rectifier** — converting the alternating EMF induced in the armature coils into unidirectional (DC) EMF at the brushes.
7. **Brushes and Brush Holders:** Carbon blocks that press against the commutator and provide the electrical interface to the external circuit.

**Function of the Commutator (specific 1-mark answer):** The commutator reverses the connection of each armature coil to the external circuit at the instant when the coil crosses the magnetic neutral axis, ensuring that the EMF at the brushes is unidirectional and that the torque on the rotor is always in the same rotational direction.

### Question 2

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
> **Differentiate between the construction of a squirrel-cage induction motor and a PMSM.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Squirrel-Cage Induction Motor | Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) |
|:--------|:-----------------------------|:--------------------------------------------|
| Stator | Three-phase distributed winding in laminated slots | Three-phase distributed winding in laminated slots (similar) |
| Rotor | Solid conducting bars (aluminium or copper) shorted by end rings — no electrical connections to the outside | Permanent magnets (NdFeB / SmCo) mounted on the rotor surface or embedded inside |
| Slip Rings / Brushes | **Not required** | Not required |
| Speed Behaviour | Runs at a speed slightly less than synchronous (slip > 0) | Runs at **synchronous speed** — no slip |
| External Resistance to Rotor | Cannot be added | Not applicable (no rotor winding) |
| Rotor Cooling | Self-cooled (no insulation needed) | Requires thermal management (magnets demagnetize at high temperature) |
| Rotor Robustness | Very high (no insulation) | Medium (magnets can be damaged or demagnetized by fault currents) |
| Manufacturing Cost | Low (simple rotor) | Higher (rare-earth magnet material) |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Pattern]**
> **(a)** Explain the working principle of a three-phase induction motor with a clear sketch of the rotating magnetic field (RMF) and derive the expression for synchronous speed. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A three-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a slip of 4% at full load. Determine the synchronous speed, the actual rotor speed, and the frequency of rotor currents. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle and Synchronous Speed (7 marks)

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

A three-phase induction motor operates on the principle of a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)**. The three-phase stator winding is supplied by a balanced three-phase AC supply. Each phase is spatially displaced by **120° electrical** around the stator periphery. Because of this spatial displacement and the **120° time-phase shift** between the phase currents, the resultant magnetic field produced by the three phases is not stationary but **rotates in space** at a constant angular velocity.

The RMF cuts the stationary rotor conductors. According to **Faraday's Law**, this time-varying flux linkage induces an EMF in the rotor bars. The induced EMF circulates a current in the shorted rotor bars (since the squirrel cage is a closed loop). By **Lenz's Law**, the direction of this induced current is such that it opposes the very cause that produced it — i.e., the rotor experiences a force that drags it in the direction of the RMF. The rotor therefore accelerates and begins to rotate in the same direction as the RMF.

The rotor speed $N_r$ can never reach the synchronous speed $N_s$ of the RMF; if it did, there would be no relative motion, no flux cutting, no induced EMF, and no torque. Therefore, the rotor always runs at a speed **slightly less** than synchronous, with the difference expressed as **slip $s$**.

**Derivation of Synchronous Speed (3 marks):**

In one complete electrical cycle, the RMF advances by **2 poles**. If the supply frequency is $f$ Hz, the RMF completes $2f$ pole-pairs per second. With a total of $P$ poles, the revolutions per second are $\frac{2f}{P}$, and in rpm:

$$\boxed{N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}\,\text{rpm}}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Number of poles $P = 4$
- Supply frequency $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$
- Slip $s = 4\% = 0.04$

**Step 1 — Synchronous speed.**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\,\text{rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Actual rotor speed.**

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \times 0.96 = 1440\,\text{rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency of rotor currents.**

$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2\,\text{Hz}$$

**Final Answer Boxed:**

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|:---------|:------|:-----|
| Synchronous speed $N_s$ | **1500** | rpm |
| Actual rotor speed $N_r$ | **1440** | rpm |
| Rotor current frequency $f_r$ | **2** | Hz |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Option 2** (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Pattern]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat constructional sketch, explain the working of a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) and derive the EMF equation. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A PMSM has 8 poles, runs at 3000 rpm on a 200 Hz supply, has 200 turns per phase, a flux per pole of 0.05 Wb, and a winding factor of 0.96. Calculate the RMS phase EMF. If the motor delivers 10 kW mechanical output at this speed, calculate the developed electromagnetic torque. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Working of PMSM and EMF Derivation (7 marks)

**Construction (2 marks):**
A PMSM has two main parts:
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core with three-phase distributed windings placed in slots — similar to an induction motor stator.
- **Rotor:** Permanent magnets (typically NdFeB) mounted on the surface (SPM) or buried inside the rotor lamination (IPM) of a steel shaft.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
When the three-phase stator winding is supplied with balanced three-phase AC, it produces a **rotating magnetic field** at the synchronous speed $N_s = 120 f / P$. The permanent magnets on the rotor experience a force due to the stator field; the rotor "locks" with the rotating field and rotates in synchronism. There is **no slip** in a PMSM (unlike an induction motor). The motor cannot start on its own from a standstill under a heavy load — it requires either a variable-frequency drive (VFD) ramping from zero or auxiliary starting aids.

**EMF Equation (2 marks):**
The flux linking each phase varies sinusoidally as $\phi(t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$. Applying Faraday's law to $N$ turns per phase and using the form factor $1.11$:

$$\boxed{E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \Phi_m \, K_w}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Number of poles $P = 8 \Rightarrow p = 4$ pole pairs
- Rotor speed $N_r = 3000\,\text{rpm}$ (synchronous, so $N_s = 3000\,\text{rpm}$)
- Supply frequency $f = 200\,\text{Hz}$
- Turns per phase $N_{ph} = 200$
- Flux per pole $\Phi_m = 0.05\,\text{Wb}$
- Winding factor $K_w = 0.96$
- Mechanical output power $P_{out} = 10\,\text{kW}$

**Step 1 — RMS phase EMF.**

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 200 \times 200 \times 0.05 \times 0.96$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 200 \times 200 \times 0.048 = 4.44 \times 1920 = 8524.8\,\text{V}$$

**Verification of synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 200}{8} = \frac{24000}{8} = 3000\,\text{rpm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Angular speed in rad/s.**

$$\omega_m = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 3000}{60} = 100\pi = 314.159\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Developed torque.**

For an approximate calculation, the developed torque is found from the mechanical output and angular speed:

$$\tau_{dev} = \frac{P_{out}}{\omega_m} = \frac{10{,}000}{314.159} = 31.83\,\text{Nm}$$

**Final Answer Boxed:**

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|:---------|:------|:-----|
| RMS Phase EMF $E_{ph}$ | **8524.8** | V |
| Synchronous speed $N_s$ | **3000** | rpm |
| Angular speed $\omega_m$ | **314.16** | rad/s |
| Developed torque $\tau$ | **31.83** | Nm |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Confusing mechanical and electrical frequency:** Some students mistakenly use $f = P/2$ instead of the supply frequency. The frequency is **always** the supply frequency unless otherwise specified.
> 2. **Forgetting the winding factor $K_w$:** The EMF equation is incomplete without $K_w$ for a distributed winding. Marks will be deducted.
> 3. **Using peak EMF instead of RMS:** The KTU question typically asks for the **RMS** value. The factor $4.44$ is the RMS form factor for a sinusoid.
> 4. **Unit of $\omega$ in torque calculation:** When you compute $P = \tau \omega$, the $\omega$ must be in **rad/s**, not rpm. Convert using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.
> 5. **Neglecting losses:** In an exam problem, the "developed torque" (from $P_{out} / \omega_m$) is **not** the same as the electromagnetic torque inside the air gap. The developed torque is slightly higher to account for rotational losses (friction, windage). For an approximate problem, $P_{out} \approx P_{mech}$, so this is acceptable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Basic Principles and Construction (Module 3)**

- [x] Every EV traction machine ultimately operates on **Faraday's Law of Induction** ($e = -d\psi/dt$) and **Lorentz Force** ($F = BIL$).
- [x] The **synchronous speed** of an AC machine is $N_s = 120 f / P$. It is the actual rotor speed for **PMSM** and **synchronous generators**, but only the field's speed for **induction motors**.
- [x] The **slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ is **zero** for PMSM and **non-zero** for IM.
- [x] **Squirrel-cage IM rotor** has no windings, no slip rings, no brushes — extremely robust and cheap.
- [x] **PMSM rotor** has permanent magnets; the torque equation is $\tau = \frac{3}{2} p [\psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q]$.
- [x] **Reluctance torque** in PMSM exists only for **Interior PMSM (IPM)** where $L_d \neq L_q$. It is zero for surface-mounted PMSM.
- [x] **BLDC** has **trapezoidal** back-EMF and is driven by **rectangular 120° block currents** (six-step commutation).
- [x] **PMSM** has **sinusoidal** back-EMF and is driven by **sinusoidal currents** (FOC / SVPWM).
- [x] **BLDC vs PMSM** — both are brushless PM machines, but differ in **back-EMF shape**, **winding distribution**, and **current waveform**.
- [x] **SRM torque** is $\tau = \frac{1}{2} i^2 dL/d\theta$ — purely reluctance-based, no permanent magnets.
- [x] **SRM rotor** has **no windings, no magnets, no brushes** — the simplest, most rugged, and cheapest to manufacture.
- [x] **AC EMF equation** is $E = 4.44 f N \Phi K_w$ — the 4.44 factor is the form factor ($1.11$) times the average-to-peak ratio ($4$).
- [x] **PMSM and BLDC** dominate modern EV traction due to **high efficiency, high power density, and regenerative braking** capability.
- [x] **DC machines** are largely obsolete for EV traction due to brush maintenance and lower efficiency.
- [x] For KTU 2024 derivations, always start from **Faraday's law** and clearly state all assumptions.
- [x] For KTU 2024 numerical problems, always **box the final answer** with proper units — this is a board-exam best practice.
- [x] The **winding factor** $K_w$ typically lies in the range **0.95 to 0.98**; never use $K_w = 1$ unless explicitly stated as a concentrated winding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regenerative Braking - Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Regenerative Braking - Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Regenerative Braking** is an energy recovery mechanism employed in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs) and Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) that converts the vehicle's **kinetic energy** during deceleration or braking into **electrical energy**, which is then stored in the traction battery or energy storage system (ESS). This process is achieved by operating the electric traction motor as a **generator**, thereby converting mechanical energy at the wheels into electrical energy through electromagnetic induction.

The fundamental principle relies on the **reversibility of the electric machine** — the same motor that propels the vehicle forward can function as a generator when driven by the wheel's rotational inertia during braking.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — The Bicycle Dynamo**
> Think of regenerative braking like the small **dynamo (generator)** that older bicycles used to power their headlights. When you pedal harder, the dynamo spins faster and generates more electricity to light the bulb. Now, reverse the situation — instead of *using* electricity to spin the motor, you let the *moving wheels* spin the motor, which now acts as a generator and *produces* electricity.

In a regenerative braking system:
- **Driving Mode**: Battery → Motor (Electrical Energy → Mechanical Energy → Vehicle Motion)
- **Braking Mode**: Wheels → Motor-as-Generator → Battery (Kinetic Energy → Electrical Energy → Stored)

It is essentially **"recycling" the vehicle's momentum** that would otherwise be wasted as heat in conventional friction brakes.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Standard Values)**
> - **Regenerative Braking Efficiency**: Typically **60% – 70%** under ideal urban driving conditions
> - **Maximum Theoretical Efficiency**: Up to **90%** for purely electric regenerative systems
> - **Standard Battery State of Charge (SOC) Range for Regen**: **20% – 80%** (regen is disabled beyond this to prevent overcharging)
> - **Counter-electromotive force (Back EMF) constant**: Denoted as **K_e (V·s/rad)**
> - **Motor torque constant**: Denoted as **K_t (N·m/A)**

---

### 1.4 Why Regenerative Braking is Essential in HEVs/EVs

| Aspect | Conventional Friction Braking | Regenerative Braking |
|--------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| Energy Outcome | Wasted as heat | Recovered as electrical energy |
| Brake Pad Wear | High (friction-based) | Minimal |
| Range Impact in EVs | Negligible | Increases driving range by **10% – 30%** |
| Environmental Footprint | High thermal pollution | Energy efficient |
| Brake Feel | Consistent | Blended with friction brakes |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Breakdown

> [!NOTE] **Core Operating Principle**
> The principle of regenerative braking is rooted in **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, which states that a changing magnetic flux through a conductor induces an electromotive force (EMF).

**Step-by-step operating logic:**

1. **Driver initiates braking**: The brake pedal is pressed, sending a signal to the **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** or **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)**.

2. **VCU evaluates operating conditions**: The controller checks:
   - Current vehicle speed ($v$)
   - Battery State of Charge (SOC)
   - Driver brake demand
   - Wheel slip conditions

3. **Traction motor transitions to generator mode**: The power electronics inverter reverses its switching sequence, disconnecting the battery as a source and reconfiguring the motor's stator windings for generation.

4. **Wheels drive the motor**: The vehicle's kinetic energy (via the drivetrain) now spins the rotor of the traction motor *passively*.

5. **Back EMF generation**: According to Faraday's Law, the rotating magnetic field of the rotor induces an EMF in the stator windings:
   $$E_b = K_e \cdot \phi \cdot \omega_m$$

6. **Electrical energy flows to battery**: The generated current is rectified (in AC motors) and fed back into the traction battery through a controlled DC-DC converter.

7. **Decelerating torque appears**: The motor, now resisting rotation as a generator, applies a **counter-torque** to the drivetrain, decelerating the vehicle.

8. **Friction brakes supplement if needed**: For high deceleration demands or low-speed conditions, conventional hydraulic brakes engage to provide the remaining braking force.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Formula Table for Board Examinations**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic Energy of moving vehicle | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $E_b = K_e \cdot \phi \cdot \omega_m$ | Back EMF in motor/generator | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $T_{gen} = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Generator torque from armature current | N·m |
| 4 | $P_{gen} = T_{gen} \cdot \omega_m$ | Instantaneous generated power | Watts (W) |
| 5 | $\omega_m = \tfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from RPM | rad/s |
| 6 | $v = \omega_w \cdot r_w$ | Vehicle speed from wheel rotation | m/s |
| 7 | $E_{recovered} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$ | Energy recovered during braking | Joules (J) |
| 8 | $\eta_{regen} = \tfrac{P_{electrical}}{P_{mechanical}}$ | Regenerative braking efficiency | Dimensionless (%) |
| 9 | $F_{brake} = \tfrac{T_{gen}}{r_w} \cdot \eta_{trans}$ | Equivalent braking force at wheels | Newtons (N) |
| 10 | $P_{max} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^3$ | Aerodynamic drag power (loss during braking) | Watts (W) |
| 11 | $a_{dec} = \tfrac{F_{brake}}{m_{eff}}$ | Deceleration achieved | m/s² |
| 12 | $SOC_{final} = SOC_{initial} + \tfrac{E_{recovered}}{E_{battery,cap}}$ | Change in battery SOC | Dimensionless |

Where:
- $m$ = vehicle mass (kg)
- $v$ = vehicle velocity (m/s)
- $K_e$ = EMF constant (V·s/rad)
- $K_t$ = Torque constant (N·m/A)
- $\phi$ = Magnetic flux per pole (Wb)
- $\omega_m$ = Motor angular velocity (rad/s)
- $I_a$ = Armature current (A)
- $r_w$ = Wheel radius (m)
- $\eta_{regen}$ = Regenerative efficiency
- $\eta_{trans}$ = Transmission efficiency
- $C_d$ = Drag coefficient (≈ **0.25 – 0.35** for typical EVs)
- $A$ = Frontal area (m²)
- $\rho$ = Air density ≈ **1.225 kg/m³**

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE] **Why this matters in industry**
> Regenerative braking is not just an academic concept — it is the **backbone of EV range optimization**. In real-world testing (WLTP and EPA cycles), regenerative braking contributes approximately:
> - **15% – 25% range extension** in city driving
> - **5% – 10% range extension** in highway driving
> - Up to **30% extension** in stop-and-go traffic conditions

This technology is also foundational for **future vehicle-to-grid (V2G)** systems, where recovered energy can later be discharged back to the power grid.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Energy Recovery During Braking

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU Board Problem):**
A BEV of mass $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$ decelerates from $v_1 = 72 \text{ km/h}$ to $v_2 = 36 \text{ km/h}$ using regenerative braking. The overall drivetrain efficiency is $\eta_{drivetrain} = 0.90$, and the regenerative system efficiency is $\eta_{regen} = 0.65$. The wheel radius is $r_w = 0.32 \text{ m}$. Determine:
1. The kinetic energy dissipated during braking.
2. The electrical energy recovered and stored in the battery.
3. The average braking force at the wheels.

---

#### Step 1: Convert speeds to SI units

$$v_1 = 72 \text{ km/h} = 72 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 20 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_2 = 36 \text{ km/h} = 36 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 10 \text{ m/s}$$

*(Conversion logic: 1 km/h = 1000 m / 3600 s = 1/3.6 m/s)*

---

#### Step 2: Calculate the change in kinetic energy

The total kinetic energy lost by the vehicle during braking:

$$\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} m v_1^2 - \frac{1}{2} m v_2^2$$

$$\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (20)^2 - \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (10)^2$$

$$\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (400 - 100)$$

$$\Delta KE = 750 \times 300$$

$$\boxed{\Delta KE = 225{,}000 \text{ J} = 225 \text{ kJ}}$$

---

#### Step 3: Calculate the electrical energy recovered

The energy recovered accounts for both drivetrain losses and conversion losses in the motor-generator-battery system:

$$E_{recovered} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain} \cdot \Delta KE$$

$$E_{recovered} = 0.65 \times 0.90 \times 225{,}000$$

$$E_{recovered} = 0.585 \times 225{,}000$$

$$\boxed{E_{recovered} = 131{,}625 \text{ J} \approx 131.6 \text{ kJ}}$$

*(Conversion logic: Of the 225 kJ of kinetic energy available, 58.5% is converted into usable electrical energy stored in the battery. The remaining 41.5% is lost as heat in the motor windings, inverter switching losses, and rolling resistance.)*

---

#### Step 4: Calculate the average braking force at the wheels

Using the work-energy theorem, the work done by the braking force equals the kinetic energy dissipated:

$$W_{brake} = F_{brake} \cdot d = \Delta KE$$

First, calculate the distance traveled during braking using kinematics. Assuming uniform deceleration:

$$v_2^2 = v_1^2 - 2 a \cdot d$$

$$d = \frac{v_1^2 - v_2^2}{2 a}$$

Deceleration $a$ can be found:

$$a = \frac{v_1^2 - v_2^2}{2 d}$$

Alternative approach using average velocity:

$$d = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} \cdot t$$

Time required:
$$t = \frac{v_1 - v_2}{a}$$

A cleaner approach is to use the work-energy relation directly:

$$F_{brake,avg} = \frac{\Delta KE}{d}$$

Using average speed $\bar{v} = 15 \text{ m/s}$ and assuming $t = 2 \text{ s}$ (typical deceleration period):

$$d = \bar{v} \cdot t = 15 \times 2 = 30 \text{ m}$$

$$F_{brake,avg} = \frac{225{,}000}{30}$$

$$\boxed{F_{brake,avg} = 7{,}500 \text{ N}}$$

*(Conversion logic: This braking force is supplied partly by the regenerative system (proportional to the motor's generator torque at the wheel) and partly by the friction brakes, controlled by the brake blending algorithm.)*

---

#### Step 5: Calculate equivalent motor generator torque

The torque applied at the wheels translates to a motor torque via the gear ratio. Assuming a single-speed transmission with gear ratio $G = 9:1$:

$$T_{wheel} = F_{brake,avg} \cdot r_w = 7{,}500 \times 0.32 = 2{,}400 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T_{motor} = \frac{T_{wheel}}{G \cdot \eta_{trans}} = \frac{2{,}400}{9 \times 0.95}$$

$$\boxed{T_{motor} = 280.7 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Vehicle Parameters on Regenerative Braking

| Parameter | Symbol | Effect on Energy Recovery | KTU Board Relevance |
|-----------|--------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| Vehicle mass | $m$ | $\propto m$ (higher mass = more KE available) | ★★★ Very High |
| Initial velocity | $v_1$ | $\propto v_1^2$ | ★★★ Very High |
| Final velocity | $v_2$ | Reduces available KE | ★★ High |
| Motor-generator efficiency | $\eta_m$ | Linear improvement | ★★★ Very High |
| Battery SOC | $SOC$ | Regen disabled when SOC > 80% | ★★ High |
| Temperature | $T$ | Affects battery acceptance | ★ Moderate |
| Gear ratio | $G$ | Affects motor speed during regen | ★★★ Very High |
| Brake pedal position | - | Modulates regen vs friction blend | ★ Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Regenerative Braking Energy Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete functional architecture of a regenerative braking system, showing how energy flows from the wheels back to the battery during deceleration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver presses brake pedal] --> B[Brake Pedal Sensor BPS]
    B --> C[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    C --> D{Battery SOC Check}
    D -- SOC within 20 to 80 percent --> E[Enable Regenerative Mode]
    D -- SOC above 80 percent --> F[Friction Brakes Only]
    E --> G[Power Electronics Inverter]
    G --> H[Traction Motor now operates as Generator]
    H --> I[Wheels rotate motor via Drivetrain]
    I --> J[Back EMF Eb induced in stator windings]
    J --> K[Rectified DC current flows back]
    K --> L[DC DC Converter voltage regulation]
    L --> M[Traction Battery Energy Storage]
    M --> N[SOC increases]
    H --> O[Counter torque decelerates vehicle]
    O --> P[Friction brakes supplement if needed]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Motor Dual Mode Operation (Motor vs Generator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DRIVING_MODE [Driving Mode - Propulsion]
        A1[Battery] --> A2[DC DC Converter]
        A2 --> A3[Inverter - DC to AC]
        A3 --> A4[Traction Motor - consumes power]
        A4 --> A5[Drivetrain]
        A5 --> A6[Wheels - Vehicle accelerates]
    end
    subgraph BRAKING_MODE [Braking Mode - Regeneration]
        B1[Wheels - Vehicle decelerates] --> B2[Drivetrain]
        B2 --> B3[Traction Motor - now acts as generator]
        B3 --> B4[Inverter - AC to DC rectification]
        B4 --> B5[DC DC Converter - voltage boost]
        B5 --> B6[Battery - SOC increases]
    end
```

---

### 4.3 Component-Level Block Diagram of Regenerative Braking System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Brake Pedal Assembly] --> M2[Brake Master Cylinder]
    M2 --> M3[Hydraulic Brake Lines]
    M3 --> M4[Friction Brake Calipers]
    M2 --> M5[Brake Pedal Position Sensor]
    M5 --> M6[VCU HCU Electronic Control Unit]
    M6 --> M7[Motor Controller Inverter]
    M7 --> M8[Traction Motor acting as Generator]
    M8 --> M9[Drivetrain and Wheels]
    M8 --> M10[Regenerated Electrical Energy]
    M10 --> M11[Battery Management System BMS]
    M11 --> M12[High Voltage Traction Battery]
    M12 --> M11
```

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation of the Schematic**
> This architecture highlights the **dual-path braking system** typical in HEVs and BEVs. The **mechanical (hydraulic) path** through the master cylinder provides fail-safe friction braking. The **electrical path** through the VCU and motor controller enables energy recovery. The **brake blending algorithm** in the VCU dynamically proportions the braking demand between these two paths.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define regenerative braking. State two of its main advantages over conventional friction braking.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Regenerative braking is an energy recovery mechanism in HEVs and BEVs where the traction motor is operated as a generator during deceleration to convert the vehicle's kinetic energy into electrical energy, which is stored in the traction battery.

**Two Main Advantages:**
1. **Energy Recovery & Range Extension**: Unlike friction brakes that dissipate kinetic energy as heat, regenerative braking recovers 10% – 30% of the vehicle's kinetic energy, directly extending the driving range of EVs.
2. **Reduced Brake Wear**: Since the electric motor provides a significant portion of the braking force, the mechanical friction brake pads experience far less wear, reducing maintenance costs and extending service life.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction and explain its relevance to regenerative braking.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Faraday's Law:** The induced electromotive force (EMF) in a closed loop is equal to the negative rate of change of magnetic flux through the loop:
> $$E_b = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$
> where $N$ is the number of turns and $\phi$ is the magnetic flux.

**Relevance to Regenerative Braking:**
In a regenerative braking system, when the wheels drive the rotor of the traction motor during deceleration, the magnetic flux linking the stator windings changes continuously due to rotor motion. By Faraday's Law, this induces a **Back EMF (Counter EMF)** in the stator windings, which drives current back into the battery. This is the fundamental electromagnetic principle enabling energy conversion from mechanical (kinetic) form to electrical form.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of regenerative braking in a Battery Electric Vehicle with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the VCU and the brake blending algorithm.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A BEV of mass **1800 kg** decelerates uniformly from **90 km/h** to **30 km/h**. The overall regenerative efficiency is **0.70** and drivetrain efficiency is **0.92**. Determine:
1. The kinetic energy dissipated.
2. The electrical energy recovered.
3. If the deceleration occurs over **40 m**, find the average braking force.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The working principle involves the following sequence of events:

1. **Driver Input**: When the driver presses the brake pedal, the Brake Pedal Position Sensor (BPPS) sends a signal to the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU).

2. **VCU Decision Logic**: The VCU continuously monitors the **battery SOC**, **vehicle speed**, **motor temperature**, and **brake pedal position**. Based on these inputs, it decides the proportion of braking to be done by the regenerative system vs. the friction brakes.

3. **Motor as Generator**: The power electronics inverter reconfigures the traction motor to operate as a generator. The wheels drive the motor through the drivetrain, and the rotating magnetic field induces a Back EMF in the stator windings.

4. **Energy Storage**: The induced AC voltage is rectified by the inverter into DC, regulated by the DC-DC converter, and stored in the high-voltage traction battery under the supervision of the Battery Management System (BMS).

5. **Deceleration Torque**: The electromagnetic interaction between the rotor and stator produces a counter-torque that opposes the rotation, decelerating the vehicle.

**Role of VCU**: The VCU acts as the brain of the regenerative braking system. It executes the **brake blending algorithm**, which determines the optimal split between regenerative and friction braking based on real-time conditions. For example, at high SOC (>80%), regen is disabled to protect the battery, and friction brakes take over completely.

**Role of Brake Blending Algorithm**: This algorithm ensures smooth, safe, and efficient braking. It calculates the **maximum regenerative torque possible** at the current motor speed and battery charging acceptance rate, then assigns any remaining braking demand to the friction brakes.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 1800 \text{ kg}$
- $v_1 = 90 \text{ km/h} = 25 \text{ m/s}$
- $v_2 = 30 \text{ km/h} = 8.33 \text{ m/s}$
- $\eta_{regen} = 0.70$
- $\eta_{drivetrain} = 0.92$
- Distance $d = 40 \text{ m}$

**Part (b.1) — Kinetic Energy Dissipated:**

$$\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

$$\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} \times 1800 \times ((25)^2 - (8.33)^2)$$

$$\Delta KE = 900 \times (625 - 69.39)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta KE = 900 \times 555.61 = 500{,}049 \text{ J} \approx 500.05 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Part (b.2) — Electrical Energy Recovered:**
