$$E_{recovered} = \eta_{regen} \times \eta_{drivetrain} \times \Delta KE$$

$$E_{recovered} = 0.70 \times 0.92 \times 500{,}049$$

$$\boxed{E_{recovered} = 0.644 \times 500{,}049 = 322{,}031.5 \text{ J} \approx 322.03 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Part (b.3) — Average Braking Force:**

Using the work-energy theorem:
$$F_{brake,avg} \times d = \Delta KE$$

$$F_{brake,avg} = \frac{\Delta KE}{d} = \frac{500{,}049}{40}$$

$$\boxed{F_{brake,avg} = 12{,}501.2 \text{ N} \approx 12.5 \text{ kN}}$$

*(Conversion logic: This corresponds to a deceleration of $a = F/m = 12{,}501.2/1800 = 6.94 \text{ m/s}^2$, which is a strong deceleration but achievable in EVs with strong regen.)*

---

#### **Question B — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain how a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) operates as a generator during regenerative braking in an EV. State the governing electrical equations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare regenerative braking with conventional friction braking in terms of **energy efficiency, brake component wear, range impact, environmental effect, and braking consistency at low speeds**.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

A **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** is the most commonly used traction motor in modern BEVs. During regenerative braking:

1. **Initial State (Propulsion)**: The PMSM is fed with three-phase AC from the inverter, creating a rotating magnetic field in the stator that drags the permanent magnet rotor along, producing motion.

2. **Transition to Generation**: When the driver brakes, the inverter stops feeding active power to the motor. The kinetic energy of the moving vehicle now drives the rotor through the wheels.

3. **Flux Variation**: The rotor's permanent magnets create a constant magnetic field. As the rotor turns (driven by the wheels), the magnetic flux linking each stator phase winding varies sinusoidally with rotor position.

4. **Back EMF Induction**: According to Faraday's Law, this varying flux induces a three-phase AC Back EMF in the stator windings:
   $$E_b = K_e \cdot \phi \cdot \omega_m$$

5. **Rectification**: The inverter (now operating as an active rectifier) converts this three-phase AC into DC.

6. **Power Flow to Battery**: The DC power is routed through a step-up/step-down DC-DC converter and stored in the battery.

**Governing Electrical Equations:**

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \phi \cdot \omega_m$$

$$V_{terminal} = E_b - I_a R_a \quad (\text{neglecting inductance for steady state})$$

$$T_{gen} = K_t \cdot I_a$$

$$P_{gen} = 3 \cdot V_{phase} \cdot I_{phase} \cdot \cos\phi$$

Where $\cos\phi$ is the power factor during generation (typically close to 1 for PMSM).

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Regenerative Braking | Conventional Friction Braking |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Energy Efficiency** | High (60–70% of KE recovered as electrical energy) | Very low (nearly 100% of KE wasted as heat) |
| **Brake Component Wear** | Minimal wear on brake pads since motor provides most deceleration | High wear due to friction contact between pads and rotors |
| **Range Impact** | Increases EV range by 10%–30% | No range impact; only dissipates energy |
| **Environmental Effect** | Reduces overall energy consumption and emissions | Generates brake dust (PM2.5 particles) and thermal pollution |
| **Braking Consistency at Low Speeds** | Poor — regen effectiveness drops sharply below ~10 km/h; becomes zero at standstill | Consistent and reliable across all speeds including standstill |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> **Common mistakes students make on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting drivetrain efficiency in calculations** — many students directly multiply KE by $\eta_{regen}$ only and lose 1–2 marks. Always multiply by the combined drivetrain + motor + charging efficiency.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** — km/h to m/s conversion is frequently done incorrectly. Remember: $v(\text{m/s}) = v(\text{km/h}) / 3.6$.
> 3. **Confusing motor torque constant and EMF constant** — In SI units, $K_e$ (in V·s/rad) numerically equals $K_t$ (in N·m/A). Do not treat them as independent variables in board problems.
> 4. **Ignoring SOC limits** — When asked "why regenerative braking is disabled at high SOC?", students often skip mentioning **battery overcharge protection**.
> 5. **In Part A, writing vague definitions** — The board expects you to mention *at least* the term "traction motor as generator" and "kinetic to electrical energy conversion" for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Regenerative braking converts **kinetic energy → electrical energy → stored chemical energy** in the battery.
- ✅ The traction motor **reverses its role** — it acts as a **generator** during braking.
- ✅ The governing principle is **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**: $E_b = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$.
- ✅ **Back EMF** in the motor/generator: $E_b = K_e \cdot \phi \cdot \omega_m$.
- ✅ **Generator torque** opposes motion: $T_{gen} = K_t \cdot I_a$.
- ✅ **Energy recovered** depends on **square of velocity**, so high-speed braking recovers more energy than low-speed braking.
- ✅ **Regenerative efficiency** typically ranges from **60% to 70%** in real-world EVs.
- ✅ Regenerative braking is **disabled when battery SOC > 80%** (or per manufacturer threshold) to prevent overcharging.
- ✅ Regenerative braking is **ineffective below ~10 km/h** — friction brakes handle low-speed and final stop.
- ✅ The **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** controls brake blending between regen and friction.
- ✅ **Energy formula**: $E_{recovered} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$.
- ✅ **Deceleration force**: $F_{brake} = \Delta KE / d$ (from work-energy theorem).
- ✅ **Brake blending algorithm** ensures smooth pedal feel and safe deceleration.
- ✅ Regenerative braking extends EV range by **10% – 30%** in urban driving cycles.
- ✅ The technology is foundational for **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** applications in future smart grids.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram
topics.content:
# BLOCK DIAGRAM OF HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Block Diagram --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs), a **Block Diagram** is a standardized schematic representation that depicts the functional architecture of the powertrain. It illustrates the **interconnection, energy flow paths, and control signal pathways** between the principal energy sources, energy converters, transmission elements, and the final drive (wheels). According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for PEMET863, the block diagram serves as the foundational visual language to describe how mechanical, electrical, and electrochemical energy streams are routed between: - **Energy Sources:** Internal Combustion Engine (ICE), Fuel Cell (FC), Battery Pack, Ultra-Capacitor, Solar Array. - **Energy Converters:** Electric Motor/Generator (M/G), Traction Motor, Power Electronics Inverter. - **Energy Storage:** Battery Management System (BMS), DC-Link Capacitor. - **Transmission:** Clutch, Planetary Gear Set, Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT), Final Drive. - **Vehicle Dynamics Block:** Wheels, Brakes, Regenerative Braking Interface. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** A block diagram is a graphical formalism where each rectangular block represents a component performing a defined energy conversion, and the arrows (unidirectional or bidirectional) indicate the direction of **power flow** (mechanical, electrical, or hydraulic) or **control signal flow**. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — The Human Body as a Hybrid Vehicle** > > Imagine the human body: > - **Stomach (Battery)** stores chemical energy. > - **Lungs (Fuel Cell/ICE)** take in fuel (food/air) and convert it to usable energy on demand. > - **Muscles (Electric Motor)** actually move the body (wheels). > - **Brain (ECU/VCU)** decides whether to use stored or freshly generated energy. > > A **block diagram** of the body would simply be boxes labelled "Stomach," "Lungs," "Muscles" connected by arrows showing energy flow. The HEV block diagram does the *exact same thing* for a vehicle — it tells the engineer **"where does the energy come from, where does it go, and which path does it take under different driving conditions."** --- ### 1.3 Why Block Diagrams Are the *First Step* in HEV Design For a KTU student, understanding block diagrams is **non-negotiable** because: 1. They form the basis of all **subsequent mathematical modelling** (Module 4 of PEMET863). 2. They allow **comparison** between Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel architectures. 3. They highlight **regenerative braking paths** (an exclusive KTU exam favorite). 4. They expose **degree-of-freedom (DOF)** analysis — a critical concept in HEV design. > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module-3 outcomes (CO3) explicitly state that students must be able to *"represent and interpret the block diagram configurations of Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel hybrid electric drivetrains."* --- ### 1.4 Standard Symbols & Conventions in HEV Block Diagrams | Symbol | Meaning | Notes | |---|---|---| | Thick Solid Arrow (→) | Mechanical Power Path | Torque × Speed | | Thin Solid Arrow (→) | Electrical Power Path | V × I | | Dashed Arrow (╌╌>) | Control Signal Path | Low-power ECU/VCU signals | | Double-Headed Arrow (↔) | Bidirectional Power Flow | Motor ↔ Generator (regen) | | Block Label `M` | Electric Motor | Traction Motor | | Block Label `G` | Electric Generator | Coupled to ICE in SHEV | | Block Label `ICE` | Internal Combustion Engine | Primary Heat Engine | | Block Label `B` | Battery / Energy Storage | High-Voltage Traction Battery | > [!NOTE] > **Bidirectional arrows** are *exclusive* to electric machines — they denote motoring (battery → wheels) and generating (wheels → battery, during regenerative braking) modes. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Canonical Block-Diagram Configurations in HEV/EVs The KTU 2024 syllabus consolidates the entire world of HEV/EV powertrains into **five fundamental block-diagram configurations**. Mastery of these five is equivalent to mastering Module 3. --- ### 2.1.1 Configuration 1 — Pure Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) **Architecture Description:** A **single energy source** (battery) feeds a **single energy converter** (traction motor) which drives the wheels. **Block Sequence:** $$\text{Battery} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Traction Motor} \rightarrow \text{Transmission} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$ **Operational Notes:** - Zero tailpipe emissions. - No ICE, no generator. - Regenerative braking is the *only* mechanism for energy recovery. - Range anxiety is the primary limitation. --- ### 2.1.2 Configuration 2 — Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV) **Architecture Description:** The ICE does **NOT** mechanically drive the wheels. Instead, it drives a generator to produce electricity, which then either charges the battery or directly powers the traction motor. **Block Sequence (Two Parallel Paths):** $$\text{Path A: } ICE \rightarrow \text{Generator} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Traction Motor} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$ $$\text{Path B: } Battery \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Traction Motor} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Favorite:** In SHEV, the ICE has **zero mechanical coupling** to the wheels. Therefore, the ICE can always operate at its **Maximum Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) point** — this is the primary advantage of the series configuration. **Mechanical Decoupling Equation:** $$P_{mech, \rightarrow wheels}^{ICE} = 0 \quad \text{(No mechanical path from ICE to wheels)}$$ --- ### 2.1.3 Configuration 3 — Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV) **Architecture Description:** **Both** the ICE and the electric motor are **mechanically coupled** to the wheels through a torque-coupling device (clutch, gear, or chain). They can deliver power to the wheels simultaneously or independently. **Block Sequence:** $$ICE \rightarrow \bigotimes \text{Torque Coupler} \bigotimes \rightarrow \text{Transmission} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$ $$Battery \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Motor} \rightarrow \bigotimes \text{Torque Coupler} \bigotimes$$ **Torque Equation at the Coupler:** $$T_{wheels} = T_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gear} + T_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gear}$$ **Advantages over SHEV:** - Lower conversion losses (no double energy conversion). - Both power sources can simultaneously propel the vehicle → **higher total power capability**. --- ### 2.1.4 Configuration 4 — Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid **Architecture Description:** Combines the best of Series and Parallel. A **planetary gear set (PGS)** acts as the power-split device. **Block Sequence:** - **Mechanical Path:** $ICE \rightarrow \text{Planetary Gear Set (PGS)} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$ - **Electrical Path:** $ICE \rightarrow \text{Generator} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Traction Motor (MG2)} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$ - **Battery Path:** $Battery \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \leftrightarrow MG2$ **Planetary Gear Kinematics (Willis Equation):** $$\frac{N_s + N_r}{N_s} \cdot \omega_s + \frac{N_r}{N_s} \cdot \omega_r = 0$$ where $N_s$ = sun gear teeth, $N_r$ = ring gear teeth, $\omega_s$ and $\omega_r$ are their respective angular velocities. > [!NOTE] > The Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS) is the **canonical commercial example** of this configuration. Expect a question on the THS architecture in your KTU ESE. --- ### 2.1.5 Configuration 5 — Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV) **Architecture Description:** A **fuel cell stack** (typically Proton Exchange Membrane — PEMFC) generates electricity from compressed hydrogen. This electricity drives the traction motor, with a **battery/ultra-capacitor** buffer for transient loads. **Block Sequence:** $$H_2 \text{ Tank} \rightarrow \text{Fuel Cell Stack} \rightarrow \text{DC/DC Converter} \rightarrow \text{DC Bus} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Motor} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$ $$\text{Ultra-Capacitor/Battery} \leftrightarrow \text{DC Bus} \leftrightarrow \text{Inverter}$$ **Critical Insight:** The fuel cell has **slow dynamic response**; therefore, the ultra-capacitor (NOT the battery, in most designs) handles regenerative braking transients to avoid fuel cell stack degradation. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula | Description | Units | Applicable Configuration | |---|---|---|---| | $P_{wheel} = T_{wheel} \cdot \omega_{wheel}$ | Mechanical power at wheels | W (Watts) | All | | $P_{ICE, electric} = \eta_{gen} \cdot P_{ICE, mech}$ | ICE → Generator conversion | W | SHEV, Series-Parallel | | $P_{batt, out} = V_{oc} \cdot I - I^2 \cdot R_{int}$ | Battery discharge power | W | BEV, PHEV, FCEV | | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{trans}$ | Total drivetrain efficiency | dimensionless | SHEV | | $T_{wheel} = i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot (T_{ICE} + T_{motor})$ | Parallel coupler torque | Nm | PHEV | | $\omega_s \cdot N_s + \omega_r \cdot N_r = \omega_c \cdot (N_s + N_r)$ | Planetary gear Willis equation | rad/s | Series-Parallel | | $P_{regen,max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{regen}$ | Max recoverable braking energy | W | BEV, PHEV | | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{P_{FC}}{LHV_{H_2} \cdot \eta_{FC}}$ | Hydrogen consumption | kg/s | FCEV | | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \frac{1}{Q_{bat}} \int_{0}^{t} I_{bat} \, d\tau$ | State of Charge | dimensionless | All battery-equipped | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note on Units:** Battery capacity $Q_{bat}$ must be in **Ampere-hours (Ah)**, and current $I_{bat}$ in **Amperes (A)**, with time in **hours (h)** — a common unit-conversion pitfall flagged by KTU examiners. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The block diagram is the **language of HEV design**. It is used to: 1. **Compare architectures** quantitatively (e.g., Toyota Prius vs. Tesla Model S). 2. **Identify control intervention points** — the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) commands only flow through blocks that have control arrows. 3. **Perform DOF (Degree of Freedom) analysis** to determine how many independent torque/speed variables exist. 4. **Serve as the input to MATLAB/Simulink models** — every KTU project starts with a block diagram. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Flow Analysis in a Series HEV **Problem Setup:** A Series HEV has the following component efficiencies: - ICE thermal efficiency: $\eta_{ICE} = 0.35$ - Generator efficiency: $\eta_{gen} = 0.92$ - Inverter efficiency: $\eta_{inv} = 0.95$ - Traction motor efficiency: $\eta_{motor} = 0.90$ - Transmission efficiency: $\eta_{trans} = 0.95$ If the fuel energy input is $E_{fuel} = 100 \text{ MJ}$, calculate the energy delivered to the wheels, $E_{wheel}$. **Step-by-Step Derivation:** **Step 1:** Apply the block-diagram power-flow chain. In a SHEV, energy passes through every block sequentially. Therefore: $$E_{wheel} = E_{fuel} \cdot \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{trans}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values: $$E_{wheel} = 100 \times 0.35 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \text{ MJ}$$ **Step 3:** Multiply progressively (KTU examiners expect stepwise multiplication, not a single calculator line): $$100 \times 0.35 = 35.00 \text{ MJ}$$ $$35.00 \times 0.92 = 32.20 \text{ MJ}$$ $$32.20 \times 0.95 = 30.59 \text{ MJ}$$ $$30.59 \times 0.90 = 27.531 \text{ MJ}$$ $$27.531 \times 0.95 = 26.154 \text{ MJ}$$ **Step 4:** Final rounded answer: $$E_{wheel} \approx 26.15 \text{ MJ}$$ **Overall drivetrain efficiency:** $$\eta_{overall} = 0.35 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.2615$$ $$\eta_{overall} \approx 26.15\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight:** This is precisely *why* series hybrids suffer from low overall efficiency when the ICE is the sole energy source — every block in the chain is a conversion loss. KTU frequently tests this realization. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parallel HEV Torque Split at the Coupler **Problem Setup:** A parallel HEV is cruising at constant speed. The ICE delivers $T_{ICE} = 120 \text{ Nm}$ at $\omega_{ICE} = 2500 \text{ rpm}$. The electric motor supplements with $T_{motor} = 80 \text{ Nm}$ at $\omega_{motor} = 2500 \text{ rpm}$ (motor coupled directly to ICE through a fixed-ratio gear $i_g = 1.5$, final drive $i_0 = 3.2$). Calculate the total wheel torque and wheel power. **Step-by-Step Derivation:** **Step 1:** Combine torques at the mechanical coupler (they add in a parallel architecture): $$T_{shaft} = (T_{ICE} + T_{motor}) \cdot \eta_{coupler}$$ Assuming $\eta_{coupler} = 1.0$ (ideal): $$T_{shaft} = 120 + 80 = 200 \text{ Nm}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the transmission gear ratio to get wheel torque: $$T_{wheel} = T_{shaft} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_{trans}$$ $$T_{wheel} = 200 \times 1.5 \times 3.2 \times 0.96$$ $$T_{wheel} = 200 \times 1.5 = 300 \text{ Nm}$$ $$T_{wheel} = 300 \times 3.2 = 960 \text{ Nm}$$ $$T_{wheel} = 960 \times 0.96 = 921.6 \text{ Nm}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate wheel angular velocity (speed reduces by the same gear ratio): $$\omega_{wheel} = \frac{\omega_{ICE}}{i_g \cdot i_0} = \frac{2500 \times \frac{2\pi}{60}}{1.5 \times 3.2}$$ $$\omega_{ICE} = \frac{2500 \times 2\pi}{60} = \frac{5000\pi}{60} = 261.799 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\omega_{wheel} = \frac{261.799}{4.8} = 54.54 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 4:** Calculate wheel power: $$P_{wheel} = T_{wheel} \cdot \omega_{wheel} = 921.6 \times 54.54$$ $$P_{wheel} \approx 50{,}264 \text{ W} \approx 50.26 \text{ kW}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Planetary Gear Speed Relation (Series-Parallel HEV) **Problem Setup:** A Toyota-THS-style planetary gear set has $N_s = 30$ (sun), $N_r = 78$ (ring), and the carrier (c) holds the planet gears. If $\omega_{ring} = 600 \text{ rpm}$ and $\omega_{carrier} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$, find $\omega_{sun}$. **Step-by-Step Derivation:** **Step 1:** Apply the fundamental Willis equation: $$(\omega_s - \omega_c) \cdot N_s + (\omega_r - \omega_c) \cdot N_r = 0$$ **Step 2:** Expand: $$N_s \cdot \omega_s - N_s \cdot \omega_c + N_r \cdot \omega_r - N_r \cdot \omega_c = 0$$ **Step 3:** Isolate $\omega_s$: $$N_s \cdot \omega_s = (N_s + N_r) \cdot \omega_c - N_r \cdot \omega_r$$ $$\omega_s = \frac{(N_s + N_r) \cdot \omega_c - N_r \cdot \omega_r}{N_s}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute numerical values: $$\omega_s = \frac{(30 + 78) \cdot 1500 - 78 \cdot 600}{30}$$ $$\omega_s = \frac{108 \cdot 1500 - 78 \cdot 600}{30}$$ $$\omega_s = \frac{162{,}000 - 46{,}800}{30}$$ $$\omega_s = \frac{115{,}200}{30}$$ $$\omega_s = 3840 \text{ rpm}$$ **Interpretation:** The sun gear (connected to Generator MG1 in Toyota THS) spins much faster than the carrier (connected to ICE) — this speed differential is exactly how MG1 acts as a **fast-reacting electrical "brake"** to control ICE speed without a mechanical clutch. This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question** pattern. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of HEV Configurations | Parameter | BEV | SHEV | PHEV | Series-Parallel | FCEV | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Mechanical paths ICE → Wheels | **0** | **0** | **1** | **1** | **0** | | Number of Electric Machines | 1 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 1 | | Battery Required (kWh) | 40–100 | 1–5 | 5–20 | 1–5 | 0–2 | | Energy Conversions (ICE → Wheel) | N/A | **4** | **2** | 2–4 (mode-dependent) | N/A | | Overall Efficiency (ICE path) | N/A | **Low** | **High** | **High** | N/A | | ICE Can Operate at Best BSFC? | N/A | **Yes** | **No** | **Yes** | N/A | | Regenerative Braking Capable? | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | | Typical Commercial Example | Tesla Model 3 | Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1) | Honda Insight | Toyota Prius | Toyota Mirai | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) ```mermaid flowchart LR Bat[Battery Pack
High Voltage DC] --> Inv[Power Electronics
Inverter DC to AC] Inv --> Mot[Traction Motor
3 Phase AC] Mot --> Trans[Transmission
Single Speed Gearbox] Trans --> Wheels[Wheels
Final Drive] Wheels -. Regen Braking .-> Mot Mot -. Regen Braking .-> Inv Inv -. Regen Braking .-> Bat ECU[VCU
Vehicle Control Unit] -. Control .-> Inv ECU -. Control .-> Mot ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV) ```mermaid flowchart LR Fuel[Fuel Tank
Petrol or Diesel] --> ICE[Internal Combustion
Engine] ICE --> Gen[Electric
Generator] Gen --> Rec[Rectifier
AC to DC] Rec --> DCBus[DC Bus
High Voltage] Bat[Battery Pack] <--> DCBus DCBus --> Inv[Inverter
DC to AC] Inv --> Mot[Traction Motor
3 Phase AC] Mot --> Trans[Transmission
Fixed Ratio] Trans --> Wheels[Wheels] VCU[VCU
Vehicle Control Unit] -. Control .-> ICE VCU -. Control .-> Inv VCU -. Control .-> Mot ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Powertrain ICE[Internal Combustion
Engine] --> Clutch1[Clutch 1] Clutch1 --> Coupler[Torque
Coupler] Bat[Battery Pack] --> Inv2[Inverter] Inv2 <--> Mot[Electric
Motor] Mot --> Clutch2[Clutch 2] Clutch2 --> Coupler Coupler --> Trans[Transmission
Manual or CVT] Trans --> Final[Final Drive
Differential] Final --> Wheels[Wheels] end Wheels -. Regen .-> Mot Mot -. Regen .-> Bat VCU[VCU
Vehicle Control Unit] -. Control .-> ICE VCU -. Control .-> Clutch1 VCU -. Control .-> Clutch2 VCU -. Control .-> Mot ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Series-Parallel (Power-Split) HEV ```mermaid flowchart LR ICE[Internal Combustion
Engine] --> Carrier[Planetary Gear
Set Carrier] Carrier --> Ring[Ring Gear
to Wheels] Carrier --> Sun[Sun Gear] Sun --> MG1[MG1
Generator] MG1 --> Inv[Inverter
DC to AC] Bat[Battery Pack] <--> DCBus[DC Bus] MG1 --> DCBus DCBus --> Inv Inv --> MG2[MG2
Traction Motor] MG2 --> RingGear[Ring Gear
Output to Wheels] Ring --> Wheels[Wheels] RingGear --> Wheels Wheels -. Regen .-> MG2 VCU[VCU
Vehicle Control Unit] -. Control .-> ICE VCU -. Control .-> MG1 VCU -. Control .-> MG2 ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram — Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV) ```mermaid flowchart LR H2Tank[Hydrogen
High Pressure Tank] --> FCStack[Fuel Cell Stack
PEMFC] FCStack --> DCDC[DC to DC
Converter] DCDC --> DCBus[DC Bus
High Voltage] UC[Ultra Capacitor
or Battery Buffer] <--> DCBus DCBus --> Inv[Inverter
DC to AC] Inv --> Mot[Traction Motor
3 Phase AC] Mot --> Trans[Transmission] Trans --> Wheels[Wheels] Wheels -. Regen .-> Mot Mot -. Regen .-> Inv Inv -. Regen .-> DCBus DCBus -. Regen .-> UC VCU[VCU
Vehicle Control Unit] -. Control .-> FCStack VCU -. Control .-> DCDC VCU -. Control .-> Inv VCU -. Control .-> Mot ``` ## 4.6 Unified Comparative Block Diagram (All Architectures Side-by-Side) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A[BEV] A1[Battery] --> A2[Motor] --> A3[Wheels] end subgraph B[Series HEV] B1[ICE] --> B2[Generator] --> B3[Inverter] B4[Battery] --> B3 B3 --> B5[Motor] --> B6[Wheels] end subgraph C[Parallel HEV] C1[ICE] --> C2[Coupler] --> C4[Wheels] C3[Motor] --> C2 C5[Battery] --> C6[Inverter] --> C3 end subgraph D[Series Parallel] D1[ICE] --> D2[PGS Carrier] D2 --> D3[Sun to MG1] D2 --> D4[Ring to Wheels] D5[MG2] --> D4 D6[Battery] --> D7[Inverter] D3 --> D7 D7 --> D5 end subgraph E[FCEV] E1[H2 Tank] --> E2[Fuel Cell] --> E3[DC Bus] E4[Ultra Cap] --> E3 E3 --> E5[Inverter] --> E6[Motor] --> E7[Wheels] end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** **Q:** Draw the block diagram of a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV) and state **one** key advantage of this configuration. **Model Answer:** **Block Diagram:** $$\text{Fuel Tank} \rightarrow \text{ICE} \rightarrow \text{Generator} \rightarrow \text{Rectifier/DC Bus} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Traction Motor} \rightarrow \text{Transmission} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$ The battery is connected in parallel at the DC bus. **Key Advantage (any one of the following):** > The ICE is mechanically decoupled from the wheels, allowing it to operate **always** at its Maximum Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) point, irrespective of vehicle speed. This results in **superior fuel economy and lower emissions** compared to a conventional ICE vehicle. *(3 marks: 2 for correct block diagram, 1 for advantage)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Q:** Differentiate between the block diagrams of a Series and a Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle in terms of (a) number of mechanical paths from ICE to wheels, and (b) number of electric machines. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Series HEV | Parallel HEV | |---|---|---| | (a) Mechanical paths from ICE to wheels | **Zero** (ICE drives only the generator) | **One** (ICE mechanically coupled to wheels via clutch & transmission) | | (b) Number of electric machines | **Two** (one generator + one traction motor) | **One** (only traction motor; acts as motor and generator) | *(3 marks: 1.5 each for parts a and b)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (1 × 14 = 14 Marks — INTERNAL CHOICE) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Internal Choice – A]** **(a)** Draw the block diagram of a Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid Electric Vehicle and identify **each block** and the role of the **planetary gear set**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A planetary gear set in a Toyota-THS-type HEV has $N_s = 30$, $N_r = 78$. When the ICE (on the carrier) runs at $\omega_c = 1800 \text{ rpm}$ and the wheels (on the ring) rotate at $\omega_r = 450 \text{ rpm}$, calculate the speed of the sun gear (connected to MG1 generator). Explain the significance of this speed value. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Block Diagram (4 marks for diagram + 3 marks for explanation):** The block diagram is as shown in Section 4.4 above, consisting of the following principal blocks: 1. **ICE (Internal Combustion Engine):** Primary mechanical energy source, mounted on the **carrier** of the planetary gear set. 2. **Planetary Gear Set (PGS):** The **power-split device**. It has three ports: Sun (→ MG1), Carrier (← ICE), Ring (→ MG2 + Wheels). 3. **MG1 (Generator):** Permanently engaged to the **sun gear**. Converts mechanical power from the sun gear to electrical power (and vice versa, for ICE cranking). 4. **DC Bus:** Common electrical node connecting MG1, MG2, and the battery. 5. **Battery Pack:** Energy storage, connected to DC bus via a bidirectional DC-DC converter. 6. **Inverter:** Converts DC bus voltage to 3-phase AC for MG2. 7. **MG2 (Traction Motor):** Connected to the **ring gear** (or via a reduction gear to the ring). Drives the wheels. 8. **Wheels:** Final mechanical output. **Role of the Planetary Gear Set (PGS):** > The PGS is the **heart** of the power-split architecture. It splits the ICE's mechanical power into two paths: an **electrical path** (via MG1 → Battery/MG2) and a **mechanical path** (via the ring gear → wheels). It also acts as a **continuously variable transmission (CVT)** without physical gear shifting, by electronically controlling the MG1 speed. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Write the Willis equation for the planetary gear: $$(\omega_s - \omega_c) \cdot N_s + (\omega_r - \omega_c) \cdot N_r = 0$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $N_s = 30$, $N_r = 78$, $\omega_c = 1800$, $\omega_r = 450$: $$(\omega_s - 1800) \cdot 30 + (450 - 1800) \cdot 78 = 0$$ **Step 3:** Expand each term: $$30\omega_s - 54000 + (-1350) \cdot 78 = 0$$ $$30\omega_s - 54000 - 105300 = 0$$ **Step 4:** Combine constants: $$30\omega_s - 159300 = 0$$ **Step 5:** Solve for $\omega_s$: $$\omega_s = \frac{159300}{30} = 5310 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 6:** State significance: > The sun gear (and hence MG1) must spin at **5310 rpm**, which is **nearly 3× the ICE speed**. This high-speed operation of MG1 acts as an **electromagnetic "speed governor"** for the ICE, allowing the VCU to keep the ICE at its optimal BSFC point regardless of vehicle speed. Without the PGS, this would require a complex multi-speed transmission. *(Distribution: 1 mark for Willis equation, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for arithmetic, 2 marks for significance explanation.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Internal Choice – B]** **(a)** Draw the complete block diagram of a **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)**. Explain the role of the **ultra-capacitor** in the architecture. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A parallel HEV has an ICE delivering $T_{ICE} = 150 \text{ Nm}$ and an electric motor delivering $T_{motor} = 90 \text{ Nm}$. Both are coupled to the transmission through a fixed gear ratio $i_g = 1.4$ and final drive $i_0 = 3.5$. Transmission efficiency is 0.94. Calculate (i) the total wheel torque, and (ii) the total wheel power if the shaft speed is 2200 rpm. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) FCEV Block Diagram and Ultra-Capacitor Role (7 marks):** Block diagram: as per Section 4.5. The principal blocks are: 1. **Hydrogen Storage Tank** (typically 700 bar compressed H₂). 2. **Fuel Cell Stack (PEMFC)** — generates DC electricity from H₂ and O₂. 3. **DC-DC Converter** — boosts/steps down the fuel cell's variable voltage to match the DC bus voltage. 4. **DC Bus** — common high-voltage electrical node. 5. **Ultra-Capacitor (or Battery)** — connected to DC bus, acts as energy buffer. 6. **Inverter** — converts DC to 3-phase AC for the traction motor. 7. **Traction Motor + Transmission + Wheels**. **Role of the Ultra-Capacitor (3 marks):** > The PEM fuel cell has a **slow dynamic response** (its output cannot change rapidly due to gas-flow, humidity, and thermal constraints). During **acceleration**, the traction motor demands a sudden surge of power that the fuel cell cannot deliver alone. The ultra-capacitor supplies this **transient power boost**. Similarly, during **regenerative braking**, the ultra-capacitor absorbs the high-peak regenerative current that would otherwise damage the fuel cell stack. The ultra-capacitor is preferred over the battery for this role because it has a **much higher power density** and **virtually unlimited cycle life**. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Wheel Torque:** **Step 1:** Combine torques at the coupler: $$T_{shaft} = T_{ICE} + T_{motor} = 150 + 90 = 240 \text{ Nm}$$ **Step 2:** Apply gear ratios and efficiency: $$T_{wheel} = T_{shaft} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_{trans}$$ $$T_{wheel} = 240 \times 1.4 \times 3.5 \times 0.94$$ $$T_{wheel} = 240 \times 1.4 = 336 \text{ Nm}$$ $$T_{wheel} = 336 \times 3.5 = 1176 \text{ Nm}$$ $$T_{wheel} = 1176 \times 0.94 = 1105.44 \text{ Nm}$$ $$\boxed{T_{wheel} \approx 1105.44 \text{ Nm}}$$ **(ii) Wheel Power:** **Step 1:** Convert shaft speed to rad/s: $$\omega_{shaft} = 2200 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = \frac{4400\pi}{60} = \frac{220\pi}{3} \approx 230.38 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2:** Compute shaft power: $$P_{shaft} = T_{shaft} \cdot \omega_{shaft} = 240 \times 230.38 = 55{,}291.2 \text{ W}$$ **Step 3:** Apply transmission efficiency: $$P_{wheel} = P_{shaft} \cdot \eta_{trans} = 55{,}291.2 \times 0.94 \approx 51{,}973.7 \text{ W}$$ **Verification using wheel-side quantities:** $$\omega_{wheel} = \frac{\omega_{shaft}}{i_g \cdot i_0} = \frac{230.38}{1.4 \times 3.5} = \frac{230.38}{4.9} \approx 47.02 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$P_{wheel} = T_{wheel} \cdot \omega_{wheel} = 1105.44 \times 47.02 \approx 51{,}977 \text{ W} \checkmark$$ $$\boxed{P_{wheel} \approx 51.97 \text{ kW}}$$ *(Distribution: Part (i) — 1 mark formula, 2 marks arithmetic, Part (ii) — 1 mark conversion, 2 marks arithmetic, 1 mark verification.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones** > > 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion from rpm to rad/s** — KTU strictly enforces SI units; leaving rpm in a final answer loses 1 mark. > 2. **Drawing block diagrams without labeling arrows** — bidirectional vs unidirectional arrows are mandatory; the examiner will deduct 0.5–1 mark if missing. > 3. **Using `|` (vertical pipe) inside markdown tables** — never use `|x|` for absolute value in a formula sheet; always use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent table syntax breakage. > 4. **Confusing MG1 (Generator) with MG2 (Traction Motor) in the Toyota THS** — MG1 is on the **sun gear**, MG2 is on the **ring gear**. Reversing them is a common 2-mark loss. > 5. **Omitting the Willis equation derivation steps** — partial-credit is only awarded for *each shown step*. Skipping directly to the answer loses 2 marks. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Block diagrams show POWER FLOW, not just connectivity** — always draw arrows and label them. - **Five canonical configurations:** BEV, SHEV, PHEV, Series-Parallel (Power-Split), FCEV. - **Series HEV:** ICE has **NO mechanical path** to wheels → ICE decoupled → best BSFC operation. - **Parallel HEV:** ICE and motor **share** a mechanical path → additive torque at the coupler. - **Series-Parallel:** Uses a **planetary gear set** as the power-split device → Toyota THS is the gold standard. - **Willis Equation** for planetary gears: $$(\omega_s - \omega_c) N_s + (\omega_r - \omega_c) N_r = 0$$ - **Bidirectional arrows** always denote the **regenerative braking** path. - **FCEV** uses a fuel cell + ultra-capacitor combo because of the slow dynamic response of PEMFC. - **Number of electric machines:** BEV=1, SHEV=2, PHEV=1, Series-Parallel=2, FCEV=1. - **ICE always on carrier, MG1 always on sun, MG2 always on ring** in Toyota-THS architecture. - **Standard KTU notation:** $P$ = power, $T$ = torque, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $\eta$ = efficiency, $N$ = gear teeth, $SOC$ = state of charge. - **Master the formulae** for series power flow, parallel torque summation, and planetary kinematics — these three alone account for ~70% of numerical questions in Module 3.

topics.title: Selection of Gear Ratio
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection of Gear Ratio

# ⚙️ Selection of Gear Ratio – Premium Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Gear Ratio Selection** in the context of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs) is the engineering process of determining the optimal transmission ratio (or set of ratios) between the traction motor and the drive wheels such that the vehicle simultaneously satisfies its **top speed requirement**, **gradeability requirement**, and **acceleration performance requirement**, while keeping the electric motor operating within its **base speed** and **maximum speed** boundaries.

Mathematically, the overall (first) gear ratio $i_g$ is defined as:

$$i_g = \frac{N_m}{N_w} = \frac{T_w}{T_m} = \frac{\omega_m}{\omega_w}$$

where $N_m$ is motor rotational speed (rpm), $N_w$ is wheel rotational speed (rpm), $T_w$ is the wheel-side torque, $T_m$ is the motor torque, and $\omega_m$, $\omega_w$ are the corresponding angular velocities in rad/s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Design of HEV):**
> Gear ratio selection is treated as the bridge between *motor characteristic curves* and *vehicle dynamic equations*. It dictates whether a chosen motor can deliver the demanded tractive effort across the entire drive cycle.

### 🌐 Real-World Analogy – "The Bicycle Gears"

Think of gear ratio selection like choosing gears on a **mountain bicycle**:
- When you face a **steep uphill climb**, you choose a **low gear** (small chainring, big cog) → easy to pedal, more torque at the wheel, but low top speed.
- When you ride on a **flat highway**, you choose a **high gear** (big chainring, small cog) → harder to start, but you can cruise at high speed.

An electric vehicle faces the **same dilemma** in a single package. The motor has a fixed torque/speed envelope, and the gear ratio decides how that envelope is *mapped* onto the road. The selection must be a **compromise** — like a single-gear bicycle designed to handle both hills and highways.

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-Speed vs Multi-Speed in EVs:**
> Most modern Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) like the **Tata Nexon EV** and **Tesla Model 3** use a **single-speed transmission (direct drive or fixed reduction)** because electric motors already deliver peak torque from zero rpm. However, multi-speed gearboxes (e.g., **2-speed in Porsche Taycan**) are gaining importance for high-performance EVs to balance top speed with launch acceleration.

### 🎯 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Bold

| Parameter | Standard Value/Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Vehicle Mass (m)** | kg | Curbs + Payload |
| **Coefficient of Rolling Resistance ($\mu_{rr}$)** | 0.008 – 0.015 | For car tires on asphalt |
| **Aerodynamic Drag Coefficient ($C_d$)** | 0.25 – 0.35 | Typical sedan |
| **Frontal Area ($A_f$)** | m² | ~ 2.0 – 2.5 m² for cars |
| **Air Density ($\rho_a$)** | **1.25 kg/m³** (at 20 °C, sea level) | Standard ISO condition |
| **Gravitational Acceleration (g)** | **9.81 m/s²** | Standard Earth gravity |
| **Drive Cycle Top Speed ($V_{max}$)** | km/h or m/s | KTU typical = 120–160 km/h |
| **Maximum Grade ($\alpha_{max}$)** | degrees or % | 30% grade ≈ 16.7° |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🧠 The "Why" Behind Gear Ratio Selection

The electric traction motor has two fundamentally important speed points:
1. **Base Speed ($\omega_{base}$)** — below which the motor delivers **constant maximum torque**.
2. **Maximum Speed ($\omega_{max}$)** — above which the motor cannot rotate without structural damage.

Between $\omega_{base}$ and $\omega_{max}$, the motor operates in the **constant power region** (field-weakening zone). The gear ratio must be chosen so that:
- At **vehicle top speed ($V_{max}$)**, the motor reaches (or just touches) $\omega_{max}$.
- At **maximum gradeability ($\alpha_{max}$)**, the motor operates at (or below) $\omega_{base}$, where maximum torque is available.

If the ratio is **too low** (numerically small) → vehicle will be slow on highways. If the ratio is **too high** (numerically large) → vehicle will struggle to reach $V_{max}$ and motor will be operating beyond safe RPM at top speed.

### 📐 The Three Constraint Equations (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Tractive Force at Wheel Required for Top Speed**

$$F_{t,top} = m g \mu_{rr} + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f V_{max}^2$$

**Step 2 — Tractive Force at Wheel Required for Maximum Grade (at low speed)**

$$F_{t,grade} = m g (\mu_{rr} \cos\alpha_{max} + \sin\alpha_{max})$$

**Step 3 — Wheel Torque Required (Tractive Force × Wheel Radius)**

$$T_{w,req} = F_t \cdot r_w$$

**Step 4 — Required Wheel Speed (at top speed)**

$$\omega_w = \frac{V_{max}}{r_w}$$

**Step 5 — Translate to Motor Side Using Gear Ratio**

$$T_m = \frac{T_w}{i_g \cdot \eta_g}, \qquad \omega_m = \omega_w \cdot i_g$$

where $\eta_g$ is the gearbox efficiency (typically **0.95 – 0.97** for a single-stage reduction).

**Step 6 — Check Feasibility:**

The selected gear ratio must satisfy **both** constraints simultaneously:

$$\frac{T_{m,peak}}{i_g \cdot \eta_g} \geq T_{w,grade}, \qquad \omega_{m,max} \geq \omega_w \cdot i_g$$

### 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Equations to Memorize for KTU Board Exam**

| # | Equation | Purpose | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_t = m g \mu_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f V^2$ | Total tractive force on flat road | N |
| 2 | $F_t = m g (\mu_{rr}\cos\alpha + \sin\alpha)$ | Tractive force up a grade | N |
| 3 | $T_w = F_t \cdot r_w$ | Required wheel torque | N·m |
| 4 | $\omega_w = V / r_w$ | Wheel angular velocity | rad/s |
| 5 | $i_g = \omega_m / \omega_w$ | Gear ratio definition | – |
| 6 | $i_g = T_w / (T_m \cdot \eta_g)$ | Torque transformation | – |
| 7 | $P_{tractive} = F_t \cdot V$ | Power at wheels | W |
| 8 | $P_{motor} = T_m \cdot \omega_m$ | Mechanical power at motor shaft | W |
| 9 | $a = \frac{F_t - F_{resistive}}{m \cdot \delta}$ | Acceleration capability (with mass factor $\delta$) | m/s² |
| 10 | $\sin\alpha = \frac{F_t}{mg} - \mu_{rr}$ | Maximum grade angle | rad |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> This exact methodology is used by **OEM design teams** (Tata Motors, Mahindra Electric, Ather Energy) during the concept stage of an EV. It determines whether a candidate motor (say, a **100 kW PMSM**) can be paired with a candidate battery pack to meet the marketing-specified $0$–$100$ km/h time and **gradeability on a 30% incline**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔬 Derivative #1: Gear Ratio from Top-Speed Constraint

The **top-speed constraint** dictates the *upper bound* on the gear ratio. Beyond this ratio, the motor would have to spin faster than its permissible $\omega_{max}$ to drive the car at $V_{max}$.

Starting from kinematic relation:

$$\omega_w = \frac{V_{max}}{r_w}$$

Multiplying both sides by the gear ratio to get the motor speed:

$$\omega_m = i_g \cdot \omega_w = i_g \cdot \frac{V_{max}}{r_w}$$

Applying the motor maximum speed limit $\omega_m \leq \omega_{m,max}$:

$$i_g \cdot \frac{V_{max}}{r_w} \leq \omega_{m,max}$$

$$\boxed{\,i_g \leq \frac{\omega_{m,max} \cdot r_w}{V_{max}}\,}$$

Therefore, the **maximum allowable gear ratio from top-speed** is:

$$i_{g,max}^{(V)} = \frac{\omega_{m,max} \cdot r_w}{V_{max}}$$

### 🔬 Derivative #2: Gear Ratio from Gradeability Constraint

For a vehicle to climb a maximum grade $\alpha_{max}$ at low speed (typically 20–30 km/h cruising on incline), the motor must deliver enough torque. The wheel torque required is:

$$T_{w,req} = F_{t,grade} \cdot r_w = \left[ m g (\mu_{rr}\cos\alpha_{max} + \sin\alpha_{max}) \right] \cdot r_w$$

The motor can supply at most $T_{m,peak}$. The torque relationship across the gearbox is:

$$T_w = T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g$$

Setting motor peak torque delivered to the wheels $\geq$ required wheel torque:

$$T_{m,peak} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g \geq \left[ m g (\mu_{rr}\cos\alpha_{max} + \sin\alpha_{max}) \right] \cdot r_w$$

Solving for the gear ratio:

$$\boxed{\,i_g \geq \frac{ m g (\mu_{rr}\cos\alpha_{max} + \sin\alpha_{max}) \cdot r_w }{ T_{m,peak} \cdot \eta_g }\,}$$

Therefore, the **minimum allowable gear ratio from gradeability** is:

$$i_{g,min}^{(grade)} = \frac{ m g (\mu_{rr}\cos\alpha_{max} + \sin\alpha_{max}) \cdot r_w }{ T_{m,peak} \cdot \eta_g }$$

### 🔬 Derivative #3: Acceleration Performance Check

Once a candidate gear ratio is fixed, the maximum acceleration is:

$$a_{max} = \frac{F_t - F_{resistive}}{m \cdot \delta}$$

where $\delta$ is the **rotational inertia factor** (≈ 1.05–1.10 for EVs). The tractive force at the wheel:

$$F_t = \frac{T_{m,peak} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g}{r_w}$$

Substituting:

$$a_{max} = \frac{ \frac{T_{m,peak} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g}{r_w} - m g \mu_{rr} }{ m \cdot \delta } = \frac{ T_{m,peak} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g - m g \mu_{rr} r_w }{ m \cdot \delta \cdot r_w }$$

### 🧮 Worked-Out Analytical Example (KTU Board Standard)

> **[Numerical Problem — Style of KTU University Exam]**
>
> Design (select) the gear ratio for an electric vehicle with the following data:
> - Vehicle mass $m = 1500$ kg
> - Wheel radius $r_w = 0.32$ m
> - Top speed $V_{max} = 144$ km/h $= 40$ m/s
> - Maximum grade $\alpha_{max} = 30\%$ (= 16.7°)
> - Rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$
> - Motor peak torque $T_{m,peak} = 250$ N·m
> - Motor maximum speed $\omega_{m,max} = 12{,}000$ rpm $= 1256.6$ rad/s
> - Gearbox efficiency $\eta_g = 0.96$
>
> **Check whether the design is feasible and find the optimum gear ratio.**

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Top-Speed Gear Ratio Limit**

Convert top speed to m/s: $V_{max} = 144 / 3.6 = 40$ m/s

$$i_{g,max}^{(V)} = \frac{\omega_{m,max} \cdot r_w}{V_{max}} = \frac{1256.6 \times 0.32}{40} = \frac{402.11}{40} = 10.05$$

**Step 2 — Gradeability Gear Ratio Limit**

Compute grade angle: $\tan^{-1}(0.30) = 16.7°$. Using $\sin 16.7° \approx 0.2873$, $\cos 16.7° \approx 0.9578$.

Numerator of gradeability formula:

$$m g (\mu_{rr}\cos\alpha_{max} + \sin\alpha_{max}) \cdot r_w$$

$$= 1500 \times 9.81 \times (0.012 \times 0.9578 + 0.2873) \times 0.32$$

Inside parentheses:

$$0.012 \times 0.9578 = 0.01149$$

$$0.01149 + 0.2873 = 0.2988$$

Multiplying:

$$1500 \times 9.81 = 14{,}715$$

$$14{,}715 \times 0.2988 = 4396.8$$

$$4396.8 \times 0.32 = 1406.97 \text{ N·m}$$

Denominator:

$$T_{m,peak} \cdot \eta_g = 250 \times 0.96 = 240 \text{ N·m}$$

Therefore:

$$i_{g,min}^{(grade)} = \frac{1406.97}{240} = 5.86$$

**Step 3 — Feasibility Window**

$$\boxed{\,5.86 \leq i_g \leq 10.05\,}$$

**Step 4 — Select Optimum**

A common KTU approach is to choose the **geometric mean** for a balanced design:

$$i_g^* = \sqrt{i_{g,min} \cdot i_{g,max}} = \sqrt{5.86 \times 10.05} = \sqrt{58.89} = 7.67$$

Or in practice, the designer picks the **higher ratio** (closer to 8–9) for better launch and grade performance, accepting a small compromise on top speed (motor still operates below $\omega_{m,max}$ at $V_{max}$).

**Step 5 — Verification**

Motor speed at $V_{max}$:

$$\omega_m = i_g \cdot \frac{V_{max}}{r_w} = 7.67 \times \frac{40}{0.32} = 7.67 \times 125 = 958.75 \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting to rpm: $958.75 \times 60 / (2\pi) = 9156$ rpm $\leq 12{,}000$ rpm ✓

Motor torque on grade:

$$T_w = 250 \times 7.67 \times 0.96 = 1840.8 \text{ N·m}$$

Tractive force:

$$F_t = \frac{1840.8}{0.32} = 5752.5 \text{ N}$$

Required for grade: $m g (\mu_{rr}\cos\alpha + \sin\alpha) = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.2988 = 4397$ N

Since $5752.5 \text{ N} > 4397 \text{ N}$ ✓ — Design is feasible.

### 📊 Analytical Matrix — Gear Ratio Selection Trade-offs

| Design Choice | Advantage | Disadvantage | When to Choose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High $i_g$ (8–10)** | Better launch, hill climb, off-the-line torque | Reduced top speed, motor hits $\omega_{max}$ early | Heavy vehicles, city buses, trucks |
| **Low $i_g$ (4–6)** | Higher top speed, efficient highway cruise | Weak low-end, struggles on grades | Sports EVs, long-range sedans |
| **Optimum (Geometric mean)** | Balanced performance | Neither best at hill nor highway | Mass-market passenger EVs |
| **Two-speed gearbox** | Best of both worlds | Added complexity, cost, weight | Premium performance EVs (Porsche Taycan) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 📊 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Gear Ratio Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Vehicle Performance Targets"] --> B["Define Vmax and Grade alpha"]
    A --> C["Define Acceleration 0 to 100 km per h"]
    D["Motor Specifications"] --> E["Peak Torque Tm peak"]
    D --> F["Max Speed wm max"]
    G["Vehicle Parameters"] --> H["Mass m and Wheel Radius rw"]
    G --> I["Cdd Aft and mu rr"]
    B --> J["Compute Tractive Force at Top Speed"]
    B --> K["Compute Tractive Force on Grade"]
    J --> L["Apply Top Speed Constraint"]
    K --> M["Apply Gradeability Constraint"]
    F --> L
    E --> M
    H --> L
    H --> M
    I --> J
    I --> K
    L --> N["ig max from Vmax"]
    M --> O["ig min from Grade"]
    N --> P["Feasibility Window Check"]
    O --> P
    P --> Q["Window Feasible?"]
    Q --> R["Select Optimum ig using Geometric Mean"] 
    Q --> S["Revise Motor or Vehicle Specs"]
    R --> T["Verify wm and Tm at Operating Points"]
    T --> U["Final Gear Ratio Selected"]
```

### 📊 Torque-Speed Operating Envelope Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["Electric Motor Envelope"] --> C["Constant Torque Region"]
    M --> P["Constant Power Region"]
    C --> CG["Low Gear High ig"]
    P --> HG["High Gear Low ig"]
    CG --> W1["Wheel Torque High at Low Speed"]
    HG --> W2["Wheel Torque Low at High Speed"]
    W1 --> R1["Used for Hill Climbing"]
    W2 --> R2["Used for Highway Cruising"]
```

### 📊 Conceptual Hierarchy of Selection Criteria

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root["Gear Ratio Selection Criteria"] --> A1["Top Speed Constraint"]
    Root --> A2["Gradeability Constraint"]
    Root --> A3["Acceleration Constraint"]
    Root --> A4["Energy Efficiency"]
    Root --> A5["Drive Cycle Compliance"]
    A1 --> A1a["Defines Maximum Allowable ig"]
    A2 --> A2a["Defines Minimum Required ig"]
    A3 --> A3a["Validates Mid-Range Performance"]
    A4 --> A4a["Shifts Selection toward Cruise Efficiency"]
    A5 --> A5a["Validates Against ECE15 or FTP75"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exam:**
> If asked to draw a block diagram of the design process in the exam, draw **three nested boxes**: (1) Outer = Vehicle Specifications, (2) Middle = Motor Specifications, (3) Inner = Output → Gear Ratio. Label the two constraint arrows clearly. This is a **3-mark guaranteed question** in module tests.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**
*State and explain the two fundamental constraints used for the selection of gear ratio in an electric vehicle.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The gear ratio $i_g$ of an electric vehicle must satisfy two primary constraints derived from the vehicle performance requirements:

**1. Top-Speed Constraint (3 Marks – 1 per point)**
The motor must not exceed its maximum permissible speed $\omega_{m,max}$ when the vehicle achieves its target top speed $V_{max}$. This gives an *upper bound* on the gear ratio:

$$i_g \leq \frac{\omega_{m,max} \cdot r_w}{V_{max}}$$

**2. Gradeability Constraint**
The motor must be able to deliver sufficient tractive force at the wheels to climb the maximum specified grade $\alpha_{max}$. This gives a *lower bound* on the gear ratio:

$$i_g \geq \frac{ m g (\mu_{rr}\cos\alpha_{max} + \sin\alpha_{max}) \cdot r_w }{ T_{m,peak} \cdot \eta_g }$$

A feasible design is only possible when the *lower bound is less than the upper bound*.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
*Why do most modern BEVs use a single-speed transmission instead of multi-speed gearboxes? Justify.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Point 1 (1 Mark):** Electric motors deliver **peak torque from zero rpm**, unlike ICE engines which need a clutch and multiple gears to stay within a narrow useful RPM band. Hence, a single fixed reduction gear is mechanically sufficient for typical driving.

**Point 2 (1 Mark):** A single-speed gearbox improves **efficiency** by eliminating shift losses, reduces **weight and packaging volume**, lowers **manufacturing cost**, and improves **reliability** (no synchromesh, no shift actuator).

**Point 3 (1 Mark):** However, for very high-performance EVs (e.g., > 250 km/h top speed combined with sub-3-second 0–100 km/h time), a **2-speed transmission** (e.g., Porsche Taycan rear axle) is used to optimize both launch and high-speed cruise efficiency.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 style]*

**(a)** Derive the equations for the *maximum and minimum* allowable gear ratio for an electric vehicle from top-speed and gradeability constraints, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An electric car has the following specifications:
- Curb weight $m = 1800$ kg
- Wheel radius $r_w = 0.33$ m
- Maximum speed $V_{max} = 160$ km/h
- Maximum grade climb: 25% slope
- $\mu_{rr} = 0.013$, $C_d A_f = 0.65$ m², $\rho_a = 1.25$ kg/m³
- Motor peak torque $T_{m,peak} = 320$ N·m, $\omega_{m,max} = 10{,}000$ rpm
- Gearbox efficiency $\eta_g = 0.95$

Calculate (i) the feasible range of gear ratio, (ii) the optimum gear ratio, and (iii) the maximum acceleration the vehicle can achieve from rest. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (14 Marks):**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

*(Assumptions — 1 Mark)*
- Neglect drivetrain inertia for steady-state analysis.
- Single reduction gearbox (one forward gear).
- No wheel slip; rolling resistance constant with speed.

*(Top-Speed Constraint — 3 Marks)*

Wheel speed at vehicle top speed:

$$\omega_w = \frac{V_{max}}{r_w}$$

Motor speed must not exceed $\omega_{m,max}$:

$$\omega_m = i_g \cdot \omega_w \leq \omega_{m,max}$$

$$i_g \cdot \frac{V_{max}}{r_w} \leq \omega_{m,max} \implies \boxed{\,i_{g,max} = \frac{\omega_{m,max} \cdot r_w}{V_{max}}\,}$$

*(Gradeability Constraint — 3 Marks)*

Required tractive force on grade:

$$F_{t,grade} = m g (\mu_{rr}\cos\alpha_{max} + \sin\alpha_{max})$$

Required wheel torque:

$$T_{w,req} = F_{t,grade} \cdot r_w = m g (\mu_{rr}\cos\alpha_{max} + \sin\alpha_{max}) \cdot r_w$$

Available wheel torque from motor:

$$T_{w,avail} = T_{m,peak} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g$$

Setting available ≥ required:

$$\boxed{\,i_{g,min} = \frac{m g (\mu_{rr}\cos\alpha_{max} + \sin\alpha_{max}) \cdot r_w}{T_{m,peak} \cdot \eta_g}\,}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**(i) Feasible Range (3 Marks)**

Convert $V_{max}$ to m/s: $160 / 3.6 = 44.44$ m/s
Convert $\omega_{m,max}$ to rad/s: $10{,}000 \times 2\pi / 60 = 1047.2$ rad/s

Top-speed bound:

$$i_{g,max} = \frac{1047.2 \times 0.33}{44.44} = \frac{345.58}{44.44} = 7.78$$

Grade angle: $\tan^{-1}(0.25) = 14.04°$; $\sin\alpha = 0.2425$, $\cos\alpha = 0.9701$

Gradeability numerator:

$$1800 \times 9.81 \times (0.013 \times 0.9701 + 0.2425) \times 0.33$$

$$= 17{,}658 \times (0.01261 + 0.2425) \times 0.33$$

$$= 17{,}658 \times 0.25511 \times 0.33 = 1486.6 \text{ N·m}$$

Gradeability denominator:

$$T_{m,peak} \cdot \eta_g = 320 \times 0.95 = 304 \text{ N·m}$$

$$i_{g,min} = \frac{1486.6}{304} = 4.89$$

Feasible window: $4.89 \leq i_g \leq 7.78$ ✓

**(ii) Optimum Gear Ratio (2 Marks)**

$$i_g^* = \sqrt{4.89 \times 7.78} = \sqrt{38.04} = 6.17$$

Round up to nearest practical ratio: **$i_g \approx 6.5$**

**(iii) Maximum Acceleration from Rest (2 Marks)**

At launch, motor is at base speed, delivering peak torque. Wheel tractive force:

$$F_t = \frac{T_{m,peak} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g}{r_w} = \frac{320 \times 6.5 \times 0.95}{0.33} = \frac{1976}{0.33} = 5987.9 \text{ N}$$

Rolling resistance at near-zero speed: $\approx m g \mu_{rr} = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.013 = 229.5$ N

Taking $\delta \approx 1.08$ (rotational inertia factor):

$$a_{max} = \frac{5987.9 - 229.5}{1800 \times 1.08} = \frac{5758.4}{1944} = 2.96 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Convert to $0$–$100$ km/h time (theoretical): $100$ km/h $= 27.78$ m/s

$$t = \frac{V}{a} = \frac{27.78}{2.96} \approx 9.4 \text{ seconds}$$

(Actual time will be longer due to motor torque tapering as speed rises.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 style]*

**(a)** With the help of a clearly labeled block diagram, describe the complete design methodology for selecting the gear ratio of a hybrid electric vehicle. Discuss the role of each performance constraint. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A HEV uses a 75 kW PMSM with peak torque of 240 N·m and maximum speed of 14,000 rpm. The vehicle target top speed is 180 km/h, and it must climb a 20% grade. Vehicle mass = 1400 kg, wheel radius = 0.31 m, $\mu_{rr} = 0.011$, $\eta_g = 0.96$. Determine if a single-speed transmission is feasible, and if so, recommend the optimum ratio. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (14 Marks):**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Methodology (7 Marks)**

*(Suggested Block Diagram — 3 Marks)*

```
[Vehicle Specs] → [Drive Cycle Targets] → [Motor Specs]
        ↓                  ↓                   ↓
        └──→ [Top-Speed Constraint] ←──────────┘
                      ↓
              [Gear Ratio Upper Limit ig_max]
                      
        └──→ [Gradeability Constraint] ←──────┘
                      ↓
              [Gear Ratio Lower Limit ig_min]
                      
              [Feasibility Window Check]
                      ↓
              [Optimum ig Selected]
                      ↓
              [Acceleration Verification]
```

*(Discussion — 4 Marks)*

- **Top-Speed Constraint**: Limits $i_g$ from above (motor RPM protection).
- **Gradeability Constraint**: Limits $i_g$ from below (torque availability).
- **Acceleration Constraint**: Validates the chosen $i_g$ gives acceptable $0$–$100$ km/h time within the constant-torque region of the motor.
- **Efficiency Constraint**: Ensures the chosen ratio keeps the motor near its best-efficiency operating point during typical cruise (50–80 km/h).

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)**

Convert: $V_{max} = 180/3.6 = 50$ m/s; $\omega_{m,max} = 14000 \times 2\pi/60 = 1466.08$ rad/s

Grade: 20% → $\alpha = 11.31°$; $\sin\alpha = 0.1961$, $\cos\alpha = 0.9806$

**(i) Top-speed bound (2 Marks):**

$$i_{g,max} = \frac{1466.08 \times 0.31}{50} = \frac{454.48}{50} = 9.09$$

**(ii) Gradeability bound (2 Marks):**

Numerator: $1400 \times 9.81 \times (0.011 \times 0.9806 + 0.1961) \times 0.31$

Inside parentheses: $0.01079 + 0.1961 = 0.2069$

$$= 1400 \times 9.81 \times 0.2069 \times 0.31 = 13{,}734 \times 0.2069 \times 0.31 = 880.8 \text{ N·m}$$

Denominator: $240 \times 0.96 = 230.4$ N·m

$$i_{g,min} = \frac{880.8}{230.4} = 3.82$$

**(iii) Feasibility (1 Mark):**

$$3.82 \leq i_g \leq 9.09 \quad ✓ \text{ Single-speed is feasible}$$

**(iv) Optimum (2 Marks):**

$$i_g^* = \sqrt{3.82 \times 9.09} = \sqrt{34.72} = 5.89$$

**Recommended gear ratio: $i_g \approx 6.0$ (rounded to nearest practical value).**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 Marks):** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s, or rpm to rad/s. Always check unit consistency. $V_{max}$ in gear-ratio formula **must** be in m/s.
> 2. **Misidentifying the Constraint (–2 Marks):** Some students confuse which constraint gives the upper bound and which gives the lower bound. Memorize: *Top-speed → upper bound; Gradeability → lower bound.*
> 3. **Forgetting Gearbox Efficiency (–1 Mark):** Always include $\eta_g$ in the torque transformation formula.
> 4. **Wrong Trigonometric Values (–1 Mark):** When a problem states "30% grade," it means $\tan\alpha = 0.30$, not $\alpha = 30°$. Use $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.30) = 16.7°$.
> 5. **Skipping the Verification Step (–1 Mark):** KTU examiners **reward** students who verify $i_g$ by plugging it back to check motor RPM and tractive force.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Gear Ratio Definition**: $i_g = \omega_m / \omega_w = T_w / (T_m \cdot \eta_g)$.
- ✅ **Two constraints** govern selection: top-speed (upper bound) and gradeability (lower bound).
- ✅ **Top-Speed Formula**: $i_{g,max} = \omega_{m,max} \cdot r_w / V_{max}$.
- ✅ **Gradeability Formula**: $i_{g,min} = m g (\mu_{rr}\cos\alpha_{max} + \sin\alpha_{max}) \cdot r_w / (T_{m,peak} \cdot \eta_g)$.
- ✅ **Feasibility Condition**: $i_{g,min} < i_{g,max}$ — if violated, redesign motor or vehicle.
- ✅ **Optimum Ratio**: Geometric mean $i_g^* = \sqrt{i_{g,min} \cdot i_{g,max}}$ is a balanced KTU-style answer.
- ✅ **Standard Values**: $\rho_a = 1.25$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\eta_g = 0.95$–$0.97$.
- ✅ **Grade Conversion**: 30% grade means $\tan\alpha = 0.30$, NOT $\alpha = 30°$.
- ✅ **Single-speed vs Multi-speed**: EVs prefer single-speed due to wide motor torque band; multi-speed is reserved for performance flagships.
- ✅ **Always include verification** of motor RPM and tractive force in board exam answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Kinds of Gearboxes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Different Kinds of Gearboxes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Gearbox in an HEV/EV Context?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **gearbox (transmission)** is a mechanical power transmission system that modifies the torque–speed characteristics delivered by the prime mover (ICE in HEVs, traction motor in EVs) to the driving wheels, by selectively changing the gear ratio between the input shaft and the output shaft. In Hybrid and Electric Vehicles, the gearbox architecture is fundamentally different from conventional ICE vehicles because electric motors deliver peak torque at **zero RPM**, eliminating the need for multi-speed gearboxes in most pure-EV applications.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Bicycle Gear" Mental Model

Imagine riding a bicycle up a steep hill. When the slope becomes steep, you shift to a **low gear** (small chainring, large sprocket). This trades speed for **torque** — your legs turn faster, but the wheel turns slower with greater pushing force. Conversely, on a flat road, you shift to a **high gear** to cruise efficiently at speed with less pedalling effort.

A vehicle gearbox does exactly the same thing:
- **Low gear ratio (numerically high, e.g., 4:1)** → Multiplies torque, reduces speed → for launch/acceleration/hill climbing.
- **High gear ratio (numerically low, e.g., 1:1 or overdrive)** → Less torque, higher speed → for cruising.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant / Metric:** Gear ratio $i = \dfrac{N_{in}}{N_{out}} = \dfrac{\omega_{in}}{\omega_{out}} = \dfrac{T_{out}}{T_{in}}$ (for an ideal lossless gear pair), where $N$ = number of teeth, $\omega$ = angular speed, $T$ = torque. Typical automotive ratios lie between **2.5:1 and 4.5:1** for the lowest gear, and as low as **0.6:1 (overdrive)** for the top gear.

### 1.2 Why Gearboxes Matter in HEVs/EVs — The Prime Mover Dictates the Design

| Prime Mover | Torque Curve Characteristic | Typical Gearbox Need |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** | Zero torque at 0 RPM, narrow power band (~1500–6000 RPM) | **Multi-speed (5–10 gears)** absolutely required |
| **Electric Traction Motor** | Peak torque at 0 RPM, flat torque curve up to base speed, then constant power | **Single-speed reducer** is often sufficient |
| **Hybrid (ICE + Motor)** | Combined characteristic — complex | **Power-split planetary** or specialized AT/DCT |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU PEMET863 Module 3, you must distinguish between gearboxes for **conventional hybrids (HEVs)**, **plug-in hybrids (PHEVs)**, and **battery electric vehicles (BEVs)** — each has a fundamentally different transmission requirement.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Automotive Gearboxes (Taxonomy)

```
Automotive Transmissions
├── Manual Transmission (MT)
│   ├── Sliding Mesh
│   └── Constant Mesh / Synchromesh
├── Automatic Transmission (AT)
│   ├── Torque Converter + Planetary Gearset
│   └── CVT (Continuously Variable Transmission)
├── Automated Manual Transmission (AMT)
├── Dual Clutch Transmission (DCT)
├── EV Single-Speed Reducer
└── Hybrid Power-Split Devices (Planetary e-CVT)
```

## 2.2 Detailed Operating Principle — Block-by-Block

### A. Manual Transmission (MT)
- Driver-operated clutch + gear lever.
- **Constant-mesh** type uses synchronizer rings to match shaft speeds before engagement.
- Still common in entry-level ICE cars; **rare in HEVs/EVs** because the electric motor handles the launch.

### B. Automatic Transmission (Conventional AT)
- Uses a **hydraulic torque converter** (fluid coupling) to replace the friction clutch.
- Internally uses a **Ravigneaux or Simpson planetary gearset** with multiple friction bands and clutches to achieve 4–10 forward ratios.
- **Pros:** Smooth, refined, no driver input.
- **Cons:** ~10–15% parasitic losses, heavy, complex hydraulic control.

### C. Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)
- Uses a **steel belt/push-belt running between two variable-diameter pulleys** (or a toroidal traction drive).
- The effective gear ratio $i_{eff}$ varies **infinitely** between minimum and maximum.
- Mathematically:

$$i_{eff} = \frac{R_{drive\_pulley}}{R_{driven\_pulley}}$$

where $R$ is the effective pulley radius (set by hydraulic pressure).

- **Application in HEVs:** Used in **Honda i-MMD** system (parallel hybrid).

### D. Automated Manual Transmission (AMT)
- A conventional manual gearbox with **actuators replacing the clutch pedal and gear lever**.
- Faster shifts than a human (modern AMTs shift in ~150–300 ms).
- **Used in:** Some mild hybrids (Suzuki SHVS early generations), Formula race cars.
- **Drawback:** Torque interruption during shifts — undesirable in traction-motor-driven EVs.

### E. Dual Clutch Transmission (DCT)
- Two clutches, two input shafts, **odd gears on one shaft, even gears on the other**.
- Pre-selects the next gear → near-instantaneous shift.
- **Examples:** VW DSG, Porsche PDK, used in **Audi e-tron GT (2-speed rear)**.

### F. EV Single-Speed Reducer
- One fixed gear ratio (typically **8–12:1** reduction).
- No clutch, no torque converter.
- **Why single speed works for EVs:**
  1. Electric motor has flat torque curve from 0 RPM.
  2. Power = $T \times \omega$ — the constant-power region of the motor extends over a wide speed range.
  3. Eliminates shifting losses, complexity, and cost.

### G. Hybrid Power-Split Device (Planetary e-CVT — Toyota THS)
- The cornerstone of the **Toyota Hybrid System (THS)**.
- Uses an **Epicyclic/Planetary gear set** with three nodes:
  - **Sun gear** → connected to **Generator (MG1)**
  - **Planet carrier** → connected to **ICE**
  - **Ring gear** → connected to **Traction Motor (MG2)** and **final drive**
- This is **not a gearbox** in the traditional sense — it is a **mechanical power-split continuously variable transmission** that allows ICE and MG2 to operate at optimal speeds simultaneously, while MG1 acts as a generator/runner.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbols | Units / Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ | $N$ = teeth, $\omega$ = rad/s, $T$ = N·m | $i \in [0.6,\ 5.0]$ | Basic gear ratio (lossless ideal) |
| 2 | $\eta_{gear} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}}$ | $\eta$ = efficiency | $\eta \approx 0.96$–$0.98$ per mesh | Spur gear mesh efficiency |
| 3 | $P_{required} = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{\eta_{drivetrain}}$ | $F$ = tractive force (N), $v$ = velocity (m/s) | — | Vehicle power demand |
| 4 | $T_{wheel} = T_{motor} \cdot i_{gear} \cdot i_{final}$ | $i_{final}$ = differential ratio | — | Torque at wheels |
| 5 | $N_{sun} + N_{ring} = N_{carrier}$ (constraint for simple planetary) | $N$ = teeth count | — | Kinematic constraint of planetary gear |
| 6 | $\omega_{carrier} = \dfrac{N_{sun}\cdot\omega_{sun} + N_{ring}\cdot\omega_{ring}}{N_{sun} + N_{ring}}$ | $\omega$ = rad/s | — | Willis equation for planetary |
| 7 | $v_{max} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_w \cdot N_{max,motor}}{60 \cdot i_{gear} \cdot i_{final}}$ | $D_w$ = wheel diameter (m) | — | Max vehicle speed from motor RPM |
| 8 | $T_{max,launch} = T_{peak,motor} \cdot i_{gear} \cdot i_{final}$ | N·m | — | Max starting torque |
| 9 | $R_{CVT} = \dfrac{r_{drive}}{r_{driven}}$, varies continuously | — | $R \in [2.5,\ 0.5]$ | CVT ratio envelope |
| 10 | $\dot{W}_{planetary} = 0$ (no power loss in ideal mesh) | W | — | Energy conservation in planetary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The planetary gear set is **the single most important kinematic device** in modern hybrid powertrains. Almost every KTU question on HEV transmissions tests either the **Willis equation** or the **power flow diagram** of a planetary set. Master equation (6) above — it is the mathematical heart of Toyota THS, Ford Hybrid, and GM Voltec.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fundamental Gear Ratio and Torque/Speed Trade-off

We start from the **energy conservation principle** applied to an ideal gear pair (no friction losses).

**Step 1:** Define input power and output power.

$$P_{in} = T_1 \cdot \omega_1$$

**Step 2:** Define output power.

$$P_{out} = T_2 \cdot \omega_2$$

**Step 3:** Apply ideal energy conservation (no losses).

$$P_{in} = P_{out}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expressions.

$$T_1 \cdot \omega_1 = T_2 \cdot \omega_2$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to form the ratio.

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}$$

**Step 6:** Define gear ratio $i$ as the ratio of input speed to output speed.

$$i = \frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore, the output torque is:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot i = T_1 \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** When the input gear has **more teeth** than the output gear ($N_1 > N_2$, so $i > 1$), the output shaft turns **slower** but with **more torque** — this is **gear reduction**, the typical first-gear behaviour in a car.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Willis Equation for a Simple Planetary Gearset

This is **the most critical derivation for KTU exams on HEV gearboxes**. A simple planetary gearset has:
- 1 Sun gear (S)
- 1 Ring gear (R)
- 1 Planet carrier (C) carrying 3 (or more) planet gears

**Step 1:** Consider the geometry. A planet gear rolls between the sun and the ring. Let the pitch radii be $r_S$, $r_R$.

**Step 2:** The pitch radius of the ring equals the sum of the sun radius and twice the planet radius.

$$r_R = r_S + 2r_P$$

**Step 3:** Number of teeth is proportional to pitch radius (same module). Therefore:

$$N_R = N_S + 2N_P$$

**Step 4:** Consider the velocity at the contact point between the sun and a planet. If the carrier is stationary, the sun's pitch-point velocity equals the planet's pitch-point velocity.

$$\omega_S \cdot r_S = -\omega_P \cdot r_P$$

(The negative sign is because the planet spins in the opposite direction to the sun when the carrier is fixed.)

**Step 5:** With the carrier rotating at $\omega_C$, the velocity of the sun's center is $\omega_C \cdot r_S$, and similarly for the ring.

**Step 6:** Use the relative motion method. In the carrier's reference frame:

$$\omega_S^{rel} = \omega_S - \omega_C, \quad \omega_R^{rel} = \omega_R - \omega_C$$

**Step 7:** The contact between sun and planet in the carrier frame gives:

$$(\omega_S - \omega_C) \cdot r_S = -(\omega_P - \omega_C) \cdot r_P$$

**Step 8:** Similarly, planet-to-ring contact in the carrier frame (no sign change because ring is external gear):

$$(\omega_P - \omega_C) \cdot r_P = -(\omega_R - \omega_C) \cdot r_R$$

**Step 9:** Eliminate $\omega_P$ from both equations. After algebraic manipulation (eliminating $r_P$ via $r_P = (r_R - r_S)/2$ and substituting), we obtain the **Willis equation**:

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{\omega_S - \omega_C}{\omega_R - \omega_C} = -\dfrac{N_R}{N_S}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The negative sign** indicates that sun and ring rotate in **opposite directions** when the carrier is held fixed. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

**Step 10:** Solve for the carrier speed:

$$\omega_C = \frac{N_S \cdot \omega_S + N_R \cdot \omega_R}{N_S + N_R}$$

This is equation (6) in your cheat sheet — memorize it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Flow in a Power-Split Hybrid (Toyota THS)

In the Toyota Hybrid System, the carrier is connected to the ICE, the sun to MG1 (generator), and the ring to MG2 (traction motor) + final drive.

**Step 1:** Let $\omega_{ICE} = \omega_C$, $\omega_{MG1} = \omega_S$, $\omega_{out} = \omega_R$.

**Step 2:** From the Willis equation:

$$\omega_C = \frac{N_S \omega_S + N_R \omega_R}{N_S + N_R}$$

**Step 3:** The mechanical power flowing into the planetary set is the sum of power at each port (since the mesh is lossless).

$$P_{ICE} = P_{MG1} + P_{out}$$

**Step 4:** The ICE power:

$$P_{ICE} = T_{ICE} \cdot \omega_C$$

**Step 5:** The MG1 acts as a generator when $\omega_S$ is opposite in sign to $\omega_C$, so it absorbs power:

$$P_{MG1} = T_{MG1} \cdot \omega_S$$

**Step 6:** The output power to the wheels (via MG2 plus mechanical path):

$$P_{out} = T_{out} \cdot \omega_R$$

**Step 7:** From the Willis relation, we can write:

$$T_{MG1} = -T_{ICE} \cdot \frac{N_S}{N_S + N_R}, \quad T_{out} = T_{ICE} \cdot \frac{N_R}{N_S + N_R}$$

(These follow from force balance on the planet carrier, since $T \propto N$ in an ideal mesh.)

**Step 8:** Therefore, the **torque split ratio** is purely a function of the gear teeth numbers:

$$\frac{T_{out}}{T_{ICE}} = \frac{N_R}{N_S + N_R} = 1 + k$$

where $k = N_S/N_R$ is the **kinematic ratio** of the planetary.

**Step 9:** For Toyota's typical design, $N_R/N_S \approx 2.6$, so:

$$\frac{T_{out}}{T_{ICE}} \approx \frac{2.6}{1 + 2.6} = 0.722$$

**Step 10:** The remaining ~28% of ICE torque flows through MG1 → electrical path → MG2 → wheels. This is the **electrical power-split** that gives the e-CVT its name.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Gearbox Type | Efficiency | Torque Interrupt | Complexity | Weight | Cost | HEV/EV Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Manual (MT) | 96–98% | Yes (clutch) | Low | Low | Low | ❌ Not used in EVs; rare in HEVs |
| Torque-Converter AT | 85–90% | Minimal | High | Heavy | High | ✅ Used in some HEVs (Ford, GM) |
| CVT | 88–92% | None (smooth) | Medium | Medium | Medium | ✅ Honda i-MMD |
| AMT | 95–97% | Yes (shift) | Low | Low | Low | ⚠ Mild hybrids, low-cost |
| DCT | 94–97% | Near-zero | High | Medium | High | ✅ Audi e-tron, Porsche Taycan |
| Single-Speed Reducer | 97–98% | None | Very Low | Very Low | Very Low | ✅✅ **Best for pure EVs** |
| Planetary e-CVT (Power-Split) | 90–94% | None | High | Medium | Medium | ✅✅ **Best for full HEVs** (Toyota THS) |

---
