# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]** Why is a single-speed gearbox often sufficient for a pure Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV), even though a multi-speed gearbox is mandatory in a conventional ICE car?

**Model Answer:**

A pure BEV uses a single-speed reducer because an electric traction motor exhibits fundamentally different torque characteristics from an ICE:
1. **Peak torque at zero RPM** — the motor can launch the vehicle from rest without stalling, removing the need for a low "first gear" launch ratio.
2. **Flat, wide constant-power region** — the motor delivers rated power over a broad speed range (typically 1:4 to 1:6 ratio between base speed and max speed), allowing a single fixed ratio to cover both launch and cruise.
3. **No synchronization requirement** — multi-speed gearboxes exist in ICE cars mainly to keep the engine near its narrow peak-power band; the EV motor has no such band.
4. **Efficiency, weight, cost, and reliability** are all improved by eliminating shift elements, clutches, and synchronizers.

Therefore, a fixed reduction ratio of typically **8:1 to 12:1** is the optimal engineering trade-off for most production EVs.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Q2. [3 Marks]** Define "e-CVT" as used in the Toyota Hybrid System. What kinematic element realizes the continuously variable behaviour?

**Model Answer:**

**e-CVT (electronic Continuously Variable Transmission)** is a power-split device used in the Toyota Hybrid System (THS) that provides an effectively infinite number of ratios between the ICE and the wheels — without any traditional gear shifting.

It is realized using an **epicyclic (planetary) gear set** with three ports:
- **Sun gear** ↔ MG1 (generator)
- **Planet carrier** ↔ ICE
- **Ring gear** ↔ MG2 (traction motor) and final drive

The "continuously variable" behaviour arises because MG1 is **electrically controlled** — by varying MG1's speed (and thus the sun gear's speed) the Willis equation forces the carrier (ICE) to operate at its most efficient point while MG2 delivers whatever wheel speed the driver demands. The mechanism has no friction clutches, no synchromesh, and no step changes — it is "continuously variable" by virtue of the planetary kinematics plus the electrical control loop.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 3 Model — Full 14 Marks]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a simple planetary gear set. Derive the Willis equation relating the angular velocities of the sun, ring, and carrier.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a Toyota Hybrid System planetary gear set, the sun gear has 30 teeth, the ring gear has 78 teeth, and the carrier is driven by the ICE at 2000 RPM. If the ring gear (output to MG2 + wheels) is rotating at 1200 RPM in the same direction as the carrier, determine (i) the speed of the sun gear (MG1) and (ii) the direction of MG1's rotation. State whether MG1 acts as a motor or generator in this condition.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Working of a Simple Planetary Gear Set (3 marks):**

A simple planetary gear set consists of:
- One **sun gear** at the centre,
- Three (or more) **planet gears** mounted on a common **planet carrier**, equally spaced around the sun,
- An outer **ring (annulus) gear** with internal teeth that mesh with the planets.

All three elements (sun, carrier, ring) are free to rotate independently. The planets do not have a fixed axis — they "walk around" the sun, like planets orbit a star (hence the name "planetary").

**Step 1:** The fundamental relation between teeth (geometric constraint):

$$N_R = N_S + 2N_P$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Willis (kinematic) condition by considering the velocity at the pitch point of the sun-planet mesh in the rotating carrier reference frame.

$$(\omega_S - \omega_C) \cdot r_S = -(\omega_P^{rel}) \cdot r_P$$

**Step 3:** For the planet-ring mesh (ring is an internal gear, so sign is preserved):

$$(\omega_P^{rel}) \cdot r_P = -(\omega_R - \omega_C) \cdot r_R$$

**Step 4:** Eliminate $\omega_P^{rel}$ and substitute $r = mN/2$ (with module $m$):

$$(\omega_S - \omega_C) \cdot N_S = -(\omega_R - \omega_C) \cdot N_R$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to obtain the Willis equation:

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{\omega_S - \omega_C}{\omega_R - \omega_C} = -\dfrac{N_R}{N_S}\;}$$

The negative sign confirms that sun and ring rotate in opposite senses when the carrier is held stationary.

*(Use the planetary sketch in Section 4.1 for the 1-mark diagram.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $N_S = 30$, $N_R = 78$
- $\omega_C = 2000$ RPM (ICE)
- $\omega_R = 1200$ RPM (ring, in same direction as carrier)
- Find: $\omega_S$

**Step 1:** Apply the Willis equation. Take the sign convention that direction of $\omega_C$ is positive.

$$\omega_S - \omega_C = -\frac{N_R}{N_S}(\omega_R - \omega_C)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$\omega_S - 2000 = -\frac{78}{30}(1200 - 2000)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio.

$$\frac{78}{30} = 2.6$$

**Step 4:** Compute the bracketed term.

$$1200 - 2000 = -800 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply.

$$-2.6 \times (-800) = +2080 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $\omega_S$.

$$\omega_S = 2000 + 2080 = 4080 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 7:** Sign interpretation.

Since $\omega_S = +4080$ RPM is **positive**, the sun gear (MG1) rotates in the **same direction as the carrier (ICE)**.

**Step 8:** Mode of operation.

The ICE is driving the carrier at 2000 RPM, and the ring is being **slowed down** (1200 RPM, i.e., the load is decelerating relative to the carrier). To maintain the Willis constraint, MG1 must spin **faster than the carrier** in the same direction. The planet gears are pushing the sun forward. In this case, the ICE is supplying mechanical power to the planetary set; the sun gear is **receiving mechanical power and driving MG1 as a generator**, which charges the HV battery.

**Final Answer:**
- $\omega_{MG1} = 4080$ RPM
- Direction: same as ICE
- Mode: **Generator** (battery charging)

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the single-speed reducer used in a typical BEV with a 7-speed DCT used in a premium ICE vehicle. Discuss efficiency, packaging, cost, performance, and the fundamental reason a single speed suffices in a BEV.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A BEV uses a single-speed gearbox with $i = 9.5$ and a final-drive ratio of $3.2$. The traction motor has a peak torque of $360$ N·m and a maximum speed of $16{,}000$ RPM. The wheel diameter (including tyre) is $0.65$ m. Compute:
   1. The maximum torque delivered to the road.
   2. The maximum vehicle speed.
   3. The gradeability (in %) at low speed, ignoring rolling and aerodynamic losses. Take vehicle mass $m = 1800$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Parameter | BEV Single-Speed Reducer | 7-Speed DCT (ICE) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of ratios | 1 (fixed) | 7 (with 2 reverse) |
| Efficiency | 97–98% (one mesh only) | 94–97% (two clutches, two meshes) |
| Mass | ~15–25 kg | ~80–110 kg |
| Cost | Low | High (2 clutches, mechatronics) |
| Shift time | N/A (no shift) | 200–400 ms |
| Torque interrupt | None | Near-zero (pre-select) |
| Packaging | Very compact | Large |
| Suitability for source torque | Perfect for flat motor curve | Engineered for narrow ICE band |

**Fundamental Reason (3 marks):** An EV traction motor develops peak torque from 0 RPM, has a constant-torque region up to base speed, and a constant-power region from base speed to maximum speed. This eliminates the need for a launch gear, an overdrive cruise gear, and intermediate ratios — all of which exist in an ICE gearbox only to keep the narrow-band engine in its peak-efficiency sweet spot. Therefore, a single fixed ratio optimally chosen between launch and top-speed constraints is mechanically sufficient.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $i_{gear} = 9.5$
- $i_{final} = 3.2$
- $T_{motor,peak} = 360$ N·m
- $N_{motor,max} = 16{,}000$ RPM
- $D_w = 0.65$ m  → radius $r_w = 0.325$ m
- $m = 1800$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Total gear reduction.**

$$i_{total} = i_{gear} \times i_{final} = 9.5 \times 3.2 = 30.4$$

**Step 2: Maximum road torque.**

$$T_{road} = T_{motor} \times i_{total} = 360 \times 30.4 = 10{,}944 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3: Convert motor max speed to wheel max speed (RPM).**

$$N_{wheel,max} = \frac{N_{motor,max}}{i_{total}} = \frac{16{,}000}{30.4} \approx 526.3 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4: Convert wheel RPM to linear speed (m/s).**

$$v_{max} = \frac{2 \pi r_w N_{wheel,max}}{60}$$

$$v_{max} = \frac{2 \pi (0.325)(526.3)}{60} \approx \frac{1074.6}{60} \approx 17.91 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5: Convert to km/h.**

$$v_{max} \approx 17.91 \times 3.6 \approx 64.5 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 6: Gradeability at low speed (ignoring $F_{roll}$ and $F_{aero}$).**

The tractive force at low speed equals the road torque divided by the wheel radius:

$$F_{trac} = \frac{T_{road}}{r_w} = \frac{10{,}944}{0.325} \approx 33{,}675 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7:** The grade-climbing force is $F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$. Equating:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{F_{trac}}{m \cdot g} = \frac{33{,}675}{1800 \times 9.81} = \frac{33{,}675}{17{,}658} \approx 1.907$$

**Step 8:** Since $\sin\theta$ cannot exceed 1, the BEV can theoretically climb a **vertical wall** (grade = ∞) at low speed. In practice, tyre traction limits apply:

$$\mu_{max,req} = \frac{F_{trac}}{m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta} \geq \tan\theta$$

For level ground ($\theta = 0$): $\mu_{req} = F_{trac} / (mg) \approx 1.91$. This is well above typical dry asphalt $\mu \approx 0.9$–$1.0$, confirming the vehicle is **traction-limited, not torque-limited**, at low speed on flat ground.

**Step 9:** The maximum grade (in %) on which the tyres do not slip on level ground would actually be the *peak torque-limited* condition. Since the torque exceeds traction, the practical grade is limited by $\mu$:

$$\text{Grade}_{max}(\%) \leq \mu \times 100 \approx 90\%\text{–}100\%$$

which corresponds to $\theta \approx 42°$–$45°$.

**Final Answer:**
1. $T_{road,max} = 10{,}944$ N·m
2. $v_{max} \approx 64.5$ km/h
3. The vehicle is **traction-limited**, not torque-limited, at low speed — gradeability on dry asphalt is limited by tyre-road friction to about **90%–100% grade**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Gearbox Questions:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors in Willis equation** — KTU examiners consistently deduct 1–2 marks for forgetting the **negative sign** between sun and ring terms. Always write the equation in the standard form $\dfrac{\omega_S - \omega_C}{\omega_R - \omega_C} = -\dfrac{N_R}{N_S}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the final drive ratio** in torque/speed calculations — many students multiply motor torque by $i_{gear}$ only, missing $i_{final}$. The **total drivetrain ratio** must include both.
> 3. **Confusing direction of MG1** with whether it is a motor or generator — direction alone does NOT determine mode. The **power flow direction** (energy in vs out) determines it. Use $P = T \cdot \omega$ and the sign of $T$ relative to $\omega$.
> 4. **Stating "single speed is better than multi-speed for EVs" without justification** — must always explain that EV motors have flat torque curves, no idle stall, and a wide constant-power region.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — use **SI units throughout** (rad/s, N·m, m/s) for the derivation steps, but **convert to RPM and km/h** at the end to match the question's expected output.
> 6. **Skipping the planetary gear sketch** in 7-mark questions — diagrams carry **at least 1 mark** in the KTU valuation key; never skip them.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Planetary gear set** = Sun + Planet Carrier + Ring; the **Willis equation** $\dfrac{\omega_S - \omega_C}{\omega_R - \omega_C} = -\dfrac{N_R}{N_S}$ is the **single most important formula** of this module.
- 🔑 **Tooth count constraint** for a simple planetary: $N_R = N_S + 2N_P$.
- 🔑 **EV single-speed reducer** is preferred because electric motors deliver peak torque at 0 RPM and have a flat, wide power band — ratios of **8:1 to 12:1** are typical.
- 🔑 **DCT** uses two clutches and two input shafts; **odd gears on shaft 1, even gears on shaft 2**; pre-selects next gear for sub-300 ms shifts.
- 🔑 **CVT** uses a steel belt between two variable-diameter pulleys; ratio envelope typically 0.5–2.5; used in **Honda i-MMD**.
- 🔑 **e-CVT** is a **power-split device**, not a friction-based CVT; it uses a planetary gear set + electrical control of MG1 to achieve infinite ratios.
- 🔑 **Toyota THS** — Sun ↔ MG1, Carrier ↔ ICE, Ring ↔ MG2 + wheels.
- 🔑 **AMT** = manual gearbox with actuators; has torque interruption; cheap and used in some mild hybrids.
- 🔑 **Conventional AT** = torque converter + planetary gearset + hydraulic bands; complex, heavy, 85–90% efficient.
- 🔑 **Power conservation** in ideal mesh: $P_{in} = P_{out}$ ⇒ $T_1 \omega_1 = T_2 \omega_2$ ⇒ $i = N_1/N_2 = \omega_1/\omega_2 = T_2/T_1$.
- 🔑 **Gradeability and tractive force** are limited by **the smaller of** (motor torque × total ratio / wheel radius) **and** ($\mu \times m \times g \times \cos\theta$).
- 🔑 **Maximum vehicle speed** $v_{max} = \dfrac{2\pi r_w N_{motor,max}}{60 \cdot i_{total}}$.
- 🔑 **Always include both** $i_{gear}$ **and** $i_{final}$ in KTU calculations — examiners test this every year.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EV Motor Sizing - Need of Gearbox
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** EV Motor Sizing - Need of Gearbox

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is EV Motor Sizing?

**EV Motor Sizing** is the systematic engineering procedure of determining the optimal power rating (in kW) and torque rating (in Nm) of an electric traction motor such that the vehicle meets its prescribed performance targets, namely:

- **Maximum gradeability** (steepest climb angle)
- **Maximum cruising/peak speed** (top speed in km/h)
- **Acceleration performance** (0–60 km/h or 0–100 km/h time)
- **Range and energy economy** (kWh per 100 km)
- **Payload capacity** (Gross Vehicle Weight, GVW)

It is a constrained optimization problem where the motor must deliver the *minimum* continuous rating that still satisfies the *worst-case* dynamic load demanded by the vehicle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (PEMET863 – Module 3):**
> Motor sizing is the foundational step before selecting a transmission, battery pack, and controller. A wrongly sized motor either over-stresses the battery (if oversized) or fails to meet performance targets (if undersized).

## 1.2 What is a Gearbox in the EV Context?

A **gearbox** (also called a *reducer* or *transmission*) is a mechanical assembly of gear trains that steps down the high-speed, low-torque output of the electric motor to a low-speed, high-torque output at the drive wheels, while also reversing the direction of rotation where required.

In an EV, the gearbox is typically a **single-speed reducer** with a fixed gear ratio (e.g., 8:1 or 10:1), unlike the multi-speed transmissions common in Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicles.

## 1.3 The "Need of Gearbox" — Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Bicycle Gear System:**
> Imagine you are cycling up a steep hill. In a *low gear* (small chainring, large rear cog), the pedals turn many times for every wheel revolution. You climb easily, but cannot go fast. In a *high gear* (large chainring, small rear cog), you fly on flat roads, but your legs cannot push hard enough uphill. An electric motor behaves similarly: it prefers to spin fast (up to 10,000–20,000 rpm for PMSMs), but the wheels only need to rotate at ~600–1200 rpm at 150 km/h. A gearbox is the mechanical "translator" that matches the motor's happy high-speed zone to the wheels' slow but powerful rotation zone.

The key insight is that an EV motor's **natural Constant-Power Region** (high speed, low torque) does NOT match the wheel's demand for **high torque at low speed**. A gearbox bridges this mismatch.

## 1.4 Why a Gearbox is NECESSARY in an EV

| S/N | Reason | Engineering Justification |
|----|--------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Torque Multiplication** | Motors have limited low-end torque; gear ratio multiplies wheel torque by factor *G* |
| 2 | **Speed Reduction** | Reduces 10,000 rpm motor to ~600 rpm wheel speed |
| 3 | **Operating Point Optimization** | Keeps motor in high-efficiency sweet spot (>90%) during most of the drive cycle |
| 4 | **Overload Protection** | Prevents motor stall during launch and hill climb |
| 5 | **Reverse Motion** | Provides reverse gear without needing a second motor |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vehicle Dynamics Force Balance

The total **tractive force** ($F_t$) required at the tire–road contact patch is the sum of four resistive forces. This is the **governing equation** for every EV motor-sizing problem.

$$F_t = F_r + F_{aero} + F_g + F_{acc}$$

where each component has a specific physical origin and formula.

## 2.2 Component-by-Component Breakdown

### (a) Rolling Resistance Force ($F_r$)
Caused by tyre deformation and friction at the contact patch.

$$F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

where $C_{rr}$ is the coefficient of rolling resistance (typically **0.008–0.015** for radial tyres).

### (b) Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{aero}$)
Caused by air pushing against the frontal area of the vehicle.

$$F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

where $\rho$ is the air density (**1.225 kg/m³** at sea level), $C_d$ is the drag coefficient, and $A_f$ is the frontal area.

### (c) Gradient / Climbing Force ($F_g$)
The component of vehicle weight acting along the slope.

$$F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

### (d) Acceleration Force ($F_{acc}$)
Includes a rotational inertia factor $\delta$ (mass factor) for rotating parts.

$$F_{acc} = m \cdot \delta \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

## 2.3 From Tractive Force to Motor Torque

The torque required at the motor shaft is derived using the wheel radius ($r_w$) and the overall gear ratio ($G$):

$$T_{motor} = \frac{F_t \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_{gbx}}$$

> The factor $G$ in the denominator tells us directly why a **larger gear ratio reduces motor torque demand** — this is the *primary engineering reason* for using a gearbox.

## 2.4 Motor Power and the Constant-Power Region

The mechanical power demand at the wheels is:

$$P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v$$

After accounting for gearbox efficiency:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_{gbx}}$$

EV motors are designed to operate in a **Constant-Torque Region** (low speed, e.g., 0–base speed) and a **Constant-Power Region** (above base speed, e.g., 1× to 3× or 4× base speed). A single-speed gearbox is sized so that:

- The **low end of the ratio** allows the motor to deliver maximum torque to launch the vehicle.
- The **high end of the ratio** allows the motor to reach its maximum speed to achieve the top vehicle speed.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $F_t = F_r + F_{aero} + F_g + F_{acc}$ | Total tractive force | N |
| 2 | $F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha)$ | Rolling resistance | N |
| 3 | $F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag | N |
| 4 | $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ | Climbing force | N |
| 5 | $F_{acc} = m \cdot \delta \cdot a$ | Acceleration force | N |
| 6 | $T_{motor} = (F_t \cdot r_w) / (G \cdot \eta_{gbx})$ | Required motor torque | Nm |
| 7 | $P_{motor} = (F_t \cdot v) / \eta_{gbx}$ | Required motor power | W |
| 8 | $G = (N_{motor}) / (N_{wheel})$ | Gear ratio (speed ratio) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $v_{max} = (N_{motor,max} \cdot r_w) / G$ | Maximum vehicle speed | m/s |
| 10 | $T_{wheel,max} = T_{motor,max} \cdot G \cdot \eta_{gbx}$ | Maximum wheel torque | Nm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Test Conditions:**
> Air density $\rho$ = **1.225 kg/m³**, gravity $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**, gearbox efficiency $\eta_{gbx}$ = **0.95–0.97** for a single-stage helical reducer.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The motor-sizing + gearbox-selection workflow is used by every EV OEM (Tesla, Tata, BYD, Hyundai, etc.) during the **concept phase** of vehicle development. It feeds directly into:

- Battery pack sizing (kWh capacity)
- Inverter sizing (kVA rating)
- Thermal management design (cooling loop capacity)
- Vehicle control unit (VCU) calibration

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gear Ratio Required for a Given Top Speed

The motor's maximum rotational speed must translate, through the gearbox, to the wheel's maximum rotational speed. Let us derive this step by step.

**Step 1 — Define the wheel's required rotational speed for the target top speed:**

The vehicle moves at $v_{max}$ m/s and the wheel has radius $r_w$ m. The linear distance covered per wheel revolution is the circumference:

$$\text{Circumference} = 2 \pi r_w$$

Therefore, the wheel rotational speed in revolutions per second is:

$$N_{wheel,rev/s} = \frac{v_{max}}{2 \pi r_w}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to revolutions per minute (rpm):**

$$N_{wheel,rpm} = \frac{v_{max}}{2 \pi r_w} \times 60 = \frac{60 \cdot v_{max}}{2 \pi r_w} = \frac{30 \cdot v_{max}}{\pi r_w}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of gear ratio:**

By definition, $G = N_{motor} / N_{wheel}$. Therefore, the *minimum* gear ratio that lets the motor reach the top speed is:

$$G_{min} = \frac{N_{motor,max}}{N_{wheel,max}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3:**

$$G_{min} = \frac{N_{motor,max} \cdot \pi \cdot r_w}{30 \cdot v_{max}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert $v_{max}$ from km/h to m/s** (a frequent source of mark loss):

$$v_{max,m/s} = v_{max,km/h} \times \frac{5}{18}$$

**Final consolidated equation:**

$$\boxed{G_{min} = \frac{N_{motor,max} \cdot \pi \cdot r_w \cdot 18}{30 \cdot v_{max,km/h} \cdot 5} = \frac{N_{motor,max} \cdot \pi \cdot r_w \cdot 3}{v_{max,km/h} \cdot 25}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Gear Ratio Required for a Given Gradeability

For a given maximum grade angle $\alpha_{max}$ at a low speed (where aerodynamic drag is negligible), the tractive force reduces to:

$$F_t \approx F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha_{max})$$

We want the motor to deliver torque $T_{motor,max}$ to overcome this force. From the torque relation:

$$T_{motor,max} = \frac{F_t \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_{gbx}} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha_{max}) \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_{gbx}}$$

**Rearranging for the maximum permissible gear ratio** (i.e., the *highest* $G$ that still allows the climb):

$$G_{max} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha_{max}) \cdot r_w}{T_{motor,max} \cdot \eta_{gbx}}$$

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU-style):**
A small electric city car has the following data:
- Kerb mass $m$ = 1200 kg (including 200 kg battery)
- Wheel radius $r_w$ = 0.30 m
- Frontal area $A_f$ = 2.2 m²
- Drag coefficient $C_d$ = 0.30
- Rolling resistance $C_{rr}$ = 0.012
- Gearbox efficiency $\eta_{gbx}$ = 0.96
- Motor max speed $N_{motor,max}$ = 10,000 rpm
- Motor max torque $T_{motor,max}$ = 210 Nm
- Target top speed $v_{max}$ = 140 km/h
- Target max grade $\alpha_{max}$ = 30 % (i.e., $\tan\alpha = 0.30$)

**Required:** (a) Suitable gear ratio, (b) Verify gradeability.

### Solution (a) — Gear Ratio from Top Speed

Convert top speed to m/s:

$$v_{max} = 140 \times \tfrac{5}{18} = 38.89 \text{ m/s}$$

Apply the derived equation from §3.1:

$$\begin{aligned}
G_{min} &= \frac{N_{motor,max} \cdot \pi \cdot r_w}{30 \cdot v_{max}} \\
&= \frac{10000 \times \pi \times 0.30}{30 \times 38.89} \\
&= \frac{9424.78}{1166.67} \\
&= 8.08
\end{aligned}$$

So $G \approx 8.08$ to hit 140 km/h. We round **up** to the next standard ratio: $G$ = **8.5** (or 8.16 if a planetary set is used).

### Solution (b) — Gradeability Check

For a 30 % grade, $\sin\alpha = 0.30 / \sqrt{1 + 0.30^2} = 0.30 / 1.0440 = 0.2873$.

Required tractive force (ignoring drag at low speed):

$$F_t = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.2873 = 3382 \text{ N}$$

Available motor torque after gearbox (using G = 8.5):

$$T_{wheel} = T_{motor,max} \cdot G \cdot \eta_{gbx} = 210 \times 8.5 \times 0.96 = 1713.6 \text{ Nm}$$

Tractive force this produces:

$$F_{t,available} = \frac{T_{wheel}}{r_w} = \frac{1713.6}{0.30} = 5712 \text{ N}$$

Since $F_{t,available}$ (5712 N) > $F_t$ required (3382 N), the vehicle **CAN climb the 30 % grade with a margin of 68.9 %**. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always check BOTH constraints (top speed and gradeability). The chosen gear ratio must satisfy $G_{min}$ (for top speed) AND $G_{max}$ (for gradeability) simultaneously. If the two ranges do not overlap, the motor is incorrectly sized.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Single-Speed vs Multi-Speed Gearbox

| Design Criterion | Single-Speed Reducer | Multi-Speed Transmission |
|------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Cost & Weight | Low | High |
| Efficiency | 95–97 % | 88–92 % (multi-mesh losses) |
| Packaging | Compact | Bulky |
| Top Speed vs Acceleration Trade-off | Compromised | Both optimized |
| NVH (Noise) | Low | Higher (shift events) |
| Typical Application | Tesla Model 3, Nissan Leaf, Tata Nexon EV | Porsche Taycan, BMW i8, Audi e-tron GT |
| KTU Exam Example | 8:1 fixed reducer | 2-speed dual-clutch |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "EVs do NOT need a gearbox because motors have a wide constant-power region." This is **incomplete**. A gearbox is still required for **torque multiplication** at launch. The *type* of gearbox (single-speed vs multi-speed) is the actual design choice.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — EV Powertrain with Sizing Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vehicle Performance Targets] --> B[Top Speed]
    A --> C[Max Grade Angle]
    A --> D[0 to 100 km/h Time]
    B --> E[Required Wheel Power]
    C --> F[Required Tractive Force]
    D --> G[Required Acceleration Force]
    E --> H[Motor Power Sizing]
    F --> I[Motor Torque Sizing]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Motor Selection PMSM or IM]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Gear Ratio Range}
    K --> L[G_min for Top Speed]
    K --> M[G_max for Gradeability]
    L --> N[Feasible Range G_min to G_max]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Gearbox Selection]
    O --> P[Single Speed Reducer]
    O --> Q[Multi Speed Transmission]
    P --> R[Final Drive Assembly]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Wheels and Tires]
```

## 4.2 Torque-Speed Envelope Diagram (Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Motor Envelope]
    A1[Constant Torque Region 0 to N_base]
    A2[Constant Power Region N_base to N_max]
    end
    subgraph B[Wheel Demand after Gearbox]
    B1[High Torque at Low Speed for Launch]
    B2[Low Torque at High Speed for Cruising]
    end
    subgraph C[Design Outcome]
    C1[Choose G so that wheel curve fits within motor envelope]
    end
    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> C1
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
```

## 4.3 Motor-Sizing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Vehicle Specs Given] --> S1[Compute F_r at low speed]
    S1 --> S2[Compute F_g at max grade]
    S2 --> S3[Compute F_aero at v_max]
    S3 --> S4[Compute F_accel for 0 to 100 km/h]
    S4 --> S5[Sum to get F_t for each scenario]
    S5 --> Case1{Scenario with Highest Torque Demand}
    S5 --> Case2{Scenario with Highest Power Demand}
    Case1 --> Torque[SIZE TORQUE from max F_t case]
    Case2 --> Power[SIZE POWER from max F_t times v case]
    Torque --> Verify[Verify T_motor_max and N_motor_max]
    Power --> Verify
    Verify --> Gear[Compute G_min and G_max]
    Gear --> End[Select Standard Gear Ratio]
```

## 4.4 Force Flow at the Tire–Road Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Motor[Electric Motor] -->|T_motor| Gbx[Gearbox Ratio G]
    Gbx -->|T_wheel| Shaft[Drive Shaft]
    Shaft --> Diff[Differential]
    Diff -->|F_tractive| Tire[Tire Contact Patch]
    Tire --> Road[Road Surface]
    Road -->|F_resistive| F1[F_rolling]
    Road -->|F_resistive| F2[F_gradient]
    Air -->|F_resistive| F3[F_aerodynamic]
    Inertia[Vehicle Inertia] -->|F_resistive| F4[F_acceleration]
    F1 --> Sum[Total Resistance F_t]
    F2 --> Sum
    F3 --> Sum
    F4 --> Sum
    Sum --> Tire
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**"List any three reasons why a gearbox is needed in an Electric Vehicle, even though an electric motor can produce high torque at low speeds."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Torque Multiplication at Launch (1 mark):** Although EV motors have good low-end torque, it is still insufficient to launch a 1500 kg vehicle from rest with required acceleration. A gearbox (e.g., 8:1) multiplies motor torque by 8, ensuring strong launch performance.

2. **Speed Reduction for Wheel Compatibility (1 mark):** PMSM motors operate efficiently at 8000–15000 rpm, while wheels rotate at 400–800 rpm at 100 km/h. A gearbox mechanically translates the high motor rpm to a usable wheel rpm.

3. **Operating Point Optimization (1 mark):** A correctly chosen gear ratio keeps the motor within its high-efficiency Constant-Power Region (>90 % efficiency) for the majority of the drive cycle, improving range by 5–12 %.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**"Define the 'Constant Power Region' of an EV motor and explain its relevance to gear ratio selection."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** The Constant Power Region (CPR) is the speed range *above the base speed* of the motor, where the motor's power remains approximately constant (P = T × ω) and torque decreases inversely with speed ($\propto 1/N$). It typically extends from base speed to 2×–4× base speed (also called the **field-weakening region**).

- **Relevance to Gear Ratio (1 mark):** The gear ratio is chosen so that the most common cruising speed corresponds to the *middle* of the CPR, allowing the vehicle to use full motor power both for overtaking (at higher rpm) and for hill climbing (at lower rpm) without needing a gear shift.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**Q. (a)** Derive the equation for the *minimum gear ratio* required to achieve a given top vehicle speed in an electric vehicle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-wheel electric SUV has the following specifications: Kerb mass = 1800 kg, Wheel radius = 0.35 m, Frontal area = 2.6 m², $C_d$ = 0.32, $C_{rr}$ = 0.013, Gearbox efficiency = 0.95, Motor max speed = 9000 rpm, Motor max torque = 320 Nm, Top speed target = 160 km/h, Maximum grade = 25 %. Determine a suitable single-speed gear ratio and verify both the top-speed and gradeability requirements. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The vehicle at top speed $v_{max}$ m/s has wheel rotational speed (rev/s) equal to wheel linear speed divided by circumference:

$$N_{wheel,rev/s} = \frac{v_{max}}{2 \pi r_w}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Converting to rpm:

$$N_{wheel,rpm} = \frac{60 \cdot v_{max}}{2 \pi r_w} = \frac{30 \cdot v_{max}}{\pi r_w}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Converting top speed from km/h to m/s:

$$v_{max,m/s} = v_{max,km/h} \times \frac{5}{18}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substituting into the above:

$$N_{wheel,rpm} = \frac{30 \cdot v_{max,km/h} \cdot 5}{\pi r_w \cdot 18} = \frac{25 \cdot v_{max,km/h}}{3 \pi r_w}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The motor's maximum rpm must be sufficient to drive the wheel at this rpm. By definition of gear ratio:

$$G = \frac{N_{motor}}{N_{wheel}}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The *minimum* gear ratio that just achieves the top speed is:

$$G_{min} = \frac{N_{motor,max}}{N_{wheel,max}} = \frac{N_{motor,max} \cdot 3 \pi r_w}{25 \cdot v_{max,km/h}}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Rounding up to the next standard ratio is necessary because using a lower G would not allow the motor to reach its full top speed (a frequent student error — students sometimes round *down*).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert top speed (1 mark):**

$$v_{max} = 160 \times \tfrac{5}{18} = 44.44 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $G_{min}$ (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
G_{min} &= \frac{9000 \times 3 \pi \times 0.35}{25 \times 160} \\
&= \frac{29688.10}{4000} \\
&= 7.42
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Select standard ratio (1 mark):** Choose **G = 8.0** (next standard commercial ratio above 7.42).

**Step 4 — Verify top speed with G = 8.0 (1 mark):**

$$v_{achieved} = \frac{N_{motor,max} \times 3 \pi r_w}{25 \times G} = \frac{9000 \times 3 \pi \times 0.35}{25 \times 8.0} = \frac{29688}{200} = 148.4 \text{ km/h}$$

This is below the 160 km/h target, so we need a *smaller* G. Choose **G = 7.5**:

$$v_{achieved} = \frac{29688}{25 \times 7.5} = \frac{29688}{187.5} = 158.3 \text{ km/h}$$

Acceptable (within 1.2 % of target). So **G = 7.5** is chosen.

**Step 5 — Verify gradeability with G = 7.5 (2 marks):**

For 25 % grade: $\tan\alpha = 0.25$, so $\sin\alpha = 0.25 / \sqrt{1.0625} = 0.2425$.

Required tractive force at low speed:

$$F_t = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.2425 = 4282 \text{ N}$$

Available wheel torque:

$$T_{wheel} = 320 \times 7.5 \times 0.95 = 2280 \text{ Nm}$$

Available tractive force:

$$F_{t,avail} = \frac{2280}{0.35} = 6514 \text{ N}$$

Since $6514 > 4282$, the vehicle **climbs the 25 % grade with a 52 % margin** ✅

**Conclusion:** Gear ratio **G = 7.5** satisfies both top speed and gradeability.

---

### Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the torque-speed characteristic of a typical EV traction motor. Identify the Constant-Torque and Constant-Power regions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare single-speed and multi-speed gearboxes for EVs. State, with justification, the type used in the Tesla Model 3 and in the Porsche Taycan. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B (Outline for Self-Study)

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
- Draw T vs N curve (sketch: rectangular hyperbola for P = const, flat line for T = const). **(3 marks)**
- Define base speed $N_b$ as transition point. **(2 marks)**
- Mark the two regions and explain field weakening. **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
- Comparison table (4 parameters × 1 mark each) = **4 marks**
- Tesla Model 3 → Single-speed fixed reducer (efficiency + simplicity) = **1.5 marks**
- Porsche Taycan → 2-speed transmission (high top speed + launch performance) = **1.5 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Motor Sizing Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing km/h with m/s in the same equation. *Always* convert top speed to m/s *before* substituting into the tractive force or torque equations.
> 2. **Wrong Direction of Rounding (–1 mark):** Rounding the gear ratio *down* instead of *up* when selecting $G_{min}$. If the calculated $G_{min}$ is 7.42, you must choose G ≥ 7.42 (e.g., 7.5 or 8.0), never less.
> 3. **Forgetting Gearbox Efficiency (–2 marks):** The factor $\eta_{gbx}$ appears in the denominator for torque and the denominator for power. Omitting it inflates the motor size by 4–5 %.
> 4. **Averaging Forces Incorrectly (–2 marks):** Motor is sized from the *worst-case* (maximum) tractive force, not the average. Students often compute an arithmetic mean of hill and flat-road forces, which is wrong.
> 5. **Ignoring Mass Factor $\delta$ (–1 mark):** For accurate 0–100 km/h problems, the rotating mass inertia factor $\delta$ ≈ 1.05–1.15 must be included. Omitting it gives a 5–15 % error in acceleration force.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Motor Sizing** is a constrained optimization: minimum rating that meets the worst-case load.
- The governing equation is **$F_t = F_r + F_{aero} + F_g + F_{acc}$**.
- The **gearbox multiplies torque** by the ratio $G$ and reduces speed by the same factor.
- **Two constraints** must be satisfied: $G \leq G_{max}$ (for gradeability) and $G \geq G_{min}$ (for top speed).
- **Standard values to memorize:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\eta_{gbx} \approx 0.95$ for single-speed, $C_{rr} \approx 0.01$ for radial tyres, $C_d \approx 0.3$ for sedans.
- **Always convert km/h to m/s** before substituting into equations.
- **Single-speed reducers** dominate the mass-market EV segment (cost, weight, efficiency).
- **Multi-speed transmissions** are used in performance/luxury EVs (Porsche Taycan, Audi e-tron GT) for a wider sweet spot.
- The motor's **Constant-Power Region (CPR)** allows the vehicle to cruise at high speeds without a gear shift — this is the *only* reason multi-speed transmissions are not strictly necessary in commuter EVs.
- **Without a gearbox**, an EV would need a motor with 8–10× higher torque, which would mean a much larger, heavier, and more expensive motor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motor Controllers - Motor Drive Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Motor Controllers - Motor Drive Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Motor Controller and Motor Drive Components

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Motor Controller** in a Hybrid and Electric Vehicle is the electronic power-processing unit that regulates the bidirectional flow of electrical energy between the high-voltage traction battery and the electric traction motor. The **Motor Drive System** is the integrated assembly of power electronic converters, gate drivers, sensors, control logic, and thermal management subsystems that together dictate the torque, speed, and efficiency characteristics of the propulsion machine.

In the KTU 2024 PEMET863 framework, the motor drive is treated as a **cyber-physical system**: the *power layer* handles kilo-ampere currents and hundreds of volts, while the *command layer* runs vector-control algorithms at microsecond resolution. The boundary between the two is the **gate driver**, which is itself considered an exam-relevant component.

### 1.2 Components Covered in KTU Module 4

| Sl. No. | Component | Functional Role |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Traction Inverter (DC–AC)** | Converts DC bus voltage into variable-frequency, variable-amplitude AC |
| 2 | **DC–DC Converter** | Steps high-voltage DC bus down to 12 V/48 V auxiliary rail |
| 3 | **Gate Driver Circuit** | Level-shifts logic signals to switch IGBTs/MOSFETs safely |
| 4 | **Current & Voltage Sensors** | Provide closed-loop feedback for control algorithms |
| 5 | **Position/Resolver Sensor** | Reports rotor angle for Field-Oriented Control (FOC) |
| 6 | **DC-Link Capacitor Bank** | Filters ripple, absorbs regenerative energy |
| 7 | **Bus Bars & Cooling Plate** | Carry current and remove heat |
| 8 | **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) Interface** | CAN/LIN communication with vehicle |
| 9 | **Thermal Management System** | Liquid cooling loop maintaining junction temp < **150 °C** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Accelerator Pedal as a Translator"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy**
> Imagine the driver's accelerator pedal as someone who speaks only English, and the electric motor as a device that only understands Mandarin. The **motor controller is the bilingual translator in the middle**. When the driver presses the pedal by, say, 30 %, the pedal sends a weak **CAN-bus message (≈ 5 V signal)**. The controller translates this into **six high-current gate pulses (≈ ±15 V at several amps)** sent to the inverter. The inverter, in turn, sends a precise **three-phase AC waveform** to the motor at, for instance, 200 V peak and 800 Hz. The accelerator input is "small and slow"; the motor receives "large and fast" — that translation, with all its mathematical fidelity, is what makes the EV feel responsive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard EV Metric to Remember**
> A typical 2024 production EV traction inverter switches at a fundamental frequency between **5 Hz (crawl) to 1.2 kHz (highway)**, with PWM carrier frequencies of **5 kHz to 20 kHz**, depending on the silicon technology used.

### 1.4 Why Motor Controllers are Central to Module 4

The HEV architecture studied in Module 3 (series, parallel, power-split) all converge on a single bottleneck: the **motor drive**. Regardless of whether the configuration is a Toyota THS power-split or a Tesla pure-EV dual-motor layout, the controller must:

- Deliver **instantaneous torque** within milliseconds of pedal input.
- Recover **kinetic energy** during regenerative braking.
- Operate across an **ambient range of −40 °C to +85 °C**.
- Survive **vibration, dust, and water ingress** (IP67 standard).
- Maintain **electromagnetic compatibility (EMC)** so as not to disturb radio and ADAS sensors.

The KTU examiner expects students to understand that the motor controller is not an isolated box — it is the **electro-mechanical heart** that decides whether the vehicle feels sluggish or sporty, efficient or wasteful.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Motor Drive as a Closed-Loop System

The motor drive is mathematically modelled as a **multi-input multi-output (MIMO) plant** with the following controlled variables:

- **Stator currents** $i_d$, $i_q$ (in dq-frame)
- **Rotor angular position** $\theta_e$
- **DC-link voltage** $V_{dc}$
- **Coolant temperature** $T_{cool}$

The **command variables** (setpoints) are:

- Torque request $T^{*}_{em}$ (from VCU)
- Speed request $\omega^{*}_{em}$ (during cruise control mode)

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown of Operation

**Step 1 — Command Reception**
The VCU transmits a torque request over the **CAN bus (500 kbps or 2 Mbps CAN-FD)** every 10 ms. The message contains pedal percentage, gear state, brake status, and battery state-of-charge.

**Step 2 — Torque Vector Resolution**
The controller's torque-mapping algorithm converts pedal position into a target torque using a **drive-mode dependent curve**:

$$
T^{*}_{em} = f(\alpha_{pedal},\, SOC,\, T_{batt},\, v_{vehicle})
$$

**Step 3 — Current Reference Generation**
Using the field-oriented control law, the target torque is decomposed into d-axis and q-axis current references:

$$
i^{*}_{q} = \frac{T^{*}_{em}}{\frac{3}{2}\,p\, \psi_{pm}} \qquad i^{*}_{d} = 0 \text{ (MTPA strategy)}
$$

where $p$ is the number of pole pairs and $\psi_{pm}$ is the permanent magnet flux linkage.

**Step 4 — PWM Modulation**
The error between reference and measured currents is fed to **PI controllers**, whose outputs are voltage commands. These are transformed back to the stationary $\alpha\beta$ frame and modulated using **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)** to generate six gate signals.

**Step 5 — Power Stage Switching**
The gate driver energises the upper and lower IGBTs of each phase leg at the PWM carrier frequency, creating a quasi-sinusoidal AC waveform on each phase.

**Step 6 — Feedback Acquisition**
Hall-effect current sensors (typically LEM LA-series) measure each phase current. A resolver or encoder reports rotor position. The DC-link voltage sensor uses a resistive divider with isolation amplifier.

**Step 7 — Thermal Regulation**
If the IGBT junction temperature exceeds **125 °C**, the controller **derates** the output current linearly to prevent thermal runaway.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they recur every year in KTU ESE Part B questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{LL,rms} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}}\, M\, V_{dc}$ | Line-to-line RMS voltage from SVPWM inverter | V |
| 2 | $M = \dfrac{\hat{V}_{phase}}{\tfrac{V_{dc}}{2}}$ | Modulation index, $0 \le M \le \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_{boost} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}$ | Non-isolated boost converter output | V |
| 4 | $V_{buck} = D\, V_{in}$ | Non-isolated buck converter output | V |
| 5 | $L_{crit} = \dfrac{(1-D)^{2}\,R}{2\,f_{sw}}$ | Critical inductance for CCM buck | H |
| 6 | $P_{out} = \sqrt{3}\,V_{LL}\,I_{L}\,\cos\phi$ | Three-phase mechanical output | W |
| 7 | $T_{em} = \dfrac{3}{2}\,p\,\psi_{pm}\,i_{q}$ | Electromagnetic torque (PMSM) | N·m |
| 8 | $\omega_{sync} = \dfrac{120\,f}{P}$ | Synchronous electrical speed | rad/s |
| 9 | $P_{sw,loss} = f_{sw}\,(E_{on}+E_{off})\,I_{c}$ | IGBT switching loss | W |
| 10 | $\eta_{drive} = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{P_{dc}} \approx 0.94\text{–}0.97$ | Drive efficiency (typical EV) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $i^{2}t = \int_{0}^{t_{sc}} i^{2}(t)\,dt$ | Short-circuit withstand (fuse/IGBT rating) | A²s |
| 12 | $C_{dc} \ge \dfrac{I_{peak}}{\Delta V_{dc} \cdot 2\pi f_{sw}}$ | Minimum DC-link capacitance | F |

**Notation Glossary:**
- $M$ — modulation index
- $D$ — duty cycle
- $f_{sw}$ — switching frequency
- $p$ — pole pairs
- $E_{on}$, $E_{off}$ — switching energies from datasheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Memorise**
> - $M_{linear,SVPWM,max} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$ (over-modulation starts above this)
> - $D_{boost,max} \approx 0.85$ (above this, inductor ripple becomes uncontrolled)
> - $T_{j,max}$ for IGBT = **150 °C**, for SiC MOSFET = **175 °C**

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Formulas

The formula set above enables three practical engineering tasks central to KTU Module 4 outcomes:

1. **Component Sizing**: Given a 100 kW traction motor with 800 V battery, calculate the required IGBT current rating. Solving $I_{c,peak} \approx \dfrac{P_{peak}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{LL}\,\cos\phi}$ yields ≈ **240 A continuous**, hence a 600 A module is selected.

2. **Thermal Design**: Switching loss $P_{sw,loss}$ computed at $f_{sw}$ = 10 kHz with $E_{on}+E_{off}$ = 5 mJ gives **50 W per switch**, total **300 W** for 6 switches — this dictates liquid-coolant flow rate.

3. **Efficiency Estimation**: Using $\eta_{drive} \approx 0.96$ and motor efficiency $\approx 0.94$, the **powertrain efficiency** ≈ 0.90, which is the benchmark all KTU numerical questions assume unless otherwise stated.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Three-Phase Inverter Line-to-Line RMS Voltage under SVPWM

**Problem Context:** A KTU numerical question often states the DC-link voltage and modulation index, asking for the fundamental line-to-line RMS voltage delivered to a PMSM.

**Step 1: Phase Voltage Peak in Linear Modulation**

For a two-level voltage source inverter under Space Vector PWM, the maximum achievable phase-voltage peak in the linear region is:

$$
\hat{V}_{phase,max} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

This arises because SVPWM uses the hexagon inscribed in the circle of radius $\tfrac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$ in the $\alpha\beta$ plane, giving 15.47 % higher DC-bus utilisation than sinusoidal PWM.

**Step 2: Generalised Phase Voltage with Modulation Index**

For any modulation index $M \le \tfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$:

$$
\hat{V}_{phase} = M \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}
$$

**Step 3: Convert Peak to RMS**

The fundamental component of the phase voltage is sinusoidal, hence:

$$
V_{phase,rms} = \frac{\hat{V}_{phase}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{M \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 4: Convert Phase RMS to Line-to-Line RMS**

For a balanced three-phase system, $V_{LL} = \sqrt{3}\, V_{phase}$:

$$
V_{LL,rms} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{M \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 5: Final Simplification**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{LL,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}}\, M\, V_{dc} \approx 0.612\, M\, V_{dc}\,}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A 400 V DC bus with $M = 1.0$ produces:

$$
V_{LL,rms} = 0.612 \times 1.0 \times 400 = 244.8 \text{ V}
$$

If $M$ is increased to its maximum linear value of $\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$:

$$
V_{LL,rms,max} = 0.612 \times 1.155 \times 400 = 282.8 \text{ V}
$$

This is the absolute maximum fundamental voltage achievable in linear SVPWM without entering the over-modulation region (where harmonics distort the waveform).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Boost Converter Voltage Gain

**Problem Context:** HEVs often use a high-voltage bus (e.g., 400 V) and need an auxiliary converter. The boost stage is also used inside inverters to step up the battery before inversion.

**Step 1: Inductor Volt-Second Balance (Steady State)**

During the switch-ON interval $DT_s$, the inductor sees $V_{in}$. During switch-OFF interval $(1-D)T_s$, the inductor sees $V_{in} - V_{out}$.

Applying volt-second balance over one switching period:

$$
V_{in} \cdot DT_s + (V_{in} - V_{out})(1-D)T_s = 0
$$

**Step 2: Expand and Simplify**

$$
V_{in}\,D + V_{in} - V_{out} - V_{in}\,D + V_{out}\,D = 0
$$

The terms $V_{in}\,D$ cancel:

$$
V_{in} - V_{out} + V_{out}\,D = 0
$$

**Step 3: Isolate $V_{out}$**

$$
V_{in} = V_{out}(1 - D)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{1-D}\,}
$$

**Step 4: Energy Storage in Inductor**

The inductor ripple current is:

$$
\Delta I_L = \frac{V_{in} \cdot D}{f_{sw} \cdot L}
$$

This sets the minimum inductance to keep current continuous:

$$
L_{min} = \frac{V_{in} \cdot D(1-D)}{f_{sw} \cdot \Delta I_L}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Electromagnetic Torque of PMSM in dq-Frame

**Step 1: dq-Frame Voltage Equations**

For a surface-mounted PMSM:

$$
v_d = R_s\,i_d + L_s\,\frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_s i_q
$$

$$
v_q = R_s\,i_q + L_s\,\frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e L_s i_d + \omega_e \psi_{pm}
$$

**Step 2: Power Input to Stator**

$$
P_{in} = \frac{3}{2}(v_d i_d + v_q i_q)
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $v_d$ and $v_q$ in Steady State**

In steady state, $\tfrac{di_d}{dt} = \tfrac{di_q}{dt} = 0$. The mechanical power $P_{mech} = \omega_m T_{em}$. Using $\omega_e = p \omega_m$:

$$
P_{mech} = \frac{3}{2}\,\omega_e\,\psi_{pm}\,i_q - \frac{3}{2}\,\omega_e\,L_s\,(i_d i_q - i_q i_d)
$$

The bracketed term vanishes identically, leaving:

$$
P_{mech} = \frac{3}{2}\, p\, \omega_m\, \psi_{pm}\, i_q
$$

**Step 4: Solve for Torque**

$$
\boxed{\,T_{em} = \frac{3}{2}\, p\, \psi_{pm}\, i_q\,}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** This is the foundational equation of FOC. It shows that **torque is linearly proportional to $i_q$** when $i_d = 0$ (Maximum Torque Per Ampere strategy), making control as intuitive as adjusting a single current.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EV Motor Drive

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete signal-flow and power-flow architecture** of a typical EV traction motor drive system. It maps every component studied in KTU Module 4 and shows both the high-power path (thick flow) and the low-power control path (thin flow).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BAT["HV Battery Pack 400 V or 800 V DC"]
    MAINCONT["Main Contactor and Precharge Resistor"]
    DCLINK["DC Link Capacitor Bank 1 to 2 mF"]
    INVU["Inverter Upper IGBTs Phase U V W"]
    INVL["Inverter Lower IGBTs Phase U V W"]
    PMSM["Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor"]
    RESOLVER["Resolver Rotor Position Sensor"]
    CURRSENS["Hall Effect Current Sensors LEM LA Series"]
    VDC["DC Link Voltage Divider Sensor"]
    GATEU["Gate Driver Board Phase U"]
    GATEV["Gate Driver Board Phase V"]
    GATEW["Gate Driver Board Phase W"]
    DSP["DSP or Microcontroller TMS320F28 Family"]
    VCU["Vehicle Control Unit VCU"]
    CANBUS["CAN FD Bus 2 Mbps"]
    AUXDCDC["Auxiliary DC DC Converter 400 V to 12 V"]
    COOL["Liquid Cooling Jacket"]
    BMS["Battery Management System"]

    BAT --> MAINCONT
    MAINCONT --> DCLINK
    DCLINK --> INVU
    DCLINK --> INVL
    INVU --> PMSM
    INVL --> PMSM
    RESOLVER -->|theta_e signal| DSP
    CURRSENS -->|i_uvw feedback| DSP
    VDC -->|V_dc feedback| DSP
    GATEU -->|gate pulses| INVU
    GATEV -->|gate pulses| INVL
    GATEW -->|gate pulses| INVL
    DSP -->|PWM 5 to 20 kHz| GATEU
    DSP -->|PWM 5 to 20 kHz| GATEV
    DSP -->|PWM 5 to 20 kHz| GATEW
    VCU -->|torque request| CANBUS
    CANBUS --> DSP
    DSP -->|status and faults| CANBUS
    BAT -->|HV DC| AUXDCDC
    BAT -->|pack data| BMS
    PMSM -.->|waste heat| COOL
    INVU -.->|switching loss heat| COOL
    INVL -.->|switching loss heat| COOL
```

## 4.2 Functional Layer Map (Three-Tier View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        A1["Driver Pedal"]
```


> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Exams**
> When asked to "draw the block diagram of an EV motor drive," the examiner expects **at minimum**: Battery → DC-Link → Inverter → Motor, **plus** the feedback loop showing current sensor → controller → gate driver → inverter. Omitting the feedback loop typically costs 2–3 marks.

---

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Two-Level Three-Phase Inverter

For exam-style sketches, the following ASCII representation captures the essence of a 2-level VSI:

```
       +Vdc                                             
        |                                              
        +----[ T1 ]-------+----[ T3 ]-------+----[ T5 ]-------+
        |     (U+)        |     (V+)        |     (W+)      |
        |                 |                 |               |
        |   Phase U       |   Phase V       |   Phase W     |
        |                 |                 |               |
        +----[ T2 ]-------+----[ T4 ]-------+----[ T6 ]-------+
        |     (U-)        |     (V-)        |     (W-)      |
        |                 |                 |               |
       -Vdc (GND)                                         
        |                                                 
       ===  (DC-Link Capacitor Cdc across +Vdc to GND)   
       
  Switching States (8 combinations):
  V0 = (0,0,0)  → All lower switches ON  → 0 V line-to-line
  V7 = (1,1,1)  → All upper switches ON  → 0 V line-to-line
  V1 to V6      → Active vectors rotating hexagon
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the major functional components of an EV traction motor drive system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The EV traction motor drive consists of six major functional blocks:
1. **DC-Link Capacitor Bank** — smooths the high-voltage bus, absorbs regenerative energy, suppresses voltage spikes during switching.
2. **Power Inverter (DC–AC)** — uses six IGBTs in three half-bridge legs to convert DC into variable-frequency three-phase AC.
3. **Gate Driver Circuit** — level-shifts logic-level PWM signals (3.3 V / 5 V) to ±15 V with 2–5 A peak current capability to switch the IGBTs.
4. **Current and Position Sensors** — Hall-effect sensors for phase currents, resolver/encoder for rotor position; provide feedback for FOC.
5. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** — runs the field-oriented control algorithm at 10–20 kHz update rate.
6. **Thermal Management Subsystem** — liquid cooling jacket maintaining junction temperature below **150 °C**.

**(1 mark per two correctly explained components, 3 marks total.)**

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Space Vector PWM, and why is it preferred over sinusoidal PWM in EV inverters?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
SVPWM treats the three-phase inverter as a single six-step voltage space vector hexagon. By switching between the **eight inverter states** (two zero states and six active states), it synthesises a rotating voltage vector of constant magnitude.

It is preferred because:
1. **Higher DC-bus utilisation** — fundamental line-to-line voltage is 15.47 % higher than SPWM for the same $V_{dc}$.
2. **Lower harmonic distortion** — pushes dominant harmonics to higher frequencies.
3. **Easier digital implementation** — only sector lookups and timer comparisons.
4. **Lower switching losses** — symmetric placement of zero vectors minimises commutations per cycle.

**(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any two advantages.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Choice A

**Question A(a) [7 Marks]:**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an electric vehicle traction motor drive. Identify the role of each block and describe the signal flow from driver input to mechanical output.*

**Model Solution:**

The block diagram of an EV traction motor drive comprises the following cascaded blocks (each labelled with its function):

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| Accelerator Pedal Sensor | Converts pedal position to 0–5 V analog signal |
| VCU | Aggregates driver intent with vehicle state (speed, gear, SOC) |
| CAN Bus | Transmits torque request at 10 ms cadence |
| DSP Controller | Runs FOC algorithm, generates PWM duty cycles |
| Gate Driver | Provides high-current gate pulses to IGBTs |
| 3-Phase Inverter | Converts DC to variable-frequency AC |
| PMSM Motor | Converts electrical to mechanical energy |
| Current Sensors | Measure phase currents, feed back to DSP |
| Resolver | Reports rotor angle for Park/Clarke transforms |
| Cooling System | Removes I²R and switching losses |

**Signal Flow (mark breakdown):**
1. (1 mark) Pedal → analog signal → VCU
2. (1 mark) VCU → CAN bus → DSP
3. (1 mark) DSP executes Park transform → outputs $v_d$, $v_q$ commands
4. (1 mark) Inverse Park → $\alpha\beta$ → SVPWM → 6 gate signals
5. (1 mark) Gate drivers switch inverter → AC current in motor windings
6. (1 mark) Motor produces torque; sensors close the feedback loop
7. (1 mark) Thermal and current derating ensure safe operation

**Question A(b) [7 Marks]:**
*A 400 V DC bus feeds a three-phase SVPWM inverter driving a PMSM. The modulation index is $M = 0.9$. Calculate (i) the peak phase voltage, (ii) the RMS line-to-line voltage, and (iii) the RMS phase current if the motor draws 80 kW at 0.9 power factor.*

**Model Solution:**

Given: $V_{dc} = 400$ V, $M = 0.9$, $P_{out} = 80$ kW, $\cos\phi = 0.9$.

**(i) Peak Phase Voltage:**

$$
\hat{V}_{phase} = M \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = 0.9 \times \frac{400}{2} = 180 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) RMS Line-to-Line Voltage:**

$$
V_{LL,rms} = 0.612 \times M \times V_{dc} = 0.612 \times 0.9 \times 400 = 220.3 \text{ V}
$$

**(iii) RMS Phase Current:**

From $P_{out} = \sqrt{3}\, V_{LL}\, I_L\, \cos\phi$:

$$
I_L = \frac{P_{out}}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{LL,rms}\, \cos\phi}
$$

$$
I_L = \frac{80\,000}{\sqrt{3} \times 220.3 \times 0.9} = \frac{80\,000}{343.4} \approx 232.9 \text{ A}
$$

**Summary of Results (1 mark for tabulation):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\hat{V}_{phase}$ | 180 V |
| $V_{LL,rms}$ | 220.3 V |
| $I_{L,rms}$ | 232.9 A |

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Choice B

**Question B(a) [7 Marks]:**
*Explain the principle of operation of a three-phase two-level voltage source inverter used as the traction inverter in EVs. With switching states, derive the expression for line-to-line RMS voltage under SVPWM.*

**Model Solution:**

A two-level VSI consists of **six controlled switches** (IGBTs with anti-parallel diodes) arranged as three half-bridge legs. Each leg can connect its output terminal to either the **+Vdc rail** (upper switch ON) or the **−Vdc rail** (lower switch ON). With three legs, there are **$2^3 = 8$ switching states**:

- **Two zero states** (V0 = 000 and V7 = 111) producing zero line-to-line voltage.
- **Six active states** (V1 to V6) producing active vectors of magnitude $\tfrac{2V_{dc}}{3}$ rotated 60° apart.

The Space Vector PWM technique synthesises any reference vector $\vec{V}_{ref}$ within the hexagon by time-averaging the two adjacent active vectors and a zero vector:

$$
\vec{V}_{ref} \cdot T_s = \vec{V}_k \cdot T_k + \vec{V}_{k+1} \cdot T_{k+1} + \vec{V}_0 \cdot T_0
$$

The peak fundamental phase voltage in linear modulation is:

$$
\hat{V}_{phase,max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

For any modulation index $M$:

$$
\hat{V}_{phase} = \frac{M \cdot V_{dc}}{2}
$$

Hence:

$$
V_{LL,rms} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{\hat{V}_{phase}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} M V_{dc}
$$

**Mark Allocation:**
1. (2 marks) Switching state diagram and explanation
2. (2 marks) Vector synthesis principle
3. (2 marks) Step-by-step derivation of $V_{LL,rms}$
4. (1 mark) Final boxed equation

**Question B(b) [7 Marks]:**
*Compare IGBT, Si-MOSFET, and SiC-MOSFET power devices for EV traction inverters. Discuss their suitability in terms of voltage rating, switching frequency, efficiency, and thermal limits.*

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Si-IGBT | Si-MOSFET | SiC-MOSFET |
|-----------|---------|-----------|------------|
| Voltage Rating | 600–1700 V | 50–200 V | 650–1700 V |
| Switching Frequency | 5–20 kHz | 50–500 kHz | 50–200 kHz |
| Typical $V_{CE(sat)}$ / $R_{DS(on)}$ | 1.7 V | 50 mΩ | 20–80 mΩ |
| Conduction Loss | Moderate | High at HV | Low |
| Switching Loss | High | Low | Very low |
| $T_{j,max}$ | 150 °C | 150 °C | 175–200 °C |
| Efficiency | 95–97 % | 90–95 % | 97–99 % |
| Cost (per A) | Low | Lowest | High (×3–5) |
| EV Application | Mainstream 400 V | Aux 12–48 V | Premium 800 V |

**Suitability Analysis:**
1. (2 marks) **Si-IGBT** — workhorse of 400 V EVs (Nissan Leaf, Hyundai Kona). Adequate efficiency, low cost, robust short-circuit withstand.
2. (2 marks) **Si-MOSFET** — only suitable for low-voltage auxiliary DC-DC converters; cannot block high traction-bus voltage.
3. (2 marks) **SiC-MOSFET** — preferred for 800 V architectures (Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP). Enables 5–10 % range gain and 2× faster charging.
4. (1 mark) **Conclusion** — SiC dominates next-gen EVs; IGBT remains in mass-market 400 V vehicles.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Motor Drive Questions**
> 1. **Confusing $V_{LL,rms}$ with $V_{phase,rms}$** — many students forget the $\sqrt{3}$ factor and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Using linear SPWM max ($M=1$) instead of SVPWM max ($M = \tfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$)** — results in under-sized inverter rating.
> 3. **Omitting the rectifier side or DC-link capacitor in block diagrams** — VCU expects at least the bidirectional power flow path.
> 4. **Writing torque as $T = K_t I$ without specifying the dq-frame context** — partial credit only; FOC expects the $\tfrac{3}{2} p \psi_{pm} i_q$ form.
> 5. **Ignoring thermal derating** — never claim an IGBT delivers full current continuously without mentioning junction temperature limit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 15 Things You MUST Remember for KTU Module 4 Exam**

- [ ] Motor drive = inverter + DC-DC converter + sensors + controller + thermal system
- [ ] SVPWM gives 15.47 % higher DC-bus utilisation than SPWM
- [ ] Linear modulation limit: $M \le \tfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$
- [ ] Three-phase VSI has 8 switching states (6 active + 2 zero)
- [ ] PMSM torque under FOC: $T_{em} = \tfrac{3}{2}\, p\, \psi_{pm}\, i_q$ with $i_d = 0$ for MTPA
- [ ] Drive efficiency typically **94–97 %**
- [ ] DC-link capacitor sized to absorb regenerative pulse and filter ripple
- [ ] Switching frequency trade-off: high $f_{sw}$ = low harmonics but high loss
- [ ] IGBT $T_{j,max}$ = 150 °C; SiC MOSFET $T_{j,max}$ = 175 °C
- [ ] Boost converter gain: $V_{out} = \tfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}$
- [ ] Buck converter gain: $V_{out} = D V_{in}$
- [ ] CAN bus operates at 500 kbps (CAN) or 2 Mbps (CAN-FD)
- [ ] Gate driver provides 2–5 A peak gate current with $\pm$15 V swing
- [ ] Resolver reports rotor angle for Park/Clarke transforms in FOC
- [ ] Liquid cooling is mandatory for traction inverters above 50 kW
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Electronic Switches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Electronic Switches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Power Electronic Switches** are semiconductor devices that act as *controllable solid-state valves*, capable of rapidly turning ON and OFF to regulate, convert, and switch electrical energy between a source and a load. In Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs), these switches form the **functional heart** of every power conversion stage — the traction inverter, the DC-DC boost/buck converters, the on-board charger, and the battery management electronics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (PEMET863 - Module 4):** "Power electronic switches are the high-power, controllable semiconductor devices — primarily IGBTs, Power MOSFETs, and emerging wide-bandgap devices (SiC, GaN) — used in EV powertrains to switch DC/AC electrical energy at high efficiency, high frequency, and high voltage/current levels with minimal losses."

### Functional Role in HEV/EV Architecture

| Power Train Block | Primary Switch Used | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Traction Inverter (DC → 3-φ AC) | **IGBT / SiC MOSFET** | Drives 3-phase PMSM/IM motor |
| DC-DC Boost Converter (Battery → DC-Link) | **Power MOSFET / IGBT** | Steps up voltage to 400V/800V |
| On-Board Charger (AC → DC) | **Power MOSFET** | Rectifies and charges HV battery |
| Auxiliary DC-DC Converter (12V supply) | **Power MOSFET** | Steps down 400V → 12V |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Electronic Water Tap"

Imagine a **high-pressure water pipeline** carrying thousands of litres of water per second to a turbine. To control that flow, you cannot use a small household tap — you need an **industrial gate valve** that can:
1. Open and close in **microseconds** (rapid switching).
2. Handle **high pressure without leaking** (high blocking voltage).
3. Allow **huge flow when open** with minimal friction (low on-state voltage drop).
4. Be controlled remotely by a **small electric signal** (gate/base drive).
5. Operate millions of times **without wear** (long switching life).

A **power electronic switch is exactly this industrial electronic gate valve** for electricity. In an EV, the "water" is current flowing from the battery to the motor, and the "small control signal" is a low-power gate pulse from the ECU/VCU.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we need them in EVs:**
> - Conventional mechanical relays are too slow (10–100 ms) and wear out.
> - Linear regulators (like in old electronics) waste energy as heat.
> - *Switching* devices operate in either **fully ON** (low loss) or **fully OFF** (no loss), giving efficiencies > 95 % in modern EVs.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Power Electronic Switches

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classification Framework** — Power switches are categorized by their **controllability**, **latching behavior**, and **drive mechanism**.

### A. Based on Controllability
1. **Uncontrollable switches** — Power Diode (conducts whenever forward biased).
2. **Semi-controllable switches** — **Thyristor (SCR)** (turn-ON is controlled, turn-OFF is by natural commutation).
3. **Fully-controllable switches** — **BJT, Power MOSFET, IGBT, GTO, GTR, SIT, MCT, SiC MOSFET, GaN HEMT**.

### B. Based on Drive Signal
- **Voltage-controlled (field effect):** MOSFET, IGBT, SIT, GaN HEMT — high input impedance, easy to drive.
- **Current-controlled (bipolar):** BJT, GTO, Thyristor — require continuous base/gate current.

### C. Based on the Three-Terminal Structure
- **Three-terminal devices:** Source, Gate, Drain (MOSFET) or Emitter, Gate, Collector (IGBT).

### D. Wide Bandgap (WBG) Devices (Modern EV Frontier)
- **SiC (Silicon Carbide) MOSFETs** — used in **Tesla Model 3, Lucid Air**.
- **GaN (Gallium Nitride) HEMTs** — emerging in compact on-board chargers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics (must be memorized for KTU):**
> - **Blocking Voltage ($V_{BR}$):** typically **600 V – 1700 V** for EV traction.
> - **Continuous Current ($I_C$):** **200 A – 1000 A** per switch.
> - **Switching Frequency ($f_{sw}$):** **5 kHz – 20 kHz** for IGBT, **> 100 kHz** for SiC/GaN.
> - **On-state Voltage ($V_{CE(sat)}$):** **1.5 V – 3.0 V** (IGBT).
> - **On-state Resistance ($R_{DS(on)}$):** **milliohms** (MOSFET).
> - **Junction Temperature ($T_j$):** up to **175 °C** (Si), **200 °C+** (SiC).

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating States of an Ideal Power Switch

A power electronic switch is a **two-state** (binary) device. It operates in one of two distinct regions:

### State 1: ON (Saturation / Conduction)
- The device behaves like a **closed switch** with a small voltage drop.
- For IGBT: $V_{CE} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5 - 3 \text{ V}$.
- For MOSFET: $V_{DS} = I_D \cdot R_{DS(on)}$.
- Current flows freely with minimal resistive loss.
- Power dissipation = $V_{drop} \times I_{load}$.

### State 2: OFF (Blocking / Cut-off)
- The device behaves like an **open switch** with infinite (ideally) resistance.
- It supports the full DC-link voltage across it ($V_{CE} = V_{DC}$).
- Current is (ideally) zero.
- Power dissipation ≈ 0.

### State 3: Linear (Active) Region — During Transitions ⚠️
- **Avoided in switching applications** because both voltage AND current are simultaneously non-zero, causing **switching losses**.
- Loss energy per transition: $E_{sw} \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_{L} \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off})$.

---

## 2.2 Static Characteristics

The **I-V curve** of a power switch defines its three operating regions:

1. **Cut-off region** — $V < V_{th}$ (gate not pulsed). No current flows.
2. **Active / Saturation region** — Device is partially ON (used in linear regulators, **not** in EV inverters).
3. **Ohmic / Triode region** — Device is fully ON. Used in switched-mode EV power conversion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Voltage ($V_{th}$):** The minimum gate-emitter voltage required to turn the device ON. For an IGBT used in EVs, $V_{th} \approx 4 - 6 \text{ V}$. Gate drive voltages are typically **+15 V (ON)** and **−15 V (OFF, for noise immunity)**.

---

## 2.3 Dynamic Characteristics — Switching Transients

During a real turn-ON or turn-OFF, the switch does not change state instantaneously. The transition involves:

- **Turn-ON delay time ($t_{d(on)}$)** — time for gate capacitance to charge to $V_{th}$.
- **Rise time ($t_r$)** — time for $V_{CE}$ to fall from 90 % to 10 % and $I_C$ to rise.
- **Turn-ON energy ($E_{on}$)** — area under the $V \cdot I$ curve during turn-ON.
- **Turn-OFF delay ($t_{d(off)}$)** — time for gate charge to be removed.
- **Fall time ($t_f$)** — time for $I_C$ to fall and $V_{CE}$ to rise.
- **Turn-OFF energy ($E_{off}$)** — area under the $V \cdot I$ curve during turn-OFF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Switching Energy:** $E_{sw} = E_{on} + E_{off}$.
> **Total Switching Power Loss:** $P_{sw} = E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw}$.
> In an EV inverter at 10 kHz, $E_{sw} \approx 1 - 5 \text{ mJ}$. Multiply by 6 switches and 10,000 times/sec → significant loss, justifying **SiC adoption**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Memorize for Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key formulas required for Module-4 numerical problems in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conduction Loss (IGBT) | $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{C,avg} + r_{ce} \cdot I_{C,rms}^2$ | Power dissipated when ON. [W] |
| 2 | Conduction Loss (MOSFET) | $P_{cond} = R_{DS(on)} \cdot I_{D,rms}^2$ | Dominant loss in MOSFETs. [W] |
| 3 | Switching Loss | $P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$ | Energy per transition $\times$ frequency. [W] |
| 4 | Total Power Loss | $P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw}$ | Must be removed by heatsink. [W] |
| 5 | Junction Temperature | $T_j = P_{total} \cdot R_{th(j-c)} + T_c$ | Thermal resistance from junction to case. [°C] |
| 6 | Case-to-Sink Resistance | $R_{th(c-s)} = \frac{T_c - T_s}{P_{total}}$ | Interface to liquid cold plate. [°C/W] |
| 7 | Heatsink (Sink) Resistance | $R_{th(s-a)} = \frac{T_s - T_a}{P_{total}}$ | For natural/forced air. [°C/W] |
| 8 | Total Thermal Resistance | $R_{th(j-a)} = R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)}$ | Series combination (junction to ambient). |
| 9 | Safe Operating Area (SOA) | $V_{CE} \cdot I_C \leq P_{rated}$ | Defines the safe $V$-$I$ envelope. |
| 10 | Switching Energy Estimate | $E_{sw} \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off})$ | Linearized approximation. [J] |
| 11 | Gate Charge (MOSFET) | $Q_g = I_{drive} \cdot t_{on}$ | Determines drive circuit design. [C] |
| 12 | Figure of Merit (WBG) | $FOM = R_{DS(on)} \cdot Q_{gg}$ | Lower = better switch. [mΩ·nC] |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by **EV powertrain design engineers** to size the inverter, select the heatsink, design the liquid-cooling loop, and ensure the IGBT/SiC module operates within the SOA for a 10–15 year vehicle lifetime.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Power Loss in an EV Inverter IGBT

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**
An IGBT module in an EV traction inverter has the following parameters:
- Saturation voltage: $V_{CE(sat)} = 2.0 \text{ V}$
- On-state resistance: $r_{ce} = 0.02 \text{ }\Omega$
- Average collector current: $I_{C,avg} = 100 \text{ A}$
- RMS collector current: $I_{C,rms} = 120 \text{ A}$
- Turn-ON energy: $E_{on} = 2.5 \text{ mJ}$
- Turn-OFF energy: $E_{off} = 3.0 \text{ mJ}$
- Switching frequency: $f_{sw} = 10 \text{ kHz}$
- Junction-to-case resistance: $R_{th(j-c)} = 0.1 \text{ °C/W}$
- Ambient temperature: $T_a = 40 \text{ °C}$

**Find:** Conduction loss, switching loss, total loss, and junction temperature.

### Step 1: Conduction Loss Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cond} &= V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{C,avg} \; + \; r_{ce} \cdot I_{C,rms}^2 \\[4pt]
&= (2.0 \text{ V})(100 \text{ A}) \; + \; (0.02 \text{ }\Omega)(120 \text{ A})^2 \\[4pt]
&= 200 \text{ W} \; + \; (0.02)(14400) \\[4pt]
&= 200 \text{ W} \; + \; 288 \text{ W} \\[4pt]
P_{cond} &= 488 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The ON-state drop contributes 200 W (constant), and the resistive slope contributes 288 W (current-dependent). Both are unavoidable when the device is conducting.

### Step 2: Switching Loss Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{sw} &= E_{on} + E_{off} \\[4pt]
&= 2.5 \text{ mJ} + 3.0 \text{ mJ} \\[4pt]
&= 5.5 \text{ mJ} = 5.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} \\[8pt]
P_{sw} &= E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw} \\[4pt]
&= (5.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}) \cdot (10 \times 10^{3} \text{ Hz}) \\[4pt]
&= (5.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (10^4) \\[4pt]
P_{sw} &= 55 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** At 10 kHz, the switch transitions 10,000 times per second. Each transition dissipates 5.5 mJ → cumulative switching loss is 55 W. Doubling the frequency to 20 kHz doubles this loss.

### Step 3: Total Power Dissipation

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= P_{cond} + P_{sw} \\[4pt]
&= 488 \text{ W} + 55 \text{ W} \\[4pt]
P_{total} &= 543 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is the *heat* that must be removed continuously. In an EV, the inverter coolant loop (typically 50/50 glycol-water at 65 °C) absorbs this heat.

### Step 4: Junction Temperature Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_j &= P_{total} \cdot R_{th(j-c)} + T_c \\[4pt]
&= (543 \text{ W})(0.1 \text{ °C/W}) + T_c \\[4pt]
T_j &= 54.3 \text{ °C} + T_c
\end{aligned}
$$

If the case temperature $T_c = 80 \text{ °C}$ (cooled by liquid cold plate):

$$
T_j = 54.3 + 80 = 134.3 \text{ °C}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is **well below the 175 °C limit** of a standard IGBT, so the design is thermally safe. Margin = 175 − 134.3 = **40.7 °C** safety buffer.

### Step 5: Comparative Check — What if SiC MOSFET is used?

For SiC MOSFET with $E_{sw}$ reduced to 0.8 mJ (typical):

$$
P_{sw}^{SiC} = (0.8 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (10^4) = 8 \text{ W}
$$

**Reduction in total loss:** $P_{sw}$ drops from 55 W to 8 W → a **7× improvement**, justifying SiC in modern 800 V EVs (Porsche Taycan, Lucid Air).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Safe Operating Area (SOA) Constraint

The SOA defines the simultaneous $V_{CE}$–$I_C$ limits a switch can sustain without failure. For **short-time (switching) SOA**:

$$
V_{CE}(t) \cdot I_C(t) \;\leq\; P_{rated} \;\; \text{ for all } t \text{ during turn-ON/OFF}
$$

Given $V_{DC} = 400 \text{ V}$, $I_L = 300 \text{ A}$, $P_{rated} = 100 \text{ kW}$:

$$
V_{DC} \cdot I_L = 400 \times 300 = 120{,}000 \text{ W} = 120 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
120 \text{ kW} > 100 \text{ kW} \Rightarrow \textbf{Violation of SOA}
$$

**Engineering Fix:** Either select a higher-rated IGBT (e.g., 1500 V / 600 A), or use a **snubber circuit** to limit the $\frac{dv}{dt}$ and $\frac{di}{dt}$ during the transition. This is exactly why EV inverter modules use **RC snubbers** or **gate-drive shaping** to stay within the SOA.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix — Key Relationships

| Quantity | Increases When | Effect on $T_j$ | Mitigation in EV |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f_{sw}$ | Higher PWM frequency | $T_j \uparrow$ | Use SiC; raise $f_{sw}$ to reduce motor ripple |
| $I_C$ | Higher torque demand | $T_j \uparrow$ | Liquid cooling; derate during regen |
| $V_{CE(sat)}$ | Cheaper IGBT module | $T_j \uparrow$ | Select low-$V_{CE(sat)}$ trench-gate IGBT |
| $R_{th(j-c)}$ | Bad solder / TIM | $T_j \uparrow$ | Use thermal interface material (TIM) |
| $T_a$ | Hot climate / under-hood | $T_j \uparrow$ | Heat pipe / cold plate to chassis |
| $t_{off}$ | Slower minority carrier | $T_j \uparrow$ | Use MOSFET (majority carrier) |

- **Purple (Sensing):** Current and junction temperature feedback to VCU for protection.
