## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three power electronic switches used in Hybrid and Electric Vehicles. Compare them based on switching frequency and voltage rating.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three primary power electronic switches used in HEV/EV applications are:

1. **IGBT (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor):**
   - Voltage-controlled, fully-controllable.
   - Voltage rating: **600 V – 1700 V**; Current rating up to **1000 A**.
   - Switching frequency: **5 – 20 kHz**.
   - *Application:* Traction inverter driving the 3-phase AC motor (most common in HEVs).

2. **Power MOSFET:**
   - Majority-carrier, voltage-controlled.
   - Voltage rating: **< 250 V** (standard Si); Current rating: low–medium.
   - Switching frequency: **up to MHz**.
   - *Application:* Low-voltage DC-DC converters and auxiliary 12 V supply in EVs.

3. **SiC MOSFET (Silicon Carbide):**
   - Wide-bandgap, voltage-controlled.
   - Voltage rating: **1200 – 1700 V**; Switching frequency: **> 100 kHz**.
   - *Application:* Next-generation 800 V EV traction inverters (Tesla Model 3, Lucid Air).

> **Comparison Summary:** SiC MOSFET offers **highest switching frequency and highest voltage**; IGBT offers **best balance** of voltage and current; Power MOSFET is **fastest** but **lowest voltage** capability. *(Award full 3 marks if all three are explained + comparison given.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Safe Operating Area (SOA) of a power electronic switch. Why is it critical in EV inverters?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Safe Operating Area (SOA)** is the **boundary on the $V_{CE}$–$I_C$ (or $V_{DS}$–$I_D$) plane** within which the power switch can be operated without suffering thermal runaway or device failure. The SOA is defined by four constraints:
1. Maximum collector current $I_{C,max}$,
2. Maximum blocking voltage $V_{CE,max}$,
3. Maximum thermal dissipation $P_{max}$,
4. Second breakdown limit (for BJTs/IGBTs).

**Why SOA is critical in EV inverters:**
- An EV traction inverter can see **transient over-currents up to 2× rated** during hard acceleration or regenerative braking.
- The **DC-link voltage spikes** during switching (due to parasitic inductance) can momentarily exceed $V_{DC}$.
- If the operating locus exceeds the SOA, the device fails catastrophically — leading to **safety-critical failures** in a moving vehicle.
- Designers use **snubbers**, **gate-drive shaping**, and **current-limiting control** to keep the operating point inside the SOA at all times.

*(Award full 3 marks for definition + 2 critical reasons.)*

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 4 — Internal Choice A]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **role of an IGBT module in an EV traction inverter**. List the key static and dynamic characteristics used to evaluate its performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IGBT in an EV inverter has the following ratings: $V_{CE(sat)} = 2.2 \text{ V}$, $r_{ce} = 0.015 \text{ }\Omega$, $I_{C,avg} = 80 \text{ A}$, $I_{C,rms} = 95 \text{ A}$, $E_{on} = 2.0 \text{ mJ}$, $E_{off} = 2.8 \text{ mJ}$, $f_{sw} = 12 \text{ kHz}$, $R_{th(j-c)} = 0.12 \text{ °C/W}$, $T_c = 75 \text{ °C}$.

Calculate:
1. Conduction loss $P_{cond}$
2. Switching loss $P_{sw}$
3. Total power loss $P_{total}$
4. Junction temperature $T_j$

Comment on whether the device operates within safe limits (max $T_j = 175 \text{ °C}$). **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

### Part (a) — IGBT in EV Traction Inverter (7 marks)

**Block diagram (textual representation for written exam):**

```
[HV Battery 400V/800V] 
        |
   [DC-Link Capacitor]
        |
   [IGBT Inverter Leg x3]  <-- 6 IGBTs + 6 anti-parallel diodes
        |
   [3-Phase AC Output: U, V, W]
        |
   [PMSM / IM Traction Motor]
        |
   [Reduction Gear] → [Wheels]
```

**Role of IGBT:**
The IGBT module is the **active switching element** in the 3-phase voltage source inverter. It converts the DC battery voltage into a pulse-width-modulated (PWM) 3-phase AC waveform that drives the traction motor. Each of the **three inverter legs** contains a **half-bridge** with **two IGBTs** (high-side and low-side), and each IGBT has an **anti-parallel freewheeling diode** to carry inductive current during motor de-energization.

**Static Characteristics (3 marks):**
1. **Output I-V curve** — defines cut-off, active, and saturation regions.
2. **Saturation voltage $V_{CE(sat)}$** — must be low to minimize conduction loss.
3. **Threshold voltage $V_{th}$** — minimum gate voltage to turn ON (typically 4–6 V).
4. **Transfer characteristic** — $I_C$ vs $V_{GE}$ curve.
5. **Blocking voltage $V_{CES}$** — maximum permissible off-state voltage.

**Dynamic Characteristics (3 marks):**
1. **Turn-ON delay $t_{d(on)}$ and rise time $t_r$.**
2. **Turn-OFF delay $t_{d(off)}$ and fall time $t_f$.**
3. **Switching energies $E_{on}$ and $E_{off}$.**
4. **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** — short-time and continuous.
5. **Diode reverse-recovery characteristics** — affects leg shoot-through risk.

> **Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for block diagram + role, 2 marks for static, 2 marks for dynamic, 1 mark for conclusion/mentioning EV application.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

#### Step 1: Conduction Loss

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cond} &= V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{C,avg} + r_{ce} \cdot I_{C,rms}^2 \\[4pt]
&= (2.2 \text{ V})(80 \text{ A}) + (0.015 \text{ }\Omega)(95 \text{ A})^2 \\[4pt]
&= 176 \text{ W} + (0.015)(9025) \\[4pt]
&= 176 \text{ W} + 135.375 \text{ W} \\[4pt]
P_{cond} &= 311.375 \text{ W} \;\;\approx \;\; 311.4 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2: Switching Loss

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{sw} &= E_{on} + E_{off} \\[4pt]
&= 2.0 \text{ mJ} + 2.8 \text{ mJ} = 4.8 \text{ mJ} = 4.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} \\[8pt]
P_{sw} &= E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw} \\[4pt]
&= (4.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J})(12 \times 10^{3} \text{ Hz}) \\[4pt]
&= 4.8 \times 12 = 57.6 \text{ W} \\[4pt]
P_{sw} &= 57.6 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Total Power Loss

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= P_{cond} + P_{sw} \\[4pt]
&= 311.4 \text{ W} + 57.6 \text{ W} \\[4pt]
P_{total} &= 369.0 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Junction Temperature

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_j &= P_{total} \cdot R_{th(j-c)} + T_c \\[4pt]
&= (369.0 \text{ W})(0.12 \text{ °C/W}) + 75 \text{ °C} \\[4pt]
&= 44.28 \text{ °C} + 75 \text{ °C} \\[4pt]
T_j &= 119.28 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Safety Assessment

$$
T_{j,\max} = 175 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\text{Safety margin} = 175 - 119.28 = 55.72 \text{ °C}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Since $T_j = 119.28 \text{ °C} < 175 \text{ °C}$, the IGBT operates **well within safe limits**, with a healthy margin of **55.72 °C**. The design is thermally robust for continuous EV operation.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark each for $P_{cond}$, $P_{sw}$, $P_{total}$, $T_j$; 1 mark for safety comment; 2 marks for correct formula selection and units.

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 4 — Internal Choice B]`**

**(a)** Compare **IGBT, Power MOSFET, and SiC MOSFET** in terms of structure, switching frequency, voltage rating, on-state drop, and typical EV application. State **two reasons why SiC MOSFETs are preferred for 800 V EV powertrains.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **switching energy loss** in a power electronic switch assuming a linear transition. An IGBT switches a load of $V_{DC} = 600 \text{ V}$ and $I_L = 250 \text{ A}$ with turn-ON and turn-OFF times of $t_{on} = 200 \text{ ns}$ and $t_{off} = 350 \text{ ns}$ respectively. The switching frequency is $f_{sw} = 8 \text{ kHz}$. Calculate the total switching power loss. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | **IGBT** | **Power MOSFET** | **SiC MOSFET** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Voltage-gated input, BJT output stage | Single MOS structure | SiC-based MOS, WBG |
| **Conduction Mechanism** | Minority carrier (BJT tail) | Majority carrier | Majority carrier |
| **Switching Frequency** | 5 – 20 kHz | Up to 1 MHz | 50 – 200 kHz+ |
| **Voltage Rating** | 600 – 1700 V | < 250 V (Si) | 1200 – 1700 V |
| **On-state Drop** | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5 - 3$ V | $I \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ (mΩ) | Very low $R_{DS(on)}$ at high $V$ |
| **EV Application** | Traction inverter (400V EVs) | 12V DC-DC, OBC | 800V traction (Taycan, Lucid, Tesla) |

**Two reasons SiC is preferred for 800 V EVs (2 marks):**
1. **Higher blocking voltage with lower on-state resistance** — SiC's 10× higher breakdown field allows thinner drift regions, giving low $R_{DS(on)}$ even at 1200 V+.
2. **Higher switching frequency possible** — SiC's lower switching losses (no tail current) allow $f_{sw} > 50 \text{ kHz}$, which reduces motor iron losses, torque ripple, and DC-link capacitor size — directly extending EV range.

*(Additional reasons accepted: Higher operating temperature $>200\text{ °C}$; better thermal conductivity of SiC substrate; smaller inverter weight/volume.)*

---

### Part (b) — Derivation + Numerical (7 marks)

#### Derivation of Switching Energy (4 marks)

During turn-ON, both $V_{CE}(t)$ and $I_C(t)$ change **simultaneously** from $(V_{DC}, 0)$ to $(V_{CE(sat)}, I_L)$. Assuming **linear transition**:

**During turn-ON (time $0$ to $t_{on}$):**

$$
V_{CE}(t) = V_{DC} - \frac{V_{DC}}{t_{on}} \cdot t \quad \text{(linear fall)}
$$

$$
I_C(t) = \frac{I_L}{t_{on}} \cdot t \quad \text{(linear rise)}
$$

**Instantaneous power:**

$$
p(t) = V_{CE}(t) \cdot I_C(t) = \left(V_{DC} - \frac{V_{DC}}{t_{on}} t\right) \cdot \left(\frac{I_L}{t_{on}} t\right)
$$

**Turn-ON energy (integrate power over $0$ to $t_{on}$):**

$$
E_{on} = \int_{0}^{t_{on}} p(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{t_{on}} \frac{V_{DC} \cdot I_L}{t_{on}^2} \left( t_{on} t - t^2 \right) dt
$$

$$
E_{on} = \frac{V_{DC} \cdot I_L}{t_{on}^2} \left[ \frac{t_{on} \cdot t^2}{2} - \frac{t^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{t_{on}} = \frac{V_{DC} \cdot I_L}{t_{on}^2} \left( \frac{t_{on}^3}{2} - \frac{t_{on}^3}{3} \right)
$$

$$
E_{on} = \frac{V_{DC} \cdot I_L}{t_{on}^2} \cdot \frac{t_{on}^3}{6} = \frac{V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot t_{on}}{6}
$$

**By symmetry, turn-OFF energy (linear fall of $I_C$ and rise of $V_{CE}$):**

$$
E_{off} = \frac{V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot t_{off}}{6}
$$

**Total switching energy per cycle:**

$$
E_{sw,total} = E_{on} + E_{off} = \frac{V_{DC} \cdot I_L}{6} \left( t_{on} + t_{off} \right)
$$

**Total switching power loss:**

$$
P_{sw} = E_{sw,total} \cdot f_{sw} = \frac{V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot f_{sw} \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off})}{6}
$$

> **Standard formula (commonly given in textbooks):** $E_{sw} \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off})$ (using a different averaging convention — coefficient 1/2 instead of 1/6 depending on whether voltage AND current are assumed to swing over the full $t_{sw}$ simultaneously). For KTU, the **1/2 factor form** is the more commonly accepted version.

#### Numerical Substitution (3 marks)

Using the **commonly accepted $1/2$ form**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{sw} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2} \cdot (600 \text{ V}) \cdot (250 \text{ A}) \cdot (200 + 350) \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 600 \cdot 250 \cdot 550 \times 10^{-9} \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \cdot 150000 \cdot 550 \times 10^{-9} \\[4pt]
&= 75000 \cdot 550 \times 10^{-9} \\[4pt]
E_{sw} &= 41.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 41.25 \text{ mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sw} &= E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw} \\[4pt]
&= (41.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}) \cdot (8 \times 10^{3} \text{ Hz}) \\[4pt]
&= 41.25 \cdot 8 = 330 \text{ W} \\[4pt]
P_{sw} &= 330 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The switching power loss is **330 W per switch**. For a 3-phase inverter (6 switches), the total switching loss is $6 \times 330 = 1980 \text{ W} \approx 2 \text{ kW}$ — a significant portion of the inverter's efficiency budget, reinforcing the case for **SiC adoption**.

> **Mark Distribution:** 4 marks for derivation (with integration steps); 2 marks for numerical substitution and $E_{sw}$; 1 mark for $P_{sw}$ result + comment.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Things to Remember — Power Electronic Switches (KTU PEMET863 Module 4):**

- ✅ A power electronic switch operates in **two states (ON/OFF)**; the *active/linear* region is avoided in EV inverters to minimize losses.
- ✅ The **three most relevant switches for EVs** are **IGBT** (standard 400 V), **Power MOSFET** (low-voltage DC-DC), and **SiC MOSFET** (next-gen 800 V traction).
- ✅ **IGBT = voltage-gated input + BJT output** (minority carrier → tail current → limits $f_{sw}$).
- ✅ **MOSFET = majority carrier** → very fast switching (MHz) but low voltage capability.
- ✅ **SiC MOSFET = wide bandgap (Eg = 3.26 eV)** → high voltage, high temperature, high frequency — the **future of EV traction**.
- ✅ **Conduction loss formula:** $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + r_{ce} \cdot I_{rms}^2$ (IGBT); $P_{cond} = R_{DS(on)} \cdot I_{rms}^2$ (MOSFET).
- ✅ **Switching loss formula:** $P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot f_{sw} \approx \frac{1}{2} V_{DC} I_L (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$.
- ✅ **Junction temperature formula:** $T_j = P_{total} \cdot R_{th(j-c)} + T_c$ (must be $< 175 \text{ °C}$ for Si IGBT).
- ✅ **Gate drive** for IGBT in EV: **+15 V (ON)**, **−15 V (OFF)** to ensure noise immunity and fast turn-OFF.
- ✅ **Anti-parallel diode** across each IGBT handles **freewheeling current** from the inductive motor load.
- ✅ **SOA (Safe Operating Area)** is a $V$-$I$ boundary — operation outside it leads to **thermal runaway** and catastrophic failure.
- ✅ **Snubber circuits** (RC/RCD) are used across IGBTs to limit $\frac{dv}{dt}$ and $\frac{di}{dt}$ during switching transients.
- ✅ **Thermal management** is critical: every 10 °C rise in $T_j$ roughly **halves** the device's operational life (Arrhenius rule).
- ✅ **SiC reduces switching loss by ~70%** compared to Si IGBT at the same $V_{DC}$ and $I_L$ — driving 800 V EV adoption.
- ✅ **Gate charge $Q_g$** determines the drive circuit's current requirement: $I_{drive} = Q_g / t_{on}$.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MOSFET and IGBT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — MOSFET and IGBT: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 MOSFET — The High-Speed Voltage Valve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MOSFET** stands for **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor**. It is a **voltage-controlled, majority-carrier (unipolar) power semiconductor device** in which the drain current is regulated by the electric field generated at the gate terminal through a thin **SiO₂ (Silicon Dioxide)** insulating layer.

In the context of the PEMET863 syllabus, the **Power MOSFET** is the workhorse of low-to-medium voltage, high-frequency switching converters used inside Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs) — particularly in **auxiliary DC-DC converters**, **on-board chargers (OBCs)**, and the low-voltage side of the **traction inverter** (typically up to **600 V**).

### 🔧 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressurized Water Tap"

Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline (the **Drain → Source** channel carrying large current). A normal tap needs you to *push hard* and *turn a mechanical screw* to release water (this is analogous to a **BJT**, which is **current-controlled** — the base needs a continuous current).

A **MOSFET**, in contrast, behaves like a **modern sensor faucet**:
- You simply bring your hand *near* the infrared sensor → an electric field forms → water flows.
- You remove your hand → the field collapses → water stops.
- **No mechanical contact. No continuous "push" current. Pure voltage control.**
- The oxide layer under the gate is like the **air gap** between your hand and the sensor — it blocks any DC gate current, yet controls a huge drain current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standards Highlight**
> - Gate-oxide breakdown field of SiO₂: ≈ **10 MV/cm**
> - Typical gate threshold voltage $V_{GS(th)}$ for power MOSFETs: **2 V to 4 V**
> - Maximum operating frequency: up to **1 MHz** (limited by switching losses, not transit time)
> - Voltage rating range commonly seen in EV auxiliaries: **40 V, 60 V, 100 V, 200 V, 400 V, 600 V, 1000 V**

---

## 1.2 IGBT — The High-Voltage Power Switch

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IGBT** stands for **Insulated-Gate Bipolar Transistor**. It is a **voltage-controlled, minority-carrier (bipolar) power semiconductor device** that combines the **easy gate-drive of a MOSFET** with the **low conduction losses of a BJT**, making it the dominant switch for high-voltage, high-current traction inverters in EVs.

In an EV traction inverter, the battery bus can range from **400 V** (standard) to **800 V** (modern architectures like the Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP, Lucid Air). At such voltages and currents of **hundreds of amps**, neither a pure MOSFET (high $R_{DS(on)}$ at high voltage) nor a pure BJT (complex drive circuitry) is ideal. The **IGBT fills this gap perfectly**.

### 🔧 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hybrid Pressure Cooker Valve"

Think of a heavy-duty industrial pressure cooker that operates at very high pressure (the **800 V DC bus** of a modern EV). You need a valve that can:
1. Be **opened/closed by a gentle touch** (MOSFET-like easy gate drive).
2. **Handle enormous pressure without leaking** (BJT-like low conduction drop).
3. **Stay cool** even when fully open and carrying huge current.

The **IGBT is exactly this hybrid valve**. It has:
- A **MOSFET input stage** at the gate (voltage-controlled, no steady current needed).
- A **BJT output stage** at the collector-emitter path (conductivity-modulated, low $V_{CE(sat)}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standards Highlight**
> - Forward voltage drop $V_{CE(sat)}$: typically **1.5 V to 3 V** at rated current
> - Voltage rating range: **600 V to 6500 V** (automotive modules typically 750 V – 1200 V)
> - Maximum switching frequency: **20 kHz to 50 kHz** (limited by tail current)
> - Operating junction temperature: up to **150 °C** (some automotive-grade to **175 °C**)

---

## 1.3 Why These Devices Matter in HEVs/EVs

The **PEMET863** course specifically highlights MOSFETs and IGBTs because they are the **muscles of every power-electronic converter** in a hybrid or electric vehicle:

| EV Subsystem | Typical Switch |
|---|---|
| Low-voltage DC-DC converter (12 V aux) | **MOSFET** (40 V – 100 V rated) |
| On-Board Charger (OBC) PFC stage | **MOSFET** (600 V – 650 V) |
| Traction Inverter (400 V bus) | **IGBT** (600 V – 750 V) |
| Traction Inverter (800 V bus) | **IGBT** (1200 V) or **SiC MOSFET** |
| Wireless charger high-frequency inverter | **GaN MOSFET** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 4 — MOSFET and IGBT: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power MOSFET — Internal Structure & Operating Principle

### 2.1.1 The n-Channel Enhancement-Mode Power MOSFET

A power MOSFET is built using a **Vertical Double-Diffused (VDMOS)** structure. Unlike the small-signal lateral MOSFET, the power device is constructed **vertically** so that the current flows from the **drain (bottom)** to the **source (top)** through the bulk of the silicon, maximizing the cross-sectional area and current-handling capability.

**Layer stack-up (from top to bottom):**

1. **Source metal contact** — connected to $n^+$ source and $p^+$ body (shorted internally to prevent parasitic BJT turn-on).
2. **p-body region** — forms the channel.
3. **n⁻ epitaxial drift region** — supports the high blocking voltage.
4. **n⁺ substrate (drain)** — bottom contact.
5. **Gate** — polysilicon, separated from the channel by a thin **SiO₂** layer (~**100 nm**).

### 2.1.2 Operating Regions (n-Channel Enhancement MOSFET)

When a positive voltage $V_{GS}$ is applied between Gate and Source:

- **Cutoff Region** ($V_{GS} < V_{GS(th)}$): No inversion layer exists. $I_D = 0$.
- **Triode / Ohmic Region** ($V_{GS} > V_{GS(th)}$ AND $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}$): A continuous conducting channel forms; the device behaves like a **voltage-controlled resistor** with $R_{DS(on)}$.
- **Saturation / Active Region** ($V_{GS} > V_{GS(th)}$ AND $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}$): The channel is "pinched off" near the drain; $I_D$ becomes nearly independent of $V_{DS}$ and depends primarily on $V_{GS}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **power-electronic switching applications** (PWM inverters in EVs), the MOSFET is operated as a **switch** — either fully ON (triode region, low $R_{DS(on)}$) or fully OFF (cutoff). It spends negligible time in the active/saturation region to minimize switching losses.

### 2.1.3 Why Power MOSFETs Are Inherently Fast

Because the MOSFET is a **majority-carrier (unipolar) device**, there is **no minority-carrier storage** in the drift region. When the gate signal is removed, the channel disappears almost instantly (within nanoseconds), allowing the device to switch at very high frequencies. This is the **key reason MOSFETs dominate high-frequency, low-voltage EV converters**.

---

## 2.2 IGBT — Internal Structure & Operating Principle

### 2.2.1 The Symmetric/Asymmetric IGBT Structure

The IGBT is essentially a **MOSFET driving a wide-base PNP BJT**. Its structure is identical to a VDMOS, **except that a p⁺ collector layer is added at the bottom** (replacing the n⁺ drain of a MOSFET).

**Layer stack-up (top to bottom):**
1. **Emitter (E)** — corresponds to the MOSFET source, heavily doped $n^+$.
2. **p-body / p⁺ body-short** — forms the channel.
3. **n⁻ drift region** — wide, lightly doped, supports high blocking voltage.
4. **n⁺ buffer layer** (in **Punch-Through / PT-IGBT**) — absorbs tail current.
5. **p⁺ collector (C)** — injects minority holes into the drift region.
6. **Collector metal contact**.

> [!NOTE]
> **NPT (Non-Punch-Through) vs. PT (Punch-Through) IGBT:**
> - **NPT-IGBT**: No n⁺ buffer; thicker n⁻ drift; rugged, square RBSOA; used in **industrial drives**.
> - **PT-IGBT**: Has n⁺ buffer; thinner drift; faster turn-off; lower $V_{CE(sat)}$; dominant in **EV traction inverters** (e.g., Infineon, Mitsubishi modules).
> - **Field-Stop (FS) IGBT**: Modern variant combining NPT ruggedness with PT low losses.

### 2.2.2 Operating Principle

When $V_{GE} > V_{GE(th)}$ (typically **5 V – 6 V**, with +15 V fully ON):
- A conducting electron channel forms at the surface (MOSFET action).
- Electrons flow from the **emitter (n⁺)** through the channel into the **n⁻ drift region**.
- These electrons **lower the potential barrier** of the p⁺ collector junction → **holes are injected** into the n⁻ drift region from the p⁺ collector.
- The **n⁻ drift region becomes flooded with both electrons and holes** → this is called **conductivity modulation**.
- The drift region's effective resistance drops by **an order of magnitude** compared to a MOSFET of the same voltage rating.

This **conductivity modulation** is the *fundamental reason* an IGBT has a much lower conduction drop than a similarly rated MOSFET at high voltages.

### 2.2.3 The Tail Current Problem

During turn-off, the injected minority carriers (holes) in the n⁻ drift region **must recombine or be swept out** before the device can block voltage. This causes a characteristic **current tail** that lasts for **0.5 µs to 2 µs**, limiting the switching frequency to typically **≤ 20 kHz** in hard-switched EV inverters.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes every key equation you must memorize for Module 4 MOSFET/IGBT problems. **Absolute-value bars use \vert** to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Conditions / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MOSFET drain current (triode) | $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS}-V_{GS(th)}) V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$ | $\mu_n$: electron mobility (cm²/V·s), $C_{ox}$: oxide capacitance per unit area (F/cm²), $W,L$: channel dimensions (µm) | For $0 \leq V_{DS} \leq V_{GS}-V_{GS(th)}$ |
| 2 | MOSFET drain current (saturation) | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{GS(th)})^2 (1+\lambda V_{DS})$ | $\lambda$: channel-length modulation (V⁻¹) | For $V_{DS} > V_{GS}-V_{GS(th)}$ |
| 3 | MOSFET on-state resistance | $R_{DS(on)} = \dfrac{V_{DS}}{I_D} \Big\vert_{V_{GS}=15V}$ | Resistance in **milli-ohms (mΩ)** | Key spec for EV converter losses |
| 4 | MOSFET conduction loss | $P_{cond,MOS} = I_{D,RMS}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | Power in **Watts** | Dominates at high current |
| 5 | MOSFET switching loss (energy) | $E_{sw,MOS} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{on}+t_{off})$ | Energy in **Joules** | $t_{on},t_{off}$: rise/fall times |
| 6 | MOSFET total switching loss | $P_{sw,MOS} = f_{sw} \cdot E_{sw,MOS}$ | Power in **Watts**, $f_{sw}$: switching frequency in Hz | Dominates at high $f_{sw}$ |
| 7 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{GS(th)})$ | Siemens (S) or A/V | Determines gate-drive speed |
| 8 | Gate charge | $Q_G = \int i_G \, dt$ | Coulombs (nC) | Determines gate-drive power |
| 9 | Gate-drive power | $P_{gate} = Q_G \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_{sw}$ | Power in **Watts** | Critical for HV-LV isolation |
| 10 | Miller plateau voltage | $V_{GP} = V_{GS(th)} + \dfrac{I_D}{g_m}$ | Volts | Flat region in gate waveform |
| 11 | IGBT on-state voltage | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx V_{CE0} + r_{CE} \cdot I_C$ | $V_{CE0}$: threshold (~0.7 V), $r_{CE}$: on-slope resistance | Linear approx of output curve |
| 12 | IGBT conduction loss | $P_{cond,IGBT} = V_{CE0} I_{C,avg} + r_{CE} I_{C,RMS}^2$ | Power in **Watts** | Dominant loss in traction inverter |
| 13 | IGBT turn-off energy | $E_{off,IGBT} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{CE} I_C \cdot t_f + E_{tail}$ | $t_f$: fall time, $E_{tail}$: tail-current contribution | Tail current = ~30–50 % of total |
| 14 | IGBT total switching loss | $P_{sw,IGBT} = f_{sw} (E_{on} + E_{off})$ | Power in **Watts** | Sum of turn-on + turn-off energies |
| 15 | Safe Operating Area (SOA) bounds | $V_{DS,max}$, $I_{D,max}$, $P_{D,max}$, $\dfrac{di}{dt}$, $\dfrac{dv}{dt}$ limits | All limits are temperature-dependent | IGBT has **square RBSOA**; MOSFET has **RRSOA** |
| 16 | Thermal resistance | $T_j = P_{loss} \cdot (R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA}) + T_A$ | $R_{\theta JC}$: junction-to-case (°C/W) | Junction limit **150 °C** |
| 17 | Figure of Merit (FOM) — MOSFET | $FOM_{MOS} = R_{DS(on)} \cdot Q_G$ | Lower is better | Used to compare MOSFETs |
| 18 | Figure of Merit (FOM) — IGBT | $FOM_{IGBT} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot E_{tsw}$ | $E_{tsw} = E_{on}+E_{off}$ | Used to compare IGBT modules |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

### In an EV Traction Inverter

A typical 100 kW traction inverter uses **6 IGBTs** (3-phase bridge) switching at **8–12 kHz** to synthesize a variable-frequency, variable-voltage AC waveform driving the PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor) of the vehicle. The **efficiency of these 6 switches directly determines the driving range** of the EV.

- Reducing $V_{CE(sat)}$ by 0.2 V at 300 A peak saves ≈ **60 W** continuous per device → ≈ **360 W** total → translates to several extra kilometers of range.
- The cooling system (water/glycol cold plate) must be designed using equation (16) above.

### In an On-Board Charger (OBC)

A 22 kW OBC uses **MOSFETs** in a totem-pole PFC running at **65 kHz – 100 kHz** (or up to 500 kHz with GaN). At these frequencies, switching losses (equations 5–6) dominate, so MOSFETs with low $Q_G$ and small $Q_{rr}$ (reverse-recovery charge) are mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 4 — MOSFET and IGBT: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: MOSFET Triode-Region Drain Current $I_D$

We begin from the gradual-channel approximation. The induced inversion charge per unit area at a position $x$ along the channel is:

$$
Q_I(x) = -C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} - V(x) \right]
$$

where $V(x)$ is the local channel potential measured from the source end. The drain current (drift component, neglecting diffusion) is:

$$
I_D = -W \cdot \mu_n \cdot Q_I(x) \cdot \dfrac{dV(x)}{dx}
$$

Substituting $Q_I(x)$:

$$
I_D \, dx = W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} - V(x) \right] dV(x)
$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ (source, $V = 0$) to $x = L$ (drain, $V = V_{DS}$):

$$
\int_0^L I_D \, dx = W \mu_n C_{ox} \int_0^{V_{DS}} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} - V(x) \right] dV(x)
$$

The left-hand side is simply $I_D \cdot L$. The right-hand side evaluates to:

$$
W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ \left( V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} \right) V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]
$$

Equating and solving for $I_D$:

$$
\boxed{\; I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[ \left( V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} \right) V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right] \;}
$$

**Engineering logic of each step:**
- The minus sign in $Q_I(x)$ reflects that the charge is negative (electrons) while $V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}$ is positive.
- The factor $W/L$ is the **aspect ratio** of the MOSFET — increasing $W$ or decreasing $L$ increases current capability. Power MOSFETs use very short channels (sub-micron) and wide cells to maximize $W/L$.
- The $V_{DS}^2/2$ term represents the **non-linear channel-pinching effect** at the drain end.

### Saturation Regime Derivation

When $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}$, the channel at the drain end is just pinched off. Substituting this into the triode equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{D,sat} &= \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[ \left( V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} \right)^2 - \dfrac{\left( V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} \right)^2}{2} \right] \\[6pt]
&= \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot \dfrac{\left( V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} \right)^2}{2} \\[6pt]
\boxed{\; I_{D,sat} &= \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left( V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} \right)^2 \;}
\end{aligned}
$$

Including channel-length modulation:

$$
\boxed{\; I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left( V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} \right)^2 \left( 1 + \lambda V_{DS} \right) \;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: IGBT on-state Voltage and Conduction Loss

The IGBT's on-state characteristic is well-approximated by a **straight-line (linear) model**:

$$
V_{CE} = V_{CE0} + r_{CE} \cdot I_C
$$

The instantaneous conduction power is:

$$
p(t) = v_{CE}(t) \cdot i_C(t) = V_{CE0} \cdot i_C(t) + r_{CE} \cdot i_C^2(t)
$$

Averaging over one switching period:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cond,IGBT} &= \dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^T p(t) \, dt \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^T V_{CE0} i_C(t) \, dt + \dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^T r_{CE} i_C^2(t) \, dt \\[6pt]
&= V_{CE0} \cdot I_{C,avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{C,RMS}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; P_{cond,IGBT} = V_{CE0} I_{C,avg} + r_{CE} I_{C,RMS}^2 \;}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** An IGBT in an EV inverter has $V_{CE0} = 1.0$ V, $r_{CE} = 10$ mΩ. The average collector current is $I_{C,avg} = 80$ A and the RMS current is $I_{C,RMS} = 120$ A. Calculate the conduction loss.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cond} &= (1.0 \text{ V})(80 \text{ A}) + (0.010 \, \Omega)(120 \text{ A})^2 \\[4pt]
&= 80 \text{ W} + (0.010)(14400) \text{ W} \\[4pt]
&= 80 \text{ W} + 144 \text{ W} \\[4pt]
&= \boxed{224 \text{ W}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **224 W per switch** × 6 switches = **1.344 kW** of conduction loss in the inverter, which the liquid cooling system must remove.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: MOSFET Switching Energy and Loss

A MOSFET transition from ON to OFF (or vice-versa) is characterized by a simultaneous overlap of voltage and current. Approximating both transitions as linear:

- Turn-on: current rises from 0 to $I_D$ in time $t_{ri}$, voltage falls from $V_{DS}$ to $V_{DS,on}$ in time $t_{fv}$.
- Turn-off: mirror process.

The energy dissipated during one turn-on is the integral of $v \cdot i$ during the overlap:

$$
E_{on} = \int_0^{t_{on}} v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt \approx \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D \cdot t_{on}
$$

Similarly for turn-off:

$$
E_{off} \approx \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D \cdot t_{off}
$$

Total switching energy per cycle:

$$
E_{sw,MOS} = E_{on} + E_{off} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{on} + t_{off})
$$

Total switching power loss (with $f_{sw}$ switching events per second):

$$
\boxed{\; P_{sw,MOS} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw} \;}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A MOSFET in a 400 V / 100 A DC-DC converter switches with $t_{on} = t_{off} = 50$ ns. Compute the switching loss at $f_{sw} = 100$ kHz.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sw,MOS} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (400 \text{ V})(100 \text{ A})(50 + 50) \times 10^{-9} \times 100 \times 10^3 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{2} (400)(100)(100 \times 10^{-9})(100 \times 10^3) \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{2} (400)(100)(10^{-2}) \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{2} (400)(1) \\[4pt]
&= \boxed{200 \text{ W}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Thermal Limit & Heatsink Sizing

The junction temperature is governed by:

$$
T_j = T_A + P_{loss} \cdot \left( R_{\theta JA} \right) = T_A + P_{loss} \cdot (R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA})
$$

For **safe operation** we require $T_j \leq T_{j,max}$ (**150 °C** for most EV-grade IGBTs/MOSFETs).

Solving for the maximum allowable loss:

$$
P_{loss,max} = \dfrac{T_{j,max} - T_A}{R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA}}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A traction inverter IGBT module has $R_{\theta JC} = 0.05$ °C/W, $R_{\theta CS} = 0.02$ °C/W, $R_{\theta SA} = 0.03$ °C/W (water-cooled cold plate). Ambient coolant $T_A = 65$ °C, $T_{j,max} = 150$ °C. Find maximum allowable loss per switch.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{loss,max} &= \dfrac{150 - 65}{0.05 + 0.02 + 0.03} = \dfrac{85}{0.10} = \boxed{850 \text{ W}}
\end{aligned}
$$

If losses exceed this, the junction overheats → thermal runaway → device failure.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MOSFET vs. IGBT

| Parameter | **Power MOSFET** | **IGBT** |
|---|---|---|
| Conduction carriers | Majority (electrons only) | Both (electrons + holes) |
| Conduction drop | Resistive: $I \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | Constant-like: $V_{CE(sat)}$ |
| On-state drop scaling with voltage | Increases strongly (drift-region $R$) | Increases weakly (conductivity modulation) |
| Voltage range | **≤ 1000 V** practical | **600 V – 6500 V** |
| Current range | Up to **~500 A** (single die) | Up to **~3600 A** (module) |
| Switching frequency | **100 kHz – 1 MHz** | **1 kHz – 50 kHz** |
| Input impedance | Very high (capacitive gate) | Very high (capacitive gate) |
| Drive power | Low | Low |
| Short-circuit capability | Poor | Good (10 µs SC withstand) |
| Tail current | None | Significant (0.5 – 2 µs) |
| Cost at high V/I | Higher | Lower |
| EV use case | OBC, DC-DC, LV auxiliaries | Traction inverter, fast chargers |

## 4.4 Switching Waveform Schematic — MOSFET Gate-Source Turn-On Sequence

> Conceptual timing diagram of the four phases of MOSFET turn-on. This is one of the most common questions in KTU Module 4.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant GS as VGS Gate-Source
    participant ID as ID Drain Current
    participant VDS as VDS Drain-Source

    Note over GS,VDS: t0: Gate Drive Initiated
    GS->>GS: Phase 1 t0-t1 Ciss charges to Vth
    Note over GS: VGS rises 0 to Vth no ID
    GS->>GS: Phase 2 t1-t2 Miller Plateau Vgp
    ID->>ID: ID rises from 0 to ID-load
    Note over ID: Channel conducts ID
    VDS->>VDS: Phase 3 t2-t3 VDS falls during Miller plateau
    Note over VDS: VDS falls from VDC to VDS-on
    VDS->>VDS: Phase 4 t3-t4 VGS reaches Vdrive 15V
    Note over GS: Full enhancement minimum Rds-on
```

# Module 4 — MOSFET and IGBT: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP, Module 4]**
**(3 Marks)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the structure of an **n-channel enhancement-type Power MOSFET**. Why is it called a **majority-carrier device**, and what advantage does this give in EV converter applications?

#### 🔑 Model Answer (3 Marks — Examiner's Key):

**Structure (1.5 Marks):** A power MOSFET uses a **vertical double-diffused (VDMOS)** structure. It has four key regions stacked vertically: **n⁺ source** on top (with shorted p⁺ body), **p-body** (channel region), **n⁻ epitaxial drift layer** (supports blocking voltage), and **n⁺ drain substrate** at the bottom. The **polysilicon gate** sits above the p-body, separated by a thin **SiO₂ layer (~100 nm)**. Current flows **vertically** from drain to source through the bulk silicon when an inversion channel forms at the surface.

**Majority-carrier definition (1 Mark):** Conduction is by **electrons only** (in n-channel) — no minority-carrier injection occurs. Thus the device is termed **unipolar / majority-carrier**.

**Advantage in EV converters (0.5 Mark):** Absence of minority-carrier storage → **no reverse-recovery charge and no tail current** → MOSFETs can switch at **very high frequencies (100 kHz – 1 MHz)** with low switching loss — ideal for **OBC, DC-DC converters, and LV auxiliaries** in HEVs/EVs.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]**
**(3 Marks)** Compare **IGBT and Power MOSFET** in terms of: (i) conduction mechanism, (ii) typical voltage rating, and (iii) switching frequency. State one specific EV application where each is preferred.

#### 🔑 Model Answer (3 Marks — Examiner's Key):

| Parameter | **Power MOSFET** | **IGBT** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Conduction | Majority-carrier (electron drift) — unipolar | Both electrons & holes — conductivity-modulated bipolar |
| (ii) Voltage rating | Up to ~1000 V | 600 V – 6500 V |
| (iii) Switching freq. | 100 kHz – 1 MHz | 1 – 50 kHz |

**EV Application (0.5 Mark):**
- **MOSFET** → On-Board Charger (OBC) PFC stage / low-voltage DC-DC converter.
- **IGBT** → Main **traction inverter** for the 3-phase motor drive.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE rule: each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Below are **two independent full-length alternatives**, exactly as they would appear in a real KTU question paper.

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**

#### **(a)** **(7 Marks)** Explain the construction and working principle of an **IGBT** with a neat diagram. Discuss why **conductivity modulation** is critical for its low on-state loss. Compare its output characteristic with that of a Power MOSFET.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (2 Marks):** The IGBT is a four-layer (n⁺–p–n⁻–p⁺) vertical device. From top: **n⁺ emitter**, **p-body** (with channel), **n⁻ drift** (thick, lightly doped), **p⁺ collector**. The **gate** is insulated by SiO₂ above the p-body — identical in concept to a MOSFET. The critical difference from a MOSFET is the **p⁺ collector layer** at the bottom, which **injects minority holes** into the drift region during conduction.

**Working Principle (2.5 Marks):** When $V_{GE} > V_{GE(th)}$ (~5 V), an electron channel forms at the surface (MOSFET action). Electrons flow from the **emitter** into the **n⁻ drift region**. This raises the local potential, **forward-biasing the p⁺ collector / n⁻ drift junction**, which causes **holes to be injected from the p⁺ collector into the n⁻ drift**. The drift region now contains a high density of **both** electrons and holes — its effective conductivity rises by ~10× — this is **conductivity modulation**.

**Why conductivity modulation matters (1 Mark):** In a MOSFET at high voltage (e.g., 600 V), the **n⁻ drift region is thick and lightly doped**, contributing most of the on-state resistance: $R_{DS(on)}$ becomes large, and conduction loss $I^2 R$ becomes unacceptable. In an IGBT, hole injection **floods the same drift region with carriers**, reducing its effective resistance dramatically and yielding a near-constant $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5 - 3$ V regardless of voltage class. This is the **fundamental reason IGBTs dominate high-voltage traction inverters**.

**Comparison with MOSFET output characteristic (1.5 Marks):**

| Feature | **MOSFET output ($I_D$-$V_{DS}$)** | **IGBT output ($I_C$-$V_{CE}$)** |
|---|---|---|
| On-state region | **Triode / ohmic** — $I \propto V$ at low $V_{DS}$ | **Quasi-saturation** then **active** region |
| Behavior above knee | **Pinch-off / saturation** — $I$ nearly flat | **Active** — $I$ increases linearly with $V_{CE}$ |
| On-state drop | Resistive, **scales with voltage class** | Nearly **constant** $V_{CE(sat)}$, scales weakly |
| Knee voltage | ≈ 0 V | ≈ 0.7 V (p–n junction built-in) |

#### **(b)** **(7 Marks)** In an EV traction inverter, an IGBT has the following specifications: $V_{CE0} = 1.2$ V, $r_{CE} = 8$ mΩ, $E_{on} = 8$ mJ, $E_{off} = 12$ mJ (including tail), $I_{C,avg} = 90$ A, $I_{C,RMS} = 140$ A, $f_{sw} = 10$ kHz, $V_{CE} = 600$ V (DC-link), $T_{j,max} = 150$ °C, $R_{\theta JC} = 0.08$ °C/W, $R_{\theta CS} = 0.02$ °C/W, $R_{\theta SA} = 0.05$ °C/W, coolant $T_A = 70$ °C. Calculate **(i)** conduction loss, **(ii)** switching loss, **(iii)** total loss per IGBT, and **(iv)** verify whether the junction stays within thermal limits.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Conduction loss (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cond} &= V_{CE0} \cdot I_{C,avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{C,RMS}^2 \\
&= (1.2)(90) + (0.008)(140)^2 \\
&= 108 + (0.008)(19600) \\
&= 108 + 156.8 \\
&= \boxed{264.8 \text{ W}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Switching loss (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sw} &= f_{sw} \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off}) \\
&= (10 \times 10^3)(8 + 12) \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 10000 \times 0.020 \\
&= \boxed{200 \text{ W}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total loss per IGBT (1 Mark):**

$$
P_{loss} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = 264.8 + 200 = \boxed{464.8 \text{ W}}
$$

**(iv) Thermal verification (2 Marks):**

Total thermal resistance:

$$
R_{\theta JA} = R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA} = 0.08 + 0.02 + 0.05 = 0.15 \text{ °C/W}
$$

Junction temperature:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_j &= T_A + P_{loss} \cdot R_{\theta JA} \\
&= 70 + (464.8)(0.15) \\
&= 70 + 69.72 \\
&= \boxed{139.72 \text{ °C}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Since $T_j = 139.72$ °C < $T_{j,max} = 150$ °C ✅, the IGBT operates safely within thermal limits**, with a margin of **10.28 °C**.

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]**

#### **(a)** **(7 Marks)** Explain the **static output and transfer characteristics** of a Power MOSFET. Derive the expression for the **drain current in the saturation region** and explain why the device is preferred for **high-frequency switching** in EV auxiliary converters.

**Model Answer:**

**Static output characteristic (2 Marks):** The $I_D$ vs. $V_{DS}$ plot for constant $V_{GS}$ shows three regions:
1. **Cutoff**: $V_{GS} < V_{GS(th)}$, $I_D \approx 0$.
2. **Triode / Ohmic**: $I_D$ rises almost linearly with $V_{DS}$; the MOSFET behaves as a voltage-controlled resistor with value $R_{DS(on)}$.
3. **Saturation / Pinch-off**: Channel is pinched off near drain; $I_D$ is approximately constant for further increase in $V_{DS}$.

**Transfer characteristic (1 Mark):** $I_D$ vs. $V_{GS}$ at fixed $V_{DS}$ — shows that $I_D = 0$ until $V_{GS}$ reaches $V_{GS(th)}$, after which $I_D$ rises as a **square-law** of $(V_{GS}-V_{GS(th)})$.

**Derivation of saturation drain current (3 Marks):**

For $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS}-V_{GS(th)}$, the channel pinches off at the drain end. Substituting $V_{DS} = V_{GS}-V_{GS(th)}$ into the triode-region equation:

$$
I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS}-V_{GS(th)})^2 - \dfrac{(V_{GS}-V_{GS(th)})^2}{2} \right]
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\boxed{\; I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{GS(th)})^2 \;}
$$

The **square-law dependence** is unique to MOSFETs and explains why doubling $V_{GS}-V_{GS(th)}$ quadruples the current.

**Why preferred for high-frequency switching (1 Mark):** The MOSFET is a **unipolar, majority-carrier device**. There is **no minority-carrier storage** in the drift region. During turn-off, the channel disappears within **nanoseconds**, and there is **no tail current**. This allows switching frequencies of **100 kHz – 1 MHz**, which directly reduces the size of magnetic components (inductors, transformers) in EV auxiliary converters — enabling compact, lightweight OBCs and DC-DC modules.

#### **(b)** **(7 Marks)** A Power MOSFET in a 48 V → 12 V EV auxiliary DC-DC converter has $R_{DS(on)} = 8$ mΩ and is switched at $f_{sw} = 200$ kHz with $V_{DS} = 48$ V, $I_D = 60$ A, $t_{on} = t_{off} = 30$ ns. Compute **(i)** conduction loss, **(ii)** switching loss per device, **(iii)** total loss if there are 4 such MOSFETs in parallel/interleaved configuration, and **(iv)** the heatsink thermal resistance required to keep $T_j \leq 125$ °C if $R_{\theta JC} = 1.0$ °C/W and ambient $T_A = 85$ °C.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Conduction loss (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cond} &= I_{D,RMS}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)} \\
&= (60)^2 \times 0.008 \\
&= 3600 \times 0.008 \\
&= \boxed{28.8 \text{ W}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Switching loss per MOSFET (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sw} &= \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{on}+t_{off}) f_{sw} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} (48)(60)(30+30) \times 10^{-9} \times 200 \times 10^3 \\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} (48)(60)(60 \times 10^{-9})(200 \times 10^3) \\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} (48)(60)(0.012) \\
&= \boxed{17.28 \text{ W}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total loss for 4 MOSFETs (2 Marks):**

Total per MOSFET: $P_{loss,1} = 28.8 + 17.28 = 46.08$ W.

Assuming current is shared equally (i.e., each carries $I_D/4 = 15$ A), recompute per device:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cond,1} &= (15)^2 \times 0.008 = 1.8 \text{ W} \\
P_{sw,1} &= \dfrac{1}{2}(48)(15)(60 \times 10^{-9})(200 \times 10^3) = 4.32 \text{ W} \\
P_{loss,1} &= 1.8 + 4.32 = 6.12 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total for 4 MOSFETs: $4 \times 6.12 = \boxed{24.48 \text{ W}}$ (much lower than a single device due to current-sharing benefit).

**(iv) Required heatsink thermal resistance (2 Marks):**

Using $T_j = T_A + P_{loss}(R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta SA})$, with each MOSFET dissipating 6.12 W:

$$
125 = 85 + 6.12 \, (1.0 + R_{\theta SA})
$$

$$
40 = 6.12 \, (1.0 + R_{\theta SA})
$$

$$
1.0 + R_{\theta SA} = \dfrac{40}{6.12} = 6.54 \text{ °C/W}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\theta SA} \leq 5.54 \text{ °C/W}}
$$

A small aluminum heatsink or direct PCB copper-pour area is sufficient.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing $I_{D,avg}$ with $I_{D,RMS}$** in conduction-loss formulas. The MOSFET uses $I_{RMS}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ while the IGBT's $V_{CE0}$ term uses $I_{avg}$. Mixing them up loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of ½** in switching-loss derivation. Examiners explicitly test whether you integrated the trapezoidal $v$-$i$ overlap correctly.
> 3. **Using $V_{CE0}$ and $r_{CE}$ for a MOSFET** (or vice-versa). These are IGBT-specific linear-model parameters; the MOSFET uses $R_{DS(on)}$.
> 4. **Ignoring the tail current** in IGBT switching loss. If a problem says "include tail-current contribution", you must add $E_{tail}$ to $E_{off}$.
> 5. **Not converting units** (ns → s, kHz → Hz, mΩ → Ω). A single unit slip can cascade and yield the wrong final answer.
> 6. **Drawing the VDMOS as a lateral MOSFET** — always show the **vertical** layered structure for a power MOSFET.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density summary — read this 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **MOSFET = unipolar, majority-carrier, voltage-controlled** — fast switching (up to 1 MHz), used in **OBC, DC-DC, LV aux**.
- ✅ **IGBT = bipolar (with MOSFET gate), voltage-controlled** — high voltage/current capability, used in **traction inverter**.
- ✅ Power MOSFET uses **VDMOS vertical structure** with **SiO₂ gate insulation**.
- ✅ IGBT has an **additional p⁺ collector layer** that injects holes → **conductivity modulation** → low $V_{CE(sat)}$.
- ✅ MOSFET triode current: $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS}-V_{GS(th)}) V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$.
- ✅ MOSFET saturation current: $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{GS(th)})^2$.
- ✅ MOSFET conduction loss: $P_{cond} = I_{RMS}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$.
- ✅ MOSFET switching loss: $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{on}+t_{off}) f_{sw}$.
- ✅ IGBT conduction loss: $P_{cond} = V_{CE0} I_{C,avg} + r_{CE} I_{C,RMS}^2$.
- ✅ IGBT switching loss: $P_{sw} = f_{sw} (E_{on}+E_{off})$ — **tail current is a major component**.
- ✅ Thermal model: $T_j = T_A + P_{loss} \cdot (R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA})$; $T_{j,max} = 150$ °C.
- ✅ **Miller plateau voltage**: $V_{GP} = V_{GS(th)} + I_D / g_m$ — flat region where $V_{DS}$ falls.
- ✅ **Gate charge $Q_G$** determines gate-drive power: $P_{gate} = Q_G \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_{sw}$.
- ✅ FOM for MOSFET: $R_{DS(on)} \cdot Q_G$ — **lower is better**.
- ✅ FOM for IGBT: $V_{CE(sat)} \cdot E_{tsw}$ — **lower is better**.
- ✅ IGBT has **square RBSOA** (rugged); MOSFET has **RRSOA** (rectangular, less rugged at high V/I).
- ✅ Always state **units explicitly** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguous units.
- ✅ Remember the **buildup**: cutoff → triode → saturation (MOSFET) and cutoff → quasi-saturation → active (IGBT).
- ✅ For exam diagrams: draw **vertical cross-section**, label all layers, mark the **oxide layer**, and indicate the **inversion channel** with an arrow.

---

*End of Module 4 — MOSFET and IGBT — KTU 2024 Scheme Study Notes*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bidirectional Switch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYDRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Course Code:** PEMET863
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bidirectional Switch
> - **Cognitive Level Focus:** Understand (L2) → Apply (L3) → Analyze (L4) — Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme Revised Bloom's Taxonomy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Bidirectional Switch** in power electronics is a fully controllable semiconductor device (or a composite arrangement of devices) that is capable of **blocking voltage of both polarities** and **conducting current in both directions** when triggered by an external gate/base control signal. It is the fundamental building block of advanced converter topologies such as **Matrix Converters**, **Multilevel Inverters** (especially CHB and FCMLI), and **Bidirectional DC-DC Converters** used in modern Hybrid and Electric Vehicle (HEV/EV) powertrains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of PEMET863 (Module 4), the Bidirectional Switch is studied as a critical sub-component enabling **regenerative braking**, **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid)** operation, and **AC-AC direct power conversion** without bulky DC-link energy storage.

## 🌉 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Way Electrical Valve"

Imagine a **water pipe with a special valve**:
- A normal (unidirectional) switch = a **one-way check valve** — water (current) flows in one direction only.
- A **Bidirectional Switch** = a **sophisticated two-way valve** controlled by a single knob (gate signal). You can:
  1. Allow current to flow in **either direction** (forward OR reverse) by simply turning the knob.
  2. **Block voltage** whether the wire is positive (+) or negative (−) on either side.
  3. In HEVs, this means the **traction motor can act as a generator** (during braking) and feed power back to the battery, OR draw power from the battery to drive the wheels.

## 🔑 Key Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 Standard Units)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (EV Application) |
|---|---|---|
| Blocking Voltage (Forward & Reverse) | $V_{DRM}, V_{RRM}$ | **600 V – 1700 V** |
| RMS On-State Current | $I_{T(RMS)}$ | **100 A – 1200 A** |
| Voltage Drop | $V_{CE(sat)}$ / $V_{DS(on)}$ | **1.5 V – 2.5 V** |
| Switching Frequency | $f_{sw}$ | **5 kHz – 20 kHz** |
| Thermal Resistance | $R_{th(j-c)}$ | **0.05 – 0.2 K/W** |

> [!NOTE]
> No single monolithic semiconductor (IGBT, MOSFET, GTO, IGCT) is inherently a true bidirectional switch. It is **realized by combining two or more unidirectional devices** with anti-parallel diodes in a specific arrangement.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## ⚙️ 2.1 The "Why" — Why Are Bidirectional Switches Essential in HEVs?

Modern EV/HEV powertrains require **four-quadrant operation** of the electric machine (Forward Motoring, Reverse Motoring, Forward Braking, Reverse Braking). This four-quadrant operation mandates:

1. **Bidirectional Power Flow:** Battery ↔ Traction Motor (motoring & regeneration).
2. **Reactive Power Handling:** AC machines (PMSM, IM) inherently require the inverter to source/sink reactive power regardless of active power flow direction.
3. **AC-AC Direct Conversion:** Matrix converters eliminate the bulky electrolytic DC-link capacitor (~30% volume reduction).
4. **Multilevel Output:** Reduces $dv/dt$ stress on motor windings (extending insulation life by 2–3×).

## 🔬 2.2 Standard Topologies of Bidirectional Switch (KTU Exam Favourite)

### **Topology 1: Common-Emitter (CE) Bidirectional Switch** ⭐ Most Common in Matrix Converters

Two IGBTs (S1, S2) connected **emitter-to-emitter** with their **anti-parallel diodes (D1, D2)**. The gate drives are referenced to a common emitter point, which **simplifies the gate driver circuit** and reduces isolated supply count.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Common-Emitter** configuration is the **industry standard** for matrix converters because it allows **single-isolated-supply gate driving**, which is critical at high PWM frequencies (5–20 kHz) used in EV traction drives.

### **Topology 2: Common-Collector (CC) Bidirectional Switch**

IGBTs connected **collector-to-collector**. Requires **two isolated gate drives** per bidirectional switch, increasing driver complexity by ~2×. Used where **lower conduction loss** is mandatory (higher current paths share common collector).

### **Topology 3: Diode-Bridged Bidirectional Switch**

A single transistor (IGBT) is placed inside a **diode bridge (4 diodes + 1 switch)**. This uses **only 1 active device** but suffers from:
- **3× higher conduction loss** (current always flows through 2 series diodes + 1 IGBT).
- Used in low-power (<5 kW) auxiliary drives.

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Conduction Loss (per switch)** | $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{rms}^2$ | Heat dissipated when switch is ON. Critical for EV inverter cooling system design. |
| 2 | **Switching Loss (per cycle)** | $E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_{L} \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off})$ | Energy lost per switching transition. Determines switching frequency limit. |
| 3 | **Total Switching Loss** | $P_{sw} = f_{sw} \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off})$ | Total switching losses over one second. |
| 4 | **Total Power Loss** | $P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw}$ | Used to size the heatsink / cold plate in EV. |
| 5 | **RMS Current through IGBT** | $I_{rms} = I_{peak} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D}{3}}$ | For sinusoidal PWM with modulation index $m$ and duty $D$. |
| 6 | **Voltage Blocking Capability** | $V_{block} \geq 1.5 \times V_{DC-link}$ | Safety derating factor (1.5×) for automotive grade. |
| 7 | **Maximum Switching Frequency** | $f_{sw,max} = \frac{P_{loss,thermal}}{E_{sw}}$ | Derived from thermal limit of the package. |
| 8 | **Efficiency of Bidirectional DC-DC** | $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \frac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}}$ | Should be **> 95%** for HEV applications. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important LaTeX Note for KTU Students:** When writing absolute values in exam answer sheets, prefer the notation $\vert x \vert$ or $(x)$ rather than the markdown pipe `\|`, which can confuse examiners when transcribed into answer scripts.

## 🏎️ Real-World Engineering Utility in HEV/EV

- **Toshiba, Infineon, and Wolfspeed** manufacture **1200 V SiC MOSFET bidirectional power modules** specifically for EV traction inverters.
- **Tesla Model 3** uses SiC-based bidirectional switches achieving **> 97% inverter efficiency**.
- **Toyota Prius** uses IGBT-based bidirectional switches in its THS-II power control unit.
- **Formula E Gen3** cars use bidirectional switches for **800 V architectures** with sub-microsecond commutation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 📐 Derivation 1: Total Conduction Loss of a Common-Emitter Bidirectional Switch

> **Problem:** An IGBT-based common-emitter bidirectional switch carries a sinusoidal current $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ with peak value $I_m = 400$ A. The IGBT has on-state voltage $V_{CE(sat)} = 1.8$ V and dynamic resistance $r_{CE} = 4$ m$\Omega$. Find the average conduction loss for **one IGBT** over a full fundamental cycle.

### Step 1: Express Instantaneous Power Dissipation
The instantaneous conduction loss in one IGBT is given by:
$$
p_{cond}(t) = v_{CE}(t) \cdot i(t) = \left[ V_{CE(sat)} + r_{CE} \cdot i(t) \right] \cdot i(t)
$$
Substituting $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$:
$$
p_{cond}(t) = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t) + r_{CE} \cdot I_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Average Over One Full Cycle
Average power over period $T = 2\pi/\omega$:
$$
P_{cond} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} p_{cond}(t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

**Term 1 (Constant voltage drop):**
$$
\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_0^{2\pi} = 0
$$
(Integral of $\sin$ over a full cycle is zero — no contribution to average.)

**Term 2 (Resistive drop):** Using $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:
$$
\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} r_{CE} \cdot I_m^2 \cdot \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t) = \frac{r_{CE} \cdot I_m^2}{4\pi} \left[ 2\pi - 0 \right] = \frac{r_{CE} \cdot I_m^2}{2}
$$

### Step 3: Final Compact Expression
$$
P_{cond,IGBT} = \frac{r_{CE} \cdot I_m^2}{2}
$$

### Step 4: Numerical Evaluation
Substituting $r_{CE} = 4 \times 10^{-3}\ \Omega$ and $I_m = 400$ A:
$$
P_{cond,IGBT} = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (400)^2}{2} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{5}}{2}
$$
$$
P_{cond,IGBT} = \frac{640}{2} = 320\ \text{W}
$$

### Step 5: Loss for the Complete Bidirectional Switch (2 IGBTs)
$$
P_{cond,total} = 2 \times 320 = 640\ \text{W}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $V_{CE(sat)}$ vanished:** The sinusoidal current has zero mean over a full cycle, so the *constant* voltage drop term integrates to zero. This is a classic KTU board exam trick — students often incorrectly add the $V_{CE} \cdot I_{avg}$ term to resistive losses.

---

## 📐 Derivation 2: Switching Loss of Bidirectional Switch in PWM Inverter

> **Problem:** A bidirectional switch in a 400 V EV inverter switches a current of $I_L = 250$ A. Turn-on time $t_{on} = 0.3\ \mu$s, turn-off time $t_{off} = 0.5\ \mu$s. Switching frequency $f_{sw} = 10$ kHz. Calculate total switching loss.

### Step 1: Energy Lost in One Turn-On
$$
E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot t_{on}
$$
$$
E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 400 \cdot 250 \cdot 0.3 \times 10^{-6} = 15 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{J} = 15\ \text{mJ}
$$

### Step 2: Energy Lost in One Turn-Off
$$
E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot t_{off}
$$
$$
E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 400 \cdot 250 \cdot 0.5 \times 10^{-6} = 25 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{J} = 25\ \text{mJ}
$$

### Step 3: Total Energy Lost Per Switching Cycle
$$
E_{sw,total} = E_{on} + E_{off} = 15 + 25 = 40\ \text{mJ}
$$

### Step 4: Total Switching Power Loss
$$
P_{sw} = f_{sw} \cdot E_{sw,total} = 10 \times 10^3 \cdot 40 \times 10^{-3}
$$
$$
P_{sw} = 400\ \text{W}
$$

### Step 5: Total Inverter Loss (Conduction + Switching) Per Bidirectional Switch
$$
P_{loss,total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = 640 + 400 = 1040\ \text{W}
$$

### Step 6: Inverter Efficiency (Assuming 75 kW Traction Drive)
$$
\eta_{inv} = 1 - \frac{1040}{75\,000} = 1 - 0.01387 = 0.9861 = 98.61\ \%
$$
> ✅ **Result Interpretation:** This efficiency ($>98\%$) is consistent with state-of-the-art **SiC-based EV traction inverters** and validates the high-efficiency promise of bidirectional switches.

---

## 📐 Derivation 3: Voltage Derating & Safety Factor

> **KTU Board Pattern Question:** Why do we use $V_{block} \geq 1.5 \times V_{DC-link}$ in EV applications?

### Step 1: Identify Stresses
- DC-link nominal voltage: $V_{DC}$
- Transient over-voltage (load dump, regen): up to **1.3 × $V_{DC}$**
- Switching overshoot (stray inductance $\sim 50$ nH): up to **0.2 × $V_{DC}$**
- Temperature derating at $T_j = 150°$C: **0.9 × rated**

### Step 2: Cumulative Derating
$$
V_{block,min} = V_{DC} \times 1.3 \times \frac{1.2}{0.9} \approx 1.73 \cdot V_{DC}
$$
For a **400 V** EV battery pack:
$$
V_{block,min} \approx 1.5 \times 400 = 600\ \text{V}
$$
Hence, **600 V – 750 V** class IGBTs are universally chosen.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 🔌 4.1 Common-Emitter Bidirectional Switch — Detailed Schematic (ASCII + Mermaid)

```
        Terminal A
           │
      ┌────┴────┐
      │  D1 ◄── │ ── S1 (IGBT, top)
      │         │
      │         ●───── Gate Drive 1 (G1)
      │         │
      └────┬────┘
           │  ◄── Common Emitter Point
      ┌────┴────┐
      │         │
      │         ●───── Gate Drive 2 (G2)
      │         │
      │  D2 ◄── │ ── S2 (IGBT, bottom)
      └────┬────┘
           │
        Terminal B
```

## 🧭 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Bidirectional Switch in EV Powertrain

```mermaid
graph TD
    bat[Battery Pack 400V] --> bdc[Bidirectional DC-DC Converter]
    bdc --> inv[Three-Phase Inverter with Bidirectional Switches]
    inv --> mtr[Traction Motor PMSM]
    mtr -. Regenerative Braking .-> inv
    inv -. Power Flow Direction Reversal .-> bdc
    bdc -. Charging of Battery .-> bat
    ctrl[VCU Vehicle Control Unit] -. Gate Signals .-> inv
    ctrl -. PWM Control .-> bdc
```

## 🏗️ 4.3 Comparison Flow Chart: CE vs CC vs Diode-Bridge

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Bidirectional Switch Topologies] --> B[Common Emitter CE]
    A --> C[Common Collector CC]
    A --> D[Diode Bridge DB]

    B --> B2[Industry Standard]
    C --> C1[2 Isolated Supplies per switch]
    C --> C2[Lower Conduction Loss]

```

## 🔁 4.4 Functional Flow: Commutation Sequence in a Matrix Converter (Bidirectional Switch Application)

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Step 1: Identify load current direction] --> stepB[Step 2: Select proper IGBT pair]
    stepB --> stepC{Step 3: Current direction?}
    stepC -->|Positive| stepD[Turn ON S1 and D1 path]
    stepC -->|Negative| stepE[Turn ON S2 and D2 path]
    stepD --> stepF[Step 4: 4-step commutation logic]
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG[Step 5: Dead-time insertion 0.5 us to prevent shoot-through]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 6: Verify voltage commutation complete]
```

## 📊 4.5 Block Architecture: Bidirectional Switch Module in EV Traction Inverter

```mermaid
graph TD
    pkg[Power Module Package] --> igbt[IGBT Die 1]
    pkg --> igbt2[IGBT Die 2]
    pkg --> diode[Anti-parallel Diode Die 1]
    pkg --> diode2[Anti-parallel Diode Die 2]
    pkg --> dcb[DCB Ceramic Substrate]
    dcb --> base[Copper Baseplate]
    base --> cold[Cold Plate Cooling]
    igbt --> gd[Gate Driver with DESAT Protection]
    igbt2 --> gd
    gd --> iso[Isolated DC-DC Supply]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 🅰️ PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define a bidirectional switch. Why is it not available as a single monolithic device?**

**Model Answer:**
A bidirectional switch is a controllable power electronic device that can **block voltage of both polarities** and **conduct current in both directions** using a single gate control input. It cannot be realized as a single monolithic device because conventional power semiconductors (IGBT, MOSFET, BJT, GTO) are inherently **unidirectional** in either voltage blocking or current conduction. Therefore, it is constructed by **combining two or more unidirectional switches** (typically IGBTs with anti-parallel diodes) in a specific configuration such as the **Common-Emitter** or **Common-Collector** arrangement. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]`
**List any three applications of bidirectional switches in HEV/EV powertrains.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Bidirectional DC-DC Converters** for interfacing 12 V auxiliary battery with 400 V traction battery.
2. **Matrix Converters** for direct AC-AC conversion in two-stage EV drivetrains.
3. **Multilevel Inverters** (CHB, FCMLI) for low-$dv/dt$ traction motor drives.
4. **Regenerative Braking** interfaces that allow reverse power flow from motor to battery.
5. **On-Board Chargers (OBC)** with V2G capability. **(Any 3 × 1 = 3 marks)**

---

## 🅱️ PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Full Descriptive with Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam, Q8 Module 4]`

**A)** **(7 marks)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the **Common-Emitter Bidirectional Switch** configuration. Discuss its advantages over the **Diode-Bridge** configuration.

**Model Solution:**

A common-emitter bidirectional switch consists of **two IGBTs ($S_1$ and $S_2$)** connected with their **emitter terminals tied together** at a common node, and each IGBT has an **anti-parallel diode** ($D_1$, $D_2$) as shown:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| $S_1, D_1$ | Conducts positive current $i > 0$ from Terminal A to B |
| $S_2, D_2$ | Conducts negative current $i < 0$ from Terminal B to A |
| Common Emitter | Single reference point for both gate drives |

**Advantages over Diode-Bridge Configuration:**
1. **Lower conduction loss** — current passes through only 1 IGBT + 1 diode (2 devices) instead of 1 IGBT + 2 diodes (3 devices).
2. **Higher efficiency** (~1–2% improvement, critical in EVs where every Watt-hour matters).
3. **Simpler thermal design** — heat dissipated in fewer dies.
4. **Faster commutation** — fewer parasitic inductances in the current path.

**Disadvantages:**
- Requires **2 isolated gate drive supplies** if both IGBTs need independent control (CC arrangement); CE arrangement needs only **1 isolated supply** per bidirectional switch. **(7 marks complete)**

---

**B)** **(7 marks)** A bidirectional switch in a 600 V EV inverter carries a peak current of 350 A. The IGBT data sheet gives: $V_{CE(sat)} = 2.0$ V, $r_{CE} = 5$ m$\Omega$, $t_{on} = 0.4\ \mu$s, $t_{off} = 0.6\ \mu$s, $f_{sw} = 8$ kHz. Calculate:
1. The conduction loss per IGBT
2. The total switching loss
3. The total power loss of the bidirectional switch

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Conduction Loss per IGBT**

Using the formula derived in Section 3:
$$
P_{cond,IGBT} = \frac{r_{CE} \cdot I_m^2}{2}
$$
Substituting $r_{CE} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \Omega$ and $I_m = 350$ A:
$$
P_{cond,IGBT} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (350)^2}{2} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.225 \times 10^5}{2} = 306.25\ \text{W}
$$

Conduction loss for the complete bidirectional switch (2 IGBTs):
$$
P_{cond,total} = 2 \times 306.25 = 612.5\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 2: Total Switching Loss**

Turn-on energy:
$$
E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 600 \cdot 350 \cdot 0.4 \times 10^{-6} = 42 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{J} = 42\ \text{mJ}
$$

Turn-off energy:
$$
E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 600 \cdot 350 \cdot 0.6 \times 10^{-6} = 63 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{J} = 63\ \text{mJ}
$$

Total switching power loss:
$$
P_{sw} = 8 \times 10^3 \cdot (42 + 63) \times 10^{-3} = 8000 \cdot 0.105 = 840\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 3: Total Power Loss**
$$
P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = 612.5 + 840 = 1452.5\ \text{W} \approx 1.45\ \text{kW}
$$

**Conclusion:** The bidirectional switch dissipates approximately **1.45 kW**, which requires **forced liquid cooling** (cold plate) in the EV traction inverter. **(7 marks complete)**

---

### ✅ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 / Internal Choice)
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Exam, Q9 Module 4]`

**A)** **(7 marks)** Compare **Common-Emitter**, **Common-Collector**, and **Diode-Bridge** bidirectional switch configurations in terms of: (i) number of active devices, (ii) conduction loss, (iii) gate drive complexity, (iv) typical application.

**Model Solution (Tabular Comparison):**

| Parameter | Common-Emitter (CE) | Common-Collector (CC) | Diode-Bridge (DB) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active Devices | **2 IGBTs** | **2 IGBTs** | **1 IGBT** |
| Diodes | 2 (anti-parallel) | 2 (anti-parallel) | 4 (bridge) |
| Conduction Devices in path | 1 IGBT + 1 Diode | 1 IGBT + 1 Diode | 1 IGBT + 2 Diodes |
| Conduction Loss | **Medium** | **Medium** | **High (3×)** |
| Isolated Gate Supplies | **1 (per switch)** | **2 (per switch)** | 1 |
| Gate Drive Complexity | Low | **High** | Lowest |
| Typical Application | Matrix Converters, HEV Inverters | High-current FCMLI | Low-power auxiliaries (<5 kW) |
| Cost | Medium | **High** | Low |

**Inference:** The **Common-Emitter** configuration offers the best trade-off and is the **industry standard** for EV traction drives. **(7 marks)**

---

**B)** **(7 marks)** Explain the role of **bidirectional switches in matrix converters** for EV applications. What is the commutation challenge in matrix converters and how is it solved using 4-step commutation?

**Model Solution:**

**Role in Matrix Converters:**
A matrix converter directly converts a 3-phase AC supply (e.g., from a generator) into a variable-frequency 3-phase AC for the traction motor, **without any DC-link energy storage**. This eliminates bulky electrolytic capacitors, reducing:
- Inverter weight by **30–40%**
- Volume by **~35%**
- Improves power density to **> 20 kW/L** (vs ~10 kW/L for conventional VSI)

Each of the **9 bidirectional switches** in a 3×3 matrix converter must block bi-directional voltage and conduct bi-directional current.

**Commutation Challenge:**
Since the input phases are **stiff voltage sources**, a **shoot-through** (short circuit between two input phases) caused by imperfect turn-off and turn-on timing can produce **catastrophic fault currents** of thousands of amperes — destroying the switches within microseconds.

**Solution: 4-Step Commutation Logic**
The 4-step commutation sequence ensures that:
1. The incoming switch is **NOT turned ON** until the outgoing switch is **fully OFF**.
2. The direction of **load current** is sensed in real time (using current transducers or shunts).
3. The correct pair of switches is activated based on current direction.

| Step | Action | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Step 1** | Turn OFF outgoing switch that is **not carrying current** | First safety action |
| **Step 2** | Turn ON the new switch that **will not create a short circuit** | Enable alternative path |
| **Step 3** | Turn OFF the previously conducting switch | Complete current transfer |
| **Step 4** | Turn ON the final switch in the new path | Restore full conduction |

**Dead-time insertion** of **0.5 – 1.0 µs** between steps prevents any overlap.

> **Result:** Commutation is achieved in ~2–4 µs, fully eliminating shoot-through risk, enabling **safe 4-quadrant operation** of the EV traction drive. **(7 marks complete)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** A bidirectional switch has **2 IGBTs** — students often compute loss for 1 IGBT and present it as the switch loss, losing **2 marks**.
> 2. **Using $V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg}$ directly:** For sinusoidal current, the mean is zero. The conduction loss is purely resistive. Adding a $V_{CE} \cdot I_{avg}$ term is a **common KTU board trap** that costs 1 mark.
> 3. **No mention of commutation:** A bidirectional switch answer without discussing **commutation sequence** and **shoot-through protection** loses at least **2 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Confusing CE and CC topologies:** The terminal connection is **emitter-to-emitter** (CE) vs **collector-to-collector** (CC). Drawing the wrong diagram loses **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **Missing units in derivation:** Always write **V, A, W, Hz, J** — unitless answers are penalized in the KTU 2024 scheme.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ A **Bidirectional Switch** blocks **bi-polar voltage** and conducts **bi-directional current**.
- ✅ It is **NOT a single device** — it is built using **2 IGBTs + 2 anti-parallel diodes** (or 1 IGBT + 4 diodes for diode-bridge).
- ✅ The **Common-Emitter (CE)** configuration is preferred in EV matrix converters due to **single isolated gate supply**.
- ✅ The **Common-Collector (CC)** configuration is used when **lower conduction loss** is critical.
- ✅ The **Diode-Bridge** configuration has **3× higher conduction loss** and is used in **low-power** systems.
- ✅ Conduction loss for sinusoidal current: $P_{cond} = \dfrac{r_{CE} \cdot I_m^2}{2}$ (per IGBT).
- ✅ Switching loss: $P_{sw} = f_{sw} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DC} I_L (t_{on} + t_{off})$.
- ✅ **Voltage derating** factor of **1.5×** is mandatory in EV applications.
- ✅ **4-step commutation** prevents shoot-through faults in matrix converters.
- ✅ Bidirectional switches enable **regenerative braking**, **V2G**, and **AC-AC direct conversion** in HEVs.
- ✅ Modern EV traction inverters using **SiC bidirectional switches** achieve **> 97–98%** efficiency.
- ✅ Always mention **dead-time** (~0.5–1.0 µs) in commutation answers to earn full marks.
- ✅ Total loss of switch = $P_{cond} + P_{sw}$ — used to size the **heatsink / cold plate**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Induction Motor Controls - Constant Speed/Frequency Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Induction Motor Controls - Constant Speed/Frequency Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Constant Voltage-to-Frequency (V/f) Control**, also known as **Scalar Control** or **Volts-per-Hertz Control**, is the fundamental open-loop speed-control strategy used for three-phase squirrel-cage induction motors (SCIM) deployed in Hybrid and Electric Vehicle (HEV/EV) powertrains. It enforces a proportional relationship between the RMS stator line voltage ($V_s$) and the fundamental supply frequency ($f_s$) such that the air-gap flux ($\Phi_m$) remains approximately constant over a wide operating range.

Mathematically, the control law is:

$$\frac{V_s}{f_s} = \text{constant} = k$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Constant V/f control is the **base strategy** upon which all advanced Field-Oriented Control (FOC) and Direct Torque Control (DTC) techniques are built. Every KTU question on induction motor drives assumes mastery of this foundation.

## 1.2 Why Induction Motors Need Speed Control

The synchronous mechanical speed of an induction motor is rigidly tied to the supply frequency by the **synchronous speed equation**:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f_s}{P}$$

where $P$ is the number of stator poles (always an even integer). Since $P$ is mechanically fixed once the motor is manufactured, the only practical way to vary motor speed in a vehicle is to **vary $f_s$** using a power electronic inverter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant to Remember:** The constant **120** in the synchronous speed equation converts electrical frequency (Hz → cycles/sec) into mechanical RPM (revolutions/min), accounting for $60$ seconds per minute and $2$ poles per electrical cycle.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Accelerator Pedal of an Electric Motor

Imagine a ceiling fan connected to a regular $50$ Hz wall socket — it always spins at the same speed. Now imagine fitting that fan with an **electronic dimmer-style knob** for rotation. That knob is essentially a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)**. 

- Turn the knob **up** → frequency rises → fan spins faster (like pressing the EV accelerator pedal).
- Turn the knob **down** → frequency drops → fan slows down (regenerative braking feel).

However, here's the engineering catch: if you simply *reduce* the frequency without adjusting the voltage, the motor behaves like a **weak person trying to lift a heavy box** — the magnetic field collapses, torque collapses, and the motor may stall. The V/f controller is the **electronic "muscle coordinator"** that simultaneously scales the voltage and frequency so the motor always has just enough "magnetic strength" (flux) to deliver the demanded torque at any speed.

## 1.4 The Slip Mechanism

The actual rotor speed $N_r$ is always *less* than the synchronous speed $N_s$ by a small percentage called the **slip** $s$:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}, \qquad N_r = N_s(1-s)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for Slip:** Think of the rotating stator magnetic field as a **moving walkway** at an airport. The rotor is the *traveler* walking on it. The traveler never quite matches the walkway speed — that small relative speed is the **slip**, and it is precisely this slip that *induces* currents in the rotor bars and produces torque. Without slip, there is no induction, and therefore no torque.

In an EV, typical full-load slip of a traction induction motor is in the range of **2% to 5%** at rated conditions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Electromagnetic Principle

The RMS air-gap EMF induced in the stator winding is given by the **transformer EMF equation** adapted for rotating machines:

$$E_1 = 4.44 \cdot f_s \cdot N_{ph} \cdot \Phi_m \cdot K_w$$

where:
- $E_1$ = RMS air-gap voltage per phase (V)
- $N_{ph}$ = number of turns per phase
- $\Phi_m$ = peak air-gap flux per pole (Wb)
- $K_w$ = winding factor (typically $0.85$–$0.95$)

Solving for the flux:

$$\Phi_m = \frac{E_1}{4.44 \cdot f_s \cdot N_{ph} \cdot K_w}$$

Under normal running conditions (ignoring the small stator impedance drop), $E_1 \approx V_s / \sqrt{3}$ (line-to-neutral) or $V_L / \sqrt{3}$ for star-connected stators. Therefore:

$$\Phi_m \approx \frac{V_s}{4.44 \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot f_s \cdot N_{ph} \cdot K_w}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Insight:** Flux $\Phi_m$ is **inversely proportional to frequency** for a fixed voltage. Doubling $f_s$ halves the flux. Halving $f_s$ doubles the flux (which can saturate the iron core). The V/f control law **prevents both flux collapse and flux saturation** by scaling $V_s$ in lockstep with $f_s$.

## 2.2 The Torque Equation Under V/f Control

Starting from the per-phase equivalent circuit referred to the stator, the electromagnetic torque developed by an induction motor is:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot \left(\frac{R_2'}{s}\right)}{\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

where $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s / (\text{no. of pole pairs})$ is the synchronous angular velocity in rad/s.

The **maximum (breakdown) torque** is obtained by differentiating with respect to slip and setting to zero:
