$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2}}$$

Since $X_1 + X_2' = 2\pi f_s (L_1 + L_2')$, at high frequencies where $X \gg R_1$:

$$T_{max} \approx \frac{3}{2\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{X_1+X_2'} = \frac{3}{2\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{2\pi f_s (L_1+L_2')}$$

Note that $\omega_s \propto f_s$, so:

$$T_{max} \propto \left(\frac{V_1}{f_s}\right)^2$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical Consequence:** When $V_1/f_s$ is held constant, the **maximum torque capability remains constant** across the entire constant-torque operating region. This is the *engineering reason* why V/f control is the workhorse of EV traction — it gives predictable torque from standstill to base speed.

## 2.3 Operating Regions of an EV Traction Induction Motor Drive

A VFD-fed induction motor drive in an EV operates in three distinct regions as speed increases from zero to maximum:

### Region I — Constant Torque Region (Below Base Speed)
- $f_s$ is varied from $0$ Hz to $f_{base}$ (typically $50$–$200$ Hz for EV).
- $V_s / f_s = \text{constant}$, so flux $\Phi_m$ is constant.
- Maximum torque and rated torque are both available.
- Voltage is linearly proportional to frequency.
- This is the **primary propulsion region** for normal city/highway driving.

### Region II — Constant Power Region (Above Base Speed)
- $f_s > f_{base}$, but $V_s$ cannot exceed the DC-link voltage of the battery/inverter, so $V_s = V_{rated}$ (constant).
- Therefore $V_s / f_s$ decreases, flux decreases — this is the **field-weakening region**.
- Maximum torque decreases as $1/f_s$ (or $1/\omega_s$), but power $P = T \cdot \omega$ remains approximately constant.
- Used for **high-speed cruising and highway acceleration** beyond base speed.

### Region III — Constant Slip / Series Region (Very High Speeds)
- Both voltage and frequency are at their hardware limits.
- Only natural droop characteristics apply.
- Rarely used in modern EVs (motor controller enforces Region II limits).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Valid Region |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|--------------|
| 1 | $N_s = 120 f_s / P$ | Synchronous speed | RPM | All |
| 2 | $s = (N_s - N_r) / N_s$ | Per-unit slip | dimensionless | All |
| 3 | $N_r = N_s(1-s)$ | Rotor speed | RPM | All |
| 4 | $\Phi_m \approx V_s / (4.44 \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot f_s \cdot N_{ph} \cdot K_w)$ | Air-gap flux | Wb | $E_1 \gg I_1 R_1$ |
| 5 | $V_s / f_s = k$ (constant) | V/f control law | V/Hz | Region I |
| 6 | $T_{max} \propto (V_s/f_s)^2$ | Max torque ∝ (V/f)² | N·m | Region I |
| 7 | $T_{max} \approx 3 V_s^2 / (2 \omega_s (X_1+X_2'))$ | Max torque (high-f approx.) | N·m | Region I |
| 8 | $P_{gap} = T_e \cdot \omega_s$ | Air-gap power | W | All |
| 9 | $P_{m} = T_e \cdot \omega_r = T_e \cdot \omega_s (1-s)$ | Mechanical power output | W | All |
| 10 | $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_{gap}$ | Rotor copper loss | W | All |
| 11 | $V_{boost} = I_1 R_1$ | Low-frequency voltage boost | V | $f_s < 5$ Hz |
| 12 | $S_{base} = 3 V_{rated} I_{rated}$ | Rated apparent power | VA | Base point |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical bars used in the table above** — every magnitude is expressed via parentheses or `\propto` to keep Mermaid/Markdown rendering safe.

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

In modern EVs (Tesla Model S/X legacy drivetrain, Toyota Prius hybrid synergy drive, Tata Nexon EV), the V/f control law is implemented in the **Motor Control Unit (MCU)** firmware, executed in real-time on a Digital Signal Processor (DSP) or microcontroller. The inverter switches at frequencies of $5$–$20$ kHz using **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)** or **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)** to synthesize a variable-frequency, variable-amplitude three-phase output from a fixed DC-link voltage ($V_{DC}$ from the high-voltage battery, typically $400$ V in modern EVs).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Synchronous Speed from Frequency

**Given:** A three-phase, 4-pole, $50$ Hz induction motor.

**Find:** Synchronous speed in RPM.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
$P = 4$ poles, $f_s = 50$ Hz.

**Step 2 — Apply the synchronous speed equation.**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f_s}{P}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot 50}{4} = \frac{6000}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to rad/s (often required in KTU problems).**

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rotor Speed from Slip

**Given:** Same motor running at full load with $s = 0.04$ ($4\%$ slip).

**Find:** Rotor speed in RPM and rad/s.

**Step 1 — Compute rotor speed in RPM.**

$$N_r = N_s(1-s) = 1500 \cdot (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \cdot 0.96 = 1440 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2 — Convert rotor speed to rad/s.**

$$\omega_r = \frac{2\pi N_r}{60} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 1440}{60} = 48\pi \approx 150.80 \text{ rad/s}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Derivation of V/f = Constant for Constant Flux

**Starting point:** Stator KVL per phase (approximate equivalent circuit at low slip, ignoring magnetizing branch shift):

$$V_1 \approx E_1 = 4.44 \cdot f_s \cdot N_{ph} \cdot \Phi_m \cdot K_w$$

**Step 1 — Solve for flux.**

$$\Phi_m = \frac{V_1}{4.44 \cdot f_s \cdot N_{ph} \cdot K_w}$$

**Step 2 — Group the constants.** Let $C = 4.44 \cdot N_{ph} \cdot K_w$ (machine constant, fixed for a given motor).

$$\Phi_m = \frac{V_1}{C \cdot f_s}$$

**Step 3 — Enforce the constant-flux requirement.** For $\Phi_m = \Phi_{rated} = \text{constant}$:

$$\frac{V_1}{f_s} = C \cdot \Phi_{rated} = \text{constant} = k$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** This is the *complete algebraic proof* that **constant V/f ⇒ constant air-gap flux**. The constant $k$ is tuned by the inverter's PWM modulation index $m_a = 2\sqrt{2} V_s / V_{DC}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Torque Invariance Under V/f Control

**Starting point:** Maximum torque formula (approximated for $R_1 \ll X_1+X_2'$):

$$T_{max} \approx \frac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{X_1 + X_2'}$$

**Step 1 — Express synchronous angular speed.**

$$\omega_s = 2\pi f_s / (\text{pole pairs}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_s \propto f_s$$

**Step 2 — Express leakage reactance.** $X_1 + X_2' = 2\pi f_s (L_1 + L_2')$, so $X \propto f_s$.

**Step 3 — Substitute into $T_{max}$.**

$$T_{max} \approx \frac{3}{2 \cdot c_1 f_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{c_2 f_s} = \frac{3}{2 c_1 c_2} \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{f_s^2}$$

where $c_1, c_2$ are proportionality constants.

**Step 4 — Apply V/f = k ⇒ $V_1 = k f_s$.**

$$T_{max} \approx \frac{3}{2 c_1 c_2} \cdot \frac{k^2 f_s^2}{f_s^2} = \frac{3 k^2}{2 c_1 c_2} = \text{constant}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Under V/f control, the maximum torque is **completely independent of frequency** in the constant-torque region. This guarantees that the EV traction motor can deliver the same peak torque whether the vehicle is creeping at $2$ km/h or cruising at $60$ km/h.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Voltage Boost at Low Frequency

**Problem:** At very low frequencies, the stator resistance drop $I_1 R_1$ becomes a significant fraction of $V_1$, so the actual air-gap EMF $E_1$ is much smaller than $V_1$. The flux therefore drops below rated value and torque is lost. To compensate, we add a **voltage boost** term $V_0$.

**Step 1 — Stator KVL (more accurate).**

$$V_1 = E_1 + I_1 R_1 + j I_1 X_1$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude relation.**

$$V_1 = \sqrt{(E_1 \cos\delta + I_1 R_1)^2 + (E_1 \sin\delta + I_1 X_1)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Approximation for low slip and low frequency ($X_1 \to 0$).**

$$V_1 \approx E_1 + I_1 R_1$$

**Step 4 — Solve for required $V_1$ to maintain $E_1 = k f_s$.**

$$V_1 = k f_s + I_1 R_1$$

**Step 5 — Define voltage boost.**

$$\boxed{V_{boost} = I_1 R_1 \quad \text{(added to the linear } V/f \text{ profile below } \approx 5 \text{ Hz)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Implementation:** The boost voltage $V_{boost}$ is typically $2$–$8\%$ of rated voltage, and is *tapered* linearly to zero as $f_s$ approaches $5$–$10$ Hz, transitioning smoothly into the pure linear V/f line.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Example — Full V/f Profile Design

**Specifications:** $5$ kW, 4-pole, 3-phase, $400$ V, $50$ Hz induction motor. Rated current $I_1 = 9.5$ A. Stator resistance $R_1 = 1.2 \ \Omega$.

**Step 1 — Compute rated V/f ratio.**

$$k = \frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}} = \frac{400}{50} = 8 \text{ V/Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Compute voltage boost.**

$$V_{boost} = I_1 R_1 = 9.5 \cdot 1.2 = 11.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Construct the V/f profile table.**

| $f_s$ (Hz) | $V_s$ (linear, V) | $V_s$ (with boost, V) | $N_s$ (RPM) | Region |
|------------|-------------------|------------------------|-------------|--------|
| 0          | 0                 | 11.4                   | 0           | Boost  |
| 5          | 40                | 51.4                   | 150         | Boost  |
| 10         | 80                | 80 (boost tapered out) | 300         | Const-T|
| 25         | 200               | 200                    | 750         | Const-T|
| 50         | 400               | 400                    | 1500        | Base   |
| 75         | 600 (clamped)     | 400 (clamped)          | 2250        | Field-W|
| 100        | 800 (clamped)     | 400 (clamped)          | 3000        | Field-W|

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
- For $f_s \le 50$ Hz: linear V/f ⇒ constant flux ⇒ constant torque capability.
- For $f_s > 50$ Hz: voltage saturates at $V_{DC}$ limit ⇒ flux decreases ⇒ constant power region.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete V/f-Controlled EV Traction Drive

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BAT[Battery Pack VDC] --> INV[Three-Phase VSI Inverter]
    CMD[Torque Command from Accelerator Pedal] --> CTRL[V/f Control Algorithm]
    SPD[Rotor Speed Sensor Tachometer] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> PWM[SPWM or SVPWM Modulator]
    PWM --> INV
    INV --> IM[Squirrel Cage Induction Motor]
    IM --> GEAR[Fixed Ratio Gearbox]
    GEAR --> WHL[EV Drive Wheels]
    IM -.-> SPD
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the V/f Controller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FREF[Frequency Reference fref] --> DIV{V/f Divider}
    VREF[Voltage Reference Vref] --> DIV
    DIV --> MOD[Modulation Index ma]
    MOD --> SIN[Sinusoidal Reference Generator]
    SIN --> PWMGEN[PWM Comparator]
    CAR[High Frequency Triangle Carrier] --> PWMGEN
    PWMGEN --> GATE[Inverter Gate Pulses G1 to G6]
    GATE --> INVPOW[Three Phase Inverter Power Stage]
    INVPOW --> MOTOR[Induction Motor]
    MOTOR --> SENS[Sensor Feedback]
    SENS --> FREF
```

## 4.3 Operating Regions of a V/f-Controlled Induction Motor Drive

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START([Motor Starts]) --> R1[Region 1 Constant Torque Zone]
    R1 --> Q1{fs less than fbase}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1
    Q1 -- No --> R2[Region 2 Constant Power Field Weakening]
    R2 --> Q2{fs less than fmax}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2
    Q2 -- No --> R3[Region 3 High Speed Series Mode]
    R3 --> STOP([Maximum Speed Reached])
    R1 -.- NOTE1[V and f scale linearly flux constant torque constant]
    R2 -.- NOTE2[V fixed at Vrated f rises flux drops torque drops 1 by fs]
    R3 -.- NOTE3[Both V and f at hardware limits]
```

## 4.4 V/f Voltage Profile (Voltage vs Frequency)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Zero Speed] --> B[Voltage Boost Region 0 to 5 Hz]
    B --> C[Linear V/f Region 5 to 50 Hz]
    C --> D[Base Point at 50 Hz 400 V]
    D --> E[Constant Voltage Region 50 to 100 Hz]
    E --> F[Maximum Frequency Limit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Profile:** The horizontal axis is frequency (Hz), vertical axis is stator RMS voltage (V). The curve rises linearly with a small initial offset (boost), then bends horizontal after base frequency, indicating the field-weakening region.

## 4.5 Speed–Torque Characteristics Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONSTV[Constant Voltage 50 Hz Only]
        CV1[Single Curve]
    end
    subgraph VFCTRL[V/f Control Multiple Frequencies]
        VF1[Curve at 50 Hz]
        VF2[Curve at 25 Hz]
        VF3[Curve at 10 Hz]
        VF4[Curve at 75 Hz Field Weakened]
    end
    CONSTV -.- OBS1[Peak Torque Varies with Frequency]
    VFCTRL -.- OBS2[Peak Torque Nearly Same Across All Curves in Region 1]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Set B]`

**Q: Define the term "volts per hertz (V/f) control" of a three-phase induction motor. Why is it preferred for EV traction applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

V/f control is a scalar speed-control technique for induction motors in which the **ratio of stator RMS voltage to supply frequency** is maintained constant throughout the operating range, i.e., $V_s / f_s = k$.

The control law is derived from the air-gap EMF equation $E_1 = 4.44 f_s N_{ph} \Phi_m K_w$, from which flux $\Phi_m \propto V_s / f_s$. By keeping $V_s / f_s$ constant, the air-gap flux is preserved at its rated value, ensuring **constant maximum torque capability** across the entire speed range.

For EV traction, this control is preferred because:
1. It delivers **constant torque from zero to base speed**, matching driver demand.
2. It enables **smooth acceleration and regenerative braking** transitions.
3. It is **simple to implement** on inverter hardware (PWM-based).
4. It avoids the complexity of closed-loop vector control during low-speed torque demand.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set A]`

**Q: What is meant by "voltage boost" in V/f control? When and why is it applied?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Voltage boost is a small **additive DC offset voltage** $V_{boost} = I_1 R_1$ that is superimposed on the linear V/f profile at very low frequencies (typically below $5$–$10$ Hz).

**Why it is needed:** At low frequency, the inductive reactance $X_1 = 2\pi f_s L_1$ becomes negligibly small, but the stator resistance drop $I_1 R_1$ remains unchanged. Consequently, the air-gap voltage $E_1 = V_1 - I_1 R_1$ becomes much smaller than $V_1$, and the flux $\Phi_m \propto E_1 / f_s$ drops below rated. This causes loss of starting torque. The voltage boost compensates for the $I_1 R_1$ drop, maintaining rated flux and ensuring adequate low-speed torque during vehicle launch.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Set A]`

**(a) Derive the relationship between stator voltage and frequency for constant-flux operation of a three-phase induction motor. Show that the maximum torque is independent of frequency when V/f is held constant. (7 marks)**

**(b) A 3-phase, 4-pole, $50$ Hz, $400$ V induction motor has full-load slip of $4\%$. The motor is to be operated from a V/f inverter at $25$ Hz. Compute (i) the required stator voltage to maintain rated flux, (ii) the new synchronous speed, and (iii) the rotor speed if the slip is maintained at $4\%$. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Write the air-gap EMF per phase.

$$E_1 = 4.44 \cdot f_s \cdot N_{ph} \cdot \Phi_m \cdot K_w$$

**Step 2:** Solve for flux.

$$\Phi_m = \frac{E_1}{4.44 \cdot f_s \cdot N_{ph} \cdot K_w}$$

**Step 3:** Since $E_1 \approx V_1$ at moderate loads and the denominator terms $4.44 N_{ph} K_w$ are machine constants, flux is proportional to $V_1 / f_s$.

**Step 4:** For $\Phi_m = \Phi_{rated}$, we require $V_1 / f_s = \text{constant} = k$.

**Step 5:** Maximum torque formula.

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2}}$$

**Step 6:** At frequencies where $X \gg R_1$, $T_{max} \approx \dfrac{3 V_1^2}{2 \omega_s (X_1+X_2')}$.

**Step 7:** Substitute $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s / p$ and $X_1+X_2' = 2\pi f_s (L_1+L_2')$.

**Step 8:** Apply $V_1 = k f_s$.

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 (k f_s)^2}{2 \cdot (2\pi f_s / p) \cdot 2\pi f_s (L_1+L_2')} = \frac{3 k^2 p}{8 \pi^2 (L_1+L_2')} = \text{constant}$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum torque is independent of frequency in the constant V/f region.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute rated V/f ratio.**

$$k = \frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}} = \frac{400}{50} = 8 \text{ V/Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Required stator voltage at $25$ Hz.**

$$V_{new} = k \cdot f_{new} = 8 \cdot 25 = 200 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — New synchronous speed at $25$ Hz, 4 poles.**

$$N_{s,new} = \frac{120 \cdot 25}{4} = 750 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4 — Rotor speed at $4\%$ slip.**

$$N_{r,new} = N_{s,new} \cdot (1 - s) = 750 \cdot (1 - 0.04) = 750 \cdot 0.96 = 720 \text{ RPM}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students **incorrectly** assume the slip remains the same in absolute RPM (e.g., $60$ RPM) rather than in *per-unit* form. Slip is a **dimensionless ratio** and must remain a *fraction* of the new synchronous speed. A drop in $N_s$ with the *same per-unit* slip gives a *smaller* absolute slip speed.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set A]`

**(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the open-loop V/f speed control scheme for a three-phase induction motor used in an electric vehicle. List the major blocks and explain the role of PWM. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the three operating regions (constant torque, constant power, and field-weakening) of an EV traction induction motor drive. Sketch the typical torque–speed and power–speed envelopes. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram Description (refer to Section 4.1):**

The major functional blocks are:
1. **Reference Generator:** Accepts accelerator pedal position and outputs $V^*$ and $f^*$ such that $V^*/f^* = k$ (constant).
2. **PWM Modulator:** Compares three sinusoidal reference waves (modulating signals at $f_s$) with a high-frequency triangular carrier wave to generate gate pulses.
3. **Three-Phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI):** Six IGBT/MOSFET switches (S1–S6) convert fixed DC-link voltage into variable-frequency, variable-amplitude three-phase AC.
4. **Induction Motor:** The squirrel-cage rotor receives the variable-frequency supply.
5. **Speed Sensor (Tachometer/Resolver):** Provides feedback of $N_r$ for closed-loop speed regulation.

**Role of PWM:** PWM synthesizes a sinusoidal current waveform of the desired fundamental frequency $f_s$ and RMS voltage $V_s$ by modulating the *duty cycle* of the inverter switches. The **modulation index** $m_a = \hat{V}_{control} / \hat{V}_{tri}$ controls the output voltage amplitude, while the **frequency of the reference sinusoid** controls $f_s$. SPWM/SVPWM enables efficient, low-harmonic, fast dynamic response — essential for EV regenerative braking transitions.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Region I — Constant Torque Region (Below Base Speed):**
- $0 \le f_s \le f_{base}$.
- $V_s$ increases linearly with $f_s$ ⇒ flux $\Phi_m$ constant.
- Torque capability remains at its rated maximum value $T_{max}$.
- This is the **primary operating region** for vehicle launch, hill climbing, and low-speed acceleration.

**Region II — Constant Power Region (Above Base Speed, Field Weakening):**
- $f_s > f_{base}$, $V_s$ clamped at $V_{DC}$.
- Flux $\Phi_m$ decreases as $1/f_s$ ⇒ $T_{max}$ decreases as $1/f_s$.
- Power $P = T \cdot \omega_s$ remains approximately constant.
- Used for **highway cruising and high-speed overtaking**.

**Region III — High-Speed Series Region:**
- $f_s$ approaches the inverter's maximum switching-limited frequency.
- Torque drops rapidly with $1/f_s^2$.
- Generally avoided; controller enforces end of Region II before reaching Region III.

**Torque–Speed and Power–Speed Envelope Sketch (textual):**

```
Torque  |  Tmax ---  ____________________________
 (N.m)  |           |
        |           |\
        |           | \
        |           |  \  Region II (1/fs)
        |           |   \
        |           |    \________________________
        |___________|______________________________
        0          Nbase                         Nmax   Speed (RPM)


Power   |                 _____________________
 (kW)   |                /
        |               /
  Prated|--------------/--------------------
        |             /
        |            /  Region I
        |___________/________________________________
        0         Nbase                          Nmax    Speed (RPM)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly state that **power is constant in Region I** (it is *torque* that is constant in Region I; power rises linearly with speed). Similarly, students must not confuse the **field-weakening region** (Region II) with the **constant-flux region** (Region I) — the voltage is *clamped* in Region II, not free to scale.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f_s / P$ — memorize as the foundational equation.
- **Slip** $s$ is per-unit; keep it dimensionless. $N_r = N_s(1-s)$.
- **V/f control** is a **scalar**, **open-loop** method that maintains **constant air-gap flux** by enforcing $V_s / f_s = k$.
- **Constant V/f ⇒ constant max torque** (in the region where $X \gg R_1$).
- **Voltage boost** $V_{boost} = I_1 R_1$ is added below $\approx 5$ Hz to compensate for stator resistance drop.
- **Three operating regions:** (1) Constant Torque (V/f linear), (2) Constant Power / Field Weakening (V clamped, f rises), (3) Series / High-speed droop.
- **Inverter** uses **PWM (SPWM or SVPWM)** with modulation index $m_a$ controlling amplitude and reference frequency controlling $f_s$.
- **In EVs**, the traction inverter switches at $5$–$20$ kHz and draws power from a $400$ V (or $800$ V in modern architectures) DC-link battery.
- **Slip is the only mechanism** by which an induction motor produces torque — no slip, no torque.
- **Above base speed**, the EV traction motor operates in **field weakening**, trading torque for speed while keeping power roughly constant.
- **V/f control does not control torque directly** — it controls flux; torque then depends on load slip. This is the chief limitation that motivates FOC/DTC in high-performance EVs.
- **Typical EV traction motor base speeds** are in the range of $3000$–$6000$ RPM (mechanical), with maximum speeds of $12000$–$18000$ RPM.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variable DC/AC Inverter with Sinusoidal PWM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Variable DC/AC Inverter with Sinusoidal PWM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Variable DC/AC Inverter** is a power electronic converter that synthesizes a controllable AC output voltage from a fixed or variable DC source (typically a high-voltage traction battery in an EV) by rapidly switching semiconductor power devices (IGBTs / MOSFETs / SiC-MOSFETs). When the switching instants are determined by **Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM)**, the inverter is classified as a **PWM-controlled Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET863, Module 4):** The inverter is the *heart* of the electric drivetrain. It interfaces the **DC battery bus** with the **AC traction motor** and must deliver variable voltage **AND** variable frequency simultaneously to achieve efficient field-weakening and constant-power operation.

Mathematically, the inverter produces an output whose fundamental component obeys:

$$v_{o,1}(t) = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{DC}}{2} \sin(\omega_s t)$$

where $m_a$ is the **amplitude modulation index**, $V_{DC}$ is the DC-link voltage, and $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$ is the desired stator electrical frequency.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water-Tap Translator"

Imagine the EV battery as a **high-pressure water tank (constant DC pressure)** and the AC traction motor as a **musical water fountain** that needs oscillating water jets to spin gracefully.

- A **direct connection** would just blast a constant stream — the motor would draw huge uncontrolled current and overheat.
- A **mechanical valve** that opens/closes very fast (a "tap") can shape the water into pulses.
- If you open the tap **wider for longer** when the fountain needs a strong push (the peak of the sine wave) and **shorter** when only a gentle push is needed (near zero crossing), the *average* water flow over each tap-cycle mimics a perfect sine wave.

That tap is the **transistor switch**, and the *intelligent timing pattern* dictating how long it stays open is **Sinusoidal PWM**.

## 1.3 Why SPWM for Electric Vehicles?

| EV Requirement | How SPWM Satisfies It |
|---|---|
| Variable speed (0 → 15,000 rpm) | Variable output frequency $f_s$ from **0 Hz to ~1 kHz** |
| Variable torque at low speed | Variable output voltage via $m_a$ (V/Hz kept constant) |
| Smooth torque, low acoustic noise | Shifts harmonics to high frequencies (around $f_{carrier}$) — easily filtered by motor inductance |
| High efficiency (>95%) | Soft-switching friendly; semiconductor losses minimized |
| Regenerative braking | Bi-directional power flow (same H-bridge, reversed $i$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Memorize for KTU:**
> - Carrier frequency $f_c$: typically **1 kHz – 20 kHz** (SiC enables >50 kHz)
> - Switching frequency $f_{sw}$: equals $f_c$ in bipolar, equals $2f_c$ in unipolar SPWM
> - DC-link voltage $V_{DC}$: **300 V – 800 V** in modern BEVs
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Three-Phase VSI

A standard two-level three-phase VSI consists of **three half-bridge legs**, each with two complementary switches (e.g., $S_1, \bar{S_1}$, $S_2, \bar{S_2}$, $S_3, \bar{S_3}$).

For each leg $k \in \{1,2,3\}$:

$$v_{kN}(t) = \begin{cases} +V_{DC}/2 & \text{if } S_k = 1 \\ -V_{DC}/2 & \text{if } S_k = 0 \end{cases}$$

The **line-to-line voltage** between phase $a$ and phase $b$ is:

$$v_{ab}(t) = v_{aN}(t) - v_{bN}(t)$$

And the **phase voltage** (assuming balanced Y-connected load with neutral $n$):

$$v_{an}(t) = v_{aN}(t) - v_{bn}(t) - v_{cn}(t))/3$$

## 2.2 The Two Reference Signals for SPWM

To generate a balanced three-phase output, three sinusoidal modulating waves are used, **120° apart**:

$$v_{control,a}(t) = V_{ctrl} \sin(\omega_s t)$$
$$v_{control,b}(t) = V_{ctrl} \sin(\omega_s t - 2\pi/3)$$
$$v_{control,c}(t) = V_{ctrl} \sin(\omega_s t - 4\pi/3)$$

These are continuously compared against a **high-frequency triangular carrier wave** $v_{tri}(t)$ of amplitude $V_{tri}$ and frequency $f_c$.

### The Switching Rule
- If $v_{control} > v_{tri}$ → **Upper switch ON** ($S_k = 1$) → leg output = $+V_{DC}/2$
- If $v_{control} < v_{tri}$ → **Lower switch ON** ($\bar{S_k} = 1$) → leg output = $-V_{DC}/2$

## 2.3 Types of SPWM

### 2.3.1 Bipolar SPWM
- The output of each leg swings between $+V_{DC}$ and $-V_{DC}$ (no zero state).
- The fundamental ripple is centered at $f_c$.
- One carrier cycle → one output pulse per phase.

### 2.3.2 Unipolar SPWM (used in H-bridge single-phase / phase-disposition in 3-φ)
- Output swings between $+V_{DC}$, $0$, and $-V_{DC}$.
- Effective ripple frequency is $2f_c$ → **smaller filter / motor inductance needed**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MASTER THESE FOR ESE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Range / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Amplitude Modulation Index | $m_a = \dfrac{V_{control,peak}}{V_{tri,peak}}$ | $0 \le m_a \le 1$ (linear); $m_a > 1$ (overmodulation) |
| 2 | Frequency Modulation Ratio | $m_f = \dfrac{f_{carrier}}{f_{reference}} = \dfrac{f_c}{f_s}$ | Odd integer preferred (e.g., 15, 21, 81) |
| 3 | Single-phase half-bridge fundamental (RMS) | $V_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{m_a \, V_{DC}}{2\sqrt{2}}$ | Volts |
| 4 | Single-phase full-bridge fundamental (RMS) | $V_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{m_a \, V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Volts |
| 5 | Three-phase line-to-line fundamental (RMS) | $V_{LL1,rms} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \, m_a \, \dfrac{V_{DC}}{2}$ | Volts |
| 6 | THD definition | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^{2} - V_{1,rms}^{2}}}{V_{1,rms}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| 7 | Dominant harmonic order | $n = j \, m_f \pm k$ | $j$ even → no even harmonics; $k = 3$ gives triplen (cancelled in 3-φ line voltage) |
| 8 | Constant V/Hz ratio (EV base speed) | $\dfrac{V_{LL1,rms}}{f_s} = \text{const}$ | Below base speed |
| 9 | Inverter DC-link voltage utilization (linear) | $\hat V_{LL,1} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m_a V_{DC}$ | Peak line voltage |
| 10 | Maximum linear output (square-wave limit) | $\hat V_{LL,1,\max} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_{DC}$ | At $m_a \to \infty$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **PIPE-SYMBOL TRAP:** Never write $\vert V_{o1} \vert$ in markdown tables — the parser breaks. Use `\vert V_{o1} \vert` or `\mid V_{o1} \mid` instead.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real EV World

1. **Tesla Model 3 / BYD Blade Platform:** Uses SiC-MOSFET inverters with **SPWM at ~10 kHz** for the rear permanent-magnet motor.
2. **Toyota Prius THS-II:** Boosted DC-link to **650 V**; SPWM keeps switching losses under control at high $f_c$.
3. **Regenerative Braking:** The same SPWM inverter is re-used in reverse — the motor acts as generator and the same legs charge the DC bus with controlled current (now $I$ is controlled, $V$ is sensed).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Output Voltage Fourier Series (Bipolar SPWM, Single-Phase Half-Bridge)

We start from a **two-level waveform** $v_{aN}(t)$ that is $+V_{DC}/2$ when the control sine exceeds the triangle, and $-V_{DC}/2$ otherwise. By the theory of double Fourier series for naturally sampled PWM (Bowes / Black technique):

$$v_{aN}(t) = \frac{V_{DC}}{2} \Big[ m_a \sin(\omega_s t) + \text{harmonic terms} \Big]$$

The complete double Fourier integral gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{aN}(t) &= \frac{V_{DC}}{2} \Big\{ m_a \sin(\omega_s t) \\
&\quad + \frac{2}{m_f \pi} \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{m} \cos\!\big(m\pi - n\omega_s t\big) \cdot \sin\!\big( (m+n)\tfrac{\pi}{2} \big) \cdot J_n(m_a m\pi) \Big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $J_n(\cdot)$ is the **Bessel function of the first kind, order $n$**.

### Step-by-step interpretation

1. **First term** $\frac{V_{DC}}{2} m_a \sin(\omega_s t)$ → the **desired fundamental** (this is what the motor "sees").
2. The double sum contains **carrier harmonics** at $m \cdot f_c$ and **sidebands** at $m f_c \pm n f_s$.
3. Coefficients are weighted by Bessel functions $J_n(m_a m \pi)$ — the smaller $m_a$, the smaller the harmonic amplitude.
4. Because we chose $m_f$ **odd**, **even harmonics of the fundamental vanish** — a key KTU result.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Trick:** For a 3-φ inverter using SPWM, all **triplen harmonics** ($n = 3, 9, 15, \ldots$) cancel in the **line-to-line voltage** because the three phases add to zero at those orders. Hence the dominant line-voltage harmonic is the **first carrier sideband** $f_c \pm 2 f_s$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the RMS Output Voltage

For a periodic PWM waveform $v_o(t)$ over one fundamental period $T_s = 1/f_s$:

$$V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T_s}\int_{0}^{T_s} v_o^{2}(t)\,dt}$$

Since the waveform only takes values $\pm V_{DC}/2$ (bipolar) and the duty cycle within each carrier cycle is $D(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[1 + m_a \sin(\omega_s t)\big]$, the instantaneous mean square is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle v_o^{2} \rangle_{f_c\text{-cycle}} &= \left(\frac{V_{DC}}{2}\right)^{2}\cdot D(t) + \left(\frac{-V_{DC}}{2}\right)^{2}\cdot (1-D(t)) \\
&= \frac{V_{DC}^{2}}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **beautiful KTU result**: the local average of $v_o^2$ is *independent of $m_a$*. Integrating over $T_s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{o,rms}^{2} &= \frac{1}{T_s}\int_{0}^{T_s} \frac{V_{DC}^{2}}{4}\,dt = \frac{V_{DC}^{2}}{4} \\
\Rightarrow V_{o,rms} &= \frac{V_{DC}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** The **total RMS** of the SPWM output is **always** $V_{DC}/2$ for a half-bridge, regardless of $m_a$! Only the **fundamental** component changes with $m_a$. The "rest" of the energy sits in the harmonics, and the motor's stator inductance $L_s$ filters them out.

## 3.3 Derivation of THD

The fundamental RMS for a half-bridge bipolar SPWM is:

$$V_{o1,rms} = \frac{m_a V_{DC}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

Therefore the harmonic RMS is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{harm,rms} &= \sqrt{V_{o,rms}^{2} - V_{o1,rms}^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{DC}}{2}\right)^{2} - \left(\frac{m_a V_{DC}}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}} \\
&= \frac{V_{DC}}{2}\sqrt{1 - \frac{m_a^{2}}{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{ \text{THD} = \frac{V_{harm,rms}}{V_{o1,rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{2 - m_a^{2}}}{m_a} \times 100\% }
$$

### Numerical Validation Table (KTU-style)

| $m_a$ | $V_{o1,rms}$ (per unit of $V_{DC}/2$) | THD (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.2 | 0.0707 | 695.0% |
| 0.5 | 0.1768 | 173.2% |
| 0.8 | 0.2828 | 75.0% |
| 1.0 | 0.3536 | 41.4% |
| 1.155 | 0.4082 | 0% (square wave, single-phase) |

> [!NOTE]
> **EV relevance:** Real EV motors typically operate at $m_a$ between **0.7 and 0.9** in the constant-torque region, balancing low THD against decent DC-bus utilization.

## 3.4 Overmodulation and the Square-Wave Limit

When $m_a > 1$, the control signal exceeds the triangle for long stretches. The output no longer follows $m_a$ linearly. The peak fundamental grows as:

$$\hat V_{o1} = \frac{V_{DC}}{2} \cdot \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \quad \text{(at } m_a \to \infty\text{, square wave)}$$

In field-weakening (high-speed EV operation), the controller **deliberately enters overmodulation** to extract maximum DC-bus utilization — at the cost of higher THD and torque ripple.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How to Spot the Question in KTU Paper

| If the question mentions… | Use this approach… |
|---|---|
| "modulation index 0.8, DC link 400 V" | Compute $V_{o1,rms} = m_a V_{DC}/(2\sqrt{2})$ for half-bridge or $m_a V_{DC}/\sqrt{2}$ for full-bridge |
| "carrier 2 kHz, fundamental 50 Hz" | $m_f = 40$ → dominant harmonic at $2 f_c \pm f_s$ (unipolar) |
| "total harmonic distortion" | Use the THD formula derived above |
| "constant V/Hz" | $V_{rms}/f_s = $ rated value (e.g., 6 V/Hz for 400 V at 67 Hz) |
| "regenerative mode" | Discuss anti-parallel diodes, current polarity reversal, same SPWM controller |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EV Traction Inverter with SPWM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BAT[Battery Pack VDC 400V] --> DCBUS[DC Link Capacitor]
    DCBUS --> INVTOP[Inverter Bridge 6 IGBTs]
    INVTOP --> MOTOR[AC Traction Motor PMSM or Induction]
    MOTOR --> SENSOR[Position and Current Sensors]
    SENSOR --> ECU[VCU ECU]
    ECU -->|Torque Request| CTRL[SPWM Modulator]
    CTRL -->|Gate Signals S1 S2 S3 S4 S5 S6| INVTOP
    CTRLREF[Sinusoidal Reference Generator] --> CTRL
    TRICAR[Triangle Carrier Generator fc] --> CTRL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** sends a torque request → the SPWM modulator synthesises three sine references phase-shifted by 120° → these are compared with a high-frequency triangle → resulting gate pulses drive the **6 IGBT legs** → filtered by the **motor's own stator inductance** into a smooth sinusoidal current.

## 4.2 SPWM Comparator Logic (Detailed Sub-Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SIN[Reference Sine vcontrol 50Hz] --> CMP[Comparator]
    TRI[Triangle vtri 2kHz] --> CMP
    CMP -->|vcontrol greater than vtri| G1[Upper Switch Gate High]
    CMP -->|vcontrol less than vtri| G2[Lower Switch Gate High]
    G1 --> S1[IGBT S1 ON]
    G2 --> S4[IGBT S4 ON]
    S1 --> OUTPH[Phase A Output van]
    S4 --> OUTPH
```

## 4.3 Waveform Generation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DC Bus 400V] --> B[Six IGBTs in Three Legs]
    B --> C{SPWM Switching}
    C --> D[Sine Reference greater than Triangle]
    C --> E[Sine Reference less than Triangle]
    D --> F[Output equals plus VDC slash 2]
    E --> G[Output equals minus VDC slash 2]
    F --> H[Fundamental plus High Frequency Harmonics]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Motor Stator Inductance Ls Filters]
    I --> J[Smooth Sinusoidal Current]
```

## 4.4 Three-Phase Inverter Topology Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LegA
        SA1[IGBT T1] --- SA2[IGBT T4]
    end
    subgraph LegB
        SB1[IGBT T3] --- SB2[IGBT T6]
    end
    subgraph LegC
        SC1[IGBT T5] --- SC2[IGBT T2]
    end
    SA1 --> VDC[VDC plus]
    SA2 --> GND[VDC minus]
    SB1 --> VDC
    SB2 --> GND
    SC1 --> VDC
    SC2 --> GND
    SA1 -.-> PHASEA[Phase A to Motor]
    SA2 -.-> PHASEA
    SB1 -.-> PHASEB[Phase B to Motor]
    SB2 -.-> PHASEB
    SC1 -.-> PHASEC[Phase C to Motor]
    SC2 -.-> PHASEC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid safety check used:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`-style `SA1`, `SA2`, `PHASEA`) — no reserved keywords. Labels are clean uppercase text — no `**`, no nested HTML. No pipelines, brackets, or quotes inside the quoted label texts.

## 4.5 Harmonic Spectrum (Conceptual Frequency Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[f] --> A[Fundamental fs 50 Hz]
    F --> B[Carrier mf fs 1 kHz]
    F --> C[Sidebands mf plus or minus 2, mf plus or minus 4]
    F --> D[2mf plus or minus 1, 2mf plus or minus 5]
    A --> DES[Desired]
    B --> UND[Undesirable Filtered by Ls]
    C --> UND
    D --> UND
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define Sinusoidal PWM. State the significance of modulation index $m_a$ in a three-phase VSI used in electric vehicles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM) is a modulation technique in which a sinusoidal reference signal (the *modulating wave*) at the desired fundamental frequency $f_s$ is compared with a high-frequency triangular *carrier wave* at $f_c$. The comparator output drives the inverter switches such that the pulse widths are proportional to the instantaneous value of the sine reference.

**Significance of $m_a$ (1.5 marks):**
- $m_a = \dfrac{V_{control,peak}}{V_{tri,peak}}$ controls the **amplitude of the fundamental output voltage**.
- For $0 \le m_a \le 1$ (linear region), the fundamental varies linearly with $m_a$ → enables **smooth torque control** in the EV's constant-torque region.
- For $m_a > 1$ (overmodulation), the relationship becomes nonlinear → used in **field-weakening / high-speed region** to maximize DC-bus utilization.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Why is the frequency modulation ratio $m_f$ chosen as an odd integer in three-phase SPWM inverters?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Elimination of even harmonics (1 mark):** Choosing $m_f$ as an **odd integer** ensures that the PWM waveform exhibits **half-wave and quarter-wave symmetry**. As a result, all **even-order harmonics of the fundamental are completely eliminated** from the phase-voltage spectrum.

2. **Cancellation of triplen harmonics in line voltage (1 mark):** With $m_f$ odd, the triplen harmonics ($n = 3, 9, 15, \ldots$) appear as the dominant components in the *phase* voltages, but these **cancel out in the line-to-line voltage** because the three phases are 120° apart. Hence the **line-voltage spectrum is clean**, with the first dominant harmonic at $f_c \pm 2 f_s$.

3. **Reduced switching losses (1 mark):** Synchronising $m_f$ to be an integer multiple of 3 (e.g., $m_f = 15, 21, 81$) further ensures exact **quarter-wave symmetry** in the three-phase system, leading to lowest THD — a critical KTU board requirement for EV-grade inverters.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **Sinusoidal PWM technique** for a single-phase full-bridge inverter. Derive the expression for the **fundamental RMS output voltage** in the linear modulation region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A three-phase VSI feeds a Y-connected PMSM traction motor from a $V_{DC} = 400$ V DC bus. The inverter uses SPWM with $m_a = 0.85$ and $f_s = 75$ Hz. Compute:
  1. Peak phase voltage $\hat V_{ph,1}$
  2. RMS line-to-line fundamental voltage $V_{LL1,rms}$
  3. Total RMS phase voltage
  4. THD of the phase voltage **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**
- Draw a comparator block accepting $v_{control}(t) = V_{ctrl}\sin(\omega_s t)$ and $v_{tri}(t)$.
- The comparator output drives the upper IGBTs of legs 1 and 2.
- The **complement** (inverter gate) drives the lower IGBTs.
- The H-bridge output $v_{ab}(t) = v_{aN}(t) - v_{bN}(t)$ swings between $+V_{DC}$, $0$, $-V_{DC}$ (unipolar) or $\pm V_{DC}$ (bipolar).

**Derivation (4 marks):**
- For a single-phase full-bridge with **bipolar** SPWM, each leg produces $v_{aN}(t) = \pm V_{DC}/2$.
- The H-bridge output is $v_{ab} = v_{aN} - v_{bN}$, which swings between $+V_{DC}$ and $-V_{DC}$.
- The fundamental component from the double Fourier series is:

$$v_{ab,1}(t) = m_a \, V_{DC} \sin(\omega_s t)$$

- Hence the **fundamental RMS** is:

$$\boxed{ V_{ab1,rms} = \frac{m_a \, V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}} }$$

- The corresponding peak value is $\hat V_{ab,1} = m_a V_{DC}$.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $V_{DC} = 400$ V, $m_a = 0.85$, $f_s = 75$ Hz (frequency is irrelevant for voltage computation).

**Step 1 — Peak phase voltage (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat V_{ph,1} &= m_a \cdot \frac{V_{DC}}{2} \\
&= 0.85 \times \frac{400}{2} \\
&= 0.85 \times 200 = 170 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — RMS line-to-line fundamental (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LL1,rms} &= \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{\hat V_{ph,1}}{\sqrt{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{3} \times \frac{170}{\sqrt{2}} \\
&= 1.732 \times 120.21 \\
&\approx \boxed{208.2 \text{ V}}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Equivalently, $V_{LL1,rms} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} m_a V_{DC} = 0.6124 \times 0.85 \times 400 \approx 208.2$ V ✓)

**Step 3 — Total RMS phase voltage (1.5 marks):**

For a half-bridge the total RMS is independent of $m_a$:

$$V_{ph,rms} = \frac{V_{DC}}{2} = \frac{400}{2} = 200 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — THD (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{THD} &= \frac{\sqrt{V_{ph,rms}^{2} - V_{ph1,rms}^{2}}}{V_{ph1,rms}} \times 100\% \\
V_{ph1,rms} &= \frac{\hat V_{ph,1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{170}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 120.21 \text{ V} \\
\text{THD} &= \frac{\sqrt{200^{2} - 120.21^{2}}}{120.21} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{40000 - 14450.4}}{120.21} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{25549.6}}{120.21} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{159.84}{120.21} \times 100\% \\
&\approx \boxed{133.0\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

(High THD in *phase voltage* is expected — most of it is at triplen orders that cancel in the line voltage, giving a much cleaner line-voltage THD ≈ 65% in practice.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of a three-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** with Sinusoidal PWM for an EV traction drive. Discuss the role of $m_a$ in constant-torque and field-weakening regions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Tesla-class inverter operates on a $V_{DC} = 350$ V bus with $m_a = 1.0$ (linear boundary) and $m_f = 21$ with $f_s = 60$ Hz. Compute the:
  1. DC-bus utilisation factor at the fundamental
  2. Frequency of the dominant harmonic in the line voltage
  3. RMS value of the line-to-line fundamental
  4. Why does the motor inductance act as a natural filter for SPWM? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Three-phase VSI with SPWM — Principle (4 marks):**
- The VSI has **three half-bridge legs** sharing a common $V_{DC}$ bus.
- Three sinusoidal references $v_{a}^{*}, v_{b}^{*}, v_{c}^{*}$ are generated 120° apart.
- Each is compared with the triangle carrier to produce gating signals for that leg.
- The phase voltage spectrum contains the **fundamental** + **carrier harmonics** $m f_c$ + **sidebands** $m f_c \pm n f_s$.
- Triplen harmonics in the **line voltage** cancel.

**Role of $m_a$ (3 marks):**
- **Constant-torque region (below base speed):** $m_a$ is varied proportionally with $f_s$ to keep $V/f = $ const. This maintains **constant air-gap flux**, ensuring maximum torque per ampere.
- **Field-weakening region (above base speed):** $V_{DC}$ is already saturated (cannot exceed it), so $m_a$ is **clamped to its maximum (~1)** while $f_s$ continues to rise. Flux reduces as $1/f_s$, allowing higher speed at the cost of reduced torque capability (constant-power region).

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $V_{DC} = 350$ V, $m_a = 1.0$, $m_f = 21$, $f_s = 60$ Hz.

**Step 1 — DC-bus utilisation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat V_{LL,1} &= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m_a V_{DC} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 1.0 \times 350 \\
&\approx 303.1 \text{ V} \\
\text{Utilisation} &= \frac{\hat V_{LL,1}}{V_{DC}} = \frac{303.1}{350} \approx \boxed{86.6\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Dominant harmonic frequency (2 marks):**

$$
f_c = m_f \times f_s = 21 \times 60 = 1260 \text{ Hz}
$$

In the **line-to-line** spectrum, the **first dominant harmonic is the first carrier sideband** $f_c \pm 2 f_s$ (because the $f_c \pm f_s$ sidebands correspond to triplen orders in phase voltage and cancel in line voltage):

$$
\boxed{f_{dom} = 1260 \pm 120 = 1140 \text{ Hz or } 1380 \text{ Hz}}
$$

**Step 3 — RMS line-to-line fundamental (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LL1,rms} &= \frac{\hat V_{LL,1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{303.1}{\sqrt{2}} \\
&\approx \boxed{214.3 \text{ V}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Motor inductance as natural filter (1.5 marks):**
- The motor's stator inductance $L_s$ presents an impedance $X_L = 2\pi f L_s$ that **increases with frequency**.
- The fundamental (60 Hz) sees low impedance → passes with little attenuation.
- High-order carrier harmonics (1.26 kHz and above) see **20× higher impedance** → currents are heavily attenuated.
- Hence the **rotor "sees" a near-sinusoidal current** even though the voltage is rectangular pulses — eliminating the need for bulky external LC filters and reducing vehicle mass. (This is *why* SPWM is so successful in EVs.)

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in VSI / SPWM questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$** in RMS conversion — peak vs. RMS mix-ups cost **2-3 marks per question**.
> 2. **Wrong DC-bus factor** — half-bridge uses $V_{DC}/2$, full-bridge uses $V_{DC}$, three-phase line voltage uses $\sqrt{3}/2$. Memorize the table in Section 2.4.
> 3. **Ignoring the 120° phase shift** — students often write three identical sines and lose the 3-φ distinction entirely.
> 4. **Confusing $m_a$ and $m_f$** — $m_a$ controls voltage amplitude, $m_f$ controls harmonic placement. These are *independent*.
> 5. **Forgetting units in THD calculation** — the answer must be a *percentage* or *per-unit*; raw decimal answers are penalised.
> 6. **Overmodulation sign error** — when $m_a > 1$, the output fundamental **does not** follow $m_a$ linearly; this must be stated explicitly to earn full credit.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inverter** = DC → AC power converter, controlled by switching at $f_c$.
- **VSI** = Voltage Source Inverter; most common in EV traction due to battery behavior (≈ voltage source).
- **SPWM** = Sinusoidal reference × triangular carrier → variable pulse widths → sinusoidal *average* output.
- **$m_a$** = $V_{control,peak}/V_{tri,peak}$; **controls output voltage amplitude**.
- **$m_f$** = $f_c/f_s$; **should be an odd integer** (preferably multiple of 3) to cancel even and triplen harmonics.
- **Linear region:** $0 \le m_a \le 1$. **Overmodulation:** $m_a > 1$. **Square wave:** $m_a \to \infty$.
- **Constant V/Hz** below base speed → constant flux → max torque.
- **Field-weakening** above base speed → $V$ saturated, $f$ rises → flux falls.
- **Total RMS of half-bridge output** = $V_{DC}/2$ (always, independent of $m_a$).
- **Fundamental RMS** = $m_a V_{DC}/(2\sqrt{2})$ (half-bridge), $m_a V_{DC}/\sqrt{2}$ (full-bridge).
- **Line-voltage line spectrum** has the **first harmonic at $f_c \pm 2 f_s$** (triplens cancel).
- **EV-relevant range:** $m_a \in [0.7, 0.9]$; $f_c \in [5, 20]$ kHz for Si/IGBT, >50 kHz for SiC.
- **DC-bus utilisation at $m_a = 1$:** $86.6\%$ for 3-φ line voltage; rises to $\approx 100\%$ only in square-wave mode.
- **Regenerative braking** uses the *same* inverter legs with reversed current direction — no extra hardware.
- **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** is computed from the ratio of harmonic RMS to fundamental RMS — lower $m_a$ gives higher THD.
- **Overmodulation trade-off:** more voltage, but more torque ripple and acoustic noise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field Orientation Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Field Orientation Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Field Orientation Control (FOC)**, also universally known as **Vector Control**, is a sophisticated variable-frequency drive (VFD) control methodology used to control three-phase AC motors — most notably **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM)** and **Induction Motors (IM)** — by controlling the stator current vector in a synchronously rotating reference frame (the d-q frame). The fundamental objective of FOC is to achieve **decoupled control of torque and flux**, replicating the natural orthogonal and independent control structure of a separately excited DC motor.

In the context of the KTU PEMET863 syllabus (Module 4), FOC is the cornerstone control algorithm for the traction motor inverter of Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) and Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs), enabling high-efficiency, high-dynamic-response operation across the entire torque-speed envelope of an EV powertrain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Examiner-Ready):**
> "Field Orientation Control is a technique in which the stator currents of a three-phase AC machine are transformed into a two-phase rotating reference frame aligned with the rotor flux, thereby allowing the torque-producing and flux-producing components of current to be controlled independently — analogous to a DC machine."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a heavy wooden log lying on the ground. You can push it in two ways:
1. **Pushing straight along the length of the log (sideways)** → you waste energy trying to slide it because the log is long.
2. **Pushing from the back end (axially along its narrow side)** → the log rolls smoothly forward with minimum effort.

This is exactly what happens inside an AC motor. The rotor has a magnetic field pointing in a specific direction. If you push current (force) **perpendicular** to the rotor's magnetic field, you get **maximum torque** (the "rolling" case). If you push it **along** the magnetic field, you only build up the field strength (flux) without producing torque (the "wasted energy" case).

In a normal three-phase AC motor, currents are sinusoidal and constantly changing direction in the stator — it is very hard for a controller to know "how much" of the current is going into torque and "how much" is going into flux. **FOC acts like a magical compass that always aligns the controller's frame of reference with the rotor's magnetic field**, so that the controller can independently dial up the **flux current ($i_d$)** and the **torque current ($i_q$)** on two separate knobs, just like a DC motor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World EV Example:** The Tesla Model 3 rear PMSM drive unit, the Toyota Prius traction motor, and the Hyundai E-GMP platform all employ FOC (vector control) running on a high-frequency DSP/Microcontroller. The driver pressing the accelerator pedal asks the FOC controller to increase the **$i_q$ reference** (torque), while $i_d$ is held at zero (no weakening) or commanded negative (field weakening at high speeds).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Number of pole pairs ($P/2$):** 2 to 8 typical for EV traction motors.
- **Rotor electrical speed ($\omega_e$):** typically up to **20,000 rpm** in modern EV motors (≈ **2094 rad/s**).
- **Switching frequency ($f_{sw}$):** 8 kHz to 20 kHz for automotive FOC (above audible range).
- **DC bus voltage ($V_{dc}$):** 400 V (standard) or 800 V (modern 800-V architectures).
- **Permanent magnet flux linkage ($\lambda_m$):** typically **0.05 Wb to 0.2 Wb** per phase.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Philosophy of FOC — Step-by-Step

The fundamental challenge of controlling an AC machine is that its stator currents and rotor flux are coupled and time-varying. FOC solves this through a sequence of mathematical coordinate transformations:

### Step 1: Three-Phase Stator Currents → Two-Phase Stationary Frame (Clarke Transform)

The three-phase stator currents $i_a$, $i_b$, $i_c$ (which sum to zero in a balanced system) are transformed into two orthogonal stationary-frame components $i_\alpha$ and $i_\beta$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this needed?** Because we only need **two independent variables** to describe any current vector in 2D space, not three. This reduces the math dimension without losing information.

### Step 2: Stationary Frame → Rotating Frame (Park Transform)

The stationary $\alpha\beta$ components are then rotated by the **rotor electrical angle $\theta_e$** to align with the rotor's d-axis (direct axis, along the rotor flux) and q-axis (quadrature axis, perpendicular to flux). The resulting components are $i_d$ (flux-producing) and $i_q$ (torque-producing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this the "magic step"?** In the d-q rotating frame, all sinusoidal quantities (which are hard to control) become **DC quantities** (which PI controllers handle perfectly). The rotor flux is "frozen" along the d-axis, so $i_d$ and $i_q$ never cross-couple due to rotation.

### Step 3: Independent Control of $i_d$ and $i_q$

Two independent **PI (Proportional-Integral) controllers** regulate $i_d$ and $i_q$ to track their reference values:
- $i_d^*$ = 0 (for surface PMSM in constant torque region) or negative (for field weakening).
- $i_q^*$ = derived from the torque command (accelerator pedal).

### Step 4: Inverse Park Transform

The control outputs $V_d$ and $V_q$ (in rotating frame) are transformed back to stationary frame $V_\alpha$ and $V_\beta$ using the rotor angle.

### Step 5: Space Vector Modulation (SVM)

The stationary-frame voltage references are fed to an **SVM block**, which generates the precise PWM switching signals for the three-phase inverter (IGBT/SiC MOSFET) bridge that drives the motor.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exams)

| # | Equation / Formula | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i_a + i_b + i_c = 0$ | Balanced three-phase constraint (reduces DOF from 3 to 2) | Amperes (A) |
| 2 | $\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix} = \dfrac{2}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix}$ | **Clarke Transform** (amplitude-invariant) | A |
| 3 | $\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}$ | **Park Transform** (rotating frame) | A |
| 4 | $\theta_e = \dfrac{P}{2}\int \omega_m \, dt$ | Electrical angle from mechanical speed | rad |
| 5 | $V_d = R_s i_d + L_d \dfrac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$ | Stator d-axis voltage equation | V |
| 6 | $V_q = R_s i_q + L_q \dfrac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e (L_d i_d + \lambda_m)$ | Stator q-axis voltage equation | V |
| 7 | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2}\cdot\dfrac{P}{2}\left[\lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]$ | Electromagnetic torque of PMSM | N·m |
| 8 | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2}\cdot\dfrac{P}{2}\lambda_m i_q$ | Simplified torque (Surface PMSM, $L_d = L_q$) | N·m |
| 9 | $\vert I_s \vert = \sqrt{i_d^2 + i_q^2}$ | Stator current vector magnitude | A |
| 10 | $\vert V_s \vert \le \dfrac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$ (SVM) | Maximum linear-modulation voltage limit | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of FOC in EV Engineering:** FOC enables regenerative braking (negative $i_q$), smooth torque ramp-up from 0 rpm (no torque ripple at standstill, unlike six-step commutation), and field-weakening for highway cruising beyond base speed. Without FOC, modern EVs would be uncontrollable.

## 2.3 Cross-Coupling Decoupling — The "Why" Behind the Two PI Controllers

A careful look at the voltage equations (formulas 5 & 6) reveals coupling terms:
- The $V_d$ equation contains $-\omega_e L_q i_q$ (a "disturbance" from q-axis).
- The $V_q$ equation contains $\omega_e(L_d i_d + \lambda_m)$ (a "disturbance" from d-axis).

To achieve truly independent control, **feed-forward decoupling** is added:

$$V_d^* = \underbrace{R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt}}_{\text{PI controller output}} \underbrace{- \omega_e L_q i_q}_{\text{decoupling term}}$$

$$V_q^* = \underbrace{R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt}}_{\text{PI controller output}} \underbrace{+ \omega_e (L_d i_d + \lambda_m)}_{\text{decoupling term}}$$

This is the difference between a poor FOC implementation (no decoupling) and a board-exam-perfect one.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Clarke Transformation (Three-Phase → Two-Phase Stationary)

### Objective
Convert the three-phase stator currents $i_a(t)$, $i_b(t)$, $i_c(t)$ into a two-axis stationary orthogonal system $i_\alpha$, $i_\beta$.

### Starting Point
Assume balanced three-phase sinusoidal currents:

$$i_a = I_m \cos(\omega t), \quad i_b = I_m \cos\!\left(\omega t - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right), \quad i_c = I_m \cos\!\left(\omega t + \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)$$

### Step 1: Define the $\alpha$-axis to coincide with Phase-A winding
By definition, the $\alpha$-axis is aligned with the a-phase magnetic axis. So the projection of the current vector onto the $\alpha$-axis is:

$$i_\alpha = K_1 \cdot i_a + K_2 \cdot (i_b + i_c)$$

where $K_1$ and $K_2$ are constants to be determined. The $\beta$-axis is $90°$ (electrical) ahead of $\alpha$, so $i_b$ leads $i_a$ by $120°$, meaning its $\alpha$-projection is $\cos(120°) = -1/2$, and its $\beta$-projection is $\sin(120°) = \sqrt{3}/2$.

### Step 2: Form the projection matrix
By symmetry and the constraint $i_a + i_b + i_c = 0$ (which gives $i_b + i_c = -i_a$), and using the amplitude-invariant normalization factor of $2/3$:

$$i_\alpha = \frac{2}{3}\left(i_a - \frac{1}{2}i_b - \frac{1}{2}i_c\right)$$

$$i_\beta = \frac{2}{3}\left(0\cdot i_a + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i_b - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i_c\right)$$

### Step 3: Compact matrix form

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix} = \frac{2}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the **Clarke Transformation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on power invariance:** Some textbooks use a $K$ factor of $\sqrt{2/3}$ for **power-invariant** Clarke transform. The $\frac{2}{3}$ factor is the **amplitude-invariant** version. The KTU board typically uses the amplitude-invariant form.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Park Transformation (Stationary $\alpha\beta$ → Rotating $dq$)

### Objective
Rotate the stationary $\alpha\beta$ frame by the rotor electrical angle $\theta_e$ to align the d-axis with the rotor flux linkage.

### Step 1: Define a 2-D rotation matrix
A standard 2-D rotation by angle $\theta_e$ (counter-clockwise) of a vector $[x, y]^T$ produces $[x', y']^T$ where:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Apply to the $\alpha\beta$ currents
The d-axis is the rotating direct axis (along rotor flux) and the q-axis is the quadrature axis ($90°$ ahead of d in the direction of rotation). Therefore:

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3: Inverse Park Transformation
To convert back from rotating frame to stationary frame, use the inverse rotation (transpose, since the matrix is orthogonal):

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & -\sin\theta_e \\ \sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Electromagnetic Torque of a PMSM in the d-q Frame

### Step 1: Start from the general power conversion
The instantaneous electrical input power to the stator is:

$$P_{in} = \frac{3}{2}\left(V_d i_d + V_q i_q\right)$$

### Step 2: Substitute the d-q voltage equations
For a PMSM, $\lambda_d = L_d i_d + \lambda_m$ and $\lambda_q = L_q i_q$ (where $\lambda_m$ is the permanent magnet flux linkage). After substituting the back-EMF and inductive terms, and isolating the mechanical power term $\omega_e \cdot T_e$:

$$P_{mech} = \frac{3}{2}\omega_e\left[\lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q)i_d i_q\right]$$

### Step 3: Solve for torque using $P_{mech} = \omega_m \cdot T_e = \frac{2}{P}\omega_e \cdot T_e$

$$T_e = \frac{P_{mech}}{\omega_m} = \frac{3}{2}\cdot\frac{P}{2}\left[\lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q)i_d i_q\right]$$

### Step 4: Special case for Surface-Mounted PMSM (SPM)
For SPM, $L_d = L_q = L_s$ (reluctance term vanishes):

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2}\cdot\frac{P}{2}\lambda_m i_q$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** The cross-product term $(L_d - L_q)i_d i_q$ is called the **reluctance torque**. It is zero for SPM but exploited in Interior PMSM (IPM) motors used in many EVs (e.g., Toyota Prius Gen 4) for additional torque and wider field-weakening range. The KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests this distinction.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 8-pole ($P/2 = 4$) PMSM has $L_d = L_q = 5$ mH, $\lambda_m = 0.1$ Wb, $R_s = 0.5\ \Omega$. It operates at $i_d = 0$ A, $i_q = 50$ A, and a mechanical speed of $3000$ rpm. Compute the electromagnetic torque.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate electrical speed. $\omega_m = 3000 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 314.16$ rad/s. So $\omega_e = (P/2)\omega_m = 4 \times 314.16 = 1256.64$ rad/s.

**Step 2:** Since $L_d = L_q$, use the simplified torque formula:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2}\cdot\frac{P}{2}\lambda_m i_q$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$T_e = 1.5 \times 4 \times 0.1 \times 50 = 30\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 4:** Verify with the full formula (the reluctance term is zero since $L_d - L_q = 0$):

$$T_e = 1.5 \times 4 \times [0.1 \times 50 + 0] = 30\ \text{N·m}\ \checkmark$$

**Step 5:** Compute the back-EMF and developed power for completeness:

$$e_q = \omega_e \lambda_m = 1256.64 \times 0.1 \approx 125.66\ \text{V}$$

$$P_{mech} = T_e \cdot \omega_m = 30 \times 314.16 = 9424.8\ \text{W} \approx 9.42\ \text{kW}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete FOC Closed-Loop Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Torque Reference Te star from Accelerator Pedal] --> B[Speed PI Controller]
    B --> C[Quadrature Current Reference Iq star]
    D[Field Weakening Algorithm] --> E[Direct Current Reference Id star]
    C --> F1[Q-Axis PI Controller]
    E --> F2[D-Axis PI Controller]
    F1 --> G1[Decoupling + Cross Coupling Compensation]
    F2 --> G2[Decoupling + Cross Coupling Compensation]
    G1 --> H[Inverse Park Transform using Theta e]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Space Vector Modulation SVM]
    I --> J[Three Phase Inverter IGBT or SiC MOSFET]
    J --> K[Three Phase PMSM Traction Motor]
    K --> L[Encoder or Resolver]
    L --> M[Position and Speed Calculation]
    M --> N[Theta e Calculation for Park]
    N --> H
    M --> B
    K --> O[Vehicle Wheels via Reducer]
```

**Diagram Reading Guide (KTU Board Style):**

1. The accelerator pedal maps to a torque request $T_e^*$ (in N·m).
2. A **speed PI loop** (outer loop) regulates motor speed by generating $i_q^*$ (torque current reference).
3. **$i_d^*$** is set to zero in the constant-torque region, but driven negative by a field-weakening algorithm above base speed.
4. Two **inner current PI loops** control $i_d$ and $i_q$ independently.
5. **Decoupling terms** ($-\omega_e L_q i_q$ and $\omega_e L_d i_d$) are added to cancel the cross-coupling.
6. **Inverse Park transform** uses the rotor angle $\theta_e$ from the encoder/resolver.
7. **SVM** generates PWM signals for the inverter.
8. The **inverter** produces variable-frequency, variable-amplitude three-phase voltages to drive the motor.

## 4.2 Mathematical Signal-Flow Architecture (Coordinate Frame Map)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[ABC Frame Three Phase Stator] -->|Clarke Transform| B[Alpha Beta Frame Stationary 2D]
    B -->|Park Transform using Theta e| C[DQ Frame Rotating with Rotor]
    C -->|PI Control plus Decoupling| D[VD VQ Voltage References Rotating Frame]
    D -->|Inverse Park Transform| E[VAlpha VBeta Stationary Frame]
    E -->|Space Vector Modulation| F[PWM Switching Pulses]
    F --> G[DC to AC Inverter Bridge]
    G --> A
```

**Frame Reference Summary Table:**

| Frame | Name | Rotates? | Variables | Controller Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **abc** | Three-phase stator | No (stationary) | $i_a, i_b, i_c$ | None (raw feedback) |
| **$\alpha\beta$** | Two-phase stationary | No (stationary) | $i_\alpha, i_\beta$ | Often sin/cos (still AC) |
| **$dq$** | Two-phase rotating | **Yes** (with rotor) | $i_d, i_q$ | **PI (DC-like)** |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Style]**
**Q: Define Field Orientation Control (FOC). What is the role of the Park transformation in FOC?**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

Field Orientation Control (FOC) is a vector control technique for three-phase AC machines in which the stator currents are controlled in a synchronously rotating reference frame aligned with the rotor flux linkage, enabling independent (decoupled) control of flux and torque — analogous to a separately excited DC motor. **(1.5 marks)**

The **Park transformation** converts the stationary $\alpha\beta$ frame currents $i_\alpha$ and $i_\beta$ into the rotating $d$-$q$ frame currents $i_d$ and $i_q$ using the rotor electrical angle $\theta_e$. Its key role is to **freeze the rotor flux along the d-axis**, transforming all sinusoidal time-varying stator quantities into DC-like quantities. This makes them suitable for control by conventional PI controllers, which cannot directly track sinusoidal references without steady-state error. **(1.5 marks)**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Frequently Asked]**
**Q: List the sequence of coordinate transformations performed in a typical FOC algorithm and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Clarke Transformation (abc → αβ):** Converts balanced three-phase stator currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$ into two orthogonal stationary-frame components $i_\alpha$ and $i_\beta$, reducing the system from three variables to two independent variables. **(1 mark)**
2. **Park Transformation (αβ → dq):** Rotates the stationary $\alpha\beta$ frame by the rotor electrical angle $\theta_e$ to align the d-axis with the rotor flux, producing DC-like $i_d$ (flux component) and $i_q$ (torque component). **(1 mark)**
3. **Inverse Park Transformation (dq → αβ):** Converts the controller's voltage outputs $V_d$ and $V_q$ (rotating frame) back to stationary-frame $V_\alpha$ and $V_\beta$ for inverter reference. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete working of Field Orientation Control (FOC) for a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) used in an electric vehicle. Clearly state the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the d-q axis voltage equations of a PMSM from the stationary-frame voltage equations using the Park transformation. Hence obtain the torque expression for both SPM and IPM machines. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)**

*Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.* The student must draw and label the following blocks (2 marks for diagram, 5 marks for explanation):

1. **Torque / Speed Reference:** The accelerator pedal maps pedal position to $T_e^*$ (in N·m), which is the outer-loop reference. (1 mark)
2. **Outer Speed PI Controller:** Compares actual $\omega_m$ with reference $\omega^*$ and outputs $i_q^*$. (1 mark)
3. **Field Weakening Block:** Computes $i_d^*$ reference — zero in the constant-torque region, negative above base speed. (1 mark)
4. **Two Inner Current PI Controllers** (one for $i_d$, one for $i_q$): Generate $V_d'$ and $V_q'$ reference voltages. (1 mark)
5. **Decoupling Feed-Forward Block:** Adds $-\omega_e L_q i_q$ to $V_d'$ and $\omega_e(L_d i_d + \lambda_m)$ to $V_q'$ to cancel cross-coupling. (1 mark)
6. **Inverse Park Transform + SVM + Inverter:** Convert $V_d, V_q$ back to three-phase PWM for the inverter. (1 mark)
7. **Encoder/Resolver + Angle Calculator:** Provide $\theta_e$ for Park transforms and $\omega_m$ for the speed loop. (1 mark)

**(b) Derivation of d-q Voltage Equations and Torque (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Stator voltage equation in stator frame (1 mark)**

$$V_{abc} = R_s I_{abc} + \frac{d}{dt}\lambda_{abc}$$

**Step 2: Apply Park transformation to flux linkage (2 marks)**

The flux linkages in d-q frame are $\lambda_d = L_d i_d + \lambda_m$ and $\lambda_q = L_q i_q$. Substituting back into the voltage equation and applying the rotation, the speed-dependent back-EMF terms emerge:

$$V_d = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$$

$$V_q = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e (L_d i_d + \lambda_m)$$

(1 mark for each equation; 0.5 mark each if the cross-coupling terms are missed)

**Step 3: Torque derivation (3 marks)**

Starting from input power $P_{in} = \frac{3}{2}(V_d i_d + V_q i_q)$, and using $P_{mech} = T_e \omega_m$:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2}\cdot\frac{P}{2}\left[\lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q)i_d i_q\right]$$

(2 marks)

- For **SPM** ($L_d = L_q$): $T_e = \frac{3}{2}\cdot\frac{P}{2}\lambda_m i_q$ (0.5 mark)
- For **IPM** ($L_d \ne L_q$): Both magnet torque ($\lambda_m i_q$) and reluctance torque ($(L_d - L_q)i_d i_q$) contribute. (0.5 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Alternate Pattern]**
**Q: (a)** Starting from the three-phase stator currents, derive the Clarke and Park transformations with their inverse forms. Clearly show the matrix equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-pole, 3-phase PMSM has $L_d = 8$ mH, $L_q = 12$ mH, $\lambda_m = 0.15$ Wb, and $R_s = 0.3\ \Omega$. It is operating with $i_d = -10$ A (field-weakening) and $i_q = 40$ A at a mechanical speed of 2500 rpm. Compute:
- (i) The electromagnetic torque developed.
- (ii) The electrical speed in rad/s.
- (iii) The d-axis and q-axis back-EMF components.
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of Clarke and Park Transforms (7 marks)**

*Refer to the full derivation in Section 3.1 and 3.2 of these notes.*

**Clarke Transform** (3 marks):

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix} = \frac{2}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix}$$

(1.5 marks for forward transform + 0.5 mark for the $2/3$ amplitude-invariant factor)

**Inverse Clarke Transform** (1 mark):

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ -\tfrac{1}{2} & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Park Transform** (1.5 marks):

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Inverse Park Transform** (0.5 mark): the transpose of the Park matrix.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:** $P = 6$ (so $P/2 = 3$), $L_d = 8$ mH, $L_q = 12$ mH, $\lambda_m = 0.15$ Wb, $R_s = 0.3\ \Omega$, $i_d = -10$ A, $i_q = 40$ A, $N = 2500$ rpm.

**(i) Electromagnetic Torque (3 marks)**

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2}\cdot\frac{P}{2}\left[\lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q\right]$$

Magnet torque term: $\lambda_m i_q = 0.15 \times 40 = 6$ Wb·A

Reluctance torque term: $(L_d - L_q) i_d i_q = (0.008 - 0.012) \times (-10) \times 40 = (-0.004) \times (-400) = 1.6$ Wb·A

Total inside brackets: $6 + 1.6 = 7.6$ Wb·A

$$T_e = 1.5 \times 3 \times 7.6 = 34.2\ \text{N·m}\ \checkmark$$

**(2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final answer)**

**(ii) Electrical Speed (2 marks)**

$$\omega_m = 2500 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 261.8\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_e = \frac{P}{2}\omega_m = 3 \times 261.8 = 785.4\ \text{rad/s}$$

**(iii) Back-EMF components (2 marks)**

$$e_d = -\omega_e L_q i_q = -785.4 \times 0.012 \times 40 = -376.99\ \text{V}$$

$$e_q = \omega_e (L_d i_d + \lambda_m) = 785.4 \times [0.008 \times (-10) + 0.15] = 785.4 \times 0.07 = 54.98\ \text{V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign convention of $i_d$:** A common error is writing $i_d = -10$ A as $+10$ A. In **field-weakening**, $i_d$ is **negative** — losing a mark on this is one of the top three KTU board deductions in FOC problems.
> - **Forgetting the $P/2$ factor:** Students often write $T_e = \frac{3}{2}\lambda_m i_q$ instead of $\frac{3}{2}\cdot\frac{P}{2}\lambda_m i_q$. This is a guaranteed 1-mark cut.
> - **Wrong angle convention in Park transform:** Some students use $+\sin\theta_e$ on the q-axis; check the textbook convention used in your course. The form above is the most widely accepted.
> - **Mixing $\omega_e$ and $\omega_m$:** Always use **electrical speed $\omega_e = (P/2)\omega_m$** in d-q equations. Using $\omega_m$ directly is a fatal error.
> - **Omitting the reluctance torque for IPM:** Always check if the motor is IPM ($L_d \ne L_q$). Skipping the second term in the torque equation costs 1 to 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **FOC = Vector Control** = Decoupled control of flux ($i_d$) and torque ($i_q$) in a synchronously rotating frame. **(HIGH FREQUENCY EXAM TOPIC)**
- **Two transformations are non-negotiable:** **Clarke (abc → αβ)** and **Park (αβ → dq)**. Forgetting either one means the controller cannot decouple flux and torque.
- **$i_d$ = flux-producing current, $i_q$ = torque-producing current** — memorize this mapping.
- **For Surface PMSM:** $L_d = L_q$, so the reluctance torque is **zero**. The torque equation simplifies to $T_e = \frac{3}{2}\cdot\frac{P}{2}\lambda_m i_q$.
- **For Interior PMSM (IPM):** $L_q > L_d$ typically, and **both magnet and reluctance torque** contribute. IPM is preferred in EVs (Toyota, Hyundai) for higher specific torque and field-weakening capability.
- **Field Weakening:** To extend speed beyond base speed, $i_d^*$ is made **negative**, opposing the PM flux. This reduces back-EMF and allows higher rotational speed.
- **The PI controllers operate in the d-q frame because signals are DC-like there** — never use PI in the abc or αβ frame for the inner current loop.
- **Rotor angle $\theta_e$ is mandatory** for both Park and Inverse Park transforms. It comes from an encoder, resolver, or sensorless observer (SMO, MRAS, EKF).
- **Decoupling feed-forward terms** ($-\omega_e L_q i_q$ in $V_d$ and $\omega_e(L_d i_d + \lambda_m)$ in $V_q$) are essential at high speeds; omitting them causes poor dynamic response.
- **SVM (Space Vector Modulation)** is the standard inverter switching technique for FOC, offering 15% higher DC-bus utilization than sinusoidal PWM.
- **Final stage of FOC:** SVM output drives the **three-phase inverter** (IGBT or SiC MOSFET bridge) which produces variable-frequency, variable-amplitude voltages for the motor.
- **Maximum linear-modulation voltage limit:** $\vert V_s \vert_{max} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$. Beyond this, the inverter enters overmodulation and the controller becomes non-linear.
- **Common KTU keywords to use in answers:** "decoupled control," "rotating reference frame," "rotor flux linkage alignment," "amplitude-invariant Clarke transform," "feed-forward decoupling," "field-weakening region."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Mnemonic:** **"Clarke flattens, Park rotates, PI regulates, SVM modulates, Inverter drives."** Master this five-step chain and any FOC question becomes a structured answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BLDC Motor Controls - Torque Control and Speed Control Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** BLDC Motor Controls - Torque Control and Speed Control Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a BLDC Motor?

A **Brushless DC (BLDC) motor** is a synchronous electric motor powered by a direct current (DC) electricity supply, where the mechanical commutation (switching of current direction in the windings) is replaced by electronic commutation controlled by an inverter and rotor position feedback (typically from Hall-effect sensors or sensorless estimators).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Terminology):**
> A BLDC motor is a **rotating electric machine** with a **permanent-magnet rotor** and a **stator with polyphase armature windings**. The rotor position feedback is used by the electronic controller to commutate the stator phases, producing a trapezoidal (or sinusoidal) back-EMF, thereby delivering smooth torque without mechanical brushes.

### Classification by Back-EMF Shape

| Type | Back-EMF Waveform | Drive Current | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BLDC (Trapezoidal)** | Trapezoidal | Square-wave (120° conduction) | EV traction, fans, pumps |
| **PMSM (Sinusoidal)** | Sinusoidal | Sinusoidal (FOC required) | High-precision servo EVs |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Electronically-Synchronized Bicyclist"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine an old ceiling fan with brushes (a brushed DC motor) — the **brushes** physically hit copper segments to keep the rotor spinning. They wear out like erasers on a pencil.
>
