> Now replace the brushes with a **smart traffic controller** (an electronic inverter) that uses **3 traffic cameras** (Hall sensors) to detect where the rotor is. The controller then gives "green signals" to the right stator coils at exactly the right moment — like a perfectly choreographed dance.
> 
> - **Voltage** = how hard you push the pedals
> - **Current** = how much torque is delivered to the wheel
> - **Speed** = how fast the pedals turn
> - **BLDC controller** = the smart traffic system deciding *which* coil gets power *when*.

## 1.3 Why Control Torque and Speed Independently in EVs?

In a hybrid/electric vehicle, the driver's accelerator pedal does **not** command speed directly — it commands **torque demand**. The vehicle control unit (VCU) translates accelerator position → torque request → motor current command. Speed is the *result* of the torque applied to the vehicle load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Why Torque Control is Paramount:**
> In an EV, the **torque loop is the inner (fastest) loop**, and the **speed loop is the outer (slower) loop**. This cascade is a fundamental KTU concept.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Back-EMF constant (Ke)**: Volts per rad/s (V·s/rad)
- **Torque constant (Kt)**: Nm per Ampere (Nm/A)
- **SI identity**: $K_e = K_t$ (when expressed in consistent SI units)
- **Typical EV traction motor ratings**: 75–150 kW, peak torque 250–500 Nm, base speed 3000–6000 RPM, max speed 12,000–15,000 RPM.
- **DC bus voltage (Vdc)**: 300–800 V in modern EVs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BLDC Motor — Fundamental Operating Principle

A BLDC motor has three stator phases (A, B, C) and a permanent-magnet rotor. The electronic controller energizes **only two phases at a time** (six-step commutation), advancing the energized pair as the rotor rotates.

### 2.1.1 Six-Step Commutation Sequence

| Step | Energized Phases | Current Path | Rotor Position (°) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A+ , B− | Vdc → A → B → GND | 0 – 60 |
| 2 | A+ , C− | Vdc → A → C → GND | 60 – 120 |
| 3 | B+ , C− | Vdc → B → C → GND | 120 – 180 |
| 4 | B+ , A− | Vdc → B → A → GND | 180 – 240 |
| 5 | C+ , A− | Vdc → C → A → GND | 240 – 300 |
| 6 | C+ , B− | Vdc → C → B → GND | 300 – 360 |

> [!NOTE]
> The Hall sensor outputs (H1, H2, H3) change state every 60° electrical, giving 6 unique codes (1–6) per electrical revolution, each mapped to one of the 6 switching states above.

## 2.2 BLDC Motor Voltage Equation (Per Phase)

For any one of the two conducting phases during a commutation step:

$$V_{dc} = 2 \cdot R_s \cdot I_a + 2 \cdot L_s \cdot \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$$

Where:
- $V_{dc}$ = DC bus voltage (V)
- $R_s$ = per-phase stator resistance (Ω)
- $L_s$ = per-phase stator inductance (H)
- $I_a$ = armature (phase) current (A)
- $E_b$ = back-EMF (V)
- The factor **2** appears because two phases are in series during conduction.

## 2.3 Back-EMF Equation

The back-EMF is induced in the stator as the permanent-magnet rotor sweeps past the stator coils:

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega_m$$

Where $\omega_m$ is the mechanical angular speed (rad/s).

## 2.4 Electromagnetic Torque Equation

$$T_e = K_t \cdot I_a$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Relationship:** For trapezoidal BLDC drives, the developed torque is **linearly proportional to the DC-link current**, making torque control as simple as **current control**.

## 2.5 Mechanical (Torque-Balance) Equation

$$T_e - T_L = J \cdot \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B \cdot \omega_m$$

Where:
- $T_L$ = load torque (Nm)
- $J$ = moment of inertia (kg·m²)
- $B$ = viscous friction coefficient (Nm·s/rad)

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 90% of KTU numerical/derivation questions on BLDC drives.**

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega_m$ | Back-EMF | Determines max safe speed at given Vdc |
| 2 | $T_e = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque | Inner current loop setpoint |
| 3 | $V_{dc} = 2R_s I_a + 2L_s \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$ | Phase voltage balance | Designing PWM duty cycle |
| 4 | $\omega_m = \frac{V_{dc} - 2R_s I_a - 2L_s \frac{dI_a}{dt}}{K_e}$ | Derived speed expression | Speed-loop PI design |
| 5 | $P_{out} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$ | Mechanical output power | Sizing EV traction motor |
| 6 | $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{T_e \cdot \omega_m}{V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc}}$ | Efficiency | Energy consumption in kWh/km |
| 7 | $f_{electrical} = \frac{P \cdot N}{120}$ | Electrical frequency (Hz) | PWM & inverter switching freq. |
| 8 | $f_{PWM} \geq 10 \cdot f_{electrical,max}$ | Sampling rule | Controller bandwidth rule |

> **Note on Symbol P:** In the table, $P$ = number of pole pairs (e.g., $P=4$ for an 8-pole motor); $N$ = mechanical speed in RPM.

## 2.7 Two Distinct Operating Regions of a BLDC Drive

### Region I — Constant Torque Region (Below Base Speed)
- **Back-EMF is low** ($E_b < V_{dc}$).
- Controller maintains **rated current** (i.e., rated torque) by **PWM duty-cycle modulation** of the DC bus.
- **V/f = constant** is the classical rule: $V \propto f$.
- Here, the motor behaves like a **variable-voltage source**.

### Region II — Constant Power Region (Above Base Speed) — **Field Weakening**
- **Back-EMF approaches $V_{dc}$**, leaving no voltage headroom for current.
- To go faster, the controller injects a **demagnetizing d-axis current** ($I_d < 0$) that opposes the rotor flux, effectively *weakening* the air-gap flux.
- Torque falls as $\dfrac{1}{\omega}$, while power remains approximately constant.
- This is **mandatory in EV traction** to extend the constant-power speed range (CPSR) for highway cruising.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Explain the two operating regions of a BLDC motor drive with the help of torque-speed characteristics."* — Master this paragraph.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Speed Equation from the Voltage Balance

We start with the per-phase steady-state voltage equation (assuming $L_s$ drop is negligible at steady state, i.e., $\frac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$):

$$V_{dc} = 2 R_s I_a + E_b$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the back-EMF definition** $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega_m$:

$$V_{dc} = 2 R_s I_a + K_e \cdot \omega_m$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the torque-current relation** $I_a = \dfrac{T_e}{K_t}$ (and using $K_t = K_e$ in SI units):

$$V_{dc} = \frac{2 R_s T_e}{K_e} + K_e \cdot \omega_m$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\omega_m$ (the no-load speed when $T_e = 0$):**

$$\omega_{m,\text{no-load}} = \frac{V_{dc}}{K_e}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the stall torque (when $\omega_m = 0$):**

$$T_{e,\text{stall}} = \frac{K_e \cdot V_{dc}}{2 R_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The stall torque is purely a function of the bus voltage, the back-EMF constant, and stator resistance — independent of any controller action. The controller can *only* move the operating point *along* this line, not *beyond* it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque-Speed Characteristic Line

Re-arranging the equation from Step 2 above:

$$\omega_m = \frac{V_{dc}}{K_e} - \frac{2 R_s}{K_e^2} \cdot T_e$$

This is a **straight line** in the $T_e$–$\omega_m$ plane, with:
- **Y-intercept** = no-load speed $= \dfrac{V_{dc}}{K_e}$
- **X-intercept** = stall torque $= \dfrac{K_e V_{dc}}{2 R_s}$
- **Slope** = $-\dfrac{2 R_s}{K_e^2}$ (negative)

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PWM Duty Cycle for Voltage Control

During PWM, the effective average voltage applied to the two conducting phases is:

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{dc}$$

Where $D$ is the PWM duty ratio (0 ≤ D ≤ 1).

**Substituting into the speed equation:**

$$\omega_m = \frac{D \cdot V_{dc} - 2 R_s I_a}{K_e}$$

**Solving for the duty ratio required to maintain a given current $I_a$ at speed $\omega_m$:**

$$D = \frac{K_e \cdot \omega_m + 2 R_s I_a}{V_{dc}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **EV Application Insight:** In a real EV, $D$ is updated every PWM period (typically 10–20 kHz). The torque controller computes the required $I_a^*$, and the current controller computes the required $D$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power and Efficiency

The mechanical output power is the product of torque and angular speed:

$$P_{out} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$$

**Step 1 — Multiply the speed equation by $T_e$:**

$$T_e \cdot \omega_m = T_e \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{K_e} - \frac{2 R_s T_e^2}{K_e^2}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that $P_{out} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$ and define copper loss** $P_{cu} = 2 R_s I_a^2 = \dfrac{2 R_s T_e^2}{K_e^2}$:

$$P_{out} = \frac{T_e V_{dc}}{K_e} - P_{cu}$$

**Step 3 — The input power is** $P_{in} = V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc} = V_{dc} \cdot I_a$ (ignoring inverter losses for a moment):

$$P_{in} = V_{dc} \cdot \frac{T_e}{K_e}$$

**Step 4 — Efficiency is therefore:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \frac{2 R_s T_e}{K_e V_{dc}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** This shows that **efficiency drops linearly with increasing torque** (i.e., during hard acceleration). This is why EVs have regenerative braking — to recover energy lost to $I^2R$ losses in the high-torque region.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Speed-Control Method Comparison

| Method | Principle | Sensors Required | Dynamic Response | Cost | KTU Typical Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Open-loop V/f** | $V/f = \text{const.}$ | None | Poor | Low | 3–5 |
| **PWM duty-cycle control** | Vary $D$ to vary average V | Hall | Moderate | Low | 5–7 |
| **Closed-loop PI speed control** | PI on speed error → $I^*$ | Hall + current | Good | Medium | 7–10 |
| **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** | d-q transformation, decouple flux & torque | Hall/encoder + 2 current | Excellent | High | 10–14 |
| **Direct Torque Control (DTC)** | Hysteresis on flux & torque | Voltage, current | Very fast | High | 10–14 |
| **Sensorless (Back-EMF zero-crossing)** | Detect $E_b$ zero crossings | None | Moderate | Low | 7–10 |
| **Sensorless (High-frequency injection)** | Inject HF voltage, observe saliency | None | Excellent at low speed | High | 10–14 |

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Field-Weakening Current Requirement

At base speed $\omega_b$, the back-EMF equals the available voltage (ignoring resistance drop):

$$K_e \cdot \omega_b \approx V_{dc} - 2 R_s I_a$$

To go *above* base speed, the controller must counteract rotor flux with a demagnetizing current $I_d$ along the rotor's d-axis. The air-gap flux linkage becomes:

$$\lambda_{m,\text{eff}} = \lambda_m - L_d \cdot I_d$$

Where $L_d$ is the d-axis inductance. The new back-EMF is:

$$E_{b,\text{new}} = K_e \cdot \omega_m \cdot \left(1 - \frac{L_d I_d}{\lambda_m}\right)$$

**Setting $E_{b,\text{new}} \leq V_{dc}$** to satisfy the voltage limit, we solve for the required $I_d$:

$$I_d \leq \frac{\lambda_m}{L_d} \left(1 - \frac{V_{dc}}{K_e \omega_m}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world EV Insight:** The maximum $I_d$ is limited by the magnet's demagnetization curve. Exceeding it **irreversibly damages the rotor magnets** — a critical KTU pitfall warning.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Cascaded Torque / Speed Control Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the standard **cascaded control** structure used in EV BLDC drives, with the **speed loop (outer)** generating the torque/current reference, and the **current loop (inner)** generating the PWM duty cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeSP[Speed Reference rpm] --> nodeSSE[Speed Error Subtractor]
    nodeV[Vehicle Speed Feedback rpm] --> nodeSSE
    nodeSSE --> nodeSPI[Speed PI Controller]
    nodeSPI --> nodeTQ[Torque Reference Nm]
    nodeTQ --> nodeTQS[To Torque to Current Conversion]
    nodeTQS --> nodeIC[Current Reference Ia star]
    nodeIA[Actual Phase Current A] --> nodeISE[Current Error Subtractor]
    nodeIC --> nodeISE
    nodeISE --> nodeIPI[Current PI Controller]
    nodeIPI --> nodePWM[PWM Duty Cycle Generator]
    nodePWM --> nodeINV[Three Phase Voltage Source Inverter]
    nodeINV --> nodeBLDC[BLDC Motor Stator]
    nodeBLDC --> nodeEMF[Back EMF and Speed]
    nodeEMF --> nodeHALL[Hall Sensor Feedback]
    nodeHALL --> nodeDEC[Commutation Decoder 6 step]
    nodeDEC --> nodeINV
    nodeEMF --> nodeV
    nodeBLDC --> nodeIA
    nodeBLDC --> nodeFW[Field Weakening Module]
    nodeFW --> nodeIC
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Six-Step Commutation Logic

This diagram shows the decision logic for the 6-step commutation based on Hall sensor inputs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeH1[Hall Sensor H1] --> nodeDEC{Hall Decoder 1 to 6}
    nodeH2[Hall Sensor H2] --> nodeDEC
    nodeH3[Hall Sensor H3] --> nodeDEC
    nodeDEC --> nodeS1[Step 1 Phases A plus B minus]
    nodeDEC --> nodeS2[Step 2 Phases A plus C minus]
    nodeDEC --> nodeS3[Step 3 Phases B plus C minus]
    nodeDEC --> nodeS4[Step 4 Phases B plus A minus]
    nodeDEC --> nodeS5[Step 5 Phases C plus A minus]
    nodeDEC --> nodeS6[Step 6 Phases C plus B minus]
    nodeS1 --> nodeINV[Inverter Gate Driver Signals]
    nodeS2 --> nodeINV
    nodeS3 --> nodeINV
    nodeS4 --> nodeINV
    nodeS5 --> nodeINV
    nodeS6 --> nodeINV
    nodeINV --> nodeMOT[BLDC Motor Torque Production]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Torque-Speed Characteristic Plot (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph R1[Region 1 Constant Torque]
        A1[Base Speed to Rated Speed] --> A2[Back EMF less than Vdc] --> A3[PWM Modulation Varies V]
    end
    subgraph R2[Region 2 Constant Power Field Weakening]
        B1[Above Base Speed] --> B2[Demagnetizing Id less than 0] --> B3[Falls as 1 over omega]
    end
    A3 --> B1
    R1 --> Z[Peak Torque Point]
    R2 --> Y[Max Speed Point]
    Z --> X[Torque Speed Line]
    Y --> X
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to interpret this in an exam:** Draw a torque (y-axis) vs speed (x-axis) plot. The line is flat (constant torque) from 0 to base speed $\omega_b$, then hyperbolically decays (constant power) up to maximum speed $\omega_{max}$. The boundary point $(\omega_b, T_{rated})$ is the corner point.

## 4.4 Sensorless Control Block Diagram (Back-EMF Zero-Crossing)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeV1[Phase Voltage V phase] --> nodeADC[ADC Sampler]
    nodeVN[Neutral Point Voltage Vn] --> nodeADC
    nodeADC --> nodeSUB[Subtract V phase minus Vn]
    nodeSUB --> nodeZCD[Zero Crossing Detector]
    nodeZCD --> nodeDELAY[30 Degree Delay]
    nodeDELAY --> nodeCOMM[Commutation Trigger]
    nodeCOMM --> nodePWM[PWM and Inverter]
    nodePWM --> nodeMOT[BLDC Motor]
    nodeMOT --> nodeV1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note — Why 30° Delay?**
> The back-EMF zero-crossing occurs 30° *after* the ideal commutation point. Therefore, the controller must delay the commutation by an additional 30° to land at the correct switching instant. This is a common 5-mark short-answer question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between the two operating regions of a BLDC motor drive with reference to its torque-speed characteristic.**

**Model Answer:**

A BLDC motor drive has two distinct operating regions:
- **Region I — Constant Torque Region (0 to base speed $\omega_b$):** The back-EMF is low, leaving sufficient voltage headroom. The controller varies the average applied voltage via PWM duty cycle to maintain the rated current and hence rated torque. The motor delivers constant torque up to base speed.
- **Region II — Constant Power Region (above $\omega_b$):** Back-EMF approaches the DC bus voltage, so the controller injects a demagnetizing d-axis current ($I_d < 0$) to weaken the air-gap flux, allowing speed to increase further. Torque falls as $1/\omega$ while power remains approximately constant. This extends the constant-power speed range required for EV cruising.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is field weakening in a BLDC motor, and why is it necessary in EV traction?**

**Model Answer:**

Field weakening is a control technique in which a **negative d-axis current ($I_d < 0$)** is injected into the stator winding to oppose and reduce the air-gap flux produced by the rotor's permanent magnets. This effectively reduces the back-EMF, allowing the motor to spin faster than its base speed without the back-EMF saturating the DC bus voltage. It is necessary in EV traction because vehicles require a wide constant-power speed range (e.g., 3:1 to 5:1) for highway cruising — without field weakening, the motor would be limited to base speed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the steady-state torque-speed characteristic of a BLDC motor driven by a six-step inverter. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Explain with a block diagram the closed-loop speed control of a BLDC motor using a PI controller, including PWM generation and current limiting. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Phase Voltage Equation** (two phases conducting at any time):

$$V_{dc} = 2 R_s I_a + 2 L_s \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$$

**Step 2 — Apply steady-state condition** $\frac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$:

$$V_{dc} = 2 R_s I_a + E_b$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back-EMF** $E_b = K_e \omega_m$:

$$V_{dc} = 2 R_s I_a + K_e \omega_m$$

**Step 4 — Substitute torque** $T_e = K_t I_a$ and use $K_t = K_e$ (SI units):

$$V_{dc} = \frac{2 R_s T_e}{K_e} + K_e \omega_m$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\omega_m$:**

$$\boxed{\omega_m = \frac{V_{dc}}{K_e} - \frac{2 R_s}{K_e^2} T_e}$$

**Step 6 — Identify boundary points:**
- No-load speed: $\omega_{0} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{K_e}$ (when $T_e = 0$).
- Stall torque: $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K_e V_{dc}}{2 R_s}$ (when $\omega_m = 0$).

**Step 7 — State the result:**
The torque-speed characteristic is a **straight line** with negative slope, passing through $(\omega_0, 0)$ and $(0, T_{stall})$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The closed-loop speed control architecture has three nested blocks:

1. **Outer Speed Loop (slow, ~1 kHz):**
   - Speed reference $\omega^*$ comes from the accelerator pedal.
   - Speed error $e_\omega = \omega^* - \omega_{feedback}$.
   - PI controller outputs the **torque reference $T_e^*$**.
   - Anti-windup and rate-limiters prevent integrator saturation during fast transients.

2. **Inner Current/Torque Loop (fast, ~10–20 kHz):**
   - Torque reference $T_e^*$ is converted to current reference $I_a^* = T_e^*/K_t$.
   - Current error $e_I = I_a^* - I_{measured}$.
   - Current PI outputs the required voltage $V^*$ (or equivalently, the PWM duty cycle $D$).
   - **Current limiting** is enforced by clamping $I_a^*$ to a maximum value (e.g., 1.5–2× rated) to protect the inverter and motor.

3. **PWM and Inverter Stage:**
   - The voltage command $V^*$ is mapped to a duty cycle $D = V^*/V_{dc}$.
   - The PWM generator compares a triangular carrier (e.g., 10 kHz) with $D$ to produce gate pulses for the 6 IGBTs/MOSFETs of the 3-phase inverter.
   - **Soft-switching** (complementary pairs with dead-time) prevents shoot-through.

4. **Feedback path:**
   - Hall sensors → rotor position → commutation decoder.
   - Current shunt / Hall-effect current sensor → $I_{measured}$.
   - Incremental encoder or back-EMF observer → $\omega_{feedback}$.

**PI Tuning Trade-off:**
- High $K_p$ → fast response, but overshoot and possible instability.
- High $K_i$ → zero steady-state error, but slow response and possible oscillation.
- Typical Ziegler-Nichols or symoptical-optimum tuning is used.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** With neat diagrams, explain the six-step trapezoidal commutation of a 3-phase BLDC motor. Show the Hall sensor truth table. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Explain the principle of sensorless control of a BLDC motor using back-EMF zero-crossing detection. What are its limitations? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A 3-phase BLDC motor with a trapezoidal back-EMF is driven by a **6-step (120° conduction) inverter**. At any instant, **only two of the three phases carry current** (one connected to +Vdc, the other to GND), while the third phase is floating. The energized pair is advanced every 60° electrical based on rotor position feedback from three Hall sensors placed 120° apart.

**Hall Sensor Truth Table:**

| Hall H1 | Hall H2 | Hall H3 | Step | Phases ON | Current Path |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | A+, B− | Vdc → A → B → GND |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 | A+, C− | Vdc → A → C → GND |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 3 | B+, C− | Vdc → B → C → GND |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 4 | B+, A− | Vdc → B → A → GND |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 5 | C+, A− | Vdc → C → A → GND |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 6 | C+, B− | Vdc → C → B → GND |

**Operating Principle:**
1. The three Hall sensors detect the polarity of the rotor magnet (N or S) as it sweeps past.
2. The Hall decoder reads the 3-bit code and selects the correct pair of inverter switches.
3. The current in the energized phases produces a stator MMF that is **advanced by ~90°** from the rotor magnet axis, producing maximum torque per ampere.
4. Each Hall transition (every 60°) advances to the next step, maintaining quasi-square-wave torque production.

**Torque ripple:** A small ripple (~10–15%) exists at each 60° step because the back-EMF is not perfectly trapezoidal and the current commutation is not instantaneous.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Principle of Back-EMF Zero-Crossing Detection:**

When one phase is floating (not conducting), the back-EMF induced in that phase can be measured. The instant the floating-phase back-EMF crosses zero, the rotor is at a known electrical position. Specifically:
- The zero-crossing occurs **30° electrical after the previous commutation**.
- Therefore, the next commutation must be triggered **30° after the zero-crossing**.

**Implementation Steps:**
1. Measure the floating phase terminal voltage $V_{ph}$ and the motor neutral point voltage $V_n$.
2. Compute the phase-to-neutral back-EMF: $E_{ph} = V_{ph} - V_n$.
3. Detect the zero-crossing of $E_{ph}$ using a comparator.
4. Add a 30° time delay (or 30° electrical / electrical-speed × 1°-electrical-per-sec).
5. Fire the next commutation step.

**Block Diagram Summary:**
- ADC samples → subtract → comparator → delay → commutation logic → inverter.

**Limitations:**
1. **No startup torque** — At zero speed, $E_b = K_e \omega = 0$, so there is no signal to detect. The motor must be started in open-loop (with predetermined commutation sequence) and then transitioned to sensorless mode once $E_b$ is large enough (typically > 5% of rated).
2. **Low-speed inaccuracy** — As $\omega \to 0$, SNR drops and timing delays become significant.
3. **PWM noise** — Switching transients corrupt the back-EMF measurement; low-pass filters or sample-and-hold techniques are needed.
4. **Mid-point voltage drift** — The neutral point is not physically accessible in most motors; virtual neutral must be synthesized using a resistor network, which has tolerance issues.
5. **Susceptible to DC bus ripple** — Voltage fluctuations in $V_{dc}$ propagate into the back-EMF measurement.

**Alternatives for low-speed sensorless:** High-frequency signal injection, observer-based methods (Sliding Mode, MRAS, Extended Kalman Filter).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the voltage equation $V_{dc} = 2R_s I_a + 2L_s \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$ (because *two* phases conduct in series). Wrong: students often write $V_{dc} = R_s I_a + L_s \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$. → **−2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing electrical and mechanical degrees.** A 60° electrical step is only 60°/P° mechanical, where P = number of pole pairs. → **−1 mark.**
> 3. **Forgetting the 30° delay** in back-EMF sensorless commutation. → **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Writing field-weakening as "increasing voltage"** — the bus voltage is *fixed* in EVs; field weakening is done by **demagnetizing d-axis current**, not by raising $V_{dc}$. → **−2 marks.**
> 5. **Confusing BLDC (trapezoidal) with PMSM (sinusoidal/FOC)** in questions that ask specifically for "trapezoidal BLDC". → **−1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ BLDC = **B**rush**L**ess **D**irect **C**urrent; permanent-magnet rotor, electronically commutated stator.
- ✅ Two phases conduct at any instant in a 6-step trapezoidal drive.
- ✅ Voltage equation: $V_{dc} = 2 R_s I_a + 2 L_s \dfrac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$.
- ✅ Back-EMF: $E_b = K_e \omega_m$.
- ✅ Torque: $T_e = K_t I_a$; in SI units, $K_e = K_t$.
- ✅ Torque–speed curve is a straight line from $(0, T_{stall})$ to $(\omega_0, 0)$.
- ✅ No-load speed: $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{K_e}$.
- ✅ Stall torque: $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K_e V_{dc}}{2 R_s}$.
- ✅ PWM duty cycle: $D = \dfrac{K_e \omega_m + 2 R_s I_a}{V_{dc}}$.
- ✅ **Two operating regions:** Constant Torque (PWM) and Constant Power (Field Weakening).
- ✅ **Field weakening** = negative d-axis current $I_d < 0$ to oppose rotor flux.
- ✅ **Cascade control:** Speed loop (outer, slow) → Torque/Current loop (inner, fast) → PWM → Inverter → Motor.
- ✅ **Hall sensors** (3, placed 120° apart) → 6 unique codes per electrical revolution.
- ✅ **Sensorless (back-EMF) commutation** requires a **30° delay** after zero-crossing.
- ✅ EV efficiency $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{2 R_s T_e}{K_e V_{dc}}$ — falls as torque rises.
- ✅ **Iron Rule of EV control:** Accelerator pedal commands **torque**, not speed.
- ✅ **Field-weakening limit:** Do **not** exceed the magnet's demagnetization current rating.
- ✅ **KTU keyword watchlist:** *trapezoidal back-EMF, six-step commutation, Hall-effect, constant torque region, constant power region, demagnetizing d-axis current, 30° delay, sensorless, observer.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles and Block Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Principles of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles

### 1.1.1 Electric Vehicle (EV) – Formal Definition
An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is a road-going automobile whose propulsion force is derived entirely (or primarily) from one or more electric motors, drawing electrical energy from a rechargeable on-board storage system (battery or fuel cell) or from an external source (catenary, inductive charging). The conversion chain is purely **electro-chemical → electrical → mechanical**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMET863):** A vehicle in which the traction energy is supplied exclusively by an electric machine, fed by an energy storage system, constitutes an Electric Vehicle. The ICE is **absent** in a pure EV (BEV).

### 1.1.2 Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) – Formal Definition
A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is a vehicle that combines **two or more distinct energy sources and powertrains** — typically an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) and an Electric Motor/Generator set — to deliver traction. The hybridization exists to optimize fuel economy, emissions, and performance beyond what either power source can achieve alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Classification Triad:**
> - **Micro-Hybrid (μHEV):** Start-Stop + brake energy recovery (12 V).
> - **Mild-Hybrid (MHEV):** 48 V motor-assist, no pure EV mode.
> - **Full-Hybrid (FHEV):** Toyota THS-II, can run in pure EV mode briefly.
> - **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV):** Externally rechargeable, ≥ 20–50 km EV range.

### 1.1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Two-Cook in One Kitchen" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a kitchen preparing a feast:
> - The **ICE** is the **gas-stove chef** — powerful, fast, loves long cooking sessions (highway driving), but wastes energy as heat (exhaust).
> - The **Electric Motor** is the **induction-chef** — silent, clean, perfect for short precision tasks (city driving, reversing), and recycles leftover steam (regenerative braking).
> - The **Battery** is the **refrigerator** — stores energy in a clean, cool, retrievable form.
> - The **ECU / VCU** is the **head chef** — decides which stove to ignite at any moment, based on the recipe (driver's torque demand) and available ingredients (SoC, fuel level).
>
> A **pure EV** is a kitchen with *only* the induction chef. A **HEV** has *both* chefs sharing the workload intelligently.

### 1.1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU-Standard)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | Tractive effort & grade calculations |
| Air density (sea level, 25 °C) | $\rho_a$ | **1.225 kg/m³** | Aerodynamic drag term |
| Wheel rolling-resistance coefficient (typical tyre) | $C_{rr}$ | **0.008 – 0.015** | Rolling resistance force |
| ICE brake-specific fuel consumption (BSFC, best point) | $bsfc$ | **220 – 250 g/kWh** | Benchmark for ICE efficiency |
| Lithium-ion cell nominal voltage | $V_{cell}$ | **3.6 – 3.7 V** | Pack sizing in EV |
| Standard drive cycle | — | **NEDC / WLTP / FTP-75** | Energy consumption certification |

---

## 1.2 Block Diagram – Philosophy in KTU Context

A **block diagram** in EV/HEV engineering is a *functional block-level architecture* that maps **energy flow**, **signal flow**, and **control hierarchy** between the vehicle's sub-systems. It is **not** a wiring schematic; it is an *engineering abstraction* that lets the designer reason about power routing, control loops, and mode-switching logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU examiners love block diagrams:**
> 1. They test whether the student knows the *role* of each component.
> 2. They test whether the student understands the *direction of power flow* under different modes (traction, regen, charging).
> 3. They are the language of OEM system engineering — failure to draw them means failure to communicate.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principles

### 2.1.1 Principle of an Electric Vehicle (BEV)
A Battery Electric Vehicle operates on a **three-stage electro-mechanical energy conversion chain**:

1. **Electrochemical Stage:** Chemical energy stored in the battery (Li-ion, LFP, NMC) is released as DC electrical energy via redox reactions at the electrodes.
2. **Power-Electronic Stage:** The DC bus voltage is conditioned by the **Power Converter (Inverter)** into a three-phase variable-frequency, variable-amplitude AC supply (or DC for BLDC).
3. **Electromechanical Stage:** The electric traction motor converts electrical energy into rotary mechanical energy at the wheels via a fixed-ratio or multi-speed transmission.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core insight:** The *torque* of an electric motor is directly proportional to the current, and its *speed* is directly proportional to the voltage. The motor controller exploits this to mimic a CVT — without an actual CVT.

### 2.1.2 Principle of a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)
In a **Series HEV**, the ICE **never mechanically drives the wheels**. The ICE drives a generator only; the generator charges the battery or feeds the traction motor via a DC link. The wheels are driven **solely** by the electric motor. Architecturally it is an EV with a *range-extender*.

**Power-flow path (Traction mode):**
$$\text{ICE} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Generator} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{DC Link} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Inverter} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Traction Motor} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Wheels}$$

**Power-flow path (Regenerative mode):**
$$\text{Wheels} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Traction Motor (as generator)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Inverter} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{DC Link} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Battery}$$

### 2.1.3 Principle of a Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV / Parallel HEV)
In a **Parallel HEV**, the ICE and the electric motor are **mechanically coupled** to the wheels (typically through a torque-coupler or planetary gear set). Either power source can propel the vehicle, **or both can do so simultaneously**. The battery is sized smaller (no need to be the sole energy buffer).

**Operating Modes:**
- **Motor-only mode** (low-speed, low-load, SoC sufficient)
- **Engine-only mode** (high-speed cruise, SoC low)
- **Combined mode** (peak torque demand, e.g., overtaking)
- **Generator mode** (ICE drives motor-as-generator to charge battery)
- **Regenerative braking mode** (wheels drive motor-as-generator)

### 2.1.4 Principle of a Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid
The **Series-Parallel HEV** (Toyota Hybrid System – THS) uses a **planetary gear set (Epicyclic gearbox)** as the *power-split device*. It functionally combines the benefits of both architectures:
- At low speeds: behaves like a Series HEV (engine drives generator).
- At high speeds: behaves like a Parallel HEV (engine drives wheels mechanically).
- MG1 acts as a generator **and** a speed-control flywheel.
- MG2 acts as the primary traction motor.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ pipe in tables. Below, all magnitudes are rendered with `$\vert$` syntax or worded to avoid breaking markdown table pipes.

| # | Quantity / Relation | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tractive force (force at wheel–road contact) | $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$ | N — sum of resistances + inertia |
| 2 | Rolling resistance force | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr}\, m\, g\, \cos\theta$ | N — $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.008$ to $0.015$ |
| 3 | Aerodynamic drag force | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho_a\, C_d\, A_f\, v^2$ | N — depends on $v^2$ |
| 4 | Hill-climbing force | $F_{hc} = m\, g\, \sin\theta$ | N — gradient component |
| 5 | Linear acceleration force | $F_{ia} = m\, a$ | N — $a$ in m/s² |
| 6 | Wheel angular speed | $\omega_w = v \,/\, r_w$ | rad/s — $r_w$ = tyre rolling radius |
| 7 | Motor torque demand at wheel | $T_{wheel} = F_t \cdot r_w$ | N·m |
| 8 | Motor speed demand | $N_{motor} = \dfrac{v \cdot 60}{2\pi\, r_w}\cdot \tau_g$ | rpm — $\tau_g$ = gear ratio |
| 9 | Power required at wheels | $P_w = F_t \cdot v$ | W |
| 10 | Battery energy (ideal) | $E_b = V_{pack}\, Q_{pack} = V_{pack} \cdot I \cdot t$ | Wh |
| 11 | Battery energy (specific) | $E_{spec} = E_b \,/\, m_{pack}$ | Wh/kg |
| 12 | State of Charge (SoC) | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 - \dfrac{1}{Q_{max}}\!\int_0^t I(\tau)\, d\tau$ | dimensionless, 0 to 1 |
| 13 | Series-HEV generator power | $P_{gen} = \eta_{gen}\, P_{ice}$ | W — sizing of MG1 |
| 14 | Parallel-HEV torque additivity (combined) | $T_{total} = T_{ice} + T_{motor}$ | N·m (neglecting losses) |
| 15 | Vehicle kinetic energy | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}\, m\, v^2$ | J — basis of regen potential |
| 16 | Regen energy recovered (ideal) | $E_{reg} = \eta_{m}\,\eta_{c}\,\eta_{b}\, E_k$ | J — $0.6$ to $0.7$ realistic |
| 17 | Power-split device (planetary) speed relation | $(1+R)\,\omega_c = \omega_s + R\,\omega_r$ | rad/s — $R$ = ring/sun teeth ratio |
| 18 | Power balance (any HEV) | $P_{ice} + P_{bat} = P_{wheel} + P_{aux}$ | W — energy conservation |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where these principles are applied in industry:**
> - **Tata Nexon EV** (BEV): Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM), 30.2 kWh pack, ~312 km ARAI range.
> - **Toyota Camry Hybrid** (Series-Parallel): THS-II planetary gear, 2.5 L Atkinson ICE + 88 kW motor.
> - **BMW i3 REx** (Series): 125 kW motor + 647 cc range-extender ICE.
> - **Mahindra Scorpio Hybrid / Maruti Suzuki mild-hybrid 48 V**: Parallel/series elements.
> - **Tata Starbus EV, Ashok Leyland Circuit-S**: Heavy-duty BEV block diagrams.
>
> Understanding the *block diagram* of each lets a maintenance engineer localize faults, and lets a controls engineer tune the **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** for optimal mode switching.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Required at the Wheels (Tractive Power)

We derive the instantaneous power demanded from the traction system to propel a vehicle of mass $m$ at velocity $v$ against all resistance forces.

### Step 1 — Write Newton's Second Law on the vehicle
The tractive force $F_t$ at the wheel–road contact must overcome the sum of resistance forces and provide the linear acceleration:

$$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute each component explicitly
- Rolling resistance: $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr}\, m\, g\, \cos\theta$
- Aerodynamic drag: $F_{ad} = \dfrac{1}{2}\, \rho_a\, C_d\, A_f\, v^2$
- Hill-climbing: $F_{hc} = m\, g\, \sin\theta$
- Inertia: $F_{ia} = m\, a = m\, \dfrac{dv}{dt}$

Hence:

$$
F_t = \mu_{rr}\, m\, g\, \cos\theta + \frac{1}{2}\, \rho_a\, C_d\, A_f\, v^2 + m\, g\, \sin\theta + m\, \frac{dv}{dt}
$$

### Step 3 — Multiply by $v$ to obtain mechanical power at the wheel
Tractive mechanical power (the *first-law* quantity the motor must deliver after drivetrain losses) is:

$$
P_w = F_t \cdot v
$$

Therefore the **complete power-demand equation** is:

$$
\boxed{
P_w = \mu_{rr}\, m\, g\, \cos\theta \cdot v \;+\; \frac{1}{2}\, \rho_a\, C_d\, A_f\, v^3 \;+\; m\, g\, \sin\theta \cdot v \;+\; m\, \frac{dv}{dt}\, v
}
$$

> **Term-by-term physical meaning (KTU expects this in 14-mark answers):**
> - $P_1 = \mu_{rr}\, m\, g\, \cos\theta \cdot v$ — power lost to tyre deformation (linear in $v$).
> - $P_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^3$ — power lost to air drag (**cubic** in $v$; dominates at highway speeds).
> - $P_3 = m\, g\, \sin\theta \cdot v$ — power needed to climb a grade.
> - $P_4 = m\, v\, \dfrac{dv}{dt}$ — power stored in vehicle kinetic energy (positive when accelerating, negative when decelerating — this is exactly what regen braking can recover).

### Step 4 — Cross-check dimensional consistency
Using $[m] = \text{kg}$, $[g] = \text{m/s}^2$, $[v] = \text{m/s}$:

$$
[P_1] = (\text{dimensionless})(\text{kg})(\text{m/s}^2)(\text{m/s}) = \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}^3 = \text{W} \;\checkmark
$$

$$
[P_2] = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}\cdot \frac{\text{N}\cdot\text{s}^2}{\text{kg}^2}\cdot \text{m}^2 \cdot \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}^3} = \text{W} \;\checkmark
$$

All terms are in **watts** — the derivation is dimensionally consistent.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Planetary Gear Power-Split Equation (Series-Parallel HEV)

A planetary (epicyclic) gear set has three elements: the **Sun gear (S)**, the **Planet carrier (C)**, and the **Ring gear (R)**. Let the sun have $N_S$ teeth, ring have $N_R$ teeth, and define the ratio:

$$R_{psd} = \frac{N_R}{N_S}$$

### Step 1 — Kinematic constraint (Willis equation)
For an epicyclic gear set with one member fixed, the speeds are related by:

$$
\frac{\omega_S - \omega_C}{\omega_R - \omega_C} = -\frac{N_R}{N_S} = -R_{psd}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for carrier speed
Rearranging:

$$
\omega_S - \omega_C = -R_{psd}\,(\omega_R - \omega_C)
$$

$$
\omega_S - \omega_C = -R_{psd}\,\omega_R + R_{psd}\,\omega_C
$$

$$
\omega_S = \omega_C - R_{psd}\,\omega_R + R_{psd}\,\omega_C
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\omega_S = (1 + R_{psd})\,\omega_C - R_{psd}\,\omega_R\;}
$$

### Step 3 — Identify the THS-II element assignments
- **Sun (S)** = MG1 (starter-generator)
- **Carrier (C)** = Engine (ICE)
- **Ring (R)** = MG2 (traction motor) + final-drive + wheels

Therefore:

$$
\omega_{MG1} = (1 + R_{psd})\,\omega_{ice} - R_{psd}\,\omega_{wheel}
$$

### Step 4 — Power balance at the planetary carrier
Because the carrier is rigid and torques sum to zero on the gear node:

$$
T_S + T_R + T_C = 0
$$

And **power** is conserved (ideal gear):

$$
T_S\,\omega_S + T_R\,\omega_R + T_C\,\omega_C = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for KTU:** The planetary set acts as a *continuously variable power-split device*. The ICE can deliver mechanical power either to the wheels (through the carrier→ring path) **or** to MG1 (carrier→sun path), and the *split ratio* is controlled by varying the speed of MG1 — which is why Toyota's THS does not need a multi-speed gearbox.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mode vs. Architecture

The following matrix maps each operating mode to the **active power-flow path** for the three principal HEV architectures. This is a high-yield KTU table.

| Operating Mode | Series HEV | Parallel HEV | Series-Parallel (THS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold start / Idle** | ICE off, battery → motor → wheels | ICE off, battery → motor → wheels | ICE off, MG2 → wheels |
| **Low-speed cruise (SoC OK)** | Battery → motor → wheels | Battery → motor → wheels (motor-only) | ICE off, MG2 → wheels |
| **Low-speed cruise (SoC low)** | ICE → gen → battery → motor → wheels | ICE → wheels (engine-only) | ICE → MG1 (gen) → battery → MG2 → wheels |
| **High-speed cruise** | ICE → gen → battery → motor → wheels | ICE → wheels (engine-only) | ICE → wheels **mechanically** (carrier path) + MG2 trims |
| **Hard acceleration** | ICE → gen (max) + battery → motor (combined) | ICE + motor → wheels (combined torque) | ICE → wheels + MG2 → wheels |
| **Braking (regen)** | Wheels → motor (gen) → battery | Wheels → motor (gen) → battery | Wheels → MG2 (gen) → battery |
| **Battery charging at standstill** | ICE → gen → battery (range-extender mode) | Not possible in pure parallel | ICE → MG1 (gen) → battery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner tip:** When asked *"Why is the Series-Parallel architecture called the best compromise?"*, the answer is: *It minimizes conversion losses at steady-state highway cruise (mechanical path), while retaining the flexibility of pure EV-mode at low speeds and the ability to charge the battery directly from the ICE at standstill. The planetary gear enables this without clutches or multi-speed gearboxes.*

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[Energy Storage\nBattery Pack DC] --> INV[Power Electronics\nInverter DC to AC]
    INV --> M[Traction Motor\nPMSM or Induction]
    M --> TR[Transmission\nSingle Speed Reducer]
    TR --> W[Wheels]
    W -.->|regen| M
    M -.->|regen| INV
    INV -.->|regen| BAT
    VCU[VCU\nVehicle Control Unit] --> INV
    VCU --> M
    AUX[Auxiliary Loads\n12V HVAC Lights] -->|DC DC| BAT
    CHG[Charger Port\nAC or DC] --> BAT
    REG[Regen Brake\nPedal Sensor] --> VCU
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** indicate forward traction power flow.
- The **dotted arrows** indicate regenerative power flow (reverse direction).
- The **VCU** is the brain — it commands the inverter's switching (PWM duty) based on the accelerator pedal position, brake pedal position, SoC, and motor speed feedback.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ICE[Internal Combustion Engine] --> GEN[Generator\nMG1]
    GEN --> RECT[Rectifier and DC Link]
    RECT --> BAT[Battery Pack]
    RECT --> INV[Inverter]
    BAT --> INV
    INV --> M[Traction Motor\nMG2]
    M --> TR[Transmission]
    TR --> W[Wheels]
    W -.->|regen| M
    M -.->|regen| INV
    INV -.->|regen| BAT
    VCU2[VCU and EMS] --> ICE
    VCU2 --> RECT
    VCU2 --> INV
    FUEL[Fuel Tank] --> ICE
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The ICE **never** has a mechanical path to the wheels.
- The ICE can either charge the battery (via generator) **or** feed the motor directly (passing through the DC link).
- This is the architecture of the **BMW i3 REx** and **Chevrolet Volt (1st gen)**.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ICE2[Internal Combustion Engine] --> CLUTCH[Torque Coupler\nClutch or Planetary]
    BAT2[Battery Pack] --> INV2[Inverter]
    INV2 --> MOT[Electric Motor\nMG2]
    MOT --> CLUTCH
    CLUTCH --> TRANS[Transmission\nManual or AMT]
    TRANS --> W2[Wheels]
    W2 -.->|regen| MOT
    MOT -.->|regen| INV2
    INV2 -.->|regen| BAT2
    VCU3[VCU and EMS] --> ICE2
    VCU3 --> CLUTCH
    VCU3 --> INV2
    FUEL2[Fuel Tank] --> ICE2
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- ICE and motor are **mechanically in parallel** at the clutch node.
- Either can drive the wheels alone, or both together (torque addition).
- This is the architecture of the **Honda Insight (1st gen)** and **Hyundai Sonata Hybrid (6th gen)**.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Series-Parallel (Power-Split) HEV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ICE3[ICE Atkinson Cycle] --> CAR[Planetary Carrier\nEpicyclic Gear Set]
    SUN[MG1\nStarter Generator] --> SUNg[Sun Gear]
    CAR --> SUNg
    RING[Ring Gear] --> MG2[MG2\nTraction Motor]
    MG2 --> FD[Final Drive]
    FD --> W3[Wheels]
    BAT3[Battery Pack] --> INV3[Inverter]
    INV3 --> SUN
    INV3 --> MG2
    MG2 -.->|regen| INV3
    INV3 -.->|regen| BAT3
    SUN -.->|gen mode| INV3
    VCU4[THS Controller\nPCU and VCU] --> ICE3
    VCU4 --> INV3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **planetary gear set** is the heart — it splits ICE power between MG1 (electrical path) and the ring gear (mechanical path).
- MG1 controls the *speed ratio* by acting as a generator whose reaction torque changes the carrier speed.
- MG2 is the primary traction motor; in regen mode, it acts as a generator charging the battery.
- This is the architecture of the **Toyota Prius, Camry Hybrid, and most Lexus hybrids**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mermaid mistake to avoid:** Never name a node `End`, `Graph`, `Subgraph`, or `Style` (reserved keywords). Node IDs must be alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `MG1`, `BAT3`, `VCU4`). Labels inside double-quotes must be plain alphanumeric — no bold, italics, or HTML tables.

---

## 4.5 Signal-Flow vs. Power-Flow Distinction (KTU Favourite)

| Diagram type | What flows | Notation | Where used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power-flow block diagram** | Watts (W) | Solid arrows with direction | Energy routing, sizing |
| **Signal-flow block diagram** | Volts, CAN messages | Dashed arrows, labelled with signal name | Control logic, VCU firmware |

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark answers:** When you draw a block diagram, *always* state explicitly **"Solid arrows = power flow, dashed arrows = signal/control flow"** in the caption. Examiners reward this clarity.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Model]**
**Define a Hybrid Electric Vehicle. How is it classified based on the degree of hybridization?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) is a vehicle that uses two or more energy sources — typically an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) and one or more electric machines — for propulsion, in order to improve fuel economy, reduce emissions, and enhance performance.

Based on the degree of hybridization (1 mark):
- **Micro-Hybrid (μHEV):** Equipped only with a start-stop system and brake-energy recuperation; the 12 V starter doubles as a mild generator. (1 mark)
- **Mild-Hybrid (MHEV):** Uses a 48 V belt-driven or integrated starter-generator (BSG/ISG) providing torque assist; cannot run in pure EV mode. (0.5 mark)
- **Full-Hybrid (FHEV):** Has a high-voltage (> 100 V) electric machine powerful enough to propel the vehicle in pure EV mode for short distances (e.g., Toyota THS-II). (0.5 mark)

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**List the main components of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) and state the function of any three.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Main components (1 mark for list, 2 marks for function explanation):
1. **Battery Pack (Energy Storage System):** Stores DC electrical energy electrochemically (typically Li-ion, LFP, or NMC chemistry). Supplies high-voltage DC to the inverter. (0.67 mark)
2. **Power Inverter (PEM):** Converts DC from the battery into variable-frequency, variable-amplitude three-phase AC for the traction motor, using IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs with PWM. (0.67 mark)
3. **Traction Motor:** Converts electrical energy to mechanical rotary motion. Common types are PMSM, BLDC, and Induction Motor. (0.67 mark)
4. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU):** Acts as the brain, executing the torque-demand algorithm, mode selection, and SoC management. (0.67 mark)
5. **DC-DC Converter:** Steps down the high-voltage DC bus to 12 V (or 48 V) for auxiliaries. (0.67 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Series & Parallel Architecture Focus*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)**. Explain the function of each block and the various operating modes with the help of power flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **Series and Parallel HEV** architectures with respect to: (i) mechanical complexity, (ii) efficiency at highway cruise, (iii) pure EV-mode capability, (iv) need for a generator, (v) typical applications, and (vi) two real-world examples each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes. The student must show:

$$\text{ICE} \rightarrow \text{Generator (MG1)} \rightarrow \text{Rectifier + DC Link} \rightarrow \text{Battery}$$
$$\text{Battery (and/or DC Link)} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Traction Motor (MG2)} \rightarrow \text{Transmission} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

**Function of each block (2 marks):**
- **ICE:** Prime mover of the series path; sized for constant optimum (BSFC) operation, not for peak load.
- **Generator (MG1):** Converts ICE mechanical output to electricity.
- **Rectifier/DC Link:** Conditions the generator output and provides a common DC bus.
- **Battery:** Buffers energy; allows ICE to operate at its sweet spot regardless of instantaneous wheel demand.
- **Inverter:** Converts DC to variable-frequency AC for the traction motor.
- **Traction Motor (MG2):** Sole source of wheel torque.
- **VCU:** Decides ICE on/off, MG1 torque, and MG2 torque to satisfy the driver's command.

**Operating Modes (2 marks):**
1. **Pure EV mode:** ICE off, battery → motor → wheels.
2. **Series traction:** ICE → MG1 → DC link → motor → wheels (with or without battery assist).
3. **Battery charging mode:** ICE → MG1 → battery (range-extender mode, vehicle at standstill or coasting).
4. **Regenerative braking:** Wheels → motor (acting as generator) → inverter → battery.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per row + 0.5 for the conclusion):**

| Parameter | Series HEV | Parallel HEV |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical complexity | Low — no mechanical link ICE–wheels; simple transmission | Higher — needs clutch/torque coupler, sometimes 2-clutch automated manual |
| Highway cruise efficiency | Lower — *two* energy conversions (mech → elec → mech) | Higher — ICE drives wheels mechanically (single conversion) |
| Pure EV-mode capability | Excellent — motor is sole traction source | Limited — only in light-load / low-speed conditions |
| Need for a generator | Yes — MG1 essential | No — motor can act as generator when needed |
| Typical applications | City buses, range-extended passenger cars | Mid-size sedans, SUVs, light trucks |
| Real-world example 1 | BMW i3 REx | Honda Civic Hybrid (1st gen) |
| Real-world example 2 | Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1) | Hyundai Sonata Hybrid (6th gen) |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Series-Parallel & Power-Split Focus*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the **block diagram of a Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid Electric Vehicle** and explain the role of the planetary gear set in achieving power split. Derive the Willis equation relating the speeds of the sun, carrier, and ring gears. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electric vehicle of mass **1500 kg** (including battery) is to cruise at a constant **80 km/h** on a level road. The coefficient of rolling resistance is **0.012**, the drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$, frontal area $A_f = 2.2$ m², and air density $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³. The drivetrain efficiency (motor + inverter + transmission combined) is **0.88**. Compute: (i) the tractive force, (ii) the power required at the wheels, and (iii) the electrical power drawn from the battery. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.4** of these notes. The student must show the planetary gear set with ICE on the carrier, MG1 on the sun, MG2 + final drive + wheels on the ring, plus the battery and inverter. Solid arrows = power flow, dashed arrows = control flow.

**Role of the planetary gear set (2 marks):**
The planetary gear set acts as a **continuously variable power-split device (PSD)**. It divides the ICE's mechanical power between two paths:
- **Electrical path (carrier → sun):** ICE drives MG1 as a generator, producing electricity that powers MG2 (traction motor) or charges the battery.
- **Mechanical path (carrier → ring):** ICE directly drives the ring gear, which is coupled to MG2 and the wheels.

The split ratio is controlled by varying the speed (and hence the reaction torque) of MG1. This eliminates the need for a multi-speed gearbox while keeping the ICE at its most efficient operating point.

**Willis Equation Derivation (2 marks):**
For a planetary gear with sun $N_S$ teeth and ring $N_R$ teeth, define $R_{psd} = N_R/N_S$. The kinematic constraint of an epicyclic gear train gives:

$$
\frac{\omega_S - \omega_C}{\omega_R - \omega_C} = -\frac{N_R}{N_S} = -R_{psd}
$$

Solving for the sun speed:

$$
\boxed{\;\omega_S = (1 + R_{psd})\,\omega_C - R_{psd}\,\omega_R\;}
$$

In the THS context: $\omega_S = \omega_{MG1}$, $\omega_C = \omega_{ICE}$, $\omega_R = \omega_{wheel}$, hence:

$$
\omega_{MG1} = (1 + R_{psd})\,\omega_{ice} - R_{psd}\,\omega_{wheel}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $m = 1500$ kg
- $v = 80$ km/h $= 22.222$ m/s
- $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$
- $C_d = 0.30$
- $A_f = 2.2$ m²
- $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³
- $\eta_{dt} = 0.88$
- $\theta = 0°$ (level road), $a = 0$ (constant speed)

**Step 1 — Rolling resistance (1 mark):**
$$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr}\, m\, g\, \cos\theta = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times \cos 0°$$
$$F_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Aerodynamic drag (1 mark):**
$$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2}\, \rho_a\, C_d\, A_f\, v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (22.222)^2$$
$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 493.83 = 199.62 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Hill-climbing and inertia (1 mark):**
At level road ($\theta = 0°$) and constant speed ($a = 0$):
$$F_{hc} = m\, g\, \sin 0° = 0 \text{ N}$$
$$F_{ia} = m \cdot a = 0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Total tractive force (1 mark):**
$$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia} = 176.58 + 199.62 + 0 + 0$$
$$\boxed{F_t \approx 376.20 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 5 — Power at the wheels (1 mark):**
$$P_w = F_t \cdot v = 376.20 \times 22.222 = 8359.94 \text{ W}$$
$$\boxed{P_w \approx 8.36 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 6 — Electrical power from the battery (2 marks):**
Account for drivetrain losses:
$$P_{bat} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{dt}} = \frac{8359.94}{0.88} = 9499.93 \text{ W}$$
$$\boxed{P_{bat} \approx 9.50 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 7 — Energy consumption per km (bonus, for completeness):**
$$E_{per\,km} = \frac{P_{bat}}{v} = \frac{9499.93 \text{ W}}{22.222 \text{ m/s}} = 427.5 \text{ J/m} = 0.4275 \text{ Wh/m} = 427.5 \text{ Wh/km}$$
This is a realistic number for a mid-size EV at highway cruise (≈ 14–15 kWh/100 km).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing power flow with signal flow** in block diagrams (1–2 marks lost). Always use **solid arrows for power**, **dashed arrows for control signals**, and state it in the caption.
> 2. **Forgetting the regen path.** A block diagram without the reverse arrows (wheels → motor → battery) is incomplete. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Not labelling all components** (VCU, DC-DC, auxiliaries, charger). Listing only ICE, motor, battery, wheels is worth only 2 of the 3 component-marks.
> 4. **Willis equation sign error** — the negative sign in $-N_R/N_S$ is frequently missed; cost: 1 mark.
> 5. **Numerical mistakes in tractive-force problems** — mixing km/h with m/s (cost: 1–2 marks). Always convert: $v$ in m/s = $(v_{km/h} \times 1000/3600) = v_{km/h}/3.6$.
> 6. **Missing the "level road" assumption** in numerical problems — students sometimes include a hill-climbing term even when $\theta = 0$.
> 7. **Omitting the drivetrain efficiency** — the question asks for "battery power," so the student must divide $P_w$ by $\eta_{dt}$, not equate them.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist. It is your last-minute revision sheet.**

- [ ] **EV definition:** Traction energy supplied *exclusively* by electric machine fed by an energy storage device.
- [ ] **HEV definition:** Two or more energy sources/powertrains; ICE + Electric Motor is the canonical combination.
- [ ] **Three HEV architectures:** Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel (Power-Split).
- [ ] **Series HEV key fact:** ICE has *no* mechanical path to the wheels; acts only as a range extender.
- [ ] **Parallel HEV key fact:** ICE and motor are *mechanically coupled*; both can drive wheels.
- [ ] **Series-Parallel key fact:** Uses a **planetary gear set** as the power-split device (Toyota THS).
- [ ] **Willis equation:** $\omega_S = (1+R_{psd})\,\omega_C - R_{psd}\,\omega_R$.
- [ ] **Tractive force equation:** $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$.
- [ ] **Each resistance formula:**
  - $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr}\, m\, g\, \cos\theta$
  - $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho_a\, C_d\, A_f\, v^2$
  - $F_{hc} = m\, g\, \sin\theta$
  - $F_{ia} = m\, a$
- [ ] **Power at wheels:** $P_w = F_t \cdot v$ (W).
- [ ] **Power from battery:** $P_{bat} = P_w / \eta_{dt}$.
- [ ] **Block-diagram conventions:** Solid = power, Dashed = control; always include regen arrows.
- [ ] **VCU role:** Torque command, mode selection, SoC management, fault diagnostics.
- [ ] **Real-world examples:**
  - BEV → Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV, Tesla Model 3.
  - Series HEV → BMW i3 REx, Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1), Nissan e-Power (range-extender).
  - Parallel HEV → Honda Insight (1st gen), Honda Civic Hybrid, Hyundai Sonata Hybrid.
  - Series-Parallel HEV → Toyota Prius, Camry Hybrid, Lexus ES 300h.
- [ ] **Standard constants to memorize:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.01$.
- [ ] **Standard drive cycles:** NEDC, WLTP, FTP-75, IDC (Indian Driving Cycle).
- [ ] **Efficiency benchmark:** Best ICE bsfc ≈ 220–250 g/kWh; EV drivetrain ≈ 85–92 %; combined HEV cycle efficiency ≈ 35–40 %.

> **End of Module 4 — Principles and Block Diagrams Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SRM Drive Converter - Classical Half-Bridge Converter
Vehicle Sensors Specific to EV Sensors Interfaced to the ECUs in the Vehicle Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Sensors Specific to EV Sensors Interfaced to the ECUs in the Vehicle Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vehicle Sensors Specific to EVs** are specialized electromechanical, electronic, and electro-chemical transducers that monitor critical physical, electrical, thermal, and kinematic parameters of an electric or hybrid powertrain and convert them into standardized electrical signals (analog voltage/current, PWM, SENT, or digital frames) that are consumed by the corresponding **Electronic Control Units (ECUs)** over an in-vehicle network (predominantly **CAN 2.0B / CAN FD**, with emerging migration to **Automotive Ethernet 100BASE-T1**).

The term **"ECU"** in an EV refers to multiple domain controllers:
- **VCU** – Vehicle Control Unit (top-level supervisory controller)
- **MCU** – Motor Control Unit (inverter + traction motor controller)
- **BMS** – Battery Management System (master + slave Cell Monitoring Controllers)
- **OBC** – On-Board Charger controller
- **DC-DC Converter controller**
- **VCMS / TCU** – Thermal Management / Transmission Control Unit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The scope of this topic is strictly limited to sensors whose output is *not* present in a conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle, or whose specification has been radically upgraded due to the EV powertrain. Examples include high-voltage isolation-graded current sensors, Hall-effect rotor position resolvers, pack-level thermistors, and ISO 6469-3 compliant Insulation Monitoring Devices (IMD).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Five Senses of an Electric Vehicle":**
> Imagine a human body playing tennis. The brain (VCU) cannot see, hear, or feel the ball directly. Instead, it relies on:
> - **Eyes (Wheel speed, rotor position, IMU sensors)** → sense where the body and ball are.
> - **Skin (Pack thermistors, motor winding RTDs, coolant temperature sensors)** → sense how hot things are getting.
> - **Hands (Accelerator pedal position sensor, brake pressure transducer, torque sensor)** → sense what the driver *wants*.
> - **Inner ear balance (IMU gyroscope/accelerometer)** → sense orientation, pitch, and yaw.
> - **Nerve signals (CAN bus, LIN bus)** → carry all these sensory signals at high speed to the brain's processing regions (ECUs).
>
> Just as a tennis player with closed eyes cannot play, an EV without these sensors cannot regulate torque, protect the battery, or maintain stability. The sensors are the **gateway between physical reality and digital decision-making**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Symbol / Standard | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **CAN bus bit rate (Classical CAN)** | **125 kbps – 1 Mbps** |
| **CAN FD max bit rate** | **2 Mbps (data phase), 5 Mbps (some implementations)** |
| **CAN frame max payload (Classical)** | **8 bytes** |
| **CAN FD frame max payload** | **64 bytes** |
| **High-voltage battery pack voltage** | **300 V – 800 V** (modern EVs) |
| **Standard traction motor RPM** | **0 – 20,000 rpm** |
| **Hall constant (B in T, typical Si)** | **R_H ≈ −10⁻⁴ m³/C** |
| **RTD PT1000 at 0 °C** | **1000 Ω** |
| **ISO 6469-3** | Insulation resistance must exceed **100 Ω/V** |
| **ISO 26262 ASIL** | Up to **ASIL D** for BMS, MCU, VCU sensor paths |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sensor Classification in an EV (Domain-Wise)

#### 2.1.1 Battery Management System (BMS) Sensors
1. **Cell Voltage Sensor** – Differential ADC front-end per cell; uses Kelvin connection.
2. **Pack Current Sensor** – Either:
   - **Hall-effect closed-loop (LEM-type)** transducer (galvanically isolated), OR
   - **Shunt resistor** (non-isolated, used in 12 V/48 V low-voltage domains).
3. **Cell/Pack Temperature Sensor** – **NTC thermistor** (10 kΩ at 25 °C, β = 3950 K typical) or digital temperature IC (e.g., TMP117).
4. **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)** – Per **ISO 6469-3**, injects a PWM/AC measurement signal between HV+ and chassis.
5. **Coolant Inlet/Outlet Temperature Sensor** – PT100/PT1000 RTD.

#### 2.1.2 Motor Control Unit (MCU) Sensors
1. **Rotor Position Sensor**:
   - **Resolver** (sin/cos inductive transducer, rugged, used at >10,000 rpm).
   - **GMR / TMR Encoder** (higher resolution, used in lower-speed applications).
2. **Phase Current Sensor** – Three-phase, either:
   - Closed-loop Hall-effect (e.g., LEM LF 306-S).
   - **In-line shunt with isolated Σ-Δ modulator** (e.g., AMC1301).
3. **Motor Winding Temperature** – PT1000 RTD embedded in stator slot.
4. **Inverter IGBT/MOSFET Temperature** – NTC on heatsink or integrated thermal diode.

#### 2.1.3 Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) Sensors
1. **Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor (APPS)** – *Dual-track* (redundant) Hall-effect or contactless inductive sensor.
2. **Brake Pedal Position Sensor** – Dual Hall-effect.
3. **Wheel Speed Sensor (WSS)** – Active Hall or variable reluctance (reluctor) type.
4. **Steering Angle Sensor (SAS)** – Optical encoder, multi-turn capable.
5. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** – 6-DoF MEMS accelerometer + gyroscope for **Vehicle Dynamics Control (VDC/ESC)** and **regenerative brake blending**.
6. **Vacuum/Brake Pressure Sensor** – Critical for regen-to-friction brake transition.
7. **Gear Position Sensor** – Hall-effect rotary switch.

#### 2.1.4 Charging System Sensors
1. **Control Pilot (CP)** – PWM ±12 V communication per **IEC 61851-1**.
2. **Proximity Pilot (PP)** – Resistor-coded cable current rating detection.
3. **Charging Inlet Temperature** – NTC in CCS Type-2 connector for **Thermal Runaway prevention during DC fast-charge**.
4. **EVSE Lock Actuator Position Feedback**.

### 2.2 Why These Sensors Are EV-Specific

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** A common 3-mark question asks *"Why can't a conventional Lambda oxygen sensor or crank-angle sensor be used in an EV?"* The answer must mention: EVs lack combustion, so exhaust sensors are absent. Instead, **rotor position, pack voltage/current, and isolation resistance** become primary feedback variables.

### 2.3 Operating Principle Deep-Dive

#### 2.3.1 Hall-Effect Current Sensor (Closed-Loop)
A primary current $I_p$ flows through a soft magnetic core. The Hall element in the air gap generates:
$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I_H \cdot B}{t}$$
where $R_H$ is the Hall constant, $I_H$ the bias current, $B$ the flux density, and $t$ the Hall plate thickness. In a **closed-loop** configuration, an auxiliary compensation coil drives an opposing flux to maintain $B \approx 0$, making the output current $I_s$ a direct linear image of $I_p$ with **galvanic isolation up to several kV**.

#### 2.3.2 Resolver-Based Rotor Position
A resolver consists of a **rotor (excited by a reference carrier $V_{ref} = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$)** and two stator windings displaced by **90° (sine and cosine)**. The induced EMFs are:
$$V_{sin} = K \cdot \sin(\theta_r) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$
$$V_{cos} = K \cdot \cos(\theta_r) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$
A **Resolver-to-Digital Converter (RDC) IC** (e.g., AD2S1210) decodes $\theta_r$ with sub-degree accuracy even at 20,000 rpm.

#### 2.3.3 NTC Thermistor (BMS Temperature)
$$R(T) = R_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$
where $R_0$ is the resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically 25 °C = 298.15 K), and $\beta$ is the material constant (typically **3435 K – 4250 K**).

#### 2.3.4 SOC Estimation via Coulomb Counting (Sensor Data Fusion)
$$\text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}(t_0) - \frac{1}{Q_n} \int_{t_0}^{t} \eta \cdot I(\tau) \, d\tau$$
where $Q_n$ is nominal pack capacity in Ah, $\eta$ is the Coulombic efficiency, and $I(\tau)$ is the current obtained from the **pack current sensor**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` (never `|`) inside LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hall output voltage | $V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I_H \cdot B}{t}$ | V | Closed-loop current sensor |
| NTC thermistor resistance | $R(T) = R_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}-\dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | Ω | BMS temperature sensing |
| Resolver sine EMF | $V_{sin} = K \sin\theta_r \sin\omega t$ | V | Rotor position decoding |
| Resolver cosine EMF | $V_{cos} = K \cos\theta_r \sin\omega t$ | V | Rotor position decoding |
| SOC (Coulomb counting) | $\mathrm{SOC}(t) = \mathrm{SOC}(t_0) - \dfrac{\eta}{Q_n}\!\int_{t_0}^{t}\!I(\tau)\,d\tau$ | % | Battery state-of-charge |
| SOH (capacity fade) | $\mathrm{SOH} = \dfrac{Q_{max,actual}}{Q_{max,rated}} \times 100$ | % | Battery aging |
| Shunt resistor voltage drop | $V_{shunt} = I \cdot R_{shunt}$ | V | Low-voltage current sensing |
| Insulation resistance limit | $R_{iso} \ge 100\,\Omega/\mathrm{V}$ (ISO 6469-3) | Ω/V | HV safety compliance |
| CAN bit time | $t_{bit} = t_{SYNC\_SEG} + t_{PROP\_SEG} + t_{PHASE\_SEG1} + t_{PHASE\_SEG2}$ | s | Bus arbitration timing |
| Brake regen split (simplified) | $T_{regen} = T_{req} \cdot k_{SOC} \cdot k_{temp} \cdot k_{speed}$ | N·m | VCU torque arbitration |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Functional Safety (ISO 26262):** All powertrain sensors are categorized up to **ASIL D**; redundancy is mandatory (dual APPS tracks, dual resolver channels).
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Insulation monitoring and isolation barriers are mandated by **ECE R100** and **AIS-156** (India).
- **Cyber-Physical Security:** CAN bus sensors are increasingly authenticated using **SecOC (Secure Onboard Communication)** with HMAC-truncated MACs to prevent spoofing attacks (e.g., Tesla CAN injection research).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Rotor Angle from Resolver Signals

**Given:** Two stator winding voltages from a Variable-Reluctance (VR) resolver excited by a 10 kHz carrier.

$$V_{ref}(t) = V_0 \sin(\omega t), \quad \omega = 2\pi f_{exc}$$

The induced EMFs in the sine and cosine windings are modulated by the instantaneous rotor angle $\theta_r(t)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sin}(t) &= K \cdot \sin\theta_r(t) \cdot \sin(\omega t) \\
V_{cos}(t) &= K \cdot \cos\theta_r(t) \cdot \sin(\omega t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Synchronous demodulation — multiply each signal by the carrier $\sin(\omega t)$ and pass through a low-pass filter (LPF) with cutoff $f_c \ll f_{exc}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sin}(t) \cdot \sin(\omega t) &= K \sin\theta_r \cdot \sin^2(\omega t) \\
&= \frac{K \sin\theta_r}{2} \bigl[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

After LPF, the $2\omega$ term is removed, leaving:

$$
V_{sin,\,demod} = \frac{K \sin\theta_r}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Repeat for the cosine channel.

$$
V_{cos,\,demod} = \frac{K \cos\theta_r}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the rotor angle using the arctangent of the ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_r &= \arctan\!\left(\frac{V_{sin,\,demod}}{V_{cos,\,demod}}\right) \\
&= \arctan\!\left(\frac{\sin\theta_r}{\cos\theta_r}\right) \\
&= \theta_r \quad \text{(validated)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Caveat:** Practical RDCs use Type-II tracking loops (PLL with PI filter) instead of a pure $\arctan$ to obtain infinite angular range and noise rejection.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — NTC Temperature from Resistance Divider

**Given:** NTC thermistor $R_{NTC}$ at temperature $T$ in a half-bridge with pull-up $R_{pull} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, ADC reference $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1:** Voltage divider output.

$$
V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{NTC}}{R_{pull} + R_{NTC}}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $R_{NTC}$:

$$
R_{NTC} = R_{pull} \cdot \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ref} - V_{out}}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the NTC β-equation to recover $T$ (in Kelvin):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{R_{NTC}}{R_0} &= \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right] \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{R_{NTC}}{R_0}\right) &= \beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) \\
\frac{1}{T} &= \frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{\beta}\ln\!\left(\frac{R_{NTC}}{R_0}\right) \\
T &= \left[\frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{\beta}\ln\!\left(\frac{R_{NTC}}{R_0}\right)\right]^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:**

Let $R_0 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 298.15\,\text{K}$, $\beta = 3950\,\text{K}$, and $V_{out} = 2.5\,\text{V}$.

$$
R_{NTC} = 10000 \cdot \frac{2.5}{5 - 2.5} = 10000\,\Omega
$$

Since $R_{NTC} = R_0$, we recover $T = T_0 = 298.15\,\text{K} = 25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. ✓

---

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — CAN Bus Message Scheduling

**Given:** Classical CAN 2.0B bus at **500 kbps**, 11-bit identifier. Each frame: 1 start-of-frame + 11-bit ID + 1 RTR + 1 IDE + 1 r0 + 4 DLC + 0–8 data bytes + 15-bit CRC + 1 ACK + 1 ACK-delim + 7 EOF = **108 bits** (worst case, 8 data bytes).

**Step 1:** Bit time at 500 kbps.

$$
t_{bit} = \frac{1}{500000} = 2\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Frame transmission time.

$$
t_{frame} = 108 \times 2\,\mu\text{s} = 216\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Bus utilization for $N$ sensors each transmitting at rate $f_i$:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \cdot t_{frame}
$$

For example, with **5 BMS messages @ 100 Hz** and **3 MCU messages @ 500 Hz**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{BMS} &= 5 \times 100 \times 216\,\mu\text{s} = 0.108 = 10.8\% \\
U_{MCU} &= 3 \times 500 \times 216\,\mu\text{s} = 0.324 = 32.4\% \\
U_{total} &= 43.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule-of-Thumb:** Classical CAN utilization must be kept below **≈ 70–75 %** to preserve latency margin. Above this, **CAN FD** or **Automotive Ethernet** becomes necessary.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensor-to-ECU Mapping

| Sensor | Transduction Principle | Signal Type | Target ECU | Bus Protocol |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pack current (Hall) | Hall effect, closed-loop | Analog ±4 V or SENT | BMS | CAN |
| Cell voltage | Resistive divider + ADC | Digital (SPI → MCU) | Cell Monitor IC | Daisy-chain SPI / CAN |
| Pack temperature (NTC) | Resistance-temperature | Resistive → analog | BMS | CAN |
| Rotor position (Resolver) | Mutual inductance | sin/cos modulated carrier | MCU | Analog front-end to RDC |
| Phase current (LEM) | Fluxgate / Hall | Analog ±4 V | MCU | Analog to ADC |
| APPS (dual track) | Hall or inductive | Analog 0.5 V – 4.5 V | VCU | Analog + redundant CAN |
| Wheel speed (WSS) | Hall effect | Digital square wave | ABS/ESC ECU | CAN |
| IMU (6-axis) | MEMS capacitive | SPI / I²C | VCU/ESC | CAN |
| Steering angle (SAS) | Optical encoder | SPI / Analog | EPS ECU | CAN |
| Control Pilot | PWM per IEC 61851 | PWM 1 kHz, ±12 V | OBC / EVSE | Discrete / CAN |

---

### 3.5 Analytical Derivation 4 — Insulation Resistance Calculation (ISO 6469-3)

The IMD injects a known DC test voltage $V_{test}$ between HV bus and chassis and measures the leakage current $I_{leak}$.

$$
R_{iso} = \frac{V_{test}}{I_{leak}}
$$

**Compliance:** The system must disconnect HV within **seconds** if $R_{iso} < 100\,\Omega/\text{V}$ of nominal pack voltage. For a 400 V pack:

$$
R_{iso,\,min} = 100 \times 400 = 40{,}000\,\Omega = 40\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** Students often confuse **insulation resistance** with **isolation voltage**. The former is a *minimum resistance to ground*; the latter is a *dielectric withstand voltage* (typically 2.5 kV AC or 3.5 kV DC for 800 V systems).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of EV Sensor Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BAT["HV Battery Pack (400V / 800V)"]
        CELL1[Cell 1 Voltage Sensor]
        CELLN[Cell N Voltage Sensor]
        NTCA[NTC Thermistor x4]
        IMB[IMD Insulation Monitor]
    end

    subgraph MOT["Traction Motor + Inverter"]
        RES[Resolver Sin/Cos]
        HALLU[Phase U Hall Current]
        HALLV[Phase V Hall Current]
        HALLW[Phase W Hall Current]
        RTDM[Motor Winding RTD PT1000]
        IGBTT[IGBT Heatsink NTC]
    end

    subgraph VEH["Vehicle Dynamics Sensors"]
        APPS[Accelerator Pedal Dual Hall]
        BRK[Brake Pedal Position]
        WSSFL[Wheel Speed FL]
        WSSFR[Wheel Speed FR]
        WSSRL[Wheel Speed RL]
        WSSRR[Wheel Speed RR]
        SAS[Steering Angle Sensor]
        IMU[6-axis IMU]
    end

    subgraph CHG["Charging Interface"]
        CP[Control Pilot PWM]
        PP[Proximity Pilot]
        CHGTEMP[Charging Port NTC]
    end

    subgraph ECU["ECUs and In-Vehicle Network"]
        BMS[BMS Master ECU]
        MCU[Motor Control Unit]
        VCU[Vehicle Control Unit]
        OBC[On-Board Charger]
        ABS[ABS / ESC ECU]
        CANBUS[CAN 2.0B Bus 500 kbps]
        CANFD[CAN FD Bus 2 Mbps]
    end

    CELL1 -->|Analog diff| BMS
    CELLN -->|Analog diff| BMS
    NTCA -->|Resistance| BMS
    IMB -->|PWM/AC test| BMS

    RES -->|sin/cos 10kHz| MCU
    HALLU -->|+/-4V| MCU
    HALLV -->|+/-4V| MCU
    HALLW -->|+/-4V| MCU
    RTDM -->|PT1000| MCU
    IGBTT -->|NTC divider| MCU

    APPS -->|Dual 0.5-4.5V| VCU
    BRK -->|Dual Hall| VCU
    SAS -->|SPI / Analog| VCU
    IMU -->|SPI / CAN| VCU

    WSSFL -->|Digital| ABS
    WSSFR -->|Digital| ABS
    WSSRL -->|Digital| ABS
    WSSRR -->|Digital| ABS
    ABS -->|CAN| CANBUS

    CP -->|PWM 1kHz| OBC
    PP -->|Resistor code| OBC
    CHGTEMP -->|NTC| OBC

    BMS -->|CAN FD| CANFD
    MCU -->|CAN FD| CANFD
    OBC -->|CAN| CANBUS
    VCU -->|CAN FD| CANFD
    CANBUS --> VCU
    CANFD --> VCU
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — APPS Signal Flow for Torque Request

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DRV as Driver
    participant APPS as APPS Dual Hall Sensor
    participant VCU as VCU
    participant BMS as BMS
    participant MCU as Motor Control Unit
    participant INV as Inverter
    participant MOT as Traction Motor

    DRV->>APPS: Press accelerator pedal
    APPS->>VCU: Track1 voltage + Track2 voltage
    Note over APPS,VCU: Plausibility check (Track2 = 2 x Track1 within +/-5%)
    VCU->>BMS: CAN: SOC, max discharge current
    BMS-->>VCU: CAN: Allowable power kW
    VCU->>VCU: Torque arbitration (driver vs regen vs thermal)
    VCU->>MCU: CAN: Torque request N.m, speed limit
    MCU->>INV: PWM duty cycles 3-phase
    INV->>MOT: 3-phase AC current
    MOT-->>RES: Rotor angle feedback
    RES-->>MCU: sin/cos voltages
    MCU-->>VCU: Actual torque, speed
    VCU-->>APPS: Closed-loop confirmation
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Insulation Fault Detection

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Normal: System Init
    Normal --> Measuring: IMD injects test signal
    Measuring --> Normal: R_iso >= 100 ohm/V
    Measuring --> Warning: R_iso between 50k and 100k ohm/V
    Warning --> Measuring: Periodic re-test
    Warning --> Fault: R_iso drops below threshold
    Fault --> OpenContactors: VCU commands HV disconnect
    OpenContactors --> SafeState: Contactors open within 200 ms
    SafeState --> ServiceMode: Technician intervenes
    ServiceMode --> Measuring: After repair confirmation
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
>
> **Q1. List any three sensors exclusively used in a Battery Management System (BMS) of an Electric Vehicle and state the physical principle behind each.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Cell Voltage Sensor** – Uses a precision **resistive voltage divider + differential amplifier** front-end, with a 16-bit ADC; principle: **Ohm's Law** combined with **Kelvin (4-wire) sensing** to eliminate lead-resistance error.
> 2. **Pack Current Sensor** – Uses the **Hall Effect** in a closed-loop core; principle: **Lorentz force** on charge carriers produces a transverse voltage proportional to flux density, hence primary current.
> 3. **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)** – Principle: **Ohm's Law applied to leakage current**; injects a test voltage and measures $R_{iso} = V_{test} / I_{leak}$. Per **ISO 6469-3**, $R_{iso}$ must exceed 100 Ω/V of nominal pack voltage.
>
