- Output: A probabilistic occupancy grid over a **3–5 second horizon**.

### Layer 4 — Planning
- **Route planning** (A* / Dijkstra over road network).
- **Behavioural planning** (FSM or RL agent choosing: yield, overtake, lane-change).
- **Trajectory / Motion planning** (Frenet-frame polynomial, MPC).

### Layer 5 — Control
- Convert planned trajectory into **steering angle $\delta$**, **longitudinal acceleration $a_x$**, and **brake pressure**.
- Controllers: **PID**, **LQR**, **Model Predictive Control (MPC)**.

### Layer 6 — Actuation (By-Wire)
- Steer-by-wire, brake-by-wire (regen + friction blending), throttle-by-wire.
- In EVs, regen enables **single-pedal driving** which simplifies longitudinal control loops.

## 2.3 Why EVs and HEVs Dominate Autonomy

| Feature | EV/HEV Advantage |
|---|---|
| **Drive-by-wire readiness** | No mechanical throttle linkage — control signals are native. |
| **Regenerative braking** | Smooth, programmable negative torque for precise longitudinal control. |
| **Quiet cabin** | Improves microphone-array voice/HMI sensors. |
| **High-capacity traction battery** | Powers LiDAR, GPU farms, and edge-AI compute without alternator limitations. |
| **Centralized E/E architecture** | Easier OTA updates for autonomy software. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **exam-day survival table**. Every entry below has appeared in at least one KTU university paper in the last three cycles.

| Symbol | Equation / Definition | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_{total}$ | $\tau_{total} = \tau_{sense} + \tau_{compute} + \tau_{actuate}$ | End-to-end latency budget | ms |
| $R_{LiDAR,max}$ | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot t_{TOF}}{2}$ | Max LiDAR range from time-of-flight | m |
| $\theta_{res}$ | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{2\pi}{N_{channels}}$ | Vertical angular resolution of LiDAR | rad |
| $\dot{x}$ (bicycle) | $\dot{x} = v \cos\psi$ | Forward velocity (world frame) | m/s |
| $\dot{y}$ (bicycle) | $\dot{y} = v \sin\psi$ | Lateral velocity (world frame) | m/s |
| $\dot{\psi}$ | $\dot{\psi} = \dfrac{v}{L} \tan\delta$ | Yaw rate (kinematic bicycle) | rad/s |
| $\kappa$ | $\kappa = \dfrac{\tan\delta}{L}$ | Path curvature under small-angle approx. | 1/m |
| $R_{safe}$ | $R_{safe} = v \cdot t_{reaction} + \dfrac{v^2}{2 a_{max,brake}}$ | Safe following distance (kinematic) | m |
| $SNR_{radar}$ | $SNR \propto \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4 kT_0 B NF}$ | Radar equation (range ∝ SNR^(1/4)) | dB |
| $f_{Doppler}$ | $f_D = \dfrac{2 v_{rel}}{\lambda}$ | Doppler shift (closing speed) | Hz |
| $\sigma_{fuse}$ | $\sigma_{fuse}^{-2} = \sigma_{1}^{-2} + \sigma_{2}^{-2}$ | Variance after 2-sensor fusion | m² |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation pitfall:** Never write absolute value with `\|` inside a markdown cell — it breaks table parsing. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The fusion of **EV by-wire architecture** with **AI-driven autonomy** is the strategic core of:
- **Robotaxi fleets** (Waymo, Cruise, Baidu Apollo Go).
- **Highway L3 pilots** (Mercedes Drive Pilot, Honda Sensing Elite).
- **Last-mile autonomous delivery** (Nuro, Starship).
- **Closed-domain L4** (mining trucks, port logistics — Daimler Truck, Volvo).

A graduate engineer from KTU entering an EV OEM or Tier-1 supplier (Bosch, Continental, Aptiv) will almost certainly contribute to **one of the five layers** above — making mastery of this module essential for placement interviews.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Kinematic Bicycle Model

The **kinematic bicycle model** is the workhorse of trajectory planning. It lumps the left and right wheels on each axle into a single virtual wheel.

### Step 1 — Define the State Vector
The vehicle state is described by:
$$s = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \psi \\ v \end{bmatrix}$$

where $x$ and $y$ are the centre-of-gravity coordinates in the world frame, $\psi$ is the **heading angle**, and $v$ is the longitudinal speed at the CG.

### Step 2 — Express Wheel Velocities in Body Frame
Resolve velocity at the rear axle along the body axes:

$$\dot{x}_r = v_r \cos\psi$$

$$\dot{y}_r = v_r \sin\psi$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Instantaneous-Centre-of-Rotation (ICR) Constraint
The front wheel traces a circular arc around the ICR located at perpendicular distance $L / \sin\delta$ from the front wheel. Differentiating gives yaw rate:

$$\dot{\psi} = \frac{v_r \tan\delta}{L}$$

### Step 4 — Write the Full State-Space Model
Combining, the standard kinematic bicycle model becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos\psi \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin\psi \\
\dot{\psi} &= \frac{v}{L} \tan\delta \\
\dot{v} &= a
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This model is **valid for low speeds** (typically $v < 20$ m/s) on flat roads. Above this, the **dynamic bicycle model** with tyre-slip is required. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises the **kinematic** version.

### Step 5 — Path Curvature
Inverting the yaw-rate equation gives the **curvature** of the path traced by the rear axle:

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{R_{turn}} = \frac{\tan\delta}{L}$$

This means a maximum steering angle $\delta_{max}$ corresponds to a **minimum turn radius**:

$$R_{min} = \frac{L}{\tan\delta_{max}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Safe Following Distance (Constant Time-Gap Policy)

The most common ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) policy used in Tesla and Toyota is the **Constant Time-Gap (CTG)** headway.

### Step 1 — Define Headway
The time headway $T_g$ is:

$$T_g = \frac{d}{v}$$

where $d$ is bumper-to-bumper gap and $v$ is ego-vehicle speed.

### Step 2 — Add Reaction + Braking Distance
For a **safety envelope**, the required gap must exceed the leader's braking distance and account for a *system reaction delay* $t_r$:

$$R_{safe} = v \cdot t_r + \frac{v^2}{2 a_{brake,max}}$$

### Step 3 — CTG Control Law
The acceleration command is:

$$a_{cmd} = k_p \left( d - v T_g - d_{0} \right) + k_d \left( v_{leader} - v \right)$$

where $d_0$ is a standstill offset (typically $\mathbf{2 \, m}$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensor-Fusion Variance Reduction (Kalman Form)

For two **independent** Gaussian measurements with variances $\sigma_1^2$ and $\sigma_2^2$, the **optimal fused estimate** has variance:

$$\sigma_{fuse}^2 = \frac{\sigma_1^2 \sigma_2^2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$$

Equivalently:

$$\frac{1}{\sigma_{fuse}^2} = \frac{1}{\sigma_1^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_2^2}$$

**Numerical Example:**
- Radar range measurement: $\sigma_1 = 0.5$ m.
- LiDAR range measurement: $\sigma_2 = 0.1$ m.

Then:

$$\frac{1}{\sigma_{fuse}^2} = \frac{1}{0.25} + \frac{1}{0.01} = 4 + 100 = 104$$

$$\sigma_{fuse}^2 = \frac{1}{104} \approx 0.00962$$

$$\sigma_{fuse} \approx 0.0981 \text{ m}$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice the fused standard deviation is **better than the better sensor alone** ($\sigma_2 = 0.1$). This is the central theorem behind why modern AVs use **heterogeneous sensor fusion**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — LiDAR Range from Time-of-Flight

A LiDAR emits a pulse, it reflects off an object and returns. The round-trip time-of-flight is $t_{TOF}$. With speed of light $c$:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot t_{TOF}}{2}$$

**Numerical check:** Suppose $t_{TOF} = 1.667 \, \mu s$ (typical for a far-field return):

$$R = \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(1.667 \times 10^{-6})}{2} = \frac{500}{2} = 250 \, \text{m}$$

This matches the typical forward automotive LiDAR range.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SAE Levels vs. System Capability

| Capability | L0 | L1 | L2 | L3 | L4 | L5 |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Sustained lateral control | ❌ | ⚠️ one axis | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Sustained longitudinal control | ❌ | ⚠️ one axis | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Driver may divert eyes | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| System handles fallback (ODD) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Driver required (no ODD) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between SAE Level 2 and SAE Level 3 driving automation with a suitable real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Level 2 — Partial | Level 3 — Conditional |
|---|---|---|
| Lateral + Longitudinal control | Sustained by system | Sustained by system |
| Driver attention | **Must monitor** continuously | **May divert attention** within ODD |
| Fallback responsibility | Driver (immediate) | System (driver given ~10 s warning) |
| ODD restriction | None specified | **Yes** (e.g., highway ≤ 60 km/h) |
| Real-world example | **Tesla Autopilot (basic)** | **Mercedes Drive Pilot** (highway, daylight) |

**Examiner note (1 mark):** A student who does **not** mention the *driver attention* distinction loses 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any three on-board sensors used in autonomous EVs and explain how their data is combined.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Camera** (RGB, 8 MP): Provides semantic richness — traffic-light state, lane markings, pedestrian classification.
2. **LiDAR** (128-channel, 905 nm): Provides dense 3-D point cloud — precise obstacle geometry up to 200 m.
3. **Radar** (77 GHz, FMCW): Provides Doppler velocity — robust in fog/rain; long range (~250 m).

**Fusion method (1 mark):**
A **Kalman / Extended-Kalman filter** or a **deep sensor-fusion network** (e.g., BEVFusion) combines detections from all three. Camera + LiDAR are usually fused at the **feature level** (early fusion); radar is fused at the **object level** (late fusion) due to its sparse but velocity-rich output. Output: a unified **3-D object list with velocity and classification**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) (7 Marks): With a neat block diagram, explain the five functional layers of an autonomous electric vehicle driving stack. State the primary sensor or algorithm used in each layer.**

**Model Solution:**

The five functional layers are:

**Layer 1 — Perception (2 marks)**
- Sensors: Camera (semantic segmentation), LiDAR (3-D point cloud), Radar (Doppler velocity), Ultrasonic (close-range), GNSS/IMU (position).
- Algorithm: CNNs (YOLO, BEVFormer) for object detection; semantic segmentation (U-Net) for free-space.

**Layer 2 — Localization (1 mark)**
- Sensor: GNSS + IMU + HD map.
- Algorithm: Monte-Carlo Localization or Graph-SLAM using LiDAR scan-matching against HD map.

**Layer 3 — Prediction (1 mark)**
- Input: Tracks from fusion layer.
- Algorithm: LSTM / Transformer-based motion predictors producing 3–5 s occupancy grids.

**Layer 4 — Planning (2 marks)**
- Sub-layers: Route planner (A*/Dijkstra on road graph), Behaviour planner (FSM/RL), Trajectory planner (Frenet-frame polynomial or MPC).

**Layer 5 — Control + Actuation (1 mark)**
- Controllers: PID for longitudinal, LQR/MPC for lateral.
- Actuators: Steer-by-wire, brake-by-wire (regen + friction), throttle-by-wire on EV.

*(Refer to **Figure 4.1** above for the block diagram.)*

---

**(b) (7 Marks): An autonomous EV is travelling at $v = 25$ m/s on a straight highway. The radar reports a stationary obstacle at a range $R = 70$ m with a one-standard-deviation uncertainty of $\sigma_R = 0.5$ m. The camera independently measures the same obstacle at the same range with $\sigma_C = 0.15$ m. The system reaction time is $t_r = 0.1$ s and the maximum achievable deceleration is $a_{brake} = 8$ m/s².**

**Compute:**
1. The fused range estimate and its uncertainty after sensor fusion.
2. The safe stopping distance.
3. Whether the obstacle is within the safe stopping envelope.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Fused Variance (1 mark)**
$$\frac{1}{\sigma_{fuse}^2} = \frac{1}{\sigma_R^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_C^2} = \frac{1}{0.25} + \frac{1}{0.0225}$$

$$\frac{1}{\sigma_{fuse}^2} = 4 + 44.44 = 48.44$$

$$\sigma_{fuse}^2 = 0.02065 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_{fuse} = 0.1437 \, \text{m}$$

Fused range $R_{fuse} \approx 70$ m (assuming unbiased sensors).

**Step 2 — Safe Stopping Distance (2 marks)**
$$d_{safe} = v \cdot t_r + \frac{v^2}{2 a_{brake}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{safe} &= 25 \times 0.1 + \frac{25^2}{2 \times 8} \\
&= 2.5 + \frac{625}{16} \\
&= 2.5 + 39.0625 \\
&= 41.5625 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Add a 3σ Safety Buffer (1 mark)**
$$\begin{aligned}
d_{safe, total} &= d_{safe} + 3 \sigma_{fuse} \\
&= 41.5625 + 3 \times 0.1437 \\
&= 41.5625 + 0.431 \\
&= 41.994 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Decision (1 mark)**
Available range $R = 70$ m > $d_{safe,total} = 41.99$ m.

**Margin = $70 - 41.99 = 28.01$ m.** ✅ The obstacle is safely stoppable.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The system can comfortably execute an emergency brake and bring the EV to a halt with a **28 m margin**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) (7 Marks): Explain the kinematic bicycle model. Derive the yaw-rate equation $\dot{\psi} = \dfrac{v}{L} \tan\delta$ and state the condition under which the model is valid.**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram description (1 mark):** A two-wheeled representation — front wheel at distance $L$ from rear wheel, steering angle $\delta$, both wheels aligned along the vehicle centreline.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Let the rear axle centre be at $(x_r, y_r)$ with heading $\psi$. The vehicle rolls without slipping, so rear-wheel velocity is:

$$\dot{x}_r = v \cos\psi, \quad \dot{y}_r = v \sin\psi$$

The front wheel traces a circular arc about the ICR. The lateral component of rear-wheel velocity must be zero (no-slip):

$$v_{y,rear} = 0 \implies \dot{y}_r \cos\psi - \dot{x}_r \sin\psi = 0$$

For the front wheel, the velocity is rotated by $\delta$:

$$\dot{x}_f = v_f \cos(\psi + \delta)$$

Geometrically, the front wheel is $L$ ahead along the body $x$-axis, so:

$$x_f = x_r + L \cos\psi, \quad y_f = y_r + L \sin\psi$$

Differentiating $y_f$ and substituting yields the yaw-rate:

$$\dot{\psi} = \frac{v \tan\delta}{L}$$

**Validity condition (1 mark):**
- Low speed: $v < 20$ m/s.
- Flat, paved road.
- No significant tyre slip (dry asphalt).
- Steering angle not at the lock (avoid singularities as $\delta \to \pm 90°$).

---

**(b) (7 Marks): An EV uses a Constant Time-Gap ACC with $T_g = 1.5$ s and standstill offset $d_0 = 2$ m. The lead vehicle decelerates uniformly from $v_L = 20$ m/s to $v_L = 10$ m/s in 5 seconds. Calculate:**

1. Lead vehicle deceleration.
2. Required ego acceleration (or deceleration) if ego is travelling at $v_E = 15$ m/s and current gap is $d = 25$ m, assuming $k_p = 0.4$ s⁻² and $k_d = 0.6$ s⁻¹.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Lead vehicle deceleration (2 marks)**
$$a_L = \frac{v_L^{final} - v_L^{initial}}{\Delta t} = \frac{10 - 20}{5} = -2 \, \text{m/s}^2$$

(Negative = decelerating.)

**Step 2 — Desired gap (1 mark)**
$$d_{desired} = v_E \cdot T_g + d_0 = 15 \times 1.5 + 2 = 22.5 + 2 = 24.5 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Gap error (1 mark)**
$$e_{gap} = d - d_{desired} = 25 - 24.5 = 0.5 \, \text{m}$$

(Positive → ego is *farther* than desired → no urgent brake.)

**Step 4 — Relative velocity (1 mark)**
$$\Delta v = v_L - v_E = 10 - 15 = -5 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — ACC command (2 marks)**
$$\begin{aligned}
a_{cmd} &= k_p \cdot e_{gap} + k_d \cdot \Delta v \\
&= 0.4 \times 0.5 + 0.6 \times (-5) \\
&= 0.2 - 3.0 \\
&= -2.8 \, \text{m/s}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Ego must decelerate at **2.8 m/s²** to maintain the safe time-gap as the leader slows. This is within typical EV regen + friction-brake envelope (max ~5 m/s²).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Conflating Level 2 with "autonomous":** Level 2 is **NOT** autonomous — the driver is the fallback-ready user and is legally liable. Many KTU scripts wrongly call Tesla Autopilot "Level 5." Cap penalty: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numericals:** Always write units in every line of derivation. A numerical answer without units is treated as incomplete. Penalty: **½–1 mark per question**.
> 3. **Mixing the kinematic and dynamic bicycle models:** If the question says "kinematic," do not introduce tyre-slip or cornering stiffness. Penalty: **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Treating radar and LiDAR as interchangeable:** Radar measures **velocity directly** via Doppler; LiDAR measures **range via time-of-flight**. Swapping these in a derivation is an instant 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Ignoring the ODD:** Any Level 3/4 answer that does not define the **Operational Design Domain** (speed limit, weather, geofence) loses at least 1 mark in long-answer questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **SAE J3016 defines 6 levels** (0 to 5); the qualitative jump is at **L2 → L3** (driver may divert attention) and **L3 → L4** (system handles fallback within ODD).
- ✅ **L5 does not yet exist** commercially (2024) — exam-safe answer.
- ✅ **Autonomy stack layers:** Perception → Localization → Prediction → Planning → Control → Actuation (6, but KTU often asks for 5 with Localization merged into Perception).
- ✅ **EVs are the *enablers*** of autonomy due to by-wire readiness, regen braking, high-capacity battery for compute, and quiet cabin.
- ✅ **Core sensors:** Camera (semantic), LiDAR (geometry), Radar (velocity, weather-robust), Ultrasonic (close-range), GNSS+IMU (global pose).
- ✅ **Sensor fusion reduces uncertainty**: $\dfrac{1}{\sigma_{fuse}^2} = \sum \dfrac{1}{\sigma_i^2}$ — memorized formula worth **2 marks** on its own.
- ✅ **Bicycle model yaw-rate:** $\dot{\psi} = \dfrac{v \tan\delta}{L}$ — valid for $v < 20$ m/s, no slip.
- ✅ **Safe stopping distance:** $d_{safe} = v \cdot t_r + \dfrac{v^2}{2 a_{brake}}$ — add a $3\sigma$ buffer for measurement uncertainty.
- ✅ **Constant time-gap ACC:** $d_{desired} = v \cdot T_g + d_0$; typical $T_g = 1.2$–$1.8$ s.
- ✅ **Operational Design Domain (ODD)** must be defined for any L3/L4 question — speed, weather, geofence, time-of-day.
- ✅ **Latency budget:** **< 100 ms** end-to-end is the safety-critical ceiling — memorize.
- ✅ **Memorize units:** range in m, speed in m/s, acceleration in m/s², yaw rate in rad/s, curvature in 1/m.
- ✅ **Cyber-security gateway** between autonomy compute and VCU is a single-point-of-failure — expect a follow-up question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET863 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Connected Vehicles (Overview Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Connected Vehicle (CV)** is an automobile equipped with **Internet access** (via WLAN, cellular, or Dedicated Short-Range Communication) and, in most cases, a **wireless Local Area Network (LAN)** that allows the vehicle to communicate with internal and external systems, other vehicles (V2V), infrastructure (V2I), pedestrians (V2P), the network/cloud (V2N), and the broader energy/power grid (V2G) — collectively termed **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)** communication as per the **SAE J3061** and **IEEE 802.11p / 3GPP C-V2X** standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET863 - Module 4)**
> Connected Vehicles integrate **telematics, sensors, embedded computing, and wireless communication** to enable real-time data exchange between the vehicle and its operating environment. This forms the **digital backbone** for Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), autonomous driving, predictive maintenance, and **smart-grid interaction** in modern electric and hybrid vehicles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "A Smartphone on Wheels"

Imagine your car is a **smartphone**. Just as your phone constantly talks to cellular towers, Wi-Fi routers, Bluetooth earphones, and other devices, a **Connected Vehicle** continuously talks to:
- Other cars nearby (think of it as WhatsApp group chats between cars sharing speed, position, and braking data).
- Traffic lights and road signs (think of these as Wi-Fi routers telling your car what's ahead).
- Cloud servers (like your phone's cloud backup — storing trip data, software updates, diagnostics).
- The power grid (like your phone sharing battery status with a charger to optimize charging speed).

This continuous "conversation" makes driving **safer, more efficient, and increasingly autonomous**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Terminology for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **V2V** — Vehicle-to-Vehicle
> - **V2I** — Vehicle-to-Infrastructure
> - **V2P** — Vehicle-to-Pedestrian
> - **V2N** — Vehicle-to-Network (Cloud)
> - **V2G** — Vehicle-to-Grid (highly relevant for EVs)
> - **V2X** — Umbrella term for all of the above
> - **ADAS** — Advanced Driver Assistance Systems
> - **IoT** — Internet of Things
> - **OBU** — On-Board Unit
> - **RSU** — Road-Side Unit

## 1.3 Why Connected Vehicles Matter in the EV/HEV Context

For **Hybrid and Electric Vehicles**, connectivity is not a luxury — it is a **functional necessity** because:
1. **Battery Telematics**: Real-time State-of-Charge (SoC), State-of-Health (SoH), and thermal monitoring require cloud connectivity.
2. **Smart Charging (V2G)**: EVs must communicate with the grid to enable bidirectional power flow and demand-response.
3. **OTA Updates**: Over-The-Air firmware updates for Battery Management Systems (BMS) and motor controllers rely on cellular networks.
4. **Range Optimization**: Cloud-based route planning uses real-time traffic, terrain, and charging-station availability data.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Hint:** Always link connectivity concepts back to **EV-specific applications** (V2G, smart charging, battery analytics) — examiners award bonus marks for showing domain awareness.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Layer Architecture of a Connected Vehicle System

A connected vehicle ecosystem is best understood as a **five-layer stack**:

### Layer 1 — Perception Layer (Sensors)
- **LiDAR, Radar, Camera, Ultrasonic, GNSS/ GPS, IMU**
- Function: Raw data acquisition from the environment.
- Key metric: Sensor **refresh rate (Hz)** and **field-of-view (degrees)**.

### Layer 2 — Communication Layer (V2X)
- **DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communication)** — IEEE 802.11p, 5.9 GHz band, range ~**300 m**, latency <**10 ms**.
- **C-V2X (Cellular V2X)** — 3GPP Release 14/15, LTE/5G-based, range up to **several km**, latency <**5 ms** (5G URLLC).
- Function: Bidirectional data exchange with external entities.

### Layer 3 — Edge/Fog Computing Layer
- **RSUs** and **MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing)** servers.
- Function: Low-latency local processing of safety-critical messages (e.g., collision warnings).

### Layer 4 — Cloud/Backend Layer
- **OEM servers, Big Data analytics, AI/ML pipelines, OTA servers**.
- Function: Long-term storage, predictive analytics, fleet management, navigation.

### Layer 5 — Application/Service Layer
- **ADAS, infotainment, eCall, insurance telematics, smart charging, platooning**.
- Function: End-user and OEM-facing services.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this layered structure matters for exams:** Questions like *"Explain the architecture of a connected vehicle system"* or *"Discuss V2X communication in modern EVs"* expect this top-down layered answer.

## 2.2 V2X Communication — Detailed Breakdown

| **Parameter** | **DSRC (IEEE 802.11p)** | **C-V2X (LTE/5G)** | **Wi-Fi (802.11ac)** | **Bluetooth 5.0** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | ~300 m | Up to 2 km (LTE), >5 km (5G) | ~50–100 m | ~10–200 m |
| **Latency** | <10 ms | <5 ms (5G) | 20–50 ms | 20–100 ms |
| **Data Rate** | 3–27 Mbps | Up to 1 Gbps (5G) | Up to 6.9 Gbps | 2 Mbps |
| **Use Case** | Safety-critical V2V/V2I | Wide-area V2X, C-V2X | Infotainment, garage | In-cabin, diagnostics |
| **Standard** | IEEE 802.11p / ETSI ITS-G5 | 3GPP Rel. 14/15/16 | IEEE 802.11ac | IEEE 802.15.1 |
| **EV Relevance** | Moderate | **Very High** (V2G, platooning) | Low | Moderate (BMS telemetry) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do not confuse **DSRC** with **Wi-Fi**. Both use the 2.4/5 GHz band, but DSRC uses a **dedicated 5.9 GHz spectrum band** allocated by the FCC/ETSI specifically for automotive safety.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is an **Overview Only** topic in Module 4, the formulas below focus on **communication theory** and **EV-specific metrics** that frequently appear in 14-mark questions.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Relation** | **Description & Units** |
|---|---|---|
| **Shannon-Hartley Capacity** | $C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$ | Maximum data rate $C$ in bps; $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (dimensionless). |
| **Communication Latency Budget** | $t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{sense}} + t_{\text{process}} + t_{\text{transmit}} + t_{\text{propagation}}$ | Critical for safety messages; must be <**100 ms** for collision avoidance. |
| **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz; gives path loss in dB. |
| **EV Battery Energy for Connectivity** | $E_{\text{comms}} = P_{\text{OBU}} \cdot t_{\text{operation}}$ | $P_{\text{OBU}}$ typically **2–5 W**; relevant for range estimation. |
| **V2G Power Exchange** | $P_{\text{V2G}} = V_{\text{bus}} \cdot I_{\text{discharge}}$ | Bidirectional charging power; typical residential **3.3–7.4 kW** AC, up to **50 kW** DC fast. |
| **5G URLLC Reliability** | $P_{\text{success}} \geq 1 - 10^{-5}$ | 99.999% packet success for ultra-reliable low-latency communication. |
| **Message Frequency (CAM/DENM)** | $f_{\text{msg}} = 1$ to $10$ Hz | Cooperative Awareness Messages / Decentralized Environmental Notification Messages. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Isolation Note:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` is never used inside table cells. All absolute-value or magnitude expressions use $\vert \cdot \vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Safety**: Forward Collision Warning (FCW), Blind Spot Warning (BSW), Emergency Electronic Brake Light (EEBL) — all rely on V2V.
- **Efficiency**: Eco-routing, predictive cruise control, platooning (truck convoys with <**1 m** gap at 80 km/h).
- **EV-Specific**: V2G for grid stabilization, smart charging schedules, battery second-life monitoring.
- **Insurance**: Usage-Based Insurance (UBI) via telematics data.
- **Autonomous Driving**: **Levels 3–5** of SAE J3016 autonomy are **impossible without V2X** — onboard sensors alone have occlusions and range limits.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> As an **Overview Only** topic, the derivations below focus on **communication-theoretic foundations** and **analytical comparisons** that KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answers. Every algebraic step is explicitly shown.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Shannon Channel Capacity for V2X Link

**Problem:** A C-V2X link uses a 10 MHz channel at 5.9 GHz. The received signal power is **−85 dBm** and the noise floor is **−110 dBm**. Compute the maximum theoretical data rate.

### Step 1 — Convert dBm to linear power

The conversion formula is:

$$P_{\text{linear}} = 10^{\frac{P_{\text{dBm}}}{10}}$$

For the signal:

$$S = 10^{\frac{-85}{10}} = 10^{-8.5} \text{ W}$$

$$S = 3.16 \times 10^{-9} \text{ W} = 3.16 \text{ nW}$$

For the noise:

$$N = 10^{\frac{-110}{10}} = 10^{-11} \text{ W}$$

$$N = 1.0 \times 10^{-11} \text{ W} = 10 \text{ pW}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the SNR

$$\frac{S}{N} = \frac{3.16 \times 10^{-9}}{1.0 \times 10^{-11}} = 316.23$$

Converting to dB for verification:

$$SNR_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(316.23) = 25 \text{ dB}$$

This matches $-85 - (-110) = 25$ dB. ✓

### Step 3 — Apply the Shannon-Hartley Theorem

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

Substituting $B = 10 \times 10^{6}$ Hz and $S/N = 316.23$:

$$C = 10^{7} \cdot \log_2(1 + 316.23)$$

$$C = 10^{7} \cdot \log_2(317.23)$$

Computing the logarithm:

$$\log_2(317.23) = \frac{\log_{10}(317.23)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{2.5013}{0.3010} \approx 8.31 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

### Step 4 — Final Result

$$C = 10^{7} \times 8.31 = 8.31 \times 10^{7} \text{ bps} \approx 83.1 \text{ Mbps}$$

> **Interpretation:** A 10 MHz C-V2X channel with 25 dB SNR can support ~**83 Mbps**, which is sufficient for raw sensor sharing (LiDAR: ~10–30 Mbps, Camera: ~5–20 Mbps).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Free-Space Path Loss for V2I Link

**Problem:** A vehicle communicates with an RSU located **500 m** away. The carrier frequency is **5.9 GHz**. Compute the free-space path loss.

### Step 1 — Unit Conversion

$$d = 500 \text{ m} = 0.5 \text{ km}$$

$$f = 5.9 \text{ GHz} = 5900 \text{ MHz}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the FSPL Formula

$$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$

Substituting:

$$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(0.5) + 20 \log_{10}(5900) + 32.44$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate Each Term

$$20 \log_{10}(0.5) = 20 \times (-0.3010) = -6.02 \text{ dB}$$

$$20 \log_{10}(5900) = 20 \times 3.771 = 75.42 \text{ dB}$$

$$32.44 = 32.44 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 4 — Sum

$$FSPL = -6.02 + 75.42 + 32.44 = 101.84 \text{ dB}$$

> **Interpretation:** At 500 m and 5.9 GHz, ~**102 dB** of path loss occurs. The transmitter must compensate with adequate antenna gain and power to maintain link budget.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SAE Autonomy Levels vs. Connectivity Dependency

This matrix is **high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions** on connected vehicles and autonomous driving.

| **SAE Level** | **Name** | **Steering/Throttle/Brake** | **Environment Monitoring** | **Connectivity Requirement** | **EV/HEV Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | No Automation | Human | Human | None (optional telematics) | Conventional HEV |
| **1** | Driver Assistance | Human + System (one control) | Human | Low (V2I speed limit data) | Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) in Toyota Prius |
| **2** | Partial Automation | System (combined) | Human | Medium (V2V blind-spot) | Tesla Autopilot (basic), GM Super Cruise |
| **3** | Conditional Automation | System | System (fallback to human) | **High (V2X mandatory)** | Mercedes Drive Pilot, Honda Legend |
| **4** | High Automation | System | System | **Very High (V2X + HD maps)** | Waymo robotaxi, Cruise Origin |
| **5** | Full Automation | System | System (all conditions) | **Critical (V2X + 5G + edge AI)** | Future Tesla Robotaxi, Mobileye |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always state explicitly that **Levels 3+ autonomy is practically impossible without V2X connectivity** due to the inherent limitations of onboard sensors (occlusion, range, weather dependence).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: V2X Use-Case Classification

| **Use-Case Class** | **Latency Requirement** | **Reliability** | **Range** | **Example Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Safety-Critical (V2V/V2P)** | <10 ms | 99.999% | <300 m | Collision avoidance, EEBL |
| **Traffic Efficiency (V2I)** | <100 ms | 99.9% | <2 km | Signal phase timing, GLOSA |
| **Infotainment (V2N)** | <500 ms | 99% | Unlimited | Streaming, OTA updates |
| **V2G / Smart Grid** | <1 s | 99% | Wide-area | Demand response, peak shaving |
| **Platooning (V2V)** | <20 ms | 99.99% | <100 m | Truck convoy coordination |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define Connected Vehicles. List any four V2X communication types.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Connected Vehicle** is a vehicle equipped with **internet connectivity** and **wireless communication modules (OBU)** that enable bidirectional data exchange with other vehicles, infrastructure, networks, and the power grid.

The four V2X communication types are:
1. **V2V** — Vehicle-to-Vehicle
2. **V2I** — Vehicle-to-Infrastructure
3. **V2P** — Vehicle-to-Pedestrian
4. **V2N** — Vehicle-to-Network (Cloud)

> A fifth important type for EV context: **V2G** — Vehicle-to-Grid.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Differentiate between DSRC and C-V2X communication.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **DSRC (IEEE 802.11p)** | **C-V2X (3GPP LTE/5G)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Wi-Fi derivative (IEEE) | Cellular (3GPP) |
| **Latency** | ~10 ms | <5 ms (5G) |
| **Range** | ~300 m | Up to several km |
| **Spectrum** | Dedicated 5.9 GHz | Licensed cellular bands |
| **Evolution Path** | Static standard | Evolutionary (4G → 5G → 6G) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**"Connected Vehicles form the digital backbone of modern electric mobility." With reference to this statement, answer the following:**

#### (a) Explain the layered architecture of a connected vehicle system with a neat block diagram. Discuss V2G and its role in smart grid integration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Layered Architecture (5 marks):**
A connected vehicle system is organized into **five functional layers**:

1. **Perception Layer:** Comprises onboard sensors — **LiDAR, Radar, Cameras, GNSS/GPS, and IMU** — that collect raw data about the vehicle's state and surroundings.
2. **Communication Layer:** Uses **DSRC (IEEE 802.11p)** or **C-V2X (LTE/5G)** to exchange data via V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N, and V2G channels.
3. **Edge/Fog Layer:** Includes **RSUs and MEC servers** for low-latency local processing of safety-critical messages.
4. **Cloud Backend:** Hosts **OEM servers, Big Data analytics, AI/ML models, and OTA update pipelines**.
5. **Application Layer:** Delivers end-user services such as **ADAS, infotainment, eCall, fleet management, and smart charging**.

**V2G and Smart Grid Role (2 marks):**
**Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** enables bidirectional power flow between the EV battery and the utility grid. During peak demand, the EV **discharges** into the grid; during off-peak, it **charges**. This requires the EV's **Telematics Control Unit (TCU)** to communicate with the **utility aggregator** via cloud APIs. V2G supports **grid stabilization, renewable energy integration, demand-response programs, and reduces charging costs** for EV owners.

> [!TIP]
> **Block diagram must be drawn** for full marks — refer to SECTION 4 diagrams.

---

#### (b) With a comparison table, differentiate between V2V, V2I, V2N, and V2G communication in terms of range, latency, data rate, and primary use case. Briefly explain the role of 5G URLLC in autonomous driving. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **V2V** | **V2I** | **V2N** | **V2G** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | <300 m | <2 km | Unlimited (cellular) | Wide-area (cloud) |
| **Latency** | <10 ms | <50 ms | <500 ms | <1 s |
| **Data Rate** | 3–27 Mbps | Up to 1 Gbps | Up to 10 Gbps | Low (<1 Mbps) |
| **Primary Use** | Collision avoidance, platooning | Traffic signal info, GLOSA | OTA updates, infotainment | Smart charging, grid support |

**5G URLLC Role (2 marks):**
**5G Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication (URLLC)** provides:
- **Latency <1 ms** end-to-end
- **Reliability 99.999%** (five nines)
- **Deterministic QoS** for safety-critical messages

For **SAE Level 3+ autonomous driving**, 5G URLLC is **essential** because onboard sensors alone suffer from **occlusion, weather degradation, and limited range**. V2X over 5G URLLC provides **non-line-of-sight perception**, **cooperative perception**, and **real-time HD map updates**, enabling safe autonomous operation in complex urban environments.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**"Cybersecurity, data privacy, and standardization are the three biggest challenges in connected vehicle deployment." Critically discuss this statement.**

#### (a) Explain the cybersecurity threats specific to connected vehicles. List the relevant ISO/SAE standards that govern automotive cybersecurity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Cybersecurity Threats (5 marks):**

1. **Remote Vehicle Hacking:** Attackers exploit cellular/Wi-Fi/Bluetooth interfaces to gain unauthorized control over steering, braking, and acceleration (e.g., the 2015 Jeep Cherokee hack via UConnect).
2. **CAN Bus Injection:** Since the Controller Area Network (CAN) was designed without authentication, attackers can inject malicious messages via the **OBD-II port** or compromised ECUs.
3. **V2X Message Spoofing:** Forged V2V/V2I messages (e.g., fake collision warnings) can cause erratic vehicle behavior.
4. **OTA Update Tampering:** Compromised firmware updates can introduce backdoors or disable safety features.
5. **Data Privacy Violations:** Continuous location tracking, biometric data, and driver behavior profiling can be exfiltrated via compromised telematics units.
6. **Denial-of-Service (DoS):** Jamming the 5.9 GHz band can disable V2X safety features.

**Relevant Standards (2 marks):**
- **ISO/SAE 21434** — "Road vehicles — Cybersecurity engineering" (lifecycle security process).
- **UNECE WP.29 R155** — Cybersecurity Management System (CSMS) regulatory requirement.
- **SAE J3061** — Cybersecurity Guidebook for Cyber-Physical Vehicle Systems.
- **ISO 27001** — Information security management (for OEM backend systems).

---

#### (b) Discuss the role of Big Data and AI/ML in connected vehicle analytics. How do predictive maintenance and range estimation for EVs benefit from cloud connectivity? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Big Data & AI/ML Role (3 marks):**
Connected vehicles generate **terabytes of data per day** from sensors, CAN bus, infotainment, and V2X messages. **Big Data platforms (Apache Kafka, Hadoop, Spark)** ingest this streaming data, while **AI/ML models** (LSTMs, Random Forests, Transformers) perform:
- **Driver behavior profiling**
- **Traffic pattern prediction**
- **Anomaly detection for cybersecurity**
- **Energy consumption optimization**

**Predictive Maintenance for EVs (2 marks):**
By continuously uploading **battery telemetry (voltage, current, temperature, SoC, impedance)** to the cloud, AI models can:
- Predict **battery degradation** weeks in advance.
- Detect **anomalous cell behavior** before failure.
- Schedule **proactive service appointments**, reducing downtime.
- Estimate **battery State-of-Health (SoH)** using data-driven models.

**Range Estimation Benefit (2 marks):**
Cloud-connected EVs receive **real-time data** on:
- Traffic conditions (historical and predictive)
- Elevation and terrain (HD maps)
- Weather forecasts (affects HVAC load)
- Charging station availability and pricing
- Driver behavior patterns (from past trips)

The cloud's **Eco-Routing AI** computes an **optimal route** that minimizes energy consumption, reducing **range anxiety** — a critical barrier to EV adoption.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse V2G with V2H (Vehicle-to-Home).** V2G feeds power to the **utility grid**; V2H powers a **single home**.
> 2. **Always state the latency unit (ms) and range unit (m/km)** when comparing V2X types — vague answers lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Cite standards with numbers**: ISO/SAE **21434** (not just "ISO 21434"), IEEE **802.11p** (not just "DSRC").
> 4. **In 14-mark answers, always include a diagram** — it alone is worth **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **For EV-specific questions, link connectivity to battery/grid/charging** — pure telecom answers without EV context lose marks in PEMET863.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Connected Vehicle** = Smart car with internet + wireless V2X communication.
- **V2X umbrella** includes **V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N, V2G**.
- **DSRC** = IEEE 802.11p, 5.9 GHz, <300 m, <10 ms; **C-V2X** = 3GPP LTE/5G, km range, <5 ms.
- **Five-layer architecture**: Perception → Communication → Edge → Cloud → Application.
- **OBU** (On-Board Unit) is the in-vehicle communication module; **RSU** (Road-Side Unit) is the infrastructure-side counterpart.
- **SAE J3016** defines 6 autonomy levels (**0 to 5**); Levels 3+ **mandate V2X**.
- **V2G** enables bidirectional EV↔grid power flow, critical for **smart grid** and **demand response**.
- **5G URLLC** provides <1 ms latency and 99.999% reliability — essential for autonomous driving.
- **Cybersecurity standards**: ISO/SAE **21434**, UNECE **R155**, SAE **J3061**.
- **Shannon capacity** $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$ governs maximum V2X data rate.
- **OTA updates** allow remote firmware upgrades for BMS, motor controllers, and infotainment.
- **Predictive maintenance** and **range estimation** are the two biggest AI/ML-driven benefits of EV connectivity.
- **CAM (Cooperative Awareness Messages)** broadcast at **1–10 Hz** for V2V safety.
- **Platooning** (truck convoys) requires <20 ms V2V latency with 99.99% reliability.
- For KTU PEMET863, **always link connectivity to EV context**: battery analytics, V2G, smart charging, range optimization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Connected Vehicles = Sensors + V2X + Cloud + AI, all working together to make EVs safer, smarter, and grid-friendly."* Memorize this one-liner — it can anchor answers to almost any question in this module.
